Vanity Fair
by
William Makepeace Thackeray
before the CURTAIN
as the manager of the performance sit before the curtain on the board
and look into the Fair, a feeling of profound melancholy come over
he in his survey of the bustling place. there be a great quantity of
eating and drink, make love and jilt, laugh and the
contrary, smoking, cheating, fighting, dancing and fiddle; there be
bully push about, buck ogle the woman, knave pick pocket,
policeman on the look-out, quack (other quack, plague take they!)
bawl in front of their booth, and yokel look up at the
tinsel dancer and poor old rouge tumbler, while the
light-finger folk be operate upon their pocket behind. yes, this
be VANITY FAIR; not a moral place certainly; nor a merry one, though
very noisy. look at the face of the actor and buffoon when they
come off from their business; and Tom Fool wash the paint off his
cheek before he sit down to dinner with his wife and the little Jack
Puddings behind the canvas. the curtain will be up presently, and he
will be turn over head and heel, and cry, "how be you?"
a man with a reflective turn of mind, walk through an exhibition of
this sort, will not be oppress, I take it, by his own or other
people's hilarity. an episode of humour or kindness touch and
amuse he here and there--a pretty child look at a gingerbread
stall; a pretty girl blushing whilst her lover talk to she and choose
her fair; poor Tom Fool, yonder behind the waggon, mumble his bone
with the honest family which live by his tumbling; but the general
impression be one more melancholy than mirthful. when you come home
you sit down in a sober, contemplative, not uncharitable frame of mind,
and apply yourself to your book or your business.
I have no other moral than this to tag to the present story of "Vanity
Fair." some people consider Fairs immoral altogether, and eschew such,
with their servant and family: very likely they be right. but
person who think otherwise, and be of a lazy, or a benevolent, or a
sarcastic mood, may perhaps like to step in for half an hour, and look
at the performance. there be scene of all sort; some dreadful
combat, some grand and lofty horse-riding, some scene of high life,
and some of very middling indeed; some love-making for the sentimental,
and some light comic business; the whole accompany by appropriate
scenery and brilliantly illuminate with the Author's own candle.
what more have the Manager of the Performance to say?--to acknowledge
the kindness with which it have be receive in all the principal town
of England through which the Show have pass, and where it have be
most favourably notice by the respected conductor of the public
Press, and by the Nobility and Gentry. he be proud to think that his
puppet have give satisfaction to the very good company in this
empire. the famous little Becky Puppet have be pronounce to be
uncommonly flexible in the joint, and lively on the wire; the Amelia
Doll, though it have have a small circle of admirer, have yet be
carve and dress with the great care by the artist; the Dobbin
Figure, though apparently clumsy, yet dance in a very amusing and
natural manner; the Little Boys' Dance have be like by some; and
please to remark the richly dress figure of the Wicked Nobleman, on
which no expense have be spar, and which Old Nick will fetch away at
the end of this singular performance.
and with this, and a profound bow to his patron, the Manager retire,
and the curtain rise.
LONDON, June 28, 1848

CHAPTER I
Chiswick Mall
while the present century be in its teen, and on one sunshiny morning
in June, there drive up to the great iron gate of Miss Pinkerton's
academy for young lady, on Chiswick Mall, a large family coach, with
two fat horse in blaze harness, drive by a fat coachman in a
three-cornered hat and wig, at the rate of four mile an hour. a black
servant, who repose on the box beside the fat coachman, uncurl his
bandy leg as soon as the equipage draw up opposite Miss Pinkerton's
shin brass plate, and as he pull the bell at least a score of
young head be see peer out of the narrow window of the stately
old brick house. Nay, the acute observer might have recognize the
little red nose of good-natured Miss Jemima Pinkerton herself, rise
over some geranium pot in the window of that lady's own drawing-room.
"it be Mrs. Sedley's coach, sister," say Miss Jemima. "Sambo, the
black servant, have just ring the bell; and the coachman have a new red
waistcoat."
"have you complete all the necessary preparation incident to Miss
Sedley's departure, Miss Jemima?" ask Miss Pinkerton herself, that
majestic lady; the Semiramis of Hammersmith, the friend of Doctor
Johnson, the correspondent of Mrs. Chapone herself.
"the girl be up at four this morning, pack her trunk, sister,"
reply Miss Jemima; "we have make she a bow-pot."
"say a bouquet, sister Jemima, 'tis more genteel."
"well, a booky as big almost as a haystack; I have put up two bottle
of the gillyflower water for Mrs. Sedley, and the receipt for make
it, in Amelia's box."
"and I trust, Miss Jemima, you have make a copy of Miss Sedley's
account. this be it, be it? very good--ninety-three pound, four
shilling. be kind enough to address it to John Sedley, Esquire, and
to seal this billet which I have write to his lady."
in Miss Jemima's eye an autograph letter of her sister, Miss
Pinkerton, be an object of as deep veneration as would have be a
letter from a sovereign. only when her pupil quit the
establishment, or when they be about to be marry, and once, when
poor Miss Birch die of the scarlet fever, be Miss Pinkerton know to
write personally to the parent of her pupil; and it be Jemima's
opinion that if anything could console Mrs. Birch for her daughter's
loss, it would be that pious and eloquent composition in which Miss
Pinkerton announce the event.
in the present instance Miss Pinkerton's "billet" be to the follow
effect:--
the Mall, Chiswick, June 15, 18
MADAM,--After her six year' residence at the Mall, I have the honour
and happiness of present Miss Amelia Sedley to her parent, as a
young lady not unworthy to occupy a fitting position in their polished
and refined circle. those virtue which characterize the young English
gentlewoman, those accomplishment which become her birth and station,
will not be find want in the amiable Miss Sedley, whose industry
and obedience have endear she to her instructor, and whose
delightful sweetness of temper have charm her AGED and her YOUTHFUL
companion.
in music, in dancing, in orthography, in every variety of embroidery
and needlework, she will be find to have realize her friend' fond
wish. in geography there be still much to be desire; and a careful
and undeviating use of the backboard, for four hour daily during the
next three year, be recommend as necessary to the acquirement of
that dignified deportment and carriage, so requisite for every young
lady of FASHION.
in the principle of religion and morality, Miss Sedley will be find
worthy of an establishment which have be honour by the presence of
the great LEXICOGRAPHER, and the patronage of the admirable Mrs.
Chapone. in leave the Mall, Miss Amelia carry with she the heart
of her companion, and the affectionate regard of her mistress, who
have the honour to subscribe herself,
Madam, your most obliged humble servant, BARBARA PINKERTON
p.s.--miss Sharp accompanie Miss Sedley. it be particularly request
that Miss Sharp's stay in Russell Square may not exceed ten day. the
family of distinction with whom she be engage, desire to avail
themselves of her service as soon as possible.
this letter complete, Miss Pinkerton proceed to write her own name,
and Miss Sedley's, in the fly-leaf of a Johnson's Dictionary--the
interesting work which she invariably present to her scholar, on
their departure from the Mall. on the cover be insert a copy of
"line address to a young lady on quit Miss Pinkerton's school,
at the Mall; by the late revere Doctor Samuel Johnson." in fact, the
Lexicographer's name be always on the lip of this majestic woman, and
a visit he have pay to she be the cause of her reputation and her
fortune.
be command by her eld sister to get "the Dictionary" from the
cupboard, Miss Jemima have extract two copy of the book from the
receptacle in question. when Miss Pinkerton have finish the
inscription in the first, Jemima, with rather a dubious and timid air,
hand she the second.
"for whom be this, Miss Jemima?" say Miss Pinkerton, with awful
coldness.
"for Becky Sharp," answer Jemima, tremble very much, and blush
over her withered face and neck, as she turn she back on her sister.
"for Becky Sharp: shebe go too."
"MISS JEMIMA!" exclaim Miss Pinkerton, in the large capital. "be
you in your sens? replace the Dixonary in the closet, and never
venture to take such a liberty in future."
"well, sister, itbe only two-and-ninepence, and poor Becky will be
miserable if she donot get one."
"send Miss Sedley instantly to I," say Miss Pinkerton. and so
venture not to say another word, poor Jemima trot off, exceedingly
flurried and nervous.
Miss Sedley's papa be a merchant in London, and a man of some wealth;
whereas Miss Sharp be an articled pupil, for whom Miss Pinkerton have
do, as she think, quite enough, without confer upon she at
part the high honour of the Dixonary.
although schoolmistress' letter be to be trust no more nor less
than churchyard epitaph; yet, as it sometimes happen that a person
depart this life who be really deserve of all the praise the stone
cutter carve over his bone; who be a good Christian, a good parent,
child, wife, or husband; who actually do leave a disconsolate family
to mourn his loss; so in academy of the male and female sex it occur
every now and then that the pupil be fully worthy of the praise
bestow by the disinterested instructor. now, Miss Amelia Sedley be a
young lady of this singular specie; and deserve not only all that
Miss Pinkerton say in her praise, but have many charming quality
which that pompous old Minerva of a woman could not see, from the
difference of rank and age between her pupil and herself.
for she could not only sing like a lark, or a Mrs. Billington, and
dance like Hillisberg or Parisot; and embroider beautifully; and spell
as well as a Dixonary itself; but she have such a kindly, smile,
tender, gentle, generous heart of her own, as win the love of everybody
who come near she, from Minerva herself down to the poor girl in the
scullery, and the one-eyed tart-woman's daughter, who be permit to
vend her ware once a week to the young lady in the Mall. she have
twelve intimate and bosom friend out of the twenty-four young lady.
even envious Miss Briggs never speak ill of she; high and mighty Miss
Saltire (Lord Dexter's granddaughter) allow that her figure be
genteel; and as for Miss Swartz, the rich woolly-haired mulatto from
St. Kitt's, on the day Amelia go away, she be in such a passion of
tear that they be oblige to send for Dr. Floss, and half tipsify
she with salvolatile. Miss Pinkerton's attachment be, as may be
suppose from the high position and eminent virtue of that lady, calm
and dignified; but Miss Jemima have already whimper several time at
the idea of Amelia's departure; and, but for fear of her sister, would
have go off in downright hysteric, like the heiress (who pay
double) of St. Kitt's. such luxury of grief, however, be only allow
to parlour-boarder. Honest Jemima have all the bill, and the washing,
and the mending, and the pudding, and the plate and crockery, and the
servant to superintend. but why speak about she? it be probable that
we shall not hear of she again from this moment to the end of time, and
that when the great filigree iron gate be once close on she, she and
her awful sister will never issue therefrom into this little world of
history.
but as we be to see a great deal of Amelia, there be no harm in
say, at the outset of our acquaintance, that she be a dear little
creature; and a great mercy it be, both in life and in novel, which
(and the latter especially) abound in villain of the most sombre sort,
that we be to have for a constant companion so guileless and
good-natured a person. as she be not a heroine, there be no need to
describe her person; indeed I be afraid that her nose be rather short
than otherwise, and her cheek a great deal too round and red for a
heroine; but her face blush with rosy health, and her lip with the
fresh of smile, and she have a pair of eye which sparkle with the
bright and honest good-humour, except indeed when they fill
with tear, and that be a great deal too often; for the silly thing
would cry over a dead canary-bird; or over a mouse, that the cat haply
have seize upon; or over the end of a novel, be it ever so stupid;
and as for say an unkind word to she, be any person hard-hearted
enough to do so--why, so much the bad for they. even Miss Pinkerton,
that austere and godlike woman, cease scold she after the first
time, and though she no more comprehend sensibility than she do
Algebra, give all master and teacher particular order to treat Miss
Sedley with the utmost gentleness, as harsh treatment be injurious to
she.
so that when the day of departure come, between her two custom of
laugh and cry, Miss Sedley be greatly puzzle how to act. she
be glad to go home, and yet most woefully sad at leave school. for
three day before, little Laura Martin, the orphan, follow she about
like a little dog. she have to make and receive at least fourteen
present--to make fourteen solemn promise of writing every week:
"send my letter under cover to my grandpapa, the Earl of Dexter," say
Miss Saltire (who, by the way, be rather shabby). "never mind the
postage, but write every day, you dear darling," say the impetuous and
woolly-head, but generous and affectionate Miss Swartz; and the
orphan little Laura Martin (who be just in round-hand), take she
friend's hand and say, look up in her face wistfully, "Amelia, when
I write to you I shall call you Mamma." all which detail, I have no
doubt, JONES, who read this book at his Club, will pronounce to be
excessively foolish, trivial, twaddling, and ultra-sentimental. yes; I
can see Jones at this minute (rather flushed with his joint of mutton
and half pint of wine), take out his pencil and score under the
word "foolish, twaddling," &c., and add to they his own remark of
"quite true." well, he be a lofty man of genius, and admire the great
and heroic in life and novel; and so have well take warning and go
elsewhere.
well, then. the flower, and the present, and the trunk, and
bonnet-box of Miss Sedley have be arrange by Mr. Sambo in the
carriage, together with a very small and weather-beat old cow's-skin
trunk with Miss Sharp's card neatly nail upon it, which be deliver
by Sambo with a grin, and pack by the coachman with a correspond
sneer--the hour for parting come; and the grief of that moment be
considerably lessen by the admirable discourse which Miss Pinkerton
address to her pupil. not that the parting speech cause Amelia to
philosophise, or that it arm she in any way with a calmness, the
result of argument; but it be intolerably dull, pompous, and tedious;
and have the fear of her schoolmistress greatly before her eye, Miss
Sedley do not venture, in her presence, to give way to any ebullition
of private grief. a seed-cake and a bottle of wine be produce in
the drawing-room, as on the solemn occasion of the visit of parent,
and these refreshment be partake of, Miss Sedley be at liberty to
depart.
"youwill go in and say good-by to Miss Pinkerton, Becky!" say Miss
Jemima to a young lady of whom nobody take any notice, and who be
come downstairs with her own bandbox.
"I suppose I must," say Miss Sharp calmly, and much to the wonder of
Miss Jemima; and the latter have knock at the door, and receive
permission to come in, Miss Sharp advance in a very unconcerned
manner, and say in French, and with a perfect accent, "Mademoiselle,
je vien vous faire me adieux."
Miss Pinkerton do not understand French; she only direct those who
do: but bite her lip and throw up her venerable and roman-nosed
head (on the top of which figure a large and solemn turban), she say,
"Miss Sharp, I wish you a good morning." as the Hammersmith Semiramis
speak, she wave one hand, both by way of adieu, and to give Miss Sharp
an opportunity of shake one of the finger of the hand which be leave
out for that purpose.
Miss Sharp only fold her own hand with a very frigid smile and bow,
and quite decline to accept the proffered honour; on which Semiramis
toss up her turban more indignantly than ever. in fact, it be a
little battle between the young lady and the old one, and the latter
be worst. "Heaven bless you, my child," say she, embrace Amelia,
and scowl the while over the girl's shoulder at Miss Sharp. "come
away, Becky," say Miss Jemima, pull the young woman away in great
alarm, and the drawing-room door close upon they for ever.
then come the struggle and part below. word refuse to tell it. all
the servant be there in the hall--all the dear friend--all the young
lady--the dancing-master who have just arrive; and there be such a
scuffling, and hugging, and kissing, and cry, with the hysterical
YOOPS of Miss Swartz, the parlour-boarder, from her room, as no pen can
depict, and as the tender heart would fain pass over. the embracing be
over; they part--that be, Miss Sedley part from her friend. Miss
Sharp have demurely enter the carriage some minute before. nobody
cry for leave she.
Sambo of the bandy leg slam the carriage door on his young weeping
mistress. he spring up behind the carriage. "stop!" cry Miss
Jemima, rush to the gate with a parcel.
"itbe some sandwich, my dear," say she to Amelia. "you may be
hungry, you know; and Becky, Becky Sharp, herebe a book for you that my
sister--that be, I--Johnson's Dixonary, you know; you mustnot leave we
without that. good-by. drive on, coachman. God bless you!"
and the kind creature retreat into the garden, overcome with emotion.
but, lo! and just as the coach drive off, Miss Sharp put her pale face
out of the window and actually fling the book back into the garden.
this almost cause Jemima to faint with terror. "well, I never"--said
she--"what an audacious"--emotion prevent she from complete either
sentence. the carriage roll away; the great gate be close; the
bell ring for the dancing lesson. the world be before the two young
lady; and so, farewell to Chiswick Mall.
CHAPTER II
in which Miss Sharp and Miss Sedley prepare to open the campaign
when Miss Sharp have perform the heroical act mention in the last
chapter, and have see the Dixonary, fly over the pavement of the
little garden, fall at length at the foot of the astonish Miss
Jemima, the young lady's countenance, which have before wear an almost
livid look of hatred, assume a smile that perhaps be scarcely more
agreeable, and she sink back in the carriage in an easy frame of mind,
saying--"so much for the Dixonary; and, thank God, Ibe out of Chiswick."
Miss Sedley be almost as flurry at the act of defiance as Miss
Jemima have be; for, consider, it be but one minute that she have leave
school, and the impression of six year be not get over in that space
of time. Nay, with some person those awe and terror of youth last
for ever and ever. I know, for instance, an old gentleman of
sixty-eight, who say to I one morning at breakfast, with a very
agitated countenance, "I dream last night that I be flog by Dr.
Raine." Fancy have carry he back five-and-fifty year in the course
of that evening. Dr. Raine and his rod be just as awful to he in
his heart, then, at sixty-eight, as they have be at thirteen. if the
Doctor, with a large birch, have appear bodily to he, even at the age
of threescore and eight, and have say in awful voice, "boy, take down
your pant--"? well, well, Miss Sedley be exceedingly alarmed at this
act of insubordination.
"how could you do so, Rebecca?" at last she say, after a pause.
"why, do you think Miss Pinkerton will come out and order I back to
the black-hole?" say Rebecca, laugh.
"no: but--"
"I hate the whole house," continue Miss Sharp in a fury. "I hope I
may never set eye on it again. I wish it be in the bottom of the
Thames, I do; and if Miss Pinkerton be there, I wouldnot pick she
out, that I wouldnot. o how I should like to see she float in the
water yonder, turban and all, with her train streaming after she, and
her nose like the beak of a wherry."
"hush!" cry Miss Sedley.
"why, will the black footman tell tale?" cry Miss Rebecca, laugh.
"he may go back and tell Miss Pinkerton that I hate she with all my
soul; and I wish he would; and I wish I have a means of prove it, too.
for two year I have only have insult and outrage from she. I have be
treat bad than any servant in the kitchen. I have never have a
friend or a kind word, except from you. I have be make to tend the
little girl in the low schoolroom, and to talk French to the Misses,
until I grow sick of my mother tongue. but that talk French to Miss
Pinkerton be capital fun, benot it? she donot know a word of
French, and be too proud to confess it. I believe it be that which
make her part with I; and so thank Heaven for French. Vive la France!
Vive l'Empereur! Vive Bonaparte!"
"o rebecca, Rebecca, for shame!" cry Miss Sedley; for this be the
great blasphemy Rebecca have as yet utter; and in those day, in
England, to say, "long live Bonaparte!" be as much as to say, "long
live Lucifer!" "how can you--how dare you have such wicked, revengeful
thought?"
"revenge may be wicked, but itbe natural," answer Miss Rebecca. "Ibe
no angel." and, to say the truth, she certainly be not.
for it may be remark in the course of this little conversation (which
take place as the coach roll along lazily by the river side) that
though Miss Rebecca Sharp have twice have occasion to thank Heaven, it
have be, in the first place, for rid she of some person whom she
hat, and secondly, for enable she to bring her enemy to some sort
of perplexity or confusion; neither of which be very amiable motif
for religious gratitude, or such as would be put forward by person of
a kind and placable disposition. Miss Rebecca be not, then, in the
least kind or placable. all the world use her ill, say this young
misanthropist, and we may be pretty certain that person whom all the
world treat ill, deserve entirely the treatment they get. the world
be a look-glass, and give back to every man the reflection of his
own face. Frown at it, and it will in turn look sourly upon you; laugh
at it and with it, and it be a jolly kind companion; and so let all
young person take their choice. this be certain, that if the world
neglect Miss Sharp, she never be know to have do a good action in
behalf of anybody; nor can it be expect that twenty-four young lady
should all be as amiable as the heroine of this work, Miss Sedley (whom
we have select for the very reason that she be the well-natured of
all, otherwise what on earth be to have prevent we from put up
Miss Swartz, or Miss Crump, or Miss Hopkins, as heroine in her place!)
it could not be expect that every one should be of the humble and
gentle temper of Miss Amelia Sedley; should take every opportunity to
vanquish Rebecca's hard-heartedness and ill-humour; and, by a thousand
kind word and office, overcome, for once at least, her hostility to
her kind.
Miss Sharp's father be an artist, and in that quality have give
lesson of draw at Miss Pinkerton's school. he be a clever man; a
pleasant companion; a careless student; with a great propensity for
run into debt, and a partiality for the tavern. when he be drunk,
he use to beat his wife and daughter; and the next morning, with a
headache, he would rail at the world for its neglect of his genius, and
abuse, with a good deal of cleverness, and sometimes with perfect
reason, the fool, his brother painter. as it be with the utmost
difficulty that he could keep himself, and as he owe money for a mile
round Soho, where he live, he think to better his circumstance by
marry a young woman of the french nation, who be by profession an
opera-girl. the humble calling of her female parent Miss Sharp never
allude to, but use to state subsequently that the Entrechats be a
noble family of Gascony, and take great pride in her descent from they.
and curious it be that as she advance in life this young lady's
ancestor increase in rank and splendour.
Rebecca's mother have have some education somewhere, and her daughter
speak French with purity and a parisian accent. it be in those day
rather a rare accomplishment, and lead to her engagement with the
orthodox Miss Pinkerton. for her mother be dead, her father,
find himself not likely to recover, after his third attack of
delirium tremen, write a manly and pathetic letter to Miss Pinkerton,
recommend the orphan child to her protection, and so descend to
the grave, after two bailiff have quarrel over his corpse. rebecca
be seventeen when she come to Chiswick, and be bind over as an
article pupil; her duty be to talk French, as we have see; and
her privilege to live cost free, and, with a few guineas a year, to
gather scrap of knowledge from the professor who attend the school.
she be small and slight in person; pale, sandy-haired, and with eye
habitually cast down: when they look up they be very large, odd,
and attractive; so attractive that the Reverend Mr. Crisp, fresh from
Oxford, and curate to the Vicar of Chiswick, the Reverend Mr.
Flowerdew, fall in love with Miss Sharp; be shoot dead by a glance of
her eye which be fire all the way across Chiswick Church from the
school-pew to the reading-desk. this infatuated young man use
sometimes to take tea with Miss Pinkerton, to whom he have be
present by his mamma, and actually propose something like marriage
in an intercepted note, which the one-eyed apple-woman be charge to
deliver. Mrs. Crisp be summon from Buxton, and abruptly carry off
her darling boy; but the idea, even, of such an eagle in the Chiswick
dovecot cause a great flutter in the breast of Miss Pinkerton, who
would have send away Miss Sharp but that she be bind to she under a
forfeit, and who never could thoroughly believe the young lady's
protestation that she have never exchange a single word with Mr.
Crisp, except under her own eye on the two occasion when she have meet
he at tea.
by the side of many tall and bounce young lady in the
establishment, Rebecca Sharp look like a child. but she have the
dismal precocity of poverty. many a dun have she talk to, and turn
away from her father's door; many a tradesman have she coax and
wheedle into good-humour, and into the granting of one meal more. she
sate commonly with her father, who be very proud of her wit, and hear
the talk of many of his wild companion--often but ill-suited for a
girl to hear. but she never have be a girl, she say; she have be a
woman since she be eight year old. oh, why do Miss Pinkerton let
such a dangerous bird into her cage?
the fact be, the old lady believe Rebecca to be the meek creature
in the world, so admirably, on the occasion when her father bring
she to Chiswick, use Rebecca to perform the part of the ingenue; and
only a year before the arrangement by which Rebecca have be admit
into her house, and when Rebecca be sixteen year old, Miss Pinkerton
majestically, and with a little speech, make she a present of a
doll--which be, by the way, the confiscate property of Miss Swindle,
discover surreptitiously nurse it in school-hour. how the father
and daughter laugh as they trudge home together after the evening
party (it be on the occasion of the speech, when all the professor
be invite) and how Miss Pinkerton would have rag have she see the
caricature of herself which the little mimic, Rebecca, manage to make
out of her doll. Becky use to go through dialogue with it; it form
the delight of Newman Street, Gerrard Street, and the artist' quarter:
and the young painter, when they come to take their gin-and-water with
their lazy, dissolute, clever, jovial senior, use regularly to ask
rebecca if Miss Pinkerton be at home: she be as well known to they,
poor soul! as Mr. Lawrence or President West. once Rebecca have the
honour to pass a few day at Chiswick; after which she bring back
Jemima, and erect another doll as Miss Jemmy: for though that honest
creature have make and give her jelly and cake enough for three
child, and a seven-shilling piece at parting, the girl's sense of
ridicule be far strong than her gratitude, and she sacrifice Miss
Jemmy quite as pitilessly as her sister.
the catastrophe come, and she be bring to the Mall as to her home.
the rigid formality of the place suffocate she: the prayer and the
meal, the lesson and the walk, which be arrange with a conventual
regularity, oppress she almost beyond endurance; and she look back
to the freedom and the beggary of the old studio in Soho with so much
regret, that everybody, herself include, fancy she be consume with
grief for her father. she have a little room in the garret, where the
maid hear she walk and sob at night; but it be with rage, and
not with grief. she have not be much of a dissembler, until now her
loneliness teach she to feign. she have never mingle in the society of
woman: her father, reprobate as he be, be a man of talent; his
conversation be a thousand time more agreeable to she than the talk
of such of her own sex as she now encounter. the pompous vanity of
the old schoolmistress, the foolish good-humour of her sister, the
silly chat and scandal of the eld girl, and the frigid correctness
of the governess equally annoy she; and she have no soft maternal
heart, this unlucky girl, otherwise the prattle and talk of the young
child, with whose care she be chiefly intrust, might have soothe
and interested she; but she live among they two year, and not one be
sorry that she go away. the gentle tender-hearted Amelia Sedley be
the only person to whom she could attach herself in the least; and who
could help attach herself to Amelia?
the happiness the superior advantage of the young woman round about
she, give Rebecca inexpressible pang of envy. "what air that girl
give herself, because she be an Earl's grand-daughter," she say of
one. "how they cringe and bow to that Creole, because of her hundred
thousand pound! I be a thousand time cleverer and more charming than
that creature, for all her wealth. I be as well breed as the Earl's
grand-daughter, for all her fine pedigree; and yet every one pass I
by here. and yet, when I be at my father's, do not the man give up
their gay ball and party in order to pass the evening with I?"
she determine at any rate to get free from the prison in which she
find herself, and now begin to act for herself, and for the first time
to make connected plan for the future.
she take advantage, therefore, of the mean of study the place offer
she; and as she be already a musician and a good linguist, she
speedily go through the little course of study which be consider
necessary for lady in those day. her music she practise
incessantly, and one day, when the girl be out, and she have remain
at home, she be overhear to play a piece so well that Minerva
thought, wisely, she could spare herself the expense of a master for
the junior, and intimate to Miss Sharp that she be to instruct they
in music for the future.
the girl refuse; and for the first time, and to the astonishment of
the majestic mistress of the school. "I be here to speak French with
the child," rebecca say abruptly, "not to teach they music, and
save money for you. give I money, and I will teach they."
Minerva be oblige to yield, and, of course, dislike she from that
day. "for five-and-thirty year," she say, and with great justice, "I
never have see the individual who have dare in my own house to
question my authority. I have nourish a viper in my bosom."
"a viper--a fiddlestick," say Miss Sharp to the old lady, almost
faint with astonishment. "you take I because I be useful. there
be no question of gratitude between we. I hate this place, and want to
leave it. I will do nothing here but what I be oblige to do."
it be in vain that the old lady ask she if she be aware she be
speak to Miss Pinkerton? rebecca laugh in her face, with a horrid
sarcastic demoniacal laughter, that almost send the schoolmistress into
fit. "give I a sum of money," say the girl, "and get rid of I--or,
if you like well, get I a good place as governess in a nobleman's
family--you can do so if you please." and in their further dispute
she always return to this point, "get I a situation--we hate each
other, and I be ready to go."
Worthy Miss Pinkerton, although she have a roman nose and a turban, and
be as tall as a grenadier, and have be up to this time an

apprentice, and in vain do battle against she, and try to overawe

before-mention plan of answer she in French, which quite rout

necessary to remove this rebel, this monster, this serpent, this

be in want of a governess, she actually recommend Miss Sharp for the

she say, "find fault with Miss Sharp's conduct, except to myself; and
must allow that her talent and accomplishment be of a high order. as
far as the head go, at least, she do credit to the educational
system pursue at my establishment."
and so the schoolmistress reconcile the recommendation to her

friend to pass a week with she at home, before she enter upon her
duty as governess in a private family.
thus the world begin for these two young lady. for Amelia it be
quite a new, fresh, brilliant world, with all the bloom upon it. it
be not quite a new one for rebecca--(indeed, if the truth must be tell

by the time the young lady reach Kensington turnpike, Amelia have
not forget her companion, but have dry her tear, and have blush

before the carriage arrive in Russell Square, a great deal of

you may be sure that she show Rebecca over every room of the house,
and everything in every one of her drawer; and her book, and her

gimcrack. she insist upon Rebecca accept the white cornelian and

white Cashmere shawl to her friend. could she not spare it? and have
not her brother Joseph just bring she two from India?
when Rebecca see the two magnificent Cashmere shawl which Joseph
Sedley have bring home to his sister, she say, with perfect truth,
"that it must be delightful to have a brother," and easily get the pity
of the tender-hearted Amelia for be alone in the world, an orphan
without friend or kindre.
"not alone," say Amelia; "you know, Rebecca, I shall always be your
friend, and love you as a sister--indeed I will."
"ah, but to have parent, as you have--kind, rich, affectionate
parent, who give you everything you ask for; and their love, which be
more precious than all! my poor papa could give I nothing, and I have
but two frock in all the world! and then, to have a brother, a dear
brother! oh, how you must love he!"
Amelia laugh.
"what! donot you love he? you, who say you love everybody?"
"yes, of course, I do--only--"
"only what?"
"only Joseph donot seem to care much whether I love he or not. he
give I two finger to shake when he arrive after ten year' absence!
he be very kind and good, but he scarcely ever speak to I; I think he
love his pipe a great deal well than his"--but here Amelia check

"benot he very rich?" say Rebecca. "they say all indian nabob be
enormously rich."
"I believe he have a very large income."
"and be your sister-in-law a nice pretty woman?"
"La! Joseph be not married," say Amelia, laugh again.
perhaps she have mention the fact already to Rebecca, but that young

"I think you must have have enough of they at Chiswick," say Amelia,

"no, it donot," say Amelia. "come in, donot be frightened. papa

CHAPTER III
Rebecca be in Presence of the Enemy
a very stout, puffy man, in buckskin and hessian boot, with several
immense neckcloth that rise almost to his nose, with a red strip
waistcoat and an apple green coat with steel button almost as large as
crown piece (it be the morning costume of a dandy or blood of those
day) be read the paper by the fire when the two girl enter, and
bounce off his arm-chair, and blush excessively, and hide his entire
face almost in his neckcloth at this apparition.
"itbe only your sister, Joseph," say Amelia, laugh and shake the
two finger which he hold out. "Ihave come home for good, you know; and
this be my friend, Miss Sharp, whom you have hear I mention."
"no, never, upon my word," say the head under the neckcloth, shake
very much--"that be, yes--what abominably cold weather, Miss"--and
herewith he fall to poke the fire with all his might, although it be
in the middle of June.
"hebe very handsome," whisper Rebecca to Amelia, rather loud.
"do you think so?" say the latter. "Iwill tell he."
"Darling! not for world," say Miss Sharp, start back as timid as a
fawn. she have previously make a respectful virgin-like curtsey to the
gentleman, and her modest eye gaze so perseveringly on the carpet
that it be a wonder how she should have find an opportunity to see
he.
"thank you for the beautiful shawl, brother," say Amelia to the fire
poker. "be they not beautiful, Rebecca?"
"o heavenly!" say Miss Sharp, and her eye go from the carpet
straight to the chandelier.
Joseph still continue a huge clattering at the poker and tong,
puff and blow the while, and turn as red as his yellow face
would allow he. "I cannot make you such handsome present, Joseph,"
continue his sister, "but while I be at school, I have embroider
for you a very beautiful pair of brace."
"Good Gad! Amelia," cry the brother, in serious alarm, "what do you
mean?" and plunge with all his might at the bell-rope, that article
of furniture come away in his hand, and increase the honest fellow's
confusion. "for heaven's sake see if my buggy's at the door. I can't
wait. I must go. d---- that groom of mine. I must go."
at this minute the father of the family walk in, rattle his seal
like a true british merchant. "whatbe the matter, Emmy?" say he.
"Joseph want I to see if his--his buggy be at the door. what be a
buggy, Papa?"
"it be a one-horse palanquin," say the old gentleman, who be a wag in
his way.
Joseph at this burst out into a wild fit of laughter; in which,
encounter the eye of Miss Sharp, he stop all of a sudden, as if
he have be shoot.
"this young lady be your friend? Miss Sharp, I be very happy to see
you. have you and Emmy be quarrel already with Joseph, that he
want to be off?"
"I promise Bonamy of our service, sir," say Joseph, "to dine with
he."
"o fie! donot you tell your mother you would dine here?"
"but in this dress itbe impossible."
"look at he, benot he handsome enough to dine anywhere, Miss Sharp?"
on which, of course, Miss Sharp look at her friend, and they both set
off in a fit of laughter, highly agreeable to the old gentleman.
"do you ever see a pair of buckskin like those at Miss Pinkerton's?"
continue he, follow up his advantage.
"gracious heaven! Father," cry Joseph.
"there now, I have hurt his feeling. Mrs. Sedley, my dear, I have
hurt your son's feeling. I have allude to his buckskin. ask Miss
Sharp if I havenot? Come, Joseph, be friend with Miss Sharp, and let
we all go to dinner."
"therebe a pillau, Joseph, just as you like it, and Papa have bring
home the good turbot in Billingsgate."
"come, come, sir, walk downstairs with Miss Sharp, and I will follow
with these two young woman," say the father, and he take an arm of
wife and daughter and walk merrily off.
if Miss Rebecca Sharp have determine in her heart upon make the
conquest of this big beau, I donot think, lady, we have any right to
blame she; for though the task of husband-hunting be generally, and
with become modesty, entrust by young person to their mamma,
recollect that Miss Sharp have no kind parent to arrange these delicate
matter for she, and that if she do not get a husband for herself,
there be no one else in the wide world who would take the trouble off
her hand. what cause young people to "come out," but the noble
ambition of matrimony? what send they troop to watering-place?
what keep they dance till five o'clock in the morning through a
whole mortal season? what cause they to labour at pianoforte sonata,
and to learn four song from a fashionable master at a guinea a lesson,
and to play the harp if they have handsome arm and neat elbow, and to
wear Lincoln Green toxophilite hat and feather, but that they may
bring down some "desirable" young man with those kill bow and
arrow of theirs? what cause respectable parent to take up their
carpet, set their house topsy-turvy, and spend a fifth of their
year's income in ball supper and iced champagne? be it sheer love of
their specie, and an unadulterated wish to see young people happy and
dancing? Psha! they want to marry their daughter; and, as honest Mrs.
Sedley have, in the depth of her kind heart, already arrange a score
of little scheme for the settlement of her Amelia, so also have our
beloved but unprotected Rebecca determine to do she very well to
secure the husband, who be even more necessary for she than for her
friend. she have a vivid imagination; she have, besides, read the Arabian
Nights and Guthrie's Geography; and it be a fact that while she be
dress for dinner, and after she have ask Amelia whether her brother
be very rich, she have build for herself a most magnificent castle in
the air, of which she be mistress, with a husband somewhere in the
background (she have not see he as yet, and his figure would not
therefore be very distinct); she have array herself in an infinity of
shawl, turban, and diamond necklace, and have mount upon an
elephant to the sound of the march in Bluebeard, in order to pay a
visit of ceremony to the Grand Mogul. Charming Alnaschar vision! it be
the happy privilege of youth to construct you, and many a fanciful
young creature besides Rebecca Sharp have indulge in these delightful
day-dream ere now!
Joseph Sedley be twelve year old than his sister Amelia. he be in
the East India Company's Civil Service, and his name appear, at the
period of which we write, in the bengal division of the East India
Register, as collector of Boggley Wollah, an honourable and lucrative
post, as everybody know: in order to know to what high post Joseph
rise in the service, the reader be refer to the same periodical.
Boggley Wollah be situate in a fine, lonely, marshy, jungly district,
famous for snipe-shooting, and where not unfrequently you may flush a
tiger. Ramgunge, where there be a magistrate, be only forty mile off,
and there be a cavalry station about thirty mile far; so Joseph
write home to his parent, when he take possession of his
collectorship. he have live for about eight year of his life, quite
alone, at this charming place, scarcely see a christian face except
twice a year, when the detachment arrive to carry off the revenue
which he have collect, to Calcutta.
luckily, at this time he catch a liver complaint, for the cure of
which he return to Europe, and which be the source of great comfort
and amusement to he in his native country. he do not live with his
family while in London, but have lodging of his own, like a gay young
bachelor. before he go to India he be too young to partake of the
delightful pleasure of a man about town, and plunge into they on his
return with considerable assiduity. he drive his horse in the Park;
he din at the fashionable tavern (for the Oriental Club be not as
yet invent); he frequent the theatre, as the mode be in those
day, or make his appearance at the opera, laboriously attire in
tight and a cocked hat.
on return to India, and ever after, he use to talk of the pleasure
of this period of his existence with great enthusiasm, and give you to
understand that he and Brummel be the lead buck of the day. but
he be as lonely here as in his jungle at Boggley Wollah. he scarcely
know a single soul in the metropolis: and be it not for his doctor,

and he find himself again at his three meal a day. he never be well
dress; but he take the hugest pain to adorn his big person, and
pass many hour daily in that occupation. his valet make a fortune

cut. when dress at length, in the afternoon, he would issue forth to
take a drive with nobody in the Park; and then would come back in order
to dress again and go and dine with nobody at the Piazza Coffee-House.
he be as vain as a girl; and perhaps his extreme shyness be one of
the result of his extreme vanity. if Miss Rebecca can get the well
of he, and at her first entrance into life, she be a young person of
no ordinary cleverness.
the first move show considerable skill. when she call Sedley a

downstair, then, they go, Joseph very red and blushing, Rebecca very

a slight snore be the only reply: the honest stockbroker be asleep,

be an invalid, Joseph Sedley content himself with a bottle of

CHAPTER IV
the Green Silk Purse

heart of her own.
as for Mr. Sedley's joke, Rebecca laugh at they with a cordiality
and perseverance which not a little pleased and soften that
good-natured gentleman. nor be it with the chief of the family alone
that Miss Sharp find favour. she interest Mrs. Blenkinsop by
evince the deep sympathy in the raspberry-jam preserving, which
operation be then go on in the Housekeeper's room; she persist in
call Sambo "Sir," and "Mr. Sambo," to the delight of that attendant;
and she apologise to the lady's maid for give her trouble in
venture to ring the bell, with such sweetness and humility, that the
Servants' Hall be almost as charm with she as the Drawing Room.
once, in look over some drawing which Amelia have send from school,
Rebecca suddenly come upon one which cause she to burst into tear and
leave the room. it be on the day when Joe Sedley make his second
appearance.
Amelia hasten after her friend to know the cause of this display of
feeling, and the good-natured girl come back without her companion,
rather affect too. "you know, her father be our drawing-master,
Mamma, at Chiswick, and use to do all the good part of our drawing."
"my love! Ibe sure I always hear Miss Pinkerton say that he do not
touch they--he only mount they." "it be call mount, Mamma.
rebecca remember the drawing, and her father work at it, and the
thought of it come upon she rather suddenly--and so, you know, she--"
"the poor child be all heart," say Mrs. Sedley.
"I wish she could stay with we another week," say Amelia.
"shebe devilish like Miss Cutler that I use to meet at Dumdum, only
fairer. shebe married now to Lance, the Artillery Surgeon. do you
know, Ma'am, that once Quintin, of the 14th, bet me--"
"o Joseph, we know that story," say Amelia, laugh. "never mind about
tell that; but persuade Mamma to write to Sir Something Crawley for
leave of absence for poor dear rebecca: here she come, her eye red
with weeping."
"Ibe well, now," say the girl, with the sweet smile possible,
take good-natured Mrs. Sedley's extended hand and kiss it
respectfully. "how kind you all be to I! all," she add, with a
laugh, "except you, Mr. Joseph."
"I!" say Joseph, meditate an instant departure "Gracious Heavens!
Good Gad! Miss Sharp!'
"yes; how could you be so cruel as to make I eat that horrid
pepper-dish at dinner, the first day I ever see you? you be not so
good to I as dear Amelia."
"he donot know you so well," cry Amelia.
"I defy anybody not to be good to you, my dear," say her mother.
"the curry be capital; indeed it be," say Joe, quite gravely.
"perhaps there be not enough citron juice in it--no, there be not."
"and the chili?"
"by Jove, how they make you cry out!" say Joe, catch by the ridicule
of the circumstance, and explode in a fit of laughter which end
quite suddenly, as usual.
"I shall take care how I let you choose for I another time," say
Rebecca, as they go down again to dinner. "I donot think man be
fond of put poor harmless girl to pain."
"by Gad, Miss Rebecca, I wouldnot hurt you for the world."
"no," say she, "I know you wouldnot"; and then she give he ever so
gentle a pressure with her little hand, and draw it back quite
frightened, and look first for one instant in his face, and then down
at the carpet-rod; and I be not prepared to say that Joe's heart do
not thump at this little involuntary, timid, gentle motion of regard on
the part of the simple girl.
it be an advance, and as such, perhaps, some lady of indisputable
correctness and gentility will condemn the action as immodest; but, you
see, poor dear Rebecca have all this work to do for herself. if a
person be too poor to keep a servant, though ever so elegant, he must
sweep his own room: if a dear girl have no dear Mamma to settle matter
with the young man, she must do it for herself. and oh, what a mercy
it be that these woman do not exercise their power oftener! we cannot
resist they, if they do. let they show ever so little inclination, and
man go down on their knee at once: old or ugly, it be all the same.
and this I set down as a positive truth. a woman with fair
opportunity, and without an absolute hump, may marry WHOM she LIKES.
only let we be thankful that the darling be like the beast of the
field, and donot know their own power. they would overcome we entirely
if they do.
"egad!" think Joseph, enter the dining-room, "I exactly begin to
feel as I do at Dumdum with Miss Cutler." many sweet little appeal,
half tender, half jocular, do Miss Sharp make to he about the dish
at dinner; for by this time she be on a footing of considerable
familiarity with the family, and as for the girl, they love each
other like sister. young unmarried girl always do, if they be in a
house together for ten day.
as if bent upon advance Rebecca's plan in every way--what must
Amelia do, but remind her brother of a promise make last Easter
holidays--"When I be a girl at school," say she, laugh--a promise
that he, Joseph, would take she to Vauxhall. "now," she say, "that
rebecca be with we, will be the very time."
"o, delightful!" say Rebecca, go to clap her hand; but she
recollect herself, and pause, like a modest creature, as she be.
"to-night be not the night," say Joe.
"well, to-morrow."
"to-morrow your Papa and I dine out," say Mrs. Sedley.
"you donot suppose that Ibe go, Mrs. Sed?" say her husband, "and
that a woman of your year and size be to catch cold, in such an
abominable damp place?"
"the child must have someone with they," cry Mrs. Sedley.
"let Joe go," say-his father, laugh. "hebe big enough." at which

Joe feel inclined to become a parricide almost.
"Undo his stay!" continue the pitiless old gentleman. "fle some
water in his face, Miss Sharp, or carry he upstairs: the dear

feather!"
"if I stand this, sir, Ibe d------!" roar Joseph.
"Order Mr. Jos's elephant, Sambo!" cry the father. "send to Exeter

the elephant, but give I and Mr. Jos a glass of Champagne. Boney
himself havenot get such in his cellar, my boy!"
a goblet of Champagne restore Joseph's equanimity, and before the

agree to take the young lady to Vauxhall.
"the girl must have a gentleman apiece," say the old gentleman. "Jos
will be sure to leave Emmy in the crowd, he will be so take up with
Miss Sharp here. send to 96, and ask George Osborne if hewill come."
at this, I donot know in the least for what reason, Mrs. Sedley look

spelt twelfth without the f?"
"that be year ago," say Amelia.
"it seem like yesterday, donot it, John?" say Mrs. Sedley to her

face, one in a laced nightcap, and one in a simple cotton one, end

to task for his cruel conduct to poor Joe.
"it be quite wicked of you, Mr. Sedley," say she, "to torment the
poor boy so."
"my dear," say the cotton-tassel in defence of his conduct, "Jos be a
great deal vainer than you ever be in your life, and thatbe say a
good deal. though, some thirty year ago, in the year seventeen
hundred and eighty--what be it?--perhap you have a right to be vain--I
donot say no. but Ihave no patience with Jos and his dandified modesty.
it be out-Josephing Joseph, my dear, and all the while the boy be only

make love to he as hard as she can; thatbe quite clear; and if she

to woman, as I be to go on 'Change every day. itbe a mercy he do not
bring we over a black daughter-in-law, my dear. but, mark my word,
the first woman who fish for he, hook he."
"she shall go off to-morrow, the little artful creature," say Mrs.
Sedley, with great energy.
"why not she as well as another, Mrs. Sedley? the girlbe a white face
at any rate. I donot care who marry he. let Joe please himself."
and presently the voice of the two speaker be hush, or be
replace by the gentle but unromantic music of the nose; and save when
the church bell toll the hour and the watchman call it, all be
silent at the house of John Sedley, Esquire, of Russell Square, and the
Stock Exchange.
when morning come, the good-natured Mrs. Sedley no long think of
execute her threat with regard to Miss Sharp; for though nothing be

abruptly dismiss she.
and as if all thing conspire in favour of the gentle Rebecca, the

for he be extremely talkative in man's society; and afterwards Miss
Amelia Sedley do the honour of the drawing-room; and these four young
person pass such a comfortable evening together, that they declare
they be rather glad of the thunder-storm than otherwise, which have
cause they to put off their visit to Vauxhall.
Osborne be Sedley's godson, and have be one of the family any time
these three-and-twenty year. at six week old, he have receive from
John Sedley a present of a silver cup; at six month old, a coral with
gold whistle and bell; from his youth upwards he be "tip"
regularly by the old gentleman at Christmas: and on go back to
school, he remember perfectly well be thrash by Joseph Sedley,
when the latter be a big, swagger hobbadyhoy, and George an
impudent urchin of ten year old. in a word, George be as familiar
with the family as such daily act of kindness and intercourse could
make he.
"do you remember, Sedley, what a fury you be in, when I cut off the
tassel of your hessian boot, and how Miss--hem!--how Amelia rescue
I from a beating, by fall down on her knee and cry out to her
brother Jos, not to beat little George?"
Jos remember this remarkable circumstance perfectly well, but vow
that he have totally forget it.
"well, do you remember come down in a gig to Dr. Swishtail's to see
I, before you go to India, and give I half a guinea and a pat on
the head? I always have an idea that you be at least seven foot high,
and be quite astonished at your return from India to find you no
tall than myself."
"how good of Mr. Sedley to go to your school and give you the money!"
exclaim Rebecca, in accent of extreme delight.
"yes, and after I have cut the tassel of his boot too. boy never
forget those tip at school, nor the giver."
"I delight in hessian boot," say rebecca. Jos Sedley, who admire
his own leg prodigiously, and always wear this ornamental chaussure,
be extremely pleased at this remark, though he draw his leg under his
chair as it be make.
"Miss Sharp!" say George Osborne, "you who be so clever an artist,
you must make a grand historical picture of the scene of the boot.
Sedley shall be represent in buckskin, and hold one of the
injure boot in one hand; by the other he shall have hold of my
shirt-frill. Amelia shall be kneel near he, with her little hand
up; and the picture shall have a grand allegorical title, as the
frontispiece have in the Medulla and the spelling-book."
"I shallnot have time to do it here," say Rebecca. "Iwill do it
when--when Ibe go." and she drop her voice, and look so sad and
piteous, that everybody feel how cruel her lot be, and how sorry they
would be to part with she.
"o that you could stay long, dear Rebecca," say Amelia.
"why?" answer the other, still more sadly. "that I may be only the
more unhap--unwilling to lose you?" and she turn away her head.
Amelia begin to give way to that natural infirmity of tear which, we
have say, be one of the defect of this silly little thing. George
Osborne look at the two young woman with a touch curiosity; and
Joseph Sedley heave something very like a sigh out of his big chest,
as he cast his eye down towards his favourite hessian boot.
"let we have some music, Miss Sedley--Amelia," say George, who feel at
that moment an extraordinary, almost irresistible impulse to seize the
above-mention young woman in his arm, and to kiss she in the face of

that they fall in love with each other at that single instant of time,
I should perhaps be tell an untruth, for the fact be that these two
young people have be breed up by their parent for this very purpose,
and their bann have, as it be, be read in their respective family
any time these ten year. they go off to the piano, which be
situate, as piano usually be, in the back drawing-room; and as it
be rather dark, Miss Amelia, in the most unaffected way in the world,
put her hand into Mr. Osborne's, who, of course, could see the way
among the chair and ottoman a great deal well than she could. but
this arrangement leave Mr. Joseph Sedley tete-a-tete with Rebecca, at
the drawing-room table, where the latter be occupy in knit a
green silk purse.
"there be no need to ask family secret," say Miss Sharp. "those two
have tell theirs."
"as soon as he get his company," say Joseph, "I believe the affair be
settle. George Osborne be a capital fellow."
"and your sister the dear creature in the world," say Rebecca.
"happy the man who win she!" with this, Miss Sharp give a great sigh.
when two unmarried person get together, and talk upon such delicate
subject as the present, a great deal of confidence and intimacy be
presently establish between they. there be no need of give a
special report of the conversation which now take place between Mr.
Sedley and the young lady; for the conversation, as may be judge from
the foregoing speciman, be not especially witty or eloquent; it seldom
be in private society, or anywhere except in very high-fly and
ingenious novel. as there be music in the next room, the talk be
carry on, of course, in a low and become tone, though, for the
matter of that, the couple in the next apartment would not have be
disturb have the talking be ever so loud, so occupied be they with
their own pursuit.
almost for the first time in his life, Mr. Sedley find himself
talk, without the least timidity or hesitation, to a person of the
other sex. Miss Rebecca ask he a great number of question about
India, which give he an opportunity of narrate many interesting
anecdote about that country and himself. he describe the ball at
Government House, and the manner in which they keep themselves cool in
the hot weather, with punkah, tattie, and other contrivance; and he
be very witty regard the number of Scotchmen whom Lord Minto, the
Governor-General, patronise; and then he describe a tiger-hunt; and
the manner in which the mahout of his elephant have be pull off his
seat by one of the infuriated animal. how delight Miss Rebecca be
at the Government ball, and how she laugh at the story of the
Scotch aide-de-camp, and call Mr. Sedley a sad wicked satirical
creature; and how frightened she be at the story of the elephant! "for
your mother's sake, dear Mr. Sedley," she say, "for the sake of all
your friend, promise never to go on one of those horrid expedition."
"Pooh, pooh, Miss Sharp," say he, pull up his shirt-collar; "the
danger make the sport only the pleasanter." he have never be but once
at a tiger-hunt, when the accident in question occur, and when he
be half kill--not by the tiger, but by the fright. and as he talk
on, he grow quite bold, and actually have the audacity to ask Miss
Rebecca for whom she be knit the green silk purse? he be quite
surprised and delight at his own graceful familiar manner.
"for any one who want a purse," reply Miss Rebecca, look at he
in the most gentle win way. Sedley be go to make one of the
most eloquent speech possible, and have begun--"O Miss Sharp, how--"
when some song which be perform in the other room come to an end,
and cause he to hear his own voice so distinctly that he stop,
blush, and blow his nose in great agitation.
"do you ever hear anything like your brother's eloquence?" whisper
Mr. Osborne to Amelia. "why, your friend have work miracle."
"the more the well," say Miss Amelia; who, like almost all woman who
be worth a pin, be a match-maker in her heart, and would have be
delighted that Joseph should carry back a wife to India. she have, too,
in the course of this few day' constant intercourse, warm into a
most tender friendship for Rebecca, and discover a million of virtue
and amiable quality in she which she have not perceive when they be
at Chiswick together. for the affection of young lady be of as rapid
growth as Jack's bean-stalk, and reach up to the sky in a night. it
be no blame to they that after marriage this Sehnsucht nach der Liebe
subside. it be what sentimentalist, who deal in very big word, call
a yearning after the Ideal, and simply mean that woman be commonly
not satisfied until they have husband and child on whom they may
centre affection, which be spend elsewhere, as it be, in small
change.
have expend her little store of song, or have stay long enough

"I give Miss Sharp warning, though," say Osborne, "that, right or

follow Mr. Joseph. rebecca sing far well than her friend (though
of course Osborne be free to keep his opinion), and exert herself to

understand, and then a number of those simple ballad which be the
fashion forty year ago, and in which british tar, our king, poor
Susan, blue-eyed Mary, and the like, be the principal theme. they
be not, it be say, very brilliant, in a musical point of view, but
contain numberless good-nature, simple appeal to the affection,
which people understand well than the milk-and-water lagrime,
sospiri, and felicita of the eternal donizettian music with which we
be favour now-a-day.
conversation of a sentimental sort, befit the subject, be carry
on between the song, to which Sambo, after he have bring the tea, the
delighted cook, and even Mrs. Blenkinsop, the housekeeper, condescend
to listen on the landing-place.
among these ditty be one, the last of the concert, and to the
follow effect:
ah! bleak and barren be the moor, ah! loud and pierce be the storm,
the cottage roof be shelter'd sure, the cottage hearth be bright and
warm--an orphan boy the lattice pass'd, and, as he mark'd its cheerful
glow, Felt doubly keen the midnight blast, and doubly cold the fall
snow.
they mark'd he as he onward prest, with faint heart and weary limb;
Kind voice bid he turn and rest, and gentle face welcome he. the
dawn be up--the guest be go, the cottage hearth be blaze still;
Heaven pity all poor wanderer lone! Hark to the wind upon the hill!
it be the sentiment of the before-mention word, "when Ibe go,"
over again. as she come to the last word, Miss Sharp's "deep-tone
voice falter." everybody feel the allusion to her departure, and to
her hapless orphan state. Joseph Sedley, who be fond of music, and
soft-hearted, be in a state of ravishment during the performance of
the song, and profoundly touch at its conclusion. if he have have the
courage; if George and Miss Sedley have remain, accord to the
former's proposal, in the farth room, Joseph Sedley's bachelorhood
would have be at an end, and this work would never have be write.
but at the close of the ditty, Rebecca quit the piano, and give
her hand to Amelia, walk away into the front drawing-room twilight;
and, at this moment, Mr. Sambo make his appearance with a tray,
contain sandwich, jelly, and some glitter glass and
decanter, on which Joseph Sedley's attention be immediately fix.
when the parent of the house of Sedley return from their
dinner-party, they find the young people so busy in talk, that they
have not hear the arrival of the carriage, and Mr. Joseph be in the
act of say, "my dear Miss Sharp, one little teaspoonful of jelly to
recruit you after your immense--your--your delightful exertion."
"Bravo, Jos!" say Mr. Sedley; on hear the bantering of which
well-know voice, Jos instantly relapse into an alarmed silence, and
quickly take his departure. he do not lie awake all night think
whether or not he be in love with Miss Sharp; the passion of love
never interfere with the appetite or the slumber of Mr. Joseph Sedley;
but he think to himself how delightful it would be to hear such song
as those after Cutcherry--what a distinguee girl she be--how she could
speak french well than the Governor-General's lady herself--and what
a sensation she would make at the Calcutta ball. "itbe evident the
poor devil's in love with I," think he. "she be just as rich as
most of the girl who come out to India. I might go far, and fare
bad, egad!" and in these meditation he fall asleep.
how Miss Sharp lie awake, thinking, will he come or not to-morrow? need
not be tell here. to-morrow come, and, as sure as fate, Mr. Joseph
Sedley make his appearance before luncheon. he have never be know
before to confer such an honour on Russell Square. George Osborne be
somehow there already (sadly "put out" Amelia, who be write to
her twelve dear friend at Chiswick Mall), and Rebecca be employ
upon her yesterday's work. as Joe's buggy drive up, and while, after
his usual thunder knock and pompous bustle at the door, the
ex-Collector of Boggley Wollah labour up stair to the drawing-room,
knowing glance be telegraph between Osborne and Miss Sedley, and
the pair, smile archly, look at Rebecca, who actually blush as
she bend her fair ringlet over her knitting. how her heart beat as
Joseph appear--Joseph, puff from the staircase in shin creak
boot--Joseph, in a new waistcoat, red with heat and nervousness, and
blushing behind his wad neckcloth. it be a nervous moment for all;
and as for Amelia, I think she be more frightened than even the people
most concerned.
Sambo, who fling open the door and announce Mr. Joseph, follow
grinning, in the Collector's rear, and bear two handsome nosegay of
flower, which the monster have actually have the gallantry to purchase
in Covent Garden Market that morning--they be not as big as the
haystack which lady carry about with they now-a-day, in cone of
filigree paper; but the young woman be delighted with the gift, as
Joseph present one to each, with an exceedingly solemn bow.
"Bravo, Jos!" cry Osborne.
"thank you, dear Joseph," say Amelia, quite ready to kiss her brother,
if he be so minded. (and I think for a kiss from such a dear
creature as Amelia, I would purchase all Mr. Lee's conservatory out
of hand.)
"o heavenly, heavenly flower!" exclaim Miss Sharp, and smell they
delicately, and hold they to her bosom, and cast up her eye to the
ceiling, in an ecstasy of admiration. perhaps she just look first
into the bouquet, to see whether there be a billet-doux hide among
the flower; but there be no letter.
"do they talk the language of flower at Boggley Wollah, Sedley?" ask
Osborne, laugh.
"Pooh, nonsense!" reply the sentimental youth. "buy them at
Nathan's; very glad you like them; and eh, Amelia, my dear, I buy a
pine-apple at the same time, which I give to Sambo. letus have it for
tiffin; very cool and nice this hot weather." rebecca say she have
never taste a pine, and long beyond everything to taste one.
so the conversation go on. I donot know on what pretext Osborne leave
the room, or why, presently, Amelia go away, perhaps to superintend
the slicing of the pine-apple; but Jos be leave alone with Rebecca, who
have resume her work, and the green silk and the shin needle be
quiver rapidly under her white slender finger.
"what a beautiful, BYOO-OOTIFUL song that be you sing last night, dear
Miss Sharp," say the Collector. "it make I cry almost; 'pon my
honour it do."
"because you have a kind heart, Mr. Joseph; all the Sedleys have, I
think."
"it keep I awake last night, and I be try to hum it this morning,
in bed; I be, upon my honour. Gollop, my doctor, come in at eleven
(for Ibe a sad invalid, you know, and see Gollop every day), and, 'gad!
there I be, sing away like--a robin."
"o you droll creature! do let I hear you sing it."
"I? no, you, Miss Sharp; my dear Miss Sharp, do sing it." "not now,
Mr. Sedley," say Rebecca, with a sigh. "my spirit be not equal to
it; besides, I must finish the purse. will you help I, Mr. Sedley?"
and before he have time to ask how, Mr. Joseph Sedley, of the East India
Company's service, be actually seat tete-a-tete with a young lady,
look at she with a most killing expression; his arm stretch out
before she in an implore attitude, and his hand bind in a web of
green silk, which she be unwind.
in this romantic position Osborne and Amelia find the interesting
pair, when they enter to announce that tiffin be ready. the skein
of silk be just wind round the card; but Mr. Jos have never speak.
"I be sure he will to-night, dear," Amelia say, as she press
Rebecca's hand; and Sedley, too, have commune with his soul, and say
to himself, "'Gad, Iwill pop the question at Vauxhall."
CHAPTER V
Dobbin of Ours
Cuff's fight with Dobbin, and the unexpected issue of that contest,
will long be remember by every man who be educate at Dr.
Swishtail's famous school. the latter Youth (who use to be call
Heigh-ho Dobbin, Gee-ho Dobbin, and by many other name indicative of
puerile contempt) be the quiet, the clumsy, and, as it seem,
the dull of all Dr. Swishtail's young gentleman. his parent be a
grocer in the city: and it be bruit abroad that he be admit into
Dr. Swishtail's academy upon what be call "mutual principles"--that
be to say, the expense of his board and schooling be defray by his
father in good, not money; and he stand there--most at the bottom of
the school--in his scraggy corduroy and jacket, through the seam of
which his great big bone be burst--as the representative of so
many pound of tea, candle, sugar, mottled-soap, plum (of which a
very mild proportion be supply for the pudding of the
establishment), and other commodity. a dreadful day it be for young
Dobbin when one of the youngster of the school, have run into the
town upon a poaching excursion for hardbake and polony, espy the
cart of Dobbin & Rudge, Grocers and Oilmen, Thames Street, London, at
the Doctor's door, discharge a cargo of the ware in which the firm
deal.
young Dobbin have no peace after that. the joke be frightful, and
merciless against he. "Hullo, Dobbin," one wag would say, "herebe
good news in the paper. sugar be ris', my boy." another would set a
sum--"if a pound of mutton-candle cost sevenpence-halfpenny, how much
must Dobbin cost?" and a roar would follow from all the circle of young
knave, usher and all, who rightly consider that the selling of good
by retail be a shameful and infamous practice, merit the contempt
and scorn of all real gentleman.
"your father's only a merchant, Osborne," Dobbin say in private to the
little boy who have bring down the storm upon he. at which the
latter reply haughtily, "my father's a gentleman, and keep his
carriage"; and Mr. William Dobbin retreat to a remote outhouse in the
playground, where he pass a half-holiday in the bitter sadness and
woe. who amongst we be there that do not recollect similar hour of
bitter, bitter childish grief? who feel injustice; who shrink before
a slight; who have a sense of wrong so acute, and so glow a gratitude
for kindness, as a generous boy? and how many of those gentle soul do
you degrade, estrange, torture, for the sake of a little loose
arithmetic, and miserable dog-latin?
now, William Dobbin, from an incapacity to acquire the rudiment of the
above language, as they be propound in that wonderful book the Eton
Latin Grammar, be compel to remain among the very last of Doctor
Swishtail's scholar, and be "take down" continually by little
fellow with pink face and pinafore when he march up with the low
form, a giant amongst they, with his downcast, stupefied look, his
dog's-eare primer, and his tight corduroy. high and low, all make
fun of he. they sew up those corduroy, tight as they be. they
cut his bed-string. they upset bucket and bench, so that he might
break his shin over they, which he never fail to do. they send he
parcel, which, when open, be find to contain the paternal soap
and candle. there be no little fellow but have his jeer and joke at
Dobbin; and he bear everything quite patiently, and be entirely dumb
and miserable.
Cuff, on the contrary, be the great chief and dandy of the Swishtail
Seminary. he smuggle wine in. he fight the town-boy. pony use
to come for he to ride home on Saturdays. he have his top-boot in his
room, in which he use to hunt in the holiday. he have a gold
repeater: and take snuff like the Doctor. he have be to the Opera,
and know the merit of the principal actor, prefer Mr. Kean to Mr.
Kemble. he could knock you off forty latin verse in an hour. he
could make french poetry. what else donot he know, or couldnot he do?
they say even the Doctor himself be afraid of he.
Cuff, the unquestioned king of the school, rule over his subject, and
bully they, with splendid superiority. this one black his shoe:
that toast his bread, other would fag out, and give he ball at
cricket during whole summer afternoon. "Figs" be the fellow whom he
despise most, and with whom, though always abuse he, and sneer
at he, he scarcely ever condescend to hold personal communication.
one day in private, the two young gentleman have have a difference. fig,
alone in the schoolroom, be blunder over a home letter; when Cuff,
enter, bid he go upon some message, of which tart be probably
the subject.
"I cannot," say Dobbin; "I want to finish my letter."
"you CAN'T?" say Mr. Cuff, lay hold of that document (in which many
word be scratch out, many be mis-spelt, on which have be spend
I donot know how much thought, and labour, and tear; for the poor
fellow be write to his mother, who be fond of he, although she be
a grocer's wife, and live in a back parlour in Thames Street). "you
CAN'T?" say Mr. Cuff: "I should like to know why, pray? cannot you
write to old Mother Figs to-morrow?"
"donot call name," Dobbin say, get off the bench very nervous.
"well, sir, will you go?" crow the cock of the school.
"put down the letter," Dobbin reply; "no gentleman readth letterth."
"well, now will you go?" say the other.
"no, I willnot. donot strike, or Iwill thmash you," roar out Dobbin,
spring to a leaden inkstand, and look so wicked, that Mr. Cuff
pause, turn down his coat sleeve again, put his hand into his
pocket, and walk away with a sneer. but he never meddle personally
with the grocer's boy after that; though we must do he the justice to
say he always speak of Mr. Dobbin with contempt behind his back.
some time after this interview, it happen that Mr. Cuff, on a
sunshiny afternoon, be in the neighbourhood of poor William Dobbin,
who be lie under a tree in the playground, spell over a favourite
copy of the Arabian Nights which he have apart from the rest of the
school, who be pursue their various sport--quite lonely, and
almost happy. if people would but leave child to themselves; if
teacher would cease to bully they; if parent would not insist upon
direct their thought, and dominate their feeling--those feeling
and thought which be a mystery to all (for how much do you and I know
of each other, of our child, of our father, of our neighbour, and
how far more beautiful and sacred be the thought of the poor lad or
girl whom you govern likely to be, than those of the dull and
world-corrupt person who rule him?)--if, I say, parent and master
would leave their child alone a little more, small harm would
accrue, although a less quantity of as in praesenti might be acquire.
well, William Dobbin have for once forget the world, and be away
with Sindbad the Sailor in the Valley of Diamonds, or with Prince Ahmed
and the Fairy Peribanou in that delightful cavern where the Prince
find she, and whither we should all like to make a tour; when shrill
cry, as of a little fellow weeping, wake up his pleasant reverie; and
look up, he see Cuff before he, belabour a little boy.
it be the lad who have peach upon he about the grocer's cart; but he
bear little malice, not at least towards the young and small. "how dare
you, sir, break the bottle?" say Cuff to the little urchin, swing a
yellow cricket-stump over he.
the boy have be instruct to get over the playground wall (at a
select spot where the broken glass have be remove from the top, and
niche make convenient in the brick); to run a quarter of a mile; to
purchase a pint of rum-shrub on credit; to brave all the Doctor's
outlying spy, and to clamber back into the playground again; during
the performance of which feat, his foot have slipt, and the bottle be
break, and the shrub have be spill, and his pantaloon have be
damage, and he appear before his employer a perfectly guilty and
trembling, though harmless, wretch.
"how dare you, sir, break it?" say Cuff; "you blunder little thief.
you drink the shrub, and now you pretend to have break the bottle.
hold out your hand, sir."
Down come the stump with a great heavy thump on the child's hand. a
moan follow. Dobbin look up. the Fairy Peribanou have flee into the
inmost cavern with Prince Ahmed: the Roc have whisk away Sindbad the
Sailor out of the Valley of Diamonds out of sight, far into the cloud:
and there be everyday life before honest William; and a big boy
beat a little one without cause.
"hold out your other hand, sir," roar Cuff to his little schoolfellow,
whose face be distort with pain. Dobbin quiver, and gather
himself up in his narrow old clothe.
"take that, you little devil!" cry Mr. Cuff, and down come the wicket
again on the child's hand.--Don't be horrify, lady, every boy at a
public school have do it. your child will so do and be do by, in
all probability. down come the wicket again; and Dobbin start up.
I cannot tell what his motive be. torture in a public school be as
much license as the knout in Russia. it would be ungentlemanlike (in
a manner) to resist it. perhaps Dobbin's foolish soul revolt against
that exercise of tyranny; or perhaps he have a hanker feeling of
revenge in his mind, and long to measure himself against that
splendid bully and tyrant, who have all the glory, pride, pomp,
circumstance, banner fly, drum beat, guard salute, in the
place. whatever may have be his incentive, however, up he sprang,
and scream out, "hold off, Cuff; donot bully that child any more; or
I'll--"
"or youwill what?" Cuff ask in amazement at this interruption. "hold
out your hand, you little beast."
"Iwill give you the bad thrash you ever have in your life," Dobbin
say, in reply to the first part of Cuff's sentence; and little
Osborne, gasp and in tear, look up with wonder and incredulity at
see this amazing champion put up suddenly to defend he: while
Cuff's astonishment be scarcely less. Fancy our late monarch George
III when he hear of the revolt of the north american colony: fancy
brazen Goliath when little David step forward and claim a meeting;
and you have the feeling of Mr. Reginald Cuff when this rencontre be
propose to he.
"after school," say he, of course; after a pause and a look, as much
as to say, "make your will, and communicate your last wish to your
friend between this time and that."
"as you please," Dobbin say. "you must be my bottle holder, Osborne."
"well, if you like," little Osborne reply; for you see his papa keep
a carriage, and he be rather ashamed of his champion.
yes, when the hour of battle come, he be almost ashamed to say, "go
it, Figs"; and not a single other boy in the place utter that cry for
the first two or three round of this famous combat; at the
commencement of which the scientific Cuff, with a contemptuous smile on
his face, and as light and as gay as if he be at a ball, plant his
blow upon his adversary, and floor that unlucky champion three time
run. at each fall there be a cheer; and everybody be anxious to
have the honour of offer the conqueror a knee.
"what a licking I shall get when itbe over," young Osborne thought,
pick up his man. "youwould well give in," he say to Dobbin; "itbe
only a thrashing, Figs, and you know Ibe use to it." but Figs, all
whose limb be in a quiver, and whose nostril be breathe rage,
