put his little bottle-holder aside, and go in for a fourth time.
as he do not in the least know how to parry the blow that be aim
at himself, and Cuff have begin the attack on the three precede
occasion, without ever allow his enemy to strike, fig now
determine that he would commence the engagement by a charge on his own
part; and accordingly, be a left-handed man, bring that arm into
action, and hit out a couple of time with all his might--once at Mr.
Cuff's left eye, and once on his beautiful roman nose.
Cuff go down this time, to the astonishment of the assembly. "well
hit, by Jove," say little Osborne, with the air of a connoisseur,
clap his man on the back. "give it he with the left, Figs my boy."
Figs's left make terrific play during all the rest of the combat. Cuff
go down every time. at the sixth round, there be almost as many
fellow shout out, "go it, Figs," as there be youth exclaim,
"go it, Cuff." at the twelfth round the latter champion be all abroad,
as the saying be, and have lose all presence of mind and power of attack
or defence. Figs, on the contrary, be as calm as a quaker. his face
be quite pale, his eye shin open, and a great cut on his
underlip bleeding profusely, give this young fellow a fierce and
ghastly air, which perhaps strike terror into many spectator.
nevertheless, his intrepid adversary prepare to close for the
thirteenth time.
if I have the pen of a Napier, or a Bell's life, I should like to
describe this combat properly. it be the last charge of the
guard--(that be, it would have be, only Waterloo have not yet take
place)--it be Ney's column breast the hill of La Haye Sainte,
bristle with ten thousand bayonet, and crown with twenty
eagle--it be the shout of the beef-eat British, as leap down
the hill they rush to hug the enemy in the savage arm of battle--in
other word, Cuff come up full of pluck, but quite reeling and
groggy, the Fig-merchant put in his left as usual on his adversary's
nose, and send he down for the last time.
"I think that will do for he," Figs say, as his opponent drop as
neatly on the green as I have see Jack Spot's ball plump into the
pocket at billiard; and the fact be, when time be call, Mr.
Reginald Cuff be not able, or do not choose, to stand up again.
and now all the boy set up such a shout for Figs as would have make
you think he have be their darling champion through the whole battle;
and as absolutely bring Dr. Swishtail out of his study, curious to
know the cause of the uproar. he threaten to flog Figs violently, of
course; but Cuff, who have come to himself by this time, and be wash
his wound, stand up and say, "itbe my fault, sir--not figs'--not
Dobbin's. I be bully a little boy; and he serve I right." by
which magnanimous speech he not only save his conqueror a whipping,
but get back all his ascendancy over the boy which his defeat have
nearly cost he.
Young Osborne write home to his parent an account of the transaction.
Sugarcane House, Richmond, March, 18--
DEAR mama,--i hope you be quite well. I should be much oblige to you
to send I a cake and five shilling. there have be a fight here
between Cuff & Dobbin. Cuff, you know, be the Cock of the School.
they fight thirteen round, and Dobbin Licked. so Cuff be now only
Second Cock. the fight be about I. Cuff be lick I for break
a bottle of milk, and Figs wouldnot stand it. we call he fig because
his father be a Grocer--Figs & Rudge, Thames St., City--I think as he
fight for I you ought to buy your Tea & Sugar at his father's. Cuff
go home every Saturday, but cannot this, because he have 2 Black Eyes.
he have a white Pony to come and fetch he, and a groom in livery on a
bay mare. I wish my Papa would let I have a Pony, and I be
your dutiful Son, GEORGE SEDLEY OSBORNE
p.s.--give my love to little Emmy. I be cut she out a Coach in
cardboard. please not a seed-cake, but a plum-cake.
in consequence of Dobbin's victory, his character rise prodigiously in
the estimation of all his schoolfellow, and the name of Figs, which
have be a byword of reproach, become as respectable and popular a
nickname as any other in use in the school. "after all, itbe not his
fault that his father's a grocer," George Osborne say, who, though a
little chap, have a very high popularity among the Swishtail youth; and
his opinion be receive with great applause. it be vote low to sneer
at Dobbin about this accident of birth. "Old Figs" grow to be a name of
kindness and endearment; and the sneak of an usher jeered at he no
long.
and Dobbin's spirit rise with his altered circumstance. he make
wonderful advance in scholastic learning. the superb Cuff himself, at
whose condescension Dobbin could only blush and wonder, help he on
with his latin verse; "coach" he in play-hour: carry he
triumphantly out of the little-boy class into the middle-sized form;
and even there get a fair place for he. it be discover, that
although dull at classical learning, at mathematic he be uncommonly
quick. to the contentment of all he pass third in algebra, and get a
french prize-book at the public Midsummer examination. you should have
see his mother's face when Telemaque (that delicious romance) be
present to he by the Doctor in the face of the whole school and the
parent and company, with an inscription to Gulielmo Dobbin. all the
boy clap hand in token of applause and sympathy. his blush, his
stumble, his awkwardness, and the number of foot which he crush as
he go back to his place, who shall describe or calculate? old Dobbin,
his father, who now respect he for the first time, give he two
guineas publicly; most of which he spend in a general tuck-out for the
school: and he come back in a tail-coat after the holiday.
Dobbin be much too modest a young fellow to suppose that this happy
change in all his circumstance arise from his own generous and manly
disposition: he choose, from some perverseness, to attribute his good
fortune to the sole agency and benevolence of little George Osborne, to
whom henceforth he vow such a love and affection as be only feel by
child--such an affection, as we read in the charming fairy-book,
uncouth Orson have for splendid young Valentine his conqueror. he fling
himself down at little Osborne's foot, and love he. even before they
be acquaint, he have admire Osborne in secret. now he be his
valet, his dog, his man Friday. he believe Osborne to be the
possessor of every perfection, to be the handsome, the brave, the
most active, the clever, the most generous of create boy. he
share his money with he: buy he uncountable present of knife,
pencil-case, gold seal, toffee, Little Warblers, and romantic book,
with large coloured picture of knight and robber, in many of which
latter you might read inscription to George Sedley Osborne, Esquire,
from his attached friend William Dobbin--the which token of homage
George receive very graciously, as become his superior merit.
so that Lieutenant Osborne, when come to Russell Square on the day of
the Vauxhall party, say to the lady, "Mrs. Sedley, Ma'am, I hope you
have room; Ihave ask Dobbin of our to come and dine here, and go with
we to Vauxhall. hebe almost as modest as Jos."
"Modesty! pooh," say the stout gentleman, cast a vainqueur look at
Miss Sharp.
"he be--but you be incomparably more graceful, Sedley," Osborne add,
laugh. "I meet he at the Bedford, when I go to look for you; and
I tell he that Miss Amelia be come home, and that we be all bent on
go out for a night's pleasuring; and that Mrs. Sedley have forgive
his break the punch-bowl at the child's party. donot you remember
the catastrophe, Ma'am, seven year ago?"
"Over Mrs. Flamingo's crimson silk gown," say good-natured Mrs.
Sedley. "what a gawky it be! and his sister be not much more
graceful. Lady Dobbin be at Highbury last night with three of they.
such figure! my dear."
"the Alderman's very rich, benot he?" Osborne say archly. "donot you
think one of the daughter would be a good spec for I, Ma'am?"
"you foolish creature! who would take you, I should like to know, with
your yellow face?"
"mine a yellow face? stop till you see Dobbin. why, he have the yellow
fever three time; twice at Nassau, and once at St. Kitts."
"well, well; yours be quite yellow enough for we. benot it, Emmy?"
Mrs. Sedley say: at which speech Miss Amelia only make a smile and a
blush; and look at Mr. George Osborne's pale interesting
countenance, and those beautiful black, curling, shin whisker,
which the young gentleman himself regard with no ordinary
complacency, she think in her little heart that in his Majesty's
army, or in the wide world, there never be such a face or such a hero.
"I donot care about Captain Dobbin's complexion," she say, "or about
his awkwardness. I shall always like he, I know," her little reason
being, that he be the friend and champion of George.
"therebe not a fine fellow in the service," Osborne say, "nor a
well officer, though he be not an Adonis, certainly." and he look
towards the glass himself with much naivete; and in so do, catch
Miss Sharp's eye fix keenly upon he, at which he blush a little,
and Rebecca think in her heart, "ah, mon beau Monsieur! I think I
have your gauge"--the little artful minx!
that evening, when Amelia come trip into the drawing-room in a
white muslin frock, prepare for conquest at Vauxhall, sing like a
lark, and as fresh as a rose--a very tall ungainly gentleman, with
large hand and foot, and large ear, set off by a closely crop head
of black hair, and in the hideous military frog coat and cock hat
of those time, advance to meet she, and make her one of the clumsy
bow that be ever perform by a mortal.
this be no other than Captain William Dobbin, of his Majesty's
Regiment of Foot, return from yellow fever, in the West Indies, to
which the fortune of the service have order his regiment, whilst so
many of his gallant comrade be reap glory in the Peninsula.
he have arrive with a knock so very timid and quiet that it be
inaudible to the lady upstairs: otherwise, you may be sure Miss
Amelia would never have be so bold as to come sing into the room.
as it be, the sweet fresh little voice go right into the Captain's
heart, and nestle there. when she hold out her hand for he to shake,
before he envelop it in his own, he pause, and thought--"Well, be it
possible--be you the little maid I remember in the pink frock, such a
short time ago--the night I upset the punch-bowl, just after I be
gazette? be you the little girl that George Osborne say should marry
he? what a bloom young creature you seem, and what a prize the
rogue have get!" all this he think, before he take Amelia's hand into
his own, and as he let his cocked hat fall.
his history since he leave school, until the very moment when we have
the pleasure of meet he again, although not fully narrate, have
yet, I think, be indicate sufficiently for an ingenious reader by
the conversation in the last page. Dobbin, the despise grocer, be
Alderman Dobbin--Alderman Dobbin be Colonel of the City Light Horse,
then burn with military ardour to resist the French Invasion.
Colonel Dobbin's corps, in which old Mr. Osborne himself be but an
indifferent corporal, have be review by the Sovereign and the Duke
of York; and the colonel and alderman have be knight. his son have
enter the army: and young Osborne follow presently in the same
regiment. they have serve in the West Indies and in Canada. their
regiment have just come home, and the attachment of Dobbin to George
Osborne be as warm and generous now as it have be when the two be
schoolboy.
so these worthy people sit down to dinner presently. they talk about
war and glory, and Boney and Lord Wellington, and the last Gazette. in
those famous day every gazette have a victory in it, and the two
gallant young man long to see their own name in the glorious list,
and curse their unlucky fate to belong to a regiment which have be
away from the chance of honour. Miss Sharp kindle with this exciting
talk, but Miss Sedley tremble and grow quite faint as she hear it.
Mr. Jos tell several of his tiger-hunt story, finish the one
about Miss Cutler and Lance the surgeon; help rebecca to everything
on the table, and himself gobble and drink a great deal.
he spring to open the door for the lady, when they retire, with the
most killing grace--and come back to the table, fill himself bumper
after bumper of claret, which he swallow with nervous rapidity.
"hebe prim himself," Osborne whisper to Dobbin, and at length the
hour and the carriage arrive for Vauxhall.
CHAPTER VI
Vauxhall
I know that the tune I be pip be a very mild one (although there be
some terrific chapter come presently), and must beg the good-natured
reader to remember that we be only discourse at present about a
stockbroker's family in Russell Square, who be take walk, or
luncheon, or dinner, or talk and make love as people do in common
life, and without a single passionate and wonderful incident to mark
the progress of their love. the argument stand thus--Osborne, in
love with Amelia, have ask an old friend to dinner and to
Vauxhall--Jos Sedley be in love with Rebecca. will he marry she? that
be the great subject now in hand.
we might have treat this subject in the genteel, or in the romantic,
or in the facetious manner. suppose we have lay the scene in Grosvenor
Square, with the very same adventure--would not some people have
listen? suppose we have show how Lord Joseph Sedley fall in love, and
the Marquis of Osborne become attach to Lady Amelia, with the full
consent of the Duke, her noble father: or instead of the supremely
genteel, suppose we have resort to the entirely low, and describe
what be go on in Mr. Sedley's kitchen--how black Sambo be in love
with the cook (as indeed he be), and how he fight a battle with the
coachman in her behalf; how the knife-boy be catch steal a cold
shoulder of mutton, and Miss Sedley's new femme de chambre refuse to
go to bed without a wax candle; such incident might be make to provoke
much delightful laughter, and be suppose to represent scene of
"life." or if, on the contrary, we have take a fancy for the terrible,
and make the lover of the new femme de chambre a professional burglar,
who burst into the house with his band, slaughter black Sambo at the
foot of his master, and carry off Amelia in her night-dress, not to
be let loose again till the third volume, we should easily have
construct a tale of thrilling interest, through the fiery chapter of
which the reader should hurry, pant. but my reader must hope for
no such romance, only a homely story, and must be content with a
chapter about Vauxhall, which be so short that it scarce deserve to be
call a chapter at all. and yet it be a chapter, and a very important
one too. be not there little chapter in everybody's life, that seem
to be nothing, and yet affect all the rest of the history?
let we then step into the coach with the Russell Square party, and be
off to the Gardens. there be barely room between Jos and Miss Sharp,
who be on the front seat. Mr. Osborne sit bodkin opposite,
between Captain Dobbin and Amelia.
every soul in the coach agree that on that night Jos would propose to
make Rebecca Sharp Mrs. Sedley. the parent at home have acquiesce in
the arrangement, though, between ourselves, old Mr. Sedley have a
feeling very much akin to contempt for his son. he say he be vain,
selfish, lazy, and effeminate. he could not endure his air as a man
of fashion, and laugh heartily at his pompous braggadocio story.
"I shall leave the fellow half my property," he say; "and he will
have, besides, plenty of his own; but as I be perfectly sure that if
you, and I, and his sister be to die to-morrow, he would say 'Good
Gad!' and eat his dinner just as well as usual, I be not go to make
myself anxious about he. let he marry whom he like. itbe no affair
of mine."
Amelia, on the other hand, as become a young woman of her prudence and
temperament, be quite enthusiastic for the match. once or twice Jos
have be on the point of say something very important to she, to
which she be most willing to lend an ear, but the fat fellow could not
be bring to unbosom himself of his great secret, and very much to his
sister's disappointment he only rid himself of a large sigh and turn
away.
this mystery serve to keep Amelia's gentle bosom in a perpetual
flutter of excitement. if she do not speak with Rebecca on the tender
subject, she compensate herself with long and intimate conversation
with Mrs. Blenkinsop, the housekeeper, who drop some hint to the
lady's-maid, who may have cursorily mention the matter to the cook,
who carry the news, I have no doubt, to all the tradesman, so that
Mr. Jos's marriage be now talk of by a very considerable number of
person in the Russell Square world.
it be, of course, Mrs. Sedley's opinion that her son would demean
himself by a marriage with an artist's daughter. "but, lor', Ma'am,"
ejaculate Mrs. Blenkinsop, "we be only grocer when we marry Mr.
S., who be a stock-broker's clerk, and we havenot five hundred pound
among we, and webe rich enough now." and Amelia be entirely of this
opinion, to which, gradually, the good-natured Mrs. Sedley be bring.
Mr. Sedley be neutral. "let Jos marry whom he like," he say; "itbe
no affair of mine. this girl have no fortune; no more have Mrs. Sedley.
she seem good-humour and clever, and will keep he in order,
perhaps. well she, my dear, than a black Mrs. Sedley, and a dozen of
mahogany grandchild."
so that everything seem to smile upon Rebecca's fortune. she take
Jos's arm, as a matter of course, on go to dinner; she have sate by
he on the box of his open carriage (a most tremendous "buck" he be,
as he sit there, serene, in state, drive his grey), and though
nobody say a word on the subject of the marriage, everybody seem to
understand it. all she want be the proposal, and ah! how rebecca
now feel the want of a mother!--a dear, tender mother, who would have
manage the business in ten minute, and, in the course of a little
delicate confidential conversation, would have extract the
interesting avowal from the bashful lip of the young man!
such be the state of affair as the carriage cross Westminster
bridge.
the party be land at the Royal Gardens in due time. as the majestic
Jos step out of the creak vehicle the crowd give a cheer for the
fat gentleman, who blush and look very big and mighty, as he walk
away with Rebecca under his arm. George, of course, take charge of
Amelia. she look as happy as a rose-tree in sunshine.
"I say, Dobbin," say George, "just look to the shawl and thing,
therebe a good fellow." and so while he pair off with Miss Sedley,
and Jos squeeze through the gate into the garden with Rebecca at his
side, honest Dobbin content himself by give an arm to the shawl,
and by pay at the door for the whole party.
he walk very modestly behind they. he be not willing to spoil
sport. about Rebecca and Jos he do not care a fig. but he think
Amelia worthy even of the brilliant George Osborne, and as he see that
good-look couple thread the walk to the girl's delight and
wonder, he watch her artless happiness with a sort of fatherly
pleasure. perhaps he feel that he would have like to have something
on his own arm besides a shawl (the people laugh at see the gawky
young officer carry this female burthen); but William Dobbin be
very little addicted to selfish calculation at all; and so long as his
friend be enjoy himself, how should he be discontent? and the
truth be, that of all the delight of the Gardens; of the hundred
thousand extra lamp, which be always light; the fiddler in cocked
hat, who play ravish melody under the gild cockle-shell in
the midst of the garden; the singer, both of comic and sentimental
ballad, who charm the ear there; the country dance, form by
bounce cockney and cockneyesse, and execute amidst jumping,
thumping and laughter; the signal which announce that Madame Saqui be
about to mount skyward on a slack-rope ascend to the star; the
hermit that always sit in the illuminated hermitage; the dark walk, so
favourable to the interview of young lover; the pot of stout hand
about by the people in the shabby old livery; and the twinkling
box, in which the happy feaster make-believe to eat slice of almost
invisible ham--of all these thing, and of the gentle Simpson, that
kind smile idiot, who, I daresay, preside even then over the
place--Captain William Dobbin do not take the slight notice.
he carry about Amelia's white cashmere shawl, and have attend
under the gilt cockle-shell, while Mrs. Salmon perform the Battle of
Borodino (a savage cantata against the Corsican upstart, who have lately
meet with his russian reverses)--mr. Dobbin try to hum it as he walk
away, and find he be hum--the tune which Amelia Sedley sing on
the stair, as she come down to dinner.
he burst out laugh at himself; for the truth be, he could sing no
well than an owl.
it be to be understand, as a matter of course, that our young people,
be in party of two and two, make the most solemn promise to keep
together during the evening, and separate in ten minute afterwards.
party at Vauxhall always do separate, but 'twa only to meet again
at supper-time, when they could talk of their mutual adventure in the
interval.
what be the adventure of Mr. Osborne and Miss Amelia? that be a
secret. but be sure of this--they be perfectly happy, and correct in
their behaviour; and as they have be in the habit of be together
any time these fifteen year, their tete-a-tete offer no particular
novelty.
but when Miss Rebecca Sharp and her stout companion lose themselves in
a solitary walk, in which there be not above five score more of
couple similarly stray, they both feel that the situation be
extremely tender and critical, and now or never be the moment Miss
Sharp thought, to provoke that declaration which be tremble on the
timid lip of Mr. Sedley. they have previously be to the panorama of
Moscow, where a rude fellow, treading on Miss Sharp's foot, cause she
to fall back with a little shriek into the arm of Mr. Sedley, and this
little incident increase the tenderness and confidence of that
gentleman to such a degree, that he tell she several of his favourite
indian story over again for, at least, the sixth time.
"how I should like to see India!" say Rebecca.
"SHOULD you?" say Joseph, with a most killing tenderness; and be no
doubt about to follow up this artful interrogatory by a question still
more tender (for he puff and pant a great deal, and Rebecca's hand,
which be place near his heart, could count the feverish pulsation of
that organ), when, oh, provoking! the bell ring for the firework, and,
a great scuffling and run take place, these interesting lover
be oblige to follow in the stream of people.
Captain Dobbin have some thought of join the party at supper: as, in
truth, he find the Vauxhall amusement not particularly lively--but he
parade twice before the box where the now united couple be meet, and
nobody take any notice of he. cover be lay for four. the mated
pair be prattle away quite happily, and Dobbin know he be as
clean forget as if he have never exist in this world.
"I should only be de trop," say the Captain, look at they rather
wistfully. "Iwould well go and talk to the hermit,"--and so he stroll
off out of the hum of man, and noise, and clatter of the banquet, into
the dark walk, at the end of which live that well-know pasteboard
Solitary. it benot very good fun for Dobbin--and, indeed, to be alone
at Vauxhall, I have find, from my own experience, to be one of the
most dismal sport ever enter into by a bachelor.
the two couple be perfectly happy then in their box: where the most
delightful and intimate conversation take place. Jos be in his glory,
order about the waiter with great majesty. he make the salad; and
uncork the Champagne; and carve the chicken; and eat and drank the
great part of the refreshment on the table. finally, he insist
upon have a bowl of rack punch; everybody have rack punch at Vauxhall.
"waiter, rack punch."
that bowl of rack punch be the cause of all this history. and why not
a bowl of rack punch as well as any other cause? be not a bowl of
prussic acid the cause of Fair Rosamond's retire from the world? be
not a bowl of wine the cause of the demise of Alexander the great, or,
at least, do not Dr. Lempriere say so?--so do this bowl of rack
punch influence the fate of all the principal character in this
"Novel without a Hero," which we be now relate. it influence their
life, although most of they do not taste a drop of it.
the young lady do not drink it; Osborne do not like it; and the
consequence be that Jos, that fat gourmand, drink up the whole
content of the bowl; and the consequence of his drink up the whole
content of the bowl be a liveliness which at first be astonishing,
and then become almost painful; for he talk and laugh so loud as to
bring score of listener round the box, much to the confusion of the
innocent party within it; and, volunteer to sing a song (which he
do in that maudlin high key peculiar to gentleman in an inebriated
state), he almost draw away the audience who be gather round the
musician in the gilt scollop-shell, and receive from his hearer a
great deal of applause.
"Brayvo, Fat un!" say one; "Angcore, Daniel Lambert!" say another;
"what a figure for the tight-rope!" exclaim another wag, to the
inexpressible alarm of the lady, and the great anger of Mr. Osborne.
"for Heaven's sake, Jos, let we get up and go," cry that gentleman,
and the young woman rise.
"stop, my dear diddle-diddle-darling," shout Jos, now as bold as a
lion, and clasp Miss Rebecca round the waist. rebecca start, but
she could not get away her hand. the laughter outside redouble. Jos
continue to drink, to make love, and to sing; and, wink and wave
his glass gracefully to his audience, challenge all or any to come in
and take a share of his punch.
Mr. Osborne be just on the point of knock down a gentleman in
top-boot, who propose to take advantage of this invitation, and a
commotion seem to be inevitable, when by the great good luck a
gentleman of the name of Dobbin, who have be walk about the
garden, step up to the box. "be off, you fool!" say this
gentleman--shoulder off a great number of the crowd, who vanish
presently before his cocked hat and fierce appearance--and he enter
the box in a most agitated state.
"Good Heavens! Dobbin, where have you be?" Osborne say, seize the
white cashmere shawl from his friend's arm, and huddle up Amelia in
it.--"make yourself useful, and take charge of Jos here, whilst I take
the lady to the carriage."
Jos be for rise to interfere--but a single push from Osborne's
finger send he puff back into his seat again, and the lieutenant
be enable to remove the lady in safety. Jos kiss his hand to
they as they retreat, and hiccup out "bless you! bless you!" then,
seize Captain Dobbin's hand, and weep in the most pitiful way, he
confide to that gentleman the secret of his love. he adore that
girl who have just go out; he have break her heart, he know he have, by
his conduct; he would marry she next morning at St. George's, Hanover
Square; hewould knock up the Archbishop of Canterbury at Lambeth: he
would, by Jove! and have he in readiness; and, act on this hint,
Captain Dobbin shrewdly induce he to leave the garden and hasten to
Lambeth Palace, and, when once out of the gate, easily convey Mr.
Jos Sedley into a hackney-coach, which deposit he safely at his
lodging.
George Osborne conduct the girl home in safety: and when the door
be close upon they, and as he walk across Russell Square, laugh
so as to astonish the watchman. Amelia look very ruefully at her
friend, as they go up stair, and kiss she, and go to bed without
any more talking.
"he must propose to-morrow," think Rebecca. "he call I his soul's
darling, four time; he squeeze my hand in Amelia's presence. he must
propose to-morrow." and so think Amelia, too. and I dare say she
think of the dress she be to wear as bridesmaid, and of the present
which she should make to her nice little sister-in-law, and of a
subsequent ceremony in which she herself might play a principal part,
&c., and &c., and &c., and &c.
oh, ignorant young creature! how little do you know the effect of rack
punch! what be the rack in the punch, at night, to the rack in the head
of a morning? to this truth I can vouch as a man; there be no headache
in the world like that cause by Vauxhall punch. through the lapse of
twenty year, I can remember the consequence of two glass! two
wine-glass! but two, upon the honour of a gentleman; and Joseph
Sedley, who have a liver complaint, have swallow at least a quart of
the abominable mixture.
that next morning, which rebecca think be to dawn upon her fortune,
find Sedley groan in agony which the pen refuse to describe.
Soda-water be not invent yet. small beer--will it be believed!--wa
the only drink with which unhappy gentleman soothe the fever of their
previous night's potation. with this mild beverage before he, George
Osborne find the ex-Collector of Boggley Wollah groan on the sofa
at his lodging. Dobbin be already in the room, good-naturedly
tend his patient of the night before. the two officer, look at
the prostrate Bacchanalian, and askance at each other, exchange the
most frightful sympathetic grin. even Sedley's valet, the most solemn
and correct of gentleman, with the muteness and gravity of an
undertaker, could hardly keep his countenance in order, as he look at
his unfortunate master.
"Mr. Sedley be uncommon wild last night, sir," he whisper in
confidence to Osborne, as the latter mount the stair. "he want to
fight the 'ackney-coachman, sir. the Capting be oblige to bring he
upstairs in his harm like a babby." a momentary smile flicker over
Mr. Brush's feature as he speak; instantly, however, they relapse
into their usual unfathomable calm, as he fling open the drawing-room
door, and announce "Mr. Hosbin."
"how be you, Sedley?" that young wag begin, after survey his
victim. "no bone break? therebe a hackney-coachman downstairs with a
black eye, and a tie-up head, vow hewill have the law of you."
"what do you mean--law?" Sedley faintly ask.
"for thrash he last night--didn't he, Dobbin? you hit out, sir,
like Molyneux. the watchman say he never see a fellow go down so
straight. ask Dobbin."
"you do have a round with the coachman," Captain Dobbin say, "and
show plenty of fight too."
"and that fellow with the white coat at Vauxhall! how Jos drive at he!
how the woman scream! by Jove, sir, it do my heart good to see you.
I think you civilian have no pluck; but Iwill never get in your way
when you be in your cup, Jos."
"I believe Ibe very terrible, when Ibe rouse," ejaculate Jos from the
sofa, and make a grimace so dreary and ludicrous, that the Captain's
politeness could restrain he no long, and he and Osborne fire off a
ring volley of laughter.
Osborne pursue his advantage pitilessly. he think Jos a milksop. he
have be revolve in his mind the marriage question pende between
Jos and Rebecca, and be not over well pleased that a member of a
family into which he, George Osborne, of the --th, be go to marry,
should make a mesalliance with a little nobody--a little upstart
governess. "you hit, you poor old fellow!" say Osborne. "you
terrible! why, man, you couldnot stand--you make everybody laugh in the
Gardens, though you be cry yourself. you be maudlin, Jos.
donot you remember sing a song?"
"a what?" Jos ask.
"a sentimental song, and call Rosa, Rebecca, whatbe her name,
Amelia's little friend--your dear diddle-diddle-darling?" and this
ruthless young fellow, seize hold of Dobbin's hand, act over the
scene, to the horror of the original performer, and in spite of
Dobbin's good-natured entreaty to he to have mercy.
"why should I spare he?" Osborne say to his friend's remonstrance,
when they quit the invalid, leave he under the hand of Doctor
Gollop. "what the deuce right have he to give himself his patronizing
air, and make fool of we at Vauxhall? whobe this little schoolgirl
that be ogle and make love to he? hang it, the family's low enough
already, without she. a governess be all very well, but Iwould rather
have a lady for my sister-in-law. Ibe a liberal man; but Ihave proper
pride, and know my own station: let she know her. and Iwill take down
that great hectoring Nabob, and prevent he from be make a great
fool than he be. thatbe why I tell he to look out, lest she bring
an action against he."
"I suppose you know well," Dobbin say, though rather dubiously. "you
always be a Tory, and your family's one of the old in England.
But--"
"come and see the girl, and make love to Miss Sharp yourself," the
lieutenant here interrupt his friend; but Captain Dobbin decline to
join Osborne in his daily visit to the young lady in Russell Square.
as George walk down Southampton row, from Holborn, he laugh as he
see, at the Sedley Mansion, in two different story two head on the
look-out.
the fact be, Miss Amelia, in the drawing-room balcony, be look very
eagerly towards the opposite side of the Square, where Mr. Osborne
dwelt, on the watch for the lieutenant himself; and Miss Sharp, from
her little bed-room on the second floor, be in observation until Mr.
Joseph's great form should heave in sight.
"Sister Anne be on the watch-tower," say he to Amelia, "but therebe
nobody come"; and laugh and enjoy the joke hugely, he describe
in the most ludicrous term to Miss Sedley, the dismal condition of her
brother.
"I think itbe very cruel of you to laugh, George," she say, look
particularly unhappy; but George only laugh the more at her piteous
and discomfited mien, persist in think the joke a most divert
one, and when Miss Sharp come downstairs, banter she with a great
deal of liveliness upon the effect of her charm on the fat civilian.
"o Miss Sharp! if you could but see he this morning," he
said--"moaning in his flowered dressing-gown--writhe on his sofa; if
you could but have see he loll out his tongue to Gollop the
apothecary."
"see whom?" say Miss Sharp.
"whom? o whom? Captain Dobbin, of course, to whom we be all so
attentive, by the way, last night."
"we be very unkind to he," Emmy say, blush very much. "I--I
quite forget he."
"of course you do," cry Osborne, still on the laugh.
"one cannot be always think about Dobbin, you know, Amelia. can one,
Miss Sharp?"
"except when he overset the glass of wine at dinner," Miss Sharp say,
with a haughty air and a toss of the head, "I never give the existence
of Captain Dobbin one single moment's consideration."
"very good, Miss Sharp, Iwill tell he," Osborne say; and as he speak
Miss Sharp begin to have a feeling of distrust and hatred towards this
young officer, which he be quite unconscious of have inspire. "he
be to make fun of I, be he?" think Rebecca. "have he be laugh
about I to Joseph? have he frighten he? perhaps he willnot come."--A
film pass over her eye, and her heart beat quite quick.
"yoube always joke," say she, smile as innocently as she could.
"joke away, Mr. George; therebe nobody to defend I." and George
Osborne, as she walk away--and Amelia look reprovingly at he--feel
some little manly compunction for have inflict any unnecessary
unkindness upon this helpless creature. "my dear Amelia," say he,
"you be too good--too kind. you donot know the world. I do. and
your little friend Miss Sharp must learn her station."
"donot you think Jos will--"
"upon my word, my dear, I donot know. he may, or may not. Ibe not his
master. I only know he be a very foolish vain fellow, and put my dear
little girl into a very painful and awkward position last night. my
dear diddle-diddle-darling!" he be off laugh again, and he do
it so drolly that Emmy laugh too.
all that day Jos never come. but Amelia have no fear about this; for
the little schemer have actually send away the page, Mr. Sambo's
aide-de-camp, to Mr. Joseph's lodging, to ask for some book he have
promise, and how he be; and the reply through Jos's man, Mr. Brush,
be, that his master be ill in bed, and have just have the doctor with
he. he must come to-morrow, she think, but she never have the
courage to speak a word on the subject to rebecca; nor do that young
woman herself allude to it in any way during the whole evening after
the night at Vauxhall.
the next day, however, as the two young lady sate on the sofa,
pretend to work, or to write letter, or to read novel, Sambo come
into the room with his usual engaging grin, with a packet under his
arm, and a note on a tray. "note from Mr. Jos, Miss," say Sambo.
how Amelia tremble as she open it!
so it run:
Dear amelia,--i send you the "Orphan of the Forest." I be too ill to
come yesterday. I leave town to-day for Cheltenham. pray excuse I,
if you can, to the amiable Miss Sharp, for my conduct at Vauxhall, and
entreat she to pardon and forget every word I may have utter when
excited by that fatal supper. as soon as I have recover, for my
health be very much shaken, I shall go to Scotland for some month, and
be
truly your, Jos Sedley
it be the death-warrant. all be over. Amelia do not dare to look
at Rebecca's pale face and burn eye, but she dropt the letter into
her friend's lap; and get up, and go upstairs to her room, and cry
her little heart out.
Blenkinsop, the housekeeper, there seek she presently with
consolation, on whose shoulder Amelia weep confidentially, and relieve
herself a good deal. "donot take on, Miss. I donot like to tell you.
but none of we in the house have like she except at fust. I sor she
with my own eye read your Ma's letter. pinner say shebe always
about your trinket-box and drawer, and everybody's drawer, and shebe
sure shebe put your white ribbing into her box."
"I give it she, I give it she," Amelia say.
but this do not alter Mrs. Blenkinsop's opinion of Miss Sharp. "I
donot trust they governess, Pinner," she remark to the maid. "they
give themselves the hair and hupstart of lady, and their wage be
no well than you nor I."
it now become clear to every soul in the house, except poor Amelia,
that rebecca should take her departure, and high and low (always with
the one exception) agree that that event should take place as speedily
as possible. our good child ransack all her drawer, cupboard,
reticule, and gimcrack box--pass in review all her gown, fichus,
tag, bobbin, lace, silk stocking, and fallal--select this thing
and that and the other, to make a little heap for Rebecca. and go
to her Papa, that generous british merchant, who have promise to give
she as many guineas as she be year old--she beg the old gentleman
to give the money to dear Rebecca, who must want it, while she lack
for nothing.
she even make George Osborne contribute, and nothing loth (for he be
as free-hand a young fellow as any in the army), he go to Bond
Street, and buy the good hat and spenser that money could buy.
"thatbe George's present to you, Rebecca, dear," say Amelia, quite
proud of the bandbox convey these gift. "what a taste he have!
therebe nobody like he."
"nobody," rebecca answer. "how thankful I be to he!" she be
think in her heart, "it be George Osborne who prevent my
marriage."--And she love George Osborne accordingly.
she make her preparation for departure with great equanimity; and
accept all the kind little Amelia's present, after just the proper
degree of hesitation and reluctance. she vow eternal gratitude to
Mrs. Sedley, of course; but do not intrude herself upon that good lady
too much, who be embarrassed, and evidently wish to avoid she. she
kiss Mr. Sedley's hand, when he present she with the purse; and
ask permission to consider he for the future as her kind, kind
friend and protector. her behaviour be so affect that he be go
to write she a cheque for twenty pound more; but he restrain his
feeling: the carriage be in wait to take he to dinner, so he
trip away with a "God bless you, my dear, always come here when you
come to town, you know.--drive to the Mansion House, James."
finally come the parting with Miss Amelia, over which picture I intend
to throw a veil. but after a scene in which one person be in earnest
and the other a perfect performer--after the tender caress, the
most pathetic tear, the smell-bottle, and some of the very good
feeling of the heart, have be call into requisition--Rebecca and
Amelia part, the former vow to love her friend for ever and ever
and ever.
CHAPTER VII
Crawley of Queen's Crawley
among the most respected of the name begin in c which the
Court-Guide contain, in the year 18--, be that of Crawley, Sir Pitt,
Baronet, Great Gaunt Street, and Queen's Crawley, hant. this
honourable name have figure constantly also in the parliamentary list
for many year, in conjunction with that of a number of other worthy
gentleman who sit in turn for the borough.
it be relate, with regard to the borough of Queen's Crawley, that
Queen Elizabeth in one of her progress, stop at Crawley to
breakfast, be so delighted with some remarkably fine Hampshire beer
which be then present to she by the Crawley of the day (a handsome
gentleman with a trim beard and a good leg), that she forthwith erect
Crawley into a borough to send two member to Parliament; and the
place, from the day of that illustrious visit, take the name of Queen's
Crawley, which it hold up to the present moment. and though, by the
lapse of time, and those mutation which age produce in empire,
city, and borough, Queen's Crawley be no long so populous a place
as it have be in Queen Bess's time--nay, be come down to that
condition of borough which use to be denominate rotten--yet, as Sir
Pitt Crawley would say with perfect justice in his elegant way,
"Rotten! be hang--it produce I a good fifteen hundred a year."
Sir Pitt Crawley (name after the great Commoner) be the son of
Walpole Crawley, first Baronet, of the Tape and Sealing-Wax Office in
the reign of George II., when he be impeach for peculation, as be
a great number of other honest gentleman of those day; and Walpole
Crawley be, as need scarcely be say, son of John Churchill Crawley,
name after the celebrated military commander of the reign of Queen
Anne. the family tree (which hang up at Queen's Crawley) furthermore
mention Charles Stuart, afterwards call Barebones Crawley, son of
the Crawley of James the First's time; and finally, Queen Elizabeth's
Crawley, who be represent as the foreground of the picture in his
fork beard and armour. out of his waistcoat, as usual, grow a tree,
on the main branch of which the above illustrious name be
inscribe. close by the name of Sir Pitt Crawley, Baronet (the subject
of the present memoir), be write that of his brother, the Reverend
Bute Crawley (the great Commoner be in disgrace when the reverend
gentleman be bear), rector of Crawley-cum-Snailby, and of various
other male and female member of the Crawley family.
Sir Pitt be first marry to Grizzel, sixth daughter of Mungo Binkie,
Lord Binkie, and cousin, in consequence, of Mr. Dundas. she bring
he two son: Pitt, name not so much after his father as after the
heaven-bear minister; and Rawdon Crawley, from the Prince of Wales's
friend, whom his Majesty George IV forget so completely. many year
after her ladyship's demise, Sir Pitt lead to the altar Rosa, daughter
of Mr. G. Dawson, of Mudbury, by whom he have two daughter, for whose
benefit Miss Rebecca Sharp be now engage as governess. it will be
see that the young lady be come into a family of very genteel
connexion, and be about to move in a much more distinguished circle
than that humble one which she have just quit in Russell Square.
she have receive her order to join her pupil, in a note which be
write upon an old envelope, and which contain the follow word:
Sir Pitt Crawley beg Miss Sharp and baggidge may be hear on Tuesday,
as I leaf for Queen's Crawley to-morrow morning erly.
Great Gaunt Street.
Rebecca have never see a Baronet, as far as she know, and as soon as
she have take leave of Amelia, and count the guineas which
good-natured Mr. Sedley have put into a purse for she, and as soon as
she have do wipe her eye with her handkerchief (which operation she
conclude the very moment the carriage have turn the corner of the
street), she begin to depict in her own mind what a Baronet must be. "I
wonder, do he wear a star?" think she, "or be it only lord that
wear star? but he will be very handsomely dress in a court suit,
with ruffle, and his hair a little powdered, like Mr. Wroughton at
Covent Garden. I suppose he will be awfully proud, and that I shall be
treat most contemptuously. still I must bear my hard lot as well as
I can--at least, I shall be amongst GENTLEFOLKS, and not with vulgar
city people": and she fall to thinking of her Russell Square friend
with that very same philosophical bitterness with which, in a certain
apologue, the fox be represent as speak of the grape.
have pass through Gaunt Square into Great Gaunt Street, the
carriage at length stop at a tall gloomy house between two other
tall gloomy house, each with a hatchment over the middle drawing-room
window; as be the custom of house in Great Gaunt Street, in which
gloomy locality death seem to reign perpetual. the shutter of the
first-floor window of Sir Pitt's mansion be close--those of the
dining-room be partially open, and the blind neatly cover up in
old newspaper.
John, the groom, who have drive the carriage alone, do not care to
descend to ring the bell; and so pray a pass milk-boy to perform
that office for he. when the bell be rung, a head appear between
the interstice of the dining-room shutter, and the door be open by
a man in drab breech and gaiter, with a dirty old coat, a foul old
neckcloth lash round his bristly neck, a shin bald head, a leering
red face, a pair of twinkle grey eye, and a mouth perpetually on the
grin.
"this Sir Pitt Crawley's?" say John, from the box.
"Ees," say the man at the door, with a nod.
"hand down these 'ere trunk then," say John.
"Hand 'n down yourself," say the porter.
"donot you see I cannot leave my hosse? come, bear a hand, my fine
feller, and Miss will give you some beer," say John, with a
horse-laugh, for he be no long respectful to Miss Sharp, as she
connexion with the family be break off, and as she have give nothing
to the servant on come away.
the bald-headed man, take his hand out of his breech pocket,
advanced on this summon, and throw Miss Sharp's trunk over his
shoulder, carry it into the house.
"take this basket and shawl, if you please, and open the door," say
Miss Sharp, and descend from the carriage in much indignation. "I
shall write to Mr. Sedley and inform he of your conduct," say she to
the groom.
"donot," reply that functionary. "I hope youhave forget nothink? Miss
'Melia's gownd--have you get they--as the lady's maid be to have 'ad?
I hope theywill fit you. shut the door, Jim, youwill get no good out of
'ER," continue John, point with his thumb towards Miss Sharp: "a
bad lot, I tell you, a bad lot," and so say, Mr. Sedley's groom
drive away. the truth be, he be attach to the lady's maid in
question, and indignant that she should have be rob of she
perquisite.
on enter the dining-room, by the order of the individual in
gaiter, Rebecca find that apartment not more cheerful than such room
usually be, when genteel family be out of town. the faithful
chamber seem, as it be, to mourn the absence of their master. the
turkey carpet have roll itself up, and retire sulkily under the
sideboard: the picture have hide their face behind old sheet of
brown paper: the ceiling lamp be muffle up in a dismal sack of brown
holland: the window-curtain have disappear under all sort of shabby
envelope: the marble bust of Sir Walpole Crawley be look from its
black corner at the bare board and the oil fire-iron, and the empty
card-rack over the mantelpiece: the cellaret have lurk away behind
the carpet: the chair be turn up head and tail along the wall:
and in the dark corner opposite the statue, be an old-fashioned crabbed
knife-box, lock and sit on a dumb waiter.
two kitchen chair, and a round table, and an attenuate old poker and
tong be, however, gather round the fire-place, as be a saucepan
over a feeble sputter fire. there be a bit of cheese and bread,
and a tin candlestick on the table, and a little black porter in a
pint-pot.
"have your dinner, I suppose? it be not too warm for you? like a drop of
beer?"
"where be Sir Pitt Crawley?" say Miss Sharp majestically.
"he, he! Ibe Sir Pitt Crawley. reklect you owe I a pint for bring
down your luggage. he, he! ask Tinker if I aynt. Mrs. Tinker, Miss
Sharp; Miss Governess, Mrs. Charwoman. Ho, ho!"
the lady address as Mrs. Tinker at this moment make her appearance
with a pipe and a paper of tobacco, for which she have be despatch a
minute before Miss Sharp's arrival; and she hand the article over to
Sir Pitt, who have take his seat by the fire.
"wherebe the farden?" say he. "I give you three halfpence. wherebe
the change, old Tinker?"
"there!" reply Mrs. Tinker, fling down the coin; "itbe only
baronet as care about farthing."
"a farthing a day be seven shilling a year," answer the M.P.; "seven
shilling a year be the interest of seven guineas. take care of your
farthing, old Tinker, and your guineas will come quite nat'ral."
"you may be sure itbe Sir Pitt Crawley, young woman," say Mrs. Tinker,
surlily; "because he look to his farthing. youwill know he well
afore long."
"and like I none the bad, Miss Sharp," say the old gentleman, with
an air almost of politeness. "I must be just before Ibe generous."
"he never give away a farthing in his life," growl Tinker.
"never, and never will: itbe against my principle. go and get another
chair from the kitchen, Tinker, if you want to sit down; and then wewill
have a bit of supper."
presently the baronet plunge a fork into the saucepan on the fire, and
withdraw from the pot a piece of tripe and an onion, which he divide
into pretty equal portion, and of which he partook with Mrs. Tinker.
"you see, Miss Sharp, when Ibe not here Tinker's on board wage: when
Ibe in town she din with the family. haw! haw! Ibe glad Miss Sharp's
not hungry, benot you, Tink?" and they fall to upon their frugal supper.
after supper Sir Pitt Crawley begin to smoke his pipe; and when it
become quite dark, he light the rushlight in the tin candlestick, and
produce from an interminable pocket a huge mass of paper, begin
read they, and put they in order.
"Ibe here on law business, my dear, and thatbe how it happen that I
shall have the pleasure of such a pretty travel companion
to-morrow."
"hebe always at law business," say Mrs. Tinker, take up the pot of
porter.
"drink and drink about," say the Baronet. "yes; my dear, Tinker be
quite right: Ihave lose and win more lawsuit than any man in England.
look here at Crawley, Bart. v. Snaffle. Iwill throw he over, or my
namebe not Pitt Crawley. podder and another versus Crawley, Bart.
overseer of Snaily parish against Crawley, Bart. they cannot prove itbe
common: Iwill defy them; the land's mine. it no more belong to the
parish than it do to you or Tinker here. Iwill beat them, if it cost
I a thousand guineas. look over the paper; you may if you like, my
dear. do you write a good hand? Iwill make you useful when webe at
Queen's Crawley, depend on it, Miss Sharp. now the dowager's dead I
want some one."
"she be as bad as he," say Tinker. "she take the law of every one of
her tradesman; and turn away forty-eight footman in four year."
"she be close--very close," say the Baronet, simply; "but she be a
valyble woman to I, and save I a steward."--And in this confidential
strain, and much to the amusement of the new-comer, the conversation
continue for a considerable time. whatever Sir Pitt Crawley's
quality might be, good or bad, he do not make the least disguise of
they. he talk of himself incessantly, sometimes in the coarse and
vulgar Hampshire accent; sometimes adopt the tone of a man of the
world. and so, with injunction to Miss Sharp to be ready at five in
the morning, he bid her good night. "youwill sleep with Tinker
to-night," he say; "itbe a big bed, and therebe room for two. Lady
Crawley die in it. good night."
Sir Pitt go off after this benediction, and the solemn Tinker,
rushlight in hand, lead the way up the great bleak stone stair, past
the great dreary drawing-room door, with the handle muffle up in
paper, into the great front bedroom, where Lady Crawley have sleep she
last. the bed and chamber be so funereal and gloomy, you might have
fancy, not only that Lady Crawley die in the room, but that her
ghost inhabit it. rebecca spring about the apartment, however, with
the great liveliness, and have peep into the huge wardrobe, and
the closet, and the cupboard, and try the drawer which be
locked, and examine the dreary picture and toilette appointment,
while the old charwoman be say her prayer. "I shouldnot like to
sleep in this yeer bed without a good conscience, Miss," say the old
woman. "therebe room for we and a half-dozen of ghost in it," say
Rebecca. "tell I all about Lady Crawley and Sir Pitt Crawley, and
everybody, my DEAR Mrs. Tinker."
but old Tinker be not to be pump by this little cross-questioner;
and signify to she that bed be a place for sleep, not
conversation, set up in her corner of the bed such a snore as only the
nose of innocence can produce. rebecca lie awake for a long, long
time, think of the morrow, and of the new world into which she be
go, and of her chance of success there. the rushlight flicker in
the basin. the mantelpiece cast up a great black shadow, over half of
a mouldy old sampler, which her defunct ladyship have work, no doubt,
and over two little family picture of young lad, one in a college
gown, and the other in a red jacket like a soldier. when she go to
sleep, Rebecca choose that one to dream about.
at four o'clock, on such a roseate summer's morning as even make great
Gaunt Street look cheerful, the faithful Tinker, have waken she
bedfellow, and bid her prepare for departure, unbarred and unbolt the
great hall door (the clanging and clapping whereof startle the
sleeping echo in the street), and take her way into Oxford Street,
summon a coach from a stand there. it be needless to particularize
the number of the vehicle, or to state that the driver be station
thus early in the neighbourhood of Swallow Street, in hope that some
young buck, reel homeward from the tavern, might need the aid of his
vehicle, and pay he with the generosity of intoxication.
it be likewise needless to say that the driver, if he have any such
hope as those above state, be grossly disappoint; and that the
worthy Baronet whom he drive to the City do not give he one single
penny more than his fare. it be in vain that Jehu appeal and
storm; that he fling down Miss Sharp's bandbox in the gutter at the
'neck, and swear he would take the law of his fare.
"youwould well not," say one of the ostler; "itbe Sir Pitt Crawley."
"so it be, Joe," cry the Baronet, approvingly; "and Iwould like to see
the man can do I."
"so should oi," say Joe, grin sulkily, and mount the Baronet's
baggage on the roof of the coach.
"keep the box for I, Leader," exclaim the Member of Parliament to the
coachman; who reply, "yes, Sir Pitt," with a touch of his hat, and
rage in his soul (for he have promise the box to a young gentleman from
Cambridge, who would have give a crown to a certainty), and Miss Sharp
be accommodate with a back seat inside the carriage, which might be
say to be carry she into the wide world.
how the young man from Cambridge sulkily put his five great-coat in
front; but be reconcile when little Miss Sharp be make to quit the
carriage, and mount up beside he--when he cover she up in one of his
Benjamins, and become perfectly good-humour--how the asthmatic
gentleman, the prim lady, who declare upon her sacred honour she have
never travel in a public carriage before (there be always such a
lady in a coach--Alas! be; for the coach, where be they?), and the
fat widow with the brandy-bottle, take their place inside--how the
porter ask they all for money, and get sixpence from the gentleman
and five greasy halfpence from the fat widow--and how the carriage at
length drive away--now thread the dark lane of Aldersgate, anon
clattering by the Blue Cupola of St. Paul's, jingle rapidly by the
stranger' entry of Fleet-Market, which, with Exeter 'Change, have now
depart to the world of shadow--how they pass the White Bear in
Piccadilly, and see the dew rise up from the market-garden of
Knightsbridge--how Turnhamgreen, Brentwood, Bagshot, be pass--need
not be tell here. but the writer of these page, who have pursue in
former day, and in the same bright weather, the same remarkable
journey, cannot but think of it with a sweet and tender regret. where
be the road now, and its merry incident of life? be there no Chelsea
or Greenwich for the old honest pimple-nosed coachman? I wonder where
be they, those good fellow? be old Weller alive or dead? and the
waiter, yea, and the inn at which they wait, and the cold round of
beef inside, and the stunted ostler, with his blue nose and clink
pail, where be he, and where be his generation? to those great
genius now in petticoat, who shall write novel for the beloved
reader's child, these man and thing will be as much legend and
history as Nineveh, or Coeur de Lion, or Jack Sheppard. for they
stage-coach will have become romance--a team of four bay as
fabulous as Bucephalus or Black Bess. ah, how their coat shine, as
the stable-man pull their clothe off, and away they go--ah, how
their tail shake, as with smoking side at the stage's end they
demurely walk away into the inn-yard. alas! we shall never hear the
horn sing at midnight, or see the pike-gate fly open any more.
Whither, however, be the light four-inside Trafalgar coach carry we?
let we be set down at Queen's Crawley without further divagation, and
see how Miss Rebecca Sharp speed there.
CHAPTER VIII
private and Confidential
Miss Rebecca Sharp to Miss Amelia Sedley, Russell Square, London.
(Free.--Pitt Crawley.)
MY dearest, SWEETEST AMELIA,
with what mingle joy and sorrow do I take up the pen to write to my
dear friend! oh, what a change between to-day and yesterday! now I
be friendless and alone; yesterday I be at home, in the sweet company
of a sister, whom I shall ever, ever cherish!
I will not tell you in what tear and sadness I pass the fatal night
in which I separate from you. you go on Tuesday to joy and
happiness, with your mother and your devoted young soldier by your
side; and I think of you all night, dance at the Perkins's, the
prettiest, I be sure, of all the young lady at the Ball. I be
bring by the groom in the old carriage to Sir Pitt Crawley's town
house, where, after John the groom have behave most rudely and
insolently to I (alas! 'twa safe to insult poverty and misfortune!),
I be give over to Sir P.'s care, and make to pass the night in an old
gloomy bed, and by the side of a horrid gloomy old charwoman, who keep
the house. I do not sleep one single wink the whole night.
Sir Pitt be not what we silly girl, when we use to read Cecilia at
Chiswick, imagine a baronet must have be. anything, indeed, less
like Lord Orville cannot be imagine. Fancy an old, stumpy, short,
vulgar, and very dirty man, in old clothe and shabby old gaiter, who
smoke a horrid pipe, and cook his own horrid supper in a saucepan.
he speak with a country accent, and swear a great deal at the old
charwoman, at the hackney coachman who drive we to the inn where the
coach go from, and on which I make the journey OUTSIDE for the
GREATER part of the WAY.
I be awaken at daybreak by the charwoman, and have arrive at the
inn, be at first place inside the coach. but, when we get to a place
call Leakington, where the rain begin to fall very heavily--will you
believe it?--I be force to come outside; for Sir Pitt be a proprietor
of the coach, and as a passenger come at Mudbury, who want an inside
place, I be oblige to go outside in the rain, where, however, a young
gentleman from Cambridge College shelter I very kindly in one of his
several great coat.
this gentleman and the guard seem to know Sir Pitt very well, and
laugh at he a great deal. they both agree in call he an old
screw; which mean a very stingy, avaricious person. he never give
any money to anybody, they say (and this meanness I hate); and the
young gentleman make I remark that we drive very slow for the last two
stage on the road, because Sir Pitt be on the box, and because he be
proprietor of the horse for this part of the journey. "but willnot I
flog them on to Squashmore, when I take the ribbon?" say the young
Cantab. "and sarve them right, Master Jack," say the guard. when I
comprehend the meaning of this phrase, and that Master Jack intend
to drive the rest of the way, and revenge himself on Sir Pitt's horse,
of course I laugh too.
a carriage and four splendid horse, cover with armorial bearing,
however, await we at Mudbury, four mile from Queen's Crawley, and we
make our entrance to the baronet's park in state. there be a fine
avenue of a mile long lead to the house, and the woman at the
lodge-gate (over the pillar of which be a serpent and a dove, the
supporter of the Crawley arm), make we a number of curtsy as she
fling open the old iron carve door, which be something like those at
odious Chiswick.
"therebe an avenue," say Sir Pitt, "a mile long. therebe six thousand
pound of timber in they there tree. do you call that nothing?" he
pronounce avenue--EVENUE, and nothing--NOTHINK, so droll; and he have a
Mr. Hodson, his hind from Mudbury, into the carriage with he, and they
talk about distraining, and sell up, and drain and subsoiling,
and a great deal about tenant and farming--much more than I could
understand. Sam Miles have be catch poach, and Peter Bailey have
go to the workhouse at last. "serve he right," say Sir Pitt; "he
and his family have be cheat I on that farm these hundred and
fifty year." some old tenant, I suppose, who could not pay his rent.
Sir Pitt might have say "he and his family," to be sure; but rich
baronet do not need to be careful about grammar, as poor governess
must be.
as we pass, I remark a beautiful church-spire rise above some old
elm in the park; and before they, in the midst of a lawn, and some
outhouse, an old red house with tall chimney cover with ivy, and
the window shin in the sun. "be that your church, sir?" I say.
"yes, hang it," (say Sir Pitt, only he use, dear, a much wickeder
word); "howbe Buty, Hodson? Buty's my brother Bute, my dear--my brother
the parson. Buty and the Beast I call he, ha, ha!"
Hodson laugh too, and then look more grave and nod his head,
say, "Ibe afraid hebe well, Sir Pitt. he be out on his pony
yesterday, look at our corn."
"look after his tithe, hang'un (only he use the same wicked word).
will brandy and water never kill he? hebe as tough as old
whatdyecallum--old Methusalem."
Mr. Hodson laugh again. "the young man be home from college. theyhave
whop John Scroggins till hebe well nigh dead."
"whop my second keeper!" roar out Sir Pitt.
"he be on the parson's ground, sir," reply Mr. Hodson; and Sir Pitt
in a fury swore that if he ever catch them poach on his ground, hewould
transport them, by the lord he would. however, he say, "Ihave sell the
presentation of the living, Hodson; none of that breed shall get it, I
war'nt"; and Mr. Hodson say he be quite right: and I have no doubt
from this that the two brother be at variance--as brother often be,
and sister too. donot you remember the two Miss Scratchleys at
Chiswick, how they use always to fight and quarrel--and Mary Box, how
she be always thump Louisa?
presently, see two little boy gather stick in the wood, Mr.
Hodson jump out of the carriage, at Sir Pitt's order, and rush upon
they with his whip. "pitch into them, Hodson," roar the baronet;
"flog their little soul out, and bring them up to the house, the
vagabond; Iwill commit them as sure as my name's Pitt." and presently we
hear Mr. Hodson's whip crack on the shoulder of the poor little
blubbering wretch, and Sir Pitt, see that the malefactor be in
custody, drive on to the hall.
all the servant be ready to meet we, and . . .
here, my dear, I be interrupt last night by a dreadful thumping at
my door: and who do you think it be? Sir Pitt Crawley in his night-cap
and dressing-gown, such a figure! as I shrink away from such a visitor,
he come forward and seize my candle. "no candle after eleven
o'clock, Miss Becky," say he. "go to bed in the dark, you pretty
little hussy" (that be what he call I), "and unless you wish I to
come for the candle every night, mind and be in bed at eleven." and
with this, he and Mr. Horrocks the butler go off laugh. you may
be sure I shall not encourage any more of their visit. they let loose
two immense bloodhound at night, which all last night be yell and
howl at the moon. "I call the dog Gorer," say Sir Pitt; "hebe
kill a man that dog have, and be master of a bull, and the mother I
use to call Flora; but now I call her Aroarer, for shebe too old to
bite. haw, haw!"
before the house of Queen's Crawley, which be an odious old-fashion
red brick mansion, with tall chimney and gable of the style of Queen
Bess, there be a terrace flank by the family dove and serpent, and on
which the great hall-door open. and oh, my dear, the great hall I be
sure be as big and as glum as the great hall in the dear castle of
Udolpho. it have a large fireplace, in which we might put half Miss
Pinkerton's school, and the grate be big enough to roast an ox at the
very least. round the room hang I donot know how many generation of
Crawleys, some with beard and ruff, some with huge wig and toe
turn out, some dress in long straight stay and gown that look as
stiff as tower, and some with long ringlet, and oh, my dear! scarcely
any stay at all. at one end of the hall be the great staircase all in
black oak, as dismal as may be, and on either side be tall door with
stag' head over they, lead to the billiard-room and the library,
and the great yellow saloon and the morning-room. I think there be
at least twenty bedroom on the first floor; one of they have the bed in
which Queen Elizabeth sleep; and I have be take by my new pupil
through all these fine apartment this morning. they be not render
less gloomy, I promise you, by have the shutter always shut; and
there be scarce one of the apartment, but when the light be let into
it, I expect to see a ghost in the room. we have a schoolroom on the
second floor, with my bedroom lead into it on one side, and that of
the young lady on the other. then there be Mr. Pitt's
apartment--Mr. Crawley, he be call--the eld son, and Mr. Rawdon
Crawley's room--he be an officer like SOMEBODY, and away with his
regiment. there be no want of room I assure you. you might lodge all
the people in Russell Square in the house, I think, and have space to
spare.
half an hour after our arrival, the great dinner-bell be rung, and I
come down with my two pupil (they be very thin insignificant little
chit of ten and eight year old). I come down in your dear muslin
gown (about which that odious Mrs. Pinner be so rude, because you give
it I); for I be to be treat as one of the family, except on company
day, when the young lady and I be to dine upstairs.
well, the great dinner-bell rang, and we all assemble in the little
drawing-room where my Lady Crawley sit. she be the second Lady
Crawley, and mother of the young lady. she be an ironmonger's
daughter, and her marriage be think a great match. she look as if
she have be handsome once, and her eye be always weep for the
loss of her beauty. she be pale and meagre and high-shoulder, and
have not a word to say for herself, evidently. her stepson Mr. Crawley,
be likewise in the room. he be in full dress, as pompous as an
undertaker. he be pale, thin, ugly, silent; he have thin leg, no
chest, hay-colour whisker, and straw-colour hair. he be the very
picture of his sainted mother over the mantelpiece--Griselda of the
noble house of Binkie.
"this be the new governess, Mr. Crawley," say Lady Crawley, come
forward and take my hand. "Miss Sharp."
"o!" say Mr. Crawley, and push his head once forward and begin again
to read a great pamphlet with which he be busy.
"I hope you will be kind to my girl," say Lady Crawley, with her pink
eye always full of tear.
"Law, Ma, of course she will," say the eld: and I see at a glance
that I need not be afraid of that woman. "my lady be serve," say the
butler in black, in an immense white shirt-frill, that look as if it
have be one of the Queen Elizabeth's ruff depict in the hall; and
so, take Mr. Crawley's arm, she lead the way to the dining-room,
whither I follow with my little pupil in each hand.
Sir Pitt be already in the room with a silver jug. he have just be
to the cellar, and be in full dress too; that is, he have take his
gaiter off, and show his little dumpy leg in black worst
stocking. the sideboard be cover with glisten old plate--old
cup, both gold and silver; old salver and cruet-stand, like Rundell
and Bridge's shop. everything on the table be in silver too, and two
footman, with red hair and canary-colour livery, stand on either
side of the sideboard.
Mr. Crawley say a long grace, and Sir Pitt say amen, and the great
silver dish-cover be remove.
"what have we for dinner, Betsy?" say the Baronet.
"Mutton broth, I believe, Sir Pitt," answer Lady Crawley.
"Mouton aux navet," add the butler gravely (pronounce, if you
please, moutongonavvy); "and the soup be potage de mouton a
l'Ecossaise. the side-dish contain pomme de terre au naturel, and
choufleur a l'eau."
"Mutton's mutton," say the Baronet, "and a devilish good thing. what
SHIP be it, horrock, and when do you kill?" "one of the black-face
Scotch, Sir Pitt: we kill on Thursday.
"who take any?"
"Steel, of Mudbury, take the saddle and two leg, Sir Pitt; but he say
the last be too young and confound woolly, Sir Pitt."
"will you take some potage, Miss ah--Miss Blunt? say Mr. Crawley.
"Capital Scotch broth, my dear," say Sir Pitt, "though they call it by
a french name."
"I believe it be the custom, sir, in decent society," say Mr. Crawley,
haughtily, "to call the dish as I have call it"; and it be serve to
we on silver soup plate by the footman in the canary coat, with the
mouton aux navet. then "ale and water" be bring, and serve to we
young lady in wine-glass. I be not a judge of ale, but I can say
with a clear conscience I prefer water.
while we be enjoy our repast, Sir Pitt take occasion to ask what
have become of the shoulder of the mutton.
"I believe they be eat in the servant' hall," say my lady, humbly.
"they be, my lady," say horrock, "and precious little else we get
there neither."
Sir Pitt burst into a horse-laugh, and continue his conversation with
Mr. Horrocks. "that there little black pig of the Kent sow's breed
must be uncommon fat now."
"itbe not quite bust, Sir Pitt," say the butler with the grave
air, at which Sir Pitt, and with he the young lady, this time, begin
to laugh violently.
"Miss Crawley, Miss Rose Crawley," say Mr. Crawley, "your laughter
strike I as be exceedingly out of place."
"never mind, my lord," say the Baronet, "wewill try the porker on
Saturday. kill un on Saturday morning, John Horrocks. Miss Sharp
adore pork, donot you, Miss Sharp?"
and I think this be all the conversation that I remember at dinner.
when the repast be conclude a jug of hot water be place before Sir
Pitt, with a case-bottle contain, I believe, rum. Mr. Horrocks
serve myself and my pupil with three little glass of wine, and a
bumper be pour out for my lady. when we retire, she take from her
work-drawer an enormous interminable piece of knitting; the young
lady begin to play at cribbage with a dirty pack of card. we have
but one candle light, but it be in a magnificent old silver
candlestick, and after a very few question from my lady, I have my
choice of amusement between a volume of sermon, and a pamphlet on the
corn-law, which Mr. Crawley have be read before dinner.
so we sit for an hour until step be hear.
"put away the card, girl," cry my lady, in a great tremor; "put
down Mr. Crawley's book, Miss Sharp"; and these order have be
scarcely obey, when Mr. Crawley enter the room.
"we will resume yesterday's discourse, young lady," say he, "and you
shall each read a page by turn; so that Miss a--Miss Short may have an
opportunity of hear you"; and the poor girl begin to spell a long
dismal sermon deliver at Bethesda Chapel, Liverpool, on behalf of the
mission for the Chickasaw Indians. be it not a charming evening?
at ten the servant be tell to call Sir Pitt and the household to
prayer. Sir Pitt come in first, very much flushed, and rather
unsteady in his gait; and after he the butler, the canary, Mr.
Crawley's man, three other man, smell very much of the stable, and
four woman, one of whom, I remark, be very much overdress, and who
fling I a look of great scorn as she plump down on her knee.
after Mr. Crawley have do harangue and expound, we receive our
candle, and then we go to bed; and then I be disturb in my
writing, as I have describe to my dearest sweetest Amelia.
good night. a thousand, thousand, thousand kiss!
Saturday.--This morning, at five, I hear the shrieking of the little
black pig. Rose and Violet introduce I to it yesterday; and to the
stable, and to the kennel, and to the gardener, who be pick fruit
to send to market, and from whom they beg hard a bunch of hot-house
grape; but he say that Sir Pitt have number every "Man Jack" of
they, and it would be as much as his place be worth to give any away.
the darling girl catch a colt in a paddock, and ask I if I would
ride, and begin to ride themselves, when the groom, come with horrid
oath, drive they away.
Lady Crawley be always knit the worsted. Sir Pitt be always tipsy,
every night; and, I believe, sit with horrock, the butler. Mr.
Crawley always read sermon in the evening, and in the morning be
lock up in his study, or else rid to Mudbury, on county business,
or to Squashmore, where he preach, on wednesday and Fridays, to the
tenant there.
a hundred thousand grateful love to your dear papa and mamma. be your
poor brother recover of his rack-punch? oh, dear! oh, dear! how man
should beware of wicked punch!
ever and ever thine own rebecca
everything consider, I think it be quite as well for our dear Amelia
Sedley, in Russell Square, that Miss Sharp and she be part. rebecca
be a droll funny creature, to be sure; and those description of the
poor lady weep for the loss of her beauty, and the gentleman "with
hay-colour whisker and straw-colour hair," be very smart,
doubtless, and show a great knowledge of the world. that she might,
when on her knee, have be think of something well than Miss
horrock's ribbon, have possibly strike both of we. but my kind reader
will please to remember that this history have "Vanity Fair" for a
title, and that Vanity Fair be a very vain, wicked, foolish place, full
of all sort of humbug and falseness and pretension. and while the
moralist, who be hold forth on the cover ( an accurate portrait of
your humble servant), profess to wear neither gown nor band, but
only the very same long-eared livery in which his congregation be
array: yet, look you, one be bind to speak the truth as far as one
know it, whether one mount a cap and bell or a shovel hat; and a
deal of disagreeable matter must come out in the course of such an
undertaking.
I have hear a brother of the story-tell trade, at Naples, preach
to a pack of good-for-nothing honest lazy fellow by the sea-shore,
work himself up into such a rage and passion with some of the villain
whose wicked deed he be describe and invent, that the audience
could not resist it; and they and the poet together would burst out
into a roar of oath and execration against the fictitious monster of
the tale, so that the hat go round, and the bajocchi tumble into it,
in the midst of a perfect storm of sympathy.
at the little Paris theatre, on the other hand, you will not only hear
the people yell out "ah gredin! ah monstre:" and curse the tyrant
of the play from the box; but the actor themselves positively refuse
to play the wicked part, such as those of infame Anglais, brutal
cossack, and what not, and prefer to appear at a small salary, in
their real character as loyal Frenchmen. I set the two story one
against the other, so that you may see that it be not from mere
mercenary motif that the present performer be desirous to show up and
trounce his villain; but because he have a sincere hatred of they,
which he cannot keep down, and which must find a vent in suitable abuse
and bad language.
I warn my "kyind friend," then, that I be go to tell a story of
harrowing villainy and complicated--but, as I trust, intensely
interesting--crime. my rascal be no milk-and-water rascal, I
promise you. when we come to the proper place we willnot spare fine
language--no, no! but when we be go over the quiet country we must
perforce be calm. a tempest in a slop-basin be absurd. we will
reserve that sort of thing for the mighty ocean and the lonely
midnight. the present chapter be very mild. other--but we will not
anticipate THOSE.
and, as we bring our character forward, I will ask leave, as a man and
a brother, not only to introduce they, but occasionally to step down
from the platform, and talk about they: if they be good and kindly, to
love they and shake they by the hand: if they be silly, to laugh at
they confidentially in the reader's sleeve: if they be wicked and
heartless, to abuse they in the strong term which politeness admit
of.
otherwise you might fancy it be I who be sneer at the practice of
devotion, which Miss Sharp find so ridiculous; that it be I who
laugh good-humouredly at the reeling old Silenus of a
baronet--whereas the laughter come from one who have no reverence
except for prosperity, and no eye for anything beyond success. such
people there be live and flourish in the world--Faithless,
Hopeless, Charityless: let we have at they, dear friend, with might
and main. some there be, and very successful too, mere quack and
fool: and it be to combat and expose such as those, no doubt, that
Laughter be make.
CHAPTER IX
Family Portraits
Sir Pitt Crawley be a philosopher with a taste for what be call low
life. his first marriage with the daughter of the noble Binkie have
be make under the auspex of his parent; and as he often tell Lady
Crawley in her lifetime she be such a confound quarrelsome high-breed
jade that when she die he be hang if he would ever take another of
her sort, at her ladyship's demise he keep his promise, and select
for a second wife Miss Rose Dawson, daughter of Mr. John Thomas Dawson,
ironmonger, of Mudbury. what a happy woman be Rose to be my Lady
Crawley!
let we set down the item of her happiness. in the first place, she
give up Peter Butt, a young man who keep company with she, and in
consequence of his disappointment in love, take to smuggling, poach,
and a thousand other bad course. then she quarrel, as in duty
bind, with all the friend and intimate of her youth, who, of course,
could not be receive by my Lady at Queen's Crawley--nor do she find
in her new rank and abode any person who be willing to welcome she.
who ever do? Sir Huddleston Fuddleston have three daughter who all
hop to be Lady Crawley. Sir Giles Wapshot's family be insult
that one of the Wapshot girl have not the preference in the marriage,
and the remain baronet of the county be indignant at their
comrade's misalliance. never mind the commoner, whom we will leave to
grumble anonymously.
Sir Pitt do not care, as he say, a brass farden for any one of they.
he have his pretty rose, and what more need a man require than to please
himself? so he use to get drunk every night: to beat his pretty rose
sometimes: to leave she in Hampshire when he go to London for the
parliamentary session, without a single friend in the wide world. even
Mrs. Bute Crawley, the Rector's wife, refuse to visit she, as she say
she would never give the pas to a tradesman's daughter.
