as the only endowment with which Nature have gift Lady Crawley be
those of pink cheek and a white skin, and as she have no sort of
character, nor talent, nor opinion, nor occupation, nor amusement,
nor that vigour of soul and ferocity of temper which often fall to the
lot of entirely foolish woman, her hold upon Sir Pitt's affection be
not very great. her rose fade out of her cheek, and the pretty
freshness leave her figure after the birth of a couple of child, and
she become a mere machine in her husband's house of no more use than
the late Lady Crawley's grand piano. be a light-complexion woman,
she wear light clothe, as most blonde will, and appear, in
preference, in draggle sea-green, or slatternly sky-blue. she work
that worst day and night, or other piece like it. she have
counterpane in the course of a few year to all the bed in Crawley.
she have a small flower-garden, for which she have rather an affection;
but beyond this no other like or dislike. when her husband be rude
to she she be apathetic: whenever he strike she she cry. she have
not character enough to take to drinking, and moan about, slipshod
and in curl-paper all day. o Vanity Fair--Vanity Fair! this might
have be, but for you, a cheery lass--Peter Butt and Rose a happy man
and wife, in a snug farm, with a hearty family; and an honest portion
of pleasure, care, hope and struggle--but a title and a coach and
four be toy more precious than happiness in Vanity Fair: and if Harry
the Eighth or Bluebeard be alive now, and want a tenth wife, do you
suppose he could not get the prettiest girl that shall be present
this season?
the languid dulness of their mamma do not, as it may be suppose,
awaken much affection in her little daughter, but they be very happy
in the servant' hall and in the stable; and the Scotch gardener
have luckily a good wife and some good child, they get a little
wholesome society and instruction in his lodge, which be the only
education bestow upon they until Miss Sharp come.
her engagement be owe to the remonstrance of Mr. Pitt Crawley, the
only friend or protector Lady Crawley ever have, and the only person,
besides her child, for whom she entertain a little feeble
attachment. Mr. Pitt take after the noble Binkies, from whom he be
descend, and be a very polite and proper gentleman. when he grow to
man's estate, and come back from Christchurch, he begin to reform the
slacken discipline of the hall, in spite of his father, who stand in
awe of he. he be a man of such rigid refinement, that he would have
starve rather than have din without a white neckcloth. once, when
just from college, and when horrock the butler bring he a letter
without place it previously on a tray, he give that domestic a look,
and administer to he a speech so cutting, that horrock ever after
tremble before he; the whole household bow to he: Lady Crawley's
curl-paper come off early when he be at home: Sir Pitt's muddy
gaiter disappear; and if that incorrigible old man still adhere to
other old habit, he never fuddle himself with rum-and-water in his
son's presence, and only talk to his servant in a very reserved and
polite manner; and those person remark that Sir Pitt never swear at
Lady Crawley while his son be in the room.
it be he who teach the butler to say, "my lady be serve," and who
insist on hand her ladyship in to dinner. he seldom speak to she,
but when he do it be with the most powerful respect; and he never let
she quit the apartment without rise in the most stately manner to
open the door, and make an elegant bow at her egress.
at Eton he be call Miss Crawley; and there, I be sorry to say, his
young brother Rawdon use to lick he violently. but though his
part be not brilliant, he make up for his lack of talent by
meritorious industry, and be never know, during eight year at
school, to be subject to that punishment which it be generally think
none but a cherub can escape.
at college his career be of course highly creditable. and here he
prepare himself for public life, into which he be to be introduce by
the patronage of his grandfather, Lord Binkie, by study the ancient
and modern orator with great assiduity, and by speak unceasingly at
the debating society. but though he have a fine flux of word, and
deliver his little voice with great pomposity and pleasure to
himself, and never advance any sentiment or opinion which be not
perfectly trite and stale, and support by a latin quotation; yet he
fail somehow, in spite of a mediocrity which ought to have insure
any man a success. he do not even get the prize poem, which all his
friend say he be sure of.
after leave college he become private Secretary to Lord Binkie, and
be then appoint Attache to the Legation at Pumpernickel, which post
he fill with perfect honour, and bring home despatch, consist
of Strasburg pie, to the Foreign Minister of the day. after remain
ten year Attache (several year after the lamented Lord Binkie's
demise), and find the advancement slow, he at length give up the
diplomatic service in some disgust, and begin to turn country gentleman.
he write a pamphlet on Malt on return to England (for he be an
ambitious man, and always like to be before the public), and take a
strong part in the Negro Emancipation question. then he become a
friend of Mr. Wilberforce's, whose politic he admire, and have that
famous correspondence with the Reverend Silas Hornblower, on the
Ashantee Mission. he be in London, if not for the Parliament session,
at least in May, for the religious meeting. in the country he be a
magistrate, and an active visitor and speaker among those destitute of
religious instruction. he be say to be pay his address to Lady
Jane Sheepshanks, Lord Southdown's third daughter, and whose sister,
Lady Emily, write those sweet tract, "the Sailor's True Binnacle," and
"the Applewoman of Finchley Common."
Miss Sharp's account of his employment at Queen's Crawley be not
caricature. he subject the servant there to the devotional
exercise before mention, in which (and so much the well) he
bring his father to join. he patronise an Independent meeting-house
in Crawley parish, much to the indignation of his uncle the Rector, and
to the consequent delight of Sir Pitt, who be induce to go himself
once or twice, which occasion some violent sermon at Crawley parish
church, direct point-blank at the Baronet's old Gothic pew there.
Honest Sir Pitt, however, do not feel the force of these discourse,
as he always take his nap during sermon-time.
Mr. Crawley be very earnest, for the good of the nation and of the
christian world, that the old gentleman should yield he up his place
in Parliament; but this the elder constantly refuse to do. both be
of course too prudent to give up the fifteen hundred a year which be
bring in by the second seat (at this period fill by Mr. Quadroon,
with carte blanche on the Slave question); indeed the family estate be
much embarrassed, and the income draw from the borough be of great
use to the house of Queen's Crawley.
it have never recover the heavy fine impose upon Walpole Crawley,
first baronet, for peculation in the Tape and Sealing Wax Office. Sir
Walpole be a jolly fellow, eager to seize and to spend money (alieni
appeten, sui profusus, as Mr. Crawley would remark with a sigh), and
in his day belove by all the county for the constant drunkenness and
hospitality which be maintain at Queen's Crawley. the cellar be
fill with burgundy then, the kennel with hound, and the stable
with gallant hunter; now, such horse as Queen's Crawley possess
go to plough, or run in the Trafalgar Coach; and it be with a team
of these very horse, on an off-day, that Miss Sharp be bring to the
Hall; for boor as he be, Sir Pitt be a stickler for his dignity while
at home, and seldom drive out but with four horse, and though he din
off boil mutton, have always three footman to serve it.
if mere parsimony could have make a man rich, Sir Pitt Crawley might
have become very wealthy--if he have be an attorney in a country town,
with no capital but his brain, it be very possible that he would have
turn they to good account, and might have achieve for himself a very
considerable influence and competency. but he be unluckily endow
with a good name and a large though encumbered estate, both of which
go rather to injure than to advance he. he have a taste for law,
which cost he many thousand yearly; and be a great deal too clever
to be rob, as he say, by any single agent, allow his affair to
be mismanage by a dozen, whom he all equally mistrust. he be such a
sharp landlord, that he could hardly find any but bankrupt tenant; and
such a close farmer, as to grudge almost the seed to the ground,
whereupon revengeful nature grudge he the crop which she grant to
more liberal husbandman. he speculate in every possible way; he work
mine; buy canal-share; horse coach; take government contract,
and be the busy man and magistrate of his county. as he would not
pay honest agent at his granite quarry, he have the satisfaction of
find that four overseer run away, and take fortune with they to
America. for want of proper precaution, his coal-mine fill with
water: the government fling his contract of damaged beef upon his
hand: and for his coach-horse, every mail proprietor in the kingdom
know that he lose more horse than any man in the country, from
underfeede and buy cheap. in disposition he be sociable, and far
from be proud; nay, he rather prefer the society of a farmer or a
horse-dealer to that of a gentleman, like my lord, his son: he be fond
of drink, of swearing, of joke with the farmer' daughter: he be
never know to give away a shilling or to do a good action, but be of
a pleasant, sly, laugh mood, and would cut his joke and drink his
glass with a tenant and sell he up the next day; or have his laugh
with the poacher he be transport with equal good humour. his
politeness for the fair sex have already be hint at by Miss Rebecca
Sharp--in a word, the whole baronetage, peerage, commonage of England,
do not contain a more cunning, mean, selfish, foolish, disreputable
old man. that blood-red hand of Sir Pitt Crawley's would be in
anybody's pocket except his own; and it be with grief and pain, that,
as admirer of the british aristocracy, we find ourselves oblige to
admit the existence of so many ill quality in a person whose name be
in Debrett.
one great cause why Mr. Crawley have such a hold over the affection of
his father, result from money arrangement. the Baronet owe his son
a sum of money out of the jointure of his mother, which he do not find
it convenient to pay; indeed he have an almost invincible repugnance to
pay anybody, and could only be bring by force to discharge his
debt. Miss Sharp calculate (for she become, as we shall hear
speedily, induct into most of the secret of the family) that the
mere payment of his creditor cost the honourable Baronet several
hundred yearly; but this be a delight he could not forego; he have a
savage pleasure in make the poor wretch wait, and in shift from
court to court and from term to term the period of satisfaction.
whatbe the good of be in Parliament, he say, if you must pay your
debt? hence, indeed, his position as a senator be not a little useful
to he.
Vanity Fair--Vanity Fair! here be a man, who could not spell, and do
not care to read--who have the habit and the cunning of a boor: whose
aim in life be pettifog: who never have a taste, or emotion, or
enjoyment, but what be sordid and foul; and yet he have rank, and
honours, and power, somehow: and be a dignitary of the land, and a
pillar of the state. he be high sheriff, and ride in a golden coach.
great minister and statesman court he; and in Vanity Fair he have a
high place than the most brilliant genius or spotless virtue.
Sir Pitt have an unmarried half-sister who inherit her mother's large
fortune, and though the Baronet propose to borrow this money of she on
mortgage, Miss Crawley decline the offer, and prefer the security
of the fund. she have signify, however, her intention of leave she
inheritance between Sir Pitt's second son and the family at the
Rectory, and have once or twice pay the debt of Rawdon Crawley in his
career at college and in the army. Miss Crawley be, in consequence, an
object of great respect when she come to Queen's Crawley, for she have a
balance at her banker's which would have make she beloved anywhere.
what a dignity it give an old lady, that balance at the banker's! how
tenderly we look at her fault if she be a relative (and may every
reader have a score of such), what a kind good-natured old creature we
find she! how the junior partner of Hobbs and Dobbs lead her smile
to the carriage with the lozenge upon it, and the fat wheezy coachman!
how, when she come to pay we a visit, we generally find an opportunity
to let our friend know her station in the world! we say (and with
perfect truth) I wish I have Miss MacWhirter's signature to a cheque for
five thousand pound. she wouldnot miss it, say your wife. she be my
aunt, say you, in an easy careless way, when your friend ask if Miss
MacWhirter be any relative. your wife be perpetually send she
little testimony of affection, your little girl work endless worst
basket, cushion, and footstool for she. what a good fire there be
in her room when she come to pay you a visit, although your wife lace
her stay without one! the house during her stay assume a festive,
neat, warm, jovial, snug appearance not visible at other season. you
yourself, dear sir, forget to go to sleep after dinner, and find
yourself all of a sudden (though you invariably lose) very fond of a
rubber. what good dinner you have--game every day, Malmsey-Madeira,
and no end of fish from London. even the servant in the kitchen share
in the general prosperity; and, somehow, during the stay of Miss
MacWhirter's fat coachman, the beer be grow much strong, and the
consumption of tea and sugar in the nursery (where she maid take her
meal) be not regard in the least. be it so, or be it not so? I
appeal to the middle class. ah, gracious power! I wish you would
send I an old aunt--a maiden aunt--an aunt with a lozenge on her
carriage, and a front of light coffee-colour hair--how my child
should work workbag for she, and my Julia and I would make she
comfortable! sweet--sweet vision! foolish--foolish dream!
CHAPTER X
Miss Sharp begin to make friend
and now, be receive as a member of the amiable family whose
portrait we have sketch in the forego page, it become naturally
Rebecca's duty to make herself, as she say, agreeable to her
benefactor, and to gain their confidence to the utmost of her power.
who can but admire this quality of gratitude in an unprotected orphan;
and, if there enter some degree of selfishness into her calculation,
who can say but that her prudence be perfectly justifiable? "I be
alone in the world," say the friendless girl. "I have nothing to look
for but what my own labour can bring I; and while that little
pink-face chit Amelia, with not half my sense, have ten thousand pound
and an establishment secure, poor rebecca (and my figure be far well
than her) have only herself and her own wit to trust to. well, let we
see if my wit cannot provide I with an honourable maintenance, and if
some day or the other I cannot show Miss Amelia my real superiority
over she. not that I dislike poor Amelia: who can dislike such a
harmless, good-natured creature?--only it will be a fine day when I can
take my place above she in the world, as why, indeed, should I not?"
thus it be that our little romantic friend form vision of the
future for herself--nor must we be scandalise that, in all her castle
in the air, a husband be the principal inhabitant. of what else have
young lady to think, but husband? of what else do their dear mamma
think? "I must be my own mamma," say rebecca; not without a tingle
consciousness of defeat, as she think over her little misadventure
with Jos Sedley.
so she wisely determine to render her position with the Queen's
Crawley family comfortable and secure, and to this end resolve to make
friend of every one around she who could at all interfere with her
comfort.
as my Lady Crawley be not one of these personage, and a woman,
moreover, so indolent and void of character as not to be of the least
consequence in her own house, Rebecca soon find that it be not at all
necessary to cultivate her good will--indeed, impossible to gain it.
she use to talk to her pupil about their "poor mamma"; and, though
she treat that lady with every demonstration of cool respect, it be
to the rest of the family that she wisely direct the chief part of
her attention.
with the young people, whose applause she thoroughly gain, her method
be pretty simple. she do not pester their young brain with too much
learning, but, on the contrary, let they have their own way in regard
to educate themselves; for what instruction be more effectual than
self-instruction? the eld be rather fond of book, and as there be
in the old library at Queen's Crawley a considerable provision of work
of light literature of the last century, both in the french and English
language (they have be purchase by the Secretary of the Tape and
Sealing Wax Office at the period of his disgrace), and as nobody ever
trouble the bookshelf but herself, Rebecca be enable agreeably,
and, as it be, in play, to impart a great deal of instruction to
Miss Rose Crawley.
she and Miss Rose thus read together many delightful French and English
work, among which may be mention those of the learn Dr. Smollett,
of the ingenious Mr. Henry Fielding, of the graceful and fantastic
Monsieur Crebillon the young, whom our immortal poet Gray so much
admire, and of the universal Monsieur de Voltaire. once, when Mr.
Crawley ask what the young people be read, the governess reply
"Smollett." "oh, Smollett," say Mr. Crawley, quite satisfied. "his
history be more dull, but by no mean so dangerous as that of Mr. Hume.
it be history you be read?" "yes," say Miss Rose; without,
however, add that it be the history of Mr. Humphrey Clinker. on
another occasion he be rather scandalise at find his sister with a
book of french play; but as the governess remark that it be for the
purpose of acquire the french idiom in conversation, he be fain to
be content. Mr. Crawley, as a diplomatist, be exceedingly proud of
his own skill in speak the french language (for he be of the world
still), and not a little pleased with the compliment which the
governess continually pay he upon his proficiency.
Miss Violet's taste be, on the contrary, more rude and boisterous
than those of her sister. she know the sequester spot where the
hen lay their egg. she could climb a tree to rob the nest of the
feather songster of their speckled spoil. and her pleasure be to
ride the young colt, and to scour the plain like Camilla. she be the
favourite of her father and of the stableman. she be the darling, and
withal the terror of the cook; for she discover the haunt of the
jam-pot, and would attack they when they be within her reach. she
and her sister be engage in constant battle. any of which
peccadillo, if Miss Sharp discover, she do not tell they to Lady
Crawley; who would have tell they to the father, or bad, to Mr.
Crawley; but promise not to tell if Miss Violet would be a good girl
and love her governess.
with Mr. Crawley Miss Sharp be respectful and obedient. she use to
consult he on passage of French which she could not understand,
though her mother be a Frenchwoman, and which he would construe to her
satisfaction: and, besides give she his aid in profane literature, he
be kind enough to select for her book of a more serious tendency, and
address to her much of his conversation. she admire, beyond measure,
his speech at the Quashimaboo-aid Society; take an interest in his
pamphlet on malt: be often affect, even to tear, by his discourse
of an evening, and would say--"Oh, thank you, sir," with a sigh, and a
look up to heaven, that make he occasionally condescend to shake hand
with she. "blood be everything, after all," would that aristocratic
religionist say. "how Miss Sharp be awaken by my word, when not one
of the people here be touch. I be too fine for they--too delicate. I
must familiarise my style--but she understand it. her mother be a
Montmorency."
indeed it be from this famous family, as it appear, that Miss Sharp,
by the mother's side, be descend. of course she do not say that her
mother have be on the stage; it would have shock Mr. Crawley's
religious scruple. how many noble emigre have this horrid revolution
plunge in poverty! she have several story about her ancestor ere
she have be many month in the house; some of which Mr. Crawley
happen to find in D'Hozier's dictionary, which be in the library,
and which strengthen his belief in their truth, and in the
high-breeding of Rebecca. be we to suppose from this curiosity and
pry into dictionary, could our heroine suppose that Mr. Crawley
be interested in her?--no, only in a friendly way. have we not state
that he be attach to Lady Jane Sheepshanks?
he take Rebecca to task once or twice about the propriety of play at
backgammon with Sir Pitt, say that it be a godless amusement, and
that she would be much well engage in read "thrump's Legacy," or
"the Blind Washerwoman of Moorfields," or any work of a more serious
nature; but Miss Sharp say her dear mother use often to play the same
game with the old Count de Trictrac and the venerable Abbe du Cornet,
and so find an excuse for this and other worldly amusement.
but it be not only by play at backgammon with the Baronet, that the
little governess render herself agreeable to her employer. she find
many different way of be useful to he. she read over, with
indefatigable patience, all those law paper, with which, before she
come to Queen's Crawley, he have promise to entertain she. she
volunteer to copy many of his letter, and adroitly alter the
spelling of they so as to suit the usage of the present day. she
become interested in everything appertain to the estate, to the
farm, the park, the garden, and the stable; and so delightful a
companion be she, that the Baronet would seldom take his
after-breakfast walk without she (and the child of course), when she
would give her advice as to the tree which be to be lop in the
shrubbery, the garden-bed to be dig, the crop which be to be cut,
the horse which be to go to cart or plough. before she have be a
year at Queen's Crawley she have quite win the Baronet's confidence; and
the conversation at the dinner-table, which before use to be hold
between he and Mr. Horrocks the butler, be now almost exclusively
between Sir Pitt and Miss Sharp. she be almost mistress of the house
when Mr. Crawley be absent, but conduct herself in her new and
exalt situation with such circumspection and modesty as not to offend
the authority of the kitchen and stable, among whom her behaviour be
always exceedingly modest and affable. she be quite a different
person from the haughty, shy, dissatisfied little girl whom we have
know previously, and this change of temper prove great prudence, a
sincere desire of amendment, or at any rate great moral courage on her
part. whether it be the heart which dictate this new system of
complaisance and humility adopt by our Rebecca, be to be prove by
she after-history. a system of hypocrisy, which last through whole
year, be one seldom satisfactorily practise by a person of
one-and-twenty; however, our reader will recollect, that, though young
in year, our heroine be old in life and experience, and we have
write to no purpose if they have not discover that she be a very
clever woman.
the elder and young son of the house of Crawley be, like the
gentleman and lady in the weather-box, never at home together--they
hat each other cordially: indeed, Rawdon Crawley, the dragoon, have a
great contempt for the establishment altogether, and seldom come
thither except when his aunt pay her annual visit.
the great good quality of this old lady have be mention. she
possess seventy thousand pound, and have almost adopt Rawdon. she
dislike her eld nephew exceedingly, and despise he as a milksop.
in return he do not hesitate to state that her soul be irretrievably
lose, and be of opinion that his brother's chance in the next world
be not a whit well. "she be a godless woman of the world," would
Mr. Crawley say; "she live with atheist and Frenchmen. my mind
shudder when I think of her awful, awful situation, and that, near as
she be to the grave, she should be so give up to vanity,
licentiousness, profaneness, and folly." in fact, the old lady decline
altogether to hear his hour's lecture of an evening; and when she come
to Queen's Crawley alone, he be oblige to pretermit his usual
devotional exercise.
"shut up your sarmon, Pitt, when Miss Crawley come down," say his
father; "she have write to say that she willnot stand the preachifying."
"o, sir! consider the servant."
"the servant be hang," say Sir Pitt; and his son think even bad
would happen be they deprive of the benefit of his instruction.
"why, hang it, Pitt!" say the father to his remonstrance. "you
wouldnot be such a flat as to let three thousand a year go out of the
family?"
"what be money compare to our soul, sir?" continue Mr. Crawley.
"you mean that the old lady willnot leave the money to you?"--and who
know but it be Mr. Crawley's meaning?
Old Miss Crawley be certainly one of the reprobate. she have a snug
little house in Park Lane, and, as she eat and drink a great deal too
much during the season in London, she go to Harrowgate or Cheltenham
for the summer. she be the most hospitable and jovial of old vestal,
and have be a beauty in her day, she say. (all old woman be
beauty once, we very well know.) she be a bel esprit, and a dreadful
Radical for those day. she have be in France (where St. just, they
say, inspire she with an unfortunate passion), and love, ever after,
french novel, french cookery, and french wine. she read Voltaire,
and have Rousseau by heart; talk very lightly about divorce, and most
energetically of the right of woman. she have picture of Mr. Fox in
every room in the house: when that statesman be in opposition, I be
not sure that she have not fling a main with he; and when he come into
office, she take great credit for bring over to he Sir Pitt and his
colleague for Queen's Crawley, although Sir Pitt would have come over
himself, without any trouble on the honest lady's part. it be needless
to say that Sir Pitt be bring to change his view after the death of
the great Whig statesman.
this worthy old lady take a fancy to Rawdon Crawley when a boy, send
he to Cambridge (in opposition to his brother at Oxford), and, when
the young man be request by the authority of the first-name
University to quit after a residence of two year, she buy he his
commission in the Life Guards Green.
a perfect and celebrated "blood," or dandy about town, be this young
officer. boxing, rat-hunting, the five court, and four-in-hand
driving be then the fashion of our british aristocracy; and he be an
adept in all these noble science. and though he belong to the
household troop, who, as it be their duty to rally round the Prince
Regent, have not show their valour in foreign service yet, Rawdon
Crawley have already (apropo of play, of which he be immoderately
fond) fight three bloody duel, in which he give ample proof of his
contempt for death.
"and for what follow after death," would Mr. Crawley observe, throw
his gooseberry-colour eye up to the ceiling. he be always think
of his brother's soul, or of the soul of those who differ with he
in opinion: it be a sort of comfort which many of the serious give
themselves.
silly, romantic Miss Crawley, far from be horrify at the courage
of her favourite, always use to pay his debt after his duel; and
would not listen to a word that be whisper against his morality.
"he will sow his wild oat," she would say, "and be worth far more than
that pule hypocrite of a brother of his."
CHAPTER XI
Arcadian simplicity
besides these honest folk at the Hall (whose simplicity and sweet
rural purity surely show the advantage of a country life over a town
one), we must introduce the reader to their relative and neighbour at
the Rectory, Bute Crawley and his wife.
the Reverend Bute Crawley be a tall, stately, jolly, shovel-hat
man, far more popular in his county than the Baronet his brother. at
college he pull stroke-oar in the Christchurch boat, and have thrash
all the good bruiser of the "town." he carry his taste for box
and athletic exercise into private life; there be not a fight within
twenty mile at which he be not present, nor a race, nor a course
match, nor a regatta, nor a ball, nor an election, nor a visitation
dinner, nor indeed a good dinner in the whole county, but he find
mean to attend it. you might see his bay mare and gig-lamp a score
of mile away from his Rectory House, whenever there be any
dinner-party at Fuddleston, or at Roxby, or at Wapshot Hall, or at the
great lord of the county, with all of whom he be intimate. he have a
fine voice; sing "a southerly wind and a cloudy sky"; and give the
"whoop" in chorus with general applause. he ride to hound in a
pepper-and-salt frock, and be one of the good fisherman in the county.
Mrs. Crawley, the rector's wife, be a smart little body, who write
this worthy divine's sermon. be of a domestic turn, and keep
the house a great deal with her daughter, she rule absolutely within
the Rectory, wisely give her husband full liberty without. he be
welcome to come and go, and dine abroad as many day as his fancy
dictate, for Mrs. Crawley be a save woman and know the price of
port wine. ever since Mrs. Bute carry off the young Rector of
Queen's Crawley (she be of a good family, daughter of the late
lieut.-colonel Hector McTavish, and she and her mother play for Bute
and win he at Harrowgate), she have be a prudent and thrifty wife to
he. in spite of her care, however, he be always in debt. it take
he at least ten year to pay off his college bill contract during
his father's lifetime. in the year 179-, when he be just clear of
these incumbrance, he give the odd of 100 to 1 (in twenty) against
Kangaroo, who win the Derby. the Rector be oblige to take up the
money at a ruinous interest, and have be struggle ever since. his
sister help he with a hundred now and then, but of course his great
hope be in her death--when "hang it" (as he would say), "Matilda must
leave I half her money."
so that the Baronet and his brother have every reason which two brother
possibly can have for be by the ear. Sir Pitt have have the well
of Bute in innumerable family transaction. young Pitt not only do
not hunt, but set up a meeting house under his uncle's very nose.
Rawdon, it be know, be to come in for the bulk of Miss Crawley's
property. these money transaction--these speculation in life and
death--these silent battle for reversionary spoil--make brother very
love towards each other in Vanity Fair. I, for my part, have know a
five-pound note to interpose and knock up a half century's attachment
between two brother; and cannot but admire, as I think what a fine and
durable thing love be among worldly people.
it cannot be suppose that the arrival of such a personage as rebecca
at Queen's Crawley, and her gradual establishment in the good grace of
all people there, could be unremarke by Mrs. Bute Crawley. Mrs. Bute,
who know how many day the sirloin of beef last at the Hall; how much
linen be get ready at the great wash; how many peach be on the
south wall; how many dos her ladyship take when she be ill--for such
point be matter of intense interest to certain person in the
country--Mrs. Bute, I say, could not pass over the Hall governess
without make every inquiry respect her history and character.
there be always the good understanding between the servant at the
Rectory and the Hall. there be always a good glass of ale in the
kitchen of the former place for the Hall people, whose ordinary drink
be very small--and, indeed, the Rector's lady know exactly how much
malt go to every barrel of Hall beer--tie of relationship exist
between the Hall and rectory domestic, as between their master; and
through these channel each family be perfectly well acquainted with
the doing of the other. that, by the way, may be set down as a
general remark. when you and your brother be friend, his doing be
indifferent to you. when you have quarrel, all his outgoing and
incoming you know, as if you be his spy.
very soon then after her arrival, Rebecca begin to take a regular place
in Mrs. Crawley's bulletin from the Hall. it be to this effect: "the
black porker's kill--weigh x stone--salt the side--pig's pudding
and leg of pork for dinner. Mr. Cramp from Mudbury, over with Sir Pitt
about put John Blackmore in gaol--Mr. Pitt at meeting (with all the
name of the people who attended)--my lady as usual--the young lady
with the governess."
then the report would come--the new governess be a rare manager--Sir
Pitt be very sweet on she--Mr. Crawley too--he be read tract to
her--"what an abandon wretch!" say little, eager, active,
black-faced Mrs. Bute Crawley.
finally, the report be that the governess have "come round"
everybody, write Sir Pitt's letter, do his business, manage his
account--have the upper hand of the whole house, my lady, Mr. Crawley,
the girl and all--at which Mrs. Crawley declare she be an artful
hussy, and have some dreadful design in view. thus the doing at the
Hall be the great food for conversation at the Rectory, and Mrs.
Bute's bright eye spy out everything that take place in the enemy's
camp--everything and a great deal besides.
Mrs. Bute Crawley to Miss Pinkerton, the Mall, Chiswick.
Rectory, Queen's Crawley, december--.
my dear madam,--although it be so many year since I profit by your
delightful and invaluable instruction, yet I have ever retain the
FONDEST and most reverential regard for Miss Pinkerton, and DEAR
Chiswick. I hope your health be good. the world and the cause of
education cannot afford to lose Miss Pinkerton for many many year.
when my friend, Lady Fuddleston, mention that her dear girl require
an instructress (I be too poor to engage a governess for mine, but be
I not educate at chiswick?)--"who," I exclaim, "can we consult but
the excellent, the incomparable Miss Pinkerton?" in a word, have you,
dear madam, any lady on your list, whose service might be make
available to my kind friend and neighbour? I assure you she will take
no governess but of your choosing.
my dear husband be pleased to say that he like everything which come
from MISS PINKERTON'S SCHOOL. how I wish I could present he and my
beloved girl to the friend of my youth, and the ADMIRED of the great
lexicographer of our country! if you ever travel into Hampshire, Mr.
Crawley beg I to say, he hop you will adorn our RURAL RECTORY with
your presence. 'tis the humble but happy home of
your affectionate Martha Crawley
P.S. Mr. Crawley's brother, the baronet, with whom we be not, alas!
upon those term of UNITY in which it become BRETHREN to DWELL, have a
governess for his little girl, who, I be tell, have the good fortune to
be educate at Chiswick. I hear various report of she; and as I have
the tender interest in my dear little niece, whom I wish, in
spite of family difference, to see among my own child--and as I
long to be attentive to any PUPIL of yours--do, my dear Miss Pinkerton,
tell I the history of this young lady, whom, for your sake, I be most
anxious to befriend.--m. C.
Miss Pinkerton to Mrs. Bute Crawley.
Johnson House, Chiswick, Dec. 18--.
dear madam,--i have the honour to acknowledge your polite
communication, to which I promptly reply. 'tis most gratify to one
in my most arduous position to find that my maternal care have
elicit a responsive affection; and to recognize in the amiable Mrs.
Bute Crawley my excellent pupil of former year, the sprightly and
accomplish Miss Martha MacTavish. I be happy to have under my charge
now the daughter of many of those who be your contemporary at my
establishment--what pleasure it would give I if your own beloved young
lady have need of my instructive superintendence!
present my respectful compliment to Lady Fuddleston, I have the
honour (epistolarily) to introduce to her ladyship my two friend, Miss
Tuffin and Miss Hawky.
either of these young lady be PERFECTLY qualified to instruct in
Greek, Latin, and the rudiment of Hebrew; in mathematic and history;
in Spanish, french, italian, and geography; in music, vocal and
instrumental; in dancing, without the aid of a master; and in the
element of natural science. in the use of the globe both be
proficient. in addition to these Miss Tuffin, who be daughter of the
late Reverend Thomas Tuffin (Fellow of Corpus College, Cambridge), can
instruct in the syriac language, and the element of constitutional
law. but as she be only eighteen year of age, and of exceedingly
pleasing personal appearance, perhaps this young lady may be
objectionable in Sir Huddleston Fuddleston's family.
Miss Letitia Hawky, on the other hand, be not personally well-favour.
she be-twenty-nine; her face be much pit with the small-pox. she
have a halt in her gait, red hair, and a trifling obliquity of vision.
both lady be endow with every MORAL and RELIGIOUS VIRTUE. their
term, of course, be such as their accomplishment merit. with my
most grateful respect to the Reverend Bute Crawley, I have the honour
to be,
Dear Madam,
your most faithful and obedient servant, Barbara Pinkerton.
P.S. the Miss Sharp, whom you mention as governess to Sir Pitt
Crawley, Bart., M.P., be a pupil of mine, and I have nothing to say in
her disfavour. though her appearance be disagreeable, we cannot control
the operation of nature: and though her parent be disreputable (her
father be a painter, several time bankrupt, and her mother, as I
have since learn, with horror, a dancer at the Opera); yet her
talent be considerable, and I cannot regret that I receive she OUT
of charity. my dread be, lest the principle of the mother--who be
represent to I as a French Countess, force to emigrate in the late
revolutionary horror; but who, as I have since find, be a person of
the very low order and moral--should at any time prove to be
HEREDITARY in the unhappy young woman whom I take as an outcast. but
her principle have hitherto be correct (I believe), and I be sure
nothing will occur to injure they in the elegant and refined circle of
the eminent Sir Pitt Crawley.
Miss Rebecca Sharp to Miss Amelia Sedley.
I have not write to my beloved Amelia for these many week past, for
what news be there to tell of the saying and doing at Humdrum Hall,
as I have christen it; and what do you care whether the turnip crop
be good or bad; whether the fat pig weigh thirteen stone or fourteen;
and whether the beast thrive well upon mangelwurzel? every day since I
last write have be like its neighbour. before breakfast, a walk with
Sir Pitt and his spud; after breakfast study (such as they be) in
the schoolroom; after schoolroom, read and write about lawyer,
lease, coal-mine, canal, with Sir Pitt (whose secretary I be
become); after dinner, Mr. Crawley's discourse on the baronet's
backgammon; during both of which amusement my lady look on with equal
placidity. she have become rather more interesting by be ail of
late, which have bring a new visitor to the Hall, in the person of a
young doctor. well, my dear, young woman need never despair. the young
doctor give a certain friend of yours to understand that, if she choose
to be Mrs. Glauber, she be welcome to ornament the surgery! I tell his
impudence that the gilt pestle and mortar be quite ornament enough; as
if I be bear, indeed, to be a country surgeon's wife! Mr. Glauber go
home seriously indispose at his rebuff, take a cool draught, and be
now quite cure. Sir Pitt applaud my resolution highly; he would be
sorry to lose his little secretary, I think; and I believe the old
wretch like I as much as it be in his nature to like any one. Marry,
indeed! and with a country apothecary, after-- No, no, one cannot so
soon forget old association, about which I will talk no more. let we
return to Humdrum Hall.
for some time past it be Humdrum Hall no long. my dear, Miss Crawley
have arrive with her fat horse, fat servant, fat spaniel--the great
rich Miss Crawley, with seventy thousand pound in the five per cent.,
whom, or I have well say which, her two brother adore. she look
very apoplectic, the dear soul; no wonder her brother be anxious
about she. you should see they struggle to settle her cushion, or
to hand her coffee! "when I come into the country," she say (for she
have a great deal of humour), "I leave my toady, Miss Briggs, at home.
my brother be my toady here, my dear, and a pretty pair they be!"
when she come into the country our hall be throw open, and for a
month, at least, you would fancy old Sir Walpole be come to life
again. we have dinner-party, and drive out in the coach-and-four the
footman put on their new canary-colour livery; we drink claret
and champagne as if we be accustomed to it every day. we have wax
candle in the schoolroom, and fire to warm ourselves with. Lady
Crawley be make to put on the bright pea-green in her wardrobe, and
my pupil leave off their thick shoe and tight old tartan pelisse,
and wear silk stocking and muslin frock, as fashionable baronet'
daughter should. Rose come in yesterday in a sad plight--the
Wiltshire sow (an enormous pet of her) run she down, and destroy a
most lovely flower lilac silk dress by dance over it--have this
happen a week ago, Sir Pitt would have swear frightfully, have box
the poor wretch's ear, and put she upon bread and water for a month.
all he say be, "Iwill serve you out, Miss, when your aunt's gone," and
laugh off the accident as quite trivial. let we hope his wrath will
have pass away before Miss Crawley's departure. I hope so, for Miss
Rose's sake, I be sure. what a charming reconciler and peacemaker money
be!
another admirable effect of Miss Crawley and her seventy thousand
pound be to be see in the conduct of the two brother Crawley. I
mean the baronet and the rector, not OUR brother--but the former, who
hate each other all the year round, become quite loving at Christmas.
I write to you last year how the abominable horse-race rector be in
the habit of preach clumsy sermon at we at church, and how Sir Pitt
snore in answer. when Miss Crawley arrive there be no such thing as
quarrelling hear of--the Hall visit the Rectory, and vice versa--the
parson and the Baronet talk about the pig and the poacher, and the
county business, in the most affable manner, and without quarrel in
their cup, I believe--indeed Miss Crawley willnot hear of their
quarrelling, and vow that she will leave her money to the Shropshire
Crawleys if they offend she. if they be clever people, those
Shropshire Crawleys, they might have it all, I think; but the
Shropshire Crawley be a clergyman like his Hampshire cousin, and
mortally offend Miss Crawley (who have flee thither in a fit of rage
against her impracticable brother) by some strait-laced notion of
morality. he would have prayer in the house, I believe.
our sermon book be shut up when Miss Crawley arrive, and Mr. Pitt,
whom she abominate, find it convenient to go to town. on the other
hand, the young dandy--"blood," I believe, be the term--Captain Crawley
make his appearance, and I suppose you will like to know what sort of
a person he be.
well, he be a very large young dandy. he be six foot high, and speak
with a great voice; and swear a great deal; and order about the
servant, who all adore he nevertheless; for he be very generous of
his money, and the domestic will do anything for he. last week the
keeper almost kill a bailiff and his man who come down from London
to arrest the Captain, and who be find lurk about the Park
wall--they beat they, duck they, and be go to shoot they for
poacher, but the baronet interfere.
the Captain have a hearty contempt for his father, I can see, and call
he an old put, an old SNOB, an old CHAW-BACON, and numberless other
pretty name. he have a dreadful reputation among the lady. he bring
his hunter home with he, live with the Squires of the county, ask
whom he please to dinner, and Sir Pitt dare not say no, for fear of
offend Miss Crawley, and miss his legacy when she die of she
apoplexy. shall I tell you a compliment the Captain pay I? I must,
it be so pretty. one evening we actually have a dance; there be Sir
Huddleston Fuddleston and his family, Sir Giles Wapshot and his young
lady, and I donot know how many more. well, I hear he say--"By
Jove, shebe a neat little filly!" mean your humble servant; and he
do I the honour to dance two country-dance with I. he get on
pretty gaily with the young squire, with whom he drink, bet, rid,
and talk about hunting and shooting; but he say the country girl be
BORES; indeed, I donot think he be far wrong. you should see the
contempt with which they look down on poor I! when they dance I sit
and play the piano very demurely; but the other night, come in rather
flush from the dining-room, and see I employ in this way, he
swear out loud that I be the good dancer in the room, and take a great
oath that he would have the fiddler from Mudbury.
"Iwill go and play a country-dance," say Mrs. Bute Crawley, very
readily (she be a little, black-face old woman in a turban, rather
crooked, and with very twinkle eye); and after the Captain and your
poor little Rebecca have perform a dance together, do you know she
actually do I the honour to compliment I upon my step! such a thing
be never hear of before; the proud Mrs. Bute Crawley, first cousin to
the Earl of Tiptoff, who willnot condescend to visit Lady Crawley, except
when her sister be in the country. Poor Lady Crawley! during most part
of these gaiety, she be upstairs take pill.
Mrs. Bute have all of a sudden take a great fancy to I. "my dear Miss
Sharp," she say, "why not bring over your girl to the Rectory?--their
cousin will be so happy to see they." I know what she mean. Signor
Clementi do not teach we the piano for nothing; at which price Mrs.
Bute hop to get a professor for her child. I can see through her
scheme, as though she tell they to I; but I shall go, as I be
determined to make myself agreeable--be it not a poor governess's duty,
who have not a friend or protector in the world? the Rector's wife pay
I a score of compliment about the progress my pupil make, and
thought, no doubt, to touch my heart--poor, simple, country soul!--as
if I care a fig about my pupil!
your India muslin and your pink silk, dear Amelia, be say to
become I very well. they be a good deal wear now; but, you know, we
poor girl cannot afford des fraiche toilette. happy, happy you! who
have but to drive to St. James's Street, and a dear mother who will
give you any thing you ask. farewell, dear girl,
your affectionate rebecca.
P.S.--I wish you could have see the face of the Miss Blackbrooks
(Admiral Blackbrook's daughter, my dear), fine young lady, with
dress from London, when Captain Rawdon select poor I for a partner!
when Mrs. Bute Crawley (whose artifice our ingenious Rebecca have so
soon discover) have procure from Miss Sharp the promise of a visit,
she induce the all-powerful Miss Crawley to make the necessary
application to Sir Pitt, and the good-natured old lady, who love to be
gay herself, and to see every one gay and happy round about she, be
quite charm, and ready to establish a reconciliation and intimacy
between her two brother. it be therefore agree that the young people
of both family should visit each other frequently for the future, and
the friendship of course last as long as the jovial old mediatrix be
there to keep the peace.
"why do you ask that scoundrel, Rawdon Crawley, to dine?" say the
Rector to his lady, as they be walk home through the park. "I
donot want the fellow. he look down upon we country people as so many
blackamoor. hebe never content unless he get my yellow-seal wine,
which cost I ten shilling a bottle, hang he! besides, hebe such an
infernal character--hebe a gambler--hebe a drunkard--hebe a profligate
in every way. he shoot a man in a duel--hebe over head and ear in
debt, and hebe rob I and mine of the good part of Miss Crawley's
fortune. Waxy say she have him"--here the Rector shake his fist at the
moon, with something very like an oath, and add, in a melancholious
tone, "--down in her will for fifty thousand; and there willnot be above
thirty to divide."
"I think shebe go," say the Rector's wife. "she be very red in
the face when we leave dinner. I be oblige to unlace she."
"she drink seven glass of champagne," say the reverend gentleman, in
a low voice; "and filthy champagne it be, too, that my brother poison
we with--but you woman never know whatbe what."
"we know nothing," say Mrs. Bute Crawley.
"she drink cherry-brandy after dinner," continue his reverence, "and
take curacao with her coffee. I wouldnot take a glass for a five-pound
note: it kill I with heartburn. she cannot stand it, Mrs.
Crawley--she must go--flesh and blood willnot bear it! and I lie five to
two, Matilda drop in a year."
indulge in these solemn speculation, and think about his debt,
and his son Jim at College, and Frank at Woolwich, and the four girl,
who be no beauty, poor thing, and would not have a penny but what
they get from the aunt's expect legacy, the Rector and his lady
walk on for a while.
"Pitt cannot be such an infernal villain as to sell the reversion of the
living. and that Methodist milksop of an eld son look to
Parliament," continue Mr. Crawley, after a pause.
"Sir Pitt Crawley will do anything," say the Rector's wife. "we must
get Miss Crawley to make he promise it to James."
"Pitt will promise anything," reply the brother. "he promise hewould
pay my college bill, when my father die; he promise hewould build the
new wing to the Rectory; he promise hewould let I have Jibb's field and
the six-acre Meadow--and much he execute his promise! and itbe to
this man's son--this scoundrel, gambler, swindler, murderer of a Rawdon
Crawley, that Matilda leave the bulk of her money. I say itbe
un-Christian. by Jove, it be. the infamous dog have get every vice
except hypocrisy, and that belong to his brother."
"Hush, my dearest love! webe in Sir Pitt's ground," interpose his
wife.
"I say he have get every vice, Mrs. Crawley. donot Ma'am, bully I.
donot he shoot Captain Marker? donot he rob young Lord Dovedale at
the Cocoa-Tree? donot he cross the fight between Bill Soames and the
Cheshire Trump, by which I lose forty pound? you know he do; and as
for the woman, why, you hear that before I, in my own magistrate's
room."
"for heaven's sake, Mr. Crawley," say the lady, "spare I the detail."
"and you ask this villain into your house!" continue the exasperate
Rector. "you, the mother of a young family--the wife of a clergyman of
the Church of England. by Jove!"
"Bute Crawley, you be a fool," say the Rector's wife scornfully.
"well, Ma'am, fool or not--and I donot say, Martha, Ibe so clever as
you be, I never do. but I willnot meet Rawdon Crawley, thatbe flat.
Iwill go over to Huddleston, that I will, and see his black greyhound,
Mrs. Crawley; and Iwill run Lancelot against he for fifty. by Jove, I
will; or against any dog in England. but I willnot meet that beast
Rawdon Crawley."
"Mr. Crawley, you be intoxicate, as usual," reply his wife. and
the next morning, when the Rector wake, and call for small beer, she
put he in mind of his promise to visit Sir Huddleston Fuddleston on
Saturday, and as he know he should have a wet night, it be agree that
he might gallop back again in time for church on Sunday morning. thus
it will be see that the parishioner of Crawley be equally happy in
their squire and in their Rector.
Miss Crawley have not long be establish at the Hall before Rebecca's
fascination have win the heart of that good-natured London rake, as
they have of the country innocent whom we have be describe. take
her accustomed drive, one day, she think fit to order that "that
little governess" should accompany she to Mudbury. before they have
return Rebecca have make a conquest of she; have make her laugh four
time, and amuse she during the whole of the little journey.
"not let Miss Sharp dine at table!" say she to Sir Pitt, who have
arrange a dinner of ceremony, and ask all the neighbour baronet.
"my dear creature, do you suppose I can talk about the nursery with
Lady Fuddleston, or discuss justice' business with that goose, old Sir
Giles Wapshot? I insist upon Miss Sharp appear. let Lady Crawley
remain upstairs, if there be no room. but little Miss Sharp! why, shebe
the only person fit to talk to in the county!"
of course, after such a peremptory order as this, Miss Sharp, the
governess, receive command to dine with the illustrious company below
stair. and when Sir Huddleston have, with great pomp and ceremony,
hand Miss Crawley in to dinner, and be prepare to take his place
by her side, the old lady cry out, in a shrill voice, "Becky Sharp!
Miss Sharp! come you and sit by I and amuse I; and let Sir
Huddleston sit by Lady Wapshot."
when the party be over, and the carriage have roll away, the
insatiable Miss Crawley would say, "come to my dressing room, Becky,
and let we abuse the company"--which, between they, this pair of
friend do perfectly. Old Sir Huddleston wheeze a great deal at
dinner; Sir Giles Wapshot have a particularly noisy manner of imbibe
his soup, and her ladyship a wink of the left eye; all of which Becky
caricature to admiration; as well as the particular of the night's
conversation; the politic; the war; the quarter-session; the famous
run with the H.H., and those heavy and dreary theme, about which
country gentleman converse. as for the Misses Wapshot's toilette and
Lady Fuddleston's famous yellow hat, Miss Sharp tear they to tatter,
to the infinite amusement of her audience.
"my dear, you be a perfect trouvaille," Miss Crawley would say. "I
wish you could come to I in London, but I couldnot make a butt of you
as I do of poor Briggs no, no, you little sly creature; you be too
clever--Isn't she, Firkin?"
Mrs. Firkin (who be dress the very small remnant of hair which
remain on Miss Crawley's pate), fling up her head and say, "I think
Miss be very clever," with the most kill sarcastic air. in fact,
Mrs. Firkin have that natural jealousy which be one of the main
principle of every honest woman.
after rebuff Sir Huddleston Fuddleston, Miss Crawley order that
Rawdon Crawley should lead she in to dinner every day, and that Becky
should follow with her cushion--or else she would have Becky's arm and
Rawdon with the pillow. "we must sit together," she say. "webe the
only three Christians in the county, my love"--in which case, it must
be confess, that religion be at a very low ebb in the county of
hant.
besides be such a fine religionist, Miss Crawley be, as we have
say, an ultra-liberal in opinion, and always take occasion to express
these in the most candid manner.
"what be birth, my dear!" she would say to rebecca--"look at my brother
Pitt; look at the Huddlestons, who have be here since Henry II; look
at poor Bute at the parsonage--be any one of they equal to you in
intelligence or breeding? equal to you--they be not even equal to poor
dear Briggs, my companion, or Bowls, my butler. you, my love, be a
little paragon--positively a little jewel--you have more brain than
half the shire--if merit have its reward you ought to be a Duchess--no,
there ought to be no duchess at all--but you ought to have no
superior, and I consider you, my love, as my equal in every respect;
and--will you put some coal on the fire, my dear; and will you pick
this dress of mine, and alter it, you who can do it so well?" so this
old philanthropist use to make her equal run of her errand, execute
her millinery, and read she to sleep with french novel, every night.
at this time, as some old reader may recollect, the genteel world have
be throw into a considerable state of excitement by two event,
which, as the paper say, might give employment to the gentleman of the
long robe. Ensign Shafton have run away with Lady Barbara Fitzurse, the
Earl of Bruin's daughter and heiress; and poor Vere Vane, a gentleman
who, up to forty, have maintain a most respectable character and
rear a numerous family, suddenly and outrageously leave his home, for
the sake of Mrs. Rougemont, the actress, who be sixty-five year of
age.
"that be the most beautiful part of dear Lord Nelson's character,"
Miss Crawley say. "he go to the deuce for a woman. there must be
good in a man who will do that. I adore all impudent matches.-- what
I like well, be for a nobleman to marry a miller's daughter, as Lord
Flowerdale do--it make all the woman so angry--I wish some great man
would run away with you, my dear; Ibe sure yoube pretty enough."
"two post-boys!--oh, it would be delightful!" Rebecca own.
"and what I like next well, be for a poor fellow to run away with a
rich girl. I have set my heart on Rawdon run away with some one."
"a rich some one, or a poor some one?"
"why, you goose! Rawdon have not a shilling but what I give he. he be
crible de dette--he must repair his fortune, and succeed in the
world."
"be he very clever?" rebecca ask.
"Clever, my love?--not an idea in the world beyond his horse, and his
regiment, and his hunting, and his play; but he must succeed--hebe so
delightfully wicked. donot you know he have hit a man, and shoot an
injure father through the hat only? hebe adore in his regiment; and
all the young man at Wattier's and the Cocoa-Tree swear by he."
when Miss Rebecca Sharp write to her beloved friend the account of the
little ball at Queen's Crawley, and the manner in which, for the first
time, Captain Crawley have distinguish she, she do not, strange to
relate, give an altogether accurate account of the transaction. the
Captain have distinguish she a great number of time before. the
Captain have meet she in a half-score of walk. the Captain have light
upon she in a half-hundred of corridor and passage. the Captain have
hang over her piano twenty time of an evening (my Lady be now
upstairs, be ill, and nobody heed she) as Miss Sharp sing. the
Captain have write her note (the good that the great blundering
dragoon could devise and spell; but dulness get on as well as any
other quality with woman). but when he put the first of the note into
the leaf of the song she be sing, the little governess, rise
and look he steadily in the face, take up the triangular missive
daintily, and wave it about as if it be a cocked hat, and she,
advance to the enemy, pop the note into the fire, and make he a
very low curtsey, and go back to her place, and begin to sing away
again more merrily than ever.
"whatbe that?" say Miss Crawley, interrupt in her after-dinner doze
by the stoppage of the music.
"itbe a false note," Miss Sharp say with a laugh; and Rawdon Crawley
fume with rage and mortification.
see the evident partiality of Miss Crawley for the new governess,
how good it be of Mrs. Bute Crawley not to be jealous, and to welcome
the young lady to the Rectory, and not only she, but Rawdon Crawley,
her husband's rival in the Old Maid's five per cent! they become very
fond of each other's society, Mrs. Crawley and her nephew. he give up
hunting; he decline entertainment at Fuddleston: he would not dine
with the mess of the depot at Mudbury: his great pleasure be to stroll
over to Crawley parsonage--whither Miss Crawley come too; and as their
mamma be ill, why not the child with Miss Sharp? so the child
(little dear!) come with Miss Sharp; and of an evening some of the
party would walk back together. not Miss Crawley--she prefer she
carriage--but the walk over the rectory field, and in at the little
park wicket, and through the dark plantation, and up the checker
avenue to Queen's Crawley, be charming in the moonlight to two such
lover of the picturesque as the Captain and Miss Rebecca.
"o those star, those star!" Miss Rebecca would say, turn she
twinkle green eye up towards they. "I feel myself almost a spirit
when I gaze upon they."
"o--ah--Gad--yes, so do I exactly, Miss Sharp," the other enthusiast
reply. "you donot mind my cigar, do you, Miss Sharp?" Miss Sharp
love the smell of a cigar out of door beyond everything in the
world--and she just taste one too, in the prettiest way possible, and
give a little puff, and a little scream, and a little giggle, and
restore the delicacy to the Captain, who twirl his moustache, and
straightway puff it into a blaze that glow quite red in the dark
plantation, and swore--"jove--aw--Gad--aw--itbe the fine segaw I ever
smoke in the world aw," for his intellect and conversation be alike
brilliant and become to a heavy young dragoon.
Old Sir Pitt, who be take his pipe and beer, and talk to John
Horrocks about a "ship" that be to be kill, espy the pair so
occupy from his study-window, and with dreadful oath swear that if
it benot for Miss Crawley, hewould take Rawdon and bundle un out of
door, like a rogue as he be.
"he be a bad'n, sure enough," Mr. Horrocks remark; "and his man
Flethers be wuss, and have make such a row in the housekeeper's room
about the dinner and hale, as no lord would make--but I think Miss
Sharp's a match for'n, Sir Pitt," he add, after a pause.
and so, in truth, she be--for father and son too.
CHAPTER XII
quite a sentimental chapter
we must now take leave of Arcadia, and those amiable people practise
the rural virtue there, and travel back to London, to inquire what have
become of Miss Amelia "we donot care a fig for she," write some
unknown correspondent with a pretty little handwriting and a pink seal
to her note. "she be fade and insipid," and add some more kind
remark in this strain, which I should never have repeat at all, but
that they be in truth prodigiously complimentary to the young lady
whom they concern.
have the beloved reader, in his experience of society, never hear
similar remark by good-natured female friend; who always wonder what
you can see in Miss Smith that be so fascinating; or what could induce
Major Jones to propose for that silly insignificant simper Miss
Thompson, who have nothing but her wax-doll face to recommend she? what
be there in a pair of pink cheek and blue eye forsooth? these dear
Moralists ask, and hint wisely that the gift of genius, the
accomplishment of the mind, the mastery of Mangnall's Questions, and a
ladylike knowledge of botany and geology, the knack of make poetry,
the power of rattle sonata in the Herz-manner, and so forth, be far
more valuable endowment for a female, than those fugitive charm which
a few year will inevitably tarnish. it be quite edify to hear
woman speculate upon the worthlessness and the duration of beauty.
but though virtue be a much fine thing, and those hapless creature
who suffer under the misfortune of good look ought to be continually
put in mind of the fate which await they; and though, very likely, the
heroic female character which lady admire be a more glorious and
beautiful object than the kind, fresh, smiling, artless, tender little
domestic goddess, whom man be incline to worship--yet the latter and
inferior sort of woman must have this consolation--that the man do
admire they after all; and that, in spite of all our kind friend'
warning and protest, we go on in our desperate error and folly, and
shall to the end of the chapter. indeed, for my own part, though I have
be repeatedly tell by person for whom I have the great respect,
that Miss Brown be an insignificant chit, and Mrs. White have nothing
but her petit minois chiffonne, and Mrs. Black have not a word to say
for herself; yet I know that I have have the most delightful
conversation with Mrs. Black (of course, my dear Madam, they be
inviolable): I see all the man in a cluster round Mrs. White's chair:
all the young fellow battle to dance with Miss Brown; and so I be
tempt to think that to be despise by her sex be a very great
compliment to a woman.
the young lady in Amelia's society do this for her very
satisfactorily. for instance, there be scarcely any point upon which
the Misses Osborne, George's sister, and the Mesdemoiselles Dobbin
agree so well as in their estimate of her very trifling merit: and
their wonder that their brother could find any charm in she. "we be
kind to she," the Misses Osborne say, a pair of fine black-browe
young lady who have have the good of governess, master, and
milliner; and they treat she with such extreme kindness and
condescension, and patronise she so insufferably, that the poor little
thing be in fact perfectly dumb in their presence, and to all outward
appearance as stupid as they think she. she make effort to like
they, as in duty bind, and as sister of her future husband. she
pass "long morning" with they--the most dreary and serious of
forenoon. she drive out solemnly in their great family coach with
they, and Miss Wirt their governess, that raw-bone vestal. they take
she to the ancient concert by way of a treat, and to the oratorio, and
to St. Paul's to see the charity child, where in such terror be she
of her friend, she almost do not dare be affect by the hymn the
child sing. their house be comfortable; their papa's table rich
and handsome; their society solemn and genteel; their self-respect
prodigious; they have the good pew at the Foundling: all their habit
be pompous and orderly, and all their amusement intolerably dull and
decorous. after every one of her visit (and oh how glad she be when
they be over!) Miss Osborne and Miss Maria Osborne, and Miss Wirt,
the vestal governess, ask each other with increase wonder, "what
could George find in that creature?"
how be this? some carping reader exclaim. how be it that Amelia, who
have such a number of friend at school, and be so beloved there, come
out into the world and be spurn by her discriminating sex? my dear
sir, there be no man at Miss Pinkerton's establishment except the old
dancing-master; and you would not have have the girl fall out about
HIM? when George, their handsome brother, run off directly after
breakfast, and din from home half-a-dozen time a week, no wonder the
neglect sister feel a little vexation. when young Bullock (of the
firm of Hulker, Bullock & Co., Bankers, Lombard Street), who have be
make up to Miss Maria the last two season, actually ask Amelia to
dance the cotillon, could you expect that the former young lady should
be please? and yet she say she be, like an artless forgiving
creature. "Ibe so delighted you like dear Amelia," she say quite
eagerly to Mr. Bullock after the dance. "shebe engaged to my brother
George; therebe not much in she, but shebe the well-natured and most
unaffected young creature: at home webe all so fond of she." dear
girl! who can calculate the depth of affection express in that
enthusiastic so?
Miss Wirt and these two affectionate young woman so earnestly and
frequently impress upon George Osborne's mind the enormity of the
sacrifice he be make, and his romantic generosity in throw
himself away upon Amelia, that Ibe not sure but that he really think
he be one of the most deserving character in the british army, and
give himself up to be love with a good deal of easy resignation.
somehow, although he leave home every morning, as be state, and din
abroad six day in the week, when his sister believe the infatuated
youth to be at Miss Sedley's apron-string: he be not always with
Amelia, whilst the world suppose he at her foot. certain it be that
on more occasion than one, when Captain Dobbin call to look for his
friend, Miss Osborne (who be very attentive to the Captain, and
anxious to hear his military story, and to know about the health of
his dear Mamma), would laughingly point to the opposite side of the
square, and say, "oh, you must go to the Sedleys' to ask for George; we
never see he from morning till night." at which kind of speech the
Captain would laugh in rather an absurd constrain manner, and turn
off the conversation, like a consummate man of the world, to some topic
of general interest, such as the Opera, the Prince's last ball at
Carlton House, or the weather--that blessing to society.
"what an innocent it be, that pet of your," Miss Maria would then say
to Miss Jane, upon the Captain's departure. "do you see how he
blush at the mention of poor George on duty?"
"itbe a pity Frederick Bullock havenot some of his modesty, Maria,"
reply the eld sister, with a toss of he head.
"Modesty! awkwardness you mean, Jane. I donot want Frederick to
trample a hole in my muslin frock, as Captain Dobbin do in yours at
Mrs. Perkins'."
"in your frock, he, he! how could he? benot he dance with Amelia?"
the fact be, when Captain Dobbin blush so, and look so awkward, he
remember a circumstance of which he do not think it be necessary to
inform the young lady, viz., that he have be call at Mr. Sedley's
house already, on the pretence of see George, of course, and George
benot there, only poor little Amelia, with rather a sad wistful face,
seat near the drawing-room window, who, after some very trifling
stupid talk, venture to ask, be there any truth in the report that
the regiment be soon to be order abroad; and have Captain Dobbin see
Mr. Osborne that day?
the regiment be not order abroad as yet; and Captain Dobbin have not
see George. "he be with his sister, most likely," the Captain say.
"should he go and fetch the truant?" so she give he her hand kindly
and gratefully: and he cross the square; and she wait and wait,
but George never come.
poor little tender heart! and so it go on hop and beat, and
longing and trusting. you see it be not much of a life to describe.
there be not much of what you call incident in it. only one feeling
all day--when will he come? only one think to sleep and wake upon. I
believe George be play billiard with Captain Cannon in Swallow
Street at the time when Amelia be ask Captain Dobbin about he; for
George be a jolly sociable fellow, and excellent in all game of skill.
once, after three day of absence, Miss Amelia put on her bonnet, and
actually invade the Osborne house. "what! leave our brother to come to
we?" say the young lady. "have you have a quarrel, Amelia? do tell
we!" no, indeed, there have be no quarrel. "who could quarrel with
he?" say she, with her eye fill with tear. she only come over
to--to see her dear friend; they have not meet for so long. and this
day she be so perfectly stupid and awkward, that the Misses Osborne
and their governess, who star after she as she go sadly away,
wonder more than ever what George could see in poor little Amelia.
of course they do. how be she to bare that timid little heart for
the inspection of those young lady with their bold black eye? it be
good that it should shrink and hide itself. I know the Misses Osborne
be excellent critic of a Cashmere shawl, or a pink satin slip; and
when Miss Turner have hers dye purple, and make into a spencer; and
when Miss Pickford have her ermine tippet twist into a muff and
trimming, I warrant you the change do not escape the two intelligent
young woman before mention. but there be thing, look you, of a
fine texture than fur or satin, and all Solomon's glory, and all the
wardrobe of the Queen of Sheba--thing whereof the beauty escape the
eye of many connoisseur. and there be sweet modest little soul on
which you light, fragrant and bloom tenderly in quiet shady place;
and there be garden-ornament, as big as brass warming-pan, that be
fit to stare the sun itself out of countenance. Miss Sedley be not of
the sunflower sort; and I say it be out of the rule of all proportion
to draw a violet of the size of a double dahlia.
no, indeed; the life of a good young girl who be in the paternal nest
as yet, cannot have many of those thrill incident to which the
heroine of romance commonly lay claim. snare or shot may take off
the old bird forage without--hawk may be abroad, from which they
escape or by whom they suffer; but the young one in the nest have a
pretty comfortable unromantic sort of existence in the down and the
straw, till it come to their turn, too, to get on the wing. while
Becky Sharp be on her own wing in the country, hop on all sort of
twig, and amid a multiplicity of trap, and peck up her food quite
harmless and successful, Amelia lie snug in her home of Russell Square;
if she go into the world, it be under the guidance of the elder;
nor do it seem that any evil could befall she or that opulent cheery
comfortable home in which she be affectionately shelter. Mamma have
her morning duty, and her daily drive, and the delightful round of
visit and shopping which form the amusement, or the profession as you
may call it, of the rich London lady. Papa conduct his mysterious
operation in the City--a stirring place in those day, when war be
rag all over Europe, and empire be be stake; when the
"Courier" newspaper have ten of thousand of subscriber; when one day
bring you a battle of Vittoria, another a burning of Moscow, or a
newsman's horn blow down Russell Square about dinner-time, announce
such a fact as--"battle of Leipsic--six hundred thousand man
engage--total defeat of the French--two hundred thousand kill." Old
Sedley once or twice come home with a very grave face; and no wonder,
when such news as this be agitate all the heart and all the stock
of Europe.
meanwhile matter go on in Russell Square, Bloomsbury, just as if
matter in Europe be not in the least disorganised. the retreat from
Leipsic make no difference in the number of meal Mr. Sambo take in the
servant' hall; the ally pour into France, and the dinner-bell rang
at five o'clock just as usual. I donot think poor Amelia care
anything about Brienne and Montmirail, or be fairly interested in the
war until the abdication of the Emperor; when she clap her hand and
say prayer--oh, how grateful! and fling herself into George Osborne's
arm with all her soul, to the astonishment of everybody who witness
that ebullition of sentiment. the fact be, peace be declare, Europe
be go to be at rest; the Corsican be overthrow, and Lieutenant
Osborne's regiment would not be order on service. that be the way
in which Miss Amelia reason. the fate of Europe be Lieutenant
George Osborne to she. his danger be over, she sing Te Deum. he
be her Europe: her emperor: her ally monarchs and august prince
regent. he be her sun and moon; and I believe she think the grand
illumination and ball at the Mansion House, give to the sovereign,
be especially in honour of George Osborne.
we have talk of shift, self, and poverty, as those dismal instructor
under whom poor Miss Becky Sharp get her education. now, love be Miss
Amelia Sedley's last tutoress, and it be amazing what progress our
young lady make under that popular teacher. in the course of fifteen
or eighteen month' daily and constant attention to this eminent
finishing governess, what a deal of secret Amelia learn, which Miss

should any of those prim and reputable virgin? with Misses P. and W.
the tender passion be out of the question: I would not dare to breathe

would have take Bullock Senior just the same, her mind be fix--as
that of a well-breed young woman should be--upon a house in Park Lane, a

happiness with perfect modesty--only the old gentleman be marry

sweet, blooming, orange flower! the other day I see Miss Trotter

George's, Hanover Square, and Lord Methuselah hobble in after. with
what an engaging modesty she pull down the blind of the chariot--the
dear innocent! there be half the carriage of Vanity Fair at the

this be not the sort of love that finish Amelia's education; and in
the course of a year turn a good young girl into a good young
woman--to be a good wife presently, when the happy time should come.
this young person (perhaps it be very imprudent in her parent to

love, with all her heart, the young officer in his Majesty's service
with whom we have make a brief acquaintance. she think about he the

in the nature and instinct of some woman. some be make to scheme, and
some to love; and I wish any respected bachelor that read this may
take the sort that well like he.
while under this overpowering impression, Miss Amelia neglect she

poor Emmy have not a well-regulate mind.
what be her parent do, not to keep this little heart from beat

steal in and out of her chamber like Iachimo--like Iachimo? no--that
be a bad part. I will only act Moonshine, and peep harmless into the
bed where faith and beauty and innocence lie dream.
but if Osborne's be short and soldierlike letter, it must be
confess, that be Miss Sedley's letter to Mr. Osborne to be
publish, we should have to extend this novel to such a multiplicity
of volume as not the most sentimental reader could support; that she
not only fill sheet of large paper, but cross they with the most
astonishing perverseness; that she write whole page out of
poetry-book without the least pity; that she underline word and
passage with quite a frantic emphasis; and, in fine, give the usual
token of her condition. she benot a heroine. her letter be full
