verse take all sort of liberty with the metre. but oh, mesdames,
if you be not allow to touch the heart sometimes in spite of syntax,
and be not to be love until you all know the difference between
trimeter and tetrameter, may all Poetry go to the deuce, and every
schoolmaster perish miserably!
CHAPTER XIII
Sentimental and otherwise
I fear the gentleman to whom Miss Amelia's letter be address be
rather an obdurate critic. such a number of note follow Lieutenant
Osborne about the country, that he become almost ashamed of the joke
of his mess-room companion regard they, and order his servant
never to deliver they except at his private apartment. he be see
light his cigar with one, to the horror of Captain Dobbin, who, it
be my belief, would have give a bank-note for the document.
for some time George strove to keep the liaison a secret. there be a
woman in the case, that he admit. "and not the first either," say
Ensign Spooney to Ensign Stubble. "that Osborne's a devil of a fellow.
there be a judge's daughter at Demerara go almost mad about he;
then there be that beautiful quadroon girl, Miss Pye, at St.
Vincent's, you know; and since hebe be home, they say hebe a regular
Don Giovanni, by Jove."
Stubble and Spooney think that to be a "regular Don Giovanni, by
Jove" be one of the fine quality a man could possess, and
Osborne's reputation be prodigious amongst the young man of the
regiment. he be famous in field-sport, famous at a song, famous on
parade; free with his money, which be bountifully supply by his
father. his coat be well make than any man's in the regiment, and
he have more of they. he be adore by the man. he could drink more
than any officer of the whole mess, include old Heavytop, the
colonel. he could spar well than Knuckles, the private (who would
have be a corporal but for his drunkenness, and who have be in the
prize-ring); and be the good batter and bowler, out and out, of the
regimental club. he ride his own horse, Greased Lightning, and win the
Garrison cup at Quebec race. there be other people besides Amelia
who worship he. stubble and Spooney think he a sort of Apollo;
Dobbin take he to be an Admirable Crichton; and Mrs. Major O'Dowd
acknowledge he be an elegant young fellow, and put she in mind of
Fitzjurld Fogarty, Lord Castlefogarty's second son.
well, Stubble and Spooney and the rest indulge in most romantic
conjecture regard this female correspondent of Osborne's--opine
that it be a Duchess in London who be in love with he--or that it
be a General's daughter, who be engage to somebody else, and madly
attach to he--or that it be a Member of Parliament's lady, who
propose four horse and an elopement--or that it be some other victim
of a passion delightfully exciting, romantic, and disgraceful to all
party, on none of which conjecture would Osborne throw the least
light, leave his young admirer and friend to invent and arrange
their whole history.
and the real state of the case would never have be know at all in
the regiment but for Captain Dobbin's indiscretion. the Captain be
eat his breakfast one day in the mess-room, while Cackle, the
assistant-surgeon, and the two above-name worthy be speculate
upon Osborne's intrigue--Stubble hold out that the lady be a
Duchess about Queen Charlotte's court, and cackle vow she be an
opera-singer of the bad reputation. at this idea Dobbin become so
move, that though his mouth be full of egg and bread-and-butter at
the time, and though he ought not to have speak at all, yet he
couldnot help blurt out, "Cackle, yoube a stupid fool. yoube
always talk nonsense and scandal. Osborne be not go to run off
with a Duchess or ruin a milliner. Miss Sedley be one of the most
charming young woman that ever live. hebe be engage to she ever so
long; and the man who call she name have well not do so in my
hearing." with which, turn exceedingly red, Dobbin cease speak,
and almost choke himself with a cup of tea. the story be over the
regiment in half-an-hour; and that very evening Mrs. Major O'Dowd write
off to her sister Glorvina at O'Dowdstown not to hurry from
Dublin--young Osborne be prematurely engage already.
she compliment the Lieutenant in an appropriate speech over a glass
of whisky-toddy that evening, and he go home perfectly furious to
quarrel with Dobbin (who have decline Mrs. Major O'Dowd's party, and
sit in his own room play the flute, and, I believe, write poetry
in a very melancholy manner)--to quarrel with Dobbin for betray his
secret.
"who the deuce ask you to talk about my affair?" Osborne shout
indignantly. "why the devil be all the regiment to know that I be
go to be marry? why be that tattle old harridan, Peggy O'Dowd,
to make free with my name at her d--d supper-table, and advertise my
engagement over the three kingdom? after all, what right have you to
say I be engage, or to meddle in my business at all, Dobbin?"
"it seem to I," Captain Dobbin begin.
"seem be hang, Dobbin," his junior interrupt he. "I be under
obligation to you, I know it, a d--d deal too well too; but I willnot be
always sermonise by you because yoube five year my senior. Ibe
hang if Iwill stand your air of superiority and infernal pity and
patronage. pity and patronage! I should like to know in what Ibe your
inferior?"
"be you engaged?" Captain Dobbin interpose.
"what the devil's that to you or any one here if I be?"
"be you ashamed of it?" Dobbin resume.
"what right have you to ask I that question, sir? I should like to
know," George say.
"Good God, you donot mean to say you want to break off?" ask Dobbin,
start up.
"in other word, you ask I if Ibe a man of honour," say Osborne,
fiercely; "be that what you mean? youhave adopt such a tone regard
I lately that Ibe ------ if Iwill bear it any more."
"what have I do? Ihave tell you you be neglect a sweet girl,
George. Ihave tell you that when you go to town you ought to go to she,
and not to the gambling-house about St. James's."
"you want your money back, I suppose," say George, with a sneer.
"of course I do--I always do, donot I?" say Dobbin. "you speak like
a generous fellow."
"no, hang it, William, I beg your pardon"--here George interpose in a
fit of remorse; "you have be my friend in a hundred way, Heaven
know. youhave get I out of a score of scrape. when Crawley of the
Guards win that sum of money of I I should have be do but for you:
I know I should. but you shouldnot deal so hardly with I; you
shouldnot be always catechise I. I be very fond of Amelia; I adore
she, and that sort of thing. donot look angry. shebe faultless; I
know she be. but you see therebe no fun in win a thing unless you
play for it. hang it: the regimentbe just back from the West Indies, I
must have a little fling, and then when Ibe married Iwill reform; I will
upon my honour, now. and--I say--Dob--don't be angry with I, and
Iwill give you a hundred next month, when I know my father will stand
something handsome; and Iwill ask Heavytop for leave, and Iwill go to
town, and see Amelia to-morrow--there now, will that satisfy you?"
"it be impossible to be long angry with you, George," say the
good-natured Captain; "and as for the money, old boy, you know if I want
it youwould share your last shilling with I."
"that I would, by Jove, Dobbin," George say, with the great
generosity, though by the way he never have any money to spare.
"only I wish you have sow those wild oats of your, George. if you
could have see poor little Miss Emmy's face when she ask I about
you the other day, you would have pitch those billiard-ball to the
deuce. go and comfort she, you rascal. go and write she a long
letter. do something to make she happy; a very little will."
"I believe shebe d--d fond of I," the Lieutenant say, with a
self-satisfy air; and go off to finish the evening with some jolly
fellow in the mess-room.
Amelia meanwhile, in Russell Square, be look at the moon, which be
shin upon that peaceful spot, as well as upon the square of the
Chatham barrack, where Lieutenant Osborne be quarter, and think
to herself how her hero be employ. perhaps he be visit the
sentry, think she; perhaps he be bivouac; perhaps he be
attend the couch of a wound comrade, or study the art of war up
in his own desolate chamber. and her kind thought speed away as if they
be angel and have wing, and fly down the river to Chatham and
Rochester, strove to peep into the barracks where George be. . . . all
thing consider, I think it be as well the gate be shut, and the
sentry allow no one to pass; so that the poor little white-rob
angel could not hear the song those young fellow be roar over
the whisky-punch.
the day after the little conversation at Chatham barrack, young
Osborne, to show that he would be as good as his word, prepare to go
to town, thereby incur Captain Dobbin's applause. "I should have
like to make she a little present," Osborne say to his friend in
confidence, "only I be quite out of cash until my father tip up." but
Dobbin would not allow this good nature and generosity to be balk,
and so accommodate Mr. Osborne with a few pound note, which the
latter take after a little faint scruple.
and I dare say he would have buy something very handsome for Amelia;
only, get off the coach in Fleet Street, he be attract by a
handsome shirt-pin in a jeweller's window, which he could not resist;
and have pay for that, have very little money to spare for indulge
in any further exercise of kindness. never mind: you may be sure it
be not his present Amelia want. when he come to Russell Square,
her face light up as if he have be sunshine. the little care,
fear, tear, timid misgiving, sleepless fancy of I donot know how
many day and night, be forget, under one moment's influence of
that familiar, irresistible smile. he beam on she from the
drawing-room door--magnificent, with ambrosial whisker, like a god.
Sambo, whose face as he announce Captain Osbin (have confer a
brevet rank on that young officer) blaze with a sympathetic grin, see
the little girl start, and flush, and jump up from her watch-place
in the window; and Sambo retreat: and as soon as the door be shut,
she go flutter to Lieutenant George Osborne's heart as if it be
the only natural home for she to nestle in. oh, thou poor panting
little soul! the very fine tree in the whole forest, with the
straight stem, and the strong arm, and the thick foliage,
wherein you choose to build and coo, may be mark, for what you know,
and may be down with a crash ere long. what an old, old simile that
be, between man and timber!
in the meanwhile, George kiss she very kindly on her forehead and
glisten eye, and be very gracious and good; and she think his
diamond shirt-pin (which she have not know he to wear before) the
prettiest ornament ever see.
the observant reader, who have mark our young Lieutenant's previous
behaviour, and have preserve our report of the brief conversation which
he have just have with Captain Dobbin, have possibly come to certain
conclusion regard the character of Mr. Osborne. some cynical
Frenchman have say that there be two party to a love-transaction:
the one who love and the other who condescend to be so treat.
perhaps the love be occasionally on the man's side; perhaps on the
lady's. perhaps some infatuate swain have ere this mistaken
insensibility for modesty, dulness for maiden reserve, mere vacuity for
sweet bashfulness, and a goose, in a word, for a swan. perhaps some
beloved female subscriber have array an ass in the splendour and glory
of her imagination; admire his dulness as manly simplicity; worship
his selfishness as manly superiority; treat his stupidity as majestic
gravity, and use he as the brilliant fairy Titania do a certain
weaver at Athens. I think I have see such comedy of error go on
in the world. but this be certain, that Amelia believe her lover to
be one of the most gallant and brilliant man in the empire: and it be
possible Lieutenant Osborne think so too.
he be a little wild: how many young man be; and donot girl like a
rake well than a milksop? he havenot sow his wild oats as yet, but
he would soon: and quit the army now that peace be proclaim; the
corsican monster lock up at Elba; promotion by consequence over; and
no chance leave for the display of his undoubted military talent and
valour: and his allowance, with Amelia's settlement, would enable they
to take a snug place in the country somewhere, in a good sporting
neighbourhood; and he would hunt a little, and farm a little; and they
would be very happy. as for remain in the army as a married man,
that be impossible. Fancy Mrs. George Osborne in lodging in a county
town; or, bad still, in the East or West Indies, with a society of
officer, and patronize by Mrs. Major O'Dowd! Amelia die with
laugh at Osborne's story about Mrs. Major O'Dowd. he love she
much too fondly to subject she to that horrid woman and her
vulgarity, and the rough treatment of a soldier's wife. he donot
care for himself--not he; but his dear little girl should take the
place in society to which, as his wife, she be entitle: and to these
proposal you may be sure she accede, as she would to any other from
the same author.
hold this kind of conversation, and build numberless castle in
the air (which Amelia adorn with all sort of flower-garden, rustic
walk, country church, Sunday school, and the like; while George have
his mind's eye direct to the stable, the kennel, and the cellar),
this young pair pass away a couple of hour very pleasantly; and as
the Lieutenant have only that single day in town, and a great deal of
most important business to transact, it be propose that Miss Emmy
should dine with her future sister-in-law. this invitation be
accept joyfully. he conduct she to his sister; where he leave she
talk and prattle in a way that astonish those lady, who
think that George might make something of she; and he then go off
to transact his business.
in a word, he go out and eat ice at a pastry-cook's shop in Charing
Cross; try a new coat in Pall Mall; drop in at the Old
slaughter', and call for Captain Cannon; play eleven game at
billiard with the Captain, of which he win eight, and return to
Russell Square half an hour late for dinner, but in very good humour.
it be not so with old Mr. Osborne. when that gentleman come from the
City, and be welcome in the drawing-room by his daughter and the
elegant Miss Wirt, they see at once by his face--which be puffy,
solemn, and yellow at the good of time--and by the scowl and twitching
of his black eyebrow, that the heart within his large white waistcoat
be disturb and uneasy. when Amelia step forward to salute he,
which she always do with great trembling and timidity, he give a surly
grunt of recognition, and drop the little hand out of his great
hirsute paw without any attempt to hold it there. he look round
gloomily at his eld daughter; who, comprehend the meaning of his
look, which ask unmistakably, "why the devil be she here?" say at
once:
"George be in town, Papa; and have go to the Horse Guards, and will be
back to dinner."
"o he be, be he? I willnot have the dinner keep wait for he, Jane";
with which this worthy man lapse into his particular chair, and then
the utter silence in his genteel, well-furnish drawing-room be only
interrupt by the alarmed ticking of the great french clock.
when that chronometer, which be surmount by a cheerful brass group
of the sacrifice of Iphigenia, toll five in a heavy cathedral tone,
Mr. Osborne pull the bell at his right hand--violently, and the
butler rush up.
"Dinner!" roar Mr. Osborne.
"Mr. George benot come in, sir," interpose the man.
"Damn Mr. George, sir. be I master of the house? DINNER!" Mr. Osborne
scowl. Amelia tremble. a telegraphic communication of eye pass
between the other three lady. the obedient bell in the low region
begin ring the announcement of the meal. the tolling over, the head
of the family thrust his hand into the great tail-pocket of his great
blue coat with brass button, and without wait for a further
announcement stride downstairs alone, scowl over his shoulder at the
four female.
"whatbe the matter now, my dear?" ask one of the other, as they rise
and trip gingerly behind the sire. "I suppose the fund be
fall," whisper Miss Wirt; and so, tremble and in silence, this
hush female company follow their dark leader. they take their
place in silence. he growl out a blessing, which sound as gruffly
as a curse. the great silver dish-cover be remove. Amelia tremble
in her place, for she be next to the awful Osborne, and alone on her
side of the table--the gap be occasion by the absence of George.
"Soup?" say Mr. Osborne, clutch the ladle, fix his eye on she,
in a sepulchral tone; and have help she and the rest, do not speak
for a while.
"take Miss Sedley's plate away," at last he say. "she cannot eat the
soup--no more can I. itbe beastly. take away the soup, Hicks, and
to-morrow turn the cook out of the house, Jane."
have conclude his observation upon the soup, Mr. Osborne make a few
curt remark respect the fish, also of a savage and satirical
tendency, and curse Billingsgate with an emphasis quite worthy of the
place. then he lapse into silence, and swallow sundry glass of
wine, look more and more terrible, till a brisk knock at the door
tell of George's arrival when everybody begin to rally.
"he could not come before. General Daguilet have keep he wait at
the Horse Guards. never mind soup or fish. give he anything--he
donot care what. Capital mutton--capital everything." his good humour
contrast with his father's severity; and he rattle on unceasingly
during dinner, to the delight of all--of one especially, who need not
be mention.
as soon as the young lady have discuss the orange and the glass of
wine which form the ordinary conclusion of the dismal banquet at Mr.
Osborne's house, the signal to make sail for the drawing-room be
give, and they all arise and depart. Amelia hop George would soon
join they there. she begin play some of his favourite waltz (then
newly import) at the great carve-legged, leather-case grand piano
in the drawing-room overhead. this little artifice do not bring he.
he be deaf to the waltz; they grow fainter and fainter; the
discomfited performer leave the huge instrument presently; and though
her three friend perform some of the loud and most brilliant new
piece of their repertoire, she do not hear a single note, but sate
thinking, and bode evil. Old Osborne's scowl, terrific always, have
never before look so deadly to she. his eye follow she out of the
room, as if she have be guilty of something. when they bring she
coffee, she start as though it be a cup of poison which Mr. Hicks,
the butler, wish to propose to she. what mystery be there lurk?
oh, those woman! they nurse and cuddle their presentiment, and make
darling of their ugly thought, as they do of their deform
child.
the gloom on the paternal countenance have also impress George Osborne
with anxiety. with such eyebrow, and a look so decidedly bilious, how
be he to extract that money from the governor, of which George be
consumedly in want? he begin praise his father's wine. that be
generally a successful mean of cajole the old gentleman.
"we never get such Madeira in the West Indies, sir, as your. Colonel
Heavytop take off three bottle of that you send I down, under his
belt the other day."
"do he?" say the old gentleman. "it stand I in eight shilling a
bottle."
"will you take six guinea a dozen for it, sir?" say George, with a
laugh. "therebe one of the great man in the kingdom want some."
"do he?" growl the senior. "wish he may get it."
"when General Daguilet be at Chatham, sir, Heavytop give he a
breakfast, and ask I for some of the wine. the General like it
just as well--want a pipe for the Commander-in-chief. hebe his Royal
Highness's right-hand man."
"it be devilish fine wine," say the Eyebrows, and they look more
good-humour; and George be go to take advantage of this
complacency, and bring the supply question on the mahogany, when the
father, relapse into solemnity, though rather cordial in manner, bade
he ring the bell for claret. "and wewill see if thatbe as good as the
Madeira, George, to which his Royal Highness be welcome, Ibe sure. and
as we be drink it, Iwill talk to you about a matter of importance."
Amelia hear the claret bell ring as she sit nervously upstairs. she
think, somehow, it be a mysterious and presentimental bell. of the
presentiment which some people be always have, some surely must
come right.
"what I want to know, George," the old gentleman say, after slowly
smack his first bumper--"what I want to know be, how you
and--ah--that little thing upstairs, be carry on?"
"I think, sir, it be not hard to see," George say, with a
self-satisfied grin. "pretty clear, sir.--what capital wine!"
"what d'you mean, pretty clear, sir?"
"why, hang it, sir, donot push I too hard. Ibe a modest man.
I--ah--I donot set up to be a lady-killer; but I do own that shebe as
devilish fond of I as she can be. anybody can see that with half an
eye."
"and you yourself?"
"why, sir, donot you order I to marry she, and benot I a good boy?
havenot our papa settle it ever so long?"
"a pretty boy, indeed. havenot I hear of your doing, sir, with Lord
Tarquin, Captain Crawley of the Guards, the honourable Mr. Deuceace and
that set. have a care sir, have a care."
the old gentleman pronounce these aristocratic name with the great
gusto. whenever he meet a great man he grovel before he, and
my-lord he as only a free-bear Briton can do. he come home and
look out his history in the peerage: he introduce his name into his
daily conversation; he brag about his Lordship to his daughter. he
fall down prostrate and bask in he as a Neapolitan beggar do in
the sun. George be alarm when he hear the name. he fear his
father might have be inform of certain transaction at play. but
the old moralist ease he by say serenely:
"well, well, young man will be young man. and the comfort to I be,
George, that live in the good society in England, as I hope you do;
as I think you do; as my mean will allow you to do--"
"thank you, sir," say George, make his point at once. "one cannot
live with these great folk for nothing; and my purse, sir, look at
it"; and he hold up a little token which have be net by Amelia, and
contain the very last of Dobbin's pound note.
"you shallnot want, sir. the british merchant's son shallnot want, sir. my
guineas be as good as theirs, George, my boy; and I donot grudge them.
call on Mr. Chopper as you go through the City to-morrow; hewill have
something for you. I donot grudge money when I know yoube in good
society, because I know that good society can never go wrong. therebe
no pride in I. I be a humbly bear man--but you have have advantage.
make a good use of them. mix with the young nobility. therebe many of
them who cannot spend a dollar to your guinea, my boy. and as for the
pink bonnet (here from under the heavy eyebrow there come a knowing
and not very pleasing leer)--why boy will be boy. only therebe one
thing I order you to avoid, which, if you do not, Iwill cut you off with
a shilling, by Jove; and thatbe gamble."
"oh, of course, sir," say George.
"but to return to the other business about Amelia: why shouldnot you
marry high than a stockbroker's daughter, George--thatbe what I want
to know?"
"itbe a family business, sir,".says George, crack filbert. "you
and Mr. Sedley make the match a hundred year ago."
"I donot deny it; but people's position alter, sir. I donot deny that
Sedley make my fortune, or rather put I in the way of acquire, by my
own talent and genius, that proud position, which, I may say, I occupy
in the tallow trade and the City of London. Ihave show my gratitude to
Sedley; and hebe try it of late, sir, as my cheque-book can show.
George! I tell you in confidence I donot like the look of Mr.
Sedley's affair. my chief clerk, Mr. Chopper, do not like the look
of them, and hebe an old file, and know 'change as well as any man in
London. Hulker & Bullock be look shy at he. hebe be dabble
on his own account I fear. they say the Jeune Amelie be his, which be
take by the Yankee privateer molasse. and thatbe flat--unless I see
Amelia's ten thousand down you donot marry she. Iwill have no lame
duck's daughter in my family. pass the wine, sir--or ring for coffee."
with which Mr. Osborne spread out the evening paper, and George know
from this signal that the colloquy be end, and that his papa be
about to take a nap.
he hurry upstairs to Amelia in the high spirit. what be it that
make he more attentive to she on that night than he have be for a
long time--more eager to amuse she, more tender, more brilliant in
talk? be it that his generous heart warm to she at the prospect of
misfortune; or that the idea of lose the dear little prize make he
value it more?
she live upon the recollection of that happy evening for many day
afterwards, remember his word; his look; the song he sing; his
attitude, as he leant over she or look at she from a distance. as it
seem to she, no night ever pass so quickly at Mr. Osborne's house
before; and for once this young person be almost provoke to be angry
by the premature arrival of Mr. Sambo with her shawl.
George come and take a tender leave of she the next morning; and then
hurry off to the City, where he visit Mr. Chopper, his father's
head man, and receive from that gentleman a document which he
exchange at Hulker & Bullock's for a whole pocketful of money. as
George enter the house, old John Sedley be pass out of the
banker's parlour, look very dismal. but his godson be much too
elated to mark the worthy stockbroker's depression, or the dreary eye
which the kind old gentleman cast upon he. Young Bullock do not come
grin out of the parlour with he as have be his willnot in former
year.
and as the swing door of Hulker, Bullock & Co. close upon Mr.
Sedley, Mr. Quill, the cashier (whose benevolent occupation it be to
hand out crisp bank-note from a drawer and dispense sovereign out of
a copper shovel), wink at Mr. Driver, the clerk at the desk on his
right. Mr. Driver wink again.
"no go," Mr. D. whisper.
"not at no price," Mr. Q. say. "Mr. George Osborne, sir, how will
you take it?" George cram eagerly a quantity of note into his
pocket, and pay Dobbin fifty pound that very evening at mess.
that very evening Amelia write he the tender of long letter. her
heart be overflow with tenderness, but it still forebode evil.
what be the cause of Mr. Osborne's dark look? she ask. have any
difference arise between he and her papa? her poor papa return so
melancholy from the City, that all be alarmed about he at home--in
fine, there be four page of love and fear and hope and
foreboding.
"poor little Emmy--dear little Emmy. how fond she be of I," George
say, as he peruse the missive--"and Gad, what a headache that mix
punch have give I!" poor little Emmy, indeed.
CHAPTER XIV
Miss Crawley at home
about this time there drive up to an exceedingly snug and
well-appoint house in Park Lane, a travel chariot with a lozenge on
the panel, a discontent female in a green veil and crimp curl on
the rumble, and a large and confidential man on the box. it be the
equipage of our friend Miss Crawley, return from hant. the
carriage window be shut; the fat spaniel, whose head and tongue
ordinarily loll out of one of they, repose on the lap of the
discontent female. when the vehicle stop, a large round bundle of
shawl be take out of the carriage by the aid of various domestic
and a young lady who accompany the heap of cloak. that bundle
contain Miss Crawley, who be convey upstairs forthwith, and put
into a bed and chamber warm properly as for the reception of an
invalid. messenger go off for her physician and medical man. they
come, consult, prescribe, vanish. the young companion of Miss
Crawley, at the conclusion of their interview, come in to receive their
instruction, and administer those antiphlogistic medicine which the
eminent man order.
Captain Crawley of the Life Guards ride up from Knightsbridge Barracks
the next day; his black charger paw the straw before his invalid
aunt's door. he be most affectionate in his inquiry regard that
amiable relative. there seem to be much source of apprehension. he
find Miss Crawley's maid (the discontent female) unusually sulky and
despondent; he find Miss Briggs, her dame de compagnie, in tear alone
in the drawing-room. she have hasten home, hear of her beloved
friend's illness. she wish to fly to her couch, that couch which
she, Briggs, have so often smooth in the hour of sickness. she be
deny admission to Miss Crawley's apartment. a stranger be
administer her medicine--a stranger from the country--an odious
Miss ... --tears choke the utterance of the dame de compagnie, and
she bury her crush affection and her poor old red nose in her
pocket handkerchief.
Rawdon Crawley send up his name by the sulky femme de chambre, and Miss
Crawley's new companion, come trip down from the sick-room, put
a little hand into his as he step forward eagerly to meet she, give
a glance of great scorn at the bewildered Briggs, and beckon the
young Guardsman out of the back drawing-room, lead he downstairs into
that now desolate dining-parlour, where so many a good dinner have be
celebrate.
here these two talk for ten minute, discuss, no doubt, the
symptom of the old invalid above stair; at the end of which period
the parlour bell be ring briskly, and answer on that instant by Mr.
Bowls, Miss Crawley's large confidential butler (who, indeed, happen
to be at the keyhole during the most part of the interview); and the
Captain come out, curl his mustachio, mount the black charger
paw among the straw, to the admiration of the little blackguard boy
collect in the street. he look in at the dining-room window,
manage his horse, which curvet and caper beautifully--for one
instant the young person might be see at the window, when her figure
vanish, and, doubtless, she go upstairs again to resume the
affect duty of benevolence.
who could this young woman be, I wonder? that evening a little dinner
for two person be lay in the dining-room--when Mrs. Firkin, the
lady's maid, push into her mistress's apartment, and bustle about
there during the vacancy occasion by the departure of the new
nurse--and the latter and Miss Briggs sit down to the neat little meal.
Briggs be so much choke by emotion that she could hardly take a
morsel of meat. the young person carve a fowl with the utmost
delicacy, and ask so distinctly for egg-sauce, that poor Briggs,
before whom that delicious condiment be place, start, make a great
clattering with the ladle, and once more fall back in the most gush
hysterical state.
"have you not well give Miss Briggs a glass of wine?" say the person
to Mr. Bowls, the large confidential man. he do so. Briggs seize it
mechanically, gasp it down convulsively, moan a little, and begin
to play with the chicken on her plate.
"I think we shall be able to help each other," say the person with
great suavity: "and shall have no need of Mr. Bowls's kind service.
Mr. Bowls, if you please, we will ring when we want you." he go
downstairs, where, by the way, he vent the most horrid curse upon
the unoffending footman, his subordinate.
"it be a pity you take on so, Miss Briggs," the young lady say, with a
cool, slightly sarcastic, air.
"my dear friend be so ill, and wo-o-on't see I," gurgle out Briggs
in an agony of renew grief.
"shebe not very ill any more. console yourself, dear Miss Briggs. she
have only overeaten herself--that be all. she be greatly well. she
will soon be quite restore again. she be weak from be cupped and
from medical treatment, but she will rally immediately. pray console
yourself, and take a little more wine."
"but why, why willnot she see I again?" Miss Briggs bleat out. "oh,
Matilda, Matilda, after three-and-twenty year' tenderness! be this the
return to your poor, poor Arabella?"
"donot cry too much, poor arabella," the other say (with ever so
little of a grin); "she only willnot see you, because she say you donot
nurse she as well as I do. itbe no pleasure to I to sit up all night.
I wish you might do it instead."
"have I not tend that dear couch for year?" Arabella say, "and
now--"
"now she prefer somebody else. well, sick people have these fancy,
and must be humour. when shebe well I shall go."
"never, never," Arabella exclaim, madly inhale her salt-bottle.
"never be well or never go, Miss Briggs?" the other say, with the same
provoke good-nature. "Pooh--she will be well in a fortnight, when I
shall go back to my little pupil at Queen's Crawley, and to their
mother, who be a great deal more sick than our friend. you need not be
jealous about I, my dear Miss Briggs. I be a poor little girl without
any friend, or any harm in I. I donot want to supplant you in Miss
Crawley's good grace. she will forget I a week after I be go: and
her affection for you have be the work of year. give I a little
wine if you please, my dear Miss Briggs, and let we be friend. Ibe
sure I want friend."
the placable and soft-hearted Briggs speechlessly push out her hand
at this appeal; but she feel the desertion most keenly for all that,
and bitterly, bitterly moan the fickleness of her Matilda. at the end
of half an hour, the meal over, Miss Rebecca Sharp (for such,
astonishing to state, be the name of she who have be describe
ingeniously as "the person" hitherto), go upstairs again to her
patient's room, from which, with the most engaging politeness, she
eliminate poor Firkin. "thank you, Mrs. Firkin, that will quite do;
how nicely you make it! I will ring when anything be want." "thank
you"; and Firkin come downstairs in a tempest of jealousy, only the
more dangerous because she be force to confine it in her own bosom.
could it be the temp which, as she pass the landing of the first

the hand of Briggs. Briggs have be on the watch. Briggs too well hear
the creak Firkin descend the stair, and the clink of the spoon and
gruel-basin the neglect female carry.
"well, Firkin?" say she, as the other enter the apartment. "well,
Jane?"
"wuss and wuss, Miss B.," Firkin say, wag her head.
"be she not well then?"
"she never speak but once, and I ask she if she feel a little more
easy, and she tell I to hold my stupid tongue. oh, Miss B., I never
think to have see this day!" and the water-work again begin to
play.
"what sort of a person be this Miss Sharp, Firkin? I little think,

dear Matilda!" Miss Briggs, it will be see by her language, be of
a literary and sentimental turn, and have once publish a volume of
poems--"trill of the Nightingale"--by subscription.

happy out of her sight. the Capting quite wild about she. Mr. Crawley
mortial jealous. since Miss C. be take ill, she willnot have nobody near
she but Miss Sharp, I cannot tell for where nor for why; and I think
somethink have bewidge everybody."
Rebecca pass that night in constant watching upon Miss Crawley; the

manner of use the handkerchief, be so completely render that Miss
Crawley become quite cheerful, to the admiration of the doctor when
they visit she, who usually find this worthy woman of the world,
when the least sickness attack she, under the most abject depression
and terror of death.
Captain Crawley come every day, and receive bulletin from Miss
Rebecca respect his aunt's health. this improve so rapidly, that

heart may imagine the smothered emotion of that sentimental female,
and the affect nature of the interview.
Miss Crawley like to have Briggs in a good deal soon. rebecca use to
mimic she to her face with the most admirable gravity, thereby
render the imitation doubly piquant to her worthy patroness.
the cause which have lead to the deplorable illness of Miss Crawley, and
her departure from her brother's house in the country, be of such an
unromantic nature that they be hardly fit to be explain in this
genteel and sentimental novel. for how be it possible to hint of a
delicate female, live in good society, that she eat and drink too

but a good doctor from Southampton be call in in time, vanquish

while everybody be attend on Miss Crawley, and messenger every

and this be the lady of Crawley herself. the good doctor shake his

Captain Rawdon get an extension of leave on his aunt's illness, and

lie sick in the state bedroom, into which you enter by the little
blue saloon.) his father be always meet he there; or if he come

one to watch the other so? a generous rivalry, no doubt, as to which

at dinner--to which meal she descend for half an hour--she keep the

she never tell until long afterwards how painful that duty be; how

what horror of death; during what long night she lie moaning, and in
almost delirious agony respect that future world which she quite
ignore when she be in good health.--picture to yourself, oh fair
young reader, a worldly, selfish, graceless, thankless, religionless
old woman, writhe in pain and fear, and without her wig. picture she
to yourself, and ere you be old, learn to love and pray!
Sharp watch this graceless bedside with indomitable patience. nothing

everything. she tell many a good story about Miss Crawley's illness in
after day--story which make the lady blush through her artificial

alert; she sleep light, have a perfectly clear conscience; and could
take that refreshment at almost any minute's warning. and so you see
very few trace of fatigue in her appearance. her face might be a
trifle paler, and the circle round her eye a little blacker than
usual; but whenever she come out from the sick-room she be always
smile, fresh, and neat, and look as trim in her little
dressing-gown and cap, as in her smart evening suit.
the Captain think so, and rave about she in uncouth convulsion. the
barb shaft of love have penetrate his dull hide. six

back to his regiment and naughty London, and not play with a poor
artless girl's feeling.
many and many a time this good-natured lady, compassionate the

see. when man of a certain sort, lady, be in love, though they see

"mark my word, Rawdon," she say. "you will have Miss Sharp one day

"what relation--my cousin, hey, Mrs. Bute? James sweet on she, hey?"

"More than that," Mrs. Bute say, with a flash from her black eye.
"not Pitt? he sha'n't have she. the sneak a'n't worthy of she. hebe
book to Lady Jane Sheepshanks."
"you man perceive nothing. you silly, blind creature--if anything

Rawdon Crawley, Esquire, give vent to a prodigious whistle, in token of

when he see Rebecca alone, he rally she about his father's attachment

"well, suppose he be fond of I. I know he be, and other too. you

she be go. she drive out with Miss Crawley that day. it be before

skirmish of this sort pass perpetually during the little

if the Baronet of Queen's Crawley have not have the fear of lose his

Miss Briggs be not formally dismiss, but her place as companion be

useful. gratitude among certain rich folk be scarcely natural or to
be think of. they take needy people's service as their due. nor
have you, o poor parasite and humble hanger-on, much reason to
complain! your friendship for Dives be about as sincere as the return
which it usually get. it be money you love, and not the man; and be
Croesus and his footman to change place you know, you poor rogue, who
would have the benefit of your allegiance.
and I be not sure that, in spite of Rebecca's simplicity and activity,
and gentleness and untire good humour, the shrewd old London lady,

it must have often cross Miss Crawley's mind that nobody do

Captain Rawdon Crawley burst into a horse-laugh by way of reply; and

"and very pretty company too, I dare say."
"quite right, Miss Sharp. right, as usual, Miss Sharp. Uncommon pretty

Captain Crawley look savagely at the Lieutenant out of his little

how be those young ladies?--not that I ought to ask."
"why not?" Mr. Osborne say, amazed.

Osborne be quite savage. the little governess patronise he and

"I think you like the City family pretty well," he say, haughtily.

of course I do. donot every girl like to come home for the

"it seem to I you donot dislike that wonderful Mr. Joseph last

thus be George utterly rout. not that Rebecca be in the right; but

he tell Amelia of what he have do, and how he have counsel Rawdon

against that little sly, scheme Rebecca.

"your friend the governess.--Don't look so astonished."

for as Rebecca be shawle she in an upper apartment, where these two

Rebecca kiss she.
and regard this delightful secret, not one syllable more be say by

some short period after the above event, and Miss Rebecca Sharp still

Mr. Crawley have tend that otherwise friendless bedside. she go out

every day in Vanity Fair.
when the demise take place, her husband be in London attend to some

the news of Lady Crawley's death provoke no more grief or comment than

on the morrow, as Rebecca be gaze from the window, she startle Miss

follow this announcement.
"my dear, I cannot see he. I willnot see he. tell Bowls not at home,

"I wawnt you back at Queen's Crawley, Miss," the baronet say, fix

house all go wrong. itbe not the same place. all my account have
get muddle agin. you must come back. do come back. dear Becky, do
come."
"come--as what, sir?" Rebecca gasp out.

your vit vor't. birth be hang. yoube as good a lady as ever I see.
youhave get more brain in your little vinger than any baronet's wife in
the county. will you come? yes or no?"
"oh, Sir Pitt!" rebecca say, very much move.
"say yes, Becky," Sir Pitt continue. "Ibe an old man, but a good'n.
Ibe good for twenty year. Iwill make you happy, zee if I donot. you
shall do what you like; spend what you like; and 'ave it all your own
way. Iwill make you a zettlement. Iwill do everything reglar. look
year!" and the old man fall down on his knee and leer at she like a
satyr.
Rebecca start back a picture of consternation. in the course of this

now, and weep some of the most genuine tear that ever fall from her
eye.
"oh, Sir Pitt!" she say. "oh, sir--i--i'm marry already."
CHAPTER XV
in which Rebecca's Husband appear for a Short Time
every reader of a sentimental turn (and we desire no other) must have
be pleased with the tableau with which the last act of our little
drama conclude; for what can be pretty than an image of Love on his
knee before Beauty?
but when Love hear that awful confession from Beauty that she be
marry already, he bounce up from his attitude of humility on the

yoube joke," the Baronet cry, after the first explosion of rage

you without a shilling to your vortune?"

"generosity be hang!" Sir Pitt roar out. "who be it tu, then,
yoube married? where be it?"
"let I come back with you to the country, sir! let I watch over you

when--when the door open, and Miss Crawley sail in.
Mrs. Firkin and Miss Briggs, who happen by chance to be at the

"it be the lady on the ground, and not the gentleman," Miss Crawley

"Ees," say the Baronet, "I do."

put her handkerchief to her eye, and nod away honest Briggs,

think you be the private feeling of Miss, no (beg her pardon) of

understanding with the omniscience of the novelist all the gentle pain

well, then, in the first place, Rebecca give way to some very sincere
and touch regret that a piece of marvellous good fortune should

natural emotion every properly regulated mind will certainly share.
what good mother be there that would not commiserate a penniless
spinster, who might have be my lady, and have share four thousand a

Becky's disappointment deserve and will command every sympathy.
I remember one night be in the fair myself, at an evening party. I
observe old Miss Toady there also present, single out for her special
attention and flattery little Mrs. Briefless, the barrister's wife,
who be of a good family certainly, but, as we all know, be as poor as
poor can be.
what, I ask in my own mind, can cause this obsequiousness on the part
of Miss Toady; have Briefless get a county court, or have his wife have a
fortune leave she? Miss Toady explain presently, with that simplicity
which distinguish all her conduct. "you know," she say, "Mrs

Cheltenham that he cannot last six month. Mrs. Briefless's papa
succeed; so you see she will be a baronet's daughter." and Toady ask
Briefless and his wife to dinner the very next week.
if the mere chance of become a baronet's daughter can procure a lady
such homage in the world, surely, surely we may respect the agony of
a young woman who have lose the opportunity of become a baronet's

carriage in London, and a box at the opera; and I would have be
present next season. all this might have be; and now--now all be
doubt and mystery.
but Rebecca be a young lady of too much resolution and energy of
character to permit herself much useless and unseemly sorrow for the
irrevocable past; so, have devote only the proper portion of regret
to it, she wisely turn she whole attention towards the future, which
be now vastly more important to she. and she survey her position,
and its hope, doubt, and chance.
in the first place, she be married--that be a great fact. Sir Pitt
know it. she be not so much surprised into the avowal, as induce to

come, or fly and avoid it until its first fury be blow over. in this

Dearest Friend,
the great crisis which we have debate about so often be COME. half of
my secret be know, and I have think and think, until I be quite
sure that now be the time to reveal the whole of the mystery. Sir Pitt
come to I this morning, and make--what do you think?--A DECLARATION in
FORM. think of that! poor little I. I might have be Lady Crawley.
how pleased Mrs. Bute would have be: and ma tante if I have take
precedence of she! I might have be somebody's mamma, instead of--o, I
tremble, I tremble, when I think how soon we must tell all!
Sir Pitt know I be married, and not know to whom, be not very much
displease as yet. Ma tante be ACTUALLY ANGRY that I should have
refuse he. but she be all kindness and graciousness. she
condescend to say I would have make he a good wife; and vow that she
will be a mother to your little rebecca. she will be shake when she
first hear the news. but need we fear anything beyond a momentary
anger? I think not: I be sure not. she dot upon you so (you
naughty, good-for-nothing man), that she would pardon you ANYTHING:
and, indeed, I believe, the next place in her heart be mine: and that
she would be miserable without I. dearest! something tell ME we shall
conquer. you shall leave that odious regiment: quit gaming, racing,
and be a good boy; and we shall all live in Park Lane, and ma tante
shall leave we all her money.
I shall try and walk to-morrow at 3 in the usual place. if Miss B.
accompany I, you must come to dinner, and bring an answer, and put
it in the third volume of Porteus's sermon. but, at all event, come
to your own
R.
to Miss Eliza Styles, at Mr. Barnet's, Saddler, Knightsbridge.
and I trust there be no reader of this little story who have not
discernment enough to perceive that the Miss Eliza Styles (an old
schoolfellow, Rebecca say, with whom she have resume an active
correspondence of late, and who use to fetch these letter from the
saddler's), wear brass spur, and large curl mustachio, and be
indeed no other than Captain Rawdon Crawley.
CHAPTER XVI
the Letter on the Pincushion
how they be married be not of the slight consequence to anybody.
what be to hinder a Captain who be a major, and a young lady who be of
age, from purchase a licence, and unite themselves at any church in
this town? who need to be tell, that if a woman have a will she will
assuredly find a way?--My belief be that one day, when Miss Sharp have
go to pass the forenoon with her dear friend Miss Amelia Sedley in
Russell Square, a lady very like she might have be see enter a
church in the City, in company with a gentleman with dye mustachio,
who, after a quarter of an hour's interval, escort she back to the
hackney-coach in waiting, and that this be a quiet bridal party.
and who on earth, after the daily experience we have, can question the
probability of a gentleman marry anybody? how many of the wise and
learn have marry their cook? do not Lord Eldon himself, the most
prudent of man, make a runaway match? be not Achilles and Ajax both
in love with their servant maid? and be we to expect a heavy dragoon
with strong desire and small brain, who have never control a
passion in his life, to become prudent all of a sudden, and to refuse
to pay any price for an indulgence to which he have a mind? if people
only make prudent marriage, what a stop to population there would be!
it seem to I, for my part, that Mr. Rawdon's marriage be one of the
honest action which we shall have to record in any portion of that
gentleman's biography which have to do with the present history. no one
will say it be unmanly to be captivate by a woman, or, be
captivate, to marry she; and the admiration, the delight, the passion,
the wonder, the unbounded confidence, and frantic adoration with which,
by degree, this big warrior get to regard the little rebecca, be
feeling which the lady at least will pronounce be not altogether
discreditable to he. when she sing, every note thrill in his dull
soul, and tingle through his huge frame. when she speak, he bring
all the force of his brain to listen and wonder. if she be jocular,
he use to revolve her joke in his mind, and explode over they half an
hour afterwards in the street, to the surprise of the groom in the
tilbury by his side, or the comrade rid with he in Rotten row. her
word be oracle to he, her small action mark by an infallible
grace and wisdom. "how she sings,--how she paint," think he. "how
she ride that kick mare at Queen's Crawley!" and he would say to
she in confidential moment, "by Jove, Beck, yoube fit to be
Commander-in-Chief, or Archbishop of Canterbury, by Jove." be his
case a rare one? and donot we see every day in the world many an honest
Hercules at the apron-string of Omphale, and great whisker samson
prostrate in Delilah's lap?
when, then, Becky tell he that the great crisis be near, and the time
for action have arrive, Rawdon express himself as ready to act under
her order, as he would be to charge with his troop at the command of
his colonel. there be no need for he to put his letter into the
third volume of Porteus. rebecca easily find a means to get rid of
Briggs, her companion, and meet her faithful friend in "the usual place"
on the next day. she have think over matter at night, and
communicate to rawdon the result of her determination. he agree, of
course, to everything; be quite sure that it be all right: that what
she propose be good; that Miss Crawley would infallibly relent, or
"come round," as he say, after a time. have Rebecca's resolution be
entirely different, he would have follow they as implicitly. "you
have head enough for both of we, Beck," say he. "yoube sure to get
we out of the scrape. I never see your equal, and Ihave meet with some
clipper in my time too." and with this simple confession of faith, the
love-stricken dragoon leave she to execute his part of the project which
she have form for the pair.
it consist simply in the hiring of quiet lodging at Brompton, or in
the neighbourhood of the barrack, for Captain and Mrs. Crawley. for
Rebecca have determine, and very prudently, we think, to fly. Rawdon
be only too happy at her resolve; he have be entreat she to take
this measure any time for week past. he prance off to engage the
lodging with all the impetuosity of love. he agree to pay two
guineas a week so readily, that the landlady regret she have ask
he so little. he order in a piano, and half a nursery-house full of
flower: and a heap of good thing. as for shawl, kid glove, silk
stocking, gold french watch, bracelet and perfumery, he send they
in with the profusion of blind love and unbounded credit. and have
relieve his mind by this outpouring of generosity, he go and din
nervously at the club, wait until the great moment of his life
should come.
the occurrence of the previous day; the admirable conduct of
Rebecca in refuse an offer so advantageous to she, the secret
unhappiness preying upon she, the sweetness and silence with which she
bear her affliction, make Miss Crawley much more tender than usual. an
event of this nature, a marriage, or a refusal, or a proposal, thrill
through a whole household of woman, and set all their hysterical
sympathy at work. as an observer of human nature, I regularly
frequent St. George's, Hanover Square, during the genteel marriage
season; and though I have never see the bridegroom's male friend give
way to tear, or the beadle and officiating clergy any way affected,
yet it be not at all uncommon to see woman who be not in the least
concerned in the operation go on--old lady who be long past
marrying, stout middle-aged female with plenty of son and daughter,
let alone pretty young creature in pink bonnet, who be on their
promotion, and may naturally take an interest in the ceremony--I say it
be quite common to see the woman present piping, sobbing, sniffle;
hide their little face in their little useless pocket-handkerchief;
and heaving, old and young, with emotion. when my friend, the
fashionable John Pimlico, marry the lovely Lady Belgravia Green
Parker, the excitement be so general that even the little snuffy old
pew-opener who let I into the seat be in tear. and wherefore? I
inquire of my own soul: she be not go to be marry.
Miss Crawley and Briggs in a word, after the affair of Sir Pitt,
indulge in the utmost luxury of sentiment, and Rebecca become an
object of the most tender interest to they. in her absence Miss
Crawley solace herself with the most sentimental of the novel in her
library. Little Sharp, with her secret grief, be the heroine of the
day.
that night Rebecca sing more sweetly and talk more pleasantly than
she have ever be hear to do in Park Lane. she twin herself round
the heart of Miss Crawley. she speak lightly and laughingly of Sir
Pitt's proposal, ridicule it as the foolish fancy of an old man; and
her eye fill with tear, and Briggs's heart with unutterable pang
of defeat, as she say she desire no other lot than to remain for ever
with her dear benefactress. "my dear little creature," the old lady
say, "I donot intend to let you stir for year, that you may depend
upon it. as for go back to that odious brother of mine after what
have pass, it be out of the question. here you stay with I and
Briggs. Briggs want to go to see her relation very often. Briggs,
you may go when you like. but as for you, my dear, you must stay and
take care of the old woman."
if Rawdon Crawley have be then and there present, instead of be at
the club nervously drink claret, the pair might have go down on
their knee before the old spinster, avow all, and be forgive in a
twinkling. but that good chance be deny to the young couple,
doubtless in order that this story might be write, in which number
of their wonderful adventure be narrate--adventure which could
never have occur to they if they have be house and shelter under
the comfortable uninteresting forgiveness of Miss Crawley.
under Mrs. Firkin's order, in the Park Lane establishment, be a young
woman from Hampshire, whose business it be, among other duty, to
knock at Miss Sharp's door with that jug of hot water which Firkin
would rather have perish than have present to the intruder. this
girl, breed on the family estate, have a brother in Captain Crawley's
troop, and if the truth be know, I daresay it would come out that
she be aware of certain arrangement, which have a great deal to do
with this history. at any rate she purchase a yellow shawl, a pair of
green boot, and a light blue hat with a red feather with three guinea
which Rebecca give she, and as little Sharp be by no mean too liberal
with her money, no doubt it be for service render that Betty Martin
be so bribe.
on the second day after Sir Pitt Crawley's offer to Miss Sharp, the sun
rise as usual, and at the usual hour Betty Martin, the upstairs maid,
knock at the door of the governess's bedchamber.
no answer be return, and she knock again. silence be still
uninterrupted; and Betty, with the hot water, open the door and
enter the chamber.
the little white dimity bed be as smooth and trim as on the day
previous, when Betty's own hand have help to make it. two little
trunk be cord in one end of the room; and on the table before the
window--on the pincushion the great fat pincushion line with pink
inside, and twill like a lady's nightcap--lie a letter. it have be
repose there probably all night.
Betty advance towards it on tiptoe, as if she be afraid to awake
it--look at it, and round the room, with an air of great wonder and
satisfaction; take up the letter, and grin intensely as she turn
it round and over, and finally carry it into Miss Briggs's room below.
how could Betty tell that the letter be for Miss Briggs, I should like
to know? all the schooling Betty have have be at Mrs. Bute Crawley's
Sunday school, and she could no more read writing than Hebrew.
"La, Miss Briggs," the girl exclaim, "o, Miss, something must have
happen--therebe nobody in Miss Sharp's room; the bed benot be slep
in, and she've run away, and leave this letter for you, Miss."
"what!" cry Briggs, drop her comb, the thin wisp of fade hair
fall over her shoulder; "an elopement! Miss Sharp a fugitive! what,
what be this?" and she eagerly break the neat seal, and, as they say,
"devour the content" of the letter address to she.
Dear Miss Briggs [the refugee write], the kind heart in the world,
as yours be, will pity and sympathise with I and excuse I. with
tear, and prayer, and blessing, I leave the home where the poor
orphan have ever meet with kindness and affection. claim even superior
to those of my benefactress call I hence. I go to my duty--to my
husband. yes, I be married. my husband command I to seek the humble
HOME which we call ours. dearest Miss Briggs, break the news as your
delicate sympathy will know how to do it--to my dear, my beloved friend
and benefactress. tell she, ere I go, I shed tear on her dear
pillow--that pillow that I have so often soothe in sickness--that I
long again to watch--oh, with what joy shall I return to dear Park
Lane! how I tremble for the answer which be to seal MY FATE! when Sir
Pitt deign to offer I his hand, an honour of which my beloved Miss
Crawley say I be deserve (my blessing go with she for judge the
poor orphan worthy to be her SISTER!) I tell Sir Pitt that I be
already a wife. even he forgive I. but my courage fail I, when I
should have tell he all--that I could not be his wife, for I be his
daughter! I be wed to the good and most generous of man--Miss
Crawley's Rawdon be MY Rawdon. at his command I open my lip, and
follow he to our humble home, as I would through the world. o, my
excellent and kind friend, intercede with my Rawdon's beloved aunt for
he and the poor girl to whom all his NOBLE race have show such
unparalleled AFFECTION. ask Miss Crawley to receive she child. I
can say no more, but blessing, blessing on all in the dear house I
leave, pray
your affectionate and GRATEFUL Rebecca Crawley. midnight.
just as Briggs have finish read this affecting and interesting
document, which reinstate she in her position as first confidante of
Miss Crawley, Mrs. Firkin enter the room. "herebe Mrs. Bute Crawley
just arrive by the mail from Hampshire, and want some tea; will you
come down and make breakfast, Miss?"
and to the surprise of Firkin, clasp her dressing-gown around she,
the wisp of hair float dishevel behind she, the little

news.
"oh, Mrs. Firkin," gasp Betty, "sech a business. Miss Sharp have a
go and run away with the Capting, and theybe off to Gretney Green!"
we would devote a chapter to describe the emotion of Mrs. Firkin, do
not the passion of her mistress occupy our genteeler muse.
when Mrs. Bute Crawley, numb with midnight travelling, and warm

conspirator below agree that they would spare the dear lady's
feeling until she appear in her drawing-room: meanwhile it be

which would not have cause any extreme delight at another period, be

gossip with her sister-in-law regard the late Lady Crawley, the

it be not until the old lady be fairly ensconce in her usual

advisable to submit she to the operation. who have not admire the
artifice and delicate approach with which woman "prepare" their
friend for bad news? Miss Crawley's two friend make such an
apparatus of mystery before they break the intelligence to she, that
they work she up to the necessary degree of doubt and alarm.
"and she refuse Sir Pitt, my dear, dear Miss Crawley, prepare yourself
for it," Mrs. Bute say, "because--because she couldnot help herself."
"of course there be a reason," Miss Crawley answer. "she like
somebody else. I tell Briggs so yesterday."
"likes somebody else!" Briggs gasp. "o my dear friend, she be
married already."
"marry already," Mrs. Bute chime in; and both sate with clasp
hand look from each other at their victim.
"send she to I, the instant she come in. the little sly wretch: how
dare she not tell I?" cry out Miss Crawley.
"she willnot come in soon. prepare yourself, dear friend--shebe go out
for a long time--she's--shebe go altogether."
"gracious goodness, and whobe to make my chocolate? send for she and
have she back; I desire that she come back," the old lady say.
"she decamp last night, Ma'am," cry Mrs. Bute.
"she leave a letter for I," Briggs exclaim. "shebe married to--"
"prepare she, for heaven's sake. donot torture she, my dear Miss
Briggs."
"shebe married to whom?" cry the spinster in a nervous fury.
"to--to a relation of--"
"she refuse Sir Pitt," cry the victim. "speak at once. donot drive
I mad."
"o Ma'am--prepare she, Miss Briggs--shebe married to Rawdon Crawley."
"Rawdon married Rebecca--governess--nobod-- get out of my house, you
fool, you idiot--you stupid old Briggs--how dare you? yoube in the
plot--you make he marry, think that Iwould leave my money from he--you
do, Martha," the poor old lady scream in hysteric sentence.
"I, Ma'am, ask a member of this family to marry a drawing-master's
daughter?"
"her mother be a Montmorency," cry out the old lady, pull at the
bell with all her might.
"her mother be an opera girl, and she have be on the stage or bad
herself," say Mrs. Bute.
Miss Crawley give a final scream, and fall back in a faint. they be
force to take she back to the room which she have just quit. one fit
of hysteric succeed another. the doctor be send for--the
apothecary arrive. Mrs. Bute take up the post of nurse by her bedside.
"her relation ought to be round about she," that amiable woman say.
she have scarcely be carry up to her room, when a new person arrive
to whom it be also necessary to break the news. this be Sir Pitt.
"wherebe Becky?" he say, come in. "wherebe her trap? shebe come
with I to Queen's Crawley."
"have you not hear the astonishing intelligence regard her
surreptitious union?" Briggs ask.
"whatbe that to I?" Sir Pitt ask. "I know shebe married. that
make no odd. tell she to come down at once, and not keep I."
"be you not aware, sir," Miss Briggs ask, "that she have leave our
roof, to the dismay of Miss Crawley, who be nearly kill by the
intelligence of Captain Rawdon's union with she?"
when Sir Pitt Crawley hear that Rebecca be married to his son, he
break out into a fury of language, which it would do no good to repeat
in this place, as indeed it send poor Briggs shudder out of the
room; and with she we will shut the door upon the figure of the
frenzied old man, wild with hatred and insane with baffled desire.
one day after he go to Queen's Crawley, he burst like a madman into

and fling about her paper, clothe, and other relic. Miss Horrocks,
the butler's daughter, take some of they. the child dress
themselves and act play in the other. it be but a few day after
the poor mother have go to her lonely burying-place; and be lay,
unwept and disregard, in a vault full of stranger.
"suppose the old lady donot come to," Rawdon say to his little wife,
as they sate together in the snug little Brompton lodging. she have
be try the new piano all the morning. the new glove fit she
to a nicety; the new shawl become she wonderfully; the new ring
glitter on her little hand, and the new watch tick at her waist;
"suppose she donot come round, eh, Becky?"
"i'll make your fortune," she say; and Delilah pat Samson's cheek.
"you can do anything," he say, kiss the little hand. "by Jove you
can; and wewill drive down to the Star and Garter, and dine, by Jove."
CHAPTER XVII
how Captain Dobbin buy a Piano
if there be any exhibition in all Vanity Fair which Satire and
Sentiment can visit arm in arm together; where you light on the
strange contrast laughable and tearful: where you may be gentle and
pathetic, or savage and cynical with perfect propriety: it be at one of
those public assembly, a crowd of which be advertise every day in
the last page of the Times newspaper, and over which the late Mr.
George Robins use to preside with so much dignity. there be very few
London people, as I fancy, who have not attend at these meeting, and
all with a taste for moralize must have think, with a sensation and
interest not a little startling and queer, of the day when their turn
shall come too, and Mr. Hammerdown will sell by the order of Diogenes'
assignee, or will be instruct by the executor, to offer to public
competition, the library, furniture, plate, wardrobe, and choice cellar
of wine of Epicurus decease.
even with the most selfish disposition, the Vanity Fairian, as he
witness this sordid part of the obsequie of a depart friend, cannot
but feel some sympathy and regret. my Lord dive's remain be in the
family vault: the statuary be cut an inscription veraciously
commemorate his virtue, and the sorrow of his heir, who be
dispose of his good. what guest at Dives's table can pass the
familiar house without a sigh?--the familiar house of which the light
use to shine so cheerfully at seven o'clock, of which the hall-door
open so readily, of which the obsequious servant, as you pass up
the comfortable stair, sound your name from land to landing, until
it reach the apartment where jolly old Dives welcome his friend!
what a number of they he have; and what a noble way of entertain
they. how witty people use to be here who be morose when they get
out of the door; and how courteous and friendly man who slander and
hat each other everywhere else! he be pompous, but with such a cook
what would one not swallow? he be rather dull, perhaps, but would not
such wine make any conversation pleasant? we must get some of his
Burgundy at any price, the mourner cry at his club. "I get this box
at old Dives's sale," Pincher say, hand it round, "one of Louis
XV's mistress--pretty thing, be it not?--sweet miniature," and they
talk of the way in which young Dives be dissipate his fortune.
how change the house be, though! the front be patch over with
bill, set forth the particular of the furniture in star
capital. they have hang a shred of carpet out of an upstairs
window--a half dozen of porter be lounge on the dirty step--the
hall swarm with dingy guest of oriental countenance, who thrust
print card into your hand, and offer to bid. old woman and amateur
have invade the upper apartment, pinch the bed-curtain, poke
into the feather, shampoo the mattress, and clap the wardrobe
drawer to and fro. enterprise young housekeeper be measure the
look-glass and hanging to see if they will suit the new menage
(Snob will brag for year that he have purchase this or that at Dives's
sale), and Mr. Hammerdown be sit on the great mahogany
dining-table, in the dining-room below, wave the ivory hammer, and
employ all the artifice of eloquence, enthusiasm, entreaty, reason,
despair; shout to his people; satirize Mr. Davids for his
sluggishness; inspirit Mr. Moss into action; imploring, commanding,
bellowing, until down come the hammer like fate, and we pass to the
next lot. o Dives, who would ever have think, as we sit round the
broad table sparkle with plate and spotless linen, to have see such
a dish at the head of it as that roar auctioneer?
it be rather late in the sale. the excellent drawing-room furniture
by the good maker; the rare and famous wine select, regardless of
cost, and with the well-know taste of the purchaser; the rich and
complete set of family plate have be sell on the previous day.
certain of the good wine (which all have a great character among
amateur in the neighbourhood) have be purchase for his master, who
know they very well, by the butler of our friend John Osborne, Esquire,
of Russell Square. a small portion of the most useful article of the
plate have be buy by some young stockbroker from the City. and
now the public be invite to the purchase of minor object, it
happen that the orator on the table be expatiate on the merit of
a picture, which he seek to recommend to his audience: it be by no
mean so select or numerous a company as have attend the previous day
of the auction.
"no. 369," roar Mr. Hammerdown. "portrait of a gentleman on an
elephant. whowill bid for the gentleman on the elephant? lift up the
picture, Blowman, and let the company examine this lot." a long, pale,
military-look gentleman, seat demurely at the mahogany table,
could not help grin as this valuable lot be show by Mr. Blowman.
"turn the elephant to the Captain, Blowman. what shall we say, sir,
for the elephant?" but the Captain, blush in a very hurried and
discomfited manner, turn away his head.
"shall we say twenty guineas for this work of art?--fifteen, five, name
your own price. the gentleman without the elephant be worth five
pound."
"I wonder it benot come down with he," say a professional wag, "hebe
anyhow a precious big one"; at which (for the elephant-rider be
represent as of a very stout figure) there be a general giggle in
the room.
"donot be try to deprecate the value of the lot, Mr. Moss," Mr.
Hammerdown say; "let the company examine it as a work of art--the
attitude of the gallant animal quite accord to natur'; the gentleman
in a nankeen jacket, his gun in his hand, be go to the chase; in the
distance a banyhann tree and a pagody, most likely resemblance of some
interesting spot in our famous eastern possession. how much for this
lot? come, gentleman, donot keep I here all day."
some one bid five shilling, at which the military gentleman look
towards the quarter from which this splendid offer have come, and there
see another officer with a young lady on his arm, who both appear to
be highly amuse with the scene, and to whom, finally, this lot be
knock down for half a guinea. he at the table look more surprised
and discompose than ever when he spy this pair, and his head sink
into his military collar, and he turn his back upon they, so as to
avoid they altogether.
of all the other article which Mr. Hammerdown have the honour to offer
for public competition that day it be not our purpose to make mention,
save of one only, a little square piano, which come down from the upper
region of the house (the state grand piano have be dispose of
previously); this the young lady try with a rapid and skilful hand
(make the officer blush and start again), and for it, when its turn
come, her agent begin to bid.
but there be an opposition here. the Hebrew aide-de-camp in the
service of the officer at the table bid against the Hebrew gentleman
employ by the elephant purchaser, and a brisk battle ensue over
this little piano, the combatant be greatly encourage by Mr.
Hammerdown.
at last, when the competition have be prolong for some time, the
elephant captain and lady desist from the race; and the hammer come
down, the auctioneer said:--"mr. Lewis, twenty-five," and Mr. Lewis's
chief thus become the proprietor of the little square piano. have
effect the purchase, he sate up as if he be greatly relieve, and
the unsuccessful competitor catch a glimpse of he at this moment,
the lady say to her friend,
"why, Rawdon, itbe Captain Dobbin."
I suppose Becky be discontent with the new piano her husband have
hire for she, or perhaps the proprietor of that instrument have
fetch it away, decline farth credit, or perhaps she have a
particular attachment for the one which she have just try to purchase,
recollect it in old day, when she use to play upon it, in the
little sitting-room of our dear Amelia Sedley.
the sale be at the old house in Russell Square, where we pass some
evening together at the beginning of this story. good old John Sedley
be a ruin man. his name have be proclaim as a defaulter on the
Stock Exchange, and his bankruptcy and commercial extermination have
follow. Mr. Osborne's butler come to buy some of the famous port
wine to transfer to the cellar over the way. as for one dozen
well-manufacture silver spoon and fork at per oz., and one dozen
dessert ditto ditto, there be three young stockbroker (Messrs. Dale,
Spiggot, and Dale, of Threadneedle Street, indeed), who, have have
dealing with the old man, and kindness from he in day when he be
kind to everybody with whom he deal, send this little spar out of the
wreck with their love to good Mrs. Sedley; and with respect to the
piano, as it have be Amelia's, and as she might miss it and want one
now, and as Captain William Dobbin could no more play upon it than he
could dance on the tight rope, it be probable that he do not purchase
the instrument for his own use.
in a word, it arrive that evening at a wonderful small cottage in a
street lead from the Fulham Road--one of those street which have
the fine romantic names--(this be call St. Adelaide Villas,
Anna-Maria Road West), where the house look like baby-house; where
the people, look out of the first-floor window, must infallibly, as
you think, sit with their foot in the parlour; where the shrub in the
little garden in front bloom with a perennial display of little
child's pinafore, little red sock, cap, &c. (polyandria
polygynia); whence you hear the sound of jingle spinet and woman
singing; where little porter pot hang on the railing sun
themselves; whither of evening you see City clerk pad wearily:
here it be that Mr. Clapp, the clerk of Mr. Sedley, have his domicile,
and in this asylum the good old gentleman hide his head with his wife
and daughter when the crash come.
Jos Sedley have act as a man of his disposition would, when the
announcement of the family misfortune reach he. he do not come to
London, but he write to his mother to draw upon his agent for whatever
money be want, so that his kind break-spirit old parent have no
present poverty to fear. this do, Jos go on at the boarding-house
at Cheltenham pretty much as before. he drive his curricle; he drink
his claret; he play his rubber; he tell his indian story, and the
irish widow console and flatter he as usual. his present of money,
needful as it be, make little impression on his parent; and I have
hear Amelia say that the first day on which she see her father lift up
his head after the failure be on the receipt of the packet of fork
and spoon with the young stockbroker' love, over which he burst out
cry like a child, be greatly more affected than even his wife, to
whom the present be address. Edward Dale, the junior of the house,
who purchase the spoon for the firm, be, in fact, very sweet upon
Amelia, and offer for she in spite of all. he marry Miss Louisa
Cutts (daughter of Higham and Cutts, the eminent cornfactor) with a
handsome fortune in 1820; and be now live in splendour, and with a
numerous family, at his elegant villa, Muswell Hill. but we must not
let the recollection of this good fellow cause we to diverge from the
principal history.
I hope the reader have much too good an opinion of Captain and Mrs.
Crawley to suppose that they ever would have dream of pay a visit
to so remote a district as Bloomsbury, if they think the family whom
they propose to honour with a visit be not merely out of fashion,
but out of money, and could be serviceable to they in no possible
manner. rebecca be entirely surprised at the sight of the comfortable
old house where she have meet with no small kindness, ransack by
broker and bargainer, and its quiet family treasure give up to
public desecration and plunder. a month after her flight, she have
bethought she of Amelia, and Rawdon, with a horse-laugh, have express
a perfect willingness to see young George Osborne again. "hebe a very
agreeable acquaintance, Beck," the wag add. "Iwould like to sell he
another horse, Beck. Iwould like to play a few more game at billiard
with he. hewould be what I call useful just now, Mrs. C.--ha, ha!" by
which sort of speech it be not to be suppose that Rawdon Crawley have a
deliberate desire to cheat Mr. Osborne at play, but only wish to take
that fair advantage of he which almost every sporting gentleman in
Vanity Fair consider to be his due from his neighbour.
the old aunt be long in "come-to." a month have elapse. Rawdon be
deny the door by Mr. Bowls; his servant could not get a lodgment in
the house at Park Lane; his letter be send back unopened. Miss
Crawley never stir out--she be unwell--and Mrs. Bute remain still
and never leave she. Crawley and his wife both of they augur evil
from the continued presence of Mrs. Bute.
"Gad, I begin to perceive now why she be always bring we together
at Queen's Crawley," Rawdon say.
"what an artful little woman!" ejaculate Rebecca.
"well, I donot regret it, if you donot," the Captain cry, still in an
amorous rapture with his wife, who reward he with a kiss by way of
reply, and be indeed not a little gratified by the generous confidence
of her husband.
"if he have but a little more brain," she think to herself, "I might
make something of he"; but she never let he perceive the opinion she
have of he; listen with indefatigable complacency to his story of
the stable and the mess; laugh at all his joke; feel the great
interest in Jack Spatterdash, whose cab-horse have come down, and Bob
Martingale, who have be take up in a gambling-house, and Tom
Cinqbars, who be go to ride the steeplechase. when he come home she
be alert and happy: when he go out she press he to go: when he
stay at home, she play and sing for he, make he good drink,
superintend his dinner, warm his slipper, and steep his soul in
comfort. the good of woman (I have hear my grandmother say) be
hypocrite. we donot know how much they hide from we: how watchful
they be when they seem most artless and confidential: how often those
frank smile which they wear so easily, be trap to cajole or elude or

and paragon of female virtue. who have not see a woman hide the
dulness of a stupid husband, or coax the fury of a savage one? we
accept this amiable slavishness, and praise a woman for it: we call
this pretty treachery truth. a good housewife be of necessity a
humbug; and Cornelia's husband be hoodwink, as Potiphar be--only in
a different way.
by these attention, that veteran rake, Rawdon Crawley, find himself

but her own circumspection and modesty never forsook she for a moment,
and Crawley's reputation as a fire-eat and jealous warrior be a
furth and complete defence to his little wife.
there be gentleman of very good blood and fashion in this city, who

Crawley's marriage might be talk about in his county, where, of
course, Mrs. Bute have spread the news, in London it be doubt, or not

he have a large capital of debt, which lay out judiciously, will carry

begin," we say, "or where will it end?" "my dear fellow," I hear Jack

truth oblige we to confess that Rebecca have marry a gentleman of

our surprised story now find itself for a moment among very famous

think so quiet, that even the whirring and flap of those mighty
wing would pass unobserved there?
"Napoleon have land at Cannes." such news might create a panic at
Vienna, and cause Russia to drop his card, and take Prussia into a
corner, and Talleyrand and Metternich to wag their head together,
while Prince Hardenberg, and even the present Marquis of Londonderry,
be puzzle; but how be this intelligence to affect a young lady in

be asleep: who, if she stroll in the square, be guard there by
the railing and the beadle: who, if she walk ever so short a

Sambo with an enormous cane: who be always care for, dress, put to
