wage? Bon Dieu, I say, be it not hard that the fateful rush of the
great imperial struggle cannot take place without affect a poor
little harmless girl of eighteen, who be occupy in billing and
cooing, or work muslin collar in Russell Square? you too, kindly,

down, here, although cower under the shelter of Holborn? yes;
Napoleon be fling his last stake, and poor little Emmy Sedley's
happiness form, somehow, part of it.
in the first place, her father's fortune be sweep down with that fatal

John Sedley spring up out of his chair to meet his wife, who run to

when one man have be under very remarkable obligation to another,

would be. to account for your own hard-heartedness and ingratitude in

basest treachery and with the most sinister motif. from a mere sense

villain--otherwise he, the persecutor, be a wretch himself.
and as a general rule, which may make all creditor who be inclined to

misfortune with great sympathy--condole with her loss and

towards his first benefactor. when she have ease her overflowing bosom
somewhat, and pour forth many of her sorrow, he have the courage to
ask actually to see Amelia, who be above in her room as usual, and
whom her mother lead tremble downstairs.
her appearance be so ghastly, and her look of despair so pathetic,
that honest William Dobbin be frightened as he behold it; and read the
most fatal foreboding in that pale fix face. after sit in his
company a minute or two, she put the packet into his hand, and say,
"take this to Captain Osborne, if you please, and--and I hope hebe
quite well--and it be very kind of you to come and see we--and we like
our new house very much. and I--I think Iwill go upstairs, Mamma, for
Ibe not very strong." and with this, and a curtsey and a smile, the
poor child go her way. the mother, as she lead she up, cast back
look of anguish towards Dobbin. the good fellow want no such
appeal. he love she himself too fondly for that. inexpressible
grief, and pity, and terror pursue he, and he come away as if he be
a criminal after see she.
when Osborne hear that his friend have find she, he make hot and
anxious inquiry regard the poor child. how be she? how do she
look? what do she say? his comrade take his hand, and look he in
the face.
"George, shebe die," William Dobbin say--and could speak no more.
there be a buxom irish servant-girl, who perform all the duty of
the little house where the Sedley family have find refuge: and this
girl have in vain, on many previous day, striven to give Amelia aid or
consolation. Emmy be much too sad to answer, or even to be aware of
the attempt the other be make in her favour.
four hour after the talk between Dobbin and Osborne, this servant-maid
come into Amelia's room, where she sate as usual, brood
silently over her letter--her little treasure. the girl, smile,
and look arch and happy, make many trial to attract poor Emmy's
attention, who, however, take no heed of she.
"Miss Emmy," say the girl.
"Ibe come," Emmy say, not look round.
"therebe a message," the maid go on. "therebe
something--somebody--sure, herebe a new letter for you--don't be read
they old one any more." and she give she a letter, which Emmy take, and
read.
"I must see you," the letter say. "Dearest Emmy--dearest
love--dearest wife, come to I."
George and her mother be outside, wait until she have read the
letter.
CHAPTER XIX
Miss Crawley at Nurse
we have see how Mrs. Firkin, the lady's maid, as soon as any event of
importance to the Crawley family come to her knowledge, feel bind to
communicate it to Mrs. Bute Crawley, at the Rectory; and have before
mention how particularly kind and attentive that good-natured lady
be to Miss Crawley's confidential servant. she have be a gracious
friend to Miss Briggs, the companion, also; and have secure the
latter's good-will by a number of those attention and promise, which
cost so little in the making, and be yet so valuable and agreeable to
the recipient. indeed every good economist and manager of a household
must know how cheap and yet how amiable these profession be, and what
a flavour they give to the most homely dish in life. who be the
blunder idiot who say that "fine word butter no parsnip"? half
the parsnip of society be serve and render palatable with no other
sauce. as the immortal Alexis Soyer can make more delicious soup for a
half-penny than an ignorant cook can concoct with pound of vegetable
and meat, so a skilful artist will make a few simple and pleasing
phrase go far than ever so much substantial benefit-stock in the
hand of a mere bungler. Nay, we know that substantial benefit often
sicken some stomach; whereas, most will digest any amount of fine
word, and be always eager for more of the same food. Mrs. Bute have
tell Briggs and Firkin so often of the depth of her affection for they;
and what she would do, if she have Miss Crawley's fortune, for friend
so excellent and attach, that the lady in question have the deep
regard for she; and feel as much gratitude and confidence as if Mrs.
Bute have load they with the most expensive favour.
Rawdon Crawley, on the other hand, like a selfish heavy dragoon as he
be, never take the least trouble to conciliate his aunt's aide-de-camp,
show his contempt for the pair with entire frankness--make
Firkin pull off his boot on one occasion--send she out in the rain on
ignominious message--and if he give she a guinea, fling it to she as
if it be a box on the ear. as his aunt, too, make a butt of Briggs,
the Captain follow the example, and level his joke at she--joke
about as delicate as a kick from his charger. whereas, Mrs. Bute
consult she in matter of taste or difficulty, admire her poetry,
and by a thousand act of kindness and politeness, show she
appreciation of Briggs; and if she make Firkin a twopenny-halfpenny
present, accompany it with so many compliment, that the
twopence-half-penny be transmute into gold in the heart of the
grateful waiting-maid, who, besides, be look forwards quite
contentedly to some prodigious benefit which must happen to she on the
day when Mrs. Bute come into her fortune.
the different conduct of these two people be point out respectfully
to the attention of person commence the world. praise everybody, I
say to such: never be squeamish, but speak out your compliment both
point-blank in a man's face, and behind his back, when you know there
be a reasonable chance of his hear it again. never lose a chance of
say a kind word. as Collingwood never see a vacant place in his
estate but he take an acorn out of his pocket and pop it in; so deal
with your compliment through life. an acorn cost nothing; but it may
sprout into a prodigious bit of timber.
in a word, during Rawdon Crawley's prosperity, he be only obey with
sulky acquiescence; when his disgrace come, there be nobody to help or
pity he. whereas, when Mrs. Bute take the command at Miss Crawley's
house, the garrison there be charm to act under such a leader,
expect all sort of promotion from her promise, her generosity, and
her kind word.
that he would consider himself beat, after one defeat, and make no
attempt to regain the position he have lose, Mrs. Bute Crawley never
allow herself to suppose. she know Rebecca to be too clever and
spirited and desperate a woman to submit without a struggle; and feel

against assault; or mine, or surprise.
in the first place, though she hold the town, be she sure of the

in the very good-of moment, if anybody tell Miss Crawley that she be,

as Monsieur de Voltaire himself could desire, but when illness overtake
she, it be aggravate by the most dreadful terror of death, and an

sick-bed homily and pious reflection be, to be sure, out of place
in mere story-book, and we be not go (after the fashion of some
novelist of the present day) to cajole the public into a sermon, when
it be only a comedy that the reader pay his money to witness. but,
without preach, the truth may surely be bear in mind, that the
bustle, and triumph, and laughter, and gaiety which Vanity Fair
exhibit in public, do not always pursue the performer into private
life, and that the most dreary depression of spirit and dismal
repentance sometimes overcome he. Recollection of the well ordain
banquet will scarcely cheer sick epicure. reminiscence of the most
become dress and brilliant ball triumph will go very little way to
console fade beauty. perhaps statesman, at a particular period of
existence, be not much gratified at think over the most triumphant
division; and the success or the pleasure of yesterday become of very

about which all of we must some day or other be speculate. o brother
wearer of motley! be there not moment when one grow sick of

friend and companion, be my amiable object--to walk with you through
the Fair, to examine the shop and the show there; and that we should
all come home after the flare, and the noise, and the gaiety, and be
perfectly miserable in private.
"if that poor man of mine have a head on his shoulder," Mrs. Bute

and, as the hatred of vice be always a progress towards virtue, Mrs.

these be the material which prudent Mrs. Bute gather together in

thus, for instance, Mrs. Bute, with the good intention no doubt in the

"your devotion, it must be confess, be admirable," Mr. Clump say,

"I have scarcely close my eye since my arrival: I give up sleep,

it be a gallant and decide triumph for Mrs. Bute. but she feel the

CHAPTER XX
in which Captain Dobbin act as the Messenger of Hymen
without know how, Captain William Dobbin find himself the great
promoter, arranger, and manager of the match between George Osborne and
Amelia. but for he it never would have take place: he could not but
confess as much to himself, and smile rather bitterly as he think
that he of all man in the world should be the person upon whom the care
of this marriage have fall. but though indeed the conducting of this
negotiation be about as painful a task as could be set to he, yet
when he have a duty to perform, Captain Dobbin be accustom to go
through it without many word or much hesitation: and, have make up
his mind completely, that if Miss Sedley be balk of her husband she
would die of the disappointment, he be determined to use all his good
endeavour to keep she alive.
I forbear to enter into minute particular of the interview between
George and Amelia, when the former be bring back to the foot (or
should we venture to say the arm?) of his young mistress by the
intervention of his friend honest William. a much hard heart than
George's would have melt at the sight of that sweet face so sadly
ravage by grief and despair, and at the simple tender accent in which
she tell her little break-hearted story: but as she do not faint when
her mother, trembling, bring Osborne to she; and as she only give
relief to her overcharged grief, by lay her head on her lover's
shoulder and there weep for a while the most tender, copious, and
refreshing tear--old Mrs. Sedley, too greatly relieve, think it be
good to leave the young person to themselves; and so quit Emmy
cry over George's hand, and kiss it humbly, as if he be her
supreme chief and master, and as if she be quite a guilty and
unworthy person need every favour and grace from he.
this prostration and sweet unrepine obedience exquisitely touch and
flatter George Osborne. he see a slave before he in that simple
yield faithful creature, and his soul within he thrill secretly
somehow at the knowledge of his power. he would be generous-minded,
Sultan as he be, and raise up this kneeling Esther and make a queen of
she: besides, her sadness and beauty touch he as much as she
submission, and so he cheer she, and raise she up and forgave she,
so to speak. all her hope and feeling, which be die and
withering, this her sun have be remove from she, bloom again and
at once, its light be restore. you would scarcely have recognise
the beaming little face upon Amelia's pillow that night as the one that
be lay there the night before, so wan, so lifeless, so careless of
all round about. the honest irish maid-servant, delight with the
change, ask leave to kiss the face that have grow all of a sudden so
rosy. Amelia put her arm round the girl's neck and kiss she with
all her heart, like a child. she be little more. she have that night
a sweet refresh sleep, like one--and what a spring of inexpressible
happiness as she wake in the morning sunshine!
"he will be here again to-day," Amelia think. "he be the great
and good of man." and the fact be, that George think he be one of
the generous creature alive: and that he be make a tremendous
sacrifice in marry this young creature.
while she and Osborne be have their delightful tete-a-tete above
stair, old Mrs. Sedley and Captain Dobbin be converse below upon
the state of the affair, and the chance and future arrangement of
the young people. Mrs. Sedley have bring the two lover together
and leave they embrace each other with all their might, like a true
woman, be of opinion that no power on earth would induce Mr. Sedley to
consent to the match between his daughter and the son of a man who have
so shamefully, wickedly, and monstrously treat he. and she tell a
long story about happy day and their early splendour, when
Osborne live in a very humble way in the New Road, and his wife be
too glad to receive some of Jos's little baby thing, with which Mrs.
Sedley accommodate she at the birth of one of Osborne's own child.
the fiendish ingratitude of that man, she be sure, have break Mr. S.'s
heart: and as for a marriage, he would never, never, never, never
consent.
"they must run away together, Ma'am," Dobbin say, laugh, "and
follow the example of Captain Rawdon Crawley, and Miss Emmy's friend
the little governess." be it possible? well she never! Mrs. Sedley
be all excitement about this news. she wish that Blenkinsop be
here to hear it: Blenkinsop always mistrust that Miss Sharp.-- what
an escape Jos have have! and she describe the already well-know
love-passage between Rebecca and the Collector of Boggley Wollah.
it be not, however, Mr. Sedley's wrath which Dobbin fear, so much as
that of the other parent concern, and he own that he have a very
considerable doubt and anxiety respect the behaviour of the
black-browed old tyrant of a Russia merchant in Russell Square. he have
forbid the match peremptorily, Dobbin think. he know what a savage
determined man Osborne be, and how he stick by his word. "the only
chance George have of reconcilement," argue his friend, "be by
distinguish himself in the come campaign. if he die they both go
together. if he fail in distinction--what then? he have some money
from his mother, I have hear enough to purchase his majority--or he
must sell out and go and dig in Canada, or rough it in a cottage in the
country." with such a partner Dobbin think he would not mind
Siberia--and, strange to say, this absurd and utterly imprudent young
fellow never for a moment consider that the want of mean to keep a
nice carriage and horse, and of an income which should enable its
possessor to entertain their friend genteelly, ought to operate as
bar to the union of George and Miss Sedley.
it be these weighty consideration which make he think too that the
marriage should take place as quickly as possible. be he anxious
himself, I wonder, to have it over?--as people, when death have
occur, like to press forward the funeral, or when a parting be
resolve upon, hasten it. it be certain that Mr. Dobbin, have take
the matter in hand, be most extraordinarily eager in the conduct of
it. he urge on George the necessity of immediate action: he show
the chance of reconciliation with his father, which a favourable
mention of his name in the Gazette must bring about. if need be he
would go himself and brave both the father in the business. at all
event, he beseech George to go through with it before the order
come, which everybody expect, for the departure of the regiment from
England on foreign service.
bent upon these hymeneal project, and with the applause and consent of
Mrs. Sedley, who do not care to break the matter personally to her
husband, Mr. Dobbin go to seek John Sedley at his house of call in
the City, the Tapioca Coffee-house, where, since his own office be
shut up, and fate have overtake he, the poor break-down old
gentleman use to betake himself daily, and write letter and receive
they, and tie they up into mysterious bundle, several of which he
carry in the flap of his coat. I donot know anything more dismal
than that business and bustle and mystery of a ruin man: those
letter from the wealthy which he show you: those wear greasy
document promise support and offer condolence which he place
wistfully before you, and on which he build his hope of restoration
and future fortune. my beloved reader have no doubt in the course of his
experience be waylay by many such a luckless companion. he take
you into the corner; he have his bundle of paper out of his gap coat
pocket; and the tape off, and the string in his mouth, and the
favourite letter select and lay before you; and who do not know
the sad eager half-crazy look which he fix on you with his hopeless
eye?
change into a man of this sort, Dobbin find the once florid, jovial,
and prosperous John Sedley. his coat, that use to be so glossy and
trim, be white at the seam, and the button show the copper. his
face have fall in, and be unshorn; his frill and neckcloth hang limp
under his bagging waistcoat. when he use to treat the boy in old
day at a coffee-house, he would shout and laugh louder than anybody
there, and have all the waiter skip round he; it be quite
painful to see how humble and civil he be to John of the Tapioca, a
blear-eyed old attendant in dingy stocking and crack pump, whose
business it be to serve glass of wafer, and bumper of ink in
pewter, and slice of paper to the frequenter of this dreary house of
entertainment, where nothing else seem to be consume. as for
William Dobbin, whom he have tip repeatedly in his youth, and who have
be the old gentleman's butt on a thousand occasion, old Sedley give
his hand to he in a very hesitating humble manner now, and call he
"Sir." a feeling of shame and remorse take possession of William Dobbin
as the break old man so receive and address he, as if he himself
have be somehow guilty of the misfortune which have bring Sedley so
low.
"I be very glad to see you, Captain Dobbin, sir," say he, after a
skulking look or two at his visitor (whose lanky figure and military
appearance cause some excitement likewise to twinkle in the blear eye
of the waiter in the cracked dancing pump, and awaken the old lady
in black, who doze among the mouldy old coffee-cup in the bar). "how
be the worthy alderman, and my lady, your excellent mother, sir?" he
look round at the waiter as he say, "my lady," as much as to say,
"Hark ye, John, I have friend still, and person of rank and
reputation, too." "be you come to do anything in my way, sir? my
young friend Dale and Spiggot do all my business for I now, until my
new office be ready; for Ibe only here temporarily, you know,
Captain. what can we do for you, sir? will you like to take anything?"
Dobbin, with a great deal of hesitation and stuttering, protest that
he be not in the least hungry or thirsty; that he have no business to
transact; that he only come to ask if Mr. Sedley be well, and to shake
hand with an old friend; and, he add, with a desperate perversion of
truth, "my mother be very well--that is, shebe be very unwell, and be
only wait for the first fine day to go out and call upon Mrs.
Sedley. how be Mrs. Sedley, sir? I hope shebe quite well." and here
he pause, reflect on his own consummate hypocrisy; for the day be
as fine, and the sunshine as bright as it ever be in Coffin Court,
where the Tapioca Coffee-house be situate: and Mr. Dobbin remember
that he have see Mrs. Sedley himself only an hour before, have drive
Osborne down to Fulham in his gig, and leave he there tete-a-tete with
Miss Amelia.
"my wife will be very happy to see her ladyship," Sedley reply,
pull out his paper. "Ihave a very kind letter here from your
father, sir, and beg my respectful compliment to he. Lady D. will
find we in rather a small house than we be accustom to receive
our friend in; but itbe snug, and the change of air do good to my
daughter, who be suffer in town rather--you remember little Emmy,
sir?--ye, suffer a good deal." the old gentleman's eye be
wander as he speak, and he be think of something else, as he
sate thrumming on his paper and fumble at the wear red tape.
"yoube a military man," he go on; "I ask you, Bill Dobbin, could any
man ever have speculate upon the return of that corsican scoundrel
from Elba? when the ally sovereign be here last year, and we give
them that dinner in the City, sir, and we see the Temple of Concord, and
the firework, and the chinese bridge in St. James's Park, could any
sensible man suppose that peace benot really conclude, after wewould
actually sing Te Deum for it, sir? I ask you, William, could I suppose
that the Emperor of Austria be a damned traitor--a traitor, and
nothing more? I donot mince word--a double-faced infernal traitor and
schemer, who mean to have his son-in-law back all along. and I say
that the escape of Boney from Elba be a damned imposition and plot,
sir, in which half the power of Europe be concern, to bring the
fund down, and to ruin this country. thatbe why Ibe here, William.
thatbe why my namebe in the Gazette. why, sir?--because I trust the
Emperor of Russia and the Prince Regent. look here. look at my
paper. look what the fund be on the 1st of March--what the french
five be when I buy for the count. and what theybe at now.
there be collusion, sir, or that villain never would have escape.
where be the English Commissioner who allow he to get away? he
ought to be shoot, sir--bring to a court-martial, and shot, by Jove."
"webe go to hunt Boney out, sir," Dobbin say, rather alarmed at
the fury of the old man, the vein of whose forehead begin to swell,
and who sate drum his paper with his clench fist. "we be go
to hunt he out, sir--the Duke's in Belgium already, and we expect
marching order every day."
"give he no quarter. bring back the villain's head, sir. shoot the
coward down, sir," Sedley roar. "Iwould enlist myself, by--; but Ibe a
break old man--ruin by that damned scoundrel--and by a parcel of
swindle thief in this country whom I make, sir, and who be roll
in their carriage now," he add, with a break in his voice.
Dobbin be not a little affect by the sight of this once kind old
friend, craze almost with misfortune and rave with senile anger.
Pity the fall gentleman: you to whom money and fair repute be the
chief good; and so, surely, be they in Vanity Fair.
"yes," he continue, "there be some viper that you warm, and they
ste you afterwards. there be some beggar that you put on
horseback, and theybe the first to ride you down. you know whom I
mean, William Dobbin, my boy. I mean a purse-proud villain in Russell
Square, whom I know without a shilling, and whom I pray and hope to see
a beggar as he be when I befriend he."
"I have hear something of this, sir, from my friend George," Dobbin
say, anxious to come to his point. "the quarrel between you and his
father have cut he up a great deal, sir. indeed, Ibe the bearer of a
message from he."
"o, thatbe your errand, be it?" cry the old man, jump up. "what!
perhaps he condole with I, do he? very kind of he, the
stiff-back prig, with his dandified air and West end swagger. hebe
hanker about my house, be he still? if my son have the courage of a
man, hewould shoot he. hebe as big a villain as his father. I willnot
have his name mention in my house. I curse the day that ever I let
he into it; and Iwould rather see my daughter dead at my foot than
married to he."
"his father's harshness be not George's fault, sir. your daughter's
love for he be as much your do as his. who be you, that you be
to play with two young people's affection and break their heart at
your will?"
"recollect itbe not his father that break the match off," old Sedley
cry out. "itbe I that forbid it. that family and mine be separate
for ever. Ibe fall low, but not so low as that: no, no. and so you
may tell the whole race--son, and father and sister, and all."
"itbe my belief, sir, that you have not the power or the right to
separate those two," Dobbin answer in a low voice; "and that if you
donot give your daughter your consent it will be her duty to marry
without it. therebe no reason she should die or live miserably because
you be wrong-headed. to my thinking, shebe just as much married as if
the bann have be read in all the church in London. and what well
answer can there be to Osborne's charge against you, as charge there
be, than that his son claim to enter your family and marry your
daughter?"
a light of something like satisfaction seem to break over old Sedley
as this point be put to he: but he still persist that with his
consent the marriage between Amelia and George should never take place.
"we must do it without," Dobbin say, smile, and tell Mr. Sedley, as
he have tell Mrs. Sedley in the day, before, the story of Rebecca's
elopement with Captain Crawley. it evidently amuse the old gentleman.
"yoube terrible fellow, you captain," say he, tie up his paper;
and his face wear something like a smile upon it, to the astonishment
of the blear-eyed waiter who now enter, and have never see such an
expression upon Sedley's countenance since he have use the dismal
coffee-house.
the idea of hit his enemy Osborne such a blow soothe, perhaps, the
old gentleman: and, their colloquy presently end, he and Dobbin
part pretty good friend.
"my sister say she have diamond as big as pigeon' egg," George say,
laugh. "how they must set off her complexion! a perfect
illumination it must be when her jewel be on her neck. her
jet-black hair be as curly as Sambo's. I dare say she wear a nose ring
when she go to court; and with a plume of feather in her top-knot
she would look a perfect Belle Sauvage."
George, in conversation with Amelia, be rally the appearance of a
young lady of whom his father and sister have lately make the
acquaintance, and who be an object of vast respect to the Russell
Square family. she be report to have I donot know how many
plantation in the West Indies; a deal of money in the fund; and three
star to her name in the East India stockholder' list. she have a
mansion in Surrey, and a house in Portland Place. the name of the rich
West India heiress have be mention with applause in the Morning
Post. Mrs. Haggistoun, Colonel Haggistoun's widow, her relative,
"chaperon" she, and keep her house. she be just from school, where
she have complete her education, and George and his sister have meet she
at an evening party at old Hulker's house, Devonshire Place (Hulker,
Bullock, and Co. be long the correspondent of her house in the West
Indies), and the girl have make the most cordial advance to she, which
the heiress have receive with great good humour. an orphan in her
position--with her money--so interesting! the Misses Osborne say.
they be full of their new friend when they return from the Hulker
ball to Miss Wirt, their companion; they have make arrangement for
continually meet, and have the carriage and drive to see she the very
next day. Mrs. Haggistoun, Colonel Haggistoun's widow, a relation of
Lord Binkie, and always talk of he, strike the dear unsophisticated
girl as rather haughty, and too much inclined to talk about her great
relation: but Rhoda be everything they could wish--the frank,
kind, most agreeable creature--want a little polish, but so
good-natured. the girl Christian-name each other at once.
"you should have see her dress for court, Emmy," Osborne cry,
laugh. "she come to my sister to show it off, before she be
present in state by my Lady Binkie, the Haggistoun's kinswoman. shebe
relate to every one, that Haggistoun. her diamond blaze out like
Vauxhall on the night we be there. (do you remember Vauxhall, Emmy,
and Jos singing to his dear diddle diddle darling?) diamond and
mahogany, my dear! think what an advantageous contrast--and the white
feather in her hair--I mean in her wool. she have earring like
chandelier; you might have light them up, by Jove--and a yellow satin
train that streele after she like the tail of a cornet."
"how old be she?" ask Emmy, to whom George be rattle away
regard this dark paragon, on the morning of their reunion--rattle
away as no other man in the world surely could.
"why the Black Princess, though she have only just leave school, must be
two or three and twenty. and you should see the hand she write! Mrs.
Colonel Haggistoun usually write her letter, but in a moment of
confidence, she put pen to paper for my sister; she spelt satin
satting, and Saint James's, Saint Jams."
"why, surely it must be Miss Swartz, the parlour boarder," Emmy say,
remember that good-natured young mulatto girl, who have be so
hysterically affect when Amelia leave Miss Pinkerton's academy.
"the very name," George say. "her father be a german Jew--a
slave-owner they say--connect with the Cannibal Islands in some way
or other. he die last year, and Miss Pinkerton have finish her
education. she can play two piece on the piano; she know three
song; she can write when Mrs. Haggistoun be by to spell for she; and
Jane and Maria already have get to love she as a sister."
"I wish they would have love I," say Emmy, wistfully. "they be
always very cold to I."
"my dear child, they would have love you if you have have two hundred
thousand pound," George reply. "that be the way in which they have
be bring up. Ours be a ready-money society. we live among banker
and City big-wig, and be hang to they, and every man, as he talk to
you, be jingle his guineas in his pocket. there be that jackass Fred
Bullock be go to marry Maria--therebe Goldmore, the East India
Director, therebe Dipley, in the tallow trade--our trade," George say,
with an uneasy laugh and a blush. "curse the whole pack of
money-grub vulgarian! I fall asleep at their great heavy dinner.
I feel ashamed in my father's great stupid party. Ihave be
accustom to live with gentleman, and man of the world and fashion,
Emmy, not with a parcel of turtle-feed tradesman. dear little woman,
you be the only person of our set who ever look, or think, or
speak like a lady: and you do it because yoube an angel and cannot help
it. donot remonstrate. you be the only lady. donot Miss Crawley
remark it, who have live in the good company in Europe? and as for
Crawley, of the Life Guards, hang it, hebe a fine fellow: and I like
he for marry the girl he have choose."
Amelia admire Mr. Crawley very much, too, for this; and trust
Rebecca would be happy with he, and hop (with a laugh) Jos would be
console. and so the pair go on prattling, as in quite early day.
Amelia's confidence be perfectly restore to she, though she
express a great deal of pretty jealousy about Miss Swartz, and
profess to be dreadfully frightened--like a hypocrite as she be--lest
George should forget she for the heiress and her money and her
estate in Saint Kitt's. but the fact be, she be a great deal too
happy to have fear or doubt or misgiving of any sort: and have
George at her side again, be not afraid of any heiress or beauty, or
indeed of any sort of danger.
when Captain Dobbin come back in the afternoon to these people--which
he do with a great deal of sympathy for they--it do his heart good to
see how Amelia have grow young again--how she laugh, and chirp, and
sing familiar old song at the piano, which be only interrupt by
the bell from without proclaim Mr. Sedley's return from the City,
before whom George receive a signal to retreat.
beyond the first smile of recognition--and even that be an hypocrisy,
for she think his arrival rather provoking--Miss Sedley do not once
notice Dobbin during his visit. but he be content, so that he see she
happy; and thankful to have be the mean of make she so.
CHAPTER XXI
A Quarrel about an Heiress
Love may be feel for any young lady endow with such quality as Miss
Swartz possess; and a great dream of ambition enter into old Mr.
Osborne's soul, which she be to realize. he encourage, with the
utmost enthusiasm and friendliness, his daughter' amiable attachment
to the young heiress, and protest that it give he the sincere
pleasure as a father to see the love of his girl so well dispose.
"you willnot find," he would say to Miss Rhoda, "that splendour and rank
to which you be accustom at the West End, my dear Miss, at our
humble mansion in Russell Square. my daughter be plain,
disinterested girl, but their heart be in the right place, and
theyhave conceive an attachment for you which do they honour--I say,
which do they honour. Ibe a plain, simple, humble british
merchant--an honest one, as my respected friend Hulker and Bullock
will vouch, who be the correspondent of your late lamented father.
youwill find we a united, simple, happy, and I think I may say
respect, family--a plain table, a plain people, but a warm welcome,
my dear Miss Rhoda--Rhoda, let I say, for my heart warm to you, it
do really. Ibe a frank man, and I like you. a glass of Champagne!
Hicks, Champagne to Miss Swartz."
there be little doubt that old Osborne believe all he say, and that
the girl be quite earnest in their protestation of affection for
Miss Swartz. People in Vanity Fair fasten on to rich folk quite
naturally. if the simple people be disposed to look not a little
kindly on great Prosperity (for I defy any member of the british public
to say that the notion of Wealth have not something awful and pleasing
to he; and you, if you be tell that the man next you at dinner have
get half a million, not to look at he with a certain interest)--if the
simple look benevolently on money, how much more do your old worldling
regard it! their affection rush out to meet and welcome money. their
kind sentiment awaken spontaneously towards the interesting possessor
of it. I know some respectable people who donot consider themselves at
liberty to indulge in friendship for any individual who have not a
certain competency, or place in society. they give a loose to their
feeling on proper occasion. and the proof be, that the major part of
the Osborne family, who have not, in fifteen year, be able to get up
a hearty regard for Amelia Sedley, become as fond of Miss Swartz in the
course of a single evening as the most romantic advocate of friendship
at first sight could desire.
what a match for George shewould be (the sister and Miss Wirt agree),
and how much well than that insignificant little Amelia! such a
dash young fellow as he be, with his good look, rank, and
accomplishment, would be the very husband for she. vision of ball
in Portland Place, presentation at Court, and introduction to half
the peerage, fill the mind of the young lady; who talk of
nothing but George and his grand acquaintance to their beloved new
friend.
Old Osborne think she would be a great match, too, for his son. he
should leave the army; he should go into Parliament; he should cut a
figure in the fashion and in the state. his blood boil with honest
british exultation, as he see the name of Osborne ennoble in the
person of his son, and think that he might be the progenitor of a
glorious line of baronet. he work in the City and on 'Change, until
he know everything relate to the fortune of the heiress, how she
money be place, and where her estate lay. Young Fred Bullock, one
of his chief informant, would have like to make a bid for her himself
(it be so the young banker express it), only he be book to Maria
Osborne. but not be able to secure she as a wife, the disinterested
Fred quite approve of she as a sister-in-law. "let George cut in
directly and win she," be his advice. "strike while the iron's hot,
you know--while shebe fresh to the town: in a few week some d----
fellow from the West end will come in with a title and a rotten
rent-roll and cut all we City man out, as Lord Fitzrufus do last year
with Miss Grogram, who be actually engage to Podder, of Podder &
Brown's. the soon it be do the well, Mr. Osborne; theybe my
sentiment," the wag say; though, when Osborne have leave the bank
parlour, Mr. Bullock remember Amelia, and what a pretty girl she be,
and how attach to George Osborne; and he give up at least ten second
of his valuable time to regret the misfortune which have befall
that unlucky young woman.
while thus George Osborne's good feeling, and his good friend and
genius, Dobbin, be carry back the truant to Amelia's foot,
George's parent and sister be arrange this splendid match for he,
which they never dream he would resist.
when the eld Osborne give what he call "a hint," there be no
possibility for the most obtuse to mistake his meaning. he call
kick a footman downstairs a hint to the latter to leave his service.
with his usual frankness and delicacy he tell Mrs. Haggistoun that he
would give she a cheque for five thousand pound on the day his son be
married to her ward; and call that proposal a hint, and consider it
a very dexterous piece of diplomacy. he give George finally such
another hint regard the heiress; and order he to marry she out of
hand, as he would have order his butler to draw a cork, or his clerk
to write a letter.
this imperative hint disturb George a good deal. he be in the very
first enthusiasm and delight of his second courtship of Amelia, which
be inexpressibly sweet to he. the contrast of her manner and
appearance with those of the heiress, make the idea of a union with the
latter appear doubly ludicrous and odious. carriage and opera-box,
think he; fancy be see in they by the side of such a mahogany
charmer as that! add to all that the junior Osborne be quite as
obstinate as the senior: when he want a thing, quite as firm in his
resolution to get it; and quite as violent when anger, as his father
in his most stern moment.
on the first day when his father formally give he the hint that he be
to place his affection at Miss Swartz's foot, George temporise with
the old gentleman. "you should have think of the matter soon,
sir," he say. "it cannot be do now, when webe expect every day to
go on foreign service. wait till my return, if I do return"; and then
he represent, that the time when the regiment be daily expect to
quit England, be exceedingly ill-choose: that the few day or week
during which they be still to remain at home, must be devote to
business and not to love-making: time enough for that when he come home
with his majority; "for, I promise you," say he, with a satisfied air,
"that one way or other you shall read the name of George Osborne in the
Gazette."
the father's reply to this be found upon the information which he
have get in the City: that the West End chap would infallibly catch
hold of the heiress if any delay take place: that if he donot marry
Miss S., he might at least have an engagement in writing, to come into
effect when he return to England; and that a man who could get ten
thousand a year by stay at home, be a fool to risk his life abroad.
"so that you would have I show up as a coward, sir, and our name
dishonour for the sake of Miss Swartz's money," George interpose.
this remark stagger the old gentleman; but as he have to reply to it,
and as his mind be nevertheless make up, he say, "you will dine here
to-morrow, sir, and every day Miss Swartz come, you will be here to
pay your respect to she. if you want for money, call upon Mr.
Chopper." thus a new obstacle be in George's way, to interfere with
his plan regard Amelia; and about which he and Dobbin have more than
one confidential consultation. his friend's opinion respect the
line of conduct which he ought to pursue, we know already. and as for
Osborne, when he be once bent on a thing, a fresh obstacle or two only
render he the more resolute.
the dark object of the conspiracy into which the chief of the Osborne
family have enter, be quite ignorant of all their plan regard she
(which, strange to say, her friend and chaperon do not divulge), and,
take all the young lady' flattery for genuine sentiment, and be,
as we have before have occasion to show, of a very warm and impetuous
nature, respond to their affection with quite a tropical ardour. and
if the truth may be tell, I dare say that she too have some selfish
attraction in the Russell Square house; and in a word, think George
Osborne a very nice young man. his whisker have make an impression upon
she, on the very first night she behold they at the ball at Messrs.
Hulkers; and, as we know, she be not the first woman who have be
charm by they. George have an air at once swaggering and melancholy,
languid and fierce. he look like a man who have passion, secret,
and private harrowing grief and adventure. his voice be rich and
deep. he would say it be a warm evening, or ask his partner to take
an ice, with a tone as sad and confidential as if he be break her
mother's death to she, or prelude a declaration of love. he trample
over all the young buck of his father's circle, and be the hero among
those third-rate man. some few sneered at he and hat he. some,
like Dobbin, fanatically admire he. and his whisker have begin to do
their work, and to curl themselves round the affection of Miss Swartz.
whenever there be a chance of meet he in Russell Square, that
simple and good-natured young woman be quite in a flurry to see she
dear Misses Osborne. she go to great expense in new gown, and
bracelet, and bonnet, and in prodigious feather. she adorn she
person with her utmost skill to please the Conqueror, and exhibit all
her simple accomplishment to win his favour. the girl would ask she,
with the great gravity, for a little music, and she would sing she
three song and play she two little piece as often as ever they ask,
and with an always increase pleasure to herself. during these
delectable entertainment, Miss Wirt and the chaperon sate by, and
con over the peerage, and talk about the nobility.
the day after George have his hint from his father, and a short time
before the hour of dinner, he be loll upon a sofa in the
drawing-room in a very become and perfectly natural attitude of
melancholy. he have be, at his father's request, to Mr. Chopper in
the City (the old-gentleman, though he give great sum to his son,
would never specify any fix allowance for he, and reward he only
as he be in the humour). he have then be to pass three hour with
Amelia, his dear little Amelia, at Fulham; and he come home to find his
sister spread in starched muslin in the drawing-room, the dowager
cackle in the background, and honest Swartz in her favourite
amber-colour satin, with turquoise bracelet, countless ring,
flower, feather, and all sort of tag and gimcrack, about as
elegantly decorate as a she chimney-sweep on May-day.
the girl, after vain attempt to engage he in conversation, talk
about fashion and the last drawing-room until he be perfectly sick of
their chatter. he contrast their behaviour with little emmy'--their
shrill voice with her tender ringing tone; their attitude and their
elbow and their starch, with her humble soft movement and modest
grace. poor Swartz be seat in a place where Emmy have be
accustomed to sit. her bejewel hand lie sprawl in her amber
satin lap. her tag and ear-ring twinkle, and her big eye roll
about. she be do nothing with perfect contentment, and think
herself charming. anything so become as the satin the sister have
never see.
"Dammy," George say to a confidential friend, "she look like a China
doll, which have nothing to do all day but to grin and wag its head. by
Jove, Will, it be all I I could do to prevent myself from throw the
sofa-cushion at she." he restrain that exhibition of sentiment,
however.
the sister begin to play the Battle of Prague. "stop that d----
thing," George howl out in a fury from the sofa. "it make I mad.
you play we something, Miss Swartz, do. sing something, anything but
the Battle of Prague."
"shall I sing 'Blue Eyed Mary' or the air from the Cabinet?" Miss
Swartz ask.
"that sweet thing from the Cabinet," the sister say.
"wehave have that," reply the misanthrope on the sofa
"I can sing 'Fluvy du Tajy,'" Swartz say, in a meek voice, "if I have
the word." it be the last of the worthy young woman's collection.
"o, 'Fleuve du Tage,'" Miss Maria cry; "we have the song," and go
off to fetch the book in which it be.
now it happen that this song, then in the height of the fashion, have
be give to the young lady by a young friend of theirs, whose name
be on the title, and Miss Swartz, have conclude the ditty with
George's applause (for he remember that it be a favourite of
Amelia's), be hop for an encore perhaps, and fiddle with the
leaf of the music, when her eye fall upon the title, and she see
"Amelia Sedley" write in the comer.
"Lor!" cry Miss Swartz, spin swiftly round on the music-stool,
"be it my Amelia? Amelia that be at Miss P.'s at Hammersmith? I know
it be. itbe she, and-- tell I about she--where be she?"
"donot mention she," Miss Maria Osborne say hastily. "her family have
disgrace itself. her father cheat Papa, and as for she, she be
never to be mention here." this be Miss Maria's return for George's
rudeness about the Battle of Prague.
"be you a friend of Amelia's?" George say, bounce up. "God bless
you for it, Miss Swartz. donot believe what the girl say. SHEbe not
to blame at any rate. shebe the best--"
"you know yoube not to speak about she, George," cry Jane. "papa
forbid it."
"whobe to prevent I?" George cry out. "I will speak of she. I say
shebe the good, the kind, the gentle, the sweetest girl in
England; and that, bankrupt or no, my sister be not fit to hold
candle to she. if you like she, go and see she, Miss Swartz; she
want friend now; and I say, God bless everybody who befriend she.
anybody who speak kindly of she be my friend; anybody who speak
against she be my enemy. thank you, Miss Swartz"; and he go up and
wrung her hand.
"George! George!" one of the sister cry imploringly.
"I say," George say fiercely, "I thank everybody who love Amelia
Sed--" he stop. Old Osborne be in the room with a face livid with
rage, and eye like hot coal.
though George have stop in his sentence, yet, his blood be up, he
be not to be cow by all the generation of Osborne; rally
instantly, he reply to the bullying look of his father, with another
so indicative of resolution and defiance that the eld man quail in
his turn, and look away. he feel that the tussle be come. "Mrs.
Haggistoun, let I take you down to dinner," he say. "give your arm to
Miss Swartz, George," and they march.
"Miss Swartz, I love Amelia, and wehave be engage almost all our
life," Osborne say to his partner; and during all the dinner, George
rattle on with a volubility which surprise himself, and make his
father doubly nervous for the fight which be to take place as soon as
the lady be go.
the difference between the pair be, that while the father be violent
and a bully, the son have thrice the nerve and courage of the parent,
and could not merely make an attack, but resist it; and find that
the moment be now come when the contest between he and his father be
to be decide, he take his dinner with perfect coolness and appetite
before the engagement begin. Old Osborne, on the contrary, be
nervous, and drink much. he flounder in his conversation with the
lady, his neighbour: George's coolness only render he more
angry. it make he half mad to see the calm way in which George,
flap his napkin, and with a swaggering bow, open the door for the
lady to leave the room; and fill himself a glass of wine, smack
it, and look his father full in the face, as if to say, "gentleman of
the Guard, fire first." the old man also take a supply of ammunition,
but his decanter clink against the glass as he try to fill it.
after give a great heave, and with a purple choke face, he then
begin. "how dare you, sir, mention that person's name before Miss
Swartz to-day, in my drawing-room? I ask you, sir, how dare you do it?"
"stop, sir," say George, "donot say dare, sir. dare benot a word to
be use to a Captain in the British Army."
"I shall say what I like to my son, sir. I can cut he off with a
shilling if I like. I can make he a beggar if I like. I will say what
I like," the elder say.
"Ibe a gentleman though I be your son, sir," George answer haughtily.
"any communication which you have to make to I, or any order which
you may please to give, I beg may be couch in that kind of language
which I be accustomed to hear."
whenever the lad assume his haughty manner, it always create either
great awe or great irritation in the parent. Old Osborne stand in
secret terror of his son as a well gentleman than himself; and
perhaps my reader may have remark in their experience of this Vanity
Fair of our, that there be no character which a low-minded man so much
mistrust as that of a gentleman.
"my father donot give I the education you have have, nor the
advantage you have have, nor the money you have have. if I have keep the
company some folk have have through MY MEANS, perhaps my son wouldnot
have any reason to brag, sir, of his SUPERIORITY and WEST END AIRS
(these word be utter in the eld Osborne's most sarcastic tone).
but it benot consider the part of a gentleman, in MY time, for a man
to insult his father. if Ihave do any such thing, mine would have
kick I downstairs, sir."
"I never insult you, sir. I say I beg you to remember your son
be a gentleman as well as yourself. I know very well that you give I
plenty of money," say George (finger a bundle of note which he have
get in the morning from Mr. Chopper). "you tell it I often enough,
sir. therebe no fear of my forget it."
"I wish youwould remember other thing as well, sir," the sire answer.
"I wish youwould remember that in this house--so long as you choose to
honour it with your COMPANY, Captain--I'm the master, and that name,
and that that--that you--that I say--"
"that what, sir?" George ask, with scarcely a sneer, fill another
glass of claret.
"----!" burst out his father with a scream oath--"that the name of
those Sedleys never be mention here, sir--not one of the whole damned
lot of them, sir."
"it benot I, sir, that introduce Miss Sedley's name. it be my
sister who speak ill of she to Miss Swartz; and by Jove Iwill defend
she wherever I go. nobody shall speak lightly of that name in my
presence. our family have do she quite enough injury already, I
think, and may leave off revile she now shebe down. Iwill shoot any
man but you who say a word against she."
"go on, sir, go on," the old gentleman say, his eye start out of
his head.
"go on about what, sir? about the way in which wehave treat that angel
of a girl? who tell I to love she? it be your doing. I might have
choose elsewhere, and look high, perhaps, than your society: but I
obey you. and now that her heart's mine you give I order to fle
it away, and punish she, kill she perhaps--for the fault of other
people. itbe a shame, by Heavens," say George, work himself up
into passion and enthusiasm as he proceed, "to play at fast and loose
with a young girl's affection--and with such an angel as that--one so
superior to the people amongst whom she live, that she might have
excited envy, only she be so good and gentle, that itbe a wonder
anybody dare to hate she. if I desert she, sir, do you suppose she
forget I?"
"I benot go to have any of this dam sentimental nonsense and humbug
here, sir," the father cry out. "there shall be no beggar-marriage
in my family. if you choose to fle away eight thousand a year, which
you may have for the asking, you may do it: but by Jove you take your
pack and walk out of this house, sir. will you do as I tell you, once
for all, sir, or will you not?"
"marry that mulatto woman?" George say, pull up his shirt-collar.
"I donot like the colour, sir. ask the black that sweep opposite
Fleet Market, sir. Ibe not go to marry a Hottentot Venus."
Mr. Osborne pull frantically at the cord by which he be accustom
to summon the butler when he want wine--and almost black in the face,
order that functionary to call a coach for Captain Osborne.
"Ihave do it," say George, come into the slaughter' an hour
afterwards, look very pale.
"what, my boy?" say Dobbin.
George tell what have pass between his father and himself.
"Iwill marry she to-morrow," he say with an oath. "I love she more
every day, Dobbin."
CHAPTER XXII
a Marriage and Part of a Honeymoon
Enemies the most obstinate and courageous cannot hold out against
starvation; so the elder Osborne feel himself pretty easy about his
adversary in the encounter we have just describe; and as soon as
George's supply fall short, confidently expect his unconditional
submission. it be unlucky, to be sure, that the lad should have
secure a stock of provision on the very day when the first encounter
take place; but this relief be only temporary, old Osborne thought,
and would but delay George's surrender. no communication pass
between father and son for some day. the former be sulky at this
silence, but not disquiet; for, as he say, he know where he could
put the screw upon George, and only wait the result of that
operation. he tell the sister the upshot of the dispute between they,
but order they to take no notice of the matter, and welcome George on
his return as if nothing have happen. his cover be lay as usual
every day, and perhaps the old gentleman rather anxiously expect he;
but he never come. some one inquire at the slaughter' regard he,
where it be say that he and his friend Captain Dobbin have leave town.
one gusty, raw day at the end of April--the rain whip the pavement
of that ancient street where the old Slaughters' Coffee-house be once
situate--George Osborne come into the coffee-room, look very
haggard and pale; although dress rather smartly in a blue coat and
brass button, and a neat buff waistcoat of the fashion of those day.
here be his friend Captain Dobbin, in blue and brass too, have
abandon the military frock and french-grey trouser, which be the
usual covering of his lanky person.
Dobbin have be in the coffee-room for an hour or more. he have try
all the paper, but could not read they. he have look at the clock
many score of time; and at the street, where the rain be pattering
down, and the people as they clink by in patten, leave long
reflection on the shin stone: he tattoo at the table: he bite his
nail most completely, and nearly to the quick (he be accustom to
ornament his great big hand in this way): he balance the tea-spoon
dexterously on the milk jug: upset it, &c., &c.; and in fact show
those sign of disquietude, and practise those desperate attempt at
amusement, which man be accustom to employ when very anxious, and
expectant, and perturb in mind.
some of his comrade, gentleman who use the room, joke he about the
splendour of his costume and his agitation of manner. one ask he if
he be go to be marry? Dobbin laugh, and say he would send his
acquaintance (Major Wagstaff of the Engineers) a piece of cake when
that event take place. at length Captain Osborne make his appearance,
very smartly dress, but very pale and agitated as we have say. he
wipe his pale face with a large yellow bandanna pocket-handkerchief
that be prodigiously scented. he shake hand with Dobbin, look at
the clock, and tell John, the waiter, to bring he some curacao. of
this cordial he swallow off a couple of glass with nervous
eagerness. his friend ask with some interest about his health.
"couldnot get a wink of sleep till daylight, Dob," say he. "infernal
headache and fever. get up at nine, and go down to the Hummums for a
bath. I say, dob, I feel just as I do on the morning I go out with
Rocket at Quebec."
"so do I," William respond. "I be a deuced deal more nervous than
you be that morning. you make a famous breakfast, I remember. eat
something now."
"yoube a good old fellow, Will. Iwill drink your health, old boy, and
farewell to--"
"no, no; two glass be enough," Dobbin interrupt he. "here, take
away the liqueur, John. have some cayenne-pepper with your fowl.
make haste though, for it be time we be there."
it be about half an hour from twelve when this brief meeting and
colloquy take place between the two captain. a coach, into which
Captain Osborne's servant put his master's desk and dressing-case, have
be in wait for some time; and into this the two gentleman hurry
under an umbrella, and the valet mount on the box, curse the rain
and the dampness of the coachman who be steam beside he. "we
shall find a well trap than this at the church-door," say he;
"thatbe a comfort." and the carriage drive on, take the road down
Piccadilly, where Apsley House and St. George's Hospital wear red
jacket still; where there be oil-lamp; where Achilles be not yet
bear; nor the Pimlico arch raise; nor the hideous equestrian monster
which pervade it and the neighbourhood; and so they drive down by
Brompton to a certain chapel near the Fulham Road there.
a chariot be in wait with four horse; likewise a coach of the kind
call glass coach. only a very few idler be collect on account
of the dismal rain.
"hang it!" say George, "I say only a pair."
"my master would have four," say Mr. Joseph Sedley's servant, who be
in waiting; and he and Mr. Osborne's man agree as they follow George
and William into the church, that it be a "reg'lar shabby turn hout;
and with scarce so much as a breakfast or a wedding faviour."
"here you be," say our old friend, Jos Sedley, come forward.
"yoube five minute late, George, my boy. what a day, eh? Demmy, itbe
like the commencement of the rainy season in Bengal. but youwill find
my carriage be watertight. come along, my mother and Emmy be in the
vestry."
Jos Sedley be splendid. he be fat than ever. his shirt collar
be high; his face be redder; his shirt-frill flaunt gorgeously
out of his variegate waistcoat. varnish boot be not invent as
yet; but the Hessians on his beautiful leg shine so, that they must
have be the identical pair in which the gentleman in the old picture
use to shave himself; and on his light green coat there bloom a fine
wedding favour, like a great white spread magnolia.
in a word, George have throw the great cast. he be go to be
marry. hence his pallor and nervousness--his sleepless night and
agitation in the morning. I have hear people who have go through
the same thing own to the same emotion. after three or four
ceremony, you get accustomed to it, no doubt; but the first dip,
everybody allow, be awful.
the bride be dress in a brown silk pelisse (as Captain Dobbin have
since inform I), and wear a straw bonnet with a pink ribbon; over
the bonnet she have a veil of white Chantilly lace, a gift from Mr.
Joseph Sedley, her brother. Captain Dobbin himself have ask leave to
present she with a gold chain and watch, which she sport on this
occasion; and her mother give she her diamond brooch--almost the only
trinket which be leave to the old lady. as the service go on, Mrs.
Sedley sit and whimper a great deal in a pew, console by the Irish
maid-servant and Mrs. Clapp from the lodging. Old Sedley would not be
present. Jos act for his father, give away the bride, whilst
Captain Dobbin step up as groomsman to his friend George.
there be nobody in the church besides the officiating person and the
small marriage party and their attendant. the two valet sit aloof
superciliously. the rain come rattle down on the window. in the
interval of the service you hear it, and the sobbing of old Mrs.
Sedley in the pew. the parson's tone echo sadly through the empty
wall. Osborne's "I will" be sound in very deep bass. Emmy's
response come flutter up to her lip from her heart, but be
scarcely hear by anybody except Captain Dobbin.
when the service be complete, Jos Sedley come forward and kiss his
sister, the bride, for the first time for many month--George's look of
gloom have go, and he seem quite proud and radiant. "itbe your turn,
William," say he, put his hand fondly upon Dobbin's shoulder; and
Dobbin go up and touch Amelia on the cheek.
then they go into the vestry and sign the register. "God bless you,
Old Dobbin," George say, grasp he by the hand, with something very
like moisture glisten in his eye. William reply only by nod
his head. his heart be too full to say much.
"write directly, and come down as soon as you can, you know," Osborne
say. after Mrs. Sedley have take an hysterical adieu of her daughter,
the pair go off to the carriage. "get out of the way, you little
devil," George cry to a small crowd of damp urchin, that be
hang about the chapel-door. the rain drive into the bride and
bridegroom's face as they pass to the chariot. the postilion'
favour draggle on their dripping jacket. the few child make a
dismal cheer, as the carriage, splash mud, drive away.
William Dobbin stand in the church-porch, look at it, a queer
figure. the small crew of spectator jeered he. he be not think
about they or their laughter.
"come home and have some tiffin, Dobbin," a voice cry behind he; as
a pudgy hand be lay on his shoulder, and the honest fellow's reverie
be interrupt. but the Captain have no heart to go a-feasting with
Jos Sedley. he put the weep old lady and her attendant into the
carriage along with Jos, and leave they without any farth word
pass. this carriage, too, drive away, and the urchin give another
sarcastical cheer.
"here, you little beggar," Dobbin say, give some sixpence amongst
they, and then go off by himself through the rain. it be all over.
they be marry, and happy, he pray God. never since he be a boy
have he feel so miserable and so lonely. he long with a heart-sick
yearning for the first few day to be over, that he might see she again.
some ten day after the above ceremony, three young man of our
acquaintance be enjoy that beautiful prospect of bow window on
the one side and blue sea on the other, which Brighton afford to the
traveller. sometimes it be towards the ocean--smile with countless
dimple, speckle with white sail, with a hundred bathing-machine
kiss the skirt of his blue garment--that the Londoner look
enrapture: sometimes, on the contrary, a lover of human nature rather
than of prospect of any kind, it be towards the bow window that he
turn, and that swarm of human life which they exhibit. from one issue
the note of a piano, which a young lady in ringlet practise six
hour daily, to the delight of the fellow-lodger: at another, lovely
Polly, the nurse-maid, may be see dandle Master Omnium in her arm:
whilst Jacob, his papa, be beheld eat prawn, and devour the
Times for breakfast, at the window below. yonder be the Misses Leery,
who be look out for the young officer of the Heavies, who be
pretty sure to be pace the cliff; or again it be a City man, with a
nautical turn, and a telescope, the size of a six-pounder, who have his
instrument pointed seaward, so as to command every pleasure-boat,
herring-boat, or bathing-machine that come to, or quit, the shore,
&c., &c. but have we any leisure for a description of Brighton?--for
Brighton, a clean Naples with genteel lazzaroni--for Brighton, that
always look brisk, gay, and gaudy, like a harlequin's jacket--for
Brighton, which use to be seven hour distant from London at the time
of our story; which be now only a hundred minute off; and which may
approach who know how much near, unless Joinville come and untimely
bombard it?
"what a monstrous fine girl that be in the lodging over the
milliner's," one of these three promenader remark to the other;
"Gad, Crawley, do you see what a wink she give I as I pass?"
"donot break her heart, Jos, you rascal," say another. "donot trifle
with her affection, you Don Juan!"
"get away," say Jos Sedley, quite pleased, and leer up at the
maid-servant in question with a most killing ogle. Jos be even more
splendid at Brighton than he have be at his sister's marriage. he have
brilliant under-waistcoat, any one of which would have set up a
moderate buck. he sport a military frock-coat, ornament with frog,
knob, black button, and meander embroidery. he have affect a
military appearance and habit of late; and he walk with his two
friend, who be of that profession, clink his boot-spur,
swagger prodigiously, and shoot death-glance at all the servant
girl who be worthy to be slay.
"what shall we do, boy, till the lady return?" the buck ask. the
lady be out to Rottingdean in his carriage on a drive.
"letus have a game at billiard," one of his friend say--the tall
one, with lacquered mustachio.
"no, dammy; no, Captain," Jos reply, rather alarmed. "no billiard
to-day, Crawley, my boy; yesterday be enough."
"you play very well," say Crawley, laugh. "donot he, Osborne? how
well he make that-five stroke, eh?"
"famous," Osborne say. "Jos be a devil of a fellow at billiard, and
at everything else, too. I wish there be any tiger-hunting about
here! we might go and kill a few before dinner. (there go a fine
girl! what an ankle, eh, Jos?) tell we that story about the tiger-hunt,
and the way you do for he in the jungle--itbe a wonderful story that,
Crawley." here George Osborne give a yawn. "itbe rather slow work,"
say he, "down here; what shall we do?"
"shall we go and look at some horse that Snaffler's just bring from
Lewes fair?" Crawley say.
"suppose we go and have some jelly at Dutton's," and the rogue Jos,
willing to kill two bird with one stone. "devilish fine gal at
Dutton's."
"suppose we go and see the Lightning come in, itbe just about time?"
George say. this advice prevail over the stable and the jelly,
they turn towards the coach-office to witness the Lightning's arrival.
as they pass, they meet the carriage--Jos Sedley's open carriage, with
its magnificent armorial bearing--that splendid conveyance in which he
use to drive, about at Cheltonham, majestic and solitary, with his
arm fold, and his hat cock; or, more happy, with lady by his
side.
two be in the carriage now: one a little person, with light hair, and
dress in the height of the fashion; the other in a brown silk
pelisse, and a straw bonnet with pink ribbon, with a rosy, round,
happy face, that do you good to behold. she check the carriage as
it near the three gentleman, after which exercise of authority she
look rather nervous, and then begin to blush most absurdly. "we have
have a delightful drive, George," she say, "and--and webe so glad to
come back; and, Joseph, donot let he be late."
"donot be lead our husband into mischief, Mr. Sedley, you wicke,
wicked man you," rebecca say, shake at Jos a pretty little finger
cover with the neat french kid glove. "no billiard, no smoking,
no naughtiness!"
"my dear Mrs. Crawley--ah now! upon my honour!" be all Jos could
ejaculate by way of reply; but he manage to fall into a tolerable
attitude, with his head lie on his shoulder, grin upwards at his
victim, with one hand at his back, which he support on his cane, and
the other hand (the one with the diamond ring) fumbling in his
shirt-frill and among his under-waistcoat. as the carriage drive off
he kiss the diamond hand to the fair lady within. he wish all
Cheltenham, all Chowringhee, all Calcutta, could see he in that
position, wave his hand to such a beauty, and in company with such a
famous buck as Rawdon Crawley of the Guards.
our young bride and bridegroom have choose Brighton as the place where
they would pass the first few day after their marriage; and have
engage apartment at the Ship Inn, enjoy themselves there in great
comfort and quietude, until Jos presently join they. nor be he the
only companion they find there. as they be come into the hotel
from a sea-side walk one afternoon, on whom should they light but
Rebecca and her husband. the recognition be immediate. rebecca fly
into the arm of her dear friend. Crawley and Osborne shake hand
together cordially enough: and Becky, in the course of a very few
hour, find mean to make the latter forget that little unpleasant
passage of word which have happen between they. "do you remember the
last time we meet at Miss Crawley's, when I be so rude to you, dear
Captain Osborne? I think you seem careless about dear Amelia. it
be that make I angry: and so pert: and so unkind: and so ungrateful.
do forgive I!" rebecca say, and she hold out her hand with so frank
and win a grace, that Osborne could not but take it. by humbly and
frankly acknowledge yourself to be in the wrong, there be no knowing,
my son, what good you may do. I know once a gentleman and very worthy
practitioner in Vanity Fair, who use to do little wrong to his
neighbour on purpose, and in order to apologise for they in an open
and manly way afterwards--and what ensue? my friend Crocky Doyle be
like everywhere, and deem to be rather impetuous--but the honest
fellow. Becky's humility pass for sincerity with George Osborne.
these two young couple have plenty of tale to relate to each other.
the marriage of either be discuss; and their prospect in life
canvass with the great frankness and interest on both side.
George's marriage be to be make known to his father by his friend
Captain Dobbin; and young Osborne tremble rather for the result of
that communication. Miss Crawley, on whom all Rawdon's hope depend,
still hold out. unable to make an entry into her house in Park Lane,
her affectionate nephew and niece have follow she to Brighton, where
they have emissary continually plant at her door.
"I wish you could see some of Rawdon's friend who be always about our
door," rebecca say, laugh. "do you ever see a dun, my dear; or a
bailiff and his man? two of the abominable wretch watch all last
week at the greengrocer's opposite, and we could not get away until
Sunday. if Aunty do not relent, what shall we do?"
Rawdon, with roar of laughter, relate a dozen amusing anecdote of
his dun, and Rebecca's adroit treatment of they. he vow with a
great oath that there be no woman in Europe who could talk a creditor
over as she could. almost immediately after their marriage, her
practice have begin, and her husband find the immense value of such a
wife. they have credit in plenty, but they have bill also in abundance,
and labour under a scarcity of ready money. do these
debt-difficulty affect Rawdon's good spirit? no. everybody in
Vanity Fair must have remark how well those live who be comfortably
and thoroughly in debt: how they deny themselves nothing; how jolly and
easy they be in their mind. Rawdon and his wife have the very good
apartment at the inn at Brighton; the landlord, as he bring in the
first dish, bow before they as to his great customer: and Rawdon
abuse the dinner and wine with an audacity which no grandee in the
land could surpass. long custom, a manly appearance, faultless boot
and clothe, and a happy fierceness of manner, will often help a man as
much as a great balance at the banker's.
the two wedding party meet constantly in each other's apartment.
after two or three night the gentleman of an evening have a little
piquet, as their wife sate and chat apart. this pastime, and the
arrival of Jos Sedley, who make his appearance in his grand open
carriage, and who play a few game at billiard with Captain Crawley,
replenish Rawdon's purse somewhat, and give he the benefit of that
ready money for which the great spirit be sometimes at a
stand-still.
so the three gentleman walk down to see the Lightning coach come in.
Punctual to the minute, the coach crowd inside and out, the guard
blow his accustomed tune on the horn--the lightning come tear
down the street, and pull up at the coach-office.
"Hullo! therebe old Dobbin," George cry, quite delighted to see his
old friend perch on the roof; and whose promise visit to Brighton
have be delay until now. "how be you, old fellow? glad yoube come
down. Emmy'll be delighted to see you," Osborne say, shake his
comrade warmly by the hand as soon as his descent from the vehicle be
effect--and then he add, in a low and agitated voice, "whatbe the
news? have you be in Russell Square? what do the governor say?
tell I everything."
Dobbin look very pale and grave. "Ihave see your father," say he.
"howbe Amelia--Mrs. George? Iwill tell you all the news presently: but
Ihave bring the great news of all: and that is--"
"out with it, old fellow," George say.
"webe order to Belgium. all the army go--guard and all.
Heavytop's get the gout, and be mad at not be able to move. O'Dowd
go in command, and we embark from Chatham next week." this news of
war could not but come with a shock upon our lover, and cause all
these gentleman to look very serious.
CHAPTER XXIII
Captain Dobbin Proceeds on his Canvass
what be the secret mesmerism which friendship possess, and under the
operation of which a person ordinarily sluggish, or cold, or timid,
become wise, active, and resolute, in another's behalf? as Alexis,
after a few pass from Dr. Elliotson, despise pain, read with the
back of his head, see mile off, look into next week, and perform
other wonder, of which, in his own private normal condition, he be
quite incapable; so you see, in the affair of the world and under the
magnetism of friendship, the modest man become bold, the shy
confident, the lazy active, or the impetuous prudent and peaceful.
what be it, on the other hand, that make the lawyer eschew his own
cause, and call in his learn brother as an adviser? and what cause
the doctor, when ail, to send for his rival, and not sit down and
examine his own tongue in the chimney Bass, or write his own
prescription at his study-table? I throw out these query for
intelligent reader to answer, who know, at once, how credulous we be,
and how sceptical, how soft and how obstinate, how firm for other and
how diffident about ourselves: meanwhile, it be certain that our
friend William Dobbin, who be personally of so comply a disposition
that if his parent have press he much, it be probable he would have
step down into the kitchen and marry the cook, and who, to far
his own interest, would have find the most insuperable difficulty in
walk across the street, find himself as busy and eager in the
conduct of George Osborne's affair, as the most selfish tactician
could be in the pursuit of his own.
whilst our friend George and his young wife be enjoy the first
blushing day of the honeymoon at Brighton, honest William be leave as
George's plenipotentiary in London, to transact all the business part
of the marriage. his duty it be to call upon old Sedley and his wife,
and to keep the former in good humour: to draw Jos and his
brother-in-law nearer together, so that Jos's position and dignity, as
collector of Boggley Wollah, might compensate for his father's loss of
station, and tend to reconcile old Osborne to the alliance: and
finally, to communicate it to the latter in such a way as should least
irritate the old gentleman.
now, before he face the head of the Osborne house with the news which
it be his duty to tell, Dobbin bethink he that it would be politic
to make friend of the rest of the family, and, if possible, have the
lady on his side. they cannot be angry in their heart, think he.
no woman ever be really angry at a romantic marriage. a little cry
out, and they must come round to their brother; when the three of we
will lay siege to old Mr. Osborne. so this machiavellian captain of
infantry cast about he for some happy mean or stratagem by which he
could gently and gradually bring the Misses Osborne to a knowledge of
their brother's secret.
by a little inquiry regard his mother's engagement, he be pretty
soon able to find out by whom of her ladyship's friend party be
give at that season; where he would be likely to meet Osborne's
sister; and, though he have that abhorrence of rout and evening
party which many sensible man, alas! entertain, he soon find one
where the Misses Osborne be to be present. make his appearance at
the ball, where he dance a couple of set with both of they, and be
prodigiously polite, he actually have the courage to ask Miss Osborne
for a few minute' conversation at an early hour the next day, when he
have, he say, to communicate to her news of the very great interest.
what be it that make she start back, and gaze upon he for a moment,
and then on the ground at her foot, and make as if she would faint on
his arm, have he not by opportunely treading on her toe, bring the
young lady back to self-control? why be she so violently agitated at
Dobbin's request? this can never be know. but when he come the next
day, Maria be not in the drawing-room with her sister, and Miss Wirt
go off for the purpose of fetch the latter, and the Captain and
Miss Osborne be leave together. they be both so silent that the
ticktock of the Sacrifice of Iphigenia clock on the mantelpiece become
quite rudely audible.
"what a nice party it be last night," Miss Osborne at length begin,
encouragingly; "and--and how yoube improve in your dancing, Captain
Dobbin. surely somebody have teach you," she add, with amiable
archness.
"you should see I dance a reel with Mrs. Major O'Dowd of our; and a
jig--do you ever see a jig? but I think anybody could dance with you,
Miss Osborne, who dance so well."
"be the Major's lady young and beautiful, Captain?" the fair questioner
continue. "ah, what a terrible thing it must be to be a soldier's
wife! I wonder they have any spirit to dance, and in these dreadful
time of war, too! o Captain Dobbin, I tremble sometimes when I think
of our dear George, and the danger of the poor soldier. be there
many married officer of the --th, Captain Dobbin?"
"upon my word, shebe play her hand rather too openly," Miss Wirt
thought; but this observation be merely parenthetic, and be not hear
through the crevice of the door at which the governess utter it.
"one of our young man be just marry," Dobbin say, now come to the
point. "it be a very old attachment, and the young couple be as poor
as church mouse." "o, how delightful! o, how romantic!" Miss Osborne
cry, as the Captain say "old attachment" and "poor." her sympathy
encourage he.
"the fine young fellow in the regiment," he continue. "not a braver
or handsomer officer in the army; and such a charming wife! how you
would like she! how you will like she when you know she, Miss
Osborne." the young lady think the actual moment have arrive, and
that Dobbin's nervousness which now come on and be visible in many
twitching of his face, in his manner of beat the ground with his
great foot, in the rapid buttoning and unbuttoning of his frock-coat,
&c.--Miss Osborne, I say, think that when he have give himself a
little air, he would unbosom himself entirely, and prepare eagerly to
listen. and the clock, in the altar on which Iphigenia be situate,
beginning, after a preparatory convulsion, to toll twelve, the mere
tolling seem as if it would last until one--so prolonged be the
knell to the anxious spinster.
"but itbe not about marriage that I come to speak--that be that
marriage--that be--no, I mean--my dear Miss Osborne, itbe about our
dear friend George," Dobbin say.
"about George?" she say in a tone so discomfit that Maria and Miss
Wirt laugh at the other side of the door, and even that abandon
wretch of a Dobbin feel inclined to smile himself; for he be not
altogether unconscious of the state of affair: George have often
banter he gracefully and say, "hang it, will, why donot you take
old Jane? shewill have you if you ask she. Iwill bet you five to two she
will."
"yes, about George, then," he continue. "there have be a difference
between he and Mr. Osborne. and I regard he so much--for you know
we have be like brother--that I hope and pray the quarrel may be
settle. we must go abroad, Miss Osborne. we may be order off at a
day's warning. who know what may happen in the campaign? donot be
agitated, dear Miss Osborne; and those two at least should part
friend."
"there have be no quarrel, Captain Dobbin, except a little usual scene
with papa," the lady say. "we be expect George back daily. what
Papa want be only for his good. he have but to come back, and Ibe
sure all will be well; and dear Rhoda, who go away from here in sad
sad anger, I know will forgive he. woman forgive but too readily,
Captain."
