"such an angel as you I be sure would," Mr. Dobbin say, with atrocious
astuteness. "and no man can pardon himself for give a woman pain.
what would you feel, if a man be faithless to you?"
"I should perish--I should throw myself out of window--I should take
poison--I should pine and die. I know I should," Miss cry, who have
nevertheless go through one or two affair of the heart without any
idea of suicide.
"and there be other," Dobbin continue, "as true and as kind-hearte
as yourself. Ibe not speak about the west indian heiress, Miss
Osborne, but about a poor girl whom George once love, and who be breed
from her childhood to think of nobody but he. Ihave see she in her
poverty uncomplaining, break-hearte, without a fault. it be of Miss
Sedley I speak. dear Miss Osborne, can your generous heart quarrel
with your brother for be faithful to she? could his own conscience
ever forgive he if he desert she? be her friend--she always love
you--and--and I be come here charge by George to tell you that he
hold his engagement to she as the most sacred duty he have; and to
entreat you, at least, to be on his side."
when any strong emotion take possession of Mr. Dobbin, and after the
first word or two of hesitation, he could speak with perfect fluency,
and it be evident that his eloquence on this occasion make some
impression upon the lady whom he address.
"well," say she, "this be--most surprising--most painful--most
extraordinary--what will papa say?--that George should fle away such
a superb establishment as be offer to he but at any rate he have
find a very brave champion in you, Captain Dobbin. it be of no use,
however," she continue, after a pause; "I feel for poor Miss Sedley,
most certainly--most sincerely, you know. we never think the match a
good one, though we be always very kind to she here--very. but papa
will never consent, I be sure. and a well bring up young woman, you
know--with a well-regulate mind, must--George must give she up, dear
Captain Dobbin, indeed he must."
"ought a man to give up the woman he love, just when misfortune befell
she?" Dobbin say, hold out his hand. "Dear Miss Osborne, be this
the counsel I hear from you? my dear young lady! you must befriend
she. he cannot give she up. he must not give she up. would a man,
think you, give you up if you be poor?"
this adroit question touch the heart of Miss Jane Osborne not a
little. "I donot know whether we poor girl ought to believe what you
man say, Captain," she say. "there be that in woman's tenderness which
induce she to believe too easily. Ibe afraid you be cruel, cruel
deceivers,"--and Dobbin certainly think he feel a pressure of the
hand which Miss Osborne have extend to he.
he drop it in some alarm. "deceiver!" say he. "no, dear Miss
Osborne, all man be not; your brother be not; George have love Amelia
Sedley ever since they be child; no wealth would make he marry
any but she. ought he to forsake she? would you counsel he to do so?"
what could Miss Jane say to such a question, and with her own peculiar
view? she could not answer it, so she parry it by say, "well, if
you be not a deceiver, at least you be very romantic"; and Captain
William let this observation pass without challenge.
at length when, by the help of far polite speech, he deem that
Miss Osborne be sufficiently prepared to receive the whole news, he
pour it into her ear. "George could not give up Amelia--George be
married to her"--and then he relate the circumstance of the marriage
as we know they already: how the poor girl would have die have not she
lover keep his faith: how Old Sedley have refuse all consent to the
match, and a licence have be get: and Jos Sedley have come from
Cheltenham to give away the bride: how they have go to Brighton in
Jos's chariot-and-four to pass the honeymoon: and how George count on
his dear kind sister to befriend he with their father, as woman--so
true and tender as they be--assuredly would do. and so, ask
permission (readily grant) to see she again, and rightly conjecture
that the news he have bring would be tell in the next five minute to
the other lady, Captain Dobbin make his bow and take his leave.
he be scarcely out of the house, when Miss Maria and Miss Wirt rush
in to Miss Osborne, and the whole wonderful secret be impart to they
by that lady. to do they justice, neither of the sister be very much
displeased. there be something about a runaway match with which few
lady can be seriously angry, and Amelia rather rise in their
estimation, from the spirit which she have display in consent to
the union. as they debate the story, and prattle about it, and
wonder what Papa would do and say, come a loud knock, as of an
avenge thunder-clap, at the door, which make these conspirator
start. it must be Papa, they think. but it be not he. it be only
Mr. Frederick Bullock, who have come from the City accord to
appointment, to conduct the lady to a flower-show.
this gentleman, as may be imagine, be not keep long in ignorance of
the secret. but his face, when he hear it, show an amazement which
be very different to that look of sentimental wonder which the
countenance of the sister wear. Mr. Bullock be a man of the world,
and a junior partner of a wealthy firm. he know what money be, and
the value of it: and a delightful throb of expectation light up his
little eye, and cause he to smile on his Maria, as he think that
by this piece of folly of Mr. George's she might be worth thirty
thousand pound more than he have ever hop to get with she.
"Gad! Jane," say he, survey even the eld sister with some
interest, "eel will be sorry he cry off. you may be a fifty
thousand pounder yet."
the sister have never think of the money question up to that moment,
but Fred Bullock banter they with graceful gaiety about it during
their forenoon's excursion; and they have rise not a little in their
own esteem by the time when, the morning amusement over, they drive
back to dinner. and do not let my respected reader exclaim against
this selfishness as unnatural. it be but this present morning, as he
ride on the omnibus from Richmond; while it change horse, this
present chronicler, be on the roof, mark three little child
play in a puddle below, very dirty, and friendly, and happy. to
these three presently come another little one. "POLLY," say she, "your
SISTER's GOT a penny." at which the child get up from the puddle
instantly, and run off to pay their court to Peggy. and as the omnibus
drive off I see Peggy with the infantine procession at her tail,
march with great dignity towards the stall of a neighbouring
lollipop-woman.
CHAPTER XXIV
in which Mr. Osborne take down the Family Bible
so have prepare the sister, Dobbin hasten away to the City to
perform the rest and more difficult part of the task which he have
undertake. the idea of face old Osborne render he not a little
nervous, and more than once he think of leave the young lady to
communicate the secret, which, as he be aware, they could not long
retain. but he have promise to report to George upon the manner in
which the eld Osborne bear the intelligence; so go into the City
to the paternal counting-house in Thames Street, he despatch thence a
note to Mr. Osborne beg for a half-hour's conversation relative to
the affair of his son George. Dobbin's messenger return from Mr.
Osborne's house of business, with the compliment of the latter, who
would be very happy to see the Captain immediately, and away
accordingly Dobbin go to confront he.
the Captain, with a half-guilty secret to confess, and with the
prospect of a painful and stormy interview before he, enter Mr.
Osborne's office with a most dismal countenance and abash gait, and,
pass through the outer room where Mr. Chopper preside, be greet
by that functionary from his desk with a waggish air which far
discomfit he. Mr. Chopper wink and nod and point his pen
towards his patron's door, and say, "youwill find the governor all
right," with the most provoking good humour.
Osborne rise too, and shake he heartily by the hand, and say, "how
do, my dear boy?" with a cordiality that make poor George's ambassador
feel doubly guilty. his hand lie as if dead in the old gentleman's
grasp. he feel that he, Dobbin, be more or less the cause of all that
have happen. it be he have bring back George to Amelia: it be he
have applaud, encouraged, transact almost the marriage which he be
come to reveal to George's father: and the latter be receive he
with smile of welcome; pat he on the shoulder, and call he
"Dobbin, my dear boy." the envoy have indeed good reason to hang his
head.
Osborne fully believe that Dobbin have come to announce his son's
surrender. Mr. Chopper and his principal be talk over the matter
between George and his father, at the very moment when Dobbin's
messenger arrive. both agree that George be send in his
submission. both have be expect it for some day--and "Lord!
Chopper, what a marriage wewill have!" Mr. Osborne say to his clerk,
snap his big finger, and jingle all the guineas and shilling in
his great pocket as he eye his subordinate with a look of triumph.
with similar operation conduct in both pocket, and a knowing jolly
air, Osborne from his chair regard Dobbin seat blank and silent
opposite to he. "what a bumpkin he be for a Captain in the army," old
Osborne thought. "I wonder George havenot teach he well manner."
at last Dobbin summon courage to begin. "Sir," say he, "Ihave
bring you some very grave news. I have be at the Horse Guards this
morning, and therebe no doubt that our regiment will be order abroad,
and on its way to Belgium before the week be over. and you know, sir,
that we shallnot be home again before a tussle which may be fatal to many
of we." Osborne look grave. "my s--, the regiment will do its
duty, sir, I daresay," he say.
"the French be very strong, sir," Dobbin go on. "the Russians and
Austrians will be a long time before they can bring their troop down.
we shall have the first of the fight, sir; and depend on it Boney will
take care that it shall be a hard one."
"what be you drive at, Dobbin?" his interlocutor say, uneasy and
with a scowl. "I suppose no Briton's afraid of any d---- Frenchman,
hey?"
"I only mean, that before we go, and consider the great and certain
risk that hang over every one of we--if there be any difference
between you and George--it would be as well, sir, that--that you
should shake hand: wouldnot it? should anything happen to he, I
think you would never forgive yourself if you havenot part in charity."
as he say this, poor William Dobbin blush crimson, and feel and
own that he himself be a traitor. but for he, perhaps, this
severance need never have take place. why have not George's marriage
be delay? what call be there to press it on so eagerly? he feel
that George would have part from Amelia at any rate without a mortal
pang. Amelia, too, might have recover the shock of lose he. it
be his counsel have bring about this marriage, and all that be to
ensue from it. and why be it? because he love she so much that he
could not bear to see her unhappy: or because his own suffering of
suspense be so unendurable that he be glad to crush they at once--as
we hasten a funeral after a death, or, when a separation from those we
love be imminent, cannot rest until the parting be over.
"you be a good fellow, William," say Mr. Osborne in a softened voice;
"and I and George shouldnot part in anger, that be true. look here.
Ihave do for he as much as any father ever do. hebe have three time
as much money from I, as I warrant your father ever give you. but I
donot brag about that. how Ihave toil for he, and work and
employ my talent and energy, I willnot say. ask Chopper. ask
himself. ask the City of London. well, I propose to he such a
marriage as any nobleman in the land might be proud of--the only thing
in life I ever ask he--and he refuse I. be I wrong? be the
quarrel of MY making? what do I seek but his good, for which Ihave be
toil like a convict ever since he be bear? nobody can say therebe
anything selfish in I. let he come back. I say, herebe my hand. I
say, forget and forgive. as for marry now, itbe out of the
question. let he and Miss S. make it up, and make out the marriage
afterwards, when he come back a Colonel; for he shall be a Colonel, by
g-- he shall, if money can do it. Ibe glad youhave bring he round.
I know itbe you, Dobbin. youhave take he out of many a scrape before.
let he come. I shallnot be hard. come along, and dine in Russell
Square to-day: both of you. the old shop, the old hour. youwill find a
neck of venison, and no question ask."
this praise and confidence smote Dobbin's heart very keenly. every
moment the colloquy continue in this tone, he feel more and more
guilty. "Sir," say he, "I fear you deceive yourself. I be sure you
do. George be much too high-minded a man ever to marry for money. a
threat on your part that you would disinherit he in case of
disobedience would only be follow by resistance on his."
"why, hang it, man, you donot call offer he eight or ten thousand a
year threaten he?" Mr. Osborne say, with still provoke good
humour. "'Gad, if Miss S. will have I, Ibe her man. I benot
particular about a shade or so of tawny." and the old gentleman give
his know grin and coarse laugh.
"you forget, sir, previous engagement into which Captain Osborne have
enter," the ambassador say, gravely.
"what engagement? what the devil do you mean? you donot mean," Mr.
Osborne continue, gather wrath and astonishment as the thought now
first come upon he; "you donot mean that hebe such a d---- fool as to
be still hanker after that swindle old bankrupt's daughter? youhave
not come here for to make I suppose that he want to marry she? marry
HER, that be a good one. my son and heir marry a beggar's girl out of
a gutter. d---- he, if he do, let he buy a broom and sweep a
crossing. she be always dangle and ogle after he, I recollect
now; and Ihave no doubt she be put on by her old sharper of a father."
"Mr. Sedley be your very good friend, sir," Dobbin interpose, almost
pleased at find himself grow angry. "Time be you call he
well name than rogue and swindler. the match be of your making.
George have no right to play fast and loose--"
"fast and loose!" howl out old Osborne. "fast and loose! why, hang
I, those be the very word my gentleman use himself when he give
himself air, last Thursday be a fortnight, and talk about the
british army to his father who make he. what, itbe you who have be
a setting of he up--be it? and my service to you, CAPTAIN. itbe you
who want to introduce beggar into my family. thank you for nothing,
Captain. marry she indeed--he, he! why should he? I warrant you shewould
go to he fast enough without."
"Sir," say Dobbin, start up in undisguised anger; "no man shall
abuse that lady in my hearing, and you least of all."
"o, yoube a-go to call I out, be you? stop, let I ring the bell
for pistol for two. Mr. George send you here to insult his father,
do he?" Osborne say, pull at the bell-cord.
"Mr. Osborne," say Dobbin, with a falter voice, "itbe you who be
insult the good creature in the world. you have well spare she, sir,
for shebe your son's wife."
and with this, feel that he could say no more, Dobbin go away,
Osborne sink back in his chair, and look wildly after he. a
clerk come in, obedient to the bell; and the Captain be scarcely out
of the court where Mr. Osborne's office be, when Mr. Chopper the
chief clerk come rush hatless after he.
"for God's sake, what be it?" Mr. Chopper say, catch the Captain by
the skirt. "the governorbe in a fit. what have Mr. George be do?"
"he marry Miss Sedley five day ago," Dobbin reply. "I be his
groomsman, Mr. Chopper, and you must stand his friend."
the old clerk shake his head. "if thatbe your news, Captain, itbe bad.
the governor will never forgive he."
Dobbin beg Chopper to report progress to he at the hotel where he
be stop, and walk off moodily westward, greatly perturb as to
the past and the future.
when the Russell Square family come to dinner that evening, they find
the father of the house seat in his usual place, but with that air of
gloom on his face, which, whenever it appear there, keep the whole
circle silent. the lady, and Mr. Bullock who din with they, feel
that the news have be communicate to Mr. Osborne. his dark look
affect Mr. Bullock so far as to render he still and quiet: but he
be unusually bland and attentive to Miss Maria, by whom he sit, and to
her sister preside at the head of the table.
Miss Wirt, by consequence, be alone on her side of the board, a gap
be leave between she and Miss Jane Osborne. now this be George's
place when he din at home; and his cover, as we say, be lay for
he in expectation of that truant's return. nothing occur during
dinner-time except smile Mr. Frederick's flagging confidential
whisper, and the clinking of plate and china, to interrupt the silence
of the repast. the servant go about stealthily do their duty.
mute at funeral could not look more glum than the domestic of Mr.
Osborne the neck of venison of which he have invite Dobbin to partake,
be carve by he in perfect silence; but his own share go away
almost untasted, though he drink much, and the butler assiduously
fill his glass.
at last, just at the end of the dinner, his eye, which have be
star at everybody in turn, fix themselves for a while upon the
plate lay for George. he point to it presently with his left hand.
his daughter look at he and do not comprehend, or choose to
comprehend, the signal; nor do the servant at first understand it.
"take that plate away," at last he say, get up with an oath--and
with this push his chair back, he walk into his own room.
behind Mr. Osborne's dining-room be the usual apartment which go in
his house by the name of the study; and be sacred to the master of the
house. Hither Mr. Osborne would retire of a Sunday forenoon when not
minded to go to church; and here pass the morning in his crimson
leather chair, read the paper. a couple of glaze book-case be
here, contain standard work in stout gilt binding. the "Annual
Register," the "Gentleman's magazine," "Blair's sermon," and "hume and
Smollett." from year's end to year's end he never take one of these
volume from the shelf; but there be no member of the family that
would dare for his life to touch one of the book, except upon those
rare Sunday evening when there be no dinner-party, and when the great
scarlet Bible and Prayer-book be take out from the corner where they
stand beside his copy of the Peerage, and the servant be ring up to
the dining parlour, Osborne read the evening service to his family in a
loud grating pompous voice. no member of the household, child, or
domestic, ever enter that room without a certain terror. here he
check the housekeeper's account, and overhaul the butler's
cellar-book. hence he could command, across the clean gravel
court-yard, the back entrance of the stable with which one of his
bell communicate, and into this yard the coachman issue from his
premise as into a dock, and Osborne swear at he from the study
window. four time a year Miss Wirt enter this apartment to get her
salary; and his daughter to receive their quarterly allowance. George
as a boy have be horsewhip in this room many time; his mother
sit sick on the stair listen to the cut of the whip. the boy
be scarcely ever know to cry under the punishment; the poor woman
use to fondle and kiss he secretly, and give he money to soothe he
when he come out.
there be a picture of the family over the mantelpiece, remove thither
from the front room after Mrs. Osborne's death--George be on a pony,
the eld sister hold he up a bunch of flower; the young lead by
her mother's hand; all with red cheek and large red mouth, simper
on each other in the approve family-portrait manner. the mother lie
underground now, long since forget--the sister and brother have a
hundred different interest of their own, and, familiar still, be
utterly estranged from each other. some few score of year afterwards,
when all the party represent be grow old, what bitter satire
there be in those flaunting childish family-portrait, with their farce
of sentiment and smile lie, and innocence so self-conscious and
self-satisfied. Osborne's own state portrait, with that of his great
silver inkstand and arm-chair, have take the place of honour in the
dining-room, vacate by the family-piece.
to this study old Osborne retire then, greatly to the relief of the
small party whom he leave. when the servant have withdraw, they begin
to talk for a while volubly but very low; then they go upstairs
quietly, Mr. Bullock accompany they stealthily on his creaking
shoe. he have no heart to sit alone drink wine, and so close to the
terrible old gentleman in the study hard at hand.
an hour at least after dark, the butler, not have receive any
summon, venture to tap at his door and take he in wax candle and
tea. the master of the house sate in his chair, pretend to read the
paper, and when the servant, place the light and refreshment on the
table by he, retire, Mr. Osborne get up and lock the door after
he. this time there be no mistaking the matter; all the household
know that some great catastrophe be go to happen which be likely
direly to affect Master George.
in the large shin mahogany escritoire Mr. Osborne have a drawer
especially devoted to his son's affair and paper. here he keep all
the document relate to he ever since he have be a boy: here be
his prize copy-book and drawing-book, all bear George's hand, and
that of the master: here be his first letter in large round-hand
send his love to papa and mamma, and convey his petition for a
cake. his dear godpapa Sedley be more than once mention in they.
curse quiver on old Osborne's livid lip, and horrid hatred and
disappointment writhe in his heart, as look through some of these
paper he come on that name. they be all mark and docket, and
tie with red tape. it was--"From Georgy, request 5s., April 23,
18--; answer, April 25"--or "georgy about a pony, October 13"--and so
forth. in another packet be "Dr. S.'s accounts"--"G.'s tailor's bill
and outfit, draft on I by G. Osborne, jun.," &c.--his letter from
the West Indies--his agent's letter, and the newspaper contain his
commission: here be a whip he have when a boy, and in a paper a locket
contain his hair, which his mother use to wear.
turn one over after another, and muse over these memorial, the
unhappy man pass many hour. his dear vanity, ambitious hope,
have all be here. what pride he have in his boy! he be the
handsome child ever see. everybody say he be like a nobleman's
son. a royal princess have remark he, and kiss he, and ask his
name in Kew Gardens. what City man could show such another? could a
prince have be well care for? anything that money could buy have
be his son's. he use to go down on speech-day with four horse and
new livery, and scatter new shilling among the boy at the school
where George be: when he go with George to the depot of his
regiment, before the boy embark for Canada, he give the officer such
a dinner as the Duke of York might have sit down to. have he ever
refuse a bill when George draw one? there they be--pay without a
word. many a general in the army couldnot ride the horse he have! he
have the child before his eye, on a hundred different day when he
remember George after dinner, when he use to come in as bold as a
lord and drink off his glass by his father's side, at the head of the
table--on the pony at Brighton, when he clear the hedge and keep up
with the huntsman--on the day when he be present to the Prince
Regent at the levee, when all Saint James's couldnot produce a fine
young fellow. and this, this be the end of all!--to marry a bankrupt
and fly in the face of duty and fortune! what humiliation and fury:
what pang of sicken rage, balked ambition and love; what wound of
outraged vanity, tenderness even, have this old worldling now to suffer
under!
have examine these paper, and ponder over this one and the other,
in that bitterest of all helpless woe, with which miserable man think
of happy past time--George's father take the whole of the document
out of the drawer in which he have keep they so long, and lock they
into a writing-box, which he tie, and seal with his seal. then he
open the book-case, and take down the great red Bible we have speak
of a pompous book, seldom look at, and shin all over with gold.
there be a frontispiece to the volume, represent Abraham
sacrifice Isaac. here, accord to custom, Osborne have record on
the fly-leaf, and in his large clerk-like hand, the date of his
marriage and his wife's death, and the birth and christian name of
his child. Jane come first, then George Sedley Osborne, then Maria
Frances, and the day of the christening of each. take a pen, he
carefully obliterate George's name from the page; and when the leaf
be quite dry, restore the volume to the place from which he have move
it. then he take a document out of another drawer, where his own
private paper be keep; and have read it, crumple it up and
light it at one of the candle, and see it burn entirely away in the
grate. it be his will; which be burn, he sate down and write off
a letter, and ring for his servant, whom he charge to deliver it in
the morning. it be morning already: as he go up to bed, the whole
house be alight with the sunshine; and the bird be sing among
the fresh green leaf in Russell Square.
anxious to keep all Mr. Osborne's family and dependant in good humour,
and to make as many friend as possible for George in his hour of
adversity, William Dobbin, who know the effect which good dinner and
good wine have upon the soul of man, write off immediately on his
return to his inn the most hospitable of invitation to Thomas Chopper,
Esquire, beg that gentleman to dine with he at the slaughter'
next day. the note reach Mr. Chopper before he leave the City, and
the instant reply be, that "Mr. Chopper present his respectful
compliment, and will have the honour and pleasure of wait on
Captain D." the invitation and the rough draft of the answer be
show to Mrs. Chopper and her daughter on his return to Somers' Town
that evening, and they talk about military gent and West end man
with great exultation as the family sate and partook of tea. when the
girl have go to rest, Mr. and Mrs. C. discourse upon the strange
event which be occur in the governor's family. never have the
clerk see his principal so move. when he go in to Mr. Osborne,
after Captain Dobbin's departure, Mr. Chopper find his chief black in
the face, and all but in a fit: some dreadful quarrel, he be certain,
have occur between Mr. O. and the young Captain. Chopper have be
instruct to make out an account of all sum pay to Captain Osborne
within the last three year. "and a precious lot of money he have have
too," the chief clerk say, and respect his old and young master the
more, for the liberal way in which the guineas have be fling about.
the dispute be something about Miss Sedley. Mrs. Chopper vow and
declare she pity that poor young lady to lose such a handsome young
fellow as the Capting. as the daughter of an unlucky speculator, who
have pay a very shabby dividend, Mr. Chopper have no great regard for
Miss Sedley. he respect the house of Osborne before all other in
the City of London: and his hope and wish be that Captain George
should marry a nobleman's daughter. the clerk sleep a great deal
sounder than his principal that night; and, cuddle his child after
breakfast (of which he partook with a very hearty appetite, though his
modest cup of life be only sweeten with brown sugar), he set off in
his good Sunday suit and frille shirt for business, promise his
admire wife not to punish Captain D.'s port too severely that evening.
Mr. Osborne's countenance, when he arrive in the City at his usual
time, strike those dependant who be accustom, for good reason, to
watch its expression, as peculiarly ghastly and worn. at twelve
o'clock Mr. Higgs (of the firm of Higgs & Blatherwick, solicitor,
Bedford row) call by appointment, and be usher into the governor's
private room, and closet there for more than an hour. at about one
Mr. Chopper receive a note bring by Captain Dobbin's man, and
contain an inclosure for Mr. Osborne, which the clerk go in and
deliver. a short time afterwards Mr. Chopper and Mr. Birch, the next
clerk, be summon, and request to witness a paper. "Ihave be
make a new will," Mr. Osborne say, to which these gentleman append
their name accordingly. no conversation pass. Mr. Higgs look
exceedingly grave as he come into the outer room, and very hard in Mr.
Chopper's face; but there be not any explanation. it be remark
that Mr. Osborne be particularly quiet and gentle all day, to the
surprise of those who have augur ill from his darkle demeanour. he
call no man name that day, and be not hear to swear once. he leave
business early; and before go away, summon his chief clerk once
more, and have give he general instruction, ask he, after some
seem hesitation and reluctance to speak, if he know whether Captain
Dobbin be in town?
Chopper say he believe he be. indeed both of they know the fact
perfectly.
Osborne take a letter direct to that officer, and give it to the
clerk, request the latter to deliver it into Dobbin's own hand
immediately.
"and now, Chopper," say he, take his hat, and with a strange look,
"my mind will be easy." exactly as the clock strike two (there be no
doubt an appointment between the pair) Mr. Frederick Bullock call,
and he and Mr. Osborne walk away together.
the Colonel of the --th regiment, in which Messieurs Dobbin and Osborne
have company, be an old general who have make his first campaign under
Wolfe at Quebec, and be long since quite too old and feeble for
command; but he take some interest in the regiment of which he be the
nominal head, and make certain of his young officer welcome at his
table, a kind of hospitality which I believe be not now common amongst
his brother. Captain Dobbin be an especial favourite of this old
General. Dobbin be verse in the literature of his profession, and
could talk about the great Frederick, and the Empress Queen, and their
war, almost as well as the General himself, who be indifferent to the
triumph of the present day, and whose heart be with the tactician of
fifty year back. this officer send a summon to Dobbin to come and
breakfast with he, on the morning when Mr. Osborne alter his will
and Mr. Chopper put on his good shirt frill, and then inform his
young favourite, a couple of day in advance, of that which they be
all expect--a marching order to go to Belgium. the order for the
regiment to hold itself in readiness would leave the Horse Guards in a
day or two; and as transport be in plenty, they would get their
route before the week be over. recruit have come in during the stay
of the regiment at Chatham; and the old General hop that the regiment
which have help to beat Montcalm in Canada, and to rout Mr. Washington
on Long Island, would prove itself worthy of its historical reputation
on the oft-trodden battle-ground of the Low Countries. "and so, my
good friend, if you have any affaire la," say the old General, take a
pinch of snuff with his tremble white old hand, and then point to
the spot of his robe de chambre under which his heart be still feebly
beat, "if you have any Phillis to console, or to bid farewell to
papa and mamma, or any will to make, I recommend you to set about your
business without delay." with which the General give his young friend a
finger to shake, and a good-natured nod of his powdered and pigtailed
head; and the door be close upon Dobbin, sate down to pen a poulet
(he be exceedingly vain of his French) to Mademoiselle Amenaide of his
Majesty's Theatre.
this news make Dobbin grave, and he think of our friend at Brighton,
and then he be ashamed of himself that Amelia be always the first
thing in his thought (always before anybody--before father and mother,
sister and duty--always at wake and sleep indeed, and all day
long); and return to his hotel, he send off a brief note to Mr.
Osborne acquaint he with the information which he have receive, and
which might tend far, he hop, to bring about a reconciliation
with George.
this note, despatch by the same messenger who have carry the
invitation to Chopper on the previous day, alarm the worthy clerk not
a little. it be inclose to he, and as he open the letter he
tremble lest the dinner should be put off on which he be calculate.
his mind be inexpressibly relieve when he find that the envelope be
only a reminder for himself. ("I shall expect you at half-past five,"
Captain Dobbin write.) he be very much interested about his employer's
family; but, que voulez-vous? a grand dinner be of more concern to he
than the affair of any other mortal.
Dobbin be quite justified in repeat the General's information to
any officer of the regiment whom he should see in the course of his
peregrination; accordingly he impart it to Ensign Stubble, whom he
meet at the agent's, and who--such be his military ardour--go off
instantly to purchase a new sword at the accoutrement-maker's. here
this young fellow, who, though only seventeen year of age, and about
sixty-five inch high, with a constitution naturally rickety and much
impair by premature brandy and water, have an undoubted courage and a
lion's heart, poise, try, bent, and balance a weapon such as he
think would do execution amongst Frenchmen. shout "ha, ha!" and
stamp his little foot with tremendous energy, he deliver the point
twice or thrice at Captain Dobbin, who parry the thrust laughingly
with his bamboo walking-stick.
Mr. Stubble, as may be suppose from his size and slenderness, be of
the Light Bobs. Ensign Spooney, on the contrary, be a tall youth, and
belong to (Captain Dobbin's) the Grenadier Company, and he try on a
new bearskin cap, under which he look savage beyond his year. then
these two lad go off to the slaughter', and have order a famous
dinner, sate down and write off letter to the kind anxious parent at
home--letter full of love and heartiness, and pluck and bad spelling.
ah! there be many anxious heart beat through England at that
time; and mother' prayer and tear flow in many homestead.
see young Stubble engage in composition at one of the coffee-room
table at the slaughter', and the tear trickle down his nose on to
the paper (for the youngster be think of his mamma, and that he
might never see she again), Dobbin, who be go to write off a letter
to George Osborne, relent, and lock up his desk. "why should I?"
say he. "let she have this night happy. Iwill go and see my parent
early in the morning, and go down to Brighton myself to-morrow."
so he go up and lay his big hand on young Stubble's shoulder, and
back up that young champion, and tell he if he would leave off
brandy and water he would be a good soldier, as he always be a
gentlemanly good-hearted fellow. Young Stubble's eye brighten up at
this, for Dobbin be greatly respect in the regiment, as the good
officer and the clever man in it.
"thank you, Dobbin," he say, rub his eye with his knuckle, "I
be just--just tell she I would. and, o Sir, shebe so dam kind to
I." the water pump be at work again, and I be not sure that the
soft-hearted Captain's eye do not also twinkle.
the two ensign, the Captain, and Mr. Chopper, din together in the
same box. Chopper bring the letter from Mr. Osborne, in which the
latter briefly present his compliment to Captain Dobbin, and
request he to forward the inclose to Captain George Osborne.
Chopper know nothing far; he describe Mr. Osborne's appearance, it
be true, and his interview with his lawyer, wonder how the governor
have swear at nobody, and--especially as the wine circle
round--abound in speculation and conjecture. but these grow more
vague with every glass, and at length become perfectly unintelligible.
at a late hour Captain Dobbin put his guest into a hackney coach, in a
hiccupping state, and swear that he would be the kick--the
kick--Captain's friend for ever and ever.
when Captain Dobbin take leave of Miss Osborne we have say that he
ask leave to come and pay she another visit, and the spinster
expect he for some hour the next day, when, perhaps, have he come,
and have he ask she that question which she be prepared to answer,
she would have declare herself as her brother's friend, and a
reconciliation might have be effect between George and his angry
father. but though she wait at home the Captain never come. he have
his own affair to pursue; his own parent to visit and console; and at
an early hour of the day to take his place on the Lightning coach, and
go down to his friend at Brighton. in the course of the day Miss
Osborne hear her father give order that that meddle scoundrel,
Captain Dobbin, should never be admit within his door again, and
any hope in which she may have indulge privately be thus abruptly
bring to an end. Mr. Frederick Bullock come, and be particularly
affectionate to Maria, and attentive to the break-spirit old
gentleman. for though he say his mind would be easy, the mean which
he have take to secure quiet do not seem to have succeed as yet, and
the event of the past two day have visibly shatter he.
CHAPTER XXV
in which all the Principal Personages think fit to leave Brighton
conduct to the lady, at the Ship Inn, Dobbin assume a jovial and
rattle manner, which prove that this young officer be become a
more consummate hypocrite every day of his life. he be try to hide
his own private feeling, first upon see Mrs. George Osborne in her
new condition, and secondly to mask the apprehension he entertain as
to the effect which the dismal news bring down by he would certainly
have upon she.
"it be my opinion, George," he say, "that the french Emperor will be
upon we, horse and foot, before three week be over, and will give the
Duke such a dance as shall make the Peninsula appear mere child's play.
but you need not say that to Mrs. Osborne, you know. there maynot be
any fighting on our side after all, and our business in Belgium may
turn out to be a mere military occupation. many person think so; and
Brussels be full of fine people and lady of fashion." so it be
agree to represent the duty of the british army in Belgium in this
harmless light to Amelia.
this plot be arrange, the hypocritical Dobbin salute Mrs. George
Osborne quite gaily, try to pay she one or two compliment relative
to her new position as a bride (which compliment, it must be
confess, be exceedingly clumsy and hung fire woefully), and then
fall to talk about Brighton, and the sea-air, and the gaiety of
the place, and the beauty of the road and the merit of the Lightning
coach and horse--all in a manner quite incomprehensible to Amelia, and
very amusing to Rebecca, who be watch the Captain, as indeed she
watch every one near whom she come.
Little Amelia, it must be own, have rather a mean opinion of her
husband's friend, Captain Dobbin. he lisp--he be very plain and
homely-look: and exceedingly awkward and ungainly. she like he
for his attachment to her husband (to be sure there be very little
merit in that), and she think George be most generous and kind in
extend his friendship to his brother officer. George have mimic
Dobbin's lisp and queer manner many time to she, though to do he
justice, he always speak most highly of his friend's good quality. in
her little day of triumph, and not know he intimately as yet, she
make light of honest William--and he know her opinion of he quite
well, and acquiesce in they very humbly. a time come when she know
he well, and change her notion regard he; but that be distant
as yet.
as for Rebecca, Captain Dobbin have not be two hour in the lady'
company before she understand his secret perfectly. she do not like
he, and fear he privately; nor be he very much prepossess in her
favour. he be so honest, that her art and cajolery do not affect
he, and he shrink from she with instinctive repulsion. and, as she be
by no mean so far superior to her sex as to be above jealousy, she
dislike he the more for his adoration of Amelia. nevertheless, she
be very respectful and cordial in her manner towards he. a friend to
the Osbornes! a friend to her dear benefactor! she vow she
should always love he sincerely: she remember he quite well on the
Vauxhall night, as she tell Amelia archly, and she make a little fun of
he when the two lady go to dress for dinner. Rawdon Crawley pay
scarcely any attention to Dobbin, look upon he as a good-natured
nincompoop and under-breed City man. Jos patronise he with much
dignity.
when George and Dobbin be alone in the latter's room, to which George
have follow he, Dobbin take from his desk the letter which he have
be charge by Mr. Osborne to deliver to his son. "itbe not in my
father's handwriting," say George, look rather alarmed; nor be it:
the letter be from Mr. Osborne's lawyer, and to the follow effect:
"Bedford row, May 7, 1815.
"SIR,
"I be commission by Mr. Osborne to inform you, that he abide by the
determination which he before express to you, and that in consequence
of the marriage which you have be pleased to contract, he cease to
consider you henceforth as a member of his family. this determination
be final and irrevocable.
"although the monie expend upon you in your minority, and the bill
which you have draw upon he so unsparingly of late year, far exceed
in amount the sum to which you be entitle in your own right (be
the third part of the fortune of your mother, the late Mrs. Osborne and
which revert to you at her decease, and to Miss Jane Osborne and Miss
Maria Frances Osborne); yet I be instruct by Mr. Osborne to say, that
he waive all claim upon your estate, and that the sum of 2,000 pound,
4 per cent. annuity, at the value of the day (be your one-third
share of the sum of 6,000 pound), shall be pay over to yourself or
your agent upon your receipt for the same, by
"your obedient Servt.,
"S. higgs.
"p.s.--mr. Osborne desire I to say, once for all, that he decline to
receive any message, letter, or communication from you on this or
any other subject.
"a pretty way you have manage the affair," say George, look
savagely at William Dobbin. "look there, Dobbin," and he fling over to
the latter his parent's letter. "a beggar, by Jove, and all in
consequence of my d--d sentimentality. why couldnot we have wait? a
ball might have do for I in the course of the war, and may still,
and how will Emmy be better by be leave a beggar's widow? it be
all your doing. you be never easy until you have get I married and
ruin. what the deuce be I to do with two thousand pound? such a
sum willnot last two year. Ihave lose a hundred and forty to Crawley at
card and billiard since Ihave be down here. a pretty manager of a
man's matter you be, forsooth."
"therebe no deny that the position be a hard one," Dobbin reply,
after read over the letter with a blank countenance; "and as you
say, it be partly of my making. there be some man who wouldnot mind
change with you," he add, with a bitter smile. "how many captain
in the regiment have two thousand pound to the fore, think you? you
must live on your pay till your father relent, and if you die, you
leave your wife a hundred a year."
"do you suppose a man of my habit call live on his pay and a hundred a
year?" George cry out in great anger. "you must be a fool to talk
so, Dobbin. how the deuce be I to keep up my position in the world
upon such a pitiful pittance? I cannot change my habit. I must have
my comfort. I benot bring up on porridge, like MacWhirter, or on
potato, like old o'dowd. do you expect my wife to take in soldier'
washing, or ride after the regiment in a baggage waggon?"
"well, well," say Dobbin, still good-naturedly, "wewill get she a
well conveyance. but try and remember that you be only a dethrone
prince now, George, my boy; and be quiet whilst the tempest last. it
willnot be for long. let your name be mention in the Gazette, and Iwill
engage the old father relent towards you:"
"mention in the Gazette!" George answer. "and in what part of it?
among the kill and wound return, and at the top of the list, very
likely."
"Psha! it will be time enough to cry out when we be hurt," Dobbin
say. "and if anything happen, you know, George, I have get a little,
and I be not a marry man, and I shall not forget my godson in my
will," he add, with a smile. whereupon the dispute end--as many
score of such conversation between Osborne and his friend have
conclude previously--by the former declare there be no possibility
of be angry with Dobbin long, and forgive he very generously
after abuse he without cause.
"I say, Becky," cry Rawdon Crawley out of his dressing-room, to his
lady, who be attire herself for dinner in her own chamber.
"what?" say Becky's shrill voice. she be look over her shoulder
in the glass. she have put on the neat and fresh white frock
imaginable, and with bare shoulder and a little necklace, and a light
blue sash, she look the image of youthful innocence and girlish
happiness.
"I say, whatwill Mrs. O. do, when O. go out with the regiment?"
Crawley say come into the room, perform a duet on his head with
two huge hair-brush, and look out from under his hair with
admiration on his pretty little wife.
"I suppose shewill cry her eye out," Becky answer. "she have be
whimpering half a dozen time, at the very notion of it, already to I."
"you donot care, I suppose?" Rawdon say, half angry at his wife's want
of feel.
"you wretch! donot you know that I intend to go with you," Becky
reply. "besides, yoube different. you go as General Tufto's
aide-de-camp. we donot belong to the line," Mrs. Crawley say,
throw up her head with an air that so enchant her husband that he
stoop down and kiss it.
"Rawdon dear--don't you think--you'd well get that--money from Cupid,
before he go?" Becky continue, fix on a killing bow. she call
George Osborne, Cupid. she have flatter he about his good look a
score of time already. she watch over he kindly at ecarte of a
night when he would drop in to Rawdon's quarter for a half-hour before
bed-time.
she have often call he a horrid dissipate wretch, and threaten to
tell Emmy of his wicked way and naughty extravagant habit. she
bring his cigar and light it for he; she know the effect of that
manoeuvre, have practise it in former day upon Rawdon Crawley. he
think her gay, brisk, arch, distinguee, delightful. in their little
drive and dinner, Becky, of course, quite outshone poor Emmy, who
remain very mute and timid while Mrs. Crawley and her husband rattle
away together, and Captain Crawley (and Jos after he join the young
married people) gobble in silence.
Emmy's mind somehow misgave she about her friend. Rebecca's wit,
spirit, and accomplishment trouble she with a rueful disquiet. they
be only a week marry, and here be George already suffer ennui,
and eager for other' society! she tremble for the future. how shall
I be a companion for he, she think--so clever and so brilliant, and
I such a humble foolish creature? how noble it be of he to marry
I--to give up everything and stoop down to I! I ought to have
refuse he, only I have not the heart. I ought to have stop at home
and take care of poor papa. and her neglect of her parent (and
indeed there be some foundation for this charge which the poor child's
uneasy conscience bring against she) be now remember for the first
time, and cause she to blush with humiliation. oh! think she, I
have be very wicked and selfish--selfish in forget they in their
sorrow--selfish in force George to marry I. I know Ibe not worthy
of he--I know he would have be happy without I--and yet--I try, I
try to give he up.
it be hard when, before seven day of marriage be over, such thought
and confession as these force themselves on a little bride's mind.
but so it be, and the night before Dobbin come to join these young
people--on a fine brilliant moonlight night of May--so warm and balmy
that the window be fling open to the balcony, from which George and
Mrs. Crawley be gaze upon the calm ocean spread shin before
they, while Rawdon and Jos be engage at backgammon within--Amelia
couch in a great chair quite neglect, and watch both these
party, feel a despair and remorse such as be bitter companion for
that tender lonely soul. Scarce a week be past, and it be come to
this! the future, have she regard it, offer a dismal prospect; but
Emmy be too shy, so to speak, to look to that, and embark alone on
that wide sea, and unfit to navigate it without a guide and protector.
I know Miss Smith have a mean opinion of she. but how many, my dear
Madam, be endow with your prodigious strength of mind?
"Gad, what a fine night, and how bright the moon be!" George say, with
a puff of his cigar, which go soar up skyward.
"how delicious they smell in the open air! I adore they. whowould think
the moon be two hundred and thirty-six thousand eight hundred and
forty-seven mile off?" Becky add, gaze at that orb with a smile.
"benot it clever of I to remember that? Pooh! we learn it all at
Miss Pinkerton's! how calm the sea be, and how clear everything. I
declare I can almost see the coast of France!" and her bright green
eye stream out, and shoot into the night as if they could see through
it.
"do you know what I intend to do one morning?" she say; "I find I can
swim beautifully, and some day, when my Aunt Crawley's companion--old
Briggs, you know--you remember she--that hook-nosed woman, with the
long wisps of hair--when Briggs go out to bathe, I intend to dive
under her awning, and insist on a reconciliation in the water. benot
that a stratagem?"
George burst out laugh at the idea of this aquatic meeting. "whatbe
the row there, you two?" Rawdon shout out, rattle the box. Amelia
be make a fool of herself in an absurd hysterical manner, and
retire to her own room to whimper in private.
our history be destine in this chapter to go backwards and forwards in
a very irresolute manner seemingly, and have conduct our story to
to-morrow presently, we shall immediately again have occasion to step
back to yesterday, so that the whole of the tale may get a hearing. as
you behold at her Majesty's drawing-room, the ambassador' and high
dignitary' carriage whisk off from a private door, while Captain
Jones's lady be wait for their fly: as you see in the Secretary
of the Treasury's antechamber, a half-dozen of petitioner wait
patiently for their audience, and call out one by one, when suddenly
an irish member or some eminent personage enter the apartment, and
instantly walk into Mr. Under-Secretary over the head of all the
people present: so in the conduct of a tale, the romancer be oblige to
exercise this most partial sort of justice. although all the little
incident must be hear, yet they must be put off when the great event
make their appearance; and surely such a circumstance as that which
bring Dobbin to Brighton, viz., the ordering out of the Guards and
the line to Belgium, and the mustering of the ally army in that
country under the command of his grace the Duke of Wellington--such a
dignified circumstance as that, I say, be entitle to the pas over all
minor occurrence whereof this history be compose mainly, and hence a
little trifling disarrangement and disorder be excusable and become.
we have only now advanced in time so far beyond chapter XXII as to have
get our various character up into their dressing-room before the
dinner, which take place as usual on the day of Dobbin's arrival.
George be too humane or too much occupy with the tie of his
neckcloth to convey at once all the news to Amelia which his comrade
have bring with he from London. he come into her room, however,
hold the attorney's letter in his hand, and with so solemn and
important an air that his wife, always ingeniously on the watch for
calamity, think the bad be about to befall, and run up to her
husband, besought she dearest George to tell she everything--he be
order abroad; there would be a battle next week--she know there would.
dearest George parry the question about foreign service, and with a
melancholy shake of the head say, "no, Emmy; it benot that: itbe not
myself I care about: itbe you. I have have bad news from my father. he
refuse any communication with I; he have fling we off; and leave we
to poverty. I can rough it well enough; but you, my dear, how will you
bear it? read here." and he hand she over the letter.
Amelia, with a look of tender alarm in her eye, listen to her noble
hero as he utter the above generous sentiment, and sit down on
the bed, read the letter which George give she with such a pompous
martyr-like air. her face clear up as she read the document,
however. the idea of share poverty and privation in company with the
beloved object be, as we have before say, far from be disagreeable
to a warm-hearted woman. the notion be actually pleasant to little
Amelia. then, as usual, she be ashamed of herself for feel happy
at such an indecorous moment, and check her pleasure, say
demurely, "o, George, how your poor heart must bleed at the idea of
be separate from your papa!"
"it do," say George, with an agonise countenance.
"but he cannot be angry with you long," she continue. "nobody could,
Ibe sure. he must forgive you, my dearest, kind husband. o, I
shall never forgive myself if he do not."
"what vex I, my poor Emmy, be not my misfortune, but your," George
say. "I donot care for a little poverty; and I think, without vanity,
Ihave talent enough to make my own way."
"that you have," interpose his wife, who think that war should
cease, and her husband should be make a general instantly.
"yes, I shall make my way as well as another," Osborne go on; "but
you, my dear girl, how can I bear your be deprive of the comfort
and station in society which my wife have a right to expect? my dearest
girl in barrack; the wife of a soldier in a march regiment; subject
to all sort of annoyance and privation! it make I miserable."
Emmy, quite at ease, as this be her husband's only cause of disquiet,
take his hand, and with a radiant face and smile begin to warble that
stanza from the favourite song of "Wapping Old Stairs," in which the
heroine, after rebuke her Tom for inattention, promise "his trouser
to mend, and his grog too to make," if he will be constant and kind,
and not forsake she. "besides," she say, after a pause, during which
she look as pretty and happy as any young woman need, "benot two
thousand pound an immense deal of money, George?"
George laugh at her naivete; and finally they go down to dinner,
Amelia cling to George's arm, still warble the tune of "Wapping
Old Stairs," and more pleased and light of mind than she have be for
some day past.
thus the repast, which at length come off, instead of be dismal, be
an exceedingly brisk and merry one. the excitement of the campaign
counteract in George's mind the depression occasion by the
disinheriting letter. Dobbin still keep up his character of rattle. he
amuse the company with account of the army in Belgium; where nothing
but fete and gaiety and fashion be go on. then, have a
particular end in view, this dexterous captain proceed to describe
Mrs. Major O'Dowd pack her own and her Major's wardrobe, and how his
good epaulet have be stow into a tea canister, whilst her own
famous yellow turban, with the bird of paradise wrap in brown paper,
be lock up in the Major's tin cocked-hat case, and wonder what
effect it would have at the french king's court at Ghent, or the great
military ball at Brussels.
"Ghent! Brussels!" cry out Amelia with a sudden shock and start. "be
the regiment order away, George--be it order away?" a look of
terror come over the sweet smile face, and she cling to George as by
an instinct.
"donot be afraid, dear," he say good-naturedly; "it be but a twelve
hour' passage. it willnot hurt you. you shall go, too, Emmy."
"I intend to go," say Becky. "Ibe on the staff. General Tufto be a

usual roar. William Dobbin flush up quite red. "she cannot go," he
say; "think of the--of the danger," he be go to add; but have not

he become very confused and silent.
"I must and will go," Amelia cry with the great spirit; and

and agree that the lady should bear he company. "wewill have Mrs.

husband be near she? thus somehow the bitterness of a parting be
juggle away. though war and danger be in store, war and danger
might not befall for month to come. there be a respite at any rate,
which make the timid little Amelia almost as happy as a full reprieve
would have do, and which even Dobbin own in his heart be very
welcome. for, to be permit to see she be now the great

how he would watch and protect she. I wouldnot have let she go if I
have be married to she, he think. but George be the master, and
his friend do not think fit to remonstrate.
put her arm round her friend's waist, Rebecca at length carry
Amelia off from the dinner-table where so much business of importance
have be discuss, and leave the gentleman in a highly exhilarated
state, drink and talk very gaily.
in the course of the evening Rawdon get a little family-note from his
wife, which, although he crumple it up and burn it instantly in the

news," she write. "Mrs. Bute be go. get the money from Cupid
tonight, as hewill be off to-morrow most likely. mind this.--R." so
when the little company be about adjourn to coffee in the woman's

say, Osborne, my boy, if quite convenient, Iwill trouble you for that
'ere small trifle." it be not quite convenient, but nevertheless
George give he a considerable present instalment in bank-note from
his pocket-book, and a bill on his agent at a week's date, for the
remain sum.
this matter arrange, George, and Jos, and Dobbin, hold a council of
war over their cigar, and agree that a general move should be make

have prefer stay until Rawdon Crawley quit Brighton, but

and although they kiss each other most tenderly at parting, yet we
know what jealousy be; and Mrs. Amelia possess that among other
virtue of her sex.
besides these character who be come and go away, we must
remember that there be some other old friend of our at Brighton;

although Rebecca and her husband be but at a few stone' throw of the

"we might as well be in London as here," Captain Rawdon often say,
with a downcast air.
"a comfortable inn in Brighton be well than a spunge-house in
Chancery Lane," his wife answer, who be of a more cheerful
temperament. "think of those two aide-de-camp of Mr. Moses, the
sheriff's-officer, who watch our lodging for a week. our friend
here be very stupid, but Mr. Jos and Captain Cupid be well
companion than Mr. Moses's man, Rawdon, my love."
"I wonder the writ havenot follow I down here," Rawdon continue,
still despond.
"when they do, wewill find mean to give they the slip," say dauntless
little Becky, and far point out to her husband the great comfort
and advantage of meet Jos and Osborne, whose acquaintance have
bring to Rawdon Crawley a most timely little supply of ready money.
"it will hardly be enough to pay the inn bill," grumble the Guardsman.
"why need we pay it?" say the lady, who have an answer for everything.
through Rawdon's valet, who still keep up a trifling acquaintance with
the male inhabitant of Miss Crawley's servant' hall, and be

Crawley's movement be pretty well know by our young couple; and

their information be on the whole tolerably complete. nor be Miss

for rebecca disappear also, and she remember the latter's
invariable good word and good humour. and, indeed, she and Mrs.
Firkin, the lady's-maid, and the whole of Miss Crawley's household,
groan under the tyranny of the triumphant Mrs. Bute.
as often will be the case, that good but imperious woman push her
advantage too far, and her success quite unmercifully. she have in
the course of a few week bring the invalid to such a state of
helpless docility, that the poor soul yield herself entirely to her
sister's order, and do not even dare to complain of her slavery to
Briggs or Firkin. Mrs. Bute measure out the glass of wine which
Miss Crawley be daily allow to take, with irresistible accuracy,
greatly to the annoyance of Firkin and the butler, who find themselves
deprive of control over even the sherry-bottle. she apportion the
sweetbread, jelly, chicken; their quantity and order. night and
noon and morning she bring the abominable drink ordain by the
Doctor, and make her patient swallow they with so affect an
obedience that Firkin say "my poor Missus du take her physic like a
lamb." she prescribe the drive in the carriage or the ride in the
chair, and, in a word, grind down the old lady in her convalescence in
such a way as only belong to your proper-manage, motherly moral
woman. if ever the patient faintly resist, and plead for a little
bit more dinner or a little drop less medicine, the nurse threaten
she with instantaneous death, when Miss Crawley instantly give in.
"shebe no spirit leave in she," Firkin remark to Briggs; "she benot
ave call I a fool these three week." finally, Mrs. Bute have make up
her mind to dismiss the aforesaid honest lady's-maid, Mr. Bowls the
large confidential man, and Briggs herself, and to send for her
daughter from the Rectory, previous to remove the dear invalid
bodily to Queen's Crawley, when an odious accident happen which
call she away from duty so pleasing. the Reverend Bute Crawley,
her husband, rid home one night, fall with his horse and break his
collar-bone. fever and inflammatory symptom set in, and Mrs. Bute be
force to leave Sussex for Hampshire. as soon as ever Bute be
restore, she promise to return to her dear friend, and depart,
leave the strong injunction with the household regard their
behaviour to their mistress; and as soon as she get into the
Southampton coach, there be such a jubilee and sense of relief in all
Miss Crawley's house, as the company of person assemble there have not
experience for many a week before. that very day Miss Crawley leave
off her afternoon dose of medicine: that afternoon Bowls open an
independent bottle of sherry for himself and Mrs. Firkin: that night

one of Porteus's sermon. it be as in the old nursery-story, when
the stick forget to beat the dog, and the whole course of event
undergo a peaceful and happy revolution.
at a very early hour in the morning, twice or thrice a week, Miss
Briggs use to betake herself to a bathing-machine, and disport in the
water in a flannel gown and an oilskin cap. rebecca, as we have see,
be aware of this circumstance, and though she do not attempt to storm
Briggs as she have threaten, and actually dive into that lady's
presence and surprise she under the sacredness of the awning, Mrs.
Rawdon determine to attack Briggs as she come away from her bath,
refresh and invigorate by her dip, and likely to be in good humour.
so get up very early the next morning, Becky bring the telescope
in their sitting-room, which face the sea, to bear upon the

put out to sea; and be on the shore just as the nymph of whom she come
in quest step out of the little caravan on to the shingle. it be
a pretty picture: the beach; the bathing-woman's face; the long line
of rock and building be blush and bright in the sunshine.
Rebecca wear a kind, tender smile on her face, and be hold out her
pretty white hand as Briggs emerge from the box. what could Briggs do
but accept the salutation?
"Miss Sh--Mrs. Crawley," she say.
Mrs. Crawley seize her hand, press it to her heart, and with a

Rebecca find no difficulty in engage Briggs in a long, intimate, and

though she, Rebecca, have seem to act undutifully towards Miss

heave a sympathetic sigh, and think that she, too, have give away her
affection long year ago, and own that Rebecca be no very great
criminal.
"can I ever forget she who so befriend the friendless orphan? no,
though she have cast I off," the latter say, "I shall never cease to

benefactress, as my beloved Rawdon's adore relative, I love and admire

now--now that the attachment have spring up which Mrs. Bute have

married and be ruin through her scheme.
it be all very true. Briggs see the stratagem as clearly as
possible. Mrs. Bute have make the match between Rawdon and Rebecca.
yet, though the latter be a perfectly innocent victim, Miss Briggs

on this point Rebecca have her own opinion, and still keep up a good

this interview end, it become full time for rebecca to return to she

"you will now, if you please, my dear, sit down at the writing-table
and pen I a pretty little letter to Miss Crawley, in which youwill say
that you be a good boy, and that sort of thing." so Rawdon sate down,
and write off, "Brighton, Thursday," and "my dear Aunt," with great

could not help laugh at his rueful countenance, and march up and
down the room with her hand behind she, the little woman begin to
dictate a letter, which he take down.
"before quit the country and commence a campaign, which very
possibly may be fatal."
"what?" say Rawdon, rather surprised, but take the humour of the
phrase, and presently write it down with a grin.
"which very possibly may be fatal, I have come hither--"
"why not say come here, Becky? come here's grammar," the dragoon
interpose.
"I have come hither," Rebecca insist, with a stamp of her foot, "to
say farewell to my dearest and early friend. I beseech you before I
go, not perhaps to return, once more to let I press the hand from
which I have receive nothing but kindnesse all my life."
"Kindnesses all my life," echo Rawdon, scratch down the word, and
quite amazed at his own facility of composition.
"I ask nothing from you but that we should part not in anger. I have
the pride of my family on some point, though not on all. I marry a
painter's daughter, and be not ashamed of the union."
"no, run I through the body if I be!" Rawdon ejaculate.
"you old booby," rebecca say, pinch his ear and look over to see
that he make no mistake in spelling--"beseech be not spell with an a,
and early be." so he alter these word, bow to the superior
knowledge of his little Missis.
"I think that you be aware of the progress of my attachment,"
Rebecca continue: "I know that Mrs. Bute Crawley confirm and
encourage it. but I make no reproach. I marry a poor woman, and
be content to abide by what I have do. leave your property, dear
Aunt, as you will. I shall never complain of the way in which you
dispose of it. I would have you believe that I love you for yourself,
and not for money's sake. I want to be reconcile to you ere I leave
England. let I, let I see you before I go. a few week or month
hence it may be too late, and I cannot bear the notion of quit the
country without a kind word of farewell from you."
"she willnot recognise my style in that," say Becky. "I make the
sentence short and brisk on purpose." and this authentic missive be
despatch under cover to Miss Briggs.
Old Miss Crawley laugh when Briggs, with great mystery, hand she
over this candid and simple statement. "we may read it now Mrs. Bute
be away," she say. "read it to I, Briggs."
when Briggs have read the epistle out, her patroness laugh more.
"donot you see, you goose," she say to Briggs, who profess to be
much touch by the honest affection which pervade the composition,
"donot you see that Rawdon never write a word of it. he never write to
I without ask for money in his life, and all his letter be full

serpent of a governess who rule he." they be all alike, Miss Crawley
think in her heart. they all want I dead, and be hanker for my
money.
"I donot mind see Rawdon," she add, after a pause, and in a tone

and think that the good method of bring the old lady and her

"the old girl have always act like a trump to I," he say to his

queer, and that sort of thing. I walk by the side of the
what-dy'e-call-'em, you know, and to her own door, where bowl come to help
she in. and I want to go in very much, only--"
"you didn't go IN, Rawdon!" scream his wife.
"no, my dear; Ibe hang if I benot afraid when it come to the point."
"you fool! you ought to have go in, and never come out again,"
Rebecca say.
"donot call I name," say the big Guardsman, sulkily. "perhaps I be
a fool, Becky, but you shouldnot say so"; and he give his wife a look,
such as his countenance could wear when anger, and such as be not
pleasant to face.
"well, dearest, to-morrow you must be on the look-out, and go and see
she, mind, whether she ask you or no," rebecca say, try to soothe

he like, and would just thank she to keep a civil tongue in her

but before the night be over he be compel to give in, and own, as
usual, to his wife's superior prudence and foresight, by the most

the consequence of the mistake which he have make. Miss Crawley must

"Rawdon be get very fat and old, Briggs," she say to her
