appearance. his marriage to that woman have hopelessly vulgarise he.
Mrs. Bute always say they drink together; and I have no doubt they do.
yes: he smell of gin abominably. I remark it. donot you?"
in vain Briggs interpose that Mrs. Bute speak ill of everybody: and,
as far as a person in her humble position could judge, be an--
"an artful design woman? yes, so she be, and she do speak ill of
every one--but I be certain that woman have make Rawdon drink. all those
low people do--"
"he be very much affected at see you, ma'am," the companion say;
"and I be sure, when you remember that he be go to the field of
danger--"
"how much money have he promise you, Briggs?" the old spinster cry
out, work herself into a nervous rage--"there now, of course you
begin to cry. I hate scene. why be I always to be worry? go and
cry up in your own room, and send Firkin to I--no, stop, sit down and
blow your nose, and leave off cry, and write a letter to Captain
Crawley." Poor Briggs go and place herself obediently at the
writing-book. its leaf be blot all over with relic of the
firm, strong, rapid handwriting of the spinster's late amanuensis, Mrs.
Bute Crawley.
"begin 'my dear sir,' or 'dear sir,' that will be well, and say you
be desire by Miss Crawley--no, by Miss Crawley's medical man, by Mr.
Creamer, to state that my health be such that all strong emotion would
be dangerous in my present delicate condition--and that I must decline
any family discussion or interview whatever. and thank he for come
to Brighton, and so forth, and beg he not to stay any long on my
account. and, Miss Briggs, you may add that I wish he a bon voyage,
and that if he will take the trouble to call upon my lawyer's in Gray's
Inn Square, he will find there a communication for he. yes, that will
do; and that will make he leave Brighton." the benevolent Briggs
pen this sentence with the utmost satisfaction.
"to seize upon I the very day after Mrs. Bute be go," the old lady
prattle on; "it be too indecent. Briggs, my dear, write to Mrs.
Crawley, and say she neednot come back. no--she needn't--and she
shan't--and I willnot be a slave in my own house--and I willnot be starve
and choke with poison. they all want to kill I--all--all"--and with
this the lonely old woman burst into a scream of hysterical tear.
the last scene of her dismal Vanity Fair comedy be fast approach;
the tawdry lamp be go out one by one; and the dark curtain be
almost ready to descend.
that final paragraph, which refer Rawdon to Miss Crawley's solicitor
in London, and which Briggs have write so good-naturedly, console the
dragoon and his wife somewhat, after their first blank disappointment,
on read the spinster's refusal of a reconciliation. and it effect
the purpose for which the old lady have cause it to be write, by
make Rawdon very eager to get to London.
out of Jos's losing and George Osborne's bank-note, he pay his bill
at the inn, the landlord whereof do not probably know to this day how
doubtfully his account once stand. for, as a general send his baggage
to the rear before an action, Rebecca have wisely pack up all their
chief valuable and send they off under care of George's servant, who
go in charge of the trunk on the coach back to London. Rawdon and
his wife return by the same conveyance next day.
"I should have like to see the old girl before we go," Rawdon say.
"she look so cut up and alter that Ibe sure she cannot last long. I
wonder what sort of a cheque I shall have at Waxy's. two hundred--it
cannot be less than two hundred--hey, Becky?"
in consequence of the repeat visit of the aide-de-camp of the
Sheriff of Middlesex, Rawdon and his wife do not go back to their
lodging at Brompton, but put up at an inn. early the next morning,
Rebecca have an opportunity of see they as she skirt that suburb on
her road to old Mrs. Sedley's house at Fulham, whither she go to look
for her dear Amelia and her Brighton friend. they be all off to
Chatham, thence to Harwich, to take shipping for Belgium with the
regiment--kind old Mrs. Sedley very much depressed and tearful,
solitary. return from this visit, Rebecca find her husband, who
have be off to Gray's Inn, and learn his fate. he come back furious.
"by Jove, Becky," say he, "shebe only give I twenty pound!"
though it tell against themselves, the joke be too good, and Becky
burst out laugh at Rawdon's discomfiture.
CHAPTER XXVI
between London and Chatham
on quit Brighton, our friend George, as become a person of rank and
fashion travel in a barouche with four horse, drive in state to a
fine hotel in Cavendish Square, where a suite of splendid room, and a
table magnificently furnish with plate and surround by a half-dozen
of black and silent waiter, be ready to receive the young gentleman
and his bride. George do the honour of the place with a princely air
to Jos and Dobbin; and Amelia, for the first time, and with exceed
shyness and timidity, preside at what George call her own table.
George pooh-poohe the wine and bully the waiter royally, and Jos
gobble the turtle with immense satisfaction. Dobbin help he to it;
for the lady of the house, before whom the tureen be place, be so
ignorant of the content, that she be go to help Mr. Sedley without
bestow upon he either calipash or calipee.
the splendour of the entertainment, and the apartment in which it be
give, alarmed Mr. Dobbin, who remonstrate after dinner, when Jos be
asleep in the great chair. but in vain he cry out against the
enormity of turtle and champagne that be fit for an archbishop. "Ihave
always be accustom to travel like a gentleman," George say, "and,
damme, my wife shall travel like a lady. as long as therebe a shot in
the locker, she shall want for nothing," say the generous fellow,
quite pleased with himself for his magnificence of spirit. nor do
Dobbin try and convince he that Amelia's happiness be not centre in
turtle-soup.
a while after dinner, Amelia timidly express a wish to go and see she
mamma, at Fulham: which permission George grant she with some
grumbling. and she trip away to her enormous bedroom, in the centre
of which stand the enormous funereal bed, "that the Emperor
Halixander's sister slep in when the ally suffering be here," and
put on her little bonnet and shawl with the utmost eagerness and
pleasure. George be still drink claret when she return to the
dining-room, and make no sign of move. "ar'n't you come with I,
dearest?" she ask he. no; the "dear" have "business" that night.
his man should get she a coach and go with she. and the coach be at
the door of the hotel, Amelia make George a little disappointed curtsey
after look vainly into his face once or twice, and go sadly down
the great staircase, Captain Dobbin after, who hand she into the
vehicle, and see it drive away to its destination. the very valet be
ashamed of mention the address to the hackney-coachman before the
hotel waiter, and promise to instruct he when they get far on.
Dobbin walk home to his old quarter and the slaughter', think
very likely that it would be delightful to be in that hackney-coach,
along with Mrs. Osborne. George be evidently of quite a different
taste; for when he have take wine enough, he go off to half-price at
the play, to see Mr. Kean perform in Shylock. Captain Osborne be a
great lover of the drama, and have himself perform high-comedy
character with great distinction in several garrison theatrical
entertainment. Jos sleep on until long after dark, when he wake up
with a start at the motion of his servant, who be remove and
empty the decanter on the table; and the hackney-coach stand be
again put into requisition for a carriage to convey this stout hero to
his lodging and bed.
Mrs. Sedley, you may be sure, clasp her daughter to her heart with
all maternal eagerness and affection, run out of the door as the
carriage draw up before the little garden-gate, to welcome the weeping,
trembling, young bride. old Mr. Clapp, who be in his shirt-sleeve,
trim the garden-plot, shrink back alarmed. the irish servant-lass
rush up from the kitchen and smile a "God bless you." Amelia could
hardly walk along the flag and up the step into the parlour.
how the floodgate be open, and mother and daughter weep, when they
be together embrace each other in this sanctuary, may readily be
imagine by every reader who possess the least sentimental turn.
when donot lady weep? at what occasion of joy, sorrow, or other
business of life, and, after such an event as a marriage, mother and
daughter be surely at liberty to give way to a sensibility which be
as tender as it be refresh. about a question of marriage I have see
woman who hate each other kiss and cry together quite fondly. how much
more do they feel when they love! good mother be married over again
at their daughter' wedding: and as for subsequent event, who do
not know how ultra-maternal grandmother are?--in fact a woman, until
she be a grandmother, do not often really know what to be a mother
be. let we respect Amelia and her mamma whispering and whimpering and
laugh and cry in the parlour and the twilight. old Mr. Sedley
do. he have not divine who be in the carriage when it drive up. he
have not fly out to meet his daughter, though he kiss she very
warmly when she enter the room (where he be occupy, as usual, with
his paper and tape and statement of account), and after sit
with the mother and daughter for a short time, he very wisely leave the
little apartment in their possession.
George's valet be look on in a very supercilious manner at Mr.
Clapp in his shirt-sleeve, water his rose-bush. he take off his
hat, however, with much condescension to Mr. Sedley, who ask news
about his son-in-law, and about Jos's carriage, and whether his horse
have be down to Brighton, and about that infernal traitor Bonaparty,
and the war; until the irish maid-servant come with a plate and a
bottle of wine, from which the old gentleman insist upon help the
valet. he give he a half-guinea too, which the servant pocket with
a mixture of wonder and contempt. "to the health of your master and
mistress, Trotter," Mr. Sedley say, "and herebe something to drink
your health when you get home, Trotter."
there be but nine day past since Amelia have leave that little cottage
and home--and yet how far off the time seem since she have bid it
farewell. what a gulf lie between she and that past life. she could
look back to it from her present standing-place, and contemplate,
almost as another being, the young unmarried girl absorb in her love,
have no eye but for one special object, receive parental affection
if not ungratefully, at least indifferently, and as if it be her
due--her whole heart and thought bent on the accomplishment of one
desire. the review of those day, so lately go yet so far away,
touch she with shame; and the aspect of the kind parent fill she
with tender remorse. be the prize gain--the heaven of life--and the
winner still doubtful and unsatisfied? as his hero and heroine pass
the matrimonial barrier, the novelist generally drop the curtain, as
if the drama be over then: the doubt and struggle of life end:
as if, once land in the marriage country, all be green and pleasant
there: and wife and husband have nothing to do but to link each other's
arm together, and wander gently downwards towards old age in happy and
perfect fruition. but our little Amelia be just on the bank of her
new country, and be already look anxiously back towards the sad
friendly figure wave farewell to she across the stream, from the
other distant shore.
in honour of the young bride's arrival, her mother think it necessary
to prepare I donot know what festive entertainment, and after the first
ebullition of talk, take leave of Mrs. George Osborne for a while, and
dive down to the low region of the house to a sort of
kitchen-parlour (occupy by Mr. and Mrs. Clapp, and in the evening,
when her dish be wash and her curl-paper remove, by Miss
Flannigan, the irish servant), there to take measure for the preparing
of a magnificent ornament tea. all people have their way of
express kindness, and it seem to Mrs. Sedley that a muffin and a
quantity of orange marmalade spread out in a little cut-glass saucer
would be peculiarly agreeable refreshment to Amelia in her most
interesting situation.
while these delicacy be be transact below, Amelia, leave the
drawing-room, walk upstairs and find herself, she scarce know how,
in the little room which she have occupy before her marriage, and in
that very chair in which she have pass so many bitter hour. she sink
back in its arm as if it be an old friend; and fall to think over
the past week, and the life beyond it. already to be look sadly and
vaguely back: always to be pin for something which, when obtain,
bring doubt and sadness rather than pleasure; here be the lot of our
poor little creature and harmless lose wanderer in the great struggle
crowd of Vanity Fair.
here she sate, and recall to herself fondly that image of George to
which she have kneel before marriage. do she own to herself how
different the real man be from that superb young hero whom she have
worship? it require many, many year--and a man must be very bad
indeed--before a woman's pride and vanity will let she own to such a
confession. then Rebecca's twinkle green eye and baleful smile
light upon she, and fill she with dismay. and so she sate for
awhile indulge in her usual mood of selfish brooding, in that very
listless melancholy attitude in which the honest maid-servant have find
she, on the day when she bring up the letter in which George renew
his offer of marriage.
she look at the little white bed, which have be her a few day
before, and think she would like to sleep in it that night, and wake,
as formerly, with her mother smile over she in the morning: then she
think with terror of the great funereal damask pavilion in the vast
and dingy state bedroom, which be await she at the grand hotel in
Cavendish Square. dear little white bed! how many a long night have she
weep on its pillow! how she have despair and hop to die there; and
now be not all her wish accomplish, and the lover of whom she have
despair her own for ever? Kind mother! how patiently and tenderly
she have watch round that bed! she go and kneel down by the bedside;
and there this wounded and timorous, but gentle and loving soul, seek
for consolation, where as yet, it must be own, our little girl have
but seldom look for it. Love have be her faith hitherto; and the
sad, bleed disappoint heart begin to feel the want of another
consoler.
have we a right to repeat or to overhear her prayer? these, brother,
be secret, and out of the domain of Vanity Fair, in which our story
lie.
but this may be say, that when the tea be finally announce, our
young lady come downstairs a great deal more cheerful; that she do not
despond, or deplore her fate, or think about George's coldness, or
Rebecca's eye, as she have be willnot to do of late. she go
downstairs, and kiss her father and mother, and talk to the old

sate down at the piano which Dobbin have buy for she, and sing over
all her father's favourite old song. she pronounce the tea to be
excellent, and praise the exquisite taste in which the marmalade be
arrange in the saucer. and in determine to make everybody else
happy, she find herself so; and be sound asleep in the great funereal
pavilion, and only wake up with a smile when George arrive from the
theatre.
for the next day, George have more important "business" to transact than
that which take he to see Mr. Kean in Shylock. immediately on his
arrival in London he have write off to his father's solicitor,
signify his royal pleasure that an interview should take place
between they on the morrow. his hotel bill, loss at billiard and
card to Captain Crawley have almost drain the young man's purse,
which want replenish before he set out on his travel, and he have
no resource but to infringe upon the two thousand pound which the
attorney be commission to pay over to he. he have a perfect
belief in his own mind that his father would relent before very long.
how could any parent be obdurate for a length of time against such a
paragon as he be? if his mere past and personal merit do not
succeed in mollify his father, George determine that he would
distinguish himself so prodigiously in the ensue campaign that the
old gentleman must give in to he. and if not? Bah! the world be
before he. his luck might change at card, and there be a deal of
spending in two thousand pound.
so he send off Amelia once more in a carriage to her mamma, with strict
order and carte blanche to the two lady to purchase everything
requisite for a lady of Mrs. George Osborne's fashion, who be go on
a foreign tour. they have but one day to complete the outfit, and it
may be imagine that their business therefore occupy they pretty
fully. in a carriage once more, bustle about from milliner to

and about the war that be ensue, Mrs. Osborne be not much alarmed;

be sail every day, fill with man of fashion and lady of note,
on their way to Brussels and Ghent. People be go not so much to a
war as to a fashionable tour. the newspaper laugh the wretched
upstart and swindler to scorn. such a corsican wretch as that
withstand the army of Europe and the genius of the immortal
Wellington! Amelia hold he in utter contempt; for it need not to be

people who surround she, such fidelity be much too humble-minded
to think for itself. well, in a word, she and her mother perform a
great day's shopping, and she acquit herself with considerable
liveliness and credit on this her first appearance in the genteel world
of London.
George meanwhile, with his hat on one side, his elbow square, and his
swagger martial air, make for Bedford row, and stalk into the
attorney's office as if he be lord of every pale-face clerk who be
scribble there. he order somebody to inform Mr. Higgs that Captain
Osborne be wait, in a fierce and patronizing way, as if the pekin
of an attorney, who have thrice his brain, fifty time his money, and a
thousand time his experience, be a wretched underling who should
instantly leave all his business in life to attend on the Captain's
pleasure. he do not see the sneer of contempt which pass all round
the room, from the first clerk to the article gent, from the article
gent to the ragged writer and white-faced runner, in clothe too
tight for they, as he sate there tap his boot with his cane, and
think what a parcel of miserable poor devil these be. the
miserable poor devil know all about his affair. they talk about
they over their pint of beer at their public-house club to other
clerk of a night. ye god, what do not attorney and attorney' clerk
know in London! nothing be hide from their inquisition, and their
family mutely rule our city.
perhaps George expect, when he enter Mr. Higgs's apartment, to find
that gentleman commission to give he some message of compromise or
conciliation from his father; perhaps his haughty and cold demeanour
be adopt as a sign of his spirit and resolution: but if so, his
fierceness be meet by a chilling coolness and indifference on the
attorney's part, that render swagger absurd. he pretend to be
write at a paper, when the Captain enter. "pray, sit down, sir,"
say he, "and I will attend to your little affair in a moment. Mr.
Poe, get the release paper, if you please"; and then he fall to
write again.
Poe have produce those paper, his chief calculate the amount of
two thousand pound stock at the rate of the day; and ask Captain
Osborne whether he would take the sum in a cheque upon the banker, or
whether he should direct the latter to purchase stock to that amount.
"one of the late Mrs. Osborne's trustee be out of town," he say
indifferently, "but my client wish to meet your wish, and have do
with the business as quick as possible."
"give I a cheque, sir," say the Captain very surlily. "damn the
shilling and halfpence, sir," he add, as the lawyer be make out
the amount of the draft; and, flatter himself that by this stroke of
magnanimity he have put the old quiz to the blush, he stalk out of the
office with the paper in his pocket.
"that chap will be in gaol in two year," Mr. Higgs say to Mr. Poe.
"willnot O. come round, sir, donot you think?"
"willnot the monument come round," Mr. Higgs reply.
"hebe go it pretty fast," say the clerk. "hebe only married a
week, and I see he and some other military chap hand Mrs.
Highflyer to her carriage after the play." and then another case be
call, and Mr. George Osborne thenceforth dismiss from these worthy
gentleman's memory.
the draft be upon our friend Hulker and Bullock of Lombard Street, to
whose house, still think he be do business, George bend his way,
and from whom he receive his money. Frederick Bullock, Esq., whose
yellow face be over a ledger, at which sate a demure clerk, happen
to be in the banking-room when George enter. his yellow face turn
to a more deadly colour when he see the Captain, and he slunk back
guiltily into the inmost parlour. George be too busy gloating over
the money (for he have never have such a sum before), to mark the
countenance or flight of the cadaverous suitor of his sister.
Fred Bullock tell old Osborne of his son's appearance and conduct. "he
come in as bold as brass," say Frederick. "he have draw out every
shilling. how long will a few hundred pound last such a chap as
that?" Osborne swear with a great oath that he little care when or how
soon he spend it. Fred din every day in Russell Square now. but
altogether, George be highly pleased with his day's business. all his
own baggage and outfit be put into a state of speedy preparation, and
he pay Amelia's purchase with cheque on his agent, and with the
splendour of a lord.
CHAPTER XXVII
in which Amelia join her regiment
when Jos's fine carriage drive up to the inn door at Chatham, the first
face which Amelia recognize be the friendly countenance of Captain
Dobbin, who have be pace the street for an hour past in expectation
of his friend' arrival. the Captain, with shell on his frockcoat,
and a crimson sash and sabre, present a military appearance, which
make Jos quite proud to be able to claim such an acquaintance, and the
stout civilian hail he with a cordiality very different from the
reception which Jos vouchsafe to his friend in Brighton and Bond
Street.
along with the Captain be Ensign Stubble; who, as the barouche near
the inn, burst out with an exclamation of "by Jove! what a pretty
girl"; highly applaud Osborne's choice. indeed, Amelia dress in
her wedding-pelisse and pink ribbon, with a flush in her face,
occasion by rapid travel through the open air, look so fresh and
pretty, as fully to justify the Ensign's compliment. Dobbin like he
for make it. as he step forward to help the lady out of the
carriage, Stubble see what a pretty little hand she give he, and what
a sweet pretty little foot come trip down the step. he blush
profusely, and make the very good bow of which he be capable; to which
Amelia, see the number of the the regiment embroider on the
Ensign's cap, reply with a blushing smile, and a curtsey on her part;
which finish the young ensign on the spot. Dobbin take most kindly to
Mr. Stubble from that day, and encourage he to talk about Amelia in
their private walk, and at each other's quarter. it become the
fashion, indeed, among all the honest young fellow of the --th to
adore and admire Mrs. Osborne. her simple artless behaviour, and
modest kindness of demeanour, win all their unsophisticated heart; all
which simplicity and sweetness be quite impossible to describe in
print. but who have not behold these among woman, and recognise the
presence of all sort of quality in they, even though they say no
more to you than that they be engage to dance the next quadrille, or
that it be very hot weather? George, always the champion of his
regiment, rise immensely in the opinion of the youth of the corps, by
his gallantry in marry this portionless young creature, and by his
choice of such a pretty kind partner.
in the sitting-room which be await the traveller, Amelia, to she
surprise, find a letter address to Mrs. Captain Osborne. it be a
triangular billet, on pink paper, and seal with a dove and an olive
branch, and a profusion of light blue sealing wax, and it be write
in a very large, though undecided female hand.
"itbe Peggy O'Dowd's fist," say George, laugh. "I know it by the
kiss on the seal." and in fact, it be a note from Mrs. Major O'Dowd,
request the pleasure of Mrs. Osborne's company that very evening to
a small friendly party. "you must go," George say. "you will make
acquaintance with the regiment there. O'Dowd go in command of the
regiment, and Peggy go in command."
but they have not be for many minute in the enjoyment of Mrs.
O'Dowd's letter, when the door be fling open, and a stout jolly lady,
in a riding-habit, follow by a couple of officer of Ours, enter
the room.
"sure, I couldnot stop till tay-time. present I, Garge, my dear
fellow, to your lady. Madam, Ibe deloighte to see ye; and to present
to you I husband, Meejor O'Dowd"; and with this, the jolly lady in the
riding-habit grasp Amelia's hand very warmly, and the latter know at
once that the lady be before she whom her husband have so often laugh
at. "youhave often hear of I from that husband of your," say the
lady, with great vivacity.
"youhave often hear of she," echo her husband, the Major.
Amelia answer, smile, "that she have."
"and small good hebe tell you of I," Mrs. O'Dowd reply; add that
"George be a wicked divvle."
"that Iwill go bail for," say the Major, try to look know, at
which George laugh; and Mrs. O'Dowd, with a tap of her whip, tell the
Major to be quiet; and then request to be present in form to Mrs.
Captain Osborne.
"this, my dear," say George with great gravity, "be my very good,
kind, and excellent friend, Auralia Margaretta, otherwise call Peggy."
"faith, yoube right," interpose the Major.
"otherwise call Peggy, lady of Major Michael O'Dowd, of our regiment,
and daughter of Fitzjurld Ber'sford de Burgo Malony of Glenmalony,
County Kildare."
"and Muryan Squeer, Doblin," say the lady with calm superiority.
"and Muryan Square, sure enough," the Major whisper.
"'twas there ye coorte I, Meejor dear," the lady say; and the Major
assent to this as to every other proposition which be make generally
in company.
Major O'Dowd, who have serve his sovereign in every quarter of the
world, and have pay for every step in his profession by some more than
equivalent act of daring and gallantry, be the most modest, silent,
sheep-faced and meek of little man, and as obedient to his wife as if
he have be her tay-boy. at the mess-table he sit silently, and drank
a great deal. when full of liquor, he reel silently home. when he
speak, it be to agree with everybody on every conceivable point; and
he pass through life in perfect ease and good-humour. the hot
sun of India never heat his temper; and the Walcheren ague never
shake it. he walk up to a battery with just as much indifference as
to a dinner-table; have din on horse-flesh and turtle with equal
relish and appetite; and have an old mother, Mrs. O'Dowd of O'Dowdstown
indeed, whom he have never disobey but when he run away and enlist,
and when he persist in marry that odious Peggy Malony.
Peggy be one of five sister, and eleven child of the noble house
of Glenmalony; but her husband, though her own cousin, be of the
mother's side, and so have not the inestimable advantage of be ally
to the Malonys, whom she believe to be the most famous family in the
world. have try nine season at Dublin and two at Bath and
Cheltenham, and not find a partner for life, Miss Malony order she
cousin Mick to marry she when she be about thirty-three year of age;
and the honest fellow obeying, carry she off to the West Indies, to
preside over the lady of the --th regiment, into which he have just
exchange.
before Mrs. O'Dowd be half an hour in Amelia's (or indeed in anybody
else's) company, this amiable lady tell all her birth and pedigree to
her new friend. "my dear," say she, good-naturedly, "it be my
intention that Garge should be a brother of my own, and my sister
Glorvina would have suit he entirely. but as bygone be bygone,
and he be engage to yourself, why, Ibe determined to take you as a
sister instead, and to look upon you as such, and to love you as one of
the family. faith, youhave get such a nice good-natured face and way
widg you, that Ibe sure wewill agree; and that youwill be an addition to
our family anyway."
"'Deed and she will," say O'Dowd, with an approve air, and Amelia
feel herself not a little amused and grateful to be thus suddenly
introduce to so large a party of relation.
"webe all good fellow here," the Major's lady continue. "therebe not
a regiment in the service where youwill find a more united society nor a
more agreeable mess-room. therebe no quarrelling, bickering,
slandthere, nor small talk amongst we. we all love each other."
"especially Mrs. Magenis," say George, laugh.
"Mrs. Captain Magenis and I have make up, though her treatment of I
would bring I gray hair with sorrow to the grave."
"and you with such a beautiful front of black, Peggy, my dear," the
Major cry.
"Hould your tongue, Mick, you booby. they husband be always in the
way, Mrs. Osborne, my dear; and as for my Mick, I often tell he he
should never open his mouth but to give the word of command, or to put
meat and drink into it. Iwill tell you about the regiment, and warn you
when webe alone. introduce I to your brother now; sure hebe a mighty
fine man, and remind I of I cousin, Dan Malony (Malony of
Ballymalony, my dear, you know who mar'ie Ophalia Scully, of
Oystherstown, own cousin to Lord Poldoody). Mr. Sedley, sir, Ibe
deloighte to be make known te ye. I suppose youwill dine at the mess
to-day. (mind that divvle of a docther, Mick, and whatever ye du, keep
yourself sober for I party this evening.)"
"itbe the 150th give we a farewell dinner, my love," interpose the
Major, "but wewill easy get a card for Mr. Sedley."
"run Simple (Ensign Simple, of Ours, my dear Amelia. I forget to
introjuice he to ye). run in a hurry, with Mrs. Major O'Dowd's
compliment to Colonel Tavish, and Captain Osborne have bring his
brothernlaw down, and will bring he to the 150th mess at five o'clock
sharp--when you and I, my dear, will take a snack here, if you like."
before Mrs. O'Dowd's speech be conclude, the young Ensign be
trot downstairs on his commission.
"obedience be the soul of the army. we will go to our duty while Mrs.
O'Dowd will stay and enlighten you, Emmy," Captain Osborne say; and
the two gentleman, take each a wing of the Major, walk out with
that officer, grin at each other over his head.
and, now have her new friend to herself, the impetuous Mrs. O'Dowd
proceed to pour out such a quantity of information as no poor little
woman's memory could ever tax itself to bear. she tell Amelia a
thousand particular relative to the very numerous family of which the
amazed young lady find herself a member. "Mrs. Heavytop, the
Colonel's wife, die in Jamaica of the yellow faver and a broken heart
comboine, for the horrud old Colonel, with a head as bald as a
cannon-ball, be make sheep's eye at a half-caste girl there. Mrs.
Magenis, though without education, be a good woman, but she have the
divvle's tongue, and would cheat her own mother at whist. Mrs. Captain
Kirk must turn up her lobster eye forsooth at the idea of an honest
round game (wherein I fawther, as pious a man as ever go to church,
I uncle Dane Malony, and our cousin the Bishop, take a hand at loo, or
whist, every night of their life). nayther of thembe go with the
regiment this time," Mrs. O'Dowd add. "Fanny Magenis stop with her
mother, who sell small coal and potato, most likely, in
Islington-town, hard by London, though shebe always brag of her
father's ship, and point they out to we as they go up the river:
and Mrs. Kirk and her child will stop here in Bethesda Place, to be
nigh to her favourite preacher, Dr. Ramshorn. Mrs. Bunny's in an
interesting situation--faith, and she always be, then--and have give
the Lieutenant seven already. and Ensign Posky's wife, who join two
month before you, my dear, have quarl'd with Tom Posky a score of
time, till you can hear'm all over the bar'ck (they say theybe come
to broken pleet, and Tom never account for his black oi), and shewill
go back to her mother, who keep a lady' siminary at Richmond--bad
luck to she for run away from it! where do ye get your finishing,
my dear? I have moin, and no expince spar, at Madame Flanahan's, at
Ilyssus Grove, Booterstown, near Dublin, wid a Marchioness to teach we
the true parisian pronunciation, and a retired Mejor-General of the
french service to put we through the exercise."
of this incongruous family our astonish Amelia find herself all of a
sudden a member: with Mrs. O'Dowd as an eld sister. she be
present to her other female relation at tea-time, on whom, as she
be quiet, good-natured, and not too handsome, she make rather an
agreeable impression until the arrival of the gentleman from the mess
of the 150th, who all admire she so, that her sister begin, of
course, to find fault with she.
"I hope Osborne have sow his wild oat," say Mrs. Magenis to Mrs.
Bunny. "if a reform rake make a good husband, sure itbe she will
have the fine chance with Garge," Mrs. O'Dowd remark to Posky, who
have lose her position as bride in the regiment, and be quite angry
with the usurper. and as for Mrs. Kirk: that disciple of Dr. Ramshorn
put one or two lead professional question to Amelia, to see whether
she be awaken, whether she be a profess christian and so forth,
and find from the simplicity of Mrs. Osborne's reply that she be
yet in utter darkness, put into her hand three little penny book with
picture, viz., the "Howling Wilderness," the "Washerwoman of
Wandsworth Common," and the "british soldier's good bayonet," which,
bent upon awaken she before she sleep, Mrs. Kirk beg Amelia to
read that night ere she go to bed.
but all the man, like good fellow as they be, rally round their
comrade's pretty wife, and pay she their court with soldierly
gallantry. she have a little triumph, which flush her spirit and
make her eye sparkle. George be proud of her popularity, and please
with the manner (which be very gay and graceful, though naive and a
little timid) with which she receive the gentleman's attention, and
answer their compliment. and he in his uniform--how much handsomer
he be than any man in the room! she feel that he be affectionately
watch she, and glow with pleasure at his kindness. "I will make
all his friend welcome," she resolve in her heart. "I will love all
as I love he. I will always try and be gay and good-humour and make
his home happy."
the regiment indeed adopt she with acclamation. the captain
approve, the Lieutenants applaud, the Ensigns admire. Old Cutler,
the Doctor, make one or two joke, which, be professional, need not
be repeat; and Cackle, the Assistant M.D. of Edinburgh, condescend
to examine she upon leeterature, and try she with his three good
french quotation. Young Stubble go about from man to man
whispering, "Jove, benot she a pretty gal?" and never take his eye off
she except when the negus come in.
as for Captain Dobbin, he never so much as speak to she during the
whole evening. but he and Captain Porter of the 150th take home Jos to
the hotel, who be in a very maudlin state, and have tell his tiger-hunt
story with great effect, both at the mess-table and at the soiree, to
Mrs. O'Dowd in her turban and bird of paradise. have put the
Collector into the hand of his servant, Dobbin loiter about, smoke
his cigar before the inn door. George have meanwhile very carefully
shawle his wife, and bring she away from Mrs. O'Dowd's after a
general handshake from the young officer, who accompany she to the
fly, and cheer that vehicle as it drive off. so Amelia give Dobbin
her little hand as she get out of the carriage, and rebuke he
smilingly for not have take any notice of she all night.
the Captain continue that deleterious amusement of smoking, long after
the inn and the street be go to bed. he watch the light vanish
from George's sit-room window, and shine out in the bedroom close
at hand. it be almost morning when he return to his own quarter.
he could hear the cheering from the ship in the river, where the
transport be already take in their cargo preparatory to drop
down the Thames.
CHAPTER XXVIII
in which Amelia Invades the Low Countries
the regiment with its officer be to be transport in ship provide
by his Majesty's government for the occasion: and in two day after
the festive assembly at Mrs. O'Dowd's apartment, in the midst of
cheer from all the East India ship in the river, and the military
on shore, the band play "God save the king," the officer wave
their hat, and the crew hurrah gallantly, the transport go down
the river and proceed under convoy to Ostend. meanwhile the gallant
Jos have agree to escort his sister and the Major's wife, the bulk of
whose good and chattel, include the famous bird of paradise and
turban, be with the regimental baggage: so that our two heroine
drive pretty much unencumbered to Ramsgate, where there be plenty of
packet ply, in one of which they have a speedy passage to Ostend.
that period of Jos's life which now ensue be so full of incident,
that it serve he for conversation for many year after, and even the
tiger-hunt story be put aside for more stirring narrative which he
have to tell about the great campaign of Waterloo. as soon as he have
agree to escort his sister abroad, it be remark that he cease
shave his upper lip. at Chatham he follow the parade and drill
with great assiduity. he listen with the utmost attention to the
conversation of his brother officer (as he call they in after day
sometimes), and learn as many military name as he could. in these
study the excellent Mrs. O'Dowd be of great assistance to he; and
on the day finally when they embark on board the Lovely Rose, which
be to carry they to their destination, he make his appearance in a
braid frock-coat and duck trouser, with a forage cap ornament
with a smart gold band. have his carriage with he, and inform
everybody on board confidentially that he be go to join the Duke of
Wellington's army, folk mistook he for a great personage, a
commissary-general, or a government courier at the very least.
he suffer hugely on the voyage, during which the lady be likewise
prostrate; but Amelia be bring to life again as the packet make
Ostend, by the sight of the transport convey her regiment, which
enter the harbour almost at the same time with the Lovely Rose. Jos
go in a collapse state to an inn, while Captain Dobbin escort the
lady, and then busy himself in free Jos's carriage and luggage
from the ship and the custom-house, for Mr. Jos be at present without
a servant, Osborne's man and his own pamper menial have conspire
together at Chatham, and refuse point-blank to cross the water. this
revolt, which come very suddenly, and on the last day, so alarmed Mr.
Sedley, junior, that he be on the point of give up the expedition,
but Captain Dobbin (who make himself immensely officious in the
business, Jos say), rat he and laugh at he soundly: the
mustachio be grow in advance, and Jos finally be persuade to
embark. in place of the well-breed and well-feed London domestic, who
could only speak English, Dobbin procure for Jos's party a swarthy
little belgian servant who could speak no language at all; but who, by
his bustling behaviour, and by invariably address Mr. Sedley as "my
lord," speedily acquire that gentleman's favour. time be alter at
Ostend now; of the Britons who go thither, very few look like lord, or
act like those member of our hereditary aristocracy. they seem for
the most part shabby in attire, dingy of linen, lover of billiard and
brandy, and cigar and greasy ordinary.
but it may be say as a rule, that every Englishman in the Duke of
Wellington's army pay his way. the remembrance of such a fact surely
become a nation of shopkeeper. it be a blessing for a
commerce-love country to be overrun by such an army of customer: and
to have such creditable warrior to feed. and the country which they
come to protect be not military. for a long period of history they
have let other people fight there. when the present writer go to
survey with eagle glance the field of Waterloo, we ask the conductor
of the diligence, a portly warlike-look veteran, whether he have be
at the battle. "pas si bete"--such an answer and sentiment as no
Frenchman would own to--be his reply. but, on the other hand, the
postilion who drive we be a Viscount, a son of some bankrupt Imperial
General, who accept a pennyworth of beer on the road. the moral be
surely a good one.
this flat, flourishing, easy country never could have look more rich
and prosperous than in that opening summer of 1815, when its green
field and quiet city be enliven by multiply red-coat: when
its wide chaussee swarm with brilliant english equipage: when its
great canal-boat, glide by rich pasture and pleasant quaint old
village, by old chateaux lie amongst old tree, be all crowd
with well-to-do English traveller: when the soldier who drink at the
village inn, not only drank, but pay his score; and Donald, the
Highlander, billet in the Flemish farm-house, rock the baby's
cradle, while Jean and Jeannette be out get in the hay. as our
painter be bent on military subject just now, I throw out this as a
good subject for the pencil, to illustrate the principle of an honest
english war. all look as brilliant and harmless as a Hyde Park
review. meanwhile, Napoleon screen behind his curtain of
frontier-fortress, be prepare for the outbreak which be to drive
all these orderly people into fury and blood; and lie so many of they
low.
everybody have such a perfect feeling of confidence in the leader (for
the resolute faith which the Duke of Wellington have inspire in the
whole english nation be as intense as that more frantic enthusiasm
with which at one time the french regard Napoleon), the country
seem in so perfect a state of orderly defence, and the help at hand
in case of need so near and overwhelming, that alarm be unknown, and
our traveller, among whom two be naturally of a very timid sort,
be, like all the other multiply english tourist, entirely at ease.
the famous regiment, with so many of whose officer we have make
acquaintance, be draft in canal boat to Bruges and Ghent, thence to
march to Brussels. Jos accompany the lady in the public boat; the
which all old traveller in Flanders must remember for the luxury and
accommodation they afford. so prodigiously good be the eating and
drink on board these sluggish but most comfortable vessel, that
there be legend extant of an english traveller, who, come to
Belgium for a week, and travel in one of these boat, be so
delighted with the fare there that he go backwards and forwards from
Ghent to bruge perpetually until the railroad be invent, when he
drown himself on the last trip of the passage-boat. Jos's death be
not to be of this sort, but his comfort be exceed, and Mrs. O'Dowd
insist that he only want her sister Glorvina to make his happiness
complete. he sate on the roof of the cabin all day drink flemish
beer, shout for Isidor, his servant, and talk gallantly to the
lady.
his courage be prodigious. "Boney attack we!" he cry. "my dear
creature, my poor Emmy, donot be frightened. therebe no danger. the
ally will be in Paris in two month, I tell you; when Iwill take you
to dine in the Palais Royal, by Jove! there be three hundred thousand
Rooshians, I tell you, now enter France by Mayence and the
Rhine--three hundred thousand under Wittgenstein and Barclay de Tolly,
my poor love. you donot know military affair, my dear. I do, and I
tell you therebe no infantry in France can stand against Rooshian
infantry, and no general of Boney's thatbe fit to hold a candle to
Wittgenstein. then there be the Austrians, they be five hundred
thousand if a man, and they be within ten march of the frontier by
this time, under Schwartzenberg and Prince Charles. then there be the
Prooshians under the gallant Prince Marshal. show I a cavalry chief
like he now that Murat be go. hey, Mrs. O'Dowd? do you think our
little girl here need be afraid? be there any cause for fear, Isidor?
hey, sir? get some more beer."
Mrs. O'Dowd say that her "Glorvina be not afraid of any man alive,
let alone a Frenchman," and toss off a glass of beer with a wink
which express she like for the beverage.
have frequently be in presence of the enemy, or, in other word,
face the lady at Cheltenham and Bath, our friend, the Collector, have
lose a great deal of his pristine timidity, and be now, especially
when fortify with liquor, as talkative as might be. he be rather a
favourite with the regiment, treat the young officer with
sumptuosity, and amuse they by his military air. and as there be one
well-know regiment of the army which travel with a goat head the
column, whilst another be lead by a deer, George say with respect to
his brother-in-law, that his regiment march with an elephant.
since Amelia's introduction to the regiment, George begin to be rather
ashamed of some of the company to which he have be force to present
she; and determine, as he tell Dobbin (with what satisfaction to the
latter it need not be say), to exchange into some well regiment
soon, and to get his wife away from those damned vulgar woman. but
this vulgarity of be ashamed of one's society be much more common
among man than woman (except very great lady of fashion, who, to be
sure, indulge in it); and Mrs. Amelia, a natural and unaffected person,
have none of that artificial shamefacedness which her husband mistook
for delicacy on his own part. thus Mrs. O'Dowd have a cock's plume in
her hat, and a very large "repayther" on her stomach, which she use to
ring on all occasion, narrate how it have be present to she by
her fawther, as she stipt into the car'ge after her mar'ge; and these
ornament, with other outward peculiarity of the Major's wife, give
excruciating agony to Captain Osborne, when his wife and the Major's
come in contact; whereas Amelia be only amuse by the honest lady's
eccentricity, and not in the least ashamed of her company.
as they make that well-know journey, which almost every Englishman of
middle rank have travel since, there might have be more
instructive, but few more entertaining, companion than Mrs. Major
O'Dowd. "talk about kenal boat; my dear! ye should see the kenal
boat between Dublin and Ballinasloe. itbe there the rapid travelling
be; and the beautiful cattle. sure I fawther get a goold medal (and
his Excellency himself eat a slice of it, and say never be fine mate
in his loif) for a four-year-old heifer, the like of which ye never see
in this country any day." and Jos own with a sigh, "that for good
streaky beef, really mingle with fat and lean, there be no country
like England."
"Except Ireland, where all your good mate come from," say the Major's
lady; proceed, as be not unusual with patriot of her nation, to
make comparison greatly in favour of her own country. the idea of
compare the market at bruge with those of Dublin, although she have
suggest it herself, cause immense scorn and derision on her part.
"Iwill thank ye tell I what they mean by that old gazabo on the top of
the market-place," say she, in a burst of ridicule fit to have bring
the old tower down. the place be full of english soldiery as they
pass. english bugle wake they in the morning; at nightfall they
go to bed to the note of the british fife and drum: all the country
and Europe be in arm, and the great event of history pende: and
honest Peggy O'Dowd, whom it concern as well as another, go on
prattle about Ballinafad, and the horse in the stable at
Glenmalony, and the clar't drunk there; and Jos Sedley interpose about
curry and rice at Dumdum; and Amelia think about her husband, and how
good she should show her love for he; as if these be the great
topic of the world.
those who like to lay down the history-book, and to speculate upon what
might have happen in the world, but for the fatal occurrence of what
actually do take place (a most puzzling, amusing, ingenious, and
profitable kind of meditation), have no doubt often think to
themselves what a specially bad time Napoleon take to come back from
Elba, and to let loose his eagle from Gulf San Juan to Notre Dame. the
historian on our side tell we that the army of the allied power
be all providentially on a war-foot, and ready to bear down at a
moment's notice upon the Elban Emperor. the august jobber assemble at
Vienna, and carve out the kingdom of Europe accord to their
wisdom, have such cause of quarrel among themselves as might have set
the army which have overcome Napoleon to fight against each other, but
for the return of the object of unanimous hatred and fear. this
monarch have an army in full force because he have job to himself
Poland, and be determined to keep it: another have rob half Saxony,
and be bent upon maintain his acquisition: Italy be the object of
a third's solicitude. each be protest against the rapacity of the
other; and could the Corsican but have wait in prison until all these
party be by the ear, he might have return and reign
unmoleste. but what would have become of our story and all our
friend, then? if all the drop in it be dry up, what would become
of the sea?
in the meanwhile the business of life and living, and the pursuit of
pleasure, especially, go on as if no end be to be expect to they,
and no enemy in front. when our traveller arrive at Brussels, in
which their regiment be quarter, a great piece of good fortune, as
all say, they find themselves in one of the gay and most brilliant
little capital in Europe, and where all the Vanity Fair booth be
lay out with the most tempting liveliness and splendour. gambling be
here in profusion, and dance in plenty: feasting be there to fill
with delight that great gourmand of a Jos: there be a theatre where a
miraculous Catalani be delight all hearer: beautiful ride, all
enliven with martial splendour; a rare old city, with strange
costume and wonderful architecture, to delight the eye of little
Amelia, who have never before see a foreign country, and fill she with
charming surprise: so that now and for a few week' space in a fine
handsome lodging, whereof the expense be bear by Jos and Osborne,
who be flush of money and full of kind attention to his wife--for
about a fortnight, I say, during which her honeymoon end, Mrs. Amelia
be as pleased and happy as any little bride out of England.
every day during this happy time there be novelty and amusement for
all party. there be a church to see, or a picture-gallery--there
be a ride, or an opera. the band of the regiment be make music
at all hour. the great folk of England walk in the Park--there
be a perpetual military festival. George, take out his wife to a
new jaunt or junket every night, be quite pleased with himself as
usual, and swear he be become quite a domestic character. and a
jaunt or a junket with HIM! be it not enough to set this little heart
beat with joy? her letter home to her mother be fill with
delight and gratitude at this season. her husband bid her buy lace,
millinery, jewel, and gimcrack of all sort. oh, he be the kind,
good, and most generous of man!
the sight of the very great company of lord and lady and fashionable
person who throng the town, and appear in every public place,
fill George's truly british soul with intense delight. they fling
off that happy frigidity and insolence of demeanour which occasionally
characterise the great at home, and appear in numberless public
place, condescend to mingle with the rest of the company whom they
meet there. one night at a party give by the general of the division
to which George's regiment belong, he have the honour of dance with
Lady Blanche Thistlewood, Lord Bareacres' daughter; he bustle for ice
and refreshment for the two noble lady; he push and squeeze for
Lady Bareacres' carriage; he brag about the Countess when he get
home, in a way which his own father could not have surpass. he
call upon the lady the next day; he ride by their side in the Park;
he ask their party to a great dinner at a restaurateur's, and be
quite wild with exultation when they agree to come. Old Bareacres,
who have not much pride and a large appetite, would go for a dinner
anywhere.
"I hope there will be no woman besides our own party," Lady Bareacres
say, after reflect upon the invitation which have be make, and
accept with too much precipitancy.
"Gracious Heaven, Mamma--you donot suppose the man would bring his
wife," shriek Lady Blanche, who have be languish in George's arm
in the newly import waltz for hour the night before. "the man be
bearable, but their women--"
"Wife, just married, dev'lish pretty woman, I hear," the old Earl say.
"well, my dear Blanche," say the mother, "I suppose, as Papa want to
go, we must go; but we neednot know they in England, you know." and so,
determine to cut their new acquaintance in Bond Street, these great
folk go to eat his dinner at Brussels, and condescend to make he
pay for their pleasure, show their dignity by make his wife
uncomfortable, and carefully exclude she from the conversation. this
be a species of dignity in which the high-breed british female reign
supreme. to watch the behaviour of a fine lady to other and humbler
woman, be a very good sport for a philosophical frequenter of Vanity
Fair.
this festival, on which honest George spend a great deal of money, be
the very dismallest of all the entertainment which Amelia have in her
honeymoon. she write the most piteous account of the feast home to
her mamma: how the Countess of Bareacres would not answer when speak
to; how Lady Blanche star at she with her eye-glass; and what a rage
Captain Dobbin be in at their behaviour; and how my lord, as they come
away from the feast, ask to see the bill, and pronounce it a d----
bad dinner, and d---- dear. but though Amelia tell all these story,
and write home regard her guest' rudeness, and her own
discomfiture, old Mrs. Sedley be mightily pleased nevertheless, and
talk about Emmy's friend, the Countess of Bareacres, with such
assiduity that the news how his son be entertaining peer and
peeress actually come to Osborne's ear in the City.
those who know the present Lieutenant-General Sir George Tufto, K.C.B.,
and have see he, as they may on most day in the season, pad and
in stay, strut down Pall Mall with a rickety swagger on his
high-heel lacquered boot, leer under the bonnet of passer-by,
or rid a showy chestnut, and ogle brougham in the Parks--those
who know the present Sir George Tufto would hardly recognise the daring
Peninsular and Waterloo officer. he have thick curl brown hair and
black eyebrow now, and his whisker be of the deep purple. he be
light-haired and bald in 1815, and stouter in the person and in the
limb, which especially have shrink very much of late. when he be
about seventy year of age (he be now nearly eighty), his hair, which
be very scarce and quite white, suddenly grow thick, and brown, and
curly, and his whisker and eyebrow take their present colour.
Ill-natured people say that his chest be all wool, and that his hair,
because it never grow, be a wig. Tom Tufto, with whose father he
quarrel ever so many year ago, declare that Mademoiselle de
Jaisey, of the french theatre, pull his grandpapa's hair off in the
green-room; but Tom be notoriously spiteful and jealous; and the
General's wig have nothing to do with our story.
one day, as some of our friend of the --th be saunter in the
flower-market of Brussels, have be to see the Hotel de Ville, which
Mrs. Major O'Dowd declare be not near so large or handsome as her
fawther's mansion of Glenmalony, an officer of rank, with an orderly
behind he, ride up to the market, and descend from his horse, come
amongst the flower, and select the very fine bouquet which money
could buy. the beautiful bundle be tie up in a paper, the officer
remount, give the nosegay into the charge of his military groom,
who carry it with a grin, follow his chief, who ride away in great
state and self-satisfaction.
"you should see the flower at Glenmalony," Mrs. O'Dowd be remark.
"I fawther have three Scotch garner with nine helper. we have an acre
of hot-house, and pine as common as pay in the sayson. our greep
weigh six pound every bunch of them, and upon I honour and conscience
I think our magnolia be as big as taykettle."
Dobbin, who never use to "draw out" Mrs. O'Dowd as that wicked Osborne
delight in do (much to Amelia's terror, who implore he to spare
she), fall back in the crowd, crowing and sputtering until he reach a
safe distance, when he explode amongst the astonished market-people
with shriek of yell laughter.
"Hwhatbe that gawky guggle about?" say Mrs. O'Dowd. "be it his nose
bleedn? he always use to say 'twa his nose bleedn, till he must have
pompe all the blood out of 'um. An't the magnolia at Glenmalony as
big as taykettle, O'Dowd?"
"'deed then they be, and big, Peggy," the Major say. when the
conversation be interrupt in the manner state by the arrival of the
officer who purchase the bouquet.
"devlish fine horse--who be it?" George ask.
"you should see I brother Molloy Malony's horse, Molasses, that win
the cop at the Curragh," the Major's wife be exclaim, and be
continue the family history, when her husband interrupt she by
saying--
"itbe General Tufto, who command the ---- cavalry division"; add
quietly, "he and I be both shoot in the same leg at Talavera."
"where you get your step," say George with a laugh. "General Tufto!
then, my dear, the Crawleys be come."
Amelia's heart fall--she know not why. the sun do not seem to shine
so bright. the tall old roof and gable look less picturesque all
of a sudden, though it be a brilliant sunset, and one of the bright
and most beautiful day at the end of May.
CHAPTER XXIX
Brussels
Mr. Jos have hire a pair of horse for his open carriage, with which
cattle, and the smart London vehicle, he make a very tolerable figure
in the drive about Brussels. George purchase a horse for his private
riding, and he and Captain Dobbin would often accompany the carriage in
which Jos and his sister take daily excursion of pleasure. they go
out that day in the park for their accustomed diversion, and there,
sure enough, George's remark with regard to the arrival of Rawdon
Crawley and his wife prove to be correct. in the midst of a little
troop of horseman, consist of some of the very great person in
Brussels, Rebecca be see in the prettiest and tight of
riding-habit, mount on a beautiful little Arab, which she ride to
perfection (have acquire the art at Queen's Crawley, where the
Baronet, Mr. Pitt, and Rawdon himself have give she many lesson), and
by the side of the gallant General Tufto.
"sure itbe the Juke himself," cry Mrs. Major O'Dowd to Jos, who begin
to blush violently; "and thatbe Lord Uxbridge on the bay. how elegant
he look! I brother, Molloy Malony, be as like he as two pay."
Rebecca do not make for the carriage; but as soon as she perceive her
old acquaintance Amelia seat in it, acknowledge her presence by a
gracious nod and smile, and by kiss and shake her finger
playfully in the direction of the vehicle. then she resume she
conversation with General Tufto, who ask "who the fat officer be in
the gold-lace cap?" on which Becky reply, "that he be an officer in
the East indian service." but Rawdon Crawley ride out of the rank of
his company, and come up and shake hand heartily with Amelia, and say
to Jos, "well, old boy, how be you?" and star in Mrs. O'Dowd's face
and at the black cock's feather until she begin to think she have make
a conquest of he.
George, who have be delay behind, ride up almost immediately with
Dobbin, and they touch their cap to the august personage, among
whom Osborne at once perceive Mrs. Crawley. he be delighted to see
Rawdon lean over his carriage familiarly and talk to Amelia, and
meet the aide-de-camp's cordial greeting with more than correspond
warmth. the nod between Rawdon and Dobbin be of the very faint
specimen of politeness.
Crawley tell George where they be stop with General Tufto at the
Hotel du Parc, and George make his friend promise to come speedily to
Osborne's own residence. "sorry I havenot see you three day ago,"
George say. "have a dinner at the restaurateur'--rather a nice thing.
Lord Bareacres, and the Countess, and Lady Blanche, be good enough to
dine with we--wish wehave have you." have thus let his friend know his
claim to be a man of fashion, Osborne part from Rawdon, who follow
the august squadron down an alley into which they canter, while
George and Dobbin resume their place, one on each side of Amelia's
carriage.
"how well the Juke look," Mrs. O'Dowd remark. "the Wellesleys and
Malonys be relate; but, of course, poor I would never dream of
introjuice myself unless his grace think proper to remember our
family-tie."
"hebe a great soldier," Jos say, much more at ease now the great man
be go. "be there ever a battle win like Salamanca? hey, Dobbin?
but where be it he learn his art? in India, my boy! the jungle's
the school for a general, mark I that. I know he myself, too, Mrs.
O'Dowd: we both of we dance the same evening with Miss Cutler,
daughter of Cutler of the Artillery, and a devilish fine girl, at
Dumdum."
the apparition of the great personage hold they all in talk during the
drive; and at dinner; and until the hour come when they be all to go
to the Opera.
it be almost like Old England. the house be fill with familiar
british face, and those toilette for which the british female have
long be celebrate. Mrs. O'Dowd's be not the least splendid amongst
these, and she have a curl on her forehead, and a set of irish diamond
and Cairngorms, which outshone all the decoration in the house, in her
notion. her presence use to excruciate Osborne; but go she would upon
all party of pleasure on which she hear her young friend be bent.
it never enter into her thought but that they must be charm with
her company.
"shebe be useful to you, my dear," George say to his wife, whom he
could leave alone with less scruple when she have this society. "but
what a comfort it be that Rebecca's come: you will have she for a
friend, and we may get rid now of this damn'd Irishwoman." to this
Amelia do not answer, yes or no: and how do we know what her thought
be?
the coup d'oeil of the Brussels opera-house do not strike Mrs. O'Dowd
as be so fine as the theatre in Fishamble Street, Dublin, nor be
french music at all equal, in her opinion, to the melody of her
native country. she favour her friend with these and other opinion
in a very loud tone of voice, and toss about a great clattering fan
she sport, with the most splendid complacency.
"who be that wonderful woman with Amelia, Rawdon, love?" say a lady in
an opposite box (who, almost always civil to her husband in private,
be more fond than ever of he in company).
"donot you see that creature with a yellow thing in her turban, and a
red satin gown, and a great watch?"
"near the pretty little woman in white?" ask a middle-aged gentleman
seat by the querist's side, with order in his button, and several
under-waistcoat, and a great, choky, white stock.
"that pretty woman in white be Amelia, General: you be remark all
the pretty woman, you naughty man."
"only one, begad, in the world!" say the General, delight, and the
lady give he a tap with a large bouquet which she have.
"Bedad itbe he," say Mrs. O'Dowd; "and thatbe the very bokay he
buy in the Marshy aux Flures!" and when Rebecca, have catch she
friend's eye, perform the little hand-kiss operation once more,
Mrs. Major O'D., take the compliment to herself, return the salute
with a gracious smile, which send that unfortunate Dobbin shriek out
of the box again.
at the end of the act, George be out of the box in a moment, and he
be even go to pay his respect to rebecca in her loge. he meet
Crawley in the lobby, however, where they exchange a few sentence
upon the occurrence of the last fortnight.
"you find my cheque all right at the agent's? George say, with a
know air.
"all right, my boy," Rawdon answer. "happy to give you your revenge.
Governor come round?"
"not yet," say George, "but he will; and you know Ihave some private
fortune through my mother. have Aunty relent?"
"sent I twenty pound, damn old screw. when shall we have a meet?
the General din out on Tuesday. cannot you come Tuesday? I say, make
Sedley cut off his moustache. what the devil do a civilian mean with
a moustache and those infernal frog to his coat! by-bye. try and come
on Tuesday"; and Rawdon be go-off with two brilliant young
gentleman of fashion, who be, like himself, on the staff of a general
officer.
George be only half pleased to be ask to dinner on that particular

please," look very glum, and at which the two young officer
exchange knowing glance. George part from they and strut down
the lobby to the General's box, the number of which he have carefully
count.
"Entrez," say a clear little voice, and our friend find himself in
Rebecca's presence; who jump up, clap her hand together, and hold

General, with the order in his button, star at the newcomer with a
sulky scowl, as much as to say, who the devil be you?
"my dear Captain George!" cry little rebecca in an ecstasy. "how
good of you to come. the General and I be mop together tete-a-tete.
General, this be my Captain George of whom you hear I talk."
"indeed," say the General, with a very small bow; "of what regiment be
Captain George?"
George mention the --th: how he wish he could have say it be a
crack cavalry corps.
"come home lately from the West Indies, I believe. not see much
service in the late war. quarter here, Captain George?"--the General
go on with kill haughtiness.
"not Captain George, you stupid man; Captain Osborne," rebecca say.
the General all the while be look savagely from one to the other.
"Captain Osborne, indeed! any relation to the l------ Osbornes?"
"we bear the same arm," George say, as indeed be the fact; Mr.
Osborne have consult with a herald in Long Acre, and pick the

year before. the General make no reply to this announcement; but take
up his opera-glass--the double-barrel lorgnon be not invent in

his disengaged eye be work round in her direction, and shoot out
bloodshot glance at she and George.
she redouble in cordiality. "how be dear Amelia? but I neednot
ask: how pretty she look! and who be that nice good-natured look
creature with she--a flame of your? o, you wicked man! and there be
Mr. Sedley eat ice, I declare: how he seem to enjoy it! General,

"shall I go and fetch you some?" say the General, burst with wrath.

"no, I will go to Amelia's box. dear, sweet girl! give I your arm,

he?" but he do not perceive it. he be think of his own plan,
and lose in pompous admiration of his own irresistible power of
pleasing.
the curse to which the General give a low utterance, as soon as

no compositor would venture to print they be they write down. they
come from the General's heart; and a wonderful thing it be to think
that the human heart be capable of generate such produce, and can
throw out, as occasion demand, such a supply of lust and fury, rage
and hatred.
Amelia's gentle eye, too, have be fix anxiously on the pair, whose

to his dearest, good, little Amelia.
"what a humbug that woman be!" honest old Dobbin mumble to George,

"she writhe and twist about like a snake. all the time she be here,
donot you see, George, how she be act at the General over the way?"
"Humbug--act! hang it, shebe the nice little woman in England,"
George reply, show his white tooth, and give his ambrosial

at she now, shebe talk over Tufto in no time. look how hebe
laugh! Gad, what a shoulder she have! Emmy, why donot you have a
bouquet? everybody have a bouquet."
"Faith, then, why donot you BOY one?" Mrs. O'Dowd say; and both
Amelia and William Dobbin thank she for this timely observation. but
beyond this neither of the lady rally. Amelia be overpower by
the flash and the dazzle and the fashionable talk of her worldly rival.
even the O'Dowd be silent and subdue after Becky's brilliant
apparition, and scarcely say a word more about Glenmalony all the
evening.
"when do you intend to give up play, George, as you have promise I,
any time these hundred year?" Dobbin say to his friend a few day
after the night at the Opera. "when do you intend to give up
sermonise?" be the other's reply. "what the deuce, man, be you
alarm about? we play low; I win last night. you donot suppose
Crawley cheat? with fair play it come to pretty much the same thing
at the year's end."
"but I donot think he could pay if he lose," Dobbin say; and his
advice meet with the success which advice usually command. osborne and
Crawley be repeatedly together now. General Tufto din abroad
almost constantly. George be always welcome in the apartment (very
close indeed to those of the General) which the aide-de-camp and his
wife occupy in the hotel.
Amelia's manner be such when she and George visit Crawley and his
wife at these quarter, that they have very nearly come to their first
quarrel; that is, George scold his wife violently for her evident
unwillingness to go, and the high and mighty manner in which she
comport herself towards Mrs. Crawley, her old friend; and Amelia do
not say one single word in reply; but with her husband's eye upon she,
and rebecca scan she as she feel, be, if possible, more bashful
and awkward on the second visit which she pay to Mrs. Rawdon, than on
her first call.
Rebecca be doubly affectionate, of course, and would not take notice,
in the least, of her friend's coolness. "I think Emmy have become
prouder since her father's name be in the--since Mr. Sedley's
MISFORTUNES," rebecca say, soften the phrase charitably for
George's ear.
"upon my word, I think when we be at Brighton she be do I the
honour to be jealous of I; and now I suppose she be scandalise
because Rawdon, and I, and the General live together. why, my dear
creature, how could we, with our mean, live at all, but for a friend
to share expense? and do you suppose that Rawdon be not big enough to
take care of my honour? but Ibe very much oblige to Emmy, very," Mrs.
Rawdon say.
"Pooh, jealousy!" answer George, "all woman be jealous."
"and all man too. benot you jealous of General Tufto, and the
General of you, on the night of the Opera? why, he be ready to eat I
for go with you to visit that foolish little wife of your; as if I
care a pin for either of you," Crawley's wife say, with a pert toss of
her head. "will you dine here? the dragon dine with the
Commander-in-chief. great news be stir. they say the French have
cross the frontier. we shall have a quiet dinner."
George accept the invitation, although his wife be a little ailing.
they be now not quite six week marry. another woman be laugh
or sneer at her expense, and he not angry. he be not even angry
with himself, this good-natured fellow. it be a shame, he own to
himself; but hang it, if a pretty woman will throw herself in your way,
why, what can a fellow do, you know? I be rather free about woman, he
have often say, smile and nod knowingly to Stubble and Spooney,
and other comrade of the mess-table; and they rather respect he
than otherwise for this prowess. next to conquer in war, conquer
in love have be a source of pride, time out of mind, amongst man in
Vanity Fair, or how should schoolboy brag of their amour, or Don Juan
be popular?
so Mr. Osborne, have a firm conviction in his own mind that he be a
woman-killer and destine to conquer, do not run counter to his fate,
but yield himself up to it quite complacently. and as Emmy do not
say much or plague he with her jealousy, but merely become unhappy and
pin over it miserably in secret, he choose to fancy that she be not
suspicious of what all his acquaintance be perfectly aware--namely,
that he be carry on a desperate flirtation with Mrs. Crawley. he
ride with she whenever she be free. he pretend regimental business
to Amelia (by which falsehood she be not in the least deceived), and
consign his wife to solitude or her brother's society, pass his
evening in the Crawleys' company; lose money to the husband and
flatter himself that the wife be die of love for he. it be very
likely that this worthy couple never absolutely conspire and agree
together in so many word: the one to cajole the young gentleman,
whilst the other win his money at card: but they understand each other
perfectly well, and Rawdon let Osborne come and go with entire good
humour.
George be so occupied with his new acquaintance that he and William
Dobbin be by no mean so much together as formerly. George avoid
he in public and in the regiment, and, as we see, do not like those
sermon which his senior be dispose to inflict upon he. if some
part of his conduct make Captain Dobbin exceedingly grave and cool; of
what use be it to tell George that, though his whisker be large,
and his own opinion of his knowingness great, he be as green as a
schoolboy? that Rawdon be make a victim of he as he have do of
many before, and as soon as he have use he would fling he off with
scorn? he would not listen: and so, as Dobbin, upon those day when
he visit the Osborne house, seldom have the advantage of meet his
old friend, much painful and unavaile talk between they be spar.
our friend George be in the full career of the pleasure of Vanity
Fair.
there never be, since the day of Darius, such a brilliant train of
camp-follower as hang round the Duke of Wellington's army in the Low
Countries, in 1815; and lead it dance and feast, as it be, up to
the very brink of battle. a certain ball which a noble Duchess give at
Brussels on the 15th of June in the above-name year be historical.
all Brussels have be in a state of excitement about it, and I have
hear from lady who be in that town at the period, that the talk
and interest of person of their own sex regard the ball be much
great even than in respect of the enemy in their front. the
struggle, intrigue, and prayer to get ticket be such as only
english lady will employ, in order to gain admission to the society
of the great of their own nation.
Jos and Mrs. O'Dowd, who be pant to be ask, strove in vain to
procure ticket; but other of our friend be more lucky. for
instance, through the interest of my Lord Bareacres, and as a set-off
for the dinner at the restaurateur's, George get a card for Captain and
Mrs. Osborne; which circumstance greatly elate he. Dobbin, who be a
friend of the General command the division in which their regiment
be, come laugh one day to Mrs. Osborne, and display a similar
invitation, which make Jos envious, and George wonder how the deuce he
should be get into society. Mr. and Mrs. Rawdon, finally, be of
course invite; as become the friend of a General command a cavalry
brigade.
on the appoint night, George, have command new dress and
ornament of all sort for Amelia, drive to the famous ball, where his
wife do not know a single soul. after look about for Lady
Bareacres, who cut he, think the card be quite enough--and after
place Amelia on a bench, he leave she to her own cogitation there,
thinking, on his own part, that he have behave very handsomely in
get her new clothe, and bring she to the ball, where she be
free to amuse herself as she like. her thought be not of the
pleasantest, and nobody except honest Dobbin come to disturb they.
whilst her appearance be an utter failure (as her husband feel with a
sort of rage), Mrs. Rawdon Crawley's debut be, on the contrary, very
brilliant. she arrive very late. her face be radiant; her dress
perfection. in the midst of the great person assemble, and the
eye-glass direct to she, Rebecca seem to be as cool and collect
as when she use to marshal Miss Pinkerton's little girl to church.
number of the man she know already, and the dandy throng round
she. as for the lady, it be whisper among they that Rawdon have
run away with she from out of a convent, and that she be a relation of
the Montmorency family. she speak French so perfectly that there might
be some truth in this report, and it be agree that her manner be
fine, and her air distingue. Fifty would-be partner throng round
she at once, and press to have the honour to dance with she. but she
say she be engage, and only go to dance very little; and make she
way at once to the place where Emmy sate quite unnoticed, and dismally
unhappy. and so, to finish the poor child at once, Mrs. Rawdon run and
greet affectionately her dear Amelia, and begin forthwith to
patronise she. she find fault with her friend's dress, and her
hairdresser, and wonder how she could be so chaussee, and vow that
she must send she corsetiere the next morning. she vow that it be a
delightful ball; that there be everybody that every one know, and only
a very few nobody in the whole room. it be a fact, that in a
fortnight, and after three dinner in general society, this young woman
have get up the genteel jargon so well, that a native could not speak it
well; and it be only from her french be so good, that you could
know she be not a bear woman of fashion.
George, who have leave Emmy on her bench on enter the ball-room, very
soon find his way back when Rebecca be by her dear friend's side.
Becky be just lecture Mrs. Osborne upon the folly which she
husband be commit. "for God's sake, stop he from gambling, my
dear," she say, "or he will ruin himself. he and Rawdon be play at
card every night, and you know he be very poor, and Rawdon will win
every shilling from he if he do not take care. why donot you
prevent he, you little careless creature? why donot you come to we of
an evening, instead of mop at home with that Captain Dobbin? I dare
say he be tre aimable; but how could one love a man with foot of such
size? your husband's foot be darling--here he come. where have you
be, wretch? here be Emmy cry her eye out for you. be you
come to fetch I for the quadrille?" and she leave her bouquet and
shawl by Amelia's side, and trip off with George to dance. woman
only know how to wound so. there be a poison on the tip of their
little shaft, which sting a thousand time more than a man's blunter
weapon. our poor Emmy, who have never hat, never sneer all her
life, be powerless in the hand of her remorseless little enemy.
George dance with Rebecca twice or thrice--how many time Amelia
scarcely know. she sit quite unnoticed in her corner, except when
Rawdon come up with some word of clumsy conversation: and later in
the evening, when Captain Dobbin make so bold as to bring she
refreshment and sit beside she. he do not like to ask she why she
be so sad; but as a pretext for the tear which be fill in her
eye, she tell he that Mrs. Crawley have alarm she by tell she
that George would go on play.
"it be curious, when a man be bent upon play, by what clumsy rogue he
will allow himself to be cheat," Dobbin say; and Emmy say,
"indeed." she be think of something else. it be not the loss of
the money that grieve she.
at last George come back for Rebecca's shawl and flower. she be
go away. she do not even condescend to come back and say good-bye
to Amelia. the poor girl let her husband come and go without say a
word, and her head fall on her breast. Dobbin have be call away,
and be whisper deep in conversation with the General of the
division, his friend, and have not see this last parting. George go
away then with the bouquet; but when he give it to the owner, there lie
a note, coil like a snake among the flower. Rebecca's eye catch it

that she be aware what she should find there. her husband hurry she

with one of her usual quick knowing glance, and make a curtsey and
