remark of Crawley's, do not hear it even, his brain be so throb
with triumph and excitement, and allow they to go away without a word.
his wife see the one part at least of the bouquet-scene. it be quite
natural that George should come at Rebecca's request to get her her
scarf and flower: it be no more than he have do twenty time before
in the course of the last few day; but now it be too much for she.
"William," she say, suddenly cling to Dobbin, who be near she,
"youhave always be very kind to I--I'm--i'm not well. take I home."
she do not know she call he by his christian name, as George be
accustom to do. he go away with she quickly. her lodging be
hard by; and they thread through the crowd without, where everything
seem to be more astir than even in the ball-room within.
George have be angry twice or thrice at find his wife up on his
return from the party which he frequent: so she go straight to
bed now; but although she do not sleep, and although the din and
clatter, and the galloping of horseman be incessant, she never hear
any of these noise, have quite other disturbance to keep she awake.
Osborne meanwhile, wild with elation, go off to a play-table, and
begin to bet frantically. he win repeatedly. "everything succeed with
I to-night," he say. but his luck at play even do not cure he of
his restlessness, and he start up after awhile, pocket his
winning, and go to a buffet, where he drink off many bumper of wine.
here, as he be rattle away to the people around, laugh loudly and
wild with spirit, Dobbin find he. he have be to the card-table to
look there for his friend. Dobbin look as pale and grave as his
comrade be flushed and jovial.
"Hullo, Dob! come and drink, old Dob! the Duke's wine be famous. give
I some more, you sir"; and he hold out a trembling glass for the
liquor.
"come out, George," say Dobbin, still gravely; "donot drink."
"drink! therebe nothing like it. drink yourself, and light up your
lantern jaw, old boy. herebe to you."
Dobbin go up and whisper something to he, at which George, give
a start and a wild hurray, toss off his glass, clap it on the
table, and walk away speedily on his friend's arm. "the enemy have
pass the Sambre," William say, "and our left be already engage.
come away. we be to march in three hour."
Away go George, his nerve quiver with excitement at the news so
long look for, so sudden when it come. what be love and intrigue
now? he think about a thousand thing but these in his rapid walk to
his quarter--his past life and future chance--the fate which might be
before he--the wife, the child perhaps, from whom unseen he might be
about to part. oh, how he wish that night's work undo! and that
with a clear conscience at least he might say farewell to the tender
and guileless be by whose love he have set such little store!
he think over his brief married life. in those few week he have
frightfully dissipate his little capital. how wild and reckless he
have be! should any mischance befall he: what be then leave for
she? how unworthy he be of she. why have he marry she? he be not
fit for marriage. why have he disobey his father, who have be always
so generous to he? Hope, remorse, ambition, tenderness, and selfish
regret fill his heart. he sate down and write to his father,
remember what he have say once before, when he be engage to fight
a duel. Dawn faintly streak the sky as he close this farewell
letter. he seal it, and kiss the superscription. he think how he
have desert that generous father, and of the thousand kindness which
the stern old man have do he.
he have look into Amelia's bedroom when he enter; she lie quiet, and
her eye seem closed, and he be glad that she be asleep. on
arrive at his quarter from the ball, he have find his regimental
servant already make preparation for his departure: the man have
understand his signal to be still, and these arrangement be very
quickly and silently make. should he go in and wake Amelia, he
think, or leave a note for her brother to break the news of departure
to she? he go in to look at she once again.
she have be awake when he first enter her room, but have keep she
eye close, so that even her wakefulness should not seem to reproach
he. but when he have return, so soon after herself, too, this timid
little heart have feel more at ease, and turn towards he as he stept
softly out of the room, she have fall into a light sleep. George come
in and look at she again, enter still more softly. by the pale
night-lamp he could see her sweet, pale face--the purple eyelid be
fringe and closed, and one round arm, smooth and white, lie outside of
the coverlet. good God! how pure she be; how gentle, how tender, and
how friendless! and he, how selfish, brutal, and black with crime!
heart-stain, and shame-stricken, he stand at the bed's foot, and
look at the sleep girl. how dare he--who be he, to pray for one
so spotless! God bless she! God bless she! he come to the bedside,
and look at the hand, the little soft hand, lie asleep; and he bend
over the pillow noiselessly towards the gentle pale face.
two fair arm close tenderly round his neck as he stoop down. "I be
awake, George," the poor child say, with a sob fit to break the little
heart that nestle so closely by his own. she be awake, poor soul,
and to what? at that moment a bugle from the Place of Arms begin
sound clearly, and be take up through the town; and amidst the
drum of the infantry, and the shrill pipe of the Scotch, the whole
city awoke.
CHAPTER XXX
"the Girl I leave behind I"
we do not claim to rank among the military novelist. our place be with
the non-combatant. when the deck be clear for action we go below
and wait meekly. we should only be in the way of the manoeuvre that
the gallant fellow be perform overhead. we shall go no far
with the --th than to the city gate: and leave Major O'Dowd to his
duty, come back to the Major's wife, and the lady and the baggage.
now the Major and his lady, who have not be invite to the ball at
which in our last chapter other of our friend figure, have much more
time to take their wholesome natural rest in bed, than be accord to
people who wish to enjoy pleasure as well as to do duty. "itbe my
belief, Peggy, my dear," say he, as he placidly pull his nightcap
over his ear, "that there will be such a ball dance in a day or two
as some of them have never hear the chune of"; and he be much more
happy to retire to rest after partaking of a quiet tumbler, than to
figure at any other sort of amusement. Peggy, for her part, would have
like to have show her turban and bird of paradise at the ball, but
for the information which her husband have give she, and which make she
very grave.
"Iwould like ye wake I about half an hour before the assembly beat," the
Major say to his lady. "call I at half-past one, Peggy dear, and see
I thing be ready. may be Iwill not come back to breakfast, Mrs. o'd."
with which word, which signify his opinion that the regiment would
march the next morning, the Major cease talking, and fall asleep.
Mrs. O'Dowd, the good housewife, array in curl paper and a camisole,
feel that her duty be to act, and not to sleep, at this juncture.
"time enough for that," she say, "when Mick's go"; and so she pack
his travel valise ready for the march, brush his cloak, his cap,
and other warlike habiliment, set they out in order for he; and
stow away in the cloak pocket a light package of portable
refreshment, and a wicker-cover flask or pocket-pistol, contain
near a pint of a remarkably sound Cognac brandy, of which she and the
Major approve very much; and as soon as the hand of the "repayther"
point to half-past one, and its interior arrangement (it have a tone
quite equal to a cathaydral, its fair owner consider) knell forth
that fatal hour, Mrs. O'Dowd wake up her Major, and have as comfortable
a cup of coffee prepare for he as any make that morning in Brussels.
and who be there will deny that this worthy lady's preparation
betoken affection as much as the fit of tear and hysteric by which
more sensitive female exhibit their love, and that their partaking
of this coffee, which they drink together while the bugle be
sound the turn-out and the drum beat in the various quarter of
the town, be not more useful and to the purpose than the outpouring of
any mere sentiment could be? the consequence be, that the Major
appear on parade quite trim, fresh, and alert, his well-shave rosy
countenance, as he sate on horseback, give cheerfulness and
confidence to the whole corps. all the officer salute she when the
regiment march by the balcony on which this brave woman stand, and
wave they a cheer as they pass; and I daresay it be not from want
of courage, but from a sense of female delicacy and propriety, that she
refrain from lead the gallant--th personally into action.
on Sundays, and at period of a solemn nature, Mrs. O'Dowd use to read
with great gravity out of a large volume of her uncle the Dean's
sermon. it have be of great comfort to she on board the transport as
they be come home, and be very nearly wrecked, on their return
from the West Indies. after the regiment's departure she betook
herself to this volume for meditation; perhaps she do not understand
much of what she be read, and her thought be elsewhere: but the
sleep project, with poor Mick's nightcap there on the pillow, be quite
a vain one. so it be in the world. Jack or Donald march away to
glory with his knapsack on his shoulder, step out briskly to the
tune of "the Girl I leave behind I." it be she who remain and
suffer--and have the leisure to think, and brood, and remember.
know how useless regret be, and how the indulgence of sentiment
only serve to make people more miserable, Mrs. Rebecca wisely
determine to give way to no vain feeling of sorrow, and bear the
parting from her husband with quite a spartan equanimity. indeed
Captain Rawdon himself be much more affected at the leave-taking than
the resolute little woman to whom he bid farewell. she have master
this rude coarse nature; and he love and worship she with all his
faculty of regard and admiration. in all his life he have never be
so happy, as, during the past few month, his wife have make he. all
former delight of turf, mess, hunting-field, and gambling-table; all
previous love and courtship of milliner, opera-dancer, and the like
easy triumph of the clumsy military Adonis, be quite insipid when
compare to the lawful matrimonial pleasure which of late he have
enjoy. she have know perpetually how to divert he; and he have find
his house and her society a thousand time more pleasant than any place
or company which he have ever frequent from his childhood until now.
and he curse his past folly and extravagance, and bemoan his vast
outlying debt above all, which must remain for ever as obstacle to
prevent his wife's advancement in the world. he have often groan over
these in midnight conversation with Rebecca, although as a bachelor
they have never give he any disquiet. he himself be strike with this
phenomenon. "hang it," he would say (or perhaps use a still strong
expression out of his simple vocabulary), "before I be married I
donot care what bill I put my name to, and so long as Moses would
wait or Levy would renew for three month, I keep on never mind.
but since Ibe marry, except renew, of course, I give you my honour
Ihave not touch a bit of stamp paper."
rebecca always know how to conjure away these mood of melancholy.
"why, my stupid love," she would say, "we have not do with your aunt
yet. if she fail we, benot there what you call the Gazette? or, stop,
when your uncle Bute's life drop, I have another scheme. the living
have always belong to the young brother, and why shouldnot you sell
out and go into the Church?" the idea of this conversion set Rawdon
into roar of laughter: you might have hear the explosion through the
hotel at midnight, and the haw-haw of the great dragoon's voice.
General Tufto hear he from his quarter on the first floor above
they; and Rebecca act the scene with great spirit, and preach
Rawdon's first sermon, to the immense delight of the General at
breakfast.
but these be mere by-go day and talk. when the final news arrive
that the campaign be open, and the troop be to march, Rawdon's
gravity become such that Becky rally he about it in a manner which
rather hurt the feeling of the Guardsman. "you donot suppose Ibe
afraid, Becky, I should think," he say, with a tremor in his voice.
"but Ibe a pretty good mark for a shot, and you see if it bring I
down, why I leave one and perhaps two behind I whom I should wish to
provide for, as I bring them into the scrape. it be no laughing
matter that, Mrs. C., anyway."
rebecca by a hundred caress and kind word try to soothe the
feeling of the wound lover. it be only when her vivacity and sense
of humour get the well of this sprightly creature (as they would do
under most circumstance of life indeed) that she would break out with
her satire, but she could soon put on a demure face. "dearest love,"
she say, "do you suppose I feel nothing?" and hastily dash
something from her eye, she look up in her husband's face with a
smile.
"look here," say he. "if I drop, let we see what there be for you. I
have have a pretty good run of luck here, and herebe two hundred and
thirty pound. I have get ten napoleon in my pocket. that be as much
as I shall want; for the General pay everything like a prince; and if
Ibe hit, why you know I cost nothing. donot cry, little woman; I may
live to vex you yet. well, I shallnot take either of my horse, but
shall ride the General's grey charger: itbe cheap, and I tell he
mine be lame. if Ibe do, those two ought to fetch you something.
Grigg offer ninety for the mare yesterday, before this confound
news come, and like a fool I wouldnot let she go under the two o's.
Bullfinch will fetch his price any day, only youwould well sell he in
this country, because the dealer have so many bill of mine, and so
Iwould rather he shouldnot go back to England. your little mare the
General give you will fetch something, and therebe no d--d livery
stable bill here as there be in London," Rawdon add, with a laugh.
"therebe that dressing-case cost I two hundred--that be, I owe two for
it; and the gold top and bottle must be worth thirty or forty.
please to put that up the spout, ma'am, with my pin, and ring, and
watch and chain, and thing. they cost a precious lot of money. Miss
Crawley, I know, pay a hundred down for the chain and ticker. gold
top and bottle, indeed! dammy, Ibe sorry I donot take more now.
Edwards press on I a silver-gilt boot-jack, and I might have have a
dressing-case fit up with a silver warming-pan, and a service of
plate. but we must make the good of what wehave get, Becky, you know."
and so, make his last disposition, Captain Crawley, who have seldom
think about anything but himself, until the last few month of his
life, when Love have obtain the mastery over the dragoon, go through
the various item of his little catalogue of effect, strive to see
how they might be turn into money for his wife's benefit, in case any
accident should befall he. he please himself by note down with a
pencil, in his big schoolboy handwriting, the various item of his
portable property which might be sell for his widow's advantage as, for
example, "my double-barril by Manton, say 40 guineas; my driving cloak,
line with sable fur, 50 pound; my duel pistol in rosewood case
(same which I shoot Captain Marker), 20 pound; my regulation
saddle-holster and housing; my Laurie ditto," and so forth, over all
of which article he make Rebecca the mistress.
faithful to his plan of economy, the Captain dress himself in his
old and shabby uniform and epaulet, leave the new behind,
under his wife's (or it might be his widow's) guardianship. and this
famous dandy of Windsor and Hyde Park go off on his campaign with a
kit as modest as that of a sergeant, and with something like a prayer
on his lip for the woman he be leave. he take she up from the
ground, and hold she in his arm for a minute, tight pressed against
his strong-beat heart. his face be purple and his eye dim, as he
put she down and leave she. he ride by his General's side, and smoke
his cigar in silence as they hasten after the troop of the General's
brigade, which precede they; and it be not until they be some mile
on their way that he leave off twirl his moustache and break silence.
and Rebecca, as we have say, wisely determined not to give way to
unavaile sentimentality on her husband's departure. she wave he an
adieu from the window, and stand there for a moment look out after
he be go. the cathedral tower and the full gable of the quaint old
house be just begin to blush in the sunrise. there have be no
rest for she that night. she be still in her pretty ball-dress, her
fair hair hang somewhat out of curl on her neck, and the circle
round her eye dark with watching. "what a fright I seem," she say,
examine herself in the glass, "and how pale this pink make one
look!" so she divest herself of this pink raiment; in do which a
note fall out from her corsage, which she pick up with a smile, and
lock into her dressing-box. and then she put her bouquet of the ball
into a glass of water, and go to bed, and sleep very comfortably.
the town be quite quiet when she wake up at ten o'clock, and partook
of coffee, very requisite and comfort after the exhaustion and grief
of the morning's occurrence.
this meal over, she resume honest Rawdon's calculation of the night
previous, and survey her position. should the bad befall, all
thing consider, she be pretty well to do. there be her own
trinket and trousseau, in addition to those which her husband have leave
behind. Rawdon's generosity, when they be first marry, have already
be describe and laud. besides these, and the little mare, the
General, her slave and worshipper, have make she many very handsome
present, in the shape of cashmere shawl buy at the auction of a
bankrupt french general's lady, and numerous tribute from the
jeweller' shop, all of which betoken her admirer's taste and
wealth. as for "ticker," as poor Rawdon call watch, her
apartment be alive with their clicking. for, happen to mention
one night that her, which Rawdon have give to she, be of English
workmanship, and go ill, on the very next morning there come to she a
little bijou mark Leroy, with a chain and cover charmingly set with
turquoise, and another sign Brequet, which be cover with pearl,
and yet scarcely big than a half-crown. General Tufto have buy
one, and Captain Osborne have gallantly present the other. Mrs.
Osborne have no watch, though, to do George justice, she might have have
one for the asking, and the honourable Mrs. Tufto in England have an old
instrument of her mother's that might have serve for the plate-warming
pan which Rawdon talk about. if Messrs. Howell and James be to
publish a list of the purchaser of all the trinket which they sell,
how surprised would some family be: and if all these ornament go
to gentleman's lawful wife and daughter, what a profusion of
jewellery there would be exhibit in the genteelest home of Vanity
Fair!
every calculation make of these valuable Mrs. Rebecca find, not
without a pungent feeling of triumph and self-satisfaction, that should
circumstance occur, she might reckon on six or seven hundred pound at
the very least, to begin the world with; and she pass the morning
disposing, order, look out, and lock up her property in the
most agreeable manner. among the note in Rawdon's pocket-book be a
draft for twenty pound on Osborne's banker. this make she think about
Mrs. Osborne. "I will go and get the draft cash," she say, "and pay
a visit afterwards to poor little Emmy." if this be a novel without a
hero, at least let we lay claim to a heroine. no man in the british
army which have march away, not the great Duke himself, could be more
cool or collect in the presence of doubt and difficulty, than the
indomitable little aide-de-camp's wife.
and there be another of our acquaintance who be also to be leave
behind, a non-combatant, and whose emotion and behaviour we have
therefore a right to know. this be our friend the ex-collector of
Boggley Wollah, whose rest be break, like other people's, by the
sounding of the bugle in the early morning. be a great sleeper,
and fond of his bed, it be possible he would have snooze on until his
usual hour of rise in the forenoon, in spite of all the drum,
bugle, and bagpipe in the british army, but for an interruption,
which do not come from George Osborne, who share Jos's quarter with
he, and be as usual occupy too much with his own affair or with
grief at part with his wife, to think of take leave of his
slumber brother-in-law--it be not George, we say, who interpose
between Jos Sedley and sleep, but Captain Dobbin, who come and rouse
he up, insist on shake hand with he before his departure.
"very kind of you," say Jos, yawning, and wish the Captain at the
deuce.
"I--I donot like to go off without say good-bye, you know," Dobbin
say in a very incoherent manner; "because you know some of we maynot
come back again, and I like to see you all well, and--and that sort of
thing, you know."
"what do you mean?" Jos ask, rub his eye. the Captain do not
in the least hear he or look at the stout gentleman in the nightcap,
about whom he profess to have such a tender interest. the hypocrite
be look and listen with all his might in the direction of
George's apartment, stride about the room, upset the chair,
beat the tattoo, bite his nail, and show other sign of great
inward emotion.
Jos have always have rather a mean opinion of the Captain, and now begin
to think his courage be somewhat equivocal. "what be it I can do for
you, Dobbin?" he say, in a sarcastic tone.
"I tell you what you can do," the Captain reply, come up to the
bed; "we march in a quarter of an hour, Sedley, and neither George nor
I may ever come back. mind you, you be not to stir from this town
until you ascertain how thing go. you be to stay here and watch over
your sister, and comfort she, and see that no harm come to she. if
anything happen to George, remember she have no one but you in the
world to look to. if it go wrong with the army, youwill see her safe
back to England; and you will promise I on your word that you will
never desert she. I know you willnot: as far as money go, you be
always free enough with that. do you want any? I mean, have you enough
gold to take you back to England in case of a misfortune?"
"Sir," say Jos, majestically, "when I want money, I know where to ask
for it. and as for my sister, you neednot tell I how I ought to
behave to she."
"you speak like a man of spirit, Jos," the other answer good-naturedly,
"and I be glad that George can leave she in such good hand.
so I may give he your word of honour, may I, that in case of extremity
you will stand by she?"
"of course, of course," answer Mr. Jos, whose generosity in money
matter Dobbin estimate quite correctly.
"and youwill see she safe out of Brussels in the event of a defeat?"
"a defeat! D---- it, sir, itbe impossible. donot try and frighten I,"
the hero cry from his bed; and Dobbin's mind be thus perfectly set
at ease now that Jos have speak out so resolutely respect his
conduct to his sister. "at least," think the Captain, "there will be
a retreat secure for she in case the bad should ensue."
if Captain Dobbin expect to get any personal comfort and satisfaction
from have one more view of Amelia before the regiment march away,
his selfishness be punish just as such odious egotism deserve to
be. the door of Jos's bedroom open into the sitting-room which be
common to the family party, and opposite this door be that of Amelia's
chamber. the bugle have waken everybody: there be no use in
concealment now. George's servant be pack in this room: Osborne
come in and out of the contiguous bedroom, fling to the man such
article as he think fit to carry on the campaign. and presently
Dobbin have the opportunity which his heart covet, and he get sight of
Amelia's face once more. but what a face it be! so white, so wild
and despair-stricken, that the remembrance of it haunt he afterwards
like a crime, and the sight smite he with inexpressible pang of
longing and pity.
she be wrap in a white morning dress, her hair fall on her
shoulder, and her large eye fix and without light. by way of
help on the preparation for the departure, and show that she too
could be useful at a moment so critical, this poor soul have take up a
sash of George's from the drawer whereon it lay, and follow he to
and fro with the sash in her hand, look on mutely as his packing
proceed. she come out and stand, lean at the wall, hold this
sash against her bosom, from which the heavy net of crimson drop
like a large stain of blood. our gentle-hearted Captain feel a guilty
shock as he look at she. "good God," think he, "and be it grief
like this I dare to pry into?" and there be no help: no mean to
soothe and comfort this helpless, speechless misery. he stand for a
moment and look at she, powerless and tear with pity, as a parent
regard an infant in pain.
at last, George take Emmy's hand, and lead she back into the bedroom,
from whence he come out alone. the parting have take place in that
moment, and he be go.
"thank Heaven that be over," George thought, bound down the stair,
his sword under his arm, as he run swiftly to the alarm ground, where
the regiment be muster, and whither troop man and officer
hurrying from their billet; his pulse be throb and his cheek
flush: the great game of war be go to be play, and he one of
the player. what a fierce excitement of doubt, hope, and pleasure!
what tremendous hazard of loss or gain! what be all the game of
chance he have ever play compare to this one? into all contest
require athletic skill and courage, the young man, from his boyhood
upwards, have fling himself with all his might. the champion of his
school and his regiment, the bravos of his companion have follow he
everywhere; from the boy' cricket-match to the garrison-race, he have
win a hundred of triumph; and wherever he go woman and man have
admire and envy he. what quality be there for which a man get
so speedy a return of applause, as those of bodily superiority,
activity, and valour? time out of mind strength and courage have be
the theme of bard and romance; and from the story of Troy down to
to-day, poetry have always choose a soldier for a hero. I wonder be it
because man be coward in heart that they admire bravery so much, and
place military valour so far beyond every other quality for reward and
worship?
so, at the sound of that stirring call to battle, George jump away
from the gentle arm in which he have be dally; not without a
feeling of shame (although his wife's hold on he have be but feeble),
that he should have be detain there so long. the same feeling of
eagerness and excitement be amongst all those friend of his of whom
we have have occasional glimpse, from the stout senior Major, who lead
the regiment into action, to little Stubble, the Ensign, who be to
bear its colour on that day.
the sun be just rise as the march begin--it be a gallant sight--the
band lead the column, play the regimental march--then come the
Major in command, rid upon Pyramus, his stout charger--then march
the grenadier, their captain at their head; in the centre be the
colour, bear by the senior and junior Ensigns--then George come
marching at the head of his company. he look up, and smile at
Amelia, and pass on; and even the sound of the music die away.
CHAPTER XXXI
in which Jos Sedley take Care of his sister
thus all the superior officer be summon on duty elsewhere, Jos
Sedley be leave in command of the little colony at Brussels, with
Amelia invalid, Isidor, his belgian servant, and the bonne, who be
maid-of-all-work for the establishment, as a garrison under he. though
he be disturb in spirit, and his rest destroy by Dobbin's
interruption and the occurrence of the morning, Jos nevertheless
remain for many hour in bed, wakeful and roll about there until
his usual hour of rise have arrive. the sun be high in the heaven,
and our gallant friend of the --th mile on their march, before the
civilian appear in his flowered dressing-gown at breakfast.
about George's absence, his brother-in-law be very easy in mind.
perhaps Jos be rather pleased in his heart that Osborne be go, for
during George's presence, the other have play but a very secondary
part in the household, and Osborne do not scruple to show his contempt
for the stout civilian. but Emmy have always be good and attentive to
he. it be she who minister to his comfort, who superintend the
dish that he like, who walk or ride with he (as she have many, too
many, opportunity of do, for where be George?) and who interpose
her sweet face between his anger and her husband's scorn. many timid
remonstrance have she utter to George in behalf of her brother, but
the former in his trenchant way cut these entreaty short. "Ibe an
honest man," he say, "and if I have a feeling I show it, as an honest
man will. how the deuce, my dear, would you have I behave
respectfully to such a fool as your brother?" so Jos be pleased with
George's absence. his plain hat, and glove on a sideboard, and the
idea that the owner be away, cause Jos I donot know what secret
thrill of pleasure. "he willnot be trouble I this morning," Jos
thought, "with his dandified air and his impudence."
"put the Captain's hat into the ante-room," he say to Isidor, the
servant.
"perhaps he willnot want it again," reply the lackey, look knowingly
at his master. he hat George too, whose insolence towards he be
quite of the English sort.
"and ask if Madame be come to breakfast," Mr. Sedley say with great
majesty, ashame to enter with a servant upon the subject of his
dislike for George. the truth be, he have abuse his brother to the
valet a score of time before.
alas! Madame could not come to breakfast, and cut the tartine that
Mr. Jos like. Madame be a great deal too ill, and have be in a
frightful state ever since her husband's departure, so her bonne say.
Jos show his sympathy by pour she out a large cup of tea it be
his way of exhibit kindness: and he improve on this; he not only
send her breakfast, but he bethink he what delicacy she would most
like for dinner.
Isidor, the valet, have look on very sulkily, while Osborne's servant
be dispose of his master's baggage previous to the Captain's
departure: for in the first place he hat Mr. Osborne, whose conduct
to he, and to all inferior, be generally overbearing (nor do the
continental domestic like to be treat with insolence as our own
well-temper servant do), and secondly, he be angry that so many
valuable should be remove from under his hand, to fall into other
people's possession when the English discomfiture should arrive. of
this defeat he and a vast number of other person in Brussels and
Belgium do not make the slight doubt. the almost universal belief
be, that the Emperor would divide the prussian and english army,
annihilate one after the other, and march into Brussels before three
day be over: when all the movable of his present master, who would
be kill, or fugitive, or prisoner, would lawfully become the
property of Monsieur Isidor.
as he help Jos through his toilsome and complicated daily toilette,
this faithful servant would calculate what he should do with the very
article with which he be decorate his master's person. he would
make a present of the silver essence-bottle and toilet knicknack to a
young lady of whom he be fond; and keep the English cutlery and the
large ruby pin for himself. it would look very smart upon one of the
fine frilled shirt, which, with the gold-laced cap and the frogged
frock coat, that might easily be cut down to suit his shape, and the
Captain's gold-head cane, and the great double ring with the ruby,
which he would have make into a pair of beautiful earring, he
calculate would make a perfect Adonis of himself, and render
Mademoiselle Reine an easy prey. "how those sleeve-button will suit
I!" think he, as he fix a pair on the fat pudgy wrist of Mr.
Sedley. "I long for sleeve-button; and the Captain's boot with brass
spur, in the next room, corbleu! what an effect they will make in the
Allee Verte!" so while Monsieur Isidor with bodily finger be hold
on to his master's nose, and shave the low part of Jos's face, his
imagination be ramble along the Green Avenue, dress out in a
frog coat and lace, and in company with Mademoiselle Reine; he be
loiter in spirit on the bank, and examine the barge sailing
slowly under the cool shadow of the tree by the canal, or refresh
himself with a mug of Faro at the bench of a beer-house on the road to
Laeken.
but Mr. Joseph Sedley, luckily for his own peace, no more know what be
pass in his domestic's mind than the respected reader, and I suspect
what John or Mary, whose wage we pay, think of ourselves. what our
servant think of us!--did we know what our intimate and dear
relation thought of we, we should live in a world that we should be
glad to quit, and in a frame of mind and a constant terror, that would
be perfectly unbearable. so Jos's man be mark his victim down, as
you see one of Mr. Paynter's assistant in Leadenhall Street ornament
an unconscious turtle with a placard on which be write, "Soup
to-morrow."
Amelia's attendant be much less selfishly dispose. few dependent
could come near that kind and gentle creature without pay their
usual tribute of loyalty and affection to her sweet and affectionate
nature. and it be a fact that Pauline, the cook, console her mistress
more than anybody whom she see on this wretched morning; for when she
find how Amelia remain for hour, silent, motionless, and haggard,
by the window in which she have place herself to watch the last
bayonet of the column as it march away, the honest girl take the
lady's hand, and say, Tenez, Madame, est-ce qu'il n'est pa aussi a
l'armee, mon homme a moi? with which she burst into tear, and Amelia
fall into her arm, do likewise, and so each pitied and soothe the
other.
several time during the forenoon Mr. Jos's Isidor go from his
lodging into the town, and to the gate of the hotel and lodging-house
round about the Parc, where the English be congregate, and
there mingle with other valet, courier, and lackey, gather such
news as be abroad, and bring back bulletin for his master's
information. almost all these gentleman be in heart partisan of the
Emperor, and have their opinion about the speedy end of the campaign.
the Emperor's proclamation from Avesnes have be distribute everywhere
plentifully in Brussels. "soldier!" it say, "this be the
anniversary of Marengo and Friedland, by which the destiny of Europe
be twice decide. then, as after Austerlitz, as after Wagram, we
be too generous. we believe in the oath and promise of prince
whom we suffer to remain upon their throne. let we march once more
to meet they. we and they, be we not still the same man? soldier!
these same Prussians who be so arrogant to-day, be three to one
against you at Jena, and six to one at Montmirail. those among you who
be prisoner in England can tell their comrade what frightful
torment they suffer on board the english hulk. Madmen! a moment
of prosperity have blind they, and if they enter into France it will
be to find a grave there!" but the partisan of the French prophesy
a more speedy extermination of the Emperor's enemy than this; and it
be agree on all hand that Prussians and British would never return
except as prisoner in the rear of the conquering army.
these opinion in the course of the day be bring to operate upon
Mr. Sedley. he be tell that the Duke of Wellington have go to try
and rally his army, the advance of which have be utterly crush the
night before.
"crush, psha!" say Jos, whose heart be pretty stout at
breakfast-time. "the Duke have go to beat the Emperor as he have
beat all his general before."
"his paper be burn, his effect be remove, and his quarter be
be get ready for the Duke of Dalmatia," Jos's informant reply. "I
have it from his own maitre d'hotel. Milor Duc de Richemont's people
be pack up everything. his Grace have flee already, and the Duchess
be only wait to see the plate pack to join the King of France at
Ostend."
"the king of France be at Ghent, fellow," reply Jos, affect
incredulity.
"he flee last night to bruge, and embark today from Ostend. the Duc
de Berri be take prisoner. those who wish to be safe have well go
soon, for the dyke will be open to-morrow, and who can fly when the
whole country be under water?"
"Nonsense, sir, we be three to one, sir, against any force Boney can
bring into the field," Mr. Sedley object; "the Austrians and the
Russians be on their march. he must, he shall be crush," Jos say,
slap his hand on the table.
"the Prussians be three to one at Jena, and he take their army and
kingdom in a week. they be six to one at Montmirail, and he
scatter they like sheep. the austrian army be come, but with the
Empress and the king of Rome at its head; and the Russians, bah! the
Russians will withdraw. no quarter be to be give to the English, on
account of their cruelty to our brave on board the infamous pontoon.
look here, here it be in black and white. herebe the proclamation of
his Majesty the Emperor and King," say the now declare partisan of
Napoleon, and take the document from his pocket, Isidor sternly
thrust it into his master's face, and already look upon the frogged
coat and valuable as his own spoil.
Jos be, if not seriously alarm as yet, at least considerably
disturb in mind. "give I my coat and cap, sir," say he, "and follow
I. I will go myself and learn the truth of these report." Isidor be
furious as Jos put on the braided frock. "Milor have well not wear
that military coat," say he; "the Frenchmen have swear not to give
quarter to a single british soldier."
"silence, sirrah!" say Jos, with a resolute countenance still, and
thrust his arm into the sleeve with indomitable resolution, in the
performance of which heroic act he be find by Mrs. Rawdon Crawley,
who at this juncture come up to visit Amelia, and enter without
ring at the antechamber door.
Rebecca be dress very neatly and smartly, as usual: her quiet sleep
after Rawdon's departure have refresh she, and her pink smile cheek
be quite pleasant to look at, in a town and on a day when everybody
else's countenance wear the appearance of the deep anxiety and
gloom. she laugh at the attitude in which Jos be discover, and
the struggle and convulsion with which the stout gentleman thrust
himself into the braid coat.
"be you prepare to join the army, Mr. Joseph?" she say. "be there
to be nobody leave in Brussels to protect we poor woman?" Jos succeed
in plunge into the coat, and come forward blush and stutter out
excuse to his fair visitor. "how be she after the event of the
morning--after the fatigue of the ball the night before?" Monsieur
Isidor disappear into his master's adjacent bedroom, bear off the
flower dressing-gown.
"how good of you to ask," say she, press one of his hand in both
her own. "how cool and collect you look when everybody else be
frightened! how be our dear little Emmy? it must have be an awful,
awful parting."
"tremendous," Jos say.
"you man can bear anything," reply the lady. "parting or danger be
nothing to you. own now that you be go to join the army and leave
we to our fate. I know you be--something tell I you be. I be so
frightened, when the thought come into my head (for I do sometimes
think of you when I be alone, Mr. Joseph), that I run off immediately
to beg and entreat you not to fly from we."
this speech might be interpret, "my dear sir, should an accident
befall the army, and a retreat be necessary, you have a very
comfortable carriage, in which I propose to take a seat." I donot know
whether Jos understand the word in this sense. but he be profoundly

can think about old Joseph Sedley!" but besides these doubt he feel
flatter at the idea Rebecca express of his courage.
he blush a good deal, and put on an air of importance. "I should like
to see the action," he say. "every man of any spirit would, you know.
Ihave see a little service in India, but nothing on this grand scale."
"you man would sacrifice anything for a pleasure," rebecca answer.
"Captain Crawley leave I this morning as gay as if he be go to a

could really have be go to the troop, this great lazy gourmand?)
oh! dear Mr. Sedley, I have come to you for comfort--for consolation.

danger into which our husband, our friend, our brave troop and
ally, be rush. and I come here for shelter, and find another of
my friend--the last remain to I--bent upon plunge into the
dreadful scene!"
"my dear madam," Jos reply, now begin to be quite soothe, "donot
be alarm. I only say I should like to go--what Briton would not?
but my duty keep I here: I cannot leave that poor creature in the
next room." and he point with his finger to the door of the chamber
in which Amelia be.
"good noble brother!" rebecca say, put her handkerchief to her

have do you injustice: you have get a heart. I think you have not."
"o, upon my honour!" Jos say, make a motion as if he would lay his

do--my dear Mrs. Crawley."
"I do, now your heart be true to your sister. but I remember two year
ago--when it be false to I!" rebecca say, fix her eye upon he
for an instant, and then turn away into the window.
Jos blush violently. that organ which he be accuse by Rebecca of

and whatever it might have be once--my heart be all his. I be

all Jos's blood tingle with delight, as he survey this victim to his

anon shrink guiltily before the vision of the jealous Rawdon

pistol load and cocked.
Rebecca's appearance strike Amelia with terror, and make her shrink

war-chronicler who write brilliant story of fight and triumph

and you donot hear widow' cry or mother' sob in the midst of the

after the first movement of terror in Amelia's mind--when Rebecca's

"Dearest Amelia, you be very unwell," the visitor say, put forth

Amelia draw back her hand--never since her life begin have that gentle

"indeed, Amelia, no," the other say, still hang down her head.

"Amelia, I protest before God, I have do my husband no wrong,"

"have you do I no wrong, Rebecca? you do not succeed, but you
try. ask your heart if you do not."
she know nothing, Rebecca think.
"he come back to I. I know he would. I know that no falsehood, no

the poor girl speak these word with a spirit and volubility which

Rebecca be of a good-natured and oblige disposition; and she like

the poor soul be still at the bedside, where Rebecca have leave she, and

at half-past two, an event occur of daily importance to Mr. Joseph:

but all of a sudden, Isidor start, and the Major's wife lay down her

as if the whole population of the city rush into the street.
CHAPTER XXXII
in which Jos take Flight, and the War be bring to a close
we of peaceful London City have never behold--and please God never
shall witness--such a scene of hurry and alarm, as that which Brussels
present. crowd rush to the Namur gate, from which direction the
noise proceed, and many ride along the level chaussee, to be in
advance of any intelligence from the army. each man ask his
neighbour for news; and even great english lord and lady
condescend to speak to person whom they do not know. the friend
of the French go abroad, wild with excitement, and prophesy the
triumph of their Emperor. the merchant close their shop, and come
out to swell the general chorus of alarm and clamour. woman rush to
the church, and crowd the chapel, and knelt and pray on the
flag and step. the dull sound of the cannon go on roll,
roll. presently carriage with traveller begin to leave the town,
gallop away by the Ghent barrier. the prophecy of the french
partisan begin to pass for fact. "he have cut the army in two," it
be say. "he be march straight on Brussels. he will overpower the
English, and be here to-night." "he will overpower the English,"
shriek Isidor to his master, "and will be here to-night." the man
bound in and out from the lodging to the street, always return
with some fresh particular of disaster. Jos's face grow pale and
paler. Alarm begin to take entire possession of the stout civilian.
all the champagne he drink bring no courage to he. before sunset he
be work up to such a pitch of nervousness as gratify his friend
Isidor to behold, who now count surely upon the spoil of the owner
of the laced coat.
the woman be away all this time. after hear the firing for a
moment, the stout Major's wife bethink she of her friend in the next
chamber, and run in to watch, and if possible to console, Amelia. the
idea that she have that helpless and gentle creature to protect, give
additional strength to the natural courage of the honest Irishwoman.
she pass five hour by her friend's side, sometimes in remonstrance,
sometimes talk cheerfully, oftener in silence and terrified mental
supplication. "I never let go her hand once," say the stout lady
afterwards, "until after sunset, when the firing be over." Pauline,
the bonne, be on her knee at church hard by, pray for son homme a
elle.
when the noise of the cannonading be over, Mrs. O'Dowd issue out of
Amelia's room into the parlour adjoining, where Jos sate with two
empty flask, and courage entirely go. once or twice he have
venture into his sister's bedroom, look very much alarmed, and as
if he would say something. but the Major's wife keep her place, and he
go away without disburthene himself of his speech. he be ashamed
to tell she that he want to fly.
but when she make her appearance in the dining-room, where he sate in

"Mrs. O'Dowd," he say, "havenot you well get Amelia ready?"

"I--I've order the carriage," he say, "and--and post-horse; Isidor

"what do you want with drive to-night?" answer the lady. "benot

"get she up," say Jos; "she must get up, I say": and he stamp his

order. itbe all over, and--"
"and what?" ask Mrs. O'Dowd.
"Ibe off for Ghent," Jos answer. "everybody be go; therebe a
place for you! we shall start in half-an-hour."
the Major's wife look at he with infinite scorn. "I donot move till
O'Dowd give I the route," say she. "you may go if you like, Mr.

"she shall go," say Jos, with another stamp of his foot. Mrs. O'Dowd

"be it her mother yoube go to take she to?" she say; "or do you

journey to ye, sir. Bon voyage, as they say, and take my counsel, and
shave off they mustachio, or theywill bring you into mischief."
"D--n!" yell out Jos, wild with fear, rage, and mortification; and
Isidor come in at this juncture, swear in his turn. "Pas de
chevaux, sacre bleu!" hiss out the furious domestic. all the horse
be go. Jos be not the only man in Brussels seize with panic that
day.
but Jos's fear, great and cruel as they be already, be destine to
increase to an almost frantic pitch before the night be over. it have
be mention how Pauline, the bonne, have son homme a elle also in the
rank of the army that have go out to meet the Emperor Napoleon. this
lover be a native of Brussels, and a belgian hussar. the troop of
his nation signalise themselves in this war for anything but courage,
and young Van Cutsum, Pauline's admirer, be too good a soldier to
disobey his Colonel's order to run away. whilst in garrison at
Brussels young Regulus (he have be bear in the revolutionary time)
find his great comfort, and pass almost all his leisure moment, in
Pauline's kitchen; and it be with pocket and holster cram full of
good thing from her larder, that he have take leave of his weeping
sweetheart, to proceed upon the campaign a few day before.
as far as his regiment be concern, this campaign be over now. they
have form a part of the division under the command of his sovereign
apparent, the Prince of Orange, and as respected length of sword and
mustachio, and the richness of uniform and equipment, Regulus and his
comrade look to be as gallant a body of man as ever trumpet sound
for.
when Ney dash upon the advance of the allied troop, carry one
position after the other, until the arrival of the great body of the
british army from Brussels change the aspect of the combat of Quatre
Bras, the squadron among which regulus rode show the great
activity in retreat before the French, and be dislodge from one
post and another which they occupy with perfect alacrity on their
part. their movement be only check by the advance of the british
in their rear. thus force to halt, the enemy's cavalry (whose
bloodthirsty obstinacy cannot be too severely reprehend) have at
length an opportunity of come to close quarter with the brave
Belgians before they; who prefer to encounter the british rather
than the French, and at once turn tail ride through the English
regiment that be behind they, and scatter in all direction. the
regiment in fact do not exist any more. it be nowhere. it have no
head-quarter. regulus find himself gallop many mile from the
field of action, entirely alone; and whither should he fly for refuge
so naturally as to that kitchen and those faithful arm in which
Pauline have so often welcome he?
at some ten o'clock the clinking of a sabre might have be hear up
the stair of the house where the Osbornes occupy a story in the
continental fashion. a knock might have be hear at the kitchen
door; and poor Pauline, come back from church, faint almost with
terror as she open it and see before she her haggard hussar. he
look as pale as the midnight dragoon who come to disturb Leonora.
Pauline would have scream, but that her cry would have call she
master, and discover her friend. she stifle her scream, then, and
lead her hero into the kitchen, give he beer, and the choice bit
from the dinner, which Jos have not have the heart to taste. the hussar
show he be no ghost by the prodigious quantity of flesh and beer
which he devour--and during the mouthful he tell his tale of
disaster.
his regiment have perform prodigy of courage, and have withstand for
a while the onset of the whole french army. but they be overwhelm
at last, as be the whole british army by this time. Ney destroy each
regiment as it come up. the Belgians in vain interpose to prevent the
butchery of the English. the Brunswickers be rout and have
flee--their Duke be kill. it be a general debacle. he seek to
drown his sorrow for the defeat in flood of beer.
Isidor, who have come into the kitchen, hear the conversation and
rush out to inform his master. "it be all over," he shriek to Jos.
"Milor Duke be a prisoner; the Duke of Brunswick be kill; the british
army be in full flight; there be only one man escape, and he be in the
kitchen now--come and hear he." so Jos totter into that apartment
where Regulus still sate on the kitchen table, and clung fast to his
flagon of beer. in the good French which he could muster, and which
be in sooth of a very ungrammatical sort, Jos beseech the hussar to
tell his tale. the disaster deepen as Regulus speak. he be the
only man of his regiment not slain on the field. he have see the Duke
of Brunswick fall, the black hussar fly, the Ecossais pound down by
the cannon. "and the --th?" gasp Jos.
"cut in piece," say the hussar--upon which Pauline cry out, "o my
mistress, ma bonne petite dame," go off fairly into hysteric, and
fill the house with her scream.
Wild with terror, Mr. Sedley know not how or where to seek for safety.
he rush from the kitchen back to the sitting-room, and cast an
appealing look at Amelia's door, which Mrs. O'Dowd have close and
lock in his face; but he remember how scornfully the latter have
receive he, and after pausing and listen for a brief space at the
door, he leave it, and resolve to go into the street, for the first
time that day. so, seize a candle, he look about for his
gold-laced cap, and find it lie in its usual place, on a
console-table, in the anteroom, place before a mirror at which Jos
use to coquet, always give his side-lock a twirl, and his cap the
proper cock over his eye, before he go forth to make appearance in
public. such be the force of habit, that even in the midst of his
terror he begin mechanically to twiddle with his hair, and arrange the
cock of his hat. then he look amazed at the pale face in the glass
before he, and especially at his mustachio, which have attain a rich
growth in the course of near seven week, since they have come into the
world. they will mistake I for a military man, think he,
remember Isidor's warning as to the massacre with which all the
defeat british army be threaten; and stagger back to his
bedchamber, he begin wildly pull the bell which summon his valet.
Isidor answer that summon. Jos have sink in a chair--he have tear off
his neckcloth, and turn down his collar, and be sit with both
his hand lift to his throat.
"coupez-moi, Isidor," shout he; "vite! coupez-moi!"
Isidor think for a moment he have go mad, and that he wish his
valet to cut his throat.
"Les moustache," gasp Joe; "les moustache--coupy, rasy, vite!"--his
French be of this sort--voluble, as we have say, but not
remarkable for grammar.
Isidor sweep off the mustachio in no time with the razor, and hear
with inexpressible delight his master's order that he should fetch a
hat and a plain coat. "Ne porty ploo--habit militair--bonn--bonny a
voo, prenny dehors"--were Jos's word--the coat and cap be at last
his property.
this gift be make, Jos select a plain black coat and waistcoat
from his stock, and put on a large white neckcloth, and a plain beaver.
if he could have get a shovel hat he would have wear it. as it be, you
would have fancy he be a flourish, large parson of the Church of
England.
"Venny maintenong," he continue, "sweevy--ally--party--dong la roo."
and so have say, he plunge swiftly down the stair of the house,
and pass into the street.
although Regulus have vow that he be the only man of his regiment or
of the allied army, almost, who have escape be cut to piece by Ney,
it appear that his statement be incorrect, and that a good number
more of the suppose victim have survive the massacre. many score of
Regulus's comrade have find their way back to Brussels, and all
agree that they have run away--fill the whole town with an idea of
the defeat of the ally. the arrival of the French be expect
hourly; the panic continue, and preparation for flight go on
everywhere. no horse! think Jos, in terror. he make Isidor inquire
of score of person, whether they have any to lend or sell, and his
heart sink within he, at the negative answer return everywhere.
should he take the journey on foot? even fear could not render that
ponderous body so active.
almost all the hotel occupy by the English in Brussels face the
Parc, and Jos wander irresolutely about in this quarter, with crowd
of other people, oppress as he be by fear and curiosity. some
family he see more happy than himself, have discover a team of
horse, and rattle through the street in retreat; other again there
be whose case be like his own, and who could not for any bribe or
entreaty procure the necessary mean of flight. amongst these
would-be fugitive, Jos remark the Lady Bareacres and her daughter,
who sate in their carriage in the porte-cochere of their hotel, all
their imperial pack, and the only drawback to whose flight be the
same want of motive power which keep Jos stationary.
Rebecca Crawley occupy apartment in this hotel; and have before this
period have sundry hostile meeting with the lady of the Bareacres
family. my Lady Bareacres cut Mrs. Crawley on the stair when they meet
by chance; and in all place where the latter's name be mention,
speak perseveringly ill of her neighbour. the Countess be shock at
the familiarity of General Tufto with the aide-de-camp's wife. the
Lady Blanche avoid she as if she have be an infectious disease.
only the Earl himself keep up a sly occasional acquaintance with she,
when out of the jurisdiction of his lady.
Rebecca have her revenge now upon these insolent enemy. if became
know in the hotel that Captain Crawley's horse have be leave behind,
and when the panic begin, Lady Bareacres condescend to send she maid
to the Captain's wife with her ladyship's compliment, and a desire to
know the price of Mrs. Crawley's horse. Mrs. Crawley return a note
with her compliment, and an intimation that it be not her custom to
transact bargain with lady' maid.
this curt reply bring the Earl in person to Becky's apartment; but he
could get no more success than the first ambassador. "send a lady's
maid to I!" Mrs. Crawley cry in great anger; "why donot my Lady
Bareacres tell I to go and saddle the horse! be it her ladyship that
want to escape, or her ladyship's femme de chambre?" and this be all
the answer that the Earl bear back to his Countess.
what will not necessity do? the Countess herself actually come to wait
upon Mrs. Crawley on the failure of her second envoy. she entreat
she to name her own price; she even offer to invite Becky to
Bareacres House, if the latter would but give she the mean of
return to that residence. Mrs. Crawley sneer at she.
"I donot want to be wait on by bailiff in livery," she say; "you
will never get back though most probably--at least not you and your
diamond together. the French will have those they will be here in two
hour, and I shall be half way to Ghent by that time. I would not sell
you my horse, no, not for the two large diamond that your ladyship
wear at the ball." Lady Bareacres tremble with rage and terror. the
diamond be sew into her habit, and secrete in my Lord's padding
and boot. "Woman, the diamond be at the banker's, and I will have
the horse," she say. rebecca laugh in her face. the infuriate
Countess go below, and sate in her carriage; her maid, her courier,
and her husband be send once more through the town, each to look for
cattle; and woe betide those who come last! her ladyship be resolve
on depart the very instant the horse arrive from any quarter--with
her husband or without he.
Rebecca have the pleasure of see her ladyship in the horseless
carriage, and keep her eye fix upon she, and bewail, in the
loud tone of voice, the Countess's perplexity. "not to be able to
get horse!" she say, "and to have all those diamond sew into the
carriage cushion! what a prize it will be for the French when they
come!--the carriage and the diamond, I mean; not the lady!" she give
this information to the landlord, to the servant, to the guest, and
the innumerable straggler about the courtyard. Lady Bareacres could
have shoot she from the carriage window.
it be while enjoy the humiliation of her enemy that Rebecca catch
sight of Jos, who make towards she directly he perceive she.
that alter, frightened, fat face, tell his secret well enough. he
too want to fly, and be on the look-out for the mean of escape. "he
shall buy my horse," think rebecca, "and Iwill ride the mare."
Jos walk up to his friend, and put the question for the hundredth
time during the past hour, "do she know where horse be to be have?"
"what, you fly?" say Rebecca, with a laugh. "I think you be the
champion of all the lady, Mr. Sedley."
"I--I'm not a military man," gasp he.
"and amelia?--who be to protect that poor little sister of your?"
ask Rebecca. "you surely would not desert she?"
"what good can I do she, suppose--suppose the enemy arrive?" Jos
answer. "theywill spare the woman; but my man tell I that they have
take an oath to give no quarter to the man--the dastardly coward."
"Horrid!" cry Rebecca, enjoy his perplexity.
"besides, I donot want to desert she," cry the brother. "she SHAN'T
be desert. there be a seat for she in my carriage, and one for you,
dear Mrs. Crawley, if you will come; and if we can get horses--" sigh
he--
"I have two to sell," the lady say. Jos could have fling himself into
her arm at the news. "get the carriage, Isidor," he cry; "wehave
find they--we have find they."
"my horse never be in harness," add the lady. "Bullfinch would kick
the carriage to piece, if you put he in the trace."
"but he be quiet to ride?" ask the civilian.
"as quiet as a lamb, and as fast as a hare," answer Rebecca.
"do you think he be up to my weight?" Jos say. he be already on his
back, in imagination, without ever so much as a thought for poor
Amelia. what person who love a horse-speculation could resist such a
temptation?
in reply, Rebecca ask he to come into her room, whither he follow
her quite breathless to conclude the bargain. Jos seldom spend a
half-hour in his life which cost he so much money. rebecca, measure
the value of the good which she have for sale by Jos's eagerness to
purchase, as well as by the scarcity of the article, put upon her
horse a price so prodigious as to make even the civilian draw back.
"she would sell both or neither," she say, resolutely. Rawdon have
order she not to part with they for a price less than that which she
specify. Lord Bareacres below would give she the same money--and with
all her love and regard for the Sedley family, her dear Mr. Joseph must
conceive that poor people must live--nobody, in a word, could be more
affectionate, but more firm about the matter of business.
Jos end by agree, as might be suppose of he. the sum he have to
give she be so large that he be oblige to ask for time; so large as
to be a little fortune to Rebecca, who rapidly calculate that with
this sum, and the sale of the residue of Rawdon's effect, and her
pension as a widow should he fall, she would now be absolutely
independent of the world, and might look her weed steadily in the face.
once or twice in the day she certainly have herself think about
fly. but her reason give her well counsel. "suppose the French
do come," think Becky, "what can they do to a poor officer's widow?
Bah! the time of sack and siege be over. we shall be let to go
home quietly, or I may live pleasantly abroad with a snug little
income."
meanwhile Jos and Isidor go off to the stable to inspect the newly
purchase cattle. Jos bid his man saddle the horse at once. he would
ride away that very night, that very hour. and he leave the valet busy
in get the horse ready, and go homeward himself to prepare for
his departure. it must be secret. he would go to his chamber by the
back entrance. he do not care to face Mrs. O'Dowd and Amelia, and own
to they that he be about to run.
by the time Jos's bargain with Rebecca be complete, and his horse
have be visit and examine, it be almost morning once more. but
though midnight be long pass, there be no rest for the city; the
people be up, the light in the house flamed, crowd be still
about the door, and the street be busy. rumour of various nature
go still from mouth to mouth: one report aver that the Prussians
have be utterly defeat; another that it be the English who have be
attack and conquer: a third that the latter have hold their ground.
this last rumour gradually get strength. no Frenchmen have make their
appearance. straggler have come in from the army bring report more
and more favourable: at last an aide-de-camp actually reach Brussels
with despatch for the Commandant of the place, who placard
presently through the town an official announcement of the success of
the ally at Quatre Bras, and the entire repulse of the French under
Ney after a six hour' battle. the aide-de-camp must have arrive
sometime while Jos and Rebecca be make their bargain together, or
the latter be inspect his purchase. when he reach his own hotel,
he find a score of its numerous inhabitant on the threshold
discoursing of the news; there be no doubt as to its truth. and he
go up to communicate it to the lady under his charge. he do not
think it be necessary to tell they how he have intend to take leave
of they, how he have buy horse, and what a price he have pay for
they.
but success or defeat be a minor matter to they, who have only think
for the safety of those they love. Amelia, at the news of the victory,
become still more agitated even than before. she be for go that
moment to the army. she besought her brother with tear to conduct she
thither. her doubt and terror reach their paroxysm; and the poor
girl, who for many hour have be plunge into stupor, rave and run
hither and thither in hysteric insanity--a piteous sight. no man
writhing in pain on the hard-fought field fifteen mile off, where lay,
after their struggle, so many of the brave--no man suffer more
keenly than this poor harmless victim of the war. Jos could not bear
the sight of her pain. he leave his sister in the charge of her stouter
female companion, and descend once more to the threshold of the
hotel, where everybody still linger, and talk, and wait for more
news.
it grow to be broad daylight as they stand here, and fresh news begin
to arrive from the war, bring by man who have be actor in the
scene. wagon and long country cart laden with wounded come roll
into the town; ghastly groan come from within they, and haggard face
look up sadly from out of the straw. Jos Sedley be look at one
of these carriage with a painful curiosity--the moan of the people
within be frightful--the weary horse could hardly pull the cart.
"stop! stop!" a feeble voice cry from the straw, and the carriage
stop opposite Mr. Sedley's hotel.
"it be George, I know it be!" cry Amelia, rush in a moment to the
balcony, with a pallid face and loose flow hair. it be not George,
however, but it be the next good thing: it be news of he.
it be poor Tom Stubble, who have march out of Brussels so gallantly
twenty-four hour before, bear the colour of the regiment, which he
have defend very gallantly upon the field. a french lancer have
spear the young ensign in the leg, who fall, still bravely hold to
his flag. at the conclusion of the engagement, a place have be find
for the poor boy in a cart, and he have be bring back to Brussels.
"Mr. Sedley, Mr. Sedley!" cry the boy, faintly, and Jos come up
almost frightened at the appeal. he have not at first distinguish who
it be that call he.
little Tom Stubble hold out his hot and feeble hand. "Ibe to be take
in here," he say. "Osborne--and--and Dobbin say I be; and you be
to give the man two napoleon: my mother will pay you." this young
fellow's thought, during the long feverish hour pass in the cart,
have be wander to his father's parsonage which he have quit only
a few month before, and he have sometimes forget his pain in that
delirium.
the hotel be large, and the people kind, and all the inmate of the
cart be take in and place on various couch. the young ensign be
convey upstairs to Osborne's quarter. Amelia and the Major's wife
have rush down to he, when the latter have recognise he from the
balcony. you may fancy the feeling of these woman when they be tell
that the day be over, and both their husband be safe; in what mute
rapture Amelia fall on her good friend's neck, and embrace she; in
what a grateful passion of prayer she fall on her knee, and thank
the power which have save her husband.
our young lady, in her fevered and nervous condition, could have have no
more salutary medicine prescribe for she by any physician than that
which chance put in her way. she and Mrs. O'Dowd watch incessantly
by the wound lad, whose pain be very severe, and in the duty thus
force upon she, Amelia have not time to brood over her personal
anxiety, or to give herself up to her own fear and foreboding after
she willnot. the young patient tell in his simple fashion the event of
the day, and the action of our friend of the gallant --th. they have
suffer severely. they have lose very many officer and man. the
Major's horse have be shoot under he as the regiment charge, and they
all think that O'Dowd be go, and that Dobbin have get his majority,
until on their return from the charge to their old ground, the Major
be discover seat on Pyramus's carcase, refresh him-self from a
case-bottle. it be Captain Osborne that cut down the french lancer
who have spear the ensign. Amelia turn so pale at the notion, that
Mrs. O'Dowd stop the young ensign in this story. and it be Captain
Dobbin who at the end of the day, though wound himself, take up the
lad in his arm and carry he to the surgeon, and thence to the cart
which be to bring he back to Brussels. and it be he who promise
the driver two louis if he would make his way to Mr. Sedley's hotel in
the city; and tell Mrs. Captain Osborne that the action be over, and
that her husband be unhurt and well.
"indeed, but he have a good heart that William Dobbin," Mrs. O'Dowd
say, "though he be always laugh at I."
Young Stubble vow there be not such another officer in the army, and
never cease his praise of the senior captain, his modesty, his
kindness, and his admirable coolness in the field. to these part of
the conversation, Amelia lend a very distracted attention: it be only
when George be speak of that she listen, and when he be not
mention, she think about he.
in tend her patient, and in thinking of the wonderful escape of the
day before, her second day pass away not too slowly with Amelia.
there be only one man in the army for she: and as long as he be
well, it must be own that its movement interest her little. all
the report which Jos bring from the street fall very vaguely on her
ear; though they be sufficient to give that timorous gentleman, and
many other people then in Brussels, every disquiet. the French have
be repulse certainly, but it be after a severe and doubtful
struggle, and with only a division of the french army. the Emperor,
with the main body, be away at Ligny, where he have utterly annihilate
the Prussians, and be now free to bring his whole force to bear upon
the ally. the Duke of Wellington be retreat upon the capital, and
a great battle must be fight under its wall probably, of which the
chance be more than doubtful. the Duke of Wellington have but twenty
thousand british troop on whom he could rely, for the Germans be raw
militia, the Belgians disaffect, and with this handful his grace have
to resist a hundred and fifty thousand man that have break into Belgium
under Napoleon. under Napoleon! what warrior be there, however
famous and skilful, that could fight at odd with he?
Jos think of all these thing, and tremble. so do all the rest of
Brussels--where people feel that the fight of the day before be but
the prelude to the great combat which be imminent. one of the
army oppose to the Emperor be scatter to the wind already. the
few English that could be bring to resist he would perish at their
post, and the conqueror would pass over their body into the city.
Woe be to those whom he find there! address be prepare, public
functionary assemble and debate secretly, apartment be get
ready, and tricoloure banner and triumphal emblem manufacture, to
welcome the arrival of his Majesty the Emperor and King.
the emigration still continue, and wherever family could find mean
of departure, they flee. when Jos, on the afternoon of the 17th of

carriage have at length roll away from the porte-cochere. the Earl
have procure a pair of horse somehow, in spite of Mrs. Crawley, and
be roll on the road to Ghent. Louis the Desired be get ready
his portmanteau in that city, too. it seem as if Misfortune be
never tired of worry into motion that unwieldy exile.
Jos feel that the delay of yesterday have be only a respite, and that
his dearly buy horse must of a surety be put into requisition. his
agony be very severe all this day. as long as there be an english
army between Brussels and Napoleon, there be no need of immediate
flight; but he have his horse bring from their distant stable, to
the stable in the court-yard of the hotel where he live; so that they
might be under his own eye, and beyond the risk of violent abduction.
Isidor watch the stable-door constantly, and have the horse saddle,
to be ready for the start. he long intensely for that event.
after the reception of the previous day, Rebecca do not care to come
near her dear Amelia. she clip the bouquet which George have bring
she, and give fresh water to the flower, and read over the letter
which he have send she. "poor wretch," she say, twirl round the
little bit of paper in her finger, "how I could crush she with
this!--and it be for a thing like this that she must break her heart,
forsooth--for a man who be stupid--a coxcomb--and who do not care for
she. my poor good Rawdon be worth ten of this creature." and then she
fall to think what she should do if--if anything happen to poor
good Rawdon, and what a great piece of luck it be that he have leave his
horse behind.
in the course of this day too, Mrs. Crawley, who see not without anger
the Bareacres party drive off, bethink she of the precaution which
the Countess have take, and do a little needlework for her own
advantage; she stitch away the major part of her trinket, bill, and
bank-note about her person, and so prepared, be ready for any
event--to fly if she think fit, or to stay and welcome the conqueror,
be he Englishman or Frenchman. and I be not sure that she do not
dream that night of become a duchess and Madame la Marechale, while
Rawdon wrap in his cloak, and make his bivouac under the rain at
Mount Saint John, be think, with all the force of his heart, about
the little wife whom he have leave behind he.
the next day be a Sunday. and Mrs. Major O'Dowd have the satisfaction
of see both her patient refresh in health and spirit by some
rest which they have take during the night. she herself have sleep on a
great chair in Amelia's room, ready to wait upon her poor friend or the
ensign, should either need her nursing. when morning come, this robust
woman go back to the house where she and her Major have their billet;
and here perform an elaborate and splendid toilette, befit the
day. and it be very possible that whilst alone in that chamber, which
her husband have inhabit, and where his cap still lie on the pillow,
and his cane stand in the corner, one prayer at least be send up to
Heaven for the welfare of the brave soldier, Michael O'Dowd.
when she return she bring her prayer-book with she, and her uncle
the Dean's famous book of sermon, out of which she never fail to
read every Sabbath; not understand all, haply, not pronounce many
of the word aright, which be long and abstruse--for the Dean be a
learn man, and love long latin word--but with great gravity, vast
emphasis, and with tolerable correctness in the main. how often have my
Mick listen to these sermon, she think, and I read in the
cabin of a calm! she propose to resume this exercise on the present
day, with Amelia and the wound ensign for a congregation. the same
service be read on that day in twenty thousand church at the same
hour; and million of british man and woman, on their knee, implored
protection of the Father of all.
they do not hear the noise which disturb our little congregation at
Brussels. much louder than that which have interrupt they two day
previously, as Mrs. O'Dowd be read the service in her good voice,
the cannon of Waterloo begin to roar.
when Jos hear that dreadful sound, he make up his mind that he would
bear this perpetual recurrence of terror no long, and would fly at
once. he rush into the sick man's room, where our three friend have
pause in their prayer, and far interrupt they by a passionate
appeal to Amelia.
"I cannot stand it any more, Emmy," he say; "I willnot stand it; and you
must come with I. I have buy a horse for you--never mind at what
price--and you must dress and come with I, and ride behind Isidor."
"God forgive I, Mr. Sedley, but you be no well than a coward," Mrs.
O'Dowd say, lay down the book.
"I say come, Amelia," the civilian go on; "never mind what she say;
why be we to stop here and be butcher by the Frenchmen?"
"you forget the --th, my boy," say the little Stubble, the wound
hero, from his bed--"and and you willnot leave I, will you, Mrs. O'Dowd?"
"no, my dear fellow," say she, go up and kiss the boy. "no harm
shall come to you while I stand by. I donot budge till I get the word
from Mick. a pretty figure Iwould be, wouldnot I, stick behind that chap
on a pillion?"
this image cause the young patient to burst out laugh in his bed,
and even make Amelia smile. "I donot ask she," Jos shout out--"i
donot ask that--that Irishwoman, but you Amelia; once for all, will you
come?"
"without my husband, Joseph?" Amelia say, with a look of wonder, and
give her hand to the Major's wife. Jos's patience be exhaust.
"good-bye, then," he say, shake his fist in a rage, and slam the

march: and mount in the court-yard. Mrs. O'Dowd hear the

look on, make many scornful remark on poor Joseph as he ride down
the street with Isidor after he in the laced cap. the horse, which
have not be exercise for some day, be lively, and spring about the
