window. such a bull in a china-shop I never see." and presently the
pair of rider disappear at a canter down the street lead in the
direction of the Ghent road, Mrs. O'Dowd pursue they with a fire of
sarcasm so long as they be in sight.
all that day from morning until past sunset, the cannon never cease to
roar. it be dark when the cannonading stop all of a sudden.
all of we have read of what occur during that interval. the tale be
in every Englishman's mouth; and you and I, who be child when the
great battle be win and lose, be never tired of hear and
recount the history of that famous action. its remembrance rankle
still in the bosom of million of the countryman of those brave man
who lose the day. they pant for an opportunity of revenge that
humiliation; and if a contest, end in a victory on their part,
should ensue, elate they in their turn, and leave its cursed legacy
of hatred and rage behind to we, there be no end to the so-call glory
and shame, and to the alternation of successful and unsuccessful
murder, in which two high-spirited nation might engage. century
hence, we Frenchmen and Englishmen might be boast and kill each
other still, carry out bravely the Devil's code of honour.
all our friend take their share and fight like man in the great
field. all day long, whilst the woman be pray ten mile away, the
line of the dauntless english infantry be receive and repel
the furious charge of the french horseman. gun which be hear at
Brussels be plough up their rank, and comrade fall, and the
resolute survivor close in. towards evening, the attack of the
French, repeat and resist so bravely, slacken in its fury. they
have other foe besides the british to engage, or be prepare for a
final onset. it come at last: the column of the Imperial Guard
march up the hill of Saint Jean, at length and at once to sweep the
English from the height which they have maintain all day, and spite of
all: unscare by the thunder of the artillery, which hurl death from
the english line--the dark roll column press on and up the hill.
it seem almost to crest the eminence, when it begin to wave and
falter. then it stop, still face the shot. then at last the
english troop rush from the post from which no enemy have be able
to dislodge they, and the Guard turn and flee.
no more firing be hear at Brussels--the pursuit roll mile away.
darkness come down on the field and city: and Amelia be pray for
George, who be lie on his face, dead, with a bullet through his
heart.
CHAPTER XXXIII
in which Miss Crawley's Relations be very anxious about her
the kind reader must please to remember--while the army be march
from Flanders, and, after its heroic action there, be advance to
take the fortification on the frontier of France, previous to an
occupation of that country--that there be a number of person live
peaceably in England who have to do with the history at present in
hand, and must come in for their share of the chronicle. during the
time of these battle and danger, old Miss Crawley be live at
Brighton, very moderately move by the great event that be go on.
the great event render the newspaper rather interesting, to be
sure, and Briggs read out the Gazette, in which Rawdon Crawley's
gallantry be mention with honour, and his promotion be presently
record.
"what a pity that young man have take such an irretrievable step in the
world!" his aunt say; "with his rank and distinction he might have
marry a brewer's daughter with a quarter of a million--like Miss
Grains; or have look to ally himself with the good family in
England. he would have have my money some day or other; or his child
would--for Ibe not in a hurry to go, Miss Briggs, although you may be
in a hurry to be rid of I; and instead of that, he be a doomed pauper,
with a dancing-girl for a wife."
"will my dear Miss Crawley not cast an eye of compassion upon the
heroic soldier, whose name be inscribe in the annal of his country's
glory?" say Miss Briggs, who be greatly excited by the Waterloo
proceeding, and love speak romantically when there be an
occasion. "have not the Captain--or the Colonel as I may now style
he--do deed which make the name of Crawley illustrious?"
"Briggs, you be a fool," say Miss Crawley: "Colonel Crawley have
drag the name of Crawley through the mud, Miss Briggs. marry a
drawing-master's daughter, indeed!--marry a dame de compagnie--for she
be no well, Briggs; no, she be just what you be--only young, and
a great deal pretty and cleverer. be you an accomplice of that
abandon wretch, I wonder, of whose vile art he become a victim, and
of whom you use to be such an admirer? yes, I daresay you be an
accomplice. but you will find yourself disappointed in my will, I can
tell you: and you will have the goodness to write to Mr. Waxy, and say
that I desire to see he immediately." Miss Crawley be now in the
habit of write to Mr. Waxy her solicitor almost every day in the
week, for her arrangement respect her property be all revoke,
and her perplexity be great as to the future disposition of her money.
the spinster have, however, rally considerably; as be prove by the
increase vigour and frequency of her sarcasm upon Miss Briggs, all
which attack the poor companion bore with meekness, with cowardice,
with a resignation that be half generous and half hypocritical--with
the slavish submission, in a word, that woman of her disposition and
station be compel to show. who have not see how woman bully woman?
what torture have man to endure, comparable to those daily repeat
shaft of scorn and cruelty with which poor woman be riddle by the
tyrant of their sex? poor victim! but we be start from our
proposition, which be, that Miss Crawley be always particularly
annoying and savage when she be rally from illness--as they say
wound tingle most when they be about to heal.
while thus approach, as all hop, to convalescence, Miss Briggs be
the only victim admit into the presence of the invalid; yet Miss
Crawley's relative afar off do not forget their beloved kinswoman,
and by a number of token, present, and kind affectionate message,
strove to keep themselves alive in her recollection.
in the first place, let we mention her nephew, Rawdon Crawley. a few
week after the famous fight of Waterloo, and after the Gazette have
make known to she the promotion and gallantry of that distinguish
officer, the Dieppe packet bring over to Miss Crawley at Brighton, a
box contain present, and a dutiful letter, from the Colonel her
nephew. in the box be a pair of french epaulet, a Cross of the
Legion of Honour, and the hilt of a sword--relic from the field of
battle: and the letter describe with a good deal of humour how the
latter belong to a command officer of the Guard, who have swear
that "the Guard die, but never surrender," be take prisoner the
next minute by a private soldier, who break the Frenchman's sword with
the butt of his musket, when Rawdon make himself master of the
shattered weapon. as for the cross and epaulet, they come from a
Colonel of french cavalry, who have fall under the aide-de-camp's arm
in the battle: and Rawdon Crawley do not know what well to do with
the spoil than to send they to his kind and most affectionate old
friend. should he continue to write to she from Paris, whither the army
be march? he might be able to give her interesting news from that
capital, and of some of Miss Crawley's old friend of the emigration,
to whom she have show so much kindness during their distress.
the spinster cause Briggs to write back to the Colonel a gracious and
complimentary letter, encourage he to continue his correspondence.
his first letter be so excessively lively and amusing that she should
look with pleasure for its successors.--"Of course, I know," she
explain to Miss Briggs, "that Rawdon could not write such a good
letter any more than you could, my poor Briggs, and that it be that
clever little wretch of a Rebecca, who dictate every word to he; but
that be no reason why my nephew should not amuse I; and so I wish to
let he understand that I be in high good humour."
I wonder whether she know that it be not only Becky who write the
letter, but that Mrs. Rawdon actually take and send home the trophy
which she buy for a few franc, from one of the innumerable pedlar
who immediately begin to deal in relic of the war. the novelist, who
know everything, know this also. be this, however, as it may, Miss
Crawley's gracious reply greatly encourage our young friend, Rawdon
and his lady, who hop for the good from their aunt's evidently
pacify humour: and they take care to entertain she with many
delightful letter from Paris, whither, as Rawdon say, they have the
good luck to go in the track of the conquering army.
to the rector's lady, who go off to tend her husband's break
collar-bone at the Rectory at Queen's Crawley, the spinster's
communication be by no mean so gracious. Mrs. Bute, that brisk,
manage, lively, imperious woman, have commit the most fatal of all
error with regard to her sister-in-law. she have not merely oppress
she and her household--she have bore Miss Crawley; and if poor Miss
Briggs have be a woman of any spirit, she might have be make happy
by the commission which her principal give she to write a letter to

improve since Mrs. Bute have leave she, and beg the latter on no
account to put herself to trouble, or quit her family for Miss
Crawley's sake. this triumph over a lady who have be very haughty and
cruel in her behaviour to Miss Briggs, would have rejoice most woman;
but the truth be, Briggs be a woman of no spirit at all, and the
moment her enemy be discomfit, she begin to feel compassion in her
favour.
"how silly I be," Mrs. Bute think, and with reason, "ever to hint
that I be come, as I do, in that foolish letter when we send Miss
Crawley the guinea-fowl. I ought to have go without a word to the

ninny Briggs, and that harpy of a femme de chambre. oh! Bute, Bute,
why do you break your collar-bone?"
why, indeed? we have see how Mrs. Bute, have the game in her hand,
have really play her card too well. she have rule over Miss Crawley's

when a favourable opportunity for rebellion come. she and her
household, however, consider that she have be the victim of horrible
selfishness and treason, and that her sacrifice in Miss Crawley's
behalf have meet with the most savage ingratitude. Rawdon's promotion,
and the honourable mention make of his name in the Gazette, fill this
good christian lady also with alarm. would his aunt relent towards he
now that he be a Lieutenant-Colonel and a C.B.? and would that odious
Rebecca once more get into favour? the Rector's wife write a sermon for
her husband about the vanity of military glory and the prosperity of
the wicked, which the worthy parson read in his good voice and without
understand one syllable of it. he have Pitt Crawley for one of his
auditor--Pitt, who have come with his two half-sister to church, which
the old Baronet could now by no mean be bring to frequent.
since the departure of Becky Sharp, that old wretch have give himself

more splendid than ever. the polite family flee the hall and its
owner in terror. Sir Pitt go about tipple at his tenant' house;
and drank rum-and-water with the farmer at Mudbury and the

to Southampton with Miss Horrocks inside: and the county people
expect, every week, as his son do in speechless agony, that his

indeed a rude burthen for Mr. Crawley to bear. his eloquence be
palsy at the missionary meeting, and other religious assembly in

speak for hour; for he feel, when he rise, that the audience say,

drink at the public house at this very moment." and once when he be

from the crowd ask, "how many be there at Queen's Crawley, Young
Squaretoes?" to the surprise of the platform, and the ruin of Mr.

would have be allow to run utterly wild (for Sir Pitt swear that no
governess should ever enter into his door again), have not Mr. Crawley,
by threaten the old gentleman, force the latter to send they to
school.
meanwhile, as we have say, whatever individual difference there might

for his difference with Sir Pitt cause Mr. Crawley to absent himself

to Mr. Crawley have be formerly mention in this history. her
ladyship and her sister live at Brighton with their mamma, the
Countess Southdown, that strong-minded woman so favourably know in
the serious world.
a few word ought to be say regard her ladyship and her noble
family, who be bind by tie of present and future relationship to the

Lordship come into Parliament (as Lord Wolsey) under the auspex of

and decidedly a serious young man. but word cannot describe the
feeling of his admirable mother, when she learn, very shortly after

worldly club, have lose largely at play at Wattier's and the Cocoa
Tree; that he have raise money on post-obit, and encumber the
family estate; that he drive four-in-hand, and patronise the ring; and
that he actually have an opera-box, where he entertain the most
dangerous bachelor company. his name be only mention with groan in
the dowager's circle.
the Lady Emily be her brother's senior by many year; and take
considerable rank in the serious world as author of some of the

piece. a mature spinster, and have but faint idea of marriage, her

believe, we owe that beautiful poem.
lead we to some sunny isle,

where the sky for ever smile,

she have correspondence with clerical gentleman in most of our East and

as for the Lady Jane, on whom, as it have be say, Mr. Pitt Crawley's

when these lady first come to their house at Brighton, it be to they

the artful diplomatist and disciple of the Machiavellian Binkie! it

it be he, too, who lay before Lady Southdown the great advantage
which might occur from an intimacy between her family and Miss
Crawley--advantage both worldly and spiritual, he say: for Miss
Crawley be now quite alone; the monstrous dissipation and alliance of
his brother Rawdon have estrange her affection from that reprobate
young man; the greedy tyranny and avarice of Mrs. Bute Crawley have
cause the old lady to revolt against the exorbitant pretension of
that part of the family; and though he himself have hold off all his
life from cultivate Miss Crawley's friendship, with perhaps an
improper pride, he think now that every becoming mean should be
take, both to save her soul from perdition, and to secure her fortune
to himself as the head of the house of Crawley.
the strong-minded Lady Southdown quite agree in both proposal of her
son-in-law, and be for convert Miss Crawley off-hand. at her own
home, both at Southdown and at Trottermore Castle, this tall and awful
missionary of the truth ride about the country in her barouche with
outrider, launch packet of tract among the cottager and tenant,
and would order Gaffer Jones to be convert, as she would order Goody
Hicks to take a James's powder, without appeal, resistance, or benefit
of clergy. my Lord Southdown, her late husband, an epileptic and
simple-minded nobleman, be in the habit of approve of everything
which his Matilda do and think. so that whatever change her own
belief might undergo (and it accommodate itself to a prodigious
variety of opinion, take from all sort of doctor among the
Dissenters) she have not the least scruple in order all her tenant
and inferior to follow and believe after she. thus whether she
receive the Reverend Saunders McNitre, the Scotch divine; or the
Reverend Luke Waters, the mild Wesleyan; or the Reverend Giles Jowls,
the illuminated Cobbler, who dub himself reverend as Napoleon
crown himself Emperor--the household, child, tenantry of my Lady
Southdown be expect to go down on their knee with her ladyship,
and say amen to the prayer of either Doctor. during these exercise
old Southdown, on account of his invalid condition, be allow to sit
in his own room, and have negus and the paper read to he. Lady Jane
be the old Earl's favourite daughter, and tend he and love he
sincerely: as for Lady Emily, the authoress of the "Washerwoman of

period, for her opinion modify afterwards) be so awful that they
use to frighten the timid old gentleman her father, and the physician
declare his fit always occur after one of her ladyship's sermon.
"I will certainly call," say Lady Southdown then, in reply to the
exhortation of her daughter's pretendu, Mr. Pitt crawley--"who be Miss
Crawley's medical man?"
Mr. Crawley mention the name of Mr. Creamer.
"a most dangerous and ignorant practitioner, my dear Pitt. I have
providentially be the mean of remove he from several house:
though in one or two instance I do not arrive in time. I could not
save poor dear General Glanders, who be die under the hand of that
ignorant man--die. he rally a little under the Podgers' pill
which I administer to he; but alas! it be too late. his death be
delightful, however; and his change be only for the well; Creamer,
my dear Pitt, must leave your aunt."
Pitt express his perfect acquiescence. he, too, have be carry
along by the energy of his noble kinswoman, and future mother-in-law.
he have be make to accept Saunders McNitre, Luke water, Giles Jowls,
Podgers' Pills, Rodgers' Pills, Pokey's elixir, every one of her
ladyship's remedy spiritual or temporal. he never leave her house
without carry respectfully away with he pile of her quack theology
and medicine. o, my dear brother and fellow-sojourner in Vanity
Fair, which among you do not know and suffer under such benevolent
despot? it be in vain you say to they, "Dear Madam, I take Podgers'
specific at your order last year, and believe in it. why, why be I to
recant and accept the Rodgers' article now?" there be no help for it;
the faithful proselytizer, if she cannot convince by argument, burst
into tear, and the refusant find himself, at the end of the contest,
take down the bolus, and say, "well, well, Rodgers' be it."
"and as for her spiritual state," continue the Lady, "that of course
must be look to immediately: with Creamer about she, she may go off
any day: and in what a condition, my dear Pitt, in what a dreadful
condition! I will send the Reverend Mr. Irons to she instantly. jane,
write a line to the Reverend Bartholomew Irons, in the third person,
and say that I desire the pleasure of his company this evening at tea
at half-past six. he be an awakening man; he ought to see Miss Crawley
before she rest this night. and emily, my love, get ready a packet of
book for Miss Crawley. put up 'a Voice from the flame,' 'a
Trumpet-warning to Jericho,' and the 'Fleshpots Broken; or, the
Converted Cannibal.'"
"and the 'Washerwoman of Finchley Common,' Mamma," say Lady Emily. "it
be as well to begin soothingly at first."
"stop, my dear lady," say Pitt, the diplomatist. "with every
deference to the opinion of my beloved and respected Lady Southdown, I
think it would be quite unadvisable to commence so early upon serious
topic with Miss Crawley. remember her delicate condition, and how
little, how very little accustomed she have hitherto be to
consideration connect with her immortal welfare."
"can we then begin too early, Pitt?" say Lady Emily, rise with six
little book already in her hand.
"if you begin abruptly, you will frighten she altogether. I know my
aunt's worldly nature so well as to be sure that any abrupt attempt at
conversion will be the very bad mean that can be employ for the
welfare of that unfortunate lady. you will only frighten and annoy
she. she will very likely fle the book away, and refuse all
acquaintance with the giver."
"you be as worldly as Miss Crawley, Pitt," say Lady Emily, toss
out of the room, her book in her hand.
"and I need not tell you, my dear Lady Southdown," Pitt continue, in a
low voice, and without heed the interruption, "how fatal a little
want of gentleness and caution may be to any hope which we may
entertain with regard to the worldly possession of my aunt. remember
she have seventy thousand pound; think of her age, and her highly
nervous and delicate condition; I know that she have destroy the will
which be make in my brother's (Colonel Crawley's) favour: it be by
soothe that wound spirit that we must lead it into the right path,
and not by frighten it; and so I think you will agree with I
that--that--'
"of course, of course," Lady Southdown remark. "Jane, my love, you
need not send that note to Mr. Irons. if her health be such that
discussion fatigue she, we will wait her amendment. I will call upon
Miss Crawley tomorrow."
"and if I might suggest, my sweet lady," Pitt say in a bland tone, "it
would be as well not to take our precious Emily, who be too
enthusiastic; but rather that you should be accompany by our sweet
and dear Lady Jane."
"most certainly, Emily would ruin everything," Lady Southdown say; and
this time agree to forego her usual practice, which be, as we have
say, before she bear down personally upon any individual whom she
propose to subjugate, to fire in a quantity of tract upon the menace
party (as a charge of the French be always precede by a furious
cannonade). Lady Southdown, we say, for the sake of the invalid's
health, or for the sake of her soul's ultimate welfare, or for the sake
of her money, agree to temporise.
the next day, the great southdown female family carriage, with the

CHAPTER XXXIV
James Crawley's Pipe be Put out
the amiable behaviour of Mr. Crawley, and Lady Jane's kind reception of

Miss Crawley allow Briggs to prattle on without interrupt she too

during their interview Pitt Crawley make a great stroke, and one which
show that, have his diplomatic career not be blight by early

with every conceivable crime, a coward and a tyrant not fit to live,

the high opinion of the Emperor Napoleon. and he speak in term of
the strong indignation of the faithless conduct of the ally
towards this dethrone monarch, who, after give himself generously up
to their mercy, be consign to an ignoble and cruel banishment, while
a bigote popish rabble be tyrannise over France in his stead.
this orthodox horror of Romish superstition save Pitt Crawley in Lady

defunct british statesman be mention when we first introduce she in

shorten her life or natural rest, yet Pitt speak to her heart when he

"and what do you think, my dear?" Miss Crawley say to the young lady,
for whom she have take a liking at first sight, as she always do for

cool as rapidly as they rise.
Lady Jane blush very much, and say "that she do not understand

and so, nothing loth to comfort a sick lady, and perhaps not sorry in

and solace many of her evening. she be so naturally good and soft,
that even Firkin be not jealous of she; and the gentle Briggs think

engoument by artless sweetness and friendship.
in the autumn evening (when Rebecca be flaunt at Paris, the gay
among the gay conqueror there, and our Amelia, our dear wound
Amelia, ah! where be she?) Lady Jane would be sit in Miss

tear of happiness which she now shed as she pretend to knit, and
look out at the splendid ocean darkle before the window, and the
lamp of heaven begin more brightly to shine--who, I say can
measure the happiness and sensibility of Briggs?
Pitt meanwhile in the dining-room, with a pamphlet on the Corn Laws or
a Missionary Register by his side, take that kind of recreation which
suit romantic and unromantic man after dinner. he sip Madeira:
build castle in the air: think himself a fine fellow: feel himself
much more in love with Jane than he have be any time these seven
year, during which their liaison have last without the slight
impatience on Pitt's part--and sleep a good deal. when the time for
coffee come, Mr. Bowls use to enter in a noisy manner, and summon
Squire Pitt, who would be find in the dark very busy with his pamphlet.
"I wish, my love, I could get somebody to play piquet with I," Miss

the candle and the coffee. "Poor Briggs can no more play than an owl,
she be so stupid" (the spinster always take an opportunity of abuse
Briggs before the servant); "and I think I should sleep well if I
have my game."
at this Lady Jane blush to the tip of her little ear, and down to
the end of her pretty finger; and when Mr. Bowls have quit the
room, and the door be quite shut, she say:
"Miss Crawley, I can play a little. I use to--to play a little with
poor dear papa."
"come and kiss I. come and kiss I this instant, you dear good little

friendly occupation Mr. Pitt find the old lady and the young one, when
he come upstairs with he pamphlet in his hand. how she do blush all
the evening, that poor Lady Jane!
it must not be imagine that Mr. Pitt Crawley's artifice escape the
attention of his dear relation at the Rectory at Queen's Crawley.
Hampshire and Sussex lie very close together, and Mrs. Bute have friend
in the latter county who take care to inform she of all, and a great
deal more than all, that pass at Miss Crawley's house at Brighton.
Pitt be there more and more. he do not come for month together to

success render the Rector's family furious, and Mrs. Bute regret
more (though she confess less) than ever her monstrous fault in so

Bowls and Firkin, that she have not a single person leave in Miss
Crawley's household to give her information of what take place there.
"it be all Bute's collar-bone," she persist in say; "if that have
not break, I never would have leave she. I be a martyr to duty and to
your odious unclerical habit of hunting, Bute."
"hunting; nonsense! it be you that frighten she, Barbara," the
divine interpose. "yoube a clever woman, but youhave get a devil of a
temper; and yoube a screw with your money, Barbara."
"youwould have be screw in gaol, Bute, if I have not keep your money."
"I know I would, my dear," say the Rector, good-naturedly. "you be a
clever woman, but you manage too well, you know": and the pious man
console himself with a big glass of port.
"what the deuce can she find in that spooney of a Pitt Crawley?" he
continue. "the fellow have not pluck enough to say Bo to a goose. I
remember when Rawdon, who be a man, and be hang to he, use to flog
he round the stable as if he be a whipping-top: and Pitt would go
howl home to his ma--ha, ha! why, either of my boy would whop he
with one hand. Jim say hebe remember at Oxford as Miss Crawley
still--the spooney.
"I say, Barbara," his reverence continue, after a pause.
"what?" say Barbara, who be bite her nail, and drum the table.
"I say, why not send Jim over to Brighton to see if he can do anything
with the old lady. hebe very near get his degree, you know. hebe
only be pluck twice--so be I--but hebe have the advantage of
Oxford and a university education. he know some of the good chap
there. he pull stroke in the Boniface boat. hebe a handsome feller.
D---- it, ma'am, letus put he on the old woman, hey, and tell he to
thrash Pitt if he say anything. ha, ha, ha!
"Jim might go down and see she, certainly," the housewife say; add
with a sigh, "if we could but get one of the girl into the house; but
she could never endure they, because they be not pretty!" those
unfortunate and well-educate woman make themselves hear from the
neighbour drawing-room, where they be thrum away, with hard
finger, an elaborate music-piece on the piano-forte, as their mother
speak; and indeed, they be at music, or at backboard, or at
geography, or at history, the whole day long. but what avail all these
accomplishment, in Vanity Fair, to girl who be short, poor, plain,
and have a bad complexion? Mrs. Bute could think of nobody but the
Curate to take one of they off her hand; and Jim come in from the
stable at this minute, through the parlour window, with a short pipe
stick in his oilskin cap, he and his father fall to talk about odd
on the St. Leger, and the colloquy between the Rector and his wife
end.
Mrs. Bute do not augur much good to the cause from the sending of her
son James as an ambassador, and see he depart in rather a despairing
mood. nor do the young fellow himself, when tell what his mission be
to be, expect much pleasure or benefit from it; but he be console by
the thought that possibly the old lady would give he some handsome
remembrance of she, which would pay a few of his most pressing bill at
the commencement of the ensue Oxford term, and so take his place by
the coach from Southampton, and be safely land at Brighton on the
same evening? with his portmanteau, his favourite bull-dog Towzer, and
an immense basket of farm and garden produce, from the dear Rectory
folk to the dear Miss Crawley. consider it be too late to disturb
the invalid lady on the first night of his arrival, he put up at an
inn, and do not wait upon Miss Crawley until a late hour in the noon
of next day.
James Crawley, when his aunt have last beheld he, be a gawky lad, at
that uncomfortable age when the voice vary between an unearthly
treble and a preternatural bass; when the face not uncommonly bloom
out with appearance for which Rowland's Kalydor be say to act as a
cure; when boy be see to shave furtively with their sister's
scissor, and the sight of other young woman produce intolerable
sensation of terror in they; when the great hand and ankle protrude
a long way from garment which have grow too tight for they; when
their presence after dinner be at once frightful to the lady, who be
whisper in the twilight in the drawing-room, and inexpressibly
odious to the gentleman over the mahogany, who be restrain from
freedom of intercourse and delightful interchange of wit by the
presence of that gawky innocence; when, at the conclusion of the second
glass, papa say, "Jack, my boy, go out and see if the evening hold
up," and the youth, willing to be free, yet hurt at not be yet a
man, quit the incomplete banquet. James, then a hobbadehoy, be now
become a young man, have have the benefit of a university education,
and acquire the inestimable polish which be gain by live in a fast
set at a small college, and contracting debt, and be rusticate,
and be pluck.
he be a handsome lad, however, when he come to present himself to his
aunt at Brighton, and good look be always a title to the fickle old
lady's favour. nor do his blush and awkwardness take away from it:
she be pleased with these healthy token of the young gentleman's
ingenuousness.
he say "he have come down for a couple of day to see a man of his
college, and--and to pay my respect to you, Ma'am, and my father's and
mother's, who hope you be well."
Pitt be in the room with Miss Crawley when the lad be announce, and
look very blank when his name be mention. the old lady have plenty
of humour, and enjoy her correct nephew's perplexity. she ask
after all the people at the Rectory with great interest; and say she
be think of pay they a visit. she praise the lad to his face,
and say he be well-grow and very much improve, and that it be a
pity his sister have not some of his good look; and find, on
inquiry, that he have take up his quarter at an hotel, would not hear
of his stop there, but bade Mr. Bowls send for Mr. James Crawley's
thing instantly; "and hark ye, Bowls," she add, with great
graciousness, "you will have the goodness to pay Mr. James's bill."
she fling Pitt a look of arch triumph, which cause that diplomatist
almost to choke with envy. much as he have ingratiate himself with his
aunt, she have never yet invite he to stay under her roof, and here
be a young whipper-snapper, who at first sight be make welcome there.
"I beg your pardon, sir," say Bowls, advance with a profound bow;
"what 'otel, sir, shall Thomas fetch the luggage from?"
"o, dam," say young James, start up, as if in some alarm, "Iwill go."
"what!" say Miss Crawley.
"the Tom Cribb's arm," say James, blush deeply.
Miss Crawley burst out laugh at this title. Mr. Bowls give one
abrupt guffaw, as a confidential servant of the family, but choke the
rest of the volley; the diplomatist only smile.
"I--I donot know any well," say James, look down. "Ihave never
be here before; it be the coachman tell I." the young story-teller!
the fact be, that on the Southampton coach, the day previous,
James Crawley have meet the Tutbury Pet, who be come to Brighton to
make a match with the Rottingdean Fibber; and enchant by the Pet's
conversation, have pass the evening in company with that scientific
man and his friend, at the inn in question.
"I--I'd well go and settle the score," James continue. "couldnot think
of ask you, Ma'am," he add, generously.
this delicacy make his aunt laugh the more.
"go and settle the bill, Bowls," she say, with a wave of her hand,
"and bring it to I."
poor lady, she do not know what she have do! "there--therebe a
little dawg," say James, look frightfully guilty. "Iwould well go for
he. he bite footmen's calf."
all the party cry out with laugh at this description; even Briggs
and Lady Jane, who be sit mute during the interview between Miss
Crawley and her nephew: and bowl, without a word, quit the room.
still, by way of punish her eld nephew, Miss Crawley persist in
be gracious to the young Oxonian. there be no limit to her
kindness or her compliment when they once begin. she tell Pitt he
might come to dinner, and insist that James should accompany she in
her drive, and parade he solemnly up and down the cliff, on the back
seat of the barouche. during all this excursion, she condescend to
say civil thing to he: she quote italian and french poetry to the
poor bewildered lad, and persist that he be a fine scholar, and be
perfectly sure he would gain a gold medal, and be a Senior Wrangler.
"haw, haw," laugh James, encourage by these compliment; "Senior
Wrangler, indeed; thatbe at the other shop."
"what be the other shop, my dear child?" say the lady.
"Senior Wranglers at Cambridge, not Oxford," say the scholar, with a
know air; and would probably have be more confidential, but that
suddenly there appear on the cliff in a tax-cart, draw by a bang-up
pony, dress in white flannel coat, with mother-of-pearl button, his
friend the Tutbury Pet and the Rottingdean Fibber, with three other
gentleman of their acquaintance, who all salute poor James there in
the carriage as he sate. this incident damp the ingenuous youth's
spirit, and no word of yea or nay could he be induce to utter during
the rest of the drive.
on his return he find his room prepare, and his portmanteau ready,
and might have remark that Mr. Bowls's countenance, when the latter
conduct he to his apartment, wear a look of gravity, wonder, and
compassion. but the thought of Mr. Bowls do not enter his head. he
be deplore the dreadful predicament in which he find himself, in a
house full of old woman, jabber french and italian, and talk
poetry to he. "reglarly up a tree, by jingo!" exclaim the modest
boy, who could not face the gentle of her sex--not even Briggs--when
she begin to talk to he; whereas, put he at Iffley Lock, and he could
out-slang the bold bargeman.
at dinner, James appear choke in a white neckcloth, and have the
honour of hand my Lady Jane downstairs, while Briggs and Mr. Crawley
follow afterwards, conduct the old lady, with her apparatus of
bundle, and shawl, and cushion. half of Briggs's time at dinner be
spend in superintend the invalid's comfort, and in cut up
chicken for her fat spaniel. James do not talk much, but he make a
point of ask all the lady to drink wine, and accept Mr.
Crawley's challenge, and consume the great part of a bottle of
champagne which Mr. Bowls be order to produce in his honour. the
lady have withdraw, and the two cousin be leave together, Pitt,
the ex-diplomatist, he come very communicative and friendly. he ask
after James's career at college--what his prospect in life be--hop
heartily he would get on; and, in a word, be frank and amiable.
James's tongue unloose with the port, and he tell his cousin his life,
his prospect, his debt, his trouble at the little-go, and his row
with the proctor, fill rapidly from the bottle before he, and
fly from Port to Madeira with joyous activity.
"the chief pleasure which my aunt have," say Mr. Crawley, fill his
glass, "be that people should do as they like in her house. this be
Liberty Hall, James, and you cannot do Miss Crawley a great kindness
than to do as you please, and ask for what you will. I know you have
all sneered at I in the country for be a tory. Miss Crawley be
liberal enough to suit any fancy. she be a Republican in principle,
and despise everything like rank or title."
"why be you go to marry an Earl's daughter?" say James.
"my dear friend, remember it be not poor Lady Jane's fault that she be
well bear," Pitt reply, with a courtly air. "she cannot help be a
lady. besides, I be a Tory, you know."
"oh, as for that," say Jim, "therebe nothing like old blood; no,
dammy, nothing like it. Ibe none of your radical. I know what it be
to be a gentleman, dammy. see the chap in a boat-race; look at the
feller in a fight; aye, look at a dawg kill rat--which be it win?
the good-blooded one. get some more port, Bowls, old boy, whilst I
buzz this bottle-here. what be I asaye?"
"I think you be speak of dog kill rat," Pitt remark mildly,
hand his cousin the decant to "buzz."
"kill rat be I? well, Pitt, be you a sporting man? do you want to
see a dawg as can kill a rat? if you do, come down with I to Tom
Corduroy's, in Castle Street Mews, and Iwill show you such a bull-terrier
as--Pooh! gammon," cry James, burst out laugh at his
own absurdity--"YOU donot care about a dawg or rat; itbe all nonsense.
Ibe blest if I think you know the difference between a dog and a duck."
"no; by the way," Pitt continue with increase blandness, "it be
about blood you be talk, and the personal advantage which people
derive from patrician birth. herebe the fresh bottle."
"blood's the word," say James, gulp the ruby fluid down. "nothing
like blood, sir, in hosse, dawgs, and man. why, only last term, just
before I be rusticate, that is, I mean just before I have the measle,
ha, ha--there be I and Ringwood of Christchurch, Bob Ringwood, Lord
Cinqbars' son, have our beer at the Bell at Blenheim, when the
Banbury bargeman offer to fight either of we for a bowl of punch. I
couldnot. my arm be in a sling; couldnot even take the drag down--a
brute of a mare of mine have fall with I only two day before, out with
the Abingdon, and I think my arm be break. well, sir, I couldnot
finish he, but Bob have his coat off at once--he stand up to the
Banbury man for three minute, and polish he off in four round
easy. Gad, how he do drop, sir, and what be it? blood, sir, all
blood."
"you donot drink, James," the ex-attache continue. "in my time at
Oxford, the man pass round the bottle a little quick than you young
fellow seem to do."
"come, come," say James, put his hand to his nose and wink at
his cousin with a pair of vinous eye, "no joke, old boy; no try it
on on I. you want to trot I out, but itbe no go. in vino verita,
old boy. Mars, Bacchus, Apollo virorum, hey? I wish my aunt would send
down some of this to the governor; itbe a precious good tap."
"you have well ask she," Machiavel continue, "or make the good of
your time now. what say the bard? 'nunc vino pellite curas, Cras
ingen iterabimus aequor,'" and the Bacchanalian, quote the above
with a House of Commons air, toss off nearly a thimbleful of wine
with an immense flourish of his glass.
at the Rectory, when the bottle of port wine be open after dinner,
the young lady have each a glass from a bottle of currant wine. Mrs.
Bute take one glass of port, honest James have a couple commonly, but as
his father grow very sulky if he make further inroad on the bottle,
the good lad generally refrain from try for more, and subside
either into the currant wine, or to some private gin-and-water in the
stable, which he enjoy in the company of the coachman and his pipe.
at Oxford, the quantity of wine be unlimited, but the quality be
inferior: but when quantity and quality unite as at his aunt's house,
James show that he could appreciate they indeed; and hardly need
any of his cousin's encouragement in drain off the second bottle
supply by Mr. Bowls.
when the time for coffee come, however, and for a return to the lady,
of whom he stand in awe, the young gentleman's agreeable frankness leave
he, and he relapse into his usual surly timidity; content himself
by say yes and no, by scowl at Lady Jane, and by upset one
cup of coffee during the evening.
if he do not speak he yawn in a pitiable manner, and his presence
throw a damp upon the modest proceeding of the evening, for Miss
Crawley and Lady Jane at their piquet, and Miss Briggs at her work,
feel that his eye be wildly fix on they, and be uneasy under
that maudlin look.
"he seem a very silent, awkward, bashful lad," say Miss Crawley to
Mr. Pitt.
"he be more communicative in man's society than with lady," Machiavel
dryly reply: perhaps rather disappointed that the port wine have not
make Jim speak more.
he have spend the early part of the next morning in write home to his
mother a most flourishing account of his reception by Miss Crawley.
but ah! he little know what evil the day be bring for he, and how
short his reign of favour be destine to be. a circumstance which Jim
have forget--a trivial but fatal circumstance--have take place at the
Cribb's arm on the night before he have come to his aunt's house. it
be no other than this--Jim, who be always of a generous disposition,
and when in his cup especially hospitable, have in the course of the
night treat the Tutbury champion and the rottingdean man, and their
friend, twice or thrice to the refreshment of gin-and-water--so that
no less than eighteen glass of that fluid at eightpence per glass
be charge in Mr. James Crawley's bill. it be not the amount of
eightpence, but the quantity of gin which tell fatally against poor
James's character, when his aunt's butler, Mr. Bowls, go down at his
mistress's request to pay the young gentleman's bill. the landlord,
fear lest the account should be refuse altogether, swear solemnly
that the young gent have consume personally every farthing's worth of
the liquor: and bowl pay the bill finally, and show it on his
return home to Mrs. Firkin, who be shock at the frightful
prodigality of gin; and take the bill to Miss Briggs as
accountant-general; who think it her duty to mention the circumstance
to her principal, Miss Crawley.
have he drunk a dozen bottle of claret, the old spinster could have
pardon he. Mr. Fox and Mr. Sheridan drink claret. gentleman drink
claret. but eighteen glass of gin consume among boxer in an
ignoble pot-house--it be an odious crime and not to be pardon
readily. everything go against the lad: he come home perfume from
the stable, whither he have be to pay his dog Towzer a visit--and
whence he be go to take his friend out for an airing, when he meet
Miss Crawley and her wheezy Blenheim spaniel, which Towzer would have
eat up have not the Blenheim flee squealing to the protection of Miss
Briggs, while the atrocious master of the bull-dog stand laugh at
the horrible persecution.
this day too the unlucky boy's modesty have likewise forsake he. he
be lively and facetious at dinner. during the repast he level one
or two joke against Pitt Crawley: he drink as much wine as upon the
previous day; and go quite unsuspiciously to the drawing-room, begin
to entertain the lady there with some choice Oxford story. he
describe the different pugilistic quality of Molyneux and Dutch Sam,
offer playfully to give Lady Jane the odd upon the Tutbury Pet
against the rottingdean man, or take they, as her ladyship choose: and
crown the pleasantry by propose to back himself against his cousin
Pitt Crawley, either with or without the glove. "and thatbe a fair
offer, my buck," he say, with a loud laugh, slap Pitt on the
shoulder, "and my father tell I to make it too, and hewill go half in
the bet, ha, ha!" so say, the engage youth nod knowingly at
poor Miss Briggs, and point his thumb over his shoulder at Pitt
Crawley in a jocular and exult manner.
Pitt be not pleased altogether perhaps, but still not unhappy in the
main. poor Jim have his laugh out: and stagger across the room with
his aunt's candle, when the old lady move to retire, and offer to
salute she with the blandest tipsy smile: and he take his own leave
and go upstairs to his bedroom perfectly satisfied with himself, and
with a pleased notion that his aunt's money would be leave to he in
preference to his father and all the rest of the family.
once up in the bedroom, one would have think he could not make
matter bad; and yet this unlucky boy do. the moon be shin very
pleasantly out on the sea, and Jim, attract to the window by the
romantic appearance of the ocean and the heaven, think he would
far enjoy they while smoke. nobody would smell the tobacco, he
think, if he cunningly open the window and keep his head and pipe
in the fresh air. this he do: but be in an excited state, poor Jim
have forget that his door be open all this time, so that the breeze
blow inward and a fine thorough draught be establish, the
cloud of tobacco be carry downstairs, and arrive with quite
undiminishe fragrance to Miss Crawley and Miss Briggs.
the pipe of tobacco finish the business: and the Bute-Crawleys never
know how many thousand pound it cost they. Firkin rush downstair
to bowl who be read out the "Fire and the fry pan" to his
aide-de-camp in a loud and ghostly voice. the dreadful secret be tell
to he by Firkin with so frighten a look, that for the first moment
Mr. Bowls and his young man think that robber be in the house, the
leg of whom have probably be discover by the woman under Miss
Crawley's bed. when make aware of the fact, however--to rush upstairs
at three step at a time to enter the unconscious James's apartment,
call out, "Mr. James," in a voice stifle with alarm, and to cry,
"for Gawd's sake, sir, stop that 'ere pipe," be the work of a minute
with Mr. Bowls. "o, Mr. James, what 'AVE you do!" he say in a voice
of the deep pathos, as he throw the implement out of the window.
"what 'ave you do, sir! missis cannot abide them."
"missis neednot smoke," say James with a frantic misplace laugh, and
think the whole matter an excellent joke. but his feeling be very
different in the morning, when Mr. Bowls's young man, who operate upon
Mr. James's boot, and bring he his hot water to shave that beard
which he be so anxiously expect, hand a note in to Mr. James in
bed, in the handwriting of Miss Briggs.
"dear sir," it say, "Miss Crawley have pass an exceedingly disturbed
night, owe to the shocking manner in which the house have be
pollute by tobacco; Miss Crawley bid I say she regret that she be
too unwell to see you before you go--and above all that she ever
induce you to remove from the ale-house, where she be sure you will be
much more comfortable during the rest of your stay at Brighton."
and herewith honest James's career as a candidate for his aunt's favour
end. he have in fact, and without know it, do what he menace to
do. he have fight his cousin Pitt with the glove.
where meanwhile be he who have be once first favourite for this race
for money? Becky and Rawdon, as we have see, be come together after
Waterloo, and be pass the winter of 1815 at Paris in great
splendour and gaiety. rebecca be a good economist, and the price poor
Jos Sedley have pay for her two horse be in itself sufficient to keep
their little establishment afloat for a year, at the least; there be
no occasion to turn into money "my pistol, the same which I shoot
Captain Marker," or the gold dressing-case, or the cloak line with
sable. Becky have it make into a pelisse for herself, in which she ride
in the Bois de Boulogne to the admiration of all: and you should have
see the scene between she and her delighted husband, whom she rejoin
after the army have enter Cambray, and when she unsewe herself, and
let out of her dress all those watch, knick-knack, bank-note,
cheque, and valuable, which she have secrete in the wadding, previous
to her meditate flight from Brussels! Tufto be charm, and Rawdon
roar with delighted laughter, and swear that she be well than any
play he ever see, by Jove. and the way in which she jockey Jos, and
which she describe with infinite fun, carry up his delight to a
pitch of quite insane enthusiasm. he believe in his wife as much as
the french soldier in Napoleon.
her success in Paris be remarkable. all the french lady vote she
charming. she speak their language admirably. she adopt at once
their grace, their liveliness, their manner. her husband be stupid
certainly--all English be stupid--and, besides, a dull husband at
Paris be always a point in a lady's favour. he be the heir of the
rich and spirituelle Miss Crawley, whose house have be open to so many
of the french noblesse during the emigration. they receive the
colonel's wife in their own hotels--"why," write a great lady to Miss
Crawley, who have buy her lace and trinket at the Duchess's own
price, and give she many a dinner during the pinch time after the
revolution--"why do not our dear Miss come to her nephew and niece,
and her attach friend in Paris? all the world raffole of the
charming Mistress and her espiegle beauty. yes, we see in her the
grace, the charm, the wit of our dear friend Miss Crawley! the king
take notice of she yesterday at the Tuileries, and we be all jealous
of the attention which Monsieur pay she. if you could have see the
spite of a certain stupid Miladi Bareacres (whose eagle-beak and toque
and feather may be see peer over the head of all assembly) when
Madame, the Duchess of Angouleme, the august daughter and companion of
king, desire especially to be present to Mrs. Crawley, as your dear
daughter and protegee, and thank she in the name of France, for all
your benevolence towards our unfortunate during their exile! she be of
all the society, of all the ball--of the ball--yes--of the dance,
no; and yet how interesting and pretty this fair creature look
surround by the homage of the man, and so soon to be a mother! to
hear she speak of you, her protectress, her mother, would bring tear
to the eye of ogre. how she love you! how we all love our
admirable, our respectable Miss Crawley!"
it be to be fear that this letter of the parisian great lady do not
by any mean advance Mrs. Becky's interest with her admirable, her
respectable, relative. on the contrary, the fury of the old spinster
be beyond bound, when she find what be Rebecca's situation, and how
audaciously she have make use of Miss Crawley's name, to get an entree
into parisian society. too much shake in mind and body to compose a
letter in the french language in reply to that of her correspondent,
she dictate to Briggs a furious answer in her own native tongue,
repudiate Mrs. Rawdon Crawley altogether, and warn the public to
beware of she as a most artful and dangerous person. but as Madame the
Duchess of X--have only be twenty year in England, she do not
understand a single word of the language, and content herself by
inform Mrs. Rawdon Crawley at their next meeting, that she have
receive a charming letter from that chere Mees, and that it be full
of benevolent thing for Mrs. Crawley, who begin seriously to have
hope that the spinster would relent.
meanwhile, she be the gay and most admire of Englishwomen: and
have a little european congress on her reception-night. Prussians and
cossack, Spanish and English--all the world be at Paris during this
famous winter: to have see the star and cordon in Rebecca's humble
saloon would have make all Baker Street pale with envy. famous warrior
ride by her carriage in the Bois, or crowd her modest little box at
the Opera. Rawdon be in the high spirit. there be no dun in
Paris as yet: there be party every day at Very's or beauvillier';
play be plentiful and his luck good. Tufto perhaps be sulky. Mrs.
Tufto have come over to Paris at her own invitation, and besides this
contretemp, there be a score of general now round Becky's chair,
and she might take her choice of a dozen bouquet when she go to the
play. Lady Bareacres and the chief of the english society, stupid and
irreproachable female, writhe with anguish at the success of the
little upstart Becky, whose poison joke quiver and rankle in
their chaste breast. but she have all the man on her side. she fight
the woman with indomitable courage, and they could not talk scandal in
any tongue but their own.
so in fete, pleasure, and prosperity, the winter of 1815-16 pass
away with Mrs. Rawdon Crawley, who accommodate herself to polite life
as if her ancestor have be people of fashion for century past--and
who from her wit, talent, and energy, indeed merit a place of honour
in Vanity Fair. in the early spring of 1816, Galignani's Journal
contain the follow announcement in an interesting corner of the
paper: "on the 26th of March--the Lady of Lieutenant-Colonel Crawley,
of the Life Guards Green--of a son and heir."
this event be copy into the London paper, out of which Miss Briggs
read the statement to Miss Crawley, at breakfast, at Brighton. the
intelligence, expect as it might have be, cause a crisis in the
affair of the Crawley family. the spinster's rage rise to its height,
and send instantly for Pitt, her nephew, and for the Lady Southdown,
from Brunswick Square, she request an immediate celebration of the
marriage which have be so long pende between the two family. and
she announce that it be her intention to allow the young couple a
thousand a year during her lifetime, at the expiration of which the
bulk of her property would be settle upon her nephew and her dear
niece, Lady Jane Crawley. Waxy come down to ratify the deed--Lord
Southdown give away his sister--she be marry by a Bishop, and not by
the Rev. Bartholomew Irons--to the disappointment of the irregular
prelate.
when they be marry, Pitt would have like to take a hymeneal tour
with his bride, as become people of their condition. but the affection
of the old lady towards Lady Jane have grow so strong, that she fairly
own she could not part with her favourite. Pitt and his wife come
therefore and live with Miss Crawley: and (greatly to the annoyance of
poor Pitt, who conceive himself a most injure character--be
subject to the humour of his aunt on one side, and of his
mother-in-law on the other) Lady Southdown, from her neighbouring
house, reign over the whole family--Pitt, Lady Jane, Miss Crawley,
Briggs, Bowls, Firkin, and all. she pitilessly dose they with her
tract and her medicine, she dismiss Creamer, she instal Rodgers,
and soon strip Miss Crawley of even the semblance of authority. the
poor soul grow so timid that she actually leave off bully Briggs any
more, and clung to her niece, more fond and terrify every day. peace
to thee, kind and selfish, vain and generous old heathen!--We shall see
thee no more. let we hope that Lady Jane support she kindly, and lead
she with gentle hand out of the busy struggle of Vanity Fair.
CHAPTER XXXV
Widow and Mother
the news of the great fight of Quatre Bras and Waterloo reach
England at the same time. the Gazette first publish the result of
the two battle; at which glorious intelligence all England thrill
with triumph and fear. particular then follow; and after the
announcement of the victory come the list of the wounded and the
slain. who can tell the dread with which that catalogue be open and
read! Fancy, at every village and homestead almost through the three
kingdom, the great news come of the battle in Flanders, and the
feeling of exultation and gratitude, bereavement and sicken dismay,
when the list of the regimental loss be go through, and it
become know whether the dear friend and relative have escape or
fall. anybody who will take the trouble of look back to a file of
the newspaper of the time, must, even now, feel at second-hand this
breathless pause of expectation. the list of casualty be carry
on from day to day: you stop in the midst as in a story which be to be
continue in our next. think what the feeling must have be as those
paper follow each other fresh from the press; and if such an
interest could be feel in our country, and about a battle where but
twenty thousand of our people be engage, think of the condition of
Europe for twenty year before, where people be fight, not by
thousand, but by million; each one of whom as he strike his enemy
wound horribly some other innocent heart far away.
the news which that famous Gazette bring to the Osbornes give a
dreadful shock to the family and its chief. the girl indulge
unrestrained in their grief. the gloom-stricken old father be still
more bear down by his fate and sorrow. he strove to think that a
judgment be on the boy for his disobedience. he dare not own that
the severity of the sentence frighten he, and that its fulfilment
have come too soon upon his curse. sometimes a shudder terror
strike he, as if he have be the author of the doom which he have
call down on his son. there be a chance before of reconciliation.
the boy's wife might have die; or he might have come back and say,
Father I have sin. but there be no hope now. he stand on the
other side of the gulf impassable, haunt his parent with sad eye.
he remember they once before so in a fever, when every one think
the lad be die, and he lie on his bed speechless, and gaze with a
dreadful gloom. good God! how the father clung to the doctor then, and
with what a sicken anxiety he follow he: what a weight of grief
be off his mind when, after the crisis of the fever, the lad
recover, and look at his father once more with eye that recognise
he. but now there be no help or cure, or chance of reconcilement:
above all, there be no humble word to soothe vanity outrage and
furious, or bring to its natural flow the poison, angry blood. and
it be hard to say which pang it be that tear the proud father's heart
most keenly--that his son should have go out of the reach of his
forgiveness, or that the apology which his own pride expect should
have escape he.
whatever his sensation might have be, however, the stem old man
would have no confidant. he never mention his son's name to his
daughter; but order the elder to place all the female of the
establishment in mourning; and desire that the male servant should be
similarly attire in deep black. all party and entertainment, of
course, be to be put off. no communication be make to his future
son-in-law, whose marriage-day have be fix: but there be enough in
Mr. Osborne's appearance to prevent Mr. Bullock from make any
inquiry, or in any way press forward that ceremony. he and the
lady whisper about it under their voice in the drawing-room
sometimes, whither the father never come. he remain constantly in
his own study; the whole front part of the house be close until
some time after the completion of the general mourning.
about three week after the 18th of June, Mr. Osborne's acquaintance,
Sir William Dobbin, call at Mr. Osborne's house in Russell Square,
with a very pale and agitated face, and insist upon see that
gentleman. usher into his room, and after a few word, which neither
the speaker nor the host understand, the former produce from an
inclosure a letter seal with a large red seal. "my son, Major
Dobbin," the Alderman say, with some hesitation, "despatch I a
letter by an officer of the --th, who arrive in town to-day. my son's
letter contain one for you, Osborne." the Alderman place the letter
on the table, and Osborne star at he for a moment or two in silence.
his look frighten the ambassador, who after look guiltily for a
little time at the grief-stricken man, hurry away without another
word.
the letter be in George's well-know bold handwriting. it be that one
which he have write before daybreak on the 16th of June, and just
before he take leave of Amelia. the great red seal be emblazon with
the sham coat of arm which Osborne have assume from the Peerage, with
"Pax in bello" for a motto; that of the ducal house with which the vain
old man try to fancy himself connect. the hand that sign it would
never hold pen or sword more. the very seal that seal it have be
rob from George's dead body as it lie on the field of battle. the
father know nothing of this, but sit and look at the letter in
terrified vacancy. he almost fall when he go to open it.
have you ever have a difference with a dear friend? how his letter,
write in the period of love and confidence, sicken and rebuke you!
what a dreary mourning it be to dwell upon those vehement protest of
dead affection! what lie epitaph they make over the corpse of love!
what dark, cruel comment upon life and vanity! Most of we have get
or write drawer full of they. they be closet-skeleton which we
keep and shun. Osborne tremble long before the letter from his dead
son.
the poor boy's letter do not say much. he have be too proud to
acknowledge the tenderness which his heart feel. he only say, that on
the eve of a great battle, he wish to bid his father farewell, and
solemnly to implore his good office for the wife--it might be for the
child--whom he leave behind he. he own with contrition that his
irregularity and his extravagance have already waste a large part of
his mother's little fortune. he thank his father for his former
generous conduct; and he promise he that if he fall on the field or
survive it, he would act in a manner worthy of the name of George
Osborne.
his english habit, pride, awkwardness perhaps, have prevent he from
say more. his father could not see the kiss George have place on
the superscription of his letter. Mr. Osborne drop it with the
bitter, deadly pang of balked affection and revenge. his son be
still beloved and unforgiven.
about two month afterwards, however, as the young lady of the family

see an elaborate monument upon the wall, where Britannia be

funereal emblem in hand; as you may see still on the wall of St.

allegory. there be a constant demand for they during the first
fifteen year of the present century.
under the memorial in question be emblazon the well-know and

if the sister have any anxiety regard the possible recognition of

very many of the brave --th, who have suffer severely upon both day

find out some of the --th easily. he know their uniform quite well,

and sate down tremble by the wound convalescent man.
"be you in Captain Osborne's company?" he say, and add, after a

the man be not of the Captain's company, but he lift up his

Osborne put a guinea into the soldier's hand, and tell he he should

in the Sergeant's company, who be also just convalescent, Osborne make

tell how much vanity lurk in our warm regard for other, and how
selfish our love be? Old Osborne do not speculate much upon the
mingle nature of his feeling, and how his instinct and selfishness
be combat together. he firmly believe that everything he do be
right, that he ought on all occasion to have his own way--and like the
sting of a wasp or serpent his hatred rush out armed and poisonous
against anything like opposition. he be proud of his hatred as of
everything else. always to be right, always to trample forward, and
never to doubt, be not these the great quality with which dullness
take the lead in the world?
as after the drive to Waterloo, Mr. Osborne's carriage be near the

it be Amelia, with the lame young Ensign by her side, and opposite to

"Mr. Osborne, Mr. Osborne!" cry Dobbin, as he ride up and hold out

and with another curse to his servant to drive on.
Dobbin lay his hand on the carriage side. "I will see you, sir," he

"from that woman?" say Osborne, fiercely.
"no," reply the other, "from your son"; at which Osborne fall back

"pray, have you any command for I, Captain Dobbin, or, I beg your

"well man be dead," Dobbin reply. "I want to speak to you about
one."
"make it short, sir," say the other with an oath, scowl at his

"I be here as his close friend," the Major resume, "and the executor

"I donot know his widow, sir," Osborne say. "let she go back to her

however, and it be about this I come to speak to you. she will be a
mother soon. will you visit the parent's offence upon the child's
head? or will you forgive the child for poor George's sake?"
Osborne break out into a rhapsody of self-praise and imprecations;--by
the first, excuse himself to his own conscience for his conduct; by
the second, exaggerate the undutifulness of George. no father in all
England could have behave more generously to a son, who have rebel
against he wickedly. he have die without even so much as confess
he be wrong. let he take the consequence of his undutifulness and
folly. as for himself, Mr. Osborne, he be a man of his word. he have
swear never to speak to that woman, or to recognize she as his son's
wife. "and thatbe what you may tell she," he conclude with an oath;
"and thatbe what I will stick to to the last day of my life."
there be no hope from that quarter then. the widow must live on her
slender pittance, or on such aid as Jos could give she. "I might tell
she, and she would not heed it," think Dobbin, sadly: for the poor
girl's thought be not here at all since her catastrophe, and,
stupefy under the pressure of her sorrow, good and evil be alike
indifferent to she.
so, indeed, be even friendship and kindness. she receive they both
uncomplainingly, and have accept they, relapse into her grief.
suppose some twelve month after the above conversation take place to
have pass in the life of our poor Amelia. she have spend the first
portion of that time in a sorrow so profound and pitiable, that we who
have be watch and describe some of the emotion of that weak and
tender heart, must draw back in the presence of the cruel grief under
which it be bleed. Tread silently round the hapless couch of the
poor prostrate soul. shut gently the door of the dark chamber wherein
she suffer, as those kind people do who nurse she through the first
month of her pain, and never leave she until heaven have send she
consolation. a day come--of almost terrified delight and wonder--when
the poor widow girl press a child upon her breast--a child, with
the eye of George who be go--a little boy, as beautiful as a
cherub. what a miracle it be to hear its first cry! how she laugh
and weep over it--how love, and hope, and prayer wake again in her
bosom as the baby nestle there. she be safe. the doctor who
attend she, and have fear for her life or for her brain, have wait
anxiously for this crisis before they could pronounce that either be
secure. it be worth the long month of doubt and dread which the
person who have constantly be with she have pass, to see her eye
once more beam tenderly upon they.
our friend Dobbin be one of they. it be he who bring she back to
England and to her mother's house; when Mrs. O'Dowd, receive a
peremptory summon from her Colonel, have be force to quit she
patient. to see Dobbin hold the infant, and to hear Amelia's laugh
of triumph as she watch he, would have do any man good who have a
sense of humour. William be the godfather of the child, and exert
his ingenuity in the purchase of cup, spoon, pap-boat, and coral
for this little Christian.
how his mother nurse he, and dress he, and live upon he; how she
drive away all nurse, and would scarce allow any hand but her own to
touch he; how she consider that the great favour she could confer
upon his godfather, Major Dobbin, be to allow the Major occasionally
to dandle he, need not be tell here. this child be her be. she
existence be a maternal caress. she envelop the feeble and

baby drink in from her bosom. of night, and when alone, she have
stealthy and intense rapture of motherly love, such as God's

high and low than reason--blind beautiful devotion which only
woman's heart know. it be William Dobbin's task to muse upon these
movement of Amelia's, and to watch her heart; and if his love make he
divine almost all the feeling which agitate it, alas! he could see
with a fatal perspicuity that there be no place there for he. and
so, gently, he bear his fate, know it, and content to bear it.
I suppose Amelia's father and mother see through the intention of the
Major, and be not ill-disposed to encourage he; for Dobbin visit
their house daily, and stay for hour with they, or with Amelia, or

and go, with the landlord's little girl, who be rather a favourite

who commonly act as mistress of the ceremony to introduce he to

to Fulham, and he descend from it, bring out a wooden horse, a
drum, a trumpet, and other warlike toy, for little Georgy, who be
scarcely six month old, and for whom the article in question be
entirely premature.
the child be asleep. "hush," say Amelia, annoyed, perhaps, at the

of toy. "go downstairs, little Mary," say he presently to the child,

CHAPTER xxxvi
how to live well on nothing a year
I suppose there be no man in this Vanity Fair of ours so little
observant as not to think sometimes about the worldly affair of his
acquaintance, or so extremely charitable as not to wonder how his
neighbour Jones, or his neighbour Smith, can make both end meet at the
end of the year. with the utmost regard for the family, for instance
(for I dine with they twice or thrice in the season), I cannot but own
that the appearance of the Jenkinses in the park, in the large barouche
with the grenadi-footman, will surprise and mystify I to my dying
day: for though I know the equipage be only job, and all the
Jenkins people be on board wage, yet those three man and the carriage
must represent an expense of six hundred a year at the very least--and
then there be the splendid dinner, the two boy at Eton, the prize
governess and master for the girl, the trip abroad, or to Eastbourne
or Worthing, in the autumn, the annual ball with a supper from Gunter's
(who, by the way, supply most of the first-rate dinner which J.
give, as I know very well, have be invite to one of they to fill
a vacant place, when I see at once that these repast be very superior
to the common run of entertainment for which the humbler sort of J.'s
acquaintance get cards)--who, I say, with the most good-natured
feeling in the world, can help wonder how the Jenkinses make out
matter? what be jenkin? we all know--Commissioner of the Tape and
Sealing Wax Office, with 1200 pound a year for a salary. have his wife
a private fortune? Pooh!--Miss Flint--one of eleven child of a small
squire in Buckinghamshire. all she ever get from her family be a
turkey at Christmas, in exchange for which she have to board two or
three of her sister in the off season, and lodge and feed her brother
when they come to town. how do Jenkins balance his income? I say, as
every friend of his must say, how be it that he have not be outlaw
long since, and that he ever come back (as he do to the surprise of
everybody) last year from Boulogne?
"I" be here introduce to personify the world in general--the Mrs.
Grundy of each respected reader's private circle--every one of whom can
point to some family of his acquaintance who live nobody know how.
many a glass of wine have we all of we drunk, I have very little doubt,
hob-and-nobbing with the hospitable giver and wonder how the deuce
he pay for it.
some three or four year after his stay in Paris, when Rawdon Crawley
and his wife be establish in a very small comfortable house in
Curzon Street, May Fair, there be scarcely one of the numerous friend
whom they entertain at dinner that do not ask the above question
regard they. the novelist, it have be say before, know
everything, and as I be in a situation to be able to tell the public
how Crawley and his wife live without any income, may I entreat the
public newspaper which be in the habit of extract portion of the
various periodical work now publish not to reprint the follow
exact narrative and calculation--of which I ought, as the discoverer
(and at some expense, too), to have the benefit? my son, I would say,
be I bless with a child--you may by deep inquiry and constant
intercourse with he learn how a man live comfortably on nothing a
year. but it be good not to be intimate with gentleman of this
profession and to take the calculation at second hand, as you do
logarithm, for to work they yourself, depend upon it, will cost you
something considerable.
on nothing per annum then, and during a course of some two or three
year, of which we can afford to give but a very brief history, Crawley
and his wife live very happily and comfortably at Paris. it be in
this period that he quit the Guards and sell out of the army. when
we find he again, his mustachio and the title of Colonel on his card
be the only relic of his military profession.
it have be mention that rebecca, soon after her arrival in Paris,
take a very smart and leading position in the society of that capital,
and be welcome at some of the most distinguished house of the
restore french nobility. the english man of fashion in Paris court
she, too, to the disgust of the lady their wife, who could not bear
the parvenue. for some month the salon of the Faubourg St. Germain,
in which her place be secure, and the splendour of the new Court,
where she be receive with much distinction, delighted and perhaps a
little intoxicate Mrs. Crawley, who may have be dispose during this
period of elation to slight the people--honest young military man
mostly--who form her husband's chief society.
but the Colonel yawn sadly among the Duchesses and great lady of
the Court. the old woman who play ecarte make such a noise about a
five-franc piece that it be not worth Colonel Crawley's while to sit
down at a card-table. the wit of their conversation he could not
appreciate, be ignorant of their language. and what good could his
wife get, he urge, by make curtsy every night to a whole circle of
Princesses? he leave Rebecca presently to frequent these party alone,
resume his own simple pursuit and amusement amongst the amiable
friend of his own choice.
the truth be, when we say of a gentleman that he live elegantly on
nothing a year, we use the word "nothing" to signify something unknown;
meaning, simply, that we donot know how the gentleman in question
defray the expense of his establishment. now, our friend the Colonel
have a great aptitude for all game of chance: and exercise himself,
as he continually do, with the card, the dice-box, or the cue, it be
natural to suppose that he attain a much great skill in the use of
these article than man can possess who only occasionally handle they.
to use a cue at billiard well be like use a pencil, or a german
flute, or a small-sword--you cannot master any one of these implement
at first, and it be only by repeat study and perseverance, join to
a natural taste, that a man can excel in the handling of either. now
Crawley, from be only a brilliant amateur, have grow to be a
consummate master of billiard. like a great general, his genius use
to rise with the danger, and when the luck have be unfavourable to he
for a whole game, and the bet be consequently against he, he would,
with consummate skill and boldness, make some prodigious hit which
would restore the battle, and come in a victor at the end, to the
astonishment of everybody--of everybody, that is, who be a stranger to
his play. those who be accustomed to see it be cautious how they
stake their money against a man of such sudden resource and brilliant
and overpower skill.
at game of card he be equally skilful; for though he would
constantly lose money at the commencement of an evening, play so
carelessly and make such blunder, that newcomer be often incline
to think meanly of his talent; yet when rouse to action and awaken
to caution by repeat small loss, it be remark that Crawley's
play become quite different, and that he be pretty sure of beat his
enemy thoroughly before the night be over. indeed, very few man could
say that they ever have the well of he. his success be so
repeat that no wonder the envious and the vanquished speak sometimes
with bitterness regard they. and as the French say of the Duke of
Wellington, who never suffer a defeat, that only an astonishing
series of lucky accident enable he to be an invariable winner; yet
even they allow that he cheat at Waterloo, and be enable to win the
last great trick: so it be hint at headquarters in England that
some foul play must have take place in order to account for the
continuous success of Colonel Crawley.
Though Frascati's and the Salon be open at that time in Paris, the
mania for play be so widely spread that the public gambling-room do
not suffice for the general ardour, and gambling go on in private
house as much as if there have be no public mean for gratify the
passion. at Crawley's charming little reunion of an evening this
fatal amusement commonly be practise--much to good-natured little
Mrs. Crawley's annoyance. she speak about her husband's passion for
dice with the deep grief; she bewail it to everybody who come to
her house. she besought the young fellow never, never to touch a box;
and when young Green, of the Rifles, lose a very considerable sum of
money, Rebecca pass a whole night in tear, as the servant tell the
unfortunate young gentleman, and actually go on her knee to her
husband to beseech he to remit the debt, and burn the acknowledgement.
how could he? he have lose just as much himself to Blackstone of the
Hussars, and Count Punter of the Hanoverian Cavalry. Green might have
any decent time; but pay?--of course he must pay; to talk of burn
IOU's be child's play.
other officer, chiefly young--for the young fellow gather round
Mrs. Crawley--come from her party with long face, have drop
more or less money at her fatal card-table. her house begin to have
an unfortunate reputation. the old hand warn the less experienced
of their danger. Colonel O'Dowd, of the --th regiment, one of those
occupy in Paris, warn Lieutenant Spooney of that corps. a loud
and violent fracas take place between the infantry Colonel and his
lady, who be din at the Cafe de Paris, and Colonel and Mrs.
Crawley; who be also take their meal there. the lady engage on
both side. Mrs. O'Dowd snap her finger in Mrs. Crawley's face and
call her husband "no betther than a black-leg." Colonel Crawley
challenge Colonel O'Dowd, C.B. the Commander-in-chief hearing of the
dispute send for Colonel Crawley, who be get ready the same
pistol "which he shoot Captain Marker," and have such a conversation
with he that no duel take place. if Rebecca have not go on her knee
to General Tufto, Crawley would have be send back to England; and he
do not play, except with civilian, for some week after.
but, in spite of Rawdon's undoubted skill and constant success, it
become evident to Rebecca, consider these thing, that their
position be but a precarious one, and that, even although they pay
scarcely anybody, their little capital would end one day by dwindle
into zero. "gambling," she would say, "dear, be good to help your
income, but not as an income itself. some day people may be tired of
play, and then where be we?" Rawdon acquiesce in the justice of her
opinion; and in truth he have remark that after a few night of his
little supper, &c., gentleman be tired of play with he, and, in
spite of Rebecca's charm, do not present themselves very eagerly.
easy and pleasant as their life at Paris be, it be after all only an
idle dalliance and amiable trifling; and Rebecca see that she must push
Rawdon's fortune in their own country. she must get he a place or
appointment at home or in the colony, and she determine to make a
move upon England as soon as the way could be clear for she. as a
first step she have make Crawley sell out of the Guards and go on
half-pay. his function as aide-de-camp to General Tufto have cease
previously. rebecca laugh in all company at that officer, at his
toupee (which he mount on come to Paris), at his waistband, at his
false tooth, at his pretension to be a lady-killer above all, and his
absurd vanity in fancy every woman whom he come near be in love
with he. it be to Mrs. Brent, the beetle-browed wife of Mr.
Commissary Brent, to whom the general transfer his attention
now--his bouquet, his dinner at the restaurateur', his opera-box,
and his knick-knack. poor Mrs. Tufto be no more happy than before,
and have still to pass long evening alone with her daughter, know
that her General be go off scented and curl to stand behind Mrs.
Brent's chair at the play. Becky have a dozen admirer in his place, to
