be sure, and could cut her rival to piece with her wit. but, as we
have say, she be grow tired of this idle social life:
opera-box and restaurateur dinner pal upon she: nosegay could
not be lay by as a provision for future year: and she could not live
upon knick-knack, laced handkerchief, and kid glove. she feel the
frivolity of pleasure and long for more substantial benefit.
at this juncture news arrive which be spread among the many creditor
of the Colonel at Paris, and which cause they great satisfaction.
Miss Crawley, the rich aunt from whom he expect his immense
inheritance, be die; the Colonel must haste to her bedside. Mrs.
Crawley and her child would remain behind until he come to reclaim
they. he depart for Calais, and have reach that place in safety,
it might have be suppose that he go to Dover; but instead he take
the diligence to Dunkirk, and thence travel to Brussels, for which
place he have a former predilection. the fact be, he owe more money at
London than at Paris; and he prefer the quiet little belgian city to
either of the more noisy capital.
her aunt be dead. Mrs. Crawley order the most intense mourning for
herself and little Rawdon. the Colonel be busy arrange the affair
of the inheritance. they could take the premier now, instead of the
little entresol of the hotel which they occupy. Mrs. Crawley and the
landlord have a consultation about the new hanging, an amicable wrangle
about the carpet, and a final adjustment of everything except the
bill. she go off in one of his carriage; her french bonne with she;
the child by her side; the admirable landlord and landlady smile
farewell to she from the gate. General Tufto be furious when he hear
she be go, and Mrs. Brent furious with he for be furious;
Lieutenant Spooney be cut to the heart; and the landlord get ready his
good apartment previous to the return of the fascinating little woman
and her husband. he _serre_ the trunk which she leave in his charge
with the great care. they have be especially recommend to he by
Madame Crawley. they be not, however, find to be particularly
valuable when open some time after.
but before she go to join her husband in the Belgic capital, Mrs.
Crawley make an expedition into England, leave behind her her little
son upon the continent, under the care of her french maid.
the parting between Rebecca and the little Rawdon do not cause either
party much pain. she have not, to say truth, see much of the young
gentleman since his birth. after the amiable fashion of french mother,
she have place he out at nurse in a village in the neighbourhood of
Paris, where little Rawdon pass the first month of his life, not
unhappily, with a numerous family of foster-brother in wooden shoe.
his father would ride over many a time to see he here, and the elder
Rawdon's paternal heart glow to see he rosy and dirty, shout
lustily, and happy in the making of mud-pie under the superintendence
of the gardener's wife, his nurse.
Rebecca do not care much to go and see the son and heir. once he
spoil a new dove-colour pelisse of her. he prefer his nurse's
caress to his mamma's, and when finally he quit that jolly nurse
and almost parent, he cry loudly for hour. he be only console by
his mother's promise that he should return to his nurse the next day;
indeed the nurse herself, who probably would have be pain at the
parting too, be tell that the child would immediately be restore to
she, and for some time await quite anxiously his return.
in fact, our friend may be say to have be among the first of that
brood of hardy english adventurer who have subsequently invade the
Continent and swindle in all the capital of Europe. the respect in
those happy day of 1817-18 be very great for the wealth and honour of
Britons. they have not then learn, as I be tell, to haggle for
bargain with the pertinacity which now distinguish they. the great
city of Europe have not be as yet open to the enterprise of our
rascal. and whereas there be now hardly a town of France or Italy in
which you shall not see some noble countryman of our own, with that
happy swagger and insolence of demeanour which we carry everywhere,
swindle inn-landlord, pass fictitious cheque upon credulous
banker, rob coach-maker of their carriage, goldsmith of their
trinket, easy traveller of their money at card, even public
library of their book--thirty year ago you need but to be a Milor
Anglais, travel in a private carriage, and credit be at your hand
wherever you choose to seek it, and gentleman, instead of cheat, be
cheat. it be not for some week after the Crawleys' departure that
the landlord of the hotel which they occupy during their residence at
Paris find out the loss which he have sustain: not until Madame
Marabou, the milliner, make repeat visit with her little bill for
article supply to Madame Crawley; not until Monsieur Didelot from
Boule d'or in the Palais Royal have ask half a dozen time whether
cette charmante Miladi who have buy watch and bracelet of he be
de retour. it be a fact that even the poor gardener's wife, who have
nurse madame's child, be never pay after the first six month for
that supply of the milk of human kindness with which she have furnish
the lusty and healthy little Rawdon. no, not even the nurse be
pay--the Crawleys be in too great a hurry to remember their trifling
debt to she. as for the landlord of the hotel, his curse against the
english nation be violent for the rest of his natural life. he ask
all traveller whether they know a certain Colonel Lor Crawley--avec sa
femme une petite dame, tre spirituelle. "ah, Monsieur!" he would
add--"il m'ont affreusement vole." it be melancholy to hear his
accent as he speak of that catastrophe.
Rebecca's object in her journey to London be to effect a kind of
compromise with her husband's numerous creditor, and by offer they
a dividend of ninepence or a shilling in the pound, to secure a return
for he into his own country. it do not become we to trace the step
which she take in the conduct of this most difficult negotiation; but,
have show they to their satisfaction that the sum which she be
empower to offer be all her husband's available capital, and have
convince they that Colonel Crawley would prefer a perpetual retirement
on the continent to a residence in this country with his debt
unsettled; have prove to they that there be no possibility of money
accrue to he from other quarter, and no earthly chance of their
get a large dividend than that which she be empower to offer,
she bring the Colonel's creditor unanimously to accept she
proposal, and purchase with fifteen hundred pound of ready money
more than ten time that amount of debt.
Mrs. Crawley employ no lawyer in the transaction. the matter be so
simple, to have or to leave, as she justly observe, that she make the
lawyer of the creditor themselves do the business. and Mr. Lewis
represent Mr. Davids, of Red Lion Square, and Mr. Moss act for
Mr. Manasseh of Cursitor Street (chief creditor of the Colonel's),
compliment his lady upon the brilliant way in which she do business,
and declare that there be no professional man who could beat she.
Rebecca receive their congratulation with perfect modesty; order a
bottle of sherry and a bread cake to the little dingy lodging where
she dwelt, while conduct the business, to treat the enemy's lawyer:
shake hand with they at part, in excellent good humour, and
return straightway to the continent, to rejoin her husband and son
and acquaint the former with the glad news of his entire liberation.
as for the latter, he have be considerably neglect during his
mother's absence by Mademoiselle Genevieve, her french maid; for that
young woman, contract an attachment for a soldier in the garrison of
Calais, forget her charge in the society of this militaire, and little
Rawdon very narrowly escape drown on Calais sand at this period,
where the absent Genevieve have leave and lose he.
and so, Colonel and Mrs. Crawley come to London: and it be at their
house in Curzon Street, May Fair, that they really show the skill
which must be possess by those who would live on the resource above
name.
CHAPTER XXXVII
the subject continue
in the first place, and as a matter of the great necessity, we be
bind to describe how a house may be get for nothing a year. these
mansion be to be have either unfurnishe, where, if you have credit
with Messrs. Gillows or banting, you can get they splendidly montee
and decorate entirely accord to your own fancy; or they be to be
let furnish, a less troublesome and complicated arrangement to most
party. it be so that Crawley and his wife prefer to hire their
house.
before Mr. Bowls come to preside over Miss Crawley's house and cellar
in Park Lane, that lady have have for a butler a Mr. Raggles, who be
bear on the family estate of Queen's Crawley, and indeed be a young
son of a gardener there. by good conduct, a handsome person and
calf, and a grave demeanour, Raggles rise from the knife-board to the
footboard of the carriage; from the footboard to the butler's pantry.
when he have be a certain number of year at the head of Miss
Crawley's establishment, where he have have good wage, fat perquisite,
and plenty of opportunity of saving, he announce that he be about
to contract a matrimonial alliance with a late cook of Miss Crawley's,
who have subsist in an honourable manner by the exercise of a mangle,
and the keeping of a small greengrocer's shop in the neighbourhood.
the truth be, that the ceremony have be clandestinely perform some
year back; although the news of Mr. Raggles' marriage be first
bring to Miss Crawley by a little boy and girl of seven and eight
year of age, whose continual presence in the kitchen have attract the
attention of Miss Briggs.
Mr. Raggles then retire and personally undertake the superintendence
of the small shop and the green. he add milk and cream, egg and
country-feed pork to his store, content himself whilst other retire
butler be vend spirit in public house, by deal in the
simple country produce. and have a good connection amongst the
butler in the neighbourhood, and a snug back parlour where he and Mrs.
Raggles receive they, his milk, cream, and egg get to be adopt by
many of the fraternity, and his profit increase every year. year
after year he quietly and modestly amass money, and when at length
that snug and complete bachelor's residence at no. 201, Curzon Street,
May Fair, lately the residence of the Honourable Frederick Deuceace,
go abroad, with its rich and appropriate furniture by the first
maker, be bring to the hammer, who should go in and purchase the
lease and furniture of the house but Charles Raggles? a part of the
money he borrow, it be true, and at rather a high interest, from a
brother butler, but the chief part he pay down, and it be with no
small pride that Mrs. Raggles find herself sleep in a bed of carve
mahogany, with silk curtain, with a prodigious cheval glass opposite
to she, and a wardrobe which would contain she, and Raggles, and all
the family.
of course, they do not intend to occupy permanently an apartment so
splendid. it be in order to let the house again that Raggles
purchase it. as soon as a tenant be find, he subside into the
greengrocer's shop once more; but a happy thing it be for he to walk
out of that tenement and into Curzon Street, and there survey his
house--his own house--with geranium in the window and a carve bronze
knocker. the footman occasionally lounge at the area rail,
treat he with respect; the cook take her green stuff at his house
and call he Mr. Landlord, and there be not one thing the tenant
do, or one dish which they have for dinner, that Raggles might not know
of, if he like.
he be a good man; good and happy. the house bring he in so
handsome a yearly income that he be determined to send his child to
good school, and accordingly, regardless of expense, Charles be send
to boarding at Dr. Swishtail's, Sugar-cane Lodge, and little Matilda to
Miss Peckover's, Laurentinum House, Clapham.
raggle love and adore the Crawley family as the author of all his
prosperity in life. he have a silhouette of his mistress in his back
shop, and a drawing of the Porter's Lodge at Queen's Crawley, do by
that spinster herself in India ink--and the only addition he make to
the decoration of the Curzon Street House be a print of Queen's
Crawley in Hampshire, the seat of Sir Walpole Crawley, Baronet, who be
represent in a gild car draw by six white horse, and pass by a
lake cover with swan, and barge contain lady in hoop, and
musician with flag and penwig. indeed Raggles think there be no
such palace in all the world, and no such august family.
as luck would have it, Raggles' house in Curzon Street be to let when
Rawdon and his wife return to London. the Colonel know it and its
owner quite well; the latter's connection with the Crawley family have
be keep up constantly, for Raggles help Mr. Bowls whenever Miss
Crawley receive friend. and the old man not only let his house to
the Colonel but officiate as his butler whenever he have company; Mrs.
Raggles operate in the kitchen below and send up dinner of which
old Miss Crawley herself might have approve. this be the way, then,
Crawley get his house for nothing; for though Raggles have to pay taxis
and rate, and the interest of the mortgage to the brother butler; and
the insurance of his life; and the charge for his child at school;
and the value of the meat and drink which his own family--and for a
time that of Colonel Crawley too--consume; and though the poor wretch
be utterly ruin by the transaction, his child be fling on the
street, and himself drive into the Fleet Prison: yet somebody must
pay even for gentleman who live for nothing a year--and so it be this
unlucky Raggles be make the representative of Colonel Crawley's
defective capital.
I wonder how many family be drive to roguery and to ruin by great
practitioner in Crawlers way?--how many great nobleman rob their petty
tradesman, condescend to swindle their poor retainer out of wretched
little sum and cheat for a few shilling? when we read that a noble
nobleman have leave for the continent, or that another noble nobleman have
an execution in his house--and that one or other owe six or seven
million, the defeat seem glorious even, and we respect the victim in
the vastness of his ruin. but who pity a poor barber who cannot get
his money for powder the footman's head; or a poor carpenter who
have ruin himself by fix up ornament and pavilion for my lady's
dejeuner; or the poor devil of a tailor whom the steward patronize,
and who have pledge all he be worth, and more, to get the livery
ready, which my lord have do he the honour to bespeak? when the great
house tumble down, these miserable wretch fall under it unnoticed:
as they say in the old legend, before a man go to the devil himself,
he send plenty of other soul thither.
Rawdon and his wife generously give their patronage to all such of Miss
Crawley's tradesman and purveyor as choose to serve they. some be
willing enough, especially the poor one. it be wonderful to see the
pertinacity with which the washerwoman from Tooting bring the cart
every Saturday, and her bill week after week. Mr. Raggles himself have
to supply the greengrocery. the bill for servant' porter at the
Fortune of War public house be a curiosity in the chronicle of beer.
every servant also be owe the great part of his wage, and thus
keep up perforce an interest in the house. nobody in fact be pay.
not the blacksmith who open the lock; nor the glazier who mend the
pane; nor the jobber who let the carriage; nor the groom who drive it;
nor the butcher who provide the leg of mutton; nor the coal which
roast it; nor the cook who baste it; nor the servant who eat it:
and this I be give to understand be not unfrequently the way in which
people live elegantly on nothing a year.
in a little town such thing cannot be do without remark. we know
there the quantity of milk our neighbour take and espy the joint or
the fowl which be go in for his dinner. so, probably, 200 and 202
in Curzon Street might know what be go on in the house between
they, the servant communicate through the area-railing; but Crawley
and his wife and his friend do not know 200 and 202. when you come to
201 there be a hearty welcome, a kind smile, a good dinner, and a
jolly shake of the hand from the host and hostess there, just for all
the world as if they have be undisputed master of three or four
thousand a year--and so they be, not in money, but in produce and
labour--if they do not pay for the mutton, they have it: if they do
not give bullion in exchange for their wine, how should we know? never
be well claret at any man's table than at honest Rawdon's; dinner
more gay and neatly serve. his drawing-room be the pretty,
little, modest salon conceivable: they be decorate with the
great taste, and a thousand knick-knack from Paris, by Rebecca:
and when she sit at her piano trill song with a lightsome heart,
the stranger vote himself in a little paradise of domestic comfort and
agree that, if the husband be rather stupid, the wife be charming,
and the dinner the pleasantest in the world.
Rebecca's wit, cleverness, and flippancy make she speedily the vogue in
London among a certain class. you see demure chariot at her door, out
of which step very great people. you behold her carriage in the
park, surround by dandy of note. the little box in the third tier
of the opera be crowd with head constantly change; but it must be
confess that the lady hold aloof from she, and that their door
be shut to our little adventurer.
with regard to the world of female fashion and its custom, the present
writer of course can only speak at second hand. a man can no more
penetrate or under-stand those mystery than he can know what the
lady talk about when they go upstairs after dinner. it be only by
inquiry and perseverance that one sometimes get hint of those
secret; and by a similar diligence every person who tread the Pall
Mall pavement and frequent the club of this metropolis know, either
through his own experience or through some acquaintance with whom he
play at billiard or share the joint, something about the genteel
world of London, and how, as there be man (such as Rawdon Crawley,
whose position we mention before) who cut a good figure to the eye
of the ignorant world and to the apprentice in the park, who behold
they consort with the most notorious dandy there, so there be
lady, who may be call man's woman, be welcome entirely by all
the gentleman and cut or slight by all their wife. Mrs. Firebrace
be of this sort; the lady with the beautiful fair ringlet whom you see
every day in Hyde Park, surround by the great and most famous
dandy of this empire. Mrs. Rockwood be another, whose party be
announce laboriously in the fashionable newspaper and with whom you
see that all sort of ambassador and great nobleman dine; and many
more might be mention have they to do with the history at present in
hand. but while simple folk who be out of the world, or country
people with a taste for the genteel, behold these lady in their
seem glory in public place, or envy they from afar off, person who
be well instruct could inform they that these envy lady have
no more chance of establish themselves in "society," than the
benighted squire's wife in Somersetshire who read of their doing in
the Morning Post. man live about London be aware of these awful
truth. you hear how pitilessly many lady of seem rank and wealth
be exclude from this "society." the frantic effort which they make
to enter this circle, the meanness to which they submit, the insult
which they undergo, be matter of wonder to those who take human or
womankind for a study; and the pursuit of fashion under difficulty
would be a fine theme for any very great person who have the wit, the
leisure, and the knowledge of the english language necessary for the
compiling of such a history.
now the few female acquaintance whom Mrs. Crawley have know abroad not
only decline to visit she when she come to this side of the Channel,
but cut she severely when they meet in public place. it be curious to
see how the great lady forget she, and no doubt not altogether a
pleasant study to Rebecca. when Lady Bareacres meet she in the
waiting-room at the opera, she gather her daughter about she as if
they would be contaminate by a touch of Becky, and retreat a step
or two, place herself in front of they, and star at her little
enemy. to stare Becky out of countenance require a severer glance than
even the frigid old Bareacres could shoot out of her dismal eye. when
Lady de la Mole, who have ride a score of time by Becky's side at
Brussels, meet Mrs. Crawley's open carriage in Hyde Park, her ladyship
be quite blind, and could not in the least recognize her former
friend. even Mrs. Blenkinsop, the banker's wife, cut she at church.
Becky go regularly to church now; it be edify to see she enter
there with Rawdon by her side, carry a couple of large gilt
prayer-book, and afterwards go through the ceremony with the
grave resignation.
Rawdon at first feel very acutely the slight which be pass upon
his wife, and be inclined to be gloomy and savage. he talk of
call out the husband or brother of every one of the insolent woman
who do not pay a proper respect to his wife; and it be only by the
strong command and entreaty on her part that he be bring into
keep a decent behaviour. "you cannot shoot I into society," she
say good-naturedly. "remember, my dear, that I be but a governess,
and you, you poor silly old man, have the bad reputation for debt,
and dice, and all sort of wickedness. we shall get quite as many
friend as we want by and by, and in the meanwhile you must be a good
boy and obey your schoolmistress in everything she tell you to do.
when we hear that your aunt have leave almost everything to Pitt and his
wife, do you remember what a rage you be in? you would have tell all
Paris, if I have not make you keep your temper, and where would you have
be now?--in prison at Ste. Pelagie for debt, and not establish in
London in a handsome house, with every comfort about you--you be in
such a fury you be ready to murder your brother, you wicke Cain you,
and what good would have come of remain angry? all the rage in the
world willnot get we your aunt's money; and it be much well that we
should be friend with your brother's family than enemy, as those
foolish Butes be. when your father die, Queen's Crawley will be a
pleasant house for you and I to pass the winter in. if we be ruin,
you can carve and take charge of the stable, and I can be a governess
to Lady Jane's child. ruin! fiddlede-dee! I will get you a good
place before that; or Pitt and his little boy will die, and we will be
Sir Rawdon and my lady. while there be life, there be hope, my dear,
and I intend to make a man of you yet. who sell your horse for you?
who pay your debt for you?" Rawdon be oblige to confess that he
owe all these benefit to his wife, and to trust himself to her
guidance for the future.
indeed, when Miss Crawley quit the world, and that money for which
all her relative have be fight so eagerly be finally leave to
Pitt, Bute Crawley, who find that only five thousand pound have be
leave to he instead of the twenty upon which he calculate, be in such
a fury at his disappointment that he vent it in savage abuse upon his
nephew; and the quarrel always rankle between they end in an utter
breach of intercourse. Rawdon Crawley's conduct, on the other hand,
who get but a hundred pound, be such as to astonish his brother and
delight his sister-in-law, who be disposed to look kindly upon all the
member of her husband's family. he write to his brother a very frank,
manly, good-humour letter from Paris. he be aware, he say, that by
his own marriage he have forfeit his aunt's favour; and though he do
not disguise his disappointment that she should have be so entirely
relentless towards he, he be glad that the money be still keep in
their branch of the family, and heartily congratulate his brother on
his good fortune. he send his affectionate remembrance to his sister,
and hop to have her good-will for Mrs. Rawdon; and the letter
conclude with a postscript to Pitt in the latter lady's own
handwriting. she, too, beg to join in her husband's
congratulation. she should ever remember Mr. Crawley's kindness to
she in early day when she be a friendless orphan, the instructress of
his little sister, in whose welfare she still take the tender
interest. she wish he every happiness in his married life, and,
ask his permission to offer her remembrance to Lady Jane (of whose
goodness all the world inform she), she hop that one day she might
be allow to present her little boy to his uncle and aunt, and beg
to bespeak for he their good-will and protection.
Pitt Crawley receive this communication very graciously--more
graciously than Miss Crawley have receive some of Rebecca's previous
composition in Rawdon's handwriting; and as for Lady Jane, she be so
charm with the letter that she expect her husband would instantly
divide his aunt's legacy into two equal portion and send off one-half
to his brother at Paris.
to her ladyship's surprise, however, Pitt decline to accommodate his
brother with a cheque for thirty thousand pound. but he make Rawdon a
handsome offer of his hand whenever the latter should come to England
and choose to take it; and, thank Mrs. Crawley for her good opinion
of himself and Lady Jane, he graciously pronounce his willingness to
take any opportunity to serve her little boy.
thus an almost reconciliation be bring about between the brother.
when Rebecca come to town Pitt and his wife be not in London. many a
time she drive by the old door in Park Lane to see whether they have
take possession of Miss Crawley's house there. but the new family do
not make its appearance; it be only through Raggles that she hear of
their movement--how Miss Crawley's domestic have be dismiss with
decent gratuity, and how Mr. Pitt have only once make his appearance
in London, when he stop for a few day at the house, do business
with his lawyer there, and sell off all Miss Crawley's french novel
to a bookseller out of Bond Street. Becky have reason of her own which
cause she to long for the arrival of her new relation. "when Lady Jane
come," think she, "she shall be my sponsor in London society; and as
for the woman! bah! the woman will ask I when they find the man want
to see I."
an article as necessary to a lady in this position as her brougham or
her bouquet be her companion. I have always admire the way in which
the tender creature, who cannot exist without sympathy, hire an
exceedingly plain friend of their own sex from whom they be almost
inseparable. the sight of that inevitable woman in her fade gown
seat behind her dear friend in the opera-box, or occupy the back
seat of the barouche, be always a wholesome and moral one to I, as
jolly a reminder as that of the death'-head which figure in the
repast of egyptian bon-vivant, a strange sardonic memorial of Vanity
Fair. what? even batter, brazen, beautiful, conscienceless,
heartless, Mrs. Firebrace, whose father die of her shame: even
lovely, dare Mrs. Mantrap, who will ride at any fence which any man
in England will take, and who drive her grey in the park, while her
mother keep a huckster's stall in Bath still--even those who be so
bold, one might fancy they could face anything dare not face the world
without a female friend. they must have somebody to cling to, the
affectionate creature! and you will hardly see they in any public
place without a shabby companion in a dye silk, sit somewhere in
the shade close behind they.
"Rawdon," say Becky, very late one night, as a party of gentleman be
seat round her crackling drawing-room fire (for the man come to her
house to finish the night; and she have ice and coffee for they, the
good in London): "I must have a sheep-dog."
"a what?" say Rawdon, look up from an ecarte table.
"a sheep-dog!" say young Lord Southdown. "my dear Mrs. Crawley, what
a fancy! why not have a danish dog? I know of one as big as a
camel-leopard, by Jove. it would almost pull your brougham. or a
persian greyhound, eh? (I propose, if you please); or a little pug that
would go into one of Lord Steyne's snuff-box? therebe a man at
bayswater get one with such a nose that you might--I mark the king and
play--that you might hang your hat on it."
"I mark the trick," Rawdon gravely say. he attend to his game
commonly and donot much meddle with the conversation, except when it
be about horse and bet.
"what can you want with a shepherd's dog?" the lively little Southdown
continue.
"I mean a MORAL shepherd's dog," say Becky, laugh and look up at
Lord Steyne.
"what the devil's that?" say his Lordship.
"a dog to keep the wolf off I," Rebecca continue. "a companion."
"dear little innocent lamb, you want one," say the marquis; and his
jaw thrust out, and he begin to grin hideously, his little eye leer
towards Rebecca.
the great Lord of Steyne be stand by the fire sip coffee. the
fire crackle and blaze pleasantly there be a score of candle
sparkle round the mantel piece, in all sort of quaint sconce, of
gilt and bronze and porcelain. they light up Rebecca's figure to
admiration, as she sit on a sofa cover with a pattern of gaudy
flower. she be in a pink dress that look as fresh as a rose; her
dazzle white arm and shoulder be half-cover with a thin hazy
scarf through which they sparkle; her hair hang in curl round her
neck; one of her little foot peep out from the fresh crisp fold of
the silk: the prettiest little foot in the prettiest little sandal in
the fine silk stocking in the world.
the candle light up Lord Steyne's shin bald head, which be
fringe with red hair. he have thick bushy eyebrow, with little
twinkle bloodshot eye, surround by a thousand wrinkle. his jaw
be underhung, and when he laugh, two white buck-tooth protrude
themselves and glisten savagely in the midst of the grin. he have be
dining with royal personage, and wear his garter and ribbon. a short
man be his Lordship, broad-cheste and bow-legged, but proud of the
fineness of his foot and ankle, and always caress his garter-knee.
"and so the shepherd be not enough," say he, "to defend his lambkin?"
"the shepherd be too fond of play at card and go to his club,"
answer Becky, laugh.
"'Gad, what a debauch Corydon!" say my lord--"what a mouth for a
pipe!"
"I take your three to two," here say Rawdon, at the card-table.
"Hark at Meliboeus," snarl the noble marquis; "hebe pastorally
occupy too: hebe shear a Southdown. what an innocent mutton, hey?
Damme, what a snowy fleece!"
Rebecca's eye shoot out gleam of scornful humour. "my lord," she say,
"you be a knight of the order." he have the collar round his neck,
indeed--a gift of the restore prince of Spain.
Lord Steyne in early life have be notorious for his daring and his
success at play. he have sit up two day and two night with Mr. Fox at
hazard. he have win money of the most august personage of the realm:
he have win his marquisate, it be say, at the gaming-table; but he do
not like an allusion to those bygone fredaine. Rebecca see the scowl
gathering over his heavy brow.
she rise up from her sofa and go and take his coffee cup out of his
hand with a little curtsey. "yes," she say, "I must get a watchdog.
but he willnot bark at you." and, go into the other drawing-room, she
sit down to the piano and begin to sing little french song in such a
charming, thrill voice that the mollified nobleman speedily follow
she into that chamber, and might be see nod his head and bow
time over she.
Rawdon and his friend meanwhile play ecarte until they have enough.
the Colonel win; but, say that he win ever so much and often, night
like these, which occur many time in the week--his wife have all
the talk and all the admiration, and he sit silent without the
circle, not comprehend a word of the joke, the allusion, the
mystical language within--must have be rather wearisome to the
ex-dragoon.
"how be Mrs. Crawley's husband?" Lord Steyne use to say to he by way
of a good day when they meet; and indeed that be now his avocation in
life. he be Colonel Crawley no more. he be Mrs. Crawley's husband.
about the little Rawdon, if nothing have be say all this while, it be
because he be hide upstairs in a garret somewhere, or have crawl
below into the kitchen for companionship. his mother scarcely ever
take notice of he. he pass the day with his french bonne as long
as that domestic remain in Mr. Crawley's family, and when the
Frenchwoman go away, the little fellow, howl in the loneliness of
the night, have compassion take on he by a housemaid, who take he out
of his solitary nursery into her bed in the garret hard by and
comfort he.
Rebecca, my Lord Steyne, and one or two more be in the drawing-room
take tea after the opera, when this shouting be hear overhead.
"itbe my cherub cry for his nurse," she say. she do not offer to
move to go and see the child. "donot agitate your feeling by go to
look for he," say Lord Steyne sardonically. "bah!" reply the other,
with a sort of blush, "hewill cry himself to sleep"; and they fall to
talk about the opera.
Rawdon have steal off though, to look after his son and heir; and come
back to the company when he find that honest dolly be console the
child. the Colonel's dressing-room be in those upper region. he
use to see the boy there in private. they have interview together
every morning when he shave; Rawdon minor sit on a box by his
father's side and watch the operation with never-cease pleasure.
he and the sire be great friend. the father would bring he
sweetmeat from the dessert and hide they in a certain old epaulet box,
where the child go to seek they, and laugh with joy on discover
the treasure; laugh, but not too loud: for mamma be below asleep
and must not be disturb. she do not go to rest till very late and
seldom rise till after noon.
Rawdon buy the boy plenty of picture-book and cram his nursery
with toy. its wall be cover with picture paste up by the
father's own hand and purchase by he for ready money. when he be
off duty with Mrs. Rawdon in the park, he would sit up here, pass
hour with the boy; who ride on his chest, who pull his great
mustachio as if they be drive-rein, and spend day with he in
indefatigable gambol. the room be a low room, and once, when the
child be not five year old, his father, who be toss he wildly up
in his arm, hit the poor little chap's skull so violently against the
ceiling that he almost drop the child, so terrified be he at the
disaster.
Rawdon minor have make up his face for a tremendous howl--the severity
of the blow indeed authorize that indulgence; but just as he be go
to begin, the father interpose.
"for God's sake, Rawdy, donot wake Mamma," he cry. and the child,
look in a very hard and piteous way at his father, bit his lip,
clench his hand, and donot cry a bit. Rawdon tell that story at
the club, at the mess, to everybody in town. "by Gad, sir," he
explain to the public in general, "what a good pluck one that boy
of mine be--what a trump he be! I half-send his head through the
ceiling, by Gad, and he wouldnot cry for fear of disturb his mother."
sometimes--once or twice in a week--that lady visit the upper region
in which the child live. she come like a vivified figure out of the
Magasin des Modes--blandly smile in the most beautiful new clothe
and little glove and boot. wonderful scarf, lace, and jewel
glitter about she. she have always a new bonnet on, and flower
bloom perpetually in it, or else magnificent curl ostrich
feather, soft and snowy as camellias. she nod twice or thrice
patronizingly to the little boy, who look up from his dinner or from
the picture of soldier he be paint. when she leave the room, an
odour of rose, or some other magical fragrance, linger about the
nursery. she be an unearthly be in his eye, superior to his
father--to all the world: to be worship and admire at a distance.
to drive with that lady in the carriage be an awful rite: he sit up
in the back seat and do not dare to speak: he gaze with all his eye
at the beautifully dress Princess opposite to he. gentleman on
splendid prance horse come up and smile and talk with she. how
her eye beam upon all of they! her hand use to quiver and wave
gracefully as they pass. when he go out with she he have his new
red dress on. his old brown holland be good enough when he stay at
home. sometimes, when she be away, and dolly his maid be make his
bed, he come into his mother's room. it be as the abode of a fairy to
he--a mystic chamber of splendour and delight. there in the wardrobe
hang those wonderful robe--pink and blue and many-tinted. there be
the jewel-case, silver-clasp, and the wondrous bronze hand on the
dressing-table, glisten all over with a hundred ring. there be
the cheval-glass, that miracle of art, in which he could just see his
own wonder head and the reflection of Dolly (queerly distort, and
as if up in the ceiling), plump and pat the pillow of the bed.
oh, thou poor lonely little benighted boy! mother be the name for God
in the lip and heart of little child; and here be one who be
worship a stone!
now Rawdon Crawley, rascal as the Colonel be, have certain manly
tendency of affection in his heart and could love a child and a woman
still. for Rawdon minor he have a great secret tenderness then, which
do not escape Rebecca, though she do not talk about it to her
husband. it do not annoy she: she be too good-natured. it only
increase her scorn for he. he feel somehow ashamed of this paternal
softness and hide it from his wife--only indulge in it when alone with
the boy.
he use to take he out of morning when they would go to the stable
together and to the park. little Lord Southdown, the well-natured of
man, who would make you a present of the hat from his head, and whose
main occupation in life be to buy knick-knack that he might give they
away afterwards, buy the little chap a pony not much big than a
large rat, the donor say, and on this little black shetland pygmy
young Rawdon's great father be pleased to mount the boy, and to walk
by his side in the park. it please he to see his old quarter, and
his old fellow-guardsman at Knightsbridge: he have begin to think of his
bachelorhood with something like regret. the old trooper be glad to
recognize their ancient officer and dandle the little colonel. Colonel
Crawley find dining at mess and with his brother-officer very
pleasant. "hang it, I benot clever enough for she--I know it. she
willnot miss I," he use to say: and he be right, his wife do not
miss he.
Rebecca be fond of her husband. she be always perfectly good-humour
and kind to he. she do not even show she scorn much for
he; perhaps she like he the well for be a fool. he be her
upper servant and maitre d'hotel. he go on her errand; obey she
order without question; drive in the carriage in the ring with she
without repine; take she to the opera-box, solace himself at his
club during the performance, and come punctually back to fetch she when
due. he would have like she to be a little fonder of the boy, but
even to that he reconcile himself. "hang it, you know shebe so
clever," he say, "and Ibe not literary and that, you know." for, as we
have say before, it require no great wisdom to be able to win at
card and billiard, and Rawdon make no pretension to any other sort
of skill.
when the companion come, his domestic duty become very light. his
wife encourage he to dine abroad: she would let he off duty at the
opera. "donot stay and stupefy yourself at home to-night, my dear,"
she would say. "some man be come who will only bore you. I would
not ask they, but you know itbe for your good, and now I have a
sheep-dog, I need not be afraid to be alone."
"a sheep-dog--a companion! Becky Sharp with a companion! benot it
good fun?" think Mrs. Crawley to herself. the notion tickle hugely
her sense of humour.
one Sunday morning, as Rawdon Crawley, his little son, and the pony
be take their accustomed walk in the park, they pass by an old
acquaintance of the Colonel's, Corporal Clink, of the regiment, who be
in conversation with a friend, an old gentleman, who hold a boy in his
arm about the age of little Rawdon. this other youngster have seize
hold of the Waterloo medal which the Corporal wear, and be examine
it with delight.
"good morning, your Honour," say Clink, in reply to the "how do,
Clink?" of the Colonel. "this ere young gentleman be about the little
Colonel's age, sir," continue the corporal.
"his father be a Waterloo man, too," say the old gentleman, who
carry the boy. "benot he, georgy?"
"yes," say georgy. he and the little chap on the pony be look at
each other with all their might--solemnly scan each other as
child do.
"in a line regiment," Clink say with a patronizing air.
"he be a Captain in the --th regiment," say the old gentleman rather
pompously. "Captain George Osborne, sir--perhaps you know he. he
die the death of a hero, sir, fight against the Corsican tyrant."
Colonel Crawley blush quite red. "I know he very well, sir," he
say, "and his wife, his dear little wife, sir--how be she?"
"she be my daughter, sir," say the old gentleman, put down the boy
and take out a card with great solemnity, which he hand to the
Colonel. on it written--
"Mr. Sedley, Sole Agent for the Black Diamond and Anti-Cinder Coal
Association, Bunker's Wharf, Thames Street, and Anna-Maria Cottages,
Fulham Road West."
Little Georgy go up and look at the Shetland pony.
"should you like to have a ride?" say Rawdon minor from the saddle.
"yes," say Georgy. the Colonel, who have be look at he with some
interest, take up the child and put he on the pony behind Rawdon minor.
"take hold of he, Georgy," he said--"take my little boy round the
waist--his name be Rawdon." and both the child begin to laugh.
"you willnot see a pretty pair I think, this summer's day, sir," say
the good-natured Corporal; and the Colonel, the Corporal, and old Mr.
Sedley with his umbrella, walk by the side of the child.
CHAPTER XXXVIII
a family in a very small way
we must suppose little George Osborne have ride from Knightsbridge
towards Fulham, and will stop and make inquiry at that village
regard some friend whom we have leave there. how be Mrs. Amelia
after the storm of Waterloo? be she live and thriving? what have come
of Major Dobbin, whose cab be always hanker about her premise? and
be there any news of the Collector of Boggley Wollah? the fact
concern the latter be briefly these:
our worthy fat friend Joseph Sedley return to India not long after
his escape from Brussels. either his furlough be up, or he dread to
meet any witness of his Waterloo flight. however it might be, he
go back to his duty in Bengal very soon after Napoleon have take up
his residence at St. Helena, where Jos see the ex-Emperor. to hear Mr.
Sedley talk on board ship you would have suppose that it be not the
first time he and the Corsican have meet, and that the civilian have
beard the french General at Mount St. John. he have a thousand
anecdote about the famous battle; he know the position of every
regiment and the loss which each have incur. he do not deny that he
have be concern in those victory--that he have be with the army
and carry despatch for the Duke of Wellington. and he describe
what the Duke do and say on every conceivable moment of the day of
Waterloo, with such an accurate knowledge of his grace's sentiment and
proceeding that it be clear he must have be by the conqueror's side
throughout the day; though, as a non-combatant, his name be not
mention in the public document relative to the battle. perhaps he
actually work himself up to believe that he have be engage with the
army; certain it be that he make a prodigious sensation for some time
at Calcutta, and be call Waterloo Sedley during the whole of his
subsequent stay in Bengal.
the bill which Jos have give for the purchase of those unlucky horse
be pay without question by he and his agent. he never be hear
to allude to the bargain, and nobody know for a certainty what become
of the horse, or how he get rid of they, or of Isidor, his belgian
servant, who sell a grey horse, very like the one which Jos ride, at
Valenciennes sometime during the autumn of 1815.
Jos's London agent have order to pay one hundred and twenty pound
yearly to his parent at Fulham. it be the chief support of the old
couple; for Mr. Sedley's speculation in life subsequent to his
bankruptcy do not by any mean retrieve the break old gentleman's
fortune. he try to be a wine-merchant, a coal-merchant, a commission
lottery agent, &c., &c. he send round prospectus to his friend
whenever he take a new trade, and order a new brass plate for the
door, and talk pompously about make his fortune still. but Fortune
never come back to the feeble and stricken old man. one by one his
friend drop off, and be weary of buy dear coal and bad wine
from he; and there be only his wife in all the world who fancy,
when he totter off to the City of a morning, that he be still do
any business there. at evening he crawl slowly back; and he use to
go of night to a little club at a tavern, where he dispose of the
finance of the nation. it be wonderful to hear he talk about
million, and agio, and discount, and what Rothschild be do, and
Baring Brothers. he talk of such vast sum that the gentleman of the
club (the apothecary, the undertaker, the great carpenter and builder,
the parish clerk, who be allow to come stealthily, and Mr. Clapp,
our old acquaintance,) respect the old gentleman. "I be well off
once, sir," he do not fail to tell everybody who "use the room." "my
son, sir, be at this minute chief magistrate of Ramgunge in the
Presidency of Bengal, and touch his four thousand rupee per mensem.
my daughter might be a Colonel's lady if she like. I might draw upon
my son, the first magistrate, sir, for two thousand pound to-morrow,
and Alexander would cash my bill, down sir, down on the counter, sir.
but the Sedleys be always a proud family." you and I, my dear reader,
may drop into this condition one day: for have not many of our friend
attain it? our luck may fail: our power forsake we: our place on
the board be take by well and young mime--the chance of life
roll away and leave we shatter and strand. then man will walk
across the road when they meet you--or, bad still, hold you out a
couple of finger and patronize you in a pitying way--then you will
know, as soon as your back be turn, that your friend begin with a
"poor devil, what imprudence he have commit, what chance that chap
have throw away!" well, well--a carriage and three thousand a year be
not the summit of the reward nor the end of God's judgment of man. if
quack prosper as often as they go to the wall--if zany succeed and
knave arrive at fortune, and, vice versa, share ill luck and
prosperity for all the world like the ablest and most honest amongst
we--I say, brother, the gift and pleasure of Vanity Fair cannot be
hold of any great account, and that it be probable . . . but we be
wander out of the domain of the story.
have Mrs. Sedley be a woman of energy, she would have exert it after
her husband's ruin and, occupy a large house, would have take in
boarder. the broken Sedley would have act well as the
boarding-house landlady's husband; the Munoz of private life; the
titular lord and master: the carver, house-steward, and humble husband
of the occupier of the dingy throne. I have see man of good brain
and breeding, and of good hope and vigour once, who feast squire
and keep hunter in their youth, meekly cut up leg of mutton for
rancorous old harridan and pretend to preside over their dreary
table--but Mrs. Sedley, we say, have not spirit enough to bustle about
for "a few select inmate to join a cheerful musical family," such as
one read of in the Times. she be content to lie on the shore where
fortune have strand she--and you could see that the career of this old
couple be over.
I donot think they be unhappy. perhaps they be a little prouder in
their downfall than in their prosperity. Mrs. Sedley be always a great
person for her landlady, Mrs. Clapp, when she descend and pass many
hour with she in the basement or ornament kitchen. the irish maid
Betty Flanagan's bonnet and ribbon, her sauciness, her idleness, her
reckless prodigality of kitchen candle, her consumption of tea and
sugar, and so forth occupy and amuse the old lady almost as much as
the doing of her former household, when she have Sambo and the
coachman, and a groom, and a footboy, and a housekeeper with a regiment
of female domestic--her former household, about which the good lady
talk a hundred time a day. and besides Betty Flanagan, Mrs. Sedley
have all the maid-of-all-work in the street to superintend. she know
how each tenant of the cottage pay or owe his little rent. she
step aside when Mrs. Rougemont the actress pass with her dubious
family. she fling up her head when Mrs. Pestler, the apothecary's
lady, drive by in her husband's professional one-horse chaise. she have
colloquy with the greengrocer about the pennorth of turnip which Mr.
Sedley love; she keep an eye upon the milkman and the baker's boy; and
make visitation to the butcher, who sell hundred of oxen very likely
with less ado than be make about Mrs. Sedley's loin of mutton: and
she count the potato under the joint on Sundays, on which day,
dress in her good, she go to church twice and read Blair's sermon
in the evening.
on that day, for "business" prevent he on weekday from take such
a pleasure, it be old Sedley's delight to take out his little grandson
Georgy to the neighbouring park or Kensington Gardens, to see the
soldier or to feed the duck. georgy love the redcoat, and his
grandpapa tell he how his father have be a famous soldier, and
introduce he to many sergeant and other with Waterloo medal on
their breast, to whom the old grandfather pompously present the
child as the son of Captain Osborne of the --th, who die gloriously on
the glorious eighteenth. he have be know to treat some of these
non-commission gentleman to a glass of porter, and, indeed, in their
first Sunday walk be disposed to spoil little georgy, sadly gorge
the boy with apple and parliament, to the detriment of his
health--until Amelia declare that George should never go out with his
grandpapa unless the latter promise solemnly, and on his honour, not
to give the child any cake, lollipop, or stall produce whatever.
between Mrs. Sedley and her daughter there be a sort of coolness about
this boy, and a secret jealousy--for one evening in George's very early
day, Amelia, who have be seat at work in their little parlour
scarcely remark that the old lady have quit the room, run upstairs
instinctively to the nursery at the cry of the child, who have be
asleep until that moment--and there find Mrs. Sedley in the act of
surreptitiously administer Daffy's Elixir to the infant. Amelia,
the gentle and sweetest of everyday mortal, when she find this
meddling with her maternal authority, thrill and tremble all over
with anger. her cheek, ordinarily pale, now flush up, until they
be as red as they use to be when she be a child of twelve year
old. she seize the baby out of her mother's arm and then grasp at
the bottle, leave the old lady gap at she, furious, and hold
the guilty tea-spoon.
Amelia fling the bottle crash into the fire-place. "I will not have
baby poison, Mamma," cry Emmy, rock the infant about violently
with both her arm round he and turn with flash eye at her
mother.
"Poisoned, Amelia!" say the old lady; "this language to I?"
"he shall not have any medicine but that which Mr. Pestler send for hi
n. he tell I that Daffy's Elixir be poison."
"very good: you think Ibe a murderess then," reply Mrs. Sedley.
"this be the language you use to your mother. I have meet with
misfortune: I have sink low in life: I have keep my carriage, and
now walk on foot: but I do not know I be a murderess before, and
thank you for the NEWS."
"Mamma," say the poor girl, who be always ready for tears--"you
shouldnot be hard upon I. I--I donot mean--I mean, I do not wish to
say you would to any wrong to this dear child, only--"
"oh, no, my love,--only that I be a murderess; in which case I have
well go to the Old Bailey. though I donot poison you, when you be
a child, but give you the good of education and the most expensive
master money could procure. yes; Ihave nurse five child and bury
three; and the one I love the good of all, and tend through croup,
and teethe, and measle, and hooping-cough, and bring up with
foreign master, regardless of expense, and with accomplishment at
Minerva House--which I never have when I be a girl--when I be too glad
to honour my father and mother, that I might live long in the land, and
to be useful, and not to mope all day in my room and act the fine
lady--say Ibe a murderess. ah, Mrs. Osborne! may you never nourish a
viper in your bosom, thatbe MY prayer."
"Mamma, Mamma!" cry the bewildered girl; and the child in her arm
set up a frantic chorus of shout. "a murderess, indeed! go down on
your knee and pray to God to cleanse your wicked ungrateful heart,
Amelia, and may he forgive you as I do." and Mrs. Sedley toss out of
the room, hiss out the word poison once more, and so end her
charitable benediction.
till the termination of her natural life, this breach between Mrs.
Sedley and her daughter be never thoroughly mend. the quarrel give
the eld lady numberless advantage which she do not fail to turn to
account with female ingenuity and perseverance. for instance, she
scarcely speak to Amelia for many week afterwards. she warn the
domestic not to touch the child, as Mrs. Osborne might be offend.
she ask her daughter to see and satisfy herself that there be no
poison prepare in the little daily mess that be concoct for
Georgy. when neighbour ask after the boy's health, she refer they
pointedly to Mrs. Osborne. she never venture to ask whether the baby
be well or not. she would not touch the child although he be her
grandson, and own precious darling, for she be not use to child,
and might kill it. and whenever Mr. Pestler come upon his healing
inquisition, she receive the doctor with such a sarcastic and scornful
demeanour, as make the surgeon declare that not Lady Thistlewood
herself, whom he have the honour of attend professionally, could give
herself great air than old Mrs. Sedley, from whom he never take a
fee. and very likely Emmy be jealous too, upon her own part, as what
mother be not, of those who would manage her child for she, or
become candidate for the first place in their affection. it be
certain that when anybody nurse the child, she be uneasy, and that
she would no more allow Mrs. Clapp or the domestic to dress or tend he
than she would have let they wash her husband's miniature which hang up
over her little bed--the same little bed from which the poor girl have
go to his; and to which she retire now for many long, silent,
tearful, but happy year.
in this room be all Amelia's heart and treasure. here it be that she
tend her boy and watch he through the many ill of childhood, with
a constant passion of love. the elder George return in he somehow,
only improve, and as if come back from heaven. in a hundred little
tone, look, and movement, the child be so like his father that the
widow's heart thrill as she hold he to it; and he would often ask
the cause of her tear. it be because of his likeness to his father,
she do not scruple to tell he. she talk constantly to he about
this dead father, and speak of her love for George to the innocent and
wonder child; much more than she ever have do to George himself, or
to any confidante of her youth. to her parent she never talk about
this matter, shrink from bar her heart to they. little George
very likely could understand no well than they, but into his ear she
pour her sentimental secret unreservedly, and into his only. the
very joy of this woman be a sort of grief, or so tender, at least,
that its expression be tear. her sensibility be so weak and
tremulous that perhaps they ought not to be talk about in a book. I
be tell by Dr. Pestler (now a most flourishing lady's physician, with
a sumptuous dark green carriage, a prospect of speedy knighthood, and a
house in Manchester Square) that her grief at wean the child be a
sight that would have unmanned a Herod. he be very soft-hearted many
year ago, and his wife be mortally jealous of Mrs. Amelia, then and
long afterwards.
perhaps the doctor's lady have good reason for her jealousy: most woman
share it, of those who form the small circle of Amelia's
acquaintance, and be quite angry at the enthusiasm with which the
other sex regard she. for almost all man who come near her love
she; though no doubt they would be at a loss to tell you why. she be
not brilliant, nor witty, nor wise over much, nor extraordinarily
handsome. but wherever she go she touch and charm every one of
the male sex, as invariably as she awaken the scorn and incredulity
of her own sisterhood. I think it be her weakness which be her
principal charm--a kind of sweet submission and softness, which seem
to appeal to each man she meet for his sympathy and protection. we have
see how in the regiment, though she speak but to few of George's
comrade there, all the sword of the young fellow at the mess-table
would have leapt from their scabbard to fight round she; and so it be
in the little narrow lodging-house and circle at Fulham, she interest
and please everybody. if she have be Mrs. Mango herself, of the
great house of Mango, Plantain, and Co., Crutched Friars, and the
magnificent proprietress of the Pineries, Fulham, who give summer
dejeuner frequent by Dukes and Earls, and drive about the parish
with magnificent yellow livery and bay horse, such as the royal
stable at Kensington themselves could not turn out--I say have she be
Mrs. Mango herself, or her son's wife, Lady Mary Mango (daughter of the
Earl of Castlemouldy, who condescend to marry the head of the firm),
the tradesman of the neighbourhood could not pay she more honour than
they invariably show to the gentle young widow, when she pass by
their door, or make her humble purchase at their shop.
thus it be not only Mr. Pestler, the medical man, but Mr. Linton the
young assistant, who doctor the servant maid and small tradesman,
and might be see any day read the Times in the surgery, who openly
declare himself the slave of Mrs. Osborne. he be a personable young
gentleman, more welcome at Mrs. Sedley's lodging than his principal;
and if anything go wrong with georgy, he would drop in twice or
thrice in the day to see the little chap, and without so much as the
thought of a fee. he would abstract lozenge, tamarind, and other
produce from the surgery-drawer for little Georgy's benefit, and
compound draught and mixture for he of miraculous sweetness, so
that it be quite a pleasure to the child to be ail. he and
Pestler, his chief, sit up two whole night by the boy in that
momentous and awful week when georgy have the measle; and when you
would have think, from the mother's terror, that there have never be
measle in the world before. would they have do as much for other
people? do they sit up for the folk at the Pineries, when Ralph
Plantagenet, and Gwendoline, and Guinever Mango have the same juvenile
complaint? do they sit up for little Mary Clapp, the landlord's
daughter, who actually catch the disease of little georgy? truth
compel one to say, no. they sleep quite undisturbed, at least as far
as she be concern--pronounce her to be a slight case, which would
almost cure itself, send she in a draught or two, and throw in bark
when the child rally, with perfect indifference, and just for form's
sake.
again, there be the little french chevalier opposite, who give lesson
in his native tongue at various school in the neighbourhood, and who
might be hear in his apartment of night play tremulous old
gavotte and minuet on a wheezy old fiddle. whenever this powdered and
courteous old man, who never miss a Sunday at the convent chapel at
Hammersmith, and who be in all respect, thought, conduct, and
bear utterly unlike the bearded savage of his nation, who curse
perfidious Albion, and scowl at you from over their cigar, in the
Quadrant arcade at the present day--whenever the old Chevalier de
Talonrouge speak of Mistress Osborne, he would first finish his pinch
of snuff, flick away the remain particle of dust with a graceful
wave of his hand, gather up his finger again into a bunch, and,
bring they up to his mouth, blow they open with a kiss, exclaim,
ah! la divine creature! he vow and protest that when Amelia walk
in the Brompton Lanes flower grow in profusion under her foot. he
call little Georgy Cupid, and ask he news of Venus, his mamma; and
tell the astonish Betty Flanagan that she be one of the Graces, and
the favourite attendant of the Reine des Amours.
instance might be multiply of this easily gain and unconscious
popularity. do not Mr. Binny, the mild and genteel curate of the
district chapel, which the family attend, call assiduously upon the
widow, dandle the little boy on his knee, and offer to teach he Latin,
to the anger of the elderly virgin, his sister, who keep house for he?
"there be nothing in she, Beilby," the latter lady would say. "when
she come to tea here she do not speak a word during the whole
evening. she be but a poor lackadaisical creature, and it be my belief
have no heart at all. it be only her pretty face which all you
gentleman admire so. Miss Grits, who have five thousand pound, and
expectation besides, have twice as much character, and be a thousand
time more agreeable to my taste; and if she be good-look I know
that you would think her perfection."
very likely Miss Binny be right to a great extent. it be the pretty
face which create sympathy in the heart of man, those wicked rogue.
a woman may possess the wisdom and chastity of Minerva, and we give no
heed to she, if she have a plain face. what folly will not a pair of
bright eye make pardonable? what dulness may not red lip and sweet
accent render pleasant? and so, with their usual sense of justice,
lady argue that because a woman be handsome, therefore she be a fool.
o lady, lady! there be some of you who be neither handsome nor
wise.
these be but trivial incident to recount in the life of our heroine.
her tale do not deal in wonder, as the gentle reader have already no
doubt perceive; and if a journal have be keep of her proceeding
during the seven year after the birth of her son, there would be find
few incident more remarkable in it than that of the measle, record
in the foregoing page. yes, one day, and greatly to her wonder, the
Reverend Mr. Binny, just mention, ask she to change her name of
Osborne for his own; when, with deep blush and tear in her eye and
voice, she thank he for his regard for she, express gratitude for
his attention to she and to her poor little boy, but say that she
never, never could think of any but--but the husband whom she have lose.
on the twenty-fifth of April, and the eighteenth of June, the day of
marriage and widowhood, she keep her room entirely, consecrate they
(and we do not know how many hour of solitary night-thought, her
little boy sleep in his crib by her bedside) to the memory of that
depart friend. during the day she be more active. she have to teach
George to read and to write and a little to draw. she read book, in
order that she might tell he story from they. as his eye open
and his mind expand under the influence of the outward nature round
about he, she teach the child, to the good of her humble power, to
acknowledge the Maker of all, and every night and every morning he and
she--(in that awful and touching communion which I think must bring a
thrill to the heart of every man who witness or who remember
it)--the mother and the little boy--pray to our Father together, the
mother plead with all her gentle heart, the child lisping after she
as she speak. and each time they pray to God to bless dear Papa, as
if he be alive and in the room with they. to wash and dress this
young gentleman--to take he for a run of the morning, before
breakfast, and the retreat of grandpapa for "business"--to make for he
the most wonderful and ingenious dress, for which end the thrifty
widow cut up and alter every available little bit of finery which she
possess out of her wardrobe during her marriage--for Mrs. Osborne
herself (greatly to her mother's vexation, who prefer fine clothe,
especially since her misfortune) always wear a black gown and a straw
bonnet with a black ribbon--occupy she many hour of the day. other
she have to spare, at the service of her mother and her old father. she
have take the pain to learn, and use to play cribbage with this
gentleman on the night when he do not go to his club. she sing for
he when he be so minded, and it be a good sign, for he invariably
fall into a comfortable sleep during the music. she write out his
numerous memorial, letter, prospectus, and project. it be in her
handwriting that most of the old gentleman's former acquaintance be
inform that he have become an agent for the Black Diamond and
Anti-cinder Coal Company and could supply his friend and the public
with the good coal at --s. per chaldron. all he do be to sign the
circular with his flourish and signature, and direct they in a shaky,
clerklike hand. one of these paper be send to Major Dobbin,--Regt.,
care of Messrs. Cox and Greenwood; but the Major be in Madras at
the time, have no particular call for coal. he know, though, the hand
which have write the prospectus. good God! what would he not have
give to hold it in his own! a second prospectus come out, inform
the Major that J. Sedley and Company, have establish agency at
Oporto, Bordeaux, and St. Mary's, be enable to offer to their
friend and the public generally the fine and most celebrated growth
of port, sherry, and claret wine at reasonable price and under
extraordinary advantage. act upon this hint, Dobbin furiously
canvass the governor, the commander-in-chief, the judge, the
regiment, and everybody whom he know in the Presidency, and send home
to Sedley and Co. order for wine which perfectly astonish Mr.
Sedley and Mr. Clapp, who be the Co. in the business. but no more
order come after that first burst of good fortune, on which poor old
Sedley be about to build a house in the City, a regiment of clerk, a
dock to himself, and correspondent all over the world. the old
gentleman's former taste in wine have go: the curse of the mess-room
assail major Dobbin for the vile drink he have be the mean of
introduce there; and he buy back a great quantity of the wine and
sell it at public outcry, at an enormous loss to himself. as for Jos,
who be by this time promote to a seat at the Revenue Board at
Calcutta, he be wild with rage when the post bring he out a bundle
of these bacchanalian prospectus, with a private note from his
father, tell Jos that his senior count upon he in this
enterprise, and have consign a quantity of select wine to he, as per
invoice, draw bill upon he for the amount of the same. Jos, who
would no more have it suppose that his father, Jos Sedley's father, of
the Board of Revenue, be a wine merchant ask for order, than that
he be Jack Ketch, refuse the bill with scorn, write back
contumeliously to the old gentleman, bid he to mind his own
affair; and the protest paper come back, Sedley and Co. have to
take it up, with the profit which they have make out of the Madras
venture, and with a little portion of Emmy's saving.
besides her pension of fifty pound a year, there have be five hundred
pound, as her husband's executor state, leave in the agent's hand at
the time of Osborne's demise, which sum, as George's guardian, Dobbin
propose to put out at 8 per cent in an indian house of agency. Mr.
Sedley, who think the Major have some roguish intention of his own
about the money, be strongly against this plan; and he go to the
agent to protest personally against the employment of the money in
question, when he learn, to his surprise, that there have be no such
sum in their hand, that all the late Captain's asset do not amount
to a hundred pound, and that the five hundred pound in question must
be a separate sum, of which Major Dobbin know the particular. More
than ever convince that there be some roguery, old Sedley pursue the
Major. as his daughter's near friend, he demand with a high hand
a statement of the late Captain's account. Dobbin's stammering,
blushing, and awkwardness add to the other's conviction that he have
a rogue to deal with, and in a majestic tone he tell that officer a
piece of his mind, as he call it, simply state his belief that the
Major be unlawfully detain his late son-in-law's money.
Dobbin at this lose all patience, and if his accuser have not be so
old and so broken, a quarrel might have ensue between they at the
Slaughters' Coffee-house, in a box of which place of entertainment the
gentleman have their colloquy. "come upstairs, sir," lisp out the
Major. "I insist on your come up the stair, and I will show which
be the injure party, poor George or I"; and, drag the old
gentleman up to his bedroom, he produce from his desk Osborne's
account, and a bundle of IOU's which the latter have give, who, to do
him justice, be always ready to give an iou. "he pay his bill in
England," Dobbin add, "but he have not a hundred pound in the world
when he fall. I and one or two of his brother officer make up the
little sum, which be all that we could spare, and you dare tell we
that we be try to cheat the widow and the orphan." Sedley be very
contrite and humble, though the fact be that William Dobbin have tell a
great falsehood to the old gentleman; have himself give every
shilling of the money, have bury his friend, and pay all the fee
and charge incident upon the calamity and removal of poor Amelia.
about these expense old Osborne have never give himself any trouble to
think, nor any other relative of Amelia, nor Amelia herself, indeed.
she trust to major Dobbin as an accountant, take his somewhat
confused calculation for grant, and never once suspect how much
she be in his debt.
twice or thrice in the year, accord to her promise, she write he
letter to Madras, letter all about little georgy. how he treasure
these paper! whenever Amelia write he answer, and not until then.
but he send over endless remembrance of himself to his godson and to
she. he order and send a box of scarfs and a grand ivory set of
chess-man from China. the pawn be little green and white man, with
real sword and shield; the knight be on horseback, the castle
be on the back of elephant. "Mrs. Mango's own set at the Pineries
be not so fine," Mr. Pestler remark. these chess-man be the
delight of Georgy's life, who print his first letter in
acknowledgement of this gift of his godpapa. he send over preserve
and pickle, which latter the young gentleman try surreptitiously in
the sideboard and half-kill himself with eating. he think it be a
judgement upon he for steal, they be so hot. Emmy write a
comical little account of this mishap to the Major: it please he to
think that her spirit be rally and that she could be merry
sometimes now. he send over a pair of shawl, a white one for she and
a black one with palm-leaf for her mother, and a pair of red scarf,
as winter wrapper, for old Mr. Sedley and George. the shawl be
worth fifty guineas apiece at the very least, as Mrs. Sedley know. she
wear hers in state at church at Brompton, and be congratulate by her
female friend upon the splendid acquisition. Emmy's, too, become
prettily her modest black gown. "what a pity it be she willnot think of
he!" Mrs. Sedley remark to Mrs. Clapp and to all her friend of
Brompton. "Jos never send we such present, I be sure, and grudge we
everything. it be evident that the Major be over head and ear in love
with she; and yet, whenever I so much as hint it, she turn red and
begin to cry and go and sit upstairs with her miniature. Ibe sick
of that miniature. I wish we have never see those odious purse-proud
Osbornes."
amidst such humble scene and associate George's early youth be
pass, and the boy grow up delicate, sensitive, imperious,
woman-breed--domineer the gentle mother whom he love with passionate
affection. he rule all the rest of the little world round about he.
as he grow, the elder be amazed at his haughty manner and his
constant likeness to his father. he ask question about everything,
as inquire youth will do. the profundity of his remark and
interrogatory astonish his old grandfather, who perfectly bore the
club at the tavern with story about the little lad's learning and
genius. he suffer his grandmother with a good-humour
indifference. the small circle round about he believe that the equal
of the boy do not exist upon the earth. georgy inherit his father's
pride, and perhaps think they be not wrong.
when he grow to be about six year old, Dobbin begin to write to he
very much. the Major want to hear that georgy be go to a school
and hop he would acquit himself with credit there: or would he have
a good tutor at home? it be time that he should begin to learn; and
his godfather and guardian hint that he hop to be allow to defray
the charge of the boy's education, which would fall heavily upon his
mother's straiten income. the Major, in a word, be always think
about Amelia and her little boy, and by order to his agent keep the
latter provide with picture-book, paint-box, desk, and all
conceivable implement of amusement and instruction. three day before
George's sixth birthday a gentleman in a gig, accompany by a servant,
drive up to Mr. Sedley's house and ask to see Master George Osborne:
it be Mr. Woolsey, military tailor, of Conduit Street, who come at the
Major's order to measure the young gentleman for a suit of clothe. he
have have the honour of make for the Captain, the young gentleman's
father. sometimes, too, and by the Major's desire no doubt, his
sister, the Misses Dobbin, would call in the family carriage to take
Amelia and the little boy to drive if they be so inclined. the
patronage and kindness of these lady be very uncomfortable to
Amelia, but she bear it meekly enough, for her nature be to yield;
and, besides, the carriage and its splendour give little georgy
immense pleasure. the lady beg occasionally that the child might
pass a day with they, and he be always glad to go to that fine
garden-house at Denmark Hill, where they live, and where there be
such fine grape in the hot-house and peach on the wall.
one day they kindly come over to Amelia with news which they be sure
would delight she--something very interesting about their dear William.
"what be it: be he come home?" she ask with pleasure beam in
her eye.
"oh, no--not the least--but they have very good reason to believe that
dear William be about to be marry--and to a relation of a very dear
friend of Amelia's--to Miss Glorvina O'Dowd, Sir Michael O'Dowd's
sister, who have go out to join Lady O'Dowd at Madras--a very
beautiful and accomplished girl, everybody say."
Amelia say "oh!" Amelia be very very happy indeed. but she suppose
Glorvina could not be like her old acquaintance, who be most
kind--but--but she be very happy indeed. and by some impulse of which
I cannot explain the meaning, she take George in her arm and kiss
he with an extraordinary tenderness. her eye be quite moist when
she put the child down; and she scarcely speak a word during the whole
of the drive--though she be so very happy indeed.
CHAPTER XXXIX
a Cynical chapter
our duty now take we back for a brief space to some old Hampshire
acquaintance of our, whose hope respect the disposal of their
rich kinswoman's property be so woefully disappointed. after
count upon thirty thousand pound from his sister, it be a heavy
blow to Bute Crawley to receive but five; out of which sum, when he
have pay his own debt and those of Jim, his son at college, a very
small fragment remain to portion off his four plain daughter. Mrs.
Bute never know, or at least never acknowledge, how far her own
tyrannous behaviour have tend to ruin her husband. all that woman
could do, she vow and protest she have do. be it her fault if
she do not possess those sycophantic art which her hypocritical
nephew, Pitt Crawley, practise? she wish he all the happiness which
he merit out of his ill-get gain. "at least the money will
remain in the family," she say charitably. "Pitt will never spend it,
my dear, that be quite certain; for a great miser do not exist in
England, and he be as odious, though in a different way, as his
spendthrift brother, the abandon Rawdon."
so Mrs. Bute, after the first shock of rage and disappointment, begin
to accommodate herself as good she could to her altered fortune and to
save and retrench with all her might. she instruct her daughter how
to bear poverty cheerfully, and invent a thousand notable method to
conceal or evade it. she take they about to ball and public place in
the neighbourhood, with praiseworthy energy; nay, she entertain she
friend in a hospitable comfortable manner at the Rectory, and much
more frequently than before dear Miss Crawley's legacy have fall in.
from her outward bearing nobody would have suppose that the family have
be disappoint in their expectation, or have guess from her
frequent appearance in public how she pinch and starve at home. her
girl have more milliner' furniture than they have ever enjoy before.
they appear perseveringly at the Winchester and Southampton
assembly; they penetrate to Cowes for the race-ball and
regatta-gaiety there; and their carriage, with the horse take from
the plough, be at work perpetually, until it begin almost to be
believe that the four sister have have fortune leave they by their
aunt, whose name the family never mention in public but with the most
tender gratitude and regard. I know no sort of lying which be more
frequent in Vanity Fair than this, and it may be remark how people
who practise it take credit to themselves for their hypocrisy, and
fancy that they be exceedingly virtuous and praiseworthy, because they
be able to deceive the world with regard to the extent of their mean.
Mrs. Bute certainly think herself one of the most virtuous woman in
England, and the sight of her happy family be an edify one to
stranger. they be so cheerful, so loving, so well-educate, so
simple! Martha paint flower exquisitely and furnish half the
charity bazaar in the county. Emma be a regular County Bulbul, and
her verse in the Hampshire Telegraph be the glory of its Poet's
Corner. Fanny and Matilda sing duet together, Mamma play the
piano, and the other two sister sit with their arm round each
other's waist and listen affectionately. nobody see the poor girl
drum at the duet in private. no one see Mamma drill they
rigidly hour after hour. in a word, Mrs. Bute put a good face against
fortune and keep up appearance in the most virtuous manner.
everything that a good and respectable mother could do Mrs. Bute do.
she get over yacht man from Southampton, parson from the Cathedral
close at Winchester, and officer from the barrack there. she try to
inveigle the young barrister at assize and encourage Jim to bring
home friend with whom he go out hunt with the H. H. what will
not a mother do for the benefit of her beloved one?
between such a woman and her brother-in-law, the odious Baronet at the
Hall, it be manifest that there could be very little in common. the
rupture between Bute and his brother Sir Pitt be complete; indeed,
between Sir Pitt and the whole county, to which the old man be a
scandal. his dislike for respectable society increase with age, and
the lodge-gate have not open to a gentleman's carriage-wheel since
Pitt and Lady Jane come to pay their visit of duty after their marriage.
that be an awful and unfortunate visit, never to be think of by the
