family without horror. Pitt beg his wife, with a ghastly
countenance, never to speak of it, and it be only through Mrs. Bute
herself, who still know everything which take place at the Hall, that
the circumstance of Sir Pitt's reception of his son and
daughter-in-law be ever know at all.
as they drive up the avenue of the park in their neat and
well-appoint carriage, Pitt remark with dismay and wrath great gap
among the tree--his tree--which the old Baronet be fell entirely
without license. the park wear an aspect of utter dreariness and ruin.
the drive be ill kept, and the neat carriage splash and flounder
in muddy pool along the road. the great sweep in front of the terrace
and entrance stair be black and cover with moss; the once trim
flower-bed rank and weedy. shutter be up along almost the whole
line of the house; the great hall-door be unbarred after much ringing
of the bell; an individual in ribbon be see flit up the black
oak stair, as horrock at length admit the heir of Queen's Crawley
and his bride into the hall of their father. he lead the way into Sir
Pitt's "Library," as it be call, the fume of tobacco grow
strong as Pitt and Lady Jane approach that apartment, "Sir Pitt
benot very well," horrock remark apologetically and hint that his
master be afflict with lumbago.
the library look out on the front walk and park. Sir Pitt have open
one of the window, and be bawl out thence to the postilion and
Pitt's servant, who seem to be about to take the baggage down.
"donot move none of they trunk," he cry, point with a pipe which
he hold in his hand. "itbe only a morning visit, Tucker, you fool.
Lor, what crack that off hoss have in his heel! ainot there no one at
the King's Head to rub them a little? how do, Pitt? how do, my dear?
come to see the old man, hay? 'Gad--you've a pretty face, too. you
benot like that old horse-godmother, your mother. come and give old
Pitt a kiss, like a good little gal."
the embrace disconcert the daughter-in-law somewhat, as the caress
of the old gentleman, unshorn and perfume with tobacco, might well do.
but she remember that her brother Southdown have mustachio, and
smoke cigar, and submit to the Baronet with a tolerable grace.
"Pitt have get vat," say the Baronet, after this mark of affection.
"do he read ee very long zermon, my dear? Hundredth Psalm, Evening
Hymn, hay Pitt? go and get a glass of Malmsey and a cake for my Lady
Jane, Horrocks, you great big booby, and donot stand steare there
like a fat pig. I willnot ask you to stop, my dear; youwill find it too
stoopid, and so should I too along a Pitt. Ibe an old man now, and
like my own way, and my pipe and backgammon of a night."
"I can play at backgammon, sir," say Lady Jane, laugh. "I use to
play with Papa and Miss Crawley, donot I, Mr. Crawley?"
"Lady Jane can play, sir, at the game to which you state that you be
so partial," Pitt say haughtily.
"but she wawn't stop for all that. Naw, naw, goo back to Mudbury and
give Mrs. Rincer a benefit; or drive down to the Rectory and ask Buty
for a dinner. hewill be charm to see you, you know; hebe so much
oblige to you for gettin' the old woman's money. ha, ha! some of it
will do to patch up the Hall when Ibe go."
"I perceive, sir," say Pitt with a heightened voice, "that your people
will cut down the timber."
"yee, yee, very fine weather, and seasonable for the time of year,"
Sir Pitt answer, who have suddenly grow deaf. "but Ibe gittin' old,
Pitt, now. Law bless you, you benot far from fifty yourself. but he
wear well, my pretty Lady Jane, donot he? itbe all godliness,
sobriety, and a moral life. look at I, Ibe not very fur from
fowr-score--he, he"; and he laugh, and take snuff, and leer at she
and pinch her hand.
Pitt once more bring the conversation back to the timber, but the
Baronet be deaf again in an instant.
"Ibe gittin' very old, and have be cruel bad this year with the
lumbago. I shallnot be here now for long; but Ibe glad ee've come,
daughter-in-law. I like your face, Lady Jane: itbe get none of the
damned high-boned Binkie look in it; and Iwill give ee something pretty,
my dear, to go to Court in." and he shuffle across the room to a
cupboard, from which he take a little old case contain jewel of
some value. "take that," say he, "my dear; it belong to my mother,
and afterwards to the first Lady Binkie. pretty pearl--never give them
the ironmonger's daughter. no, no. take them and put them up quick,"
say he, thrust the case into his daughter's hand, and clap the
door of the cabinet to, as horrock enter with a salver and
refreshment.
"what have you a be and give Pitt's wife?" say the individual in
ribbon, when Pitt and Lady Jane have take leave of the old gentleman.
it be Miss Horrocks, the butler's daughter--the cause of the scandal
throughout the county--the lady who reign now almost supreme at
Queen's Crawley.
the rise and progress of those Ribbons have be mark with dismay by
the county and family. the Ribbons open an account at the Mudbury
Branch Savings Bank; the Ribbons drive to church, monopolise the
pony-chaise, which be for the use of the servant at the Hall. the
domestic be dismiss at her pleasure. the Scotch gardener, who
still linger on the premise, take a pride in his wall and
hot-house, and indeed make a pretty good livelihood by the garden,
which he farm, and of which he sell the produce at Southampton, find
the Ribbons eat peach on a sunshiny morning at the south-wall, and
have his ear box when he remonstrate about this attack on his
property. he and his Scotch wife and his Scotch child, the only
respectable inhabitant of Queen's Crawley, be force to migrate,
with their good and their chattel, and leave the stately comfortable
garden to go to waste, and the flower-bed to run to seed. Poor Lady
Crawley's rose-garden become the dreary wilderness. only two or
three domestic shudder in the bleak old servant' hall. the stable
and office be vacant, and shut up, and half ruin. Sir Pitt live
in private, and booze nightly with horrock, his butler or house-steward
(as he now begin to be call), and the abandon ribbon. the
time be very much change since the period when she drive to Mudbury
in the spring-cart and call the small tradesman "Sir." it may have
be shame, or it may have be dislike of his neighbour, but the old
Cynic of Queen's Crawley hardly issue from his park-gate at all now.
he quarrel with his agent and screw his tenant by letter. his
day be pass in conduct his own correspondence; the lawyer and
farm-bailiff who have to do business with he could not reach he but
through the Ribbons, who receive they at the door of the housekeeper's
room, which command the back entrance by which they be admit;
and so the Baronet's daily perplexity increase, and his
embarrassment multiply round he.
the horror of Pitt Crawley may be imagine, as these report of his
father's dotage reach the most exemplary and correct of gentleman. he
tremble daily lest he should hear that the Ribbons be proclaim his
second legal mother-in-law. after that first and last visit, his
father's name be never mention in Pitt's polite and genteel
establishment. it be the skeleton in his house, and all the family
walk by it in terror and silence. the Countess Southdown keep on
dropping per coach at the lodge-gate the most exciting tract, tract
which ought to frighten the hair off your head. Mrs. Bute at the
parsonage nightly look out to see if the sky be red over the elm
behind which the Hall stand, and the mansion be on fire. Sir G.
Wapshot and Sir H. Fuddlestone, old friend of the house, wouldnot sit
on the bench with Sir Pitt at Quarter Sessions, and cut he dead in the
High Street of Southampton, where the reprobate stand offer his
dirty old hand to they. nothing have any effect upon he; he put his
hand into his pocket, and burst out laugh, as he scramble into
his carriage and four; he use to burst out laugh at Lady
Southdown's tract; and he laugh at his son, and at the world, and
at the Ribbons when she be angry, which be not seldom.
Miss Horrocks be instal as housekeeper at Queen's Crawley, and
rule all the domestic there with great majesty and rigour. all the
servant be instruct to address she as "Mum," or "madam"--and
there be one little maid, on her promotion, who persist in call
she "my Lady," without any rebuke on the part of the housekeeper.
"there have be well lady, and there have be worse, hester," be
Miss Horrocks' reply to this compliment of her inferior; so she rule,
have supreme power over all except her father, whom, however, she
treat with considerable haughtiness, warn he not to be too
familiar in his behaviour to one "as be to be a Baronet's lady."
indeed, she rehearse that exalt part in life with great satisfaction
to herself, and to the amusement of old Sir Pitt, who chuckle at her
air and grace, and would laugh by the hour together at her
assumption of dignity and imitation of genteel life. he swear it be
as good as a play to see she in the character of a fine dame, and he
make she put on one of the first Lady Crawley's court-dress, swear
(entirely to Miss Horrocks' own concurrence) that the dress become she
prodigiously, and threaten to drive she off that very instant to
Court in a coach-and-four. she have the ransacking of the wardrobe of
the two defunct lady, and cut and hack their posthumous finery so
as to suit her own taste and figure. and she would have like to take
possession of their jewel and trinket too; but the old Baronet have
lock they away in his private cabinet; nor could she coax or wheedle
he out of the key. and it be a fact, that some time after she leave
Queen's Crawley a copy-book belong to this lady be discover,
which show that she have take great pain in private to learn the art
of write in general, and especially of write her own name as Lady
Crawley, Lady Betsy Horrocks, Lady Elizabeth Crawley, &c.
though the good people of the Parsonage never go to the Hall and
shun the horrid old dotard its owner, yet they keep a strict
knowledge of all that happen there, and be look out every day
for the catastrophe for which Miss Horrocks be also eager. but Fate
intervene enviously and prevent she from receive the reward due to
such immaculate love and virtue.
one day the Baronet surprise "her ladyship," as he jocularly call
she, seat at that old and tuneless piano in the drawing-room, which
have scarcely be touch since Becky Sharp play quadrille upon
it--seat at the piano with the utmost gravity and squall to the
good of her power in imitation of the music which she have sometimes
hear. the little kitchen-maid on her promotion be stand at her
mistress's side, quite delighted during the operation, and wag she
head up and down and cry, "Lor, Mum, 'tis bittiful"--just like a
genteel sycophant in a real drawing-room.
this incident make the old Baronet roar with laughter, as usual. he
narrate the circumstance a dozen time to Horrocks in the course of
the evening, and greatly to the discomfiture of Miss Horrocks. he
thrum on the table as if it have be a musical instrument, and
squall in imitation of her manner of singing. he vow that such a
beautiful voice ought to be cultivate and declare she ought to have
singing-master, in which proposal she see nothing ridiculous. he be
in great spirit that night, and drink with his friend and butler an
extraordinary quantity of rum-and-water--at a very late hour the
faithful friend and domestic conduct his master to his bedroom.
half an hour afterwards there be a great hurry and bustle in the
house. light go about from window to window in the lonely desolate
old Hall, whereof but two or three room be ordinarily occupy by
its owner. presently, a boy on a pony go gallop off to Mudbury, to
the Doctor's house there. and in another hour (by which fact we
ascertain how carefully the excellent Mrs. Bute Crawley have always keep
up an understanding with the great house), that lady in her clog and
calash, the Reverend Bute Crawley, and James Crawley, her son, have
walk over from the Rectory through the park, and have enter the
mansion by the open hall-door.
they pass through the hall and the small oak parlour, on the table of
which stand the three tumbler and the empty rum-bottle which have
serve for Sir Pitt's carouse, and through that apartment into Sir
Pitt's study, where they find Miss Horrocks, of the guilty ribbon,
with a wild air, try at the press and escritoire with a bunch of
key. she drop they with a scream of terror, as little Mrs. Bute's
eye flash out at she from under her black calash.
"look at that, James and Mr. Crawley," cry Mrs. Bute, point at the
scared figure of the black-eye, guilty wench.
"he give them I; he give them I!" she cry.
"gave they you, you abandon creature!" scream Mrs. Bute. "bear
witness, Mr. Crawley, we find this good-for-nothing woman in the act
of steal your brother's property; and she will be hang, as I
always say she would."
Betsy Horrocks, quite daunt, fling herself down on her knee,
burst into tear. but those who know a really good woman be aware
that she be not in a hurry to forgive, and that the humiliation of an
enemy be a triumph to her soul.
"ring the bell, James," Mrs. Bute say. "go on ring it till the
people come." the three or four domestic resident in the deserted old
house come presently at that jangling and continued summon.
"put that woman in the strong-room," she say. "we catch she in the
act of rob Sir Pitt. Mr. Crawley, youwill make out her
committal--and, Beddoes, youwill drive she over in the spring cart, in
the morning, to Southampton Gaol."
"my dear," interpose the Magistrate and Rector--"she's only--"
"be there no handcuff?" Mrs. Bute continue, stamp in her clog.
"there use to be handcuff. wherebe the creature's abominable father?"
"he do give them I," still cry poor Betsy; "donot he, hester? you
see Sir Pitt--you know you do--give them I, ever so long ago--the day
after Mudbury fair: not that I want them. take them if you think they
benot mine." and here the unhappy wretch pull out from her pocket a
large pair of paste shoe-buckle which have excite her admiration, and
which she have just appropriate out of one of the bookcase in the
study, where they have lain.
"Law, Betsy, how could you go for to tell such a wicked story!" say
Hester, the little kitchen-maid late on her promotion--"and to Madame
Crawley, so good and kind, and his Rev'rince (with a curtsey), and you
may search all MY box, Mum, Ibe sure, and herebe my key as Ibe an
honest girl, though of pore parent and workhouse breed--and if you find
so much as a beggarly bit of lace or a silk stocking out of all the
gownd as YOU'VE have the picking of, may I never go to church agin."
"give up your key, you harden hussy," hiss out the virtuous little
lady in the calash.
"and herebe a candle, Mum, and if you please, Mum, I can show you her
room, Mum, and the press in the housekeeper's room, Mum, where she
keep heap and heap of thing, Mum," cry out the eager little
hester with a profusion of curtsey.
"hold your tongue, if you please. I know the room which the creature
occupie perfectly well. Mrs. Brown, have the goodness to come with
I, and beddoe donot you lose sight of that woman," say Mrs. Bute,
seize the candle. "Mr. Crawley, you have well go upstairs and see
that they be not murder your unfortunate brother"--and the calash,
escort by Mrs. Brown, walk away to the apartment which, as she say
truly, she know perfectly well.
Bute go upstairs and find the Doctor from Mudbury, with the
frightened Horrocks over his master in a chair. they be try to
bleed Sir Pitt Crawley.
with the early morning an express be send off to Mr. Pitt Crawley by
the Rector's lady, who assume the command of everything, and have
watch the old Baronet through the night. he have be bring back to
a sort of life; he could not speak, but seem to recognize people.
Mrs. Bute keep resolutely by his bedside. she never seem to want to
sleep, that little woman, and do not close her fiery black eye once,
though the Doctor snore in the arm-chair. horrock make some wild
effort to assert his authority and assist his master; but Mrs. Bute
call he a tipsy old wretch and bid he never show his face again in
that house, or he should be transport like his abominable daughter.
terrify by her manner, he slink down to the oak parlour where Mr.
James be, who, have try the bottle stand there and find no
liquor in it, order Mr. Horrocks to get another bottle of rum, which
he fetch, with clean glass, and to which the Rector and his son sit
down, order horrock to put down the key at that instant and never
to show his face again.
cow by this behaviour, horrock give up the key, and he and his
daughter slunk off silently through the night and give up possession of
the house of Queen's Crawley.
CHAPTER XL
in which Becky be recognize by the Family
the heir of Crawley arrive at home, in due time, after this
catastrophe, and henceforth may be say to have reign in Queen's
Crawley. for though the old Baronet survive many month, he never
recover the use of his intellect or his speech completely, and the
government of the estate devolve upon his eld son. in a strange
condition Pitt find it. Sir Pitt be always buy and mortgaging; he
have twenty man of business, and quarrel with each; quarrel with all
his tenant, and lawsuit with they; lawsuit with the lawyer;
lawsuit with the Mining and Dock Companies in which he be proprietor;
and with every person with whom he have business. to unravel these
difficulty and to set the estate clear be a task worthy of the
orderly and persevere diplomatist of Pumpernickel, and he set himself
to work with prodigious assiduity. his whole family, of course, be
transport to Queen's Crawley, whither Lady Southdown, of course, come
too; and she set about convert the parish under the Rector's nose,
and bring down her irregular clergy to the dismay of the angry Mrs
Bute. Sir Pitt have conclude no bargain for the sale of the living of
Queen's Crawley; when it should drop, her ladyship propose to take the
patronage into her own hand and present a young protege to the
Rectory, on which subject the diplomatic Pitt say nothing.
Mrs. Bute's intention with regard to Miss Betsy Horrocks be not
carry into effect, and she pay no visit to Southampton Gaol. she
and her father leave the Hall when the latter take possession of the
Crawley Arms in the village, of which he have get a lease from Sir Pitt.
the ex-butler have obtain a small freehold there likewise, which give
he a vote for the borough. the Rector have another of these vote, and
these and four other form the representative body which return the
two member for Queen's Crawley.
there be a show of courtesy keep up between the Rectory and the Hall
lady, between the young one at least, for Mrs. Bute and Lady
Southdown never could meet without battle, and gradually cease see
each other. her ladyship keep her room when the lady from the
Rectory visit their cousin at the Hall. perhaps Mr. Pitt be not
very much displeased at these occasional absence of his mamma-in-law.
he believe the Binkie family to be the great and wise and most
interesting in the world, and her ladyship and his aunt have long hold
ascendency over he; but sometimes he feel that she command he too
much. to be consider young be complimentary, doubtless, but at
six-and-forty to be treat as a boy be sometimes mortifying. Lady
Jane yield up everything, however, to her mother. she be only fond
of her child in private, and it be lucky for she that Lady
Southdown's multifarious business, her conference with minister, and
her correspondence with all the missionary of Africa, Asia, and
Australasia, &c., occupy the venerable Countess a great deal, so that
she have but little time to devote to her granddaughter, the little
Matilda, and her grandson, Master Pitt Crawley. the latter be a feeble
child, and it be only by prodigious quantity of calomel that Lady
Southdown be able to keep he in life at all.
as for Sir Pitt he retire into those very apartment where Lady
Crawley have be previously extinguish, and here be tend by Miss
Hester, the girl upon her promotion, with constant care and assiduity.
what love, what fidelity, what constancy be there equal to that of a
nurse with good wage? they smooth pillow; and make arrowroot; they
get up at night; they bear complaint and querulousness; they see the
sun shin out of door and donot want to go abroad; they sleep on
arm-chair and eat their meal in solitude; they pass long long
evening do nothing, watch the ember, and the patient's drink
simmer in the jug; they read the weekly paper the whole week
through; and Law's serious Call or the Whole Duty of Man suffice they
for literature for the year--and we quarrel with they because, when
their relation come to see they once a week, a little gin be smuggle
in in their linen basket. lady, what man's love be there that would
stand a year's nursing of the object of his affection? whereas a nurse
will stand by you for ten pound a quarter, and we think she too highly
pay. at least Mr. Crawley grumble a good deal about pay half as
much to Miss Hester for her constant attendance upon the Baronet his
father.
of sunshiny day this old gentleman be take out in a chair on the
terrace--the very chair which Miss Crawley have have at Brighton, and
which have be transport thence with a number of Lady Southdown's
effect to Queen's Crawley. Lady Jane always walk by the old man,
and be an evident favourite with he. he use to nod many time to
she and smile when she come in, and utter inarticulate deprecatory
moan when she be go away. when the door shut upon she he would
cry and sob--whereupon hester's face and manner, which be always
exceedingly bland and gentle while her lady be present, would change
at once, and she would make face at he and clench her fist and scream
out "hold your tongue, you stoopid old fool," and twirl away his chair
from the fire which he love to look at--at which he would cry more.
for this be all that be leave after more than seventy year of
cunning, and struggle, and drinking, and scheming, and sin and
selfishness--a whimpering old idiot put in and out of bed and clean
and feed like a baby.
at last a day come when the nurse's occupation be over. early one
morning, as Pitt Crawley be at his steward's and bailiff's book in
the study, a knock come to the door, and Hester present herself,
drop a curtsey, and say,
"if you please, Sir Pitt, Sir Pitt die this morning, Sir Pitt. I be
a-making of his toast, Sir Pitt, for his gruel, Sir Pitt, which he take
every morning regular at six, Sir Pitt, and--I think I hear a
moan-like, Sir Pitt--and--and--and--" she drop another curtsey.
what be it that make Pitt's pale face flush quite red? be it because
he be Sir Pitt at last, with a seat in Parliament, and perhaps future
honour in prospect? "Iwill clear the estate now with the ready money,"
he think and rapidly calculate its incumbrance and the improvement
which he would make. he would not use his aunt's money previously lest
Sir Pitt should recover and his outlay be in vain.
all the blind be pull down at the Hall and Rectory: the church
bell be toll, and the chancel hang in black; and Bute Crawley donot
go to a course meeting, but go and din quietly at Fuddleston,
where they talk about his deceased brother and young Sir Pitt over
their port. Miss Betsy, who be by this time marry to a saddler at
Mudbury, cry a good deal. the family surgeon ride over and pay his
respectful compliment, and inquiry for the health of their
ladyship. the death be talk about at Mudbury and at the Crawley
Arms, the landlord whereof have become reconcile with the Rector of
late, who be occasionally know to step into the parlour and taste Mr.
Horrocks' mild beer.
"shall I write to your brother--or will you?" ask Lady Jane of her
husband, Sir Pitt.
"I will write, of course," Sir Pitt say, "and invite he to the
funeral: it will be but become."
"and--and--Mrs. Rawdon," say Lady Jane timidly.
"Jane!" say Lady Southdown, "how can you think of such a thing?"
"Mrs. Rawdon must of course be ask," say Sir Pitt, resolutely.
"not whilst I be in the house!" say Lady Southdown.
"your ladyship will be pleased to recollect that I be the head of this
family," Sir Pitt reply. "if you please, Lady Jane, you will write a
letter to Mrs. Rawdon Crawley, request her presence upon this
melancholy occasion."
"jane, I forbid you to put pen to paper!" cry the Countess.
"I believe I be the head of this family," Sir Pitt repeat; "and
however much I may regret any circumstance which may lead to your
ladyship quit this house, must, if you please, continue to govern
it as I see fit."
Lady Southdown rise up as magnificent as Mrs. Siddons in Lady Macbeth
and order that horse might be put to her carriage. if her son and
daughter turn she out of their house, she would hide her sorrow
somewhere in loneliness and pray for their conversion to well
thought.
"we donot turn you out of our house, Mamma," say the timid Lady Jane
imploringly.
"you invite such company to it as no christian lady should meet, and I
will have my horse to-morrow morning."
"have the goodness to write, Jane, under my dictation," say Sir Pitt,
rise and throw himself into an attitude of command, like the
portrait of a Gentleman in the exhibition, "and begin. 'Queen's
Crawley, September 14, 1822.--my dear brother--'"
hear these decisive and terrible word, Lady Macbeth, who have be
wait for a sign of weakness or vacillation on the part of her
son-in-law, rise and, with a scared look, leave the library. Lady Jane
look up to her husband as if she would fain follow and soothe her
mamma, but Pitt forbid his wife to move.
"she willnot go away," he say. "she have let her house at Brighton and
have spend she last half-year's dividend. a Countess live at an inn
be a ruin woman. I have be wait long for an opportunity--to
take this--this decisive step, my love; for, as you must perceive, it
be impossible that there should be two chief in a family: and now, if
you please, we will resume the dictation. 'my dear brother, the
melancholy intelligence which it be my duty to convey to my family must
have be long anticipate by,'" &c.
in a word, Pitt have come to his kingdom, and have by good luck, or
desert rather, as he consider, assume almost all the fortune which
his other relative have expect, be determined to treat his family
kindly and respectably and make a house of Queen's Crawley once more.
it please he to think that he should be its chief. he propose to
use the vast influence that his command talent and position must
speedily acquire for he in the county to get his brother place and
his cousin decently provide for, and perhaps have a little sting of
repentance as he think that he be the proprietor of all that they
have hop for. in the course of three or four day' reign his bearing
be change and his plan quite fix: he determine to rule justly
and honestly, to depose Lady Southdown, and to be on the friendly
possible term with all the relation of his blood.
so he dictate a letter to his brother Rawdon--a solemn and elaborate
letter, contain the profound observation, couch in the long
word, and fill with wonder the simple little secretary, who write
under her husband's order. "what an orator this will be," think she,
"when he enter the House of Commons" (on which point, and on the
tyranny of Lady Southdown, Pitt have sometimes drop hint to his wife
in bed); "how wise and good, and what a genius my husband be! I
fancy he a little cold; but how good, and what a genius!"
the fact be, Pitt Crawley have get every word of the letter by heart and
have study it, with diplomatic secrecy, deeply and perfectly, long
before he think fit to communicate it to his astonished wife.
this letter, with a huge black border and seal, be accordingly
despatch by Sir Pitt Crawley to his brother the Colonel, in London.
Rawdon Crawley be but half-pleased at the receipt of it. "whatbe the
use of go down to that stupid place?" think he. "I cannot stand
be alone with Pitt after dinner, and horse there and back will cost
we twenty pound."
he carry the letter, as he do all difficulty, to Becky, upstairs
in her bedroom--with her chocolate, which he always make and take to
she of a morning.
he put the tray with the breakfast and the letter on the dressing-table,
before which Becky sit comb her yellow hair. she take up the
black-edge missive, and have read it, she jump up from the chair,
cry "Hurray!" and wave the note round her head.
"Hurray?" say Rawdon, wonder at the little figure caper about in
a stream flannel dressing-gown, with tawny lock dishevel. "hebe
not leave we anything, Becky. I have my share when I come of age."
"youwill never be of age, you silly old man," Becky reply. "run out
now to Madam Brunoy's, for I must have some mourning: and get a crape
on your hat, and a black waistcoat--I donot think youhave get one; order
it to be bring home to-morrow, so that we may be able to start on
Thursday."
"you donot mean to go?" Rawdon interpose.
"of course I mean to go. I mean that Lady Jane shall present I at
Court next year. I mean that your brother shall give you a seat in
Parliament, you stupid old creature. I mean that Lord Steyne shall
have your vote and his, my dear, old silly man; and that you shall be
an irish Secretary, or a West indian Governor: or a Treasurer, or a
Consul, or some such thing."
"posting will cost a dooce of a lot of money," grumble Rawdon.
"we might take Southdown's carriage, which ought to be present at the
funeral, as he be a relation of the family: but, no--I intend that we
shall go by the coach. theywill like it well. it seem more humble--"
"Rawdy go, of course?" the Colonel ask.
"no such thing; why pay an extra place? hebe too big to travel bodkin
between you and I. let he stay here in the nursery, and Briggs can
make he a black frock. go you, and do as I bid you. and you have well
tell Sparks, your man, that old Sir Pitt be dead and that you will come
in for something considerable when the affair be arrange. hewill
tell this to Raggles, who have be press for money, and it will
console poor Raggles." and so Becky begin sip her chocolate.
when the faithful Lord Steyne arrive in the evening, he find Becky
and her companion, who be no other than our friend Briggs, busy
cutting, rip, snipping, and tear all sort of black stuff
available for the melancholy occasion.
"Miss Briggs and I be plunge in grief and despondency for the death
of our papa," rebecca say. "Sir Pitt Crawley be dead, my lord. we
have be tear our hair all the morning, and now we be tear up
our old clothe."
"oh, Rebecca, how can you--" be all that Briggs could say as she
turn up her eye.
"oh, Rebecca, how can you--" echo my Lord. "so that old scoundrel's
dead, be he? he might have be a peer if he have play his card
well. Mr. Pitt have very nearly make he; but he rat always at the
wrong time. what an old Silenus it be!"
"I might have be Silenus's widow," say Rebecca. "donot you remember,
Miss Briggs, how you peep in at the door and see old Sir Pitt on his
knee to I?" Miss Briggs, our old friend, blush very much at this
reminiscence, and be glad when Lord Steyne order she to go
downstairs and make he a cup of tea.
Briggs be the house-dog whom Rebecca have provide as guardian of her
innocence and reputation. Miss Crawley have leave she a little annuity.
she would have be content to remain in the Crawley family with Lady
Jane, who be good to she and to everybody; but Lady southdown
dismiss poor Briggs as quickly as decency permit; and Mr. Pitt
(who think himself much injure by the uncalled-for generosity of his
deceased relative towards a lady who have only be Miss Crawley's
faithful retainer a score of year) make no objection to that exercise
of the dowager's authority. bowl and Firkin likewise receive their
legacy and their dismissal, and marry and set up a lodging-house,
accord to the custom of their kind.
Briggs try to live with her relation in the country, but find that
attempt be vain after the well society to which she have be
accustom. Briggs's friend, small tradesman, in a country town,
quarrel over Miss Briggs's forty pound a year as eagerly and more
openly than Miss Crawley's kinsfolk have for that lady's inheritance.
Briggs's brother, a radical hatter and grocer, call his sister a
purse-proud aristocrat, because she would not advance a part of her
capital to stock his shop; and she would have do so most likely, but
that their sister, a dissent shoemaker's lady, at variance with the
hatter and grocer, who go to another chapel, show how their brother
be on the verge of bankruptcy, and take possession of Briggs for a
while. the dissent shoemaker want Miss Briggs to send his son to
college and make a gentleman of he. between they the two family get
a great portion of her private saving out of she, and finally she flee
to London follow by the anathema of both, and determine to seek for
servitude again as infinitely less onerous than liberty. and
advertising in the paper that a "Gentlewoman of agreeable manner, and
accustom to the good society, be anxious to," &c., she take up her
residence with Mr. Bowls in Half Moon Street, and wait the result of
the advertisement.
so it be that she fall in with Rebecca. Mrs. Rawdon's dash little
carriage and pony be whirl down the street one day, just as Miss

in which Becky Revisits the Halls of her Ancestors

"it benot difficult to be a country gentleman's wife," rebecca think.
"I think I could be a good woman if I have five thousand a year. I
could dawdle about in the nursery and count the apricot on the wall.
I could water plant in a green-house and pick off dead leaf from the
geranium. I could ask old woman about their rheumatism and order
half-a-crown's worth of soup for the poor. I shouldnot miss it much,
out of five thousand a year. I could even drive out ten mile to dine
at a neighbour's, and dress in the fashion of the year before last. I
could go to church and keep awake in the great family pew, or go to
sleep behind the curtain, with my veil down, if I only have practice.
I could pay everybody, if I have but the money. this be what the
conjuror here pride themselves upon do. they look down with pity
upon we miserable sinner who have none. they think themselves
generous if they give our child a five-pound note, and we
contemptible if we be without one." and who know but Rebecca be
right in her speculation--and that it be only a question of money and
fortune which make the difference between she and an honest woman? if
you take temptation into account, who be to say that he be well than
his neighbour? a comfortable career of prosperity, if it do not make
people honest, at least keep they so. an alderman come from a
turtle feast will not step out of his carnage to steal a leg of mutton;
but put he to starve, and see if he will not purloin a loaf. Becky
console herself by so balance the chance and equalize the
distribution of good and evil in the world.
the old haunt, the old field and wood, the copse, pond, and

so Rebecca, during her stay at Queen's Crawley, make as many friend of

CHAPTER XLII
which Treats of the Osborne Family
considerable time have elapse since we have see our respectable
