have not improve his temper, and in more in stance than one he have
not be allow to have his own way. to be thwart in this
reasonable desire be always very injurious to the old gentleman; and
resistance become doubly exasperating when gout, age, loneliness, and
the force of many disappointment combine to weigh he down. his
stiff black hair begin to grow quite white soon after his son's death;
his-face grow redder; his hand tremble more and more as he pour out
his glass of port wine. he lead his clerk a dire life in the City:
his family at home be not much happy. I doubt if Rebecca, whom we
have see piously pray for Consols, would have exchange her poverty
and the dare-devil excitement and chance of her life for Osborne's
money and the humdrum gloom which envelop he. he have propose for
Miss Swartz, but have be reject scornfully by the partisan of that
lady, who marry she to a young sprig of Scotch nobility. he be a
man to have marry a woman out of low life and bully she dreadfully
afterwards; but no person present herself suitable to his taste, and,
instead, he tyrannize over his unmarried daughter, at home. she have a
fine carriage and fine horse and sit at the head of a table load
with the grand plate. she have a cheque-book, a prize footman to
follow she when she walk, unlimited credit, and bow and compliment
from all the tradesman, and all the appurtenance of an heiress; but
she spend a woeful time. the little charity-girl at the Foundling, the
sweeperess at the crossing, the poor under-kitchen-maid in the
servant' hall, be happy compare to that unfortunate and now
middle-aged young lady.
Frederick Bullock, Esq., of the house of Bullock, Hulker, and Bullock,
have marry Maria Osborne, not without a great deal of difficulty and
grumble on Mr. Bullock's part. George be dead and cut out of his
father's will, Frederick insist that the half of the old gentleman's
property should be settle upon his Maria, and indeed, for a long time,
refuse, "to come to the scratch" (it be Mr. Frederick's own
expression) on any other term. Osborne say Fred have agree to take
his daughter with twenty thousand, and he should bind himself to no
more. "Fred might take it, and welcome, or leave it, and go and be
hang." Fred, whose hope have be raise when George have be
disinherit, think himself infamously swindle by the old merchant,
and for some time make as if he would break off the match altogether.
Osborne withdraw his account from Bullock and Hulker's, go on 'Change
with a horsewhip which he swear he would lay across the back of a
certain scoundrel that should be nameless, and demean himself in his
usual violent manner. Jane Osborne condole with her sister Maria
during this family feud. "I always tell you, Maria, that it be your
money he love and not you," she say, soothingly.
"he select I and my money at any rate; he donot choose you and
your," reply Maria, toss up her head.
the rapture be, however, only temporary. Fred's father and senior
partner counsel he to take Maria, even with the twenty thousand
settle, half down, and half at the death of Mr. Osborne, with the
chance of the further division of the property. so he "knuckle
down," again to use his own phrase, and send old Hulker with peaceable
overture to Osborne. it be his father, he say, who would not hear
of the match, and have make the difficulty; he be most anxious to
keep the engagement. the excuse be sulkily accept by Mr. Osborne.
Hulker and Bullock be a high family of the City aristocracy, and
connect with the "nob" at the West End. it be something for the old
man to be able to say, "my son, sir, of the house of Hulker, Bullock,
and Co., sir; my daughter's cousin, Lady Mary Mango, sir, daughter of
the Right Hon. the Earl of Castlemouldy." in his imagination he see
his house people by the "nob." so he forgive young Bullock and
consent that the marriage should take place.
it be a grand affair--the bridegroom's relative give the breakfast,
their habitation be near St. George's, Hanover Square, where the
business take place. the "nob of the West End" be invite, and many
of they sign the book. Mr. Mango and Lady Mary Mango be there,
with the dear young Gwendoline and Guinever Mango as bridesmaid;
Colonel Bludyer of the Dragoon Guards (eld son of the house of
Bludyer Brothers, Mincing Lane), another cousin of the bridegroom, and
the Honourable Mrs. Bludyer; the honourable George Boulter, Lord
Levant's son, and his lady, Miss Mango that be; Lord Viscount
Castletoddy; Honourable James McMull and Mrs. McMull (formerly Miss
Swartz); and a host of fashionable, who have all marry into Lombard
Street and do a great deal to ennoble Cornhill.
the young couple have a house near Berkeley Square and a small villa at
Roehampton, among the banking colony there. Fred be consider to
have make rather a mesalliance by the lady of his family, whose
grandfather have be in a Charity School, and who be ally through
the husband with some of the good blood in England. and Maria be
bind, by superior pride and great care in the composition of she
visit-book, to make up for the defect of birth, and feel it her
duty to see her father and sister as little as possible.
that she should utterly break with the old man, who have still so many
score of thousand pound to give away, be absurd to suppose. Fred
Bullock would never allow she to do that. but she be still young and
incapable of hide her feeling; and by invite her papa and sister
to her third-rate party, and behave very coldly to they when they
come, and by avoid Russell Square, and indiscreetly beg her
father to quit that odious vulgar place, she do more harm than all
Frederick's diplomacy could repair, and perille her chance of her
inheritance like a giddy heedless creature as she be.
"so Russell Square be not good enough for Mrs. Maria, hay?" say the
old gentleman, rattle up the carriage window as he and his daughter
drive away one night from Mrs. Frederick Bullock's, after dinner. "so
she invite her father and sister to a second day's dinner (if those
side, or ontry, as she call them, benot serve yesterday, Ibe
d--d), and to meet City folk and littery man, and keep the Earls and
the Ladies, and the Honourables to herself. honourable? Damn
Honourables. I be a plain british merchant I be, and could buy the
beggarly hound over and over. lord, indeed!--why, at one of her
swarrey I see one of them speak to a dam fiddler--a fellar I despise.
and they willnot come to Russell Square, willnot they? why, Iwill lay my
life Ihave get a well glass of wine, and pay a well figure for it,
and can show a handsomer service of silver, and can lay a well dinner
on my mahogany, than ever they see on theirs--the cringing, sneaking,
stuck-up fool. drive on quick, James: I want to get back to Russell
Square--ha, ha!" and he sink back into the corner with a furious laugh.
with such reflection on his own superior merit, it be the custom of
the old gentleman not unfrequently to console himself.
Jane Osborne could not but concur in these opinion respect her
sister's conduct; and when Mrs. Frederick's first-bear, Frederick
Augustus Howard Stanley Devereux Bullock, be bear, old Osborne, who
be invite to the christening and to be godfather, content himself
with send the child a gold cup, with twenty guineas inside it for
the nurse. "thatbe more than any of your Lords will give, I'LL
warrant," he say and refuse to attend at the ceremony.
the splendour of the gift, however, cause great satisfaction to the
house of Bullock. Maria think that her father be very much pleased
with she, and Frederick augur the good for his little son and heir.
one can fancy the pang with which Miss Osborne in her solitude in
Russell Square read the Morning Post, where her sister's name occur
every now and then, in the article head "Fashionable Reunions," and
where she have an opportunity of read a description of Mrs. F.
Bullock's costume, when present at the drawing room by Lady Frederica
Bullock. Jane's own life, as we have say, admit of no such
grandeur. it be an awful existence. she have to get up of black
winter's morning to make breakfast for her scowl old father, who
would have turn the whole house out of door if his tea have not be
ready at half-past eight. she remain silent opposite to he,
listen to the urn hiss, and sit in tremor while the parent
read his paper and consume his accustomed portion of muffin and tea.
at half-past nine he rise and go to the City, and she be almost free
till dinner-time, to make visitation in the kitchen and to scold the
servant; to drive abroad and descend upon the tradesman, who be
prodigiously respectful; to leave her card and her papabe at the great
glum respectable house of their City friend; or to sit alone in the
large drawing-room, expect visitor; and work at a huge piece of
worst by the fire, on the sofa, hard by the great Iphigenia clock,
which tick and toll with mournful loudness in the dreary room. the
great glass over the mantelpiece, face by the other great console
glass at the opposite end of the room, increase and multiply between
they the brown Holland bag in which the chandelier hung, until you see
these brown Holland bag fade away in endless perspective, and this
apartment of Miss Osborne's seem the centre of a system of
drawing-room. when she remove the cordovan leather from the grand
piano and venture to play a few note on it, it sound with a
mournful sadness, startle the dismal echo of the house. George's
picture be go, and lay upstairs in a lumber-room in the garret; and
though there be a consciousness of he, and father and daughter often
instinctively know that they be think of he, no mention be ever
make of the brave and once darling son.
at five o'clock Mr. Osborne come back to his dinner, which he and his
daughter take in silence (seldom break, except when he swear and be
savage, if the cooking be not to his liking), or which they share
twice in a month with a party of dismal friend of Osborne's rank and
age. old Dr. Gulp and his lady from Bloomsbury Square; old Mr.
Frowser, the attorney, from Bedford Row, a very great man, and from his
business, hand-in-glove with the "nob at the West End"; old Colonel
Livermore, of the Bombay Army, and Mrs. Livermore, from Upper Bedford
Place; old Sergeant Toffy and Mrs. Toffy; and sometimes old Sir Thomas
Coffin and Lady Coffin, from Bedford Square. Sir Thomas be celebrate
as a hang judge, and the particular tawny port be produce when he
din with Mr. Osborne.
these people and their like give the pompous Russell Square merchant
pompous dinner back again. they have solemn rubber of whist, when
they go upstairs after drinking, and their carriage be call at
half past ten. many rich people, whom we poor devil be in the habit
of envy, lead contentedly an existence like that above describe.
Jane Osborne scarcely ever meet a man under sixty, and almost the only
bachelor who appear in their society be Mr. Smirk, the celebrated
lady' doctor.
I cannot say that nothing have occur to disturb the monotony of this
awful existence: the fact be, there have be a secret in poor Jane's
life which have make her father more savage and morose than even nature,
pride, and over-feeding have make he. this secret be connect with
Miss Wirt, who have a cousin an artist, Mr. Smee, very celebrated since
as a portrait-painter and R.A., but who once be glad enough to give
draw lesson to lady of fashion. Mr. Smee have forget where
Russell Square be now, but he be glad enough to visit it in the year
1818, when Miss Osborne have instruction from he.
Smee (formerly a pupil of Sharpe of Frith Street, a dissolute,
irregular, and unsuccessful man, but a man with great knowledge of his
art) be the cousin of Miss Wirt, we say, and introduce by she to
Miss Osborne, whose hand and heart be still free after various
incomplete love affair, feel a great attachment for this lady, and it
be believe inspire one in her bosom. Miss Wirt be the confidante of
this intrigue. I know not whether she use to leave the room where the
master and his pupil be paint, in order to give they an
opportunity for exchange those vow and sentiment which cannot be
utter advantageously in the presence of a third party; I know not
whether she hop that should her cousin succeed in carry off the
rich merchant's daughter, he would give Miss Wirt a portion of the
wealth which she have enable he to win--all that be certain be that
Mr. Osborne get some hint of the transaction, come back from the City
abruptly, and enter the drawing-room with his bamboo cane; find the
painter, the pupil, and the companion all look exceedingly pale
there; turn the former out of door with menace that he would break
every bone in his skin, and half an hour afterwards dismiss Miss Wirt
likewise, kick her trunk down the stair, trample on her
bandbox, and shake his fist at her hackney coach as it bear she
away.
Jane Osborne keep her bedroom for many day. she be not allow to
have a companion afterwards. her father swear to she that she should
not have a shilling of his money if she make any match without his
concurrence; and as he want a woman to keep his house, he do not
choose that she should marry, so that she be oblige to give up all
project with which Cupid have any share. during her papa's life, then,
she resign herself to the manner of existence here describe, and be
content to be an old maid. her sister, meanwhile, be have child
with fine name every year and the intercourse between the two grow
fainter continually. "Jane and I do not move in the same sphere of
life," Mrs. Bullock say. "I regard she as a sister, of course"--which
mean--what do it mean when a lady say that she regard Jane as a
sister?
it have be describe how the Misses Dobbin live with their father at
a fine villa at Denmark Hill, where there be beautiful graperie and
peach-tree which delight little Georgy Osborne. the Misses Dobbin,
who drive often to Brompton to see our dear Amelia, come sometimes to
Russell Square too, to pay a visit to their old acquaintance Miss
Osborne. I believe it be in consequence of the command of their
brother the Major in India (for whom their papa have a prodigious
respect), that they pay attention to Mrs. George; for the Major, the
godfather and guardian of Amelia's little boy, still hop that the
child's grandfather might be induce to relent towards he and
acknowledge he for the sake of his son. the Misses Dobbin keep Miss
Osborne acquaint with the state of Amelia's affair; how she be
live with her father and mother; how poor they be; how they
wonder what man, and such man as their brother and dear Captain
Osborne, could find in such an insignificant little chit; how she be
still, as heretofore, a namby-pamby milk-and-water affected
creature--but how the boy be really the noble little boy ever
see--for the heart of all woman warm towards young child, and the
sour spinster be kind to they.
one day, after great entreaty on the part of the Misses Dobbin,
Amelia allow little George to go and pass a day with they at Denmark
Hill--a part of which day she spend herself in writing to the Major in
India. she congratulate he on the happy news which his sister have
just convey to she. she pray for his prosperity and that of the
bride he have choose. she thank he for a thousand thousand kind
office and proof of stead fast friendship to she in her affliction.
she tell he the last news about little georgy, and how he be go to
spend that very day with his sister in the country. she underline
the letter a great deal, and she sign herself affectionately his
friend, Amelia Osborne. she forget to send any message of kindness to
Lady O'Dowd, as she willnot be--and do not mention Glorvina by name, and
only in italic, as the Major's BRIDE, for whom she beg blessing.
but the news of the marriage remove the reserve which she have keep up
towards he. she be glad to be able to own and feel how warmly and
gratefully she regard he--and as for the idea of be jealous of
Glorvina (Glorvina, indeed!), Amelia would have scout it, if an angel
from heaven have hint it to she. that night, when georgy come back in
the pony-carriage in which he rejoice, and in which he be drive by
Sir Wm. Dobbin's old coachman, he have round his neck a fine gold chain
and watch. he say an old lady, not pretty, have give it he, who
cry and kiss he a great deal. but he donot like she. he like
grape very much. and he only like his mamma. Amelia shrink and
start; the timid soul feel a presentiment of terror when she hear
that the relation of the child's father have see he.
Miss Osborne come back to give her father his dinner. he have make a
good speculation in the City, and be rather in a good humour that day,
and chance to remark the agitation under which she labour. "whatbe
the matter, Miss Osborne?" he deign to say.
the woman burst into tear. "oh, sir," she say, "Ihave see little
George. he be as beautiful as an angel--and so like he!" the old man
opposite to she do not say a word, but flush up and begin to tremble
in every limb.
CHAPTER XLIII
in which the reader have to double the cape
the astonished reader must be call upon to transport himself ten
thousand mile to the military station of Bundlegunge, in the Madras
division of our indian empire, where our gallant old friend of the
--th regiment be quarter under the command of the brave Colonel, Sir
Michael O'Dowd. time have deal kindly with that stout officer, as it
do ordinarily with man who have good stomach and good temper and
be not perplex over much by fatigue of the brain. the Colonel play
a good knife and fork at tiffin and resume those weapon with great
success at dinner. he smoke his hookah after both meal and puff as
quietly while his wife scold he as he do under the fire of the
French at Waterloo. age and heat have not diminish the activity or
the eloquence of the descendant of the Malonys and the Molloys. her
Ladyship, our old acquaintance, be as much at home at Madras as at
Brussels in the cantonment as under the tent. on the march you see
she at the head of the regiment seat on a royal elephant, a noble
sight. mount on that beast, she have be into action with tiger in
the jungle, she have be receive by native prince, who have welcome
she and Glorvina into the recess of their zenana and offer she
shawl and jewel which it go to her heart to refuse. the sentry
of all arm salute she wherever she make her appearance, and she
touch her hat gravely to their salutation. Lady O'Dowd be one of the
great lady in the Presidency of Madras--her quarrel with Lady
Smith, wife of Sir Minos Smith the puisne judge, be still remember by
some at Madras, when the Colonel's lady snap her finger in the
Judge's lady's face and say SHE'D never walk behind ever a beggarly
civilian. even now, though it be five-and-twenty year ago, people
remember Lady O'Dowd perform a jig at Government House, where she
dance down two Aides-de-Camp, a Major of Madras cavalry, and two
gentleman of the Civil Service; and, persuade by Major Dobbin, C.B.,
second in command of the --th, to retire to the supper-room, lassata
nondum satiata recessit.
Peggy O'Dowd be indeed the same as ever, kind in act and think;
impetuous in temper; eager to command; a tyrant over her Michael; a
dragon amongst all the lady of the regiment; a mother to all the
young man, whom she tend in their sickness, defend in all their
scrape, and with whom Lady Peggy be immensely popular. but the
Subalterns' and Captains' lady (the Major be unmarried) cabal against
she a good deal. they say that Glorvina give herself air and that
Peggy herself be ill tolerably domineering. she interfere with a
little congregation which Mrs. Kirk have get up and laugh the young
man away from her sermon, state that a soldier's wife have no
business to be a parson--that Mrs. Kirk would be much well mend
her husband's clothe; and, if the regiment want sermon, that she
have the fine in the world, those of her uncle, the Dean. she abruptly
put a termination to a flirtation which Lieutenant Stubble of the
regiment have commence with the Surgeon's wife, threaten to come
down upon Stubble for the money which he have borrow from she (for the
young fellow be still of an extravagant turn) unless he break off at
once and go to the Cape on sick leave. on the other hand, she house
and shelter Mrs. Posky, who flee from her bungalow one night, pursue
by her infuriate husband, wield his second brandy bottle, and
actually carry Posky through the delirium tremen and break he of
the habit of drinking, which have grow upon that officer, as all evil
habit will grow upon man. in a word, in adversity she be the good of
comforter, in good fortune the most troublesome of friend, have a
perfectly good opinion of herself always and an indomitable resolution
to have her own way.
among other point, she have make up her mind that Glorvina should marry
our old friend Dobbin. Mrs. O'Dowd know the Major's expectation and
appreciate his good quality and the high character which he enjoy
in his profession. Glorvina, a very handsome, fresh-colour,
black-haired, blue-eyed young lady, who could ride a horse, or play a
sonata with any girl out of the County Cork, seem to be the very
person destine to insure Dobbin's happiness--much more than that poor
good little weak-spur'ted Amelia, about whom he use to take on
so.--"Look at Glorvina enter a room," Mrs. O'Dowd would say, "and
compare she with that poor Mrs. Osborne, who couldnot say boo to a
goose. shewould be worthy of you, Major--you're a quiet man yourself, and
want some one to talk for ye. and though she do not come of such
good blood as the Malonys or Molloys, let I tell ye, shebe of an
ancient family that any nobleman might be proud to marry into."
but before she have come to such a resolution and determine to
subjugate major Dobbin by her endearment, it must be own that
Glorvina have practise they a good deal elsewhere. she have have a
season in Dublin, and who know how many in Cork, Killarney, and
Mallow? she have flirt with all the marriageable officer whom the
depot of her country afford, and all the bachelor squire who seem
eligible. she have be engage to be marry a half-score time in
Ireland, besides the clergyman at Bath who use she so ill. she have
flirt all the way to Madras with the Captain and chief mate of the
Ramchunder East Indiaman, and have a season at the Presidency with her
brother and Mrs. O'Dowd, who be stay there, while the Major of the
regiment be in command at the station. everybody admire she there;
everybody dance with she; but no one propose who be worth the
marrying--one or two exceedingly young subaltern sigh after she, and
a beardless civilian or two, but she reject these as beneath her
pretension--and other and young virgin than Glorvina be marry
before she. there be woman, and handsome woman too, who have this
fortune in life. they fall in love with the utmost generosity; they
ride and walk with half the Army-list, though they draw near to forty,
and yet the Misses O'Grady be the Misses O'Grady still: Glorvina
persist that but for Lady O'Dowd's unlucky quarrel with the Judge's
lady, she would have make a good match at Madras, where old Mr.
Chutney, who be at the head of the civil service (and who afterwards
marry Miss Dolby, a young lady only thirteen year of age who have
just arrive from school in Europe), be just at the point of propose
to she.
well, although Lady O'Dowd and Glorvina quarrel a great number of
time every day, and upon almost every conceivable subject--indeed, if
Mick O'Dowd have not possess the temper of an angel two such woman
constantly about his ear would have drive he out of his sens--yet
they agree between themselves on this point, that Glorvina should
marry Major Dobbin, and be determined that the Major should have no
rest until the arrangement be bring about. undismaye by forty or
fifty previous defeat, Glorvina lay siege to he. she sing irish
melody at he unceasingly. she ask he so frequently and
pathetically, will ye come to the bower? that it be a wonder how any
man of feeling could have resist the invitation. she be never tired
of inquire, if Sorrow have his young day fade, and be ready to
listen and weep like Desdemona at the story of his danger and his
campaign. it have be say that our honest and dear old friend use
to perform on the flute in private; Glorvina insist upon have duet
with he, and Lady O'Dowd would rise and artlessly quit the room when
the young couple be so engaged. Glorvina force the Major to ride
with she of morning. the whole cantonment see they set out and
return. she be constantly write note over to he at his house,
borrow his book, and score with her great pencil-mark such
passage of sentiment or humour as awaken her sympathy. she borrow
his horse, his servant, his spoon, and palanquin--no wonder that
public rumour assign she to he, and that the Major's sister in
England should fancy they be about to have a sister-in-law.
Dobbin, who be thus vigorously besiege, be in the meanwhile in a
state of the most odious tranquillity. he use to laugh when the young
fellow of the regiment joke he about Glorvina's manifest attention
to he. "bah!" say he, "she be only keep her hand in--she
practise upon I as she do upon Mrs. Tozer's piano, because itbe the
most handy instrument in the station. I be much too battered and old
for such a fine young lady as Glorvina." and so he go on rid with
she, and copy music and verse into her album, and play at chess
with she very submissively; for it be with these simple amusement that
some officer in India be accustom to while away their leisure
moment, while other of a less domestic turn hunt hog, and shoot
snipe, or gamble and smoke cheroot, and betake themselves to
brandy-and-water. as for Sir Michael O'Dowd, though his lady and her
sister both urge he to call upon the Major to explain himself and not
keep on torture a poor innocent girl in that shameful way, the old
soldier refuse point-blank to have anything to do with the conspiracy.
"Faith, the Major's big enough to choose for himself," Sir Michael
say; "hewill ask ye when he want ye"; or else he would turn the matter
off jocularly, declare that "Dobbin be too young to keep house, and
have write home to ask lave of his mamma." Nay, he go far, and
in private communication with his Major would caution and rally he,
cry, "mind your oi, Dob, my boy, they girl be bent on mischief--I
Lady have just get a box of gown from Europe, and therebe a pink satin
for Glorvina, which will finish ye, Dob, if itbe in the power of woman
or satin to move ye."
but the truth be, neither beauty nor fashion could conquer he. our
honest friend have but one idea of a woman in his head, and that one do
not in the least resemble Miss Glorvina O'Dowd in pink satin. a gentle
little woman in black, with large eye and brown hair, seldom speak,
save when speak to, and then in a voice not the least resemble Miss
Glorvina's--a soft young mother tend an infant and beckon the
Major up with a smile to look at he--a rosy-cheeked lass come
singing into the room in Russell Square or hang on George Osborne's
arm, happy and loving--there be but this image that fill our honest
Major's mind, by day and by night, and reign over it always. very
likely Amelia be not like the portrait the Major have form of she:
there be a figure in a book of fashion which his sister have in
England, and with which William have make away privately, paste it
into the lid of his desk, and fancy he see some resemblance to Mrs.
Osborne in the print, whereas I have see it, and can vouch that it be
but the picture of a high-waiste gown with an impossible doll's face
simper over it--and, perhaps, Mr. Dobbin's sentimental Amelia be no
more like the real one than this absurd little print which he
cherish. but what man in love, of we, be well informed?--or be he
much happy when he see and own his delusion? Dobbin be under this
spell. he do not bother his friend and the public much about his
feeling, or indeed lose his natural rest or appetite on account of
they. his head have grizzle since we see he last, and a line or two
of silver may be see in the soft brown hair likewise. but his
feeling be not in the least change or oldene, and his love remain
as fresh as a man's recollection of boyhood be.
we have say how the two Misses Dobbin and Amelia, the Major's
correspondent in Europe, write he letter from England, Mrs. Osborne
congratulate he with great candour and cordiality upon his
approach nuptial with Miss O'Dowd. "your sister have just kindly
visit I," Amelia write in her letter, "and inform I of an
interesting event, upon which I beg to offer my MOST SINCERE
CONGRATULATIONS. I hope the young lady to whom I hear you be to be
UNITED will in every respect prove worthy of one who be himself all
kindness and goodness. the poor widow have only her prayer to offer
and her cordial cordial wish for your PROSPERITY! georgy send his
love to his DEAR GODPAPA and hop that you will not forget he. I tell
he that you be about to form other tie, with one who I be sure
merit all your AFFECTION, but that, although such tie must of course
be the strong and most sacred, and supersede all other, yet that I
be sure the widow and the child whom you have ever protect and love
will always have a corner in your heart" the letter, which have be
before allude to, go on in this strain, protest throughout as to
the extreme satisfaction of the writer.
this letter, which arrive by the very same ship which bring out
Lady O'Dowd's box of millinery from London (and which you may be sure
Dobbin open before any one of the other packet which the mail
bring he), put the receiver into such a state of mind that Glorvina,
and her pink satin, and everything belong to she become perfectly
odious to he. the Major curse the talk of woman, and the sex in
general. everything annoy he that day--the parade be insufferably
hot and wearisome. good heaven! be a man of intellect to waste his
life, day after day, inspect cross-belt and put fool through
their manoeuvre? the senseless chatter of the young man at mess be
more than ever jarring. what care he, a man on the high road to forty,
to know how many snipe Lieutenant Smith have shoot, or what be the
performance of Ensign Brown's mare? the joke about the table fill
he with shame. he be too old to listen to the banter of the
assistant surgeon and the slang of the youngster, at which old O'Dowd,
with his bald head and red face, laugh quite easily. the old man have
listen to those joke any time these thirty year--Dobbin himself have
be fifteen year hear they. and after the boisterous dulness of
the mess-table, the quarrel and scandal of the lady of the regiment!
it be unbearable, shameful. "O Amelia, Amelia," he think, "you to
whom I have be so faithful--you reproach I! it be because you
cannot feel for I that I drag on this wearisome life. and you reward
I after year of devotion by give I your blessing upon my marriage,
forsooth, with this flaunt irish girl!" sick and sorry feel poor
William; more than ever wretched and lonely. he would like to have
do with life and its vanity altogether--so bootless and
unsatisfactory the struggle, so cheerless and dreary the prospect
seem to he. he lie all that night sleepless, and yearn to go
home. Amelia's letter have fall as a blank upon he. no fidelity, no
constant truth and passion, could move she into warmth. she would not
see that he love she. toss in his bed, he speak out to she. "good
God, Amelia!" he say, "donot you know that I only love you in the
world--you, who be a stone to I--you, whom I tend through month
and month of illness and grief, and who bid I farewell with a smile
on your face, and forget I before the door shut between we!" the
native servant lie outside his veranda beheld with wonder the
Major, so cold and quiet ordinarily, at present so passionately move
and cast down. would she have pity he have she see he? he read
over and over all the letter which he ever have from she--letter of
business relative to the little property which he have make she believe
her husband have leave to she--brief note of invitation--every scrap of
write that she have ever send to he--how cold, how kind, how
hopeless, how selfish they be!
have there be some kind gentle soul near at hand who could read and
appreciate this silent generous heart, who know but that the reign of
Amelia might have be over, and that friend William's love might have
flow into a kind channel? but there be only Glorvina of the jetty
ringlet with whom his intercourse be familiar, and this dash young
woman be not bent upon love the Major, but rather on make the
Major admire HER--a most vain and hopeless task, too, at least
consider the mean that the poor girl possess to carry it out.
she curl her hair and show her shoulder at he, as much as to say,
do ye ever see such jet ringlet and such a complexion? she grin at
he so that he might see that every tooth in her head be sound--and he
never heed all these charm. very soon after the arrival of the box
of millinery, and perhaps indeed in honour of it, Lady O'Dowd and the
lady of the King's regiment give a ball to the Company's Regiments
and the civilian at the station. Glorvina sport the kill pink
frock, and the Major, who attend the party and walk very ruefully
up and down the room, never so much as perceive the pink garment.
Glorvina dance past he in a fury with all the young subaltern of the
station, and the Major be not in the least jealous of her performance,
or angry because Captain Bangles of the Cavalry hand she to supper.
it be not jealousy, or frock, or shoulder that could move he, and
Glorvina have nothing more.
so these two be each exemplify the Vanity of this life, and each
longing for what he or she could not get. Glorvina cry with rage at
the failure. she have set her mind on the major "more than on any of
the other," she own, sob. "hewill break my heart, he will,
Peggy," she would whimper to her sister-in-law when they be good
friend; "sure every one of I frock must be take in--itbe such a
skeleton Ibe grow." fat or thin, laugh or melancholy, on
horseback or the music-stool, it be all the same to the Major. and
the Colonel, puff his pipe and listen to these complaint, would
suggest that glory should have some black frock out in the next box
from London, and tell a mysterious story of a lady in Ireland who die
of grief for the loss of her husband before she get ere a one.
while the Major be go on in this tantalize way, not propose,
and decline to fall in love, there come another ship from Europe
bring letter on board, and amongst they some more for the heartless
man. these be home letter bear an early postmark than that of
the former packet, and as Major Dobbin recognize among his the
handwriting of his sister, who always cross and recrosse her letter
to her brother--gather together all the possible bad news which she
could collect, abuse he and read he lecture with sisterly
frankness, and always leave he miserable for the day after "dear
William" have achieve the perusal of one of her epistle--the truth
must be tell that dearest William do not hurry himself to break the
seal of Miss Dobbin's letter, but wait for a particularly favourable
day and mood for do so. a fortnight before, moreover, he have
write to scold she for tell those absurd story to Mrs. Osborne,
and have despatch a letter in reply to that lady, undeceive she with
respect to the report concern he and assure she that "he have no
sort of present intention of alter his condition."
two or three night after the arrival of the second package of letter,
the Major have pass the evening pretty cheerfully at Lady O'Dowd's
house, where Glorvina think that he listen with rather more
attention than usual to the Meeting of the Wathers, the Minsthrel Boy,
and one or two other specimen of song with which she favour he (the
truth be, he be no more listen to Glorvina than to the howling of
the jackal in the moonlight outside, and the delusion be her as
usual), and have play his game at chess with she (cribbage with the
surgeon be Lady O'Dowd's favourite evening pastime), Major Dobbin take
leave of the Colonel's family at his usual hour and retire to his own
house.
there on his table, his sister's letter lie reproach he. he take
it up, ashamed rather of his negligence regard it, and prepare
himself for a disagreeable hour's communing with that crab-handed
absent relative. . . . it may have be an hour after the Major's
departure from the Colonel's house--Sir Michael be sleep the sleep
of the just; Glorvina have arrange her black ringlet in the
innumerable little bit of paper, in which it be her habit to confine
they; Lady O'Dowd, too, have go to her bed in the nuptial chamber, on
the ground-floor, and have tuck her musquito curtain round her fair
form, when the guard at the gate of the Commanding-Officer's compound
beheld Major Dobbin, in the moonlight, rush towards the house with a
swift step and a very agitated countenance, and he pass the sentinel
and go up to the window of the Colonel's bedchamber.
"O'Dowd--Colonel!" say Dobbin and keep up a great shouting.
"Heavens, Meejor!" say Glorvina of the curl-paper, put out her
head too, from her window.
"what be it, dob, I boy?" say the Colonel, expect there be a fire
in the station, or that the route have come from headquarters.
"I--I must have leave of absence. I must go to England--on the most
urgent private affair," Dobbin say.
"good heaven, what have happen!" think Glorvina, tremble with all
the papillote.
"I want to be off--now--to-night," Dobbin continue; and the Colonel
get up, come out to parley with he.
in the postscript of Miss Dobbin's cross-letter, the Major have just
come upon a paragraph, to the follow effect:--"i drive yesterday to
see your old ACQUAINTANCE, Mrs. Osborne. the wretched place they live
at, since they be bankrupt, you know--Mr. S., to judge from a BRASS
plate on the door of his hut (it be little well) be a coal-merchant.
the little boy, your godson, be certainly a fine child, though forward,
and incline to be saucy and self-willed. but we have take notice of
he as you wish it, and have introduce he to his aunt, Miss O., who
be rather pleased with he. perhaps his grandpapa, not the bankrupt
one, who be almost dot, but Mr. Osborne, of Russell Square, may be
induce to relent towards the child of your friend, his erring and
self-WILLED son. and Amelia will not be ill-disposed to give he up.
the widow be console, and be about to marry a reverend gentleman, the
Rev. Mr. Binny, one of the curate of Brompton. a poor match. but
Mrs. O. be get old, and I see a great deal of grey in her hair--she
be in very good spirit: and your little godson overate himself at
our house. Mamma send her love with that of your affectionate, Ann
Dobbin."
CHAPTER XLIV
a round-about chapter between London and Hampshire
our old friend the Crawleys' family house, in Great Gaunt Street,
still bear over its front the hatchment which have be place there as
a token of mourning for Sir Pitt Crawley's demise, yet this heraldic
emblem be in itself a very splendid and gaudy piece of furniture, and
all the rest of the mansion become more brilliant than it have ever be
during the late baronet's reign. the black outer-coating of the brick
be remove, and they appear with a cheerful, blush face streak
with white: the old bronze lion of the knocker be gild handsomely,
the railing paint, and the dismallest house in Great Gaunt Street
become the smart in the whole quarter, before the green leaf in
Hampshire have replace those yellow one which be on the tree in
Queen's Crawley Avenue when old Sir Pitt Crawley pass under they for
the last time.
a little woman, with a carriage to correspond, be perpetually see
about this mansion; an elderly spinster, accompany by a little boy,
also might be remark come thither daily. it be Miss Briggs and
little Rawdon, whose business it be to see to the inward renovation of
Sir Pitt's house, to superintend the female band engage in stitch
the blind and hanging, to poke and rummage in the drawer and
cupboard cram with the dirty relic and congregate trumpery of a
couple of generation of Lady Crawleys, and to take inventory of the
china, the glass, and other property in the closet and store-room.
Mrs. Rawdon Crawley be general-in-chief over these arrangement, with
full order from Sir Pitt to sell, barter, confiscate, or purchase
furniture, and she enjoy herself not a little in an occupation which
give full scope to her taste and ingenuity. the renovation of the
house be determine upon when Sir Pitt come to town in November to see
his lawyer, and when he pass nearly a week in Curzon Street, under
the roof of his affectionate brother and sister.
he have put up at an hotel at first, but, Becky, as soon as she hear of
the Baronet's arrival, go off alone to greet he, and return in an
hour to Curzon Street with Sir Pitt in the carriage by her side. it
be impossible sometimes to resist this artless little creature's
hospitality, so kindly be they press, so frankly and amiably
offer. Becky seize Pitt's hand in a transport of gratitude when he
agree to come. "thank you," she say, squeeze it and look into
the Baronet's eye, who blush a good deal; "how happy this will make
Rawdon!" she bustle up to Pitt's bedroom, lead on the servant, who
be carry his trunk thither. she come in herself laugh, with a
coal-scuttle out of her own room.
a fire be blaze already in Sir Pitt's apartment (it be Miss
Briggs's room, by the way, who be send upstairs to sleep with the
maid). "I know I should bring you," she say with pleasure beam in
her glance. indeed, she be really sincerely happy at have he for a
guest.
Becky make Rawdon dine out once or twice on business, while Pitt stay
with they, and the Baronet pass the happy evening alone with she and
Briggs. she go downstairs to the kitchen and actually cook little
dish for he. "benot it a good salmi?" she say; "I make it for you.
I can make you well dish than that, and will when you come to see
I."
"everything you do, you do well," say the Baronet gallantly. "the
salmi be excellent indeed."
"a poor man's wife," Rebecca reply gaily, "must make herself useful,
you know"; on which her brother-in-law vow that "she be fit to be
the wife of an Emperor, and that to be skilful in domestic duty be
surely one of the most charming of woman's quality." and Sir Pitt
thought, with something like mortification, of Lady Jane at home, and
of a certain pie which she have insist on make, and serve to he
at dinner--a most abominable pie.
besides the salmi, which be make of Lord Steyne's pheasant from his
lordship's cottage of Stillbrook, Becky give her brother-in-law a
bottle of white wine, some that Rawdon have bring with he from
France, and have pick up for nothing, the little story-teller say;
whereas the liquor be, in truth, some White Hermitage from the Marquis
of Steyne's famous cellar, which bring fire into the Baronet's
pallid cheek and a glow into his feeble frame.
then when he have drink up the bottle of petit vin blanc, she give he
her hand, and take he up to the drawing-room, and make he snug on the
sofa by the fire, and let he talk as she listen with the tender
kindly interest, sit by he, and hem a shirt for her dear
little boy. whenever Mrs. Rawdon wish to be particularly humble and
virtuous, this little shirt use to come out of her work-box. it have
get to be too small for Rawdon long before it be finish.
well, Rebecca listen to Pitt, she talk to he, she sing to he, she
coax he, and cuddle he, so that he find himself more and more
glad every day to get back from the lawyer's at Gray's Inn, to the
blaze fire in Curzon Street--a gladness in which the man of law
likewise participate, for Pitt's harangue be of the long--and so
that when he go away he feel quite a pang at depart. how pretty
she look kiss her hand to he from the carriage and wave her
handkerchief when he have take his place in the mail! she put the
handkerchief to her eye once. he pull his sealskin cap over his, as
the coach drive away, and, sink back, he think to himself how she
respect he and how he deserve it, and how Rawdon be a foolish dull
fellow who donot half-appreciate his wife; and how mum and stupid his
own wife be compare to that brilliant little Becky. Becky have hint
every one of these thing herself, perhaps, but so delicately and
gently that you hardly know when or where. and, before they part, it
be agree that the house in London should be redecorate for the next
season, and that the brother' family should meet again in the
country at Christmas.
"I wish you could have get a little money out of he," Rawdon say to
his wife moodily when the Baronet be go. "I should like to give
something to old Raggles, hang if I shouldnot. it benot right, you
know, that the old fellow should be keep out of all his money. it may
be inconvenient, and he might let to somebody else besides we, you
know."
"tell he," say Becky, "that as soon as Sir Pitt's affair be
settle, everybody will be pay, and give he a little something on
account. herebe a cheque that Pitt leave for the boy," and she take
from her bag and give her husband a paper which his brother have hand
over to she, on behalf of the little son and heir of the young branch
of the Crawleys.
the truth be, she have try personally the ground on which her husband
express a wish that she should venture--try it ever so delicately,
and find it unsafe. even at a hint about embarrassment, Sir Pitt
Crawley be off and alarm. and he begin a long speech, explain
how straiten he himself be in money matter; how the tenant would
not pay; how his father's affair, and the expense attendant upon the
demise of the old gentleman, have involve he; how he want to pay off
incumbrance; and how the banker and agent be overdraw; and Pitt
Crawley end by make a compromise with his sister-in-law and give
she a very small sum for the benefit of her little boy.
Pitt know how poor his brother and his brother's family must be. it
could not have escape the notice of such a cool and experienced old
diplomatist that Rawdon's family have nothing to live upon, and that
house and carriage be not to be keep for nothing. he know very well
that he be the proprietor or appropriator of the money, which,
accord to all proper calculation, ought to have fall to his
young brother, and he have, we may be sure, some secret pang of
remorse within he, which warn he that he ought to perform some act
of justice, or, let we say, compensation, towards these disappoint
relation. a just, decent man, not without brain, who say his
prayer, and know his catechism, and do his duty outwardly through
life, he could not be otherwise than aware that something be due to
his brother at his hand, and that morally he be Rawdon's debtor.
but, as one read in the column of the Times newspaper every now and
then, queer announcement from the Chancellor of the Exchequer,
acknowledge the receipt of 50 pound from A. B., or 10 pound from
W. T., as conscience-money, on account of taxis due by the say A. B.
or W. T., which payment the penitent beg the Right honourable
gentleman to acknowledge through the medium of the public press--so be
the Chancellor no doubt, and the reader likewise, always perfectly sure
that the above-name A. B. and W. T. be only pay a very small
instalment of what they really owe, and that the man who send up a
twenty-pound note have very likely hundred or thousand more for which
he ought to account. such, at least, be my feeling, when I see
A. B. or W. T.'s insufficient act of repentance. and I have no doubt
that Pitt Crawley's contrition, or kindness if you will, towards his
young brother, by whom he have so much profit, be only a very small
dividend upon the capital sum in which he be indebted to Rawdon. not
everybody be willing to pay even so much. to part with money be a
sacrifice beyond almost all man endow with a sense of order. there
be scarcely any man alive who do not think himself meritorious for
give his neighbour five pound. thriftless give, not from a
beneficent pleasure in giving, but from a lazy delight in spending. he
would not deny himself one enjoyment; not his opera-stall, not his
horse, not his dinner, not even the pleasure of give Lazarus the five
pound. Thrifty, who be good, wise, just, and owe no man a penny,
turn from a beggar, haggle with a hackney-coachman, or deny a poor
relation, and I doubt which be the most selfish of the two. money have
only a different value in the eye of each.
so, in a word, Pitt Crawley think he would do something for his
brother, and then think that he would think about it some other time.
and with regard to Becky, she be not a woman who expect too much
from the generosity of her neighbour, and so be quite content with
all that Pitt Crawley have do for she. she be acknowledge by the
head of the family. if Pitt would not give she anything, he would get
something for she some day. if she get no money from her
brother-in-law, she get what be as good as money--credit. Raggles be
make rather easy in his mind by the spectacle of the union between the
brother, by a small payment on the spot, and by the promise of a much
large sum speedily to be assign to he. and Rebecca tell Miss
Briggs, whose Christmas dividend upon the little sum lend by her Becky
pay with an air of candid joy, and as if her exchequer be brim
over with gold--rebecca, we say, tell Miss Briggs, in strict confidence
that she have confer with Sir Pitt, who be famous as a financier, on
Briggs's special behalf, as to the most profitable investment of Miss
B.'s remain capital; that Sir Pitt, after much consideration, have
thought of a most safe and advantageous way in which Briggs could lay
out her money; that, be especially interested in she as an attach
friend of the late Miss Crawley, and of the whole family, and that long
before he leave town, he have recommend that she should be ready with
the money at a moment's notice, so as to purchase at the most
favourable opportunity the share which Sir Pitt have in his eye. poor
Miss Briggs be very grateful for this mark of Sir Pitt's attention--it
come so unsolicited, she say, for she never should have think of
remove the money from the fund--and the delicacy enhance the
kindness of the office; and she promise to see her man of business
immediately and be ready with her little cash at the proper hour.
and this worthy woman be so grateful for the kindness of Rebecca in
the matter, and for that of her generous benefactor, the Colonel, that
she go out and spend a great part of her half-year's dividend in the
purchase of a black velvet coat for little Rawdon, who, by the way, be
grow almost too big for black velvet now, and be of a size and age
befit he for the assumption of the virile jacket and pantaloon.
he be a fine open-faced boy, with blue eye and wave flaxen hair,
sturdy in limb, but generous and soft in heart, fondly attach
himself to all who be good to he--to the pony--to Lord Southdown,
who give he the horse (he use to blush and glow all over when he see
that kind young nobleman)--to the groom who have charge of the pony--to

curious too to witness. here, as he grow to be about eight year old,
his attachment may be say to have end. the beautiful mother-vision
have fade away after a while. during near two year she have scarcely

a moment before have be rapt in delight, and listen to the music.
his mother come out and strike he violently a couple of box on the

amuse by this free and artless exhibition of Becky's temper) and flee

"it be not because it hurt I," little Rawdon gasp
out--"only--only"--sob and tear wound up the sentence in a storm. it
be the little boy's heart that be bleed. "why maynot I hear she
singing? why donot she ever sing to I--as she do to that baldheade
man with the large tooth?" he gasp out at various interval these
exclamation of rage and grief. the cook look at the housemaid, the
housemaid look knowingly at the footman--the awful kitchen inquisition
which sit in judgement in every house and know everything--sit on
Rebecca at that moment.
after this incident, the mother's dislike increase to hatred; the
consciousness that the child be in the house be a reproach and a pain
to she. his very sight annoy she. fear, doubt, and resistance
spring up, too, in the boy's own bosom. they be separate from that
day of the box on the ear.
Lord Steyne also heartily dislike the boy. when they meet by
mischance, he make sarcastic bow or remark to the child, or glare at
he with savage-look eye. Rawdon use to stare he in the face and
double his little fist in return. he know his enemy, and this
gentleman, of all who come to the house, be the one who anger he
most. one day the footman find he square his fist at Lord
Steyne's hat in the hall. the footman tell the circumstance as a good
joke to Lord Steyne's coachman; that officer impart it to Lord
Steyne's gentleman, and to the servant' hall in general. and very soon
afterwards, when Mrs. Rawdon Crawley make her appearance at Gaunt
House, the porter who unbar the gate, the servant of all uniform
in the hall, the functionary in white waistcoat, who bawl out from

gentleman in motley-colour clothe at his side. Bon Dieu! it be
awful, that servant' inquisition! you see a woman in a great party in
a splendid saloon, surround by faithful admirer, distribute
sparkle glance, dress to perfection, curl, rouge, smile and
happy--Discovery walk respectfully up to she, in the shape of a huge
powdered man with large calf and a tray of ice--with Calumny (which
be as fatal as truth) behind he, in the shape of the hulking fellow

those man at their club at the public-house to-night. jeame will tell
Chawles his notion about you over their pipe and pewter beer-pot.
some people ought to have mute for servant in Vanity Fair--mute who
could not write. if you be guilty, tremble. that fellow behind your
chair may be a Janissary with a bow-string in his plush breech
pocket. if you be not guilty, have a care of appearance, which be
as ruinous as guilt.
"be rebecca guilty or not?" the Vehmgericht of tho servant' hall have
pronounce against she.
and, I shame to say, she would not have get credit have they not
believe she to be guilty. it be the sight of the Marquis of Steyne's
carriage-lamp at her door, contemplate by Raggles, burn in the
blackness of midnight, "that kep he up," as he afterwards say, that
even more than Rebecca's art and coaxing.
and so--guiltless very likely--she be writhe and push onward
towards what they call "a position in society," and the servant be
point at she as lose and ruin. so you see Molly, the housemaid,
of a morning, watch a spider in the doorpost lie his thread and
laboriously crawl up it, until, tired of the sport, she raise she
broom and sweep away the thread and the artificer.
a day or two before Christmas, Becky, her husband and her son make
ready and go to pass the holiday at the seat of their ancestor at
Queen's Crawley. Becky would have like to leave the little brat
behind, and would have do so but for Lady Jane's urgent invitation

shallnot bother you much; at home he will be away from you in the

"where you go yourself because you want to smoke those filthy cigar,"
reply Mrs. Rawdon.
"I remember when you like them though," answer the husband.
Becky laugh; she be almost always good-humour. "that be when I
be on my promotion, Goosey," she say. "take Rawdon outside with you
and give he a cigar too if you like."
Rawdon do not warm his little son for the winter's journey in this
way, but he and Briggs wrap up the child in shawl and comforter,
and he be hoist respectfully onto the roof of the coach in the dark

delight he watch the dawn rise and make his first journey to the
place which his father still call home. it be a journey of infinite
pleasure to the boy, to whom the incident of the road afford endless
interest, his father answer to he all question connect with it
and tell he who live in the great white house to the right, and
whom the park belong to. his mother, inside the vehicle, with she
maid and her fur, her wrapper, and her scent bottle, make such a
to-do that you would have think she never have be in a stage-coach
before--much less, that she have be turn out of this very one to
make room for a pay passenger on a certain journey perform some
half-score year ago.
it be dark again when little Rawdon be waken up to enter his
uncle's carriage at Mudbury, and he sit and look out of it wonder
as the great iron gate fly open, and at the white trunk of the lime
as they sweep by, until they stop, at length, before the light
window of the Hall, which be blaze and comfortable with Christmas
welcome. the hall-door be fling open--a big fire be burn in the
great old fire-place--a carpet be down over the chequer black
flags--"It's the old Turkey one that use to be in the lady'
Gallery," think Rebecca, and the next instant be kiss Lady Jane.
she and Sir Pitt perform the same salute with great gravity; but
Rawdon, have be smoking, hang back rather from his sister-in-law,
whose two child come up to their cousin; and, while Matilda hold out
her hand and kiss he, Pitt Binkie Southdown, the son and heir, stand
aloof rather and examine he as a little dog do a big dog.
then the kind hostess conduct her guest to the snug apartment
blaze with cheerful fire. then the young lady come and knock at
Mrs. Rawdon's door, under the pretence that they be desirous to be
useful, but in reality to have the pleasure of inspect the content
of her band and bonnet-box, and her dress which, though black, be
of the new London fashion. and they tell she how much the Hall be
change for the well, and how old Lady Southdown be go, and how
Pitt be take his station in the county, as become a Crawley in fact.
then the great dinner-bell have rung, the family assemble at dinner,
at which meal Rawdon Junior be place by his aunt, the good-natured
lady of the house, Sir Pitt be uncommonly attentive to his
sister-in-law at his own right hand.
Little Rawdon exhibit a fine appetite and show a gentlemanlike
behaviour.
"I like to dine here," he say to his aunt when he have complete his
meal, at the conclusion of which, and after a decent grace by Sir Pitt,
the young son and heir be introduce, and be perch on a high
chair by the Baronet's side, while the daughter take possession of the
place and the little wine-glass prepare for she near her mother. "I
like to dine here," say Rawdon Minor, look up at his relation's
kind face.
"why?" say the good Lady Jane.
"I dine in the kitchen when I be at home," reply Rawdon Minor, "or
else with Briggs." but Becky be so engaged with the Baronet, her host,
pour out a flood of compliment and delight and rapture, and
admire young Pitt Binkie, whom she declare to be the most beautiful,
intelligent, noble-looking little creature, and so like his father,
that she do not hear the remark of her own flesh and blood at the
other end of the broad shin table.
as a guest, and it be the first night of his arrival, Rawdon the
Second be allow to sit up until the hour when tea be over, and a
great gilt book be lay on the table before Sir Pitt, all the
domestic of the family stream in, and Sir Pitt read prayer. it be
the first time the poor little boy have ever witness or hear of such
a ceremonial.
the house have be much improve even since the Baronet's brief reign,
and be pronounce by Becky to be perfect, charming, delightful, when
she survey it in his company. as for little Rawdon, who examine it
with the child for his guide, it seem to he a perfect palace of
enchantment and wonder. there be long gallery, and ancient state
bedroom, there be picture and old China, and armour. there be
the room in which Grandpapa die, and by which the child walk
with terrified look. "who be grandpapa?" he ask; and they tell he
how he use to be very old, and use to be wheel about in a
garden-chair, and they show he the garden-chair one day rot in
the out-house in which it have lain since the old gentleman have be
wheel away yonder to the church, of which the spire be glitter
over the park elm.
the brother have good occupation for several morning in examine the
improvement which have be effect by Sir Pitt's genius and economy.
and as they walk or ride, and look at they, they could talk without
too much boring each other. and Pitt take care to tell Rawdon what a
heavy outlay of money these improvement have occasion, and that a man
of land and fund property be often very hard press for twenty
pound. "there be that new lodge-gate," say Pitt, point to it
humbly with the bamboo cane, "I can no more pay for it before the
dividend in January than I can fly."
"I can lend you, Pitt, till then," Rawdon answer rather ruefully; and
they go in and look at the restore lodge, where the family arm
be just new scrap in stone, and where old Mrs. Lock, for the first
time these many long year, have tight door, sound roof, and whole
window.
CHAPTER XLV
between Hampshire and London
Sir Pitt Crawley have do more than repair fence and restore
dilapidate lodge on the Queen's Crawley estate. like a wise man he
have set to work to rebuild the injure popularity of his house and stop
up the gap and ruin in which his name have be leave by his
disreputable and thriftless old predecessor. he be elect for the
borough speedily after his father's demise; a magistrate, a member of
parliament, a county magnate and representative of an ancient family,
he make it his duty to show himself before the Hampshire public,
subscribe handsomely to the county charity, call assiduously upon
all the county folk, and lay himself out in a word to take that
position in Hampshire, and in the Empire afterwards, to which he
think his prodigious talent justly entitle he. Lady Jane be
instruct to be friendly with the Fuddlestones, and the wapshot, and
the other famous baronet, their neighbour. their carriage might
frequently be see in the Queen's Crawley avenue now; they din pretty
frequently at the Hall (where the cookery be so good that it be clear
Lady Jane very seldom have a hand in it), and in return Pitt and his
wife most energetically din out in all sort of weather and at all
sort of distance. for though Pitt do not care for joviality, be
a frigid man of poor hearth and appetite, yet he consider that to be
hospitable and condescending be quite incumbent on-his station, and
every time that he get a headache from too long an after-dinner
sitting, he feel that he be a martyr to duty. he talk about crop,
corn-law, politic, with the good country gentleman. he (who have be
formerly inclined to be a sad free-thinker on these point) enter
into poaching and game preserve with ardour. he donot hunt; he
benot a hunt man; he be a man of book and peaceful habit; but he
think that the breed of horse must be keep up in the country, and
that the breed of fox must therefore be look to, and for his part,
if his friend, Sir Huddlestone Fuddlestone, like to draw his country
and meet as of old the F. hound use to do at Queen's Crawley, he
should be happy to see he there, and the gentleman of the Fuddlestone
hunt. and to Lady Southdown's dismay too he become more orthodox in
his tendency every day; give up preach in public and attend
meeting-house; go stoutly to church; call on the Bishop and all
the Clergy at Winchester; and make no objection when the Venerable
Archdeacon Trumper ask for a game of whist. what pang must have
be those of Lady Southdown, and what an utter castaway she must have
think her son-in-law for permit such a godless diversion! and
when, on the return of the family from an oratorio at Winchester, the
Baronet announce to the young lady that he should next year very
probably take they to the "county ball," they worship he for his
kindness. Lady Jane be only too obedient, and perhaps glad herself to
go. the Dowager write off the dire description of her daughter's
worldly behaviour to the authoress of the Washerwoman of Finchley
Common at the cape; and her house in Brighton be about this time
unoccupied, return to that watering-place, her absence be not very
much deplore by her child. we may suppose, too, that rebecca, on
pay a second visit to Queen's Crawley, do not feel particularly
grieve at the absence of the lady of the medicine chest; though she
write a Christmas letter to her ladyship, in which she respectfully
recall herself to Lady Southdown's recollection, speak with gratitude
of the delight which her ladyship's conversation have give she on the
former visit, dilate on the kindness with which her ladyship have
treat she in sickness, and declare that everything at Queen's
Crawley remind she of her absent friend.
a great part of the alter demeanour and popularity of Sir Pitt
Crawley might have be trace to the counsel of that astute little
lady of Curzon Street. "you remain a Baronet--you consent to be a mere
country gentleman," she say to he, while he have be her guest in
London. "no, Sir Pitt Crawley, I know you well. I know your talent
and your ambition. you fancy you hide they both, but you can conceal
neither from I. I show Lord Steyne your pamphlet on malt. he be
familiar with it, and say it be in the opinion of the whole Cabinet
the most masterly thing that have appear on the subject. the Ministry
have its eye upon you, and I know what you want. you want to
distinguish yourself in Parliament; every one say you be the fine
speaker in England (for your speech at Oxford be still remember).
you want to be Member for the County, where, with your own vote and
your borough at your back, you can command anything. and you want to
be Baron Crawley of Queen's Crawley, and will be before you die. I see
it all. I could read your heart, Sir Pitt. if I have a husband who
possess your intellect as he do your name, I sometimes think I
should not be unworthy of he--but--but I be your kinswoman now," she
add with a laugh. "poor little penniless, I have get a little
interest--and who know, perhaps the mouse may be able to aid the
lion." Pitt Crawley be amazed and enrapture with her speech. "how
that woman comprehend I!" he say. "I never could get Jane to read
three page of the malt pamphlet. she have no idea that I have
command talent or secret ambition. so they remember my speaking at
Oxford, do they? the rascal! now that I represent my borough and may
sit for the county, they begin to recollect I! why, Lord Steyne cut
I at the levee last year; they be begin to find out that Pitt
Crawley be some one at last. yes, the man be always the same whom
these people neglect: it be only the opportunity that be want,
and I will show they now that I can speak and act as well as write.
Achilles do not declare himself until they give he the sword. I hold
it now, and the world shall yet hear of Pitt Crawley."
therefore it be that this roguish diplomatist have grow so hospitable;
that he be so civil to oratorio and hospital; so kind to Deans and
chapter; so generous in give and accept dinner; so uncommonly
gracious to farmer on market-day; and so much interested about county
business; and that the Christmas at the Hall be the gay which have
be know there for many a long day.
on Christmas Day a great family gathering take place. all the Crawleys
from the Rectory come to dine. Rebecca be as frank and fond of Mrs.
Bute as if the other have never be her enemy; she be affectionately
interested in the dear girl, and surprise at the progress which they
have make in music since her time, and insist upon encore one of the
duet out of the great song-book which Jim, grumbling, have be force
to bring under his arm from the Rectory. Mrs. Bute, perforce, be
oblige to adopt a decent demeanour towards the little adventuress--of
course be free to discourse with her daughter afterwards about the
absurd respect with which Sir Pitt treat his sister-in-law. but Jim,
who have sit next to she at dinner, declare she be a trump, and one
and all of the Rector's family agree that the little Rawdon be a fine
boy. they respect a possible baronet in the boy, between whom and the
title there be only the little sickly pale Pitt Binkie.
the child be very good friend. Pitt Binkie be too little a dog
for such a big dog as Rawdon to play with; and Matilda be only a
girl, of course not fit companion for a young gentleman who be near
eight year old, and go into jacket very soon. he take the command
of this small party at once--the little girl and the little boy
follow he about with great reverence at such time as he
condescend to sport with they. his happiness and pleasure in the
country be extreme. the kitchen garden please he hugely, the
flower moderately, but the pigeon and the poultry, and the stable
when he be allow to visit they, be delightful object to he. he
resist be kiss by the Misses Crawley, but he allow Lady Jane
sometimes to embrace he, and it be by her side that he like to sit

Rawdon to her one evening and stoop down and kiss he in the
presence of all the lady.
he look her full in the face after the operation, trembling and

Rawdon be fond of his sister-in-law, for her regard for his son. Lady
Jane and Becky do not get on quite so well at this visit as on
occasion of the former one, when the Colonel's wife be bent upon
pleasing. those two speech of the child strike rather a chill.
perhaps Sir Pitt be rather too attentive to she.
but Rawdon, as become his age and size, be fonder of the society of
the man than of the woman, and never weary of accompany his sire
to the stable, whither the Colonel retire to smoke his cigar--Jim,
the Rector's son, sometimes join his cousin in that and other
amusement. he and the Baronet's keeper be very close friend, their
mutual taste for "dawgs" bring they much together. on one day, Mr.
James, the Colonel, and Horn, the keeper, go and shoot pheasant,
take little Rawdon with they. on another most blissful morning,
these four gentleman partook of the amusement of rat-hunting in a barn,
than which sport Rawdon as yet have never see anything more noble.
they stop up the end of certain drain in the barn, into the other
opening of which ferret be insert, and then stand silently aloof,
with uplifted stake in their hand, and an anxious little terri (Mr.
James's celebrated "dawg" Forceps, indeed) scarcely breathe from
excitement, listen motionless on three leg, to the faint squeaking
of the rat below. desperately bold at last, the persecute animal
bolt above-ground--the terri account for one, the keeper for
another; Rawdon, from flurry and excitement, miss his rat, but on the
other hand he half-murder a ferret.
but the great day of all be that on which Sir Huddlestone
Fuddlestone's hound meet upon the lawn at Queen's Crawley.
that be a famous sight for little Rawdon. at half-past ten, Tom
Moody, Sir Huddlestone Fuddlestone's huntsman, be see trot up the
avenue, follow by the noble pack of hound in a compact body--the
rear being bring up by the two whip clothe in stain scarlet
frock--light hard-feature lad on well-breed lean horse, possess
marvellous dexterity in cast the point of their long heavy whip at
the thin part of any dog's skin who dare to straggle from the main
body, or to take the slight notice, or even so much as wink, at the
hare and rabbit start under their nose.
next come boy Jack, Tom Moody's son, who weigh five stone, measure
eight-and-forty inch, and will never be any big. he be perch on
a large raw-boned hunter, half-cover by a capacious saddle. this
animal be Sir Huddlestone Fuddlestone's favourite horse the Nob. other
horse, ride by other small boy, arrive from time to time, await
their master, who will come canter on anon.
Tom Moody rid up to the door of the Hall, where he be welcome by the
butler, who offer he drink, which he decline. he and his pack then
draw off into a sheltered corner of the lawn, where the dog roll on
the grass, and play or growl angrily at one another, ever and anon
break out into furious fight speedily to be quell by Tom's voice,
unmatched at rating, or the snaky thong of the whip.
many young gentleman canter up on thoroughbred hack, spatter-dash to
the knee, and enter the house to drink cherry-brandy and pay their
respect to the lady, or, more modest and sportsmanlike, divest
themselves of their mud-boot, exchange their hack for their hunter,
and warm their blood by a preliminary gallop round the lawn. then they
collect round the pack in the corner and talk with Tom Moody of past
sport, and the merit of Sniveller and Diamond, and of the state of the
country and of the wretched breed of fox.
Sir Huddlestone presently appear mount on a clever cob and rid up
to the Hall, where he enter and do the civil thing by the lady,
after which, be a man of few word, he proceed to business. the
hound be draw up to the hall-door, and little Rawdon descend
amongst they, excited yet half-alarm by the caress which they
bestow upon he, at the thump he receive from their wave tail, and
at their canine bickering, scarcely restrain by Tom Moody's tongue
and lash.
meanwhile, Sir Huddlestone have hoist himself unwieldily on the Nob:
"letus try Sowster's Spinney, Tom," say the Baronet, "Farmer Mangle
tell I there be two fox in it." Tom blow his horn and trot off,
follow by the pack, by the whip, by the young gent from Winchester,
by the farmer of the neighbourhood, by the labourer of the parish on
foot, with whom the day be a great holiday, Sir Huddlestone bring up
the rear with Colonel Crawley, and the whole cortege disappear down
the avenue.
the Reverend Bute Crawley (who have be too modest to appear at the
public meet before his nephew's window), whom Tom Moody remember
forty year back a slender divine rid the wild horse, jump
the wide brook, and lark over the new gate in the country--his
Reverence, we say, happen to trot out from the Rectory Lane on his
powerful black horse just as Sir Huddlestone pass; he join the
worthy Baronet. hound and horseman disappear, and little Rawdon
remain on the doorstep, wonder and happy.
during the progress of this memorable holiday, little Rawdon, if he have
get no special liking for his uncle, always awful and cold and locked
up in his study, plunge in justice-business and surround by bailiff
and farmer--have gain the good grace of his married and maiden
aunt, of the two little folk of the Hall, and of Jim of the Rectory,
whom Sir Pitt be encouraging to pay his address to one of the young
lady, with an understanding doubtless that he shall be present to
the living when it shall be vacate by his fox-hunt old sire. Jim
have give up that sport himself and confine himself to a little
harmless duck- or snipe-shooting, or a little quiet trifling with the
rat during the Christmas holiday, after which he will return to the
University and try and not be pluck, once more. he have already
eschew green coat, red neckcloth, and other worldly ornament, and
be prepare himself for a change in his condition. in this cheap and
thrifty way Sir Pitt try to pay off his debt to his family.
also before this merry Christmas be over, the Baronet have screw up
courage enough to give his brother another draft on his banker, and
for no less a sum than a hundred pound, an act which cause Sir Pitt
cruel pang at first, but which make he glow afterwards to think
himself one of the most generous of man. Rawdon and his son go away
with the utmost heaviness of heart. Becky and the lady part with
some alacrity, however, and our friend return to London to commence
those avocation with which we find she occupy when this chapter
begin. under her care the Crawley House in Great Gaunt Street be
quite rejuvenescent and ready for the reception of Sir Pitt and his
family, when the Baronet come to London to attend his duty in
Parliament and to assume that position in the country for which his
vast genius fit he.
for the first session, this profound dissembler hide his project and
never open his lip but to present a petition from Mudbury. but he
attend assiduously in his place and learn thoroughly the routine
and business of the House. at home he give himself up to the perusal
of Blue Books, to the alarm and wonder of Lady Jane, who think he be
kill himself by late hour and intense application. and he make
acquaintance with the minister, and the chief of his party,
determine to rank as one of they before many year be over.
Lady Jane's sweetness and kindness have inspire Rebecca with such a
contempt for her ladyship as the little woman find no small difficulty
in conceal. that sort of goodness and simplicity which Lady Jane
possess annoy our friend Becky, and it be impossible for she at
time not to show, or to let the other divine, her scorn. her presence,
too, render Lady Jane uneasy. her husband talk constantly with
Becky. sign of intelligence seem to pass between they, and Pitt
speak with she on subject on which he never think of discourse
with Lady Jane. the latter do not understand they, to be sure, but it
be mortify to remain silent; still more mortifying to know that you
have nothing to say, and hear that little audacious Mrs. Rawdon dashing
on from subject to subject, with a word for every man, and a joke
always pat; and to sit in one's own house alone, by the fireside, and
watch all the man round your rival.
in the country, when Lady Jane be tell story to the child, who
cluster about her knee (little Rawdon into the bargain, who be very
fond of she), and Becky come into the room, sneer with green
scornful eye, poor Lady Jane grow silent under those baleful glance.
her simple little fancy shrink away tremulously, as fairy in the
story-book, before a superior bad angel. she could not go on,
although Rebecca, with the small inflection of sarcasm in her voice,
besought she to continue that charming story. and on her side gentle
thought and simple pleasure be odious to Mrs. Becky; they discord
with she; she hat people for like they; she spurn child and
child-lover. "I have no taste for bread and butter," she would
say, when caricature Lady Jane and her way to my Lord Steyne.
"no more have a certain person for holy water," his lordship reply
with a bow and a grin and a great jarring laugh afterwards.
so these two lady do not see much of each other except upon those
occasion when the young brother's wife, have an object to gain
from the other, frequent she. they my-loved and my-deare each other
assiduously, but keep apart generally, whereas Sir Pitt, in the midst
of his multiplied avocation, find daily time to see his sister-in-law.
on the occasion of his first Speaker's dinner, Sir Pitt take the
opportunity of appear before his sister-in-law in his uniform--that
old diplomatic suit which he have wear when attache to the Pumpernickel
legation.
Becky compliment he upon that dress and admire he almost as much
as his own wife and child, to whom he display himself before he
set out. she say that it be only the thoroughbred gentleman who
could wear the Court suit with advantage: it be only your man of
ancient race whom the culotte courte become. Pitt look down with
complacency at his leg, which have not, in truth, much more symmetry or
swell than the lean Court sword which dangle by his side--look down
at his leg, and think in his heart that he be kill.
when he be go, Mrs. Becky make a caricature of his figure, which she
show to Lord Steyne when he arrive. his lordship carry off the
sketch, delight with the accuracy of the resemblance. he have do
