be most gracious to the new Baronet and member. Pitt be strike too
by the deference with which the great Peer treat his sister-in-law,
by her ease and sprightliness in the conversation, and by the delight
with which the other man of the party listen to her talk. Lord Steyne
make no doubt but that the Baronet have only commence his career in
public life, and expect rather anxiously to hear he as an orator; as
they be neighbour (for Great Gaunt Street lead into Gaunt Square,
whereof Gaunt House, as everybody know, form one side) my lord hop
that as soon as Lady Steyne arrive in London she would have the honour
of make the acquaintance of Lady Crawley. he leave a card upon his
neighbour in the course of a day or two, have never think fit to
notice his predecessor, though they have live near each other for near
a century past.
in the midst of these intrigue and fine party and wise and brilliant
personage Rawdon feel himself more and more isolated every day. he
be allow to go to the club more; to dine abroad with bachelor
friend; to come and go when he like, without any question be
ask. and he and Rawdon the young many a time would walk to Gaunt
Street and sit with the lady and the child there while Sir Pitt be
closet with Rebecca, on his way to the House, or on his return from
it.
the ex-Colonel would sit for hour in his brother's house very silent,
and think and do as little as possible. he be glad to be
employ of an errand; to go and make inquiry about a horse or a
servant, or to carve the roast mutton for the dinner of the child.
he be beat and cow into laziness and submission. Delilah have
imprison he and cut his hair off, too. the bold and reckless young
blood of ten-year back be subjugate and be turn into a torpid,
submissive, middle-aged, stout gentleman.
and poor Lady Jane be aware that Rebecca have captivate her husband,
although she and Mrs. Rawdon my-deare and my-love each other every
day they meet.
CHAPTER XLVI
Struggles and Trials
our friend at Brompton be meanwhile pass their Christmas after
their fashion and in a manner by no mean too cheerful.
out of the hundred pound a year, which be about the amount of her
income, the Widow Osborne have be in the habit of give up nearly
three-fourth to her father and mother, for the expense of herself and
her little boy. with #120 more, supply by Jos, this family of four
people, attend by a single irish servant who also do for Clapp and
his wife, might manage to live in decent comfort through the year, and
hold up their head yet, and be able to give a friend a dish of tea
still, after the storm and disappointment of their early life. Sedley
still maintain his ascendency over the family of Mr. Clapp, his
ex-clerk. Clapp remember the time when, sit on the edge of the
chair, he toss off a bumper to the health of "Mrs. S--, Miss Emmy,
and Mr. Joseph in India," at the merchant's rich table in Russell
Square. Time magnify the splendour of those recollection in the
honest clerk's bosom. whenever he come up from the kitchen-parlour to
the drawing-room and partook of tea or gin-and-water with Mr. Sedley,
he would say, "this be not what you be accustom to once, sir," and
as gravely and reverentially drink the health of the lady as he have
do in the day of their utmost prosperity. he think Miss 'Melia's
play the divinest music ever perform, and her the fine lady. he
never would sit down before Sedley at the club even, nor would he have
that gentleman's character abuse by any member of the society. he have
see the first man in London shake hand with Mr. S--; he say, "hewould
know he in time when Rothschild might be see on 'change with he
any day, and he owe he personally everythink."
Clapp, with the good of character and handwriting, have be able very
soon after his master's disaster to find other employment for himself.
"such a little fish as I can swim in any bucket," he use to remark,
and a member of the house from which old Sedley have secede be very
glad to make use of Mr. Clapp's service and to reward they with a
comfortable salary. in fine, all Sedley's wealthy friend have drop
off one by one, and this poor ex-dependent still remain faithfully
attach to he.
out of the small residue of her income which Amelia keep back for
herself, the widow have need of all the thrift and care possible in
order to enable she to keep she darling boy dress in such a manner as
become George Osborne's son, and to defray the expense of the little
school to which, after much misgiving and reluctance and many secret
pang and fear on her own part, she have be induce to send the lad.
she have sit up of night con lesson and spelling over crab
grammar and geography book in order to teach they to Georgy. she have
work even at the latin accidence, fondly hop that she might be
capable of instruct he in that language. to part with he all day,
to send he out to the mercy of a schoolmaster's cane and his
schoolfellow' roughness, be almost like wean he over again to
that weak mother, so tremulous and full of sensibility. he, for his
part, rush off to the school with the utmost happiness. he be
longing for the change. that childish gladness wound his mother, who
be herself so grieve to part with he. she would rather have have he
more sorry, she think, and then be deeply repentant within herself
for dare to be so selfish as to wish her own son to be unhappy.
georgy make great progress in the school, which be keep by a friend of
his mother's constant admirer, the Rev. Mr. Binny. he bring home
numberless prize and testimonial of ability. he tell his mother
countless story every night about his school-companion: and what a
fine fellow Lyons be, and what a sneak sniffin be, and how Steel's
father actually supply the meat for the establishment, whereas
Golding's mother come in a carriage to fetch he every Saturday, and
how neat have strap to his trowser--might he have straps?--and how
Bull Major be so strong (though only in Eutropius) that it be
believe he could lick the Usher, Mr. Ward, himself. so Amelia learn
to know every one of the boy in that school as well as Georgy himself,
and of night she use to help he in his exercise and puzzle she
little head over his lesson as eagerly as if she be herself go in
the morning into the presence of the master. once, after a certain
combat with Master Smith, George come home to his mother with a black
eye, and brag prodigiously to his parent and his delighted old
grandfather about his valour in the fight, in which, if the truth be
know he do not behave with particular heroism, and in which he
decidedly have the bad. but Amelia have never forgive that Smith to
this day, though he be now a peaceful apothecary near Leicester Square.
in these quiet labour and harmless care the gentle widow's life be
pass away, a silver hair or two mark the progress of time on her
head and a line deepen ever so little on her fair forehead. she
use to smile at these mark of time. "what matter it," she ask,
"for an old woman like I?" all she hop for be to live to see she
son great, famous, and glorious, as he deserve to be. she keep his
copy-book, his drawing, and composition, and show they about in
her little circle as if they be miracle of genius. she confide
some of these specimen to Miss Dobbin, to show they to Miss Osborne,
George's aunt, to show they to Mr. Osborne himself--to make that old
man repent of his cruelty and ill feeling towards he who be go.
all her husband's fault and foible she have bury in the grave with
he: she only remember the lover, who have marry she at all
sacrifice, the noble husband, so brave and beautiful, in whose arm
she have hang on the morning when he have go away to fight, and die
gloriously for his king. from heaven the hero must be smile down upon
that paragon of a boy whom he have leave to comfort and console she. we
have see how one of George's grandfather (Mr. Osborne), in his easy
chair in Russell Square, daily grow more violent and moody, and how his
daughter, with her fine carriage, and her fine horse, and her name on
half the public charity-list of the town, be a lonely, miserable,
persecute old maid. she think again and again of the beautiful
little boy, her brother's son, whom she have see. she long to be
allow to drive in the fine carriage to the house in which he live,
and she use to look out day after day as she take her solitary drive
in the park, in hope that she might see he. her sister, the banker's
lady, occasionally condescend to pay her old home and companion a
visit in Russell Square. she bring a couple of sickly child
attend by a prim nurse, and in a faint genteel giggle tone cackle
to her sister about her fine acquaintance, and how her little Frederick
be the image of Lord Claud Lollypop and her sweet Maria have be
notice by the Baroness as they be drive in their donkey-chaise at
Roehampton. she urge she to make her papa do something for the
darling. Frederick she have determine should go into the Guards; and
if they make an eld son of he (and Mr. Bullock be positively
ruin and pinch himself to death to buy land), how be the darling
girl to be provide for? "I expect you, dear," Mrs. Bullock would say,
"for of course my share of our Papa's property must go to the head of
the house, you know. Dear Rhoda McMull will disengage the whole of the
Castletoddy property as soon as poor dear Lord Castletoddy die, who be
quite epileptic; and little Macduff McMull will be Viscount
Castletoddy. both the Mr. Bludyers of Mincing Lane have settle their
fortune on Fanny Bludyer's little boy. my darling Frederick must
positively be an eld son; and--and do ask Papa to bring we back his
account in Lombard Street, will you, dear? it donot look well, his
go to Stumpy and Rowdy's." after which kind of speech, in which
fashion and the main chance be blend together, and after a kiss,
which be like the contact of an oyster--Mrs. Frederick Bullock would
gather her starch nursling and simper back into her carriage.
every visit which this leader of ton pay to her family be more
unlucky for she. her father pay more money into Stumpy and Rowdy's.
her patronage become more and more insufferable. the poor widow in the
little cottage at Brompton, guard her treasure there, little know
how eagerly some people covet it.
on that night when Jane Osborne have tell her father that she have see
his grandson, the old man have make she no reply, but he have show no
anger--and have bid her good-night on go himself to his room in
rather a kindly voice. and he must have meditate on what she say and
have make some inquiry of the Dobbin family regard her visit, for
a fortnight after it take place, he ask she where be her little
french watch and chain she use to wear?
"I buy it with my money, sir," she say in a great fright.
"go and order another like it, or a well if you can get it," say the
old gentleman and lapse again into silence.
of late the Misses Dobbin more than once repeat their entreaty to
Amelia, to allow George to visit they. his aunt have show she
inclination; perhaps his grandfather himself, they hint, might be
disposed to be reconcile to he. surely, Amelia could not refuse such
advantageous chance for the boy. nor could she, but she accede to
their overture with a very heavy and suspicious heart, be always
uneasy during the child's absence from she, and welcome he back as if
he be rescue out of some danger. he bring back money and toy, at
which the widow look with alarm and jealousy; she ask he always if
he have see any gentleman--"only old Sir William, who drive he about
in the four-wheel chaise, and Mr. Dobbin, who arrive on the
beautiful bay horse in the afternoon--in the green coat and pink
neck-cloth, with the gold-head whip, who promise to show he the
Tower of London and take he out with the Surrey hound." at last, he
say, "there be an old gentleman, with thick eyebrow, and a broad
hat, and large chain and seal." he come one day as the coachman be
lunge georgy round the lawn on the gray pony. "he look at I very
much. he shake very much. I say 'my name be Norval' after dinner.
my aunt begin to cry. she be always cry." such be George's report
on that night.
then Amelia know that the boy have see his grandfather; and look out
feverishly for a proposal which she be sure would follow, and which
come, in fact, in a few day afterwards. Mr. Osborne formally offer
to take the boy and make he heir to the fortune which he have intend
that his father should inherit. he would make Mrs. George Osborne an
allowance, such as to assure she a decent competency. if Mrs. George
Osborne propose to marry again, as Mr. O. hear be her intention, he
would not withdraw that allowance. but it must be understand that the
child would live entirely with his grandfather in Russell Square, or at
whatever other place Mr. O. should select, and that he would be
occasionally permit to see Mrs. George Osborne at her own residence.
this message be bring or read to she in a letter one day, when her
mother be from home and her father absent as usual in the City.
she be never see angry but twice or thrice in her life, and it be in
one of these mood that Mr. Osborne's attorney have the fortune to
behold she. she rise up tremble and flush very much as soon as,
after read the letter, Mr. Poe hand it to she, and she tear the
paper into a hundred fragment, which she trod on. "I marry again! I
take money to part from my child! who dare insult I by propose
such a thing? tell Mr. Osborne it be a cowardly letter, sir--a cowardly
letter--I will not answer it. I wish you good morning, sir--and she
bow I out of the room like a tragedy queen," say the lawyer who
tell the story.
her parent never remark her agitation on that day, and she never
tell they of the interview. they have their own affair to interest
they, affair which deeply interested this innocent and unconscious
lady. the old gentleman, her father, be always dabble in
speculation. we have see how the wine company and the coal company have
fail he. but, prowl about the City always eagerly and restlessly
still, he light upon some other scheme, of which he think so well
that he embark in it in spite of the remonstrance of Mr. Clapp, to
whom indeed he never dare to tell how far he have engage himself in
it. and as it be always Mr. Sedley's maxim not to talk about money
matter before woman, they have no inkling of the misfortune that be
in store for they until the unhappy old gentleman be force to make
gradual confession.
the bill of the little household, which have be settle weekly, first
fall into arrear. the remittance have not arrive from India, Mr.
Sedley tell his wife with a disturbed face. as she have pay her bill
very regularly hitherto, one or two of the tradesman to whom the poor
lady be oblige to go round ask for time be very angry at a delay
to which they be perfectly use from more irregular customer.
Emmy's contribution, pay over cheerfully without any question, keep
the little company in half-ration however. and the first six month
pass away pretty easily, old Sedley still keep up with the notion
that his share must rise and that all would be well.
no sixty pound, however, come to help the household at the end of the
half year, and it fall deeply and deep into trouble--Mrs. Sedley, who
be grow infirm and be much shake, remain silent or weep a great
deal with Mrs. Clapp in the kitchen. the butcher be particularly
surly, the grocer insolent: once or twice little georgy have grumble
about the dinner, and Amelia, who still would have be satisfied with
a slice of bread for her own dinner, could not but perceive that her
son be neglect and purchase little thing out of her private purse
to keep the boy in health.
at last they tell she, or tell she such a garbled story as people in
difficulty tell. one day, her own money have be receive, and
Amelia about to pay it over, she, who have keep an account of the money
expend by she, propose to keep a certain portion back out of her
dividend, have contract engagement for a new suit for Georgy.
then it come out that Jos's remittance be not pay, that the house
be in difficulty, which Amelia ought to have see before, her mother
say, but she care for nothing or nobody except Georgy. at this she
pass all her money across the table, without a word, to her mother,
and return to her room to cry her eye out. she have a great access of
sensibility too that day, when oblige to go and countermand the
clothe, the darling clothe on which she have set her heart for
Christmas Day, and the cut and fashion of which she have arrange in
many conversation with a small milliner, her friend.
hard of all, she have to break the matter to Georgy, who make a loud
outcry. everybody have new clothe at Christmas. the other would
laugh at he. he would have new clothe. she have promise they to
he. the poor widow have only kiss to give he. she darn the old
suit in tear. she cast about among her little ornament to see if she
could sell anything to procure the desire novelty. there be her
India shawl that Dobbin have send she. she remember in former day
go with her mother to a fine India shop on Ludgate Hill, where the
lady have all sort of dealing and bargain in these article. her
cheek flush and her eye shine with pleasure as she think of this
resource, and she kiss away George to school in the morning, smile
brightly after he. the boy feel that there be good news in her look.
pack up her shawl in a handkerchief (another of the gift of the
good Major), she hide they under her cloak and walk flushed and eager
all the way to Ludgate Hill, trip along by the park wall and
run over the crossing, so that many a man turn as she hurry by
he and look after her rosy pretty face. she calculate how she
should spend the proceed of her shawl--how, besides the clothe, she
would buy the book that he long for, and pay his half-year's
schooling; and how she would buy a cloak for her father instead of that
old great-coat which he wear. she be not mistake as to the value of
the Major's gift. it be a very fine and beautiful web, and the
merchant make a very good bargain when he give she twenty guineas for
her shawl.
she run on amazed and flurry with her rich to Darton's shop, in St.
Paul's Churchyard, and there purchase the Parents' Assistant and the
Sandford and Merton Georgy long for, and get into the coach there
with her parcel, and go home exulting. and she please herself by
writing in the fly-leaf in her neat little hand, "George Osborne, A
Christmas gift from his affectionate mother." the book be extant to
this day, with the fair delicate superscription.
she be go from her own room with the book in her hand to place
they on George's table, where he might find they on his return from
school, when in the passage, she and her mother meet. the gilt binding
of the seven handsome little volume catch the old lady's eye.
"what be those?" she say.
"some book for georgy," Amelia replied--"I--I promise they to he at
Christmas."
"book!" cry the eld lady indignantly, "book, when the whole house
want bread! book, when to keep you and your son in luxury, and your
dear father out of gaol, Ihave sell every trinket I have, the India shawl
from my back even down to the very spoon, that our tradesman mightnot
insult we, and that Mr. Clapp, which indeed he be justly entitle,
be not a hard landlord, and a civil man, and a father, might have
his rent. oh, Amelia! you break my heart with your book and that boy
of your, whom you be ruin, though part with he you will not. oh,
Amelia, may God send you a more dutiful child than I have have! therebe
Jos, desert his father in his old age; and therebe George, who might
be provide for, and who might be rich, go to school like a lord,
with a gold watch and chain round his neck--while my dear, dear old man
be without a sh--shilling." hysteric sob and cry end Mrs. Sedley's
speech--it echo through every room in the small house, whereof the
other female inmate hear every word of the colloquy.
"oh, Mother, Mother!" cry poor Amelia in reply. "you tell I
nothing--I--I promise he the book. I--I only sell my shawl this
morning. take the money--take everything"--and with quivering hand
she take out her silver, and her sovereign--her precious golden
sovereign, which she thrust into the hand of her mother, whence they
overflow and tumble, roll down the stair.
and then she go into her room, and sink down in despair and utter
misery. she see it all now. her selfishness be sacrifice the boy.
but for she he might have wealth, station, education, and his father's
place, which the eld George have forfeit for her sake. she have but
to speak the word, and her father be restore to competency and the
boy raise to fortune. oh, what a conviction it be to that tender and
stricken heart!
CHAPTER XLVII
Gaunt House
all the world know that Lord Steyne's town palace stand in Gaunt
Square, out of which Great Gaunt Street lead, whither we first
conduct Rebecca, in the time of the depart Sir Pitt Crawley.
peer over the railing and through the black tree into the garden
of the Square, you see a few miserable governess with wan-face
pupil wander round and round it, and round the dreary grass-plot in
the centre of which rise the statue of Lord Gaunt, who fight at
Minden, in a three-tail wig, and otherwise habit like a Roman
Emperor. Gaunt House occupy nearly a side of the Square. the
remain three side be compose of mansion that have pass away
into dowagerism--tall, dark house, with window-frame of stone, or
pick out of a light red. little light seem to be behind those
lean, comfortless casement now, and hospitality to have pass away
from those door as much as the laced lacquey and link-boy of old
time, who use to put out their torch in the blank iron
extinguisher that still flank the lamp over the step. brass plate
have penetrate into the square--doctor, the Diddlesex Bank Western
Branch--the English and European Reunion, &c.--it have a dreary
look--nor be my Lord Steyne's palace less dreary. all I have ever see
of it be the vast wall in front, with the rustic column at the great
gate, through which an old porter peer sometimes with a fat and gloomy
red face--and over the wall the garret and bedroom window, and the
chimney, out of which there seldom come any smoke now. for the
present Lord Steyne live at Naples, prefer the view of the Bay and
Capri and Vesuvius to the dreary aspect of the wall in Gaunt Square.
a few score yard down New Gaunt Street, and lead into Gaunt Mews
indeed, be a little modest back door, which you would not remark from
that of any of the other stable. but many a little close carriage have
stop at that door, as my informant (little Tom Eaves, who know
everything, and who show I the place) tell I. "the Prince and
Perdita have be in and out of that door, sir," he have often tell I;
"Marianne Clarke have enter it with the Duke of ------. it conduct to
the famous petit appartement of Lord Steyne--one, sir, fit up all
in ivory and white satin, another in ebony and black velvet; there be a
little banqueting-room take from Sallust's house at Pompeii, and
paint by Cosway--a little private kitchen, in which every saucepan
be silver and all the spit be gold. it be there that Egalite
Orleans roasted partridge on the night when he and the Marquis of
Steyne win a hundred thousand from a great personage at ombre. half of
the money go to the French Revolution, half to purchase Lord Gaunt's
Marquisate and Garter--and the remainder--" but it form no part of our
scheme to tell what become of the remainder, for every shilling of
which, and a great deal more, little Tom Eaves, who know everybody's
affair, be ready to account.
besides his town palace, the Marquis have castle and palace in various
quarter of the three kingdom, whereof the description may be find
in the road-book--Castle Strongbow, with its wood, on the Shannon
shore; Gaunt Castle, in Carmarthenshire, where Richard II be take
prisoner--Gauntly Hall in Yorkshire, where I have be inform there
be two hundred silver teapot for the breakfast of the guest of the
house, with everything to correspond in splendour; and Stillbrook in
Hampshire, which be my lord's farm, an humble place of residence, of
which we all remember the wonderful furniture which be sell at my
lord's demise by a late celebrate auctioneer.
the Marchioness of Steyne be of the renowned and ancient family of the
Caerlyons, Marquises of Camelot, who have preserve the old faith ever
since the conversion of the venerable Druid, their first ancestor, and
whose pedigree go far beyond the date of the arrival of King Brute in
these island. Pendragon be the title of the eld son of the house.
the son have be call arthur, Uthers, and Caradocs, from
immemorial time. their head have fall in many a loyal conspiracy.
Elizabeth chop off the head of the Arthur of her day, who have be
Chamberlain to Philip and Mary, and carry letter between the Queen
of scot and her uncle the Guises. a cadet of the house be an
officer of the great Duke and distinguish in the famous Saint
Bartholomew conspiracy. during the whole of Mary's confinement, the
house of Camelot conspire in her behalf. it be as much injure by its
charge in fit out an armament against the Spaniards, during the
time of the Armada, as by the fine and confiscation levy on it by
Elizabeth for harbouring of priest, obstinate recusancy, and popish
misdoing. a recreant of James's time be momentarily pervert from
his religion by the argument of that great theologian, and the
fortune of the family somewhat restore by his timely weakness. but
the Earl of Camelot, of the reign of Charles, return to the old creed
of his family, and they continue to fight for it, and ruin themselves
for it, as long as there be a Stuart leave to head or to instigate a
rebellion.
Lady Mary Caerlyon be bring up at a parisian convent; the Dauphiness
Marie Antoinette be her godmother. in the pride of her beauty she have
be marry--sell, it be say--to Lord Gaunt, then at Paris, who win
vast sum from the lady's brother at some of Philip of Orleans's
banquet. the Earl of Gaunt's famous duel with the Count de la Marche,
of the Grey Musqueteers, be attribute by common report to the
pretension of that officer (who have be a page, and remain a
favourite of the Queen) to the hand of the beautiful Lady Mary
Caerlyon. she be married to Lord Gaunt while the Count lie ill of his
wound, and come to dwell at Gaunt House, and to figure for a short time
in the splendid Court of the Prince of Wales. Fox have toast she.
Morris and Sheridan have write song about she. Malmesbury have make
she his good bow; Walpole have pronounce her charming; Devonshire have
be almost jealous of she; but she be scar by the wild pleasure
and gaiety of the society into which she be flung, and after she have
bear a couple of son, shrink away into a life of devout seclusion.
no wonder that my Lord Steyne, who like pleasure and cheerfulness, be
not often see after their marriage by the side of this trembling,
silent, superstitious, unhappy lady.
the before-mention Tom Eaves (who have no part in this history, except
that he know all the great folk in London, and the story and
mystery of each family) have further information regard my Lady
Steyne, which may or may not be true. "the humiliation," Tom use to
say, "which that woman have be make to undergo, in her own house, have
be frightful; Lord Steyne have make she sit down to table with woman
with whom I would rather die than allow Mrs. Eaves to associate--with
Lady Crackenbury, with Mrs. Chippenham, with Madame de la Cruchecassee,
the french secretary's wife (from every one of which ladie Tom
eave--who would have sacrifice his wife for know they--be too
glad to get a bow or a dinner) with the reigning FAVOURITE in a word.
and do you suppose that that woman, of that family, who be as proud as
the Bourbons, and to whom the Steynes be but lackey, mushroom of
yesterday (for after all, they be not of the Old Gaunts, but of a
minor and doubtful branch of the house); do you suppose, I say (the
reader must bear in mind that it be always Tom Eaves who speak) that
the Marchioness of Steyne, the haughty woman in England, would bend
down to her husband so submissively if there be not some cause? Pooh!
I tell you there be secret reason. I tell you that, in the
emigration, the Abbe de la Marche who be here and be employ in the
Quiberoon business with Puisaye and Tinteniac, be the same Colonel of
Mousquetaires Gris with whom Steyne fight in the year '86--that he and
the Marchioness meet again--that it be after the Reverend Colonel be
shoot in Brittany that Lady Steyne take to those extreme practice of
devotion which she carry on now; for she be closet with her
director every day--she be at service at Spanish Place, every morning,
Ihave watch she there--that be, Ihave happen to be pass there--and
depend on it, therebe a mystery in her case. People be not so unhappy
unless they have something to repent of," add Tom Eaves with a
know wag of his head; "and depend on it, that woman would not be so
submissive as she be if the Marquis have not some sword to hold over
she."
so, if Mr. Eaves's information be correct, it be very likely that this
lady, in her high station, have to submit to many a private indignity
and to hide many secret grief under a calm face. and let we, my
brother who have not our name in the Red Book, console ourselves by
think comfortably how miserable our better may be, and that
Damocles, who sit on satin cushion and be serve on gold plate, have
an awful sword hang over his head in the shape of a bailiff, or an
hereditary disease, or a family secret, which peep out every now and
then from the embroider arra in a ghastly manner, and will be sure
to drop one day or the other in the right place.
in compare, too, the poor man's situation with that of the great,
there be (always accord to Mr. Eaves) another source of comfort for
the former. you who have little or no patrimony to bequeath or to
inherit, may be on good term with your father or your son, whereas the
heir of a great prince, such as my Lord Steyne, must naturally be angry
at be keep out of his kingdom, and eye the occupant of it with no
very agreeable glance. "take it as a rule," this sardonic old lave
would say, "the father and eld son of all great family hate each
other. the Crown Prince be always in opposition to the crown or
hankering after it. Shakespeare know the world, my good sir, and when
he describe Prince Hal (from whose family the Gaunts pretend to be
descend, though they be no more related to John of Gaunt than you
be) try on his father's coronet, he give you a natural description
of all heir apparent. if you be heir to a dukedom and a thousand
pound a day, do you mean to say you would not wish for possession?
Pooh! and it stand to reason that every great man, have experience
this feeling towards his father, must be aware that his son entertain
it towards himself; and so they cannot but be suspicious and hostile.
"then again, as to the feeling of elder towards young son. my dear
sir, you ought to know that every eld brother look upon the cadet
of the house as his natural enemy, who deprive he of so much ready
money which ought to be his by right. I have often hear George Mac
Turk, Lord Bajazet's eld son, say that if he have his will when he
come to the title, he would do what the sultan do, and clear the
estate by chop off all his young brother' head at once; and so
the case be, more or less, with they all. I tell you they be all
Turks in their heart. Pooh! sir, they know the world." and here,
haply, a great man come up, Tom Eaves's hat would drop off his head,
and he would rush forward with a bow and a grin, which show that he
know the world too--in the tomeavesian way, that be. and have lay
out every shilling of his fortune on an annuity, Tom could afford to
bear no malice to his nephews and niece, and to have no other feeling
with regard to his better but a constant and generous desire to dine
with they.
between the Marchioness and the natural and tender regard of mother for
child, there be that cruel barrier place of difference of faith.
the very love which she might feel for her son only serve to render
the timid and pious lady more fearful and unhappy. the gulf which
separate they be fatal and impassable. she could not stretch her
weak arm across it, or draw her child over to that side away from
which her belief tell she there be no safety. during the youth of his
son, Lord Steyne, who be a good scholar and amateur casuist, have no
well sport in the evening after dinner in the country than in set
the boy' tutor, the Reverend Mr. Trail (now my Lord Bishop of Ealing)
on her ladyship's director, Father Mole, over their wine, and in
pit Oxford against St. Acheul. he cry "Bravo, Latimer! well
say, Loyola!" alternately; he promise Mole a bishopric if he would
come over, and vow he would use all his influence to get Trail a
cardinal's hat if he would secede. neither divine allow himself to
be conquer, and though the fond mother hop that her young and
favourite son would be reconcile to her church--his mother church--a
sad and awful disappointment await the devout lady--a disappointment
which seem to be a judgement upon she for the sin of her marriage.
my Lord Gaunt marry, as every person who frequent the peerage know,
the Lady Blanche Thistlewood, a daughter of the noble house of
Bareacres, before mention in this veracious history. a wing of Gaunt
House be assign to this couple; for the head of the family choose to
govern it, and while he reign to reign supreme; his son and heir,
however, live little at home, disagree with his wife, and
borrow upon post-obits such money as he require beyond the very
moderate sum which his father be disposed to allow he. the Marquis
know every shilling of his son's debt. at his lamented demise, he be
find himself to be possessor of many of his heir's bond, purchase
for their benefit, and devise by his Lordship to the child of his
young son.
as, to my Lord Gaunt's dismay, and the chuckle delight of his natural
enemy and father, the Lady Gaunt have no child--the Lord George Gaunt
be desire to return from Vienna, where he be engage in waltzing and
diplomacy, and to contract a matrimonial alliance with the Honourable
Joan, only daughter of John Johnes, First Baron Helvellyn, and head of
the firm of Jones, Brown, and Robinson, of Threadneedle Street,
Bankers; from which union spring several son and daughter, whose
doing do not appertain to this story.
the marriage at first be a happy and prosperous one. my Lord George
Gaunt could not only read, but write pretty correctly. he speak French
with considerable fluency; and be one of the fine waltzer in
Europe. with these talent, and his interest at home, there be little
doubt that his lordship would rise to the high dignity in his
profession. the lady, his wife, feel that court be her sphere, and
her wealth enable she to receive splendidly in those continental town
whither her husband's diplomatic duty lead he. there be talk of
appoint he minister, and bet be lay at the Travellers' that he
would be ambassador ere long, when of a sudden, rumour arrive of the
secretary's extraordinary behaviour. at a grand diplomatic dinner give
by his chief, he have start up and declare that a pate de foie gra
be poison. he go to a ball at the hotel of the bavarian envoy,
the Count de Springbock-Hohenlaufen, with his head shave and dress
as a Capuchin friar. it be not a masked ball, as some folk want to
persuade you. it be something queer, people whisper. his
grandfather be so. it be in the family.
his wife and family return to this country and take up their abode at
Gaunt House. Lord George give up his post on the european continent,
and be gazette to Brazil. but people know well; he never return
from that Brazil expedition--never die there--never live there--never
be there at all. he be nowhere; he be go out altogether.
"Brazil," say one gossip to another, with a grin--"Brazil be St.
John's Wood. Rio de Janeiro be a cottage surround by four wall, and
George Gaunt be accredit to a keeper, who have invest he with the
order of the Strait-Waistcoat." these be the kind of epitaph which
man pass over one another in Vanity Fair.
twice or thrice in a week, in the early morning, the poor mother
go for her sin and see the poor invalid. sometimes he laugh at she
(and his laughter be more pitiful than to hear he cry); sometimes she
find the brilliant dandy diplomatist of the Congress of Vienna
drag about a child's toy, or nurse the keeper's baby's doll.
sometimes he know she and Father Mole, her director and companion;
oftener he forget she, as he have do wife, child, love, ambition,
vanity. but he remember his dinner-hour, and use to cry if his
wine-and-water be not strong enough.
it be the mysterious taint of the blood; the poor mother have bring
it from her own ancient race. the evil have break out once or twice in
the father's family, long before Lady Steyne's sin have begin, or her
fast and tear and penance have be offer in their expiation. the
pride of the race be strike down as the first-bear of Pharaoh. the
dark mark of fate and doom be on the threshold--the tall old
threshold surmount by coronet and can heraldry.
the absent lord's child meanwhile prattle and grow on quite
unconscious that the doom be over they too. first they talk of
their father and devise plan against his return. then the name of
the live dead man be less frequently in their mouth--then not
mention at all. but the stricken old grandmother tremble to think
that these too be the inheritor of their father's shame as well as
of his honour, and watch sicken for the day when the awful
ancestral curse should come down on they.
this dark presentiment also haunt Lord Steyne. he try to lay the
horrid bedside ghost in Red Seas of wine and jollity, and lose sight of
it sometimes in the crowd and rout of his pleasure. but it always
come back to he when alone, and seem to grow more threatening with
year. "I have take your son," it say, "why not you? I may shut you
up in a prison some day like your son George. I may tap you on the
head to-morrow, and away go pleasure and honour, feast and beauty,
friend, flatterer, french cook, fine horse and house--in exchange
for a prison, a keeper, and a straw mattress like George Gaunt's." and
then my lord would defy the ghost which threaten he, for he know of
a remedy by which he could baulk his enemy.
so there be splendour and wealth, but no great happiness perchance,
behind the tall can portal of Gaunt House with its smoky coronet
and cipher. the feast there be of the grand in London, but
there be not overmuch content therewith, except among the guest who
sit at my lord's table. have he not be so great a Prince very few
possibly would have visit he; but in Vanity Fair the sin of very
great personage be look at indulgently. "nous regardon a deux
fois" (as the french lady say) before we condemn a person of my lord's
undoubted quality. some notorious carper and squeamish moralist
might be sulky with Lord Steyne, but they be glad enough to come when
he ask they.
"Lord Steyne be really too bad," Lady Slingstone say, "but everybody
go, and of course I shall see that my girl come to no harm." "his
lordship be a man to whom I owe much, everything in life," say the
Right Reverend Doctor Trail, think that the Archbishop be rather
shaky, and Mrs. Trail and the young lady would as soon have miss

bad," say little Lord Southdown to his sister, who meekly
expostulate, have hear terrific legend from her mamma with respect
to the doing at Gaunt House; "but hang it, hebe get the well dry
Sillery in Europe!" and as for Sir Pitt Crawley, bart.--sir Pitt that
pattern of decorum, Sir Pitt who have lead off at missionary meeting--he
never for one moment thought of not go too. "where you see such
person as the Bishop of Ealing and the Countess of Slingstone, you may
be pretty sure, Jane," the Baronet would say, "that we cannot be wrong.
the great rank and station of Lord Steyne put he in a position to
command people in our station in life. the Lord Lieutenant of a
County, my dear, be a respectable man. besides, George Gaunt and I
be intimate in early life; he be my junior when we be attache at
Pumpernickel together."
in a word everybody go to wait upon this great man--everybody who be
ask, as you the reader (do not say nay) or I the writer hereof would
go if we have an invitation.
CHAPTER XLVIII
in which the reader be introduce to the Very Best of company
at last Becky's kindness and attention to the chief of her husband's

which, though certainly somewhat unsubstantial, the little woman
covet with great eagerness than more positive benefit. if she do
not wish to lead a virtuous life, at least she desire to enjoy a
character for virtue, and we know that no lady in the genteel world can
possess this desideratum, until she have put on a train and feather and
have be present to her Sovereign at Court. from that august
interview they come out stamp as honest woman. the Lord Chamberlain
give they a certificate of virtue. and as dubious good or letter
be pass through an oven at quarantine, sprinkle with aromatic
vinegar, and then pronounce clean, many a lady, whose reputation would
be doubtful otherwise and liable to give infection, pass through the
wholesome ordeal of the royal presence and issue from it free from all
taint.
it might be very well for my Lady Bareacres, my Lady Tufto, Mrs. Bute
Crawley in the country, and other lady who have come into contact with
Mrs. Rawdon Crawley to cry fie at the idea of the odious little
adventuress make her curtsey before the Sovereign, and to declare
that, if dear good Queen Charlotte have be alive, she never would have
admit such an extremely ill-regulate personage into her chaste

Europe in whose high presence Mrs. Rawdon pass her examination, and
as it be, take her degree in reputation, it surely must be flat
disloyalty to doubt any more about her virtue. I, for my part, look
back with love and awe to that Great Character in history. ah, what a
high and noble appreciation of Gentlewomanhood there must have be in
Vanity Fair, when that revere and august be be invest, by the
universal acclaim of the refined and educate portion of this empire,
with the title of Premier Gentilhomme of his Kingdom. do you remember,
dear m--, oh friend of my youth, how one blissful night five-and-twenty
year since, the "Hypocrite" be act, Elliston be manager,

before the august box; the Marquis of Steyne (Lord of the Powder
Closet) and other great officer of state be behind the chair on

order, and in a rich curl head of hair--how we sing God save he!
how the house rock and shout with that magnificent music. how they
cheer, and cry, and wave handkerchief. lady weep; mother
clasp their child; some faint with emotion. People be
suffocate in the pit, shriek and groan rise up amidst the writhing
and shout mass there of his people who be, and indeed show

cannot deprive we of that. other have see Napoleon. some few still
exist who have behold Frederick the Great, Doctor Johnson, Marie
Antoinette, &c.--be it our reasonable boast to our child, that we
see George the Good, the Magnificent, the great.
well, there come a happy day in Mrs. Rawdon Crawley's existence when
this angel be admit into the paradise of a court which she covet,
her sister-in-law act as her godmother. on the appoint day, Sir
Pitt and his lady, in their great family carriage (just newly build,
and ready for the Baronet's assumption of the office of High Sheriff of

Sir Pitt, in a glitter uniform, descend and go into Curzon

against the parlour window-pane, smile and nod with all his

magnificent brocade. she step into the vehicle as if she be a

then Rawdon follow in his old Guards' uniform, which have grow

the coach be large, the lady not very big, they would hold their

noble and gentlefolk.
Becky feel as if she could bless the people out of the carriage

Becky have her weakness, and as one often see how man pride
themselves upon excellence which other be slow to perceive: how, for
instance, Comus firmly believe that he be the great tragic actor in
England; how Brown, the famous novelist, long to be consider, not a
man of genius, but a man of fashion; while Robinson, the great lawyer,
do not in the least care about his reputation in Westminster Hall,
but believe himself incomparable across country and at a five-bar

in life, and she get up the genteel with amazing assiduity, readiness,
and success. we have say, there be time when she believe herself
to be a fine lady and forget that there be no money in the chest at

walk upon, in a word. and as she go to Court in the carriage, the

deliberate, and impose that it make even Lady Jane laugh. she walk

befit an empress, and I have no doubt have she be one, she would
have become the character perfectly.
we be authorize to state that Mrs. Rawdon Crawley's costume de cour
on the occasion of her presentation to the Sovereign be of the most
elegant and brilliant description. some lady we may have see--we
who wear star and cordon and attend the St. James's assembly, or

our beloved Rebecca have no need, however, of any such a friendly halo

she do not know how much care, thought, and genius Mrs. Rawdon have

the diamond, which have create Rawdon's admiration, never go back to

what be the circumstance of the interview between Rebecca Crawley,

the particular of Becky's costume be in the newspaper--feather,

my Lord Steyne come to call a couple of hour afterwards, and look

in the kitchen make a pudding."
"I know you be, I see you through the area-railing as I drive up,"

"you see everything," she reply.
"a few thing, but not that, my pretty lady," he say good-naturedly.

that even tear will not disturb it.
"well," say the old gentleman, twiddle round his wife's card, "you
be bent on become a fine lady. you pester my poor old life out to
get you into the world. you willnot be able to hold your own there, you
silly little fool. youhave get no money."
"you will get we a place," interpose Becky, "as quick as possible."
"youhave get no money, and you want to compete with those who have. you
poor little earthenware pipkin, you want to swim down the stream along
with the great copper kettle. all woman be alike. everybody be

aux femme, look out and hold your own! how the woman will bully you!"
this be a very long speech for a man of few word like my Lord Steyne;

Briggs look up from the work-table at which she be seat in the

"if you donot turn off that abominable sheep-dog," say Lord Steyne,

"I always give my dog dinner from my own plate," say Rebecca, laugh
mischievously; and have enjoy for some time the discomfiture of my

"I cannot send she away," Becky say presently, after a pause, and in a
very sad voice. her eye fill with tear as she speak, and she
turn away her head.
"you owe she her wage, I suppose?" say the Peer.
"bad than that," say Becky, still cast down her eye; "I have
ruin she."
"ruin she? then why donot you turn she out?" the gentleman ask.
"man do that," Becky answer bitterly. "woman be not so bad as you.
last year, when we be reduce to our last guinea, she give we
everything. she shall never leave I, until we be ruin utterly
ourselves, which do not seem far off, or until I can pay she the
utmost farthing."
"------ it, how much be it?" say the Peer with an oath. and Becky,
reflect on the largeness of his mean, mention not only the sum
which she have borrow from Miss Briggs, but one of nearly double the
amount.
this cause the Lord Steyne to break out in another brief and energetic
expression of anger, at which Rebecca hold down her head the more and
cry bitterly. "I could not help it. it be my only chance. I dare
not tell my husband. he would kill I if I tell he what I have do.
I have keep it a secret from everybody but you--and you force it from
I. ah, what shall I do, Lord Steyne? for I be very, very unhappy!"
Lord Steyne make no reply except by beat the devil's tattoo and
bite his nail. at last he clap his hat on his head and fling out
of the room. rebecca do not rise from her attitude of misery until
the door slam upon he and his carriage whirl away. then she rise
up with the queer expression of victorious mischief glitter in
her green eye. she burst out laugh once or twice to herself, as
she sit at work, and sit down to the piano, she rattle away a
triumphant voluntary on the key, which make the people pause under her
window to listen to her brilliant music.
that night, there come two note from Gaunt House for the little woman,
the one contain a card of invitation from Lord and Lady Steyne to a

gray paper bear Lord Steyne's signature and the address of Messrs.
Jones, Brown, and Robinson, Lombard Street.
Rawdon hear Becky laugh in the night once or twice. it be only

say, which amuse she so. but the truth be that she be occupy
with a great number of other thought. should she pay off old Briggs
and give she her conge? should she astonish Raggles by settle his
account? she turn over all these thought on her pillow, and on the
next day, when Rawdon go out to pay his morning visit to the Club,
Mrs. Crawley (in a modest dress with a veil on) whip off in a
hackney-coach to the City: and be land at Messrs. Jones and
Robinson's bank, present a document there to the authority at the
desk, who, in reply, ask she "how she would take it?"
she gently say "she would take a hundred and fifty pound in small
note and the remainder in one note": and pass through St. Paul's
Churchyard stop there and buy the handsome black silk gown for
Briggs which money could buy; and which, with a kiss and the kind
speech, she present to the simple old spinster.
then she walk to Mr. Raggles, inquire about his child

forthcoming." it appear there have be a difference on the last

these arrangement conclude, Becky pay a visit upstairs to the

thing--in which private museum she place the one note which Messrs.
Jones and Robinson's cashier have give she.
CHAPTER XLIX
in which we enjoy three Courses and a dessert
when the lady of Gaunt House be at breakfast that morning, Lord
Steyne (who take his chocolate in private and seldom disturb the
female of his household, or see they except upon public day, or when
they cross each other in the hall, or when from his pit-box at the
opera he survey they in their box on the grand tier) his lordship, we
say, appear among the lady and the child who be assemble over
the tea and toast, and a battle royal ensue apropos of Rebecca.
"my Lady Steyne," he say, "I want to see the list for your dinner on
Friday; and I want you, if you please, to write a card for Colonel and
Mrs. Crawley."
"Blanche write they," Lady Steyne say in a flutter. "Lady Gaunt
write they."
"I will not write to that person," Lady Gaunt say, a tall and stately
lady, who look up for an instant and then down again after she have
speak. it be not good to meet Lord Steyne's eye for those who have
offend he.
"send the child out of the room. go!" say he pull at the
bell-rope. the urchin, always frighten before he, retire: their
mother would have follow too. "not you," he say. "you stop."
"my Lady Steyne," he say, "once more will you have the goodness to go
to the desk and write that card for your dinner on Friday?"
"my Lord, I will not be present at it," Lady Gaunt say; "I will go
home."
"I wish you would, and stay there. you will find the bailiff at
Bareacres very pleasant company, and I shall be free from lending
money to your relation and from your own damned tragedy air. who be
you to give order here? you have no money. youhave get no brain. you
be here to have child, and you have not have any. Gaunt's tired of
you, and George's wife be the only person in the family who donot
wish you be dead. Gaunt would marry again if you be."
"I wish I be," her ladyship answer with tear and rage in her eye.
"you, forsooth, must give yourself air of virtue, while my wife, who
be an immaculate saint, as everybody know, and never do wrong in her
life, have no objection to meet my young friend Mrs. Crawley. my Lady
Steyne know that appearance be sometimes against the good of woman;
that lie be often tell about the most innocent of they. pray, madam,
shall I tell you some little anecdote about my Lady Bareacres, your
mamma?"
"you may strike I if you like, sir, or hit any cruel blow," Lady Gaunt
say. to see his wife and daughter suffer always put his lordship
into a good humour.
"my sweet blanche," he say, "I be a gentleman, and never lie my hand
upon a woman, save in the way of kindness. I only wish to correct
little fault in your character. you woman be too proud, and sadly
lack humility, as Father Mole, Ibe sure, would tell my Lady Steyne if
he be here. you mustnot give yourselves air; you must be meek and
humble, my blessing. for all Lady Steyne know, this calumniate,
simple, good-humour Mrs. Crawley be quite innocent--even more
innocent than herself. her husband's character be not good, but it be
as good as Bareacres', who have play a little and not pay a great
deal, who cheat you out of the only legacy you ever have and leave you
a pauper on my hand. and Mrs. Crawley be not very well-bear, but she
be not bad than Fanny's illustrious ancestor, the first de la Jones."
"the money which I bring into the family, sir," Lady George cry
out--
"you purchase a contingent reversion with it," the Marquis say
darkly. "if Gaunt die, your husband may come to his honour; your
little boy may inherit they, and who know what besides? in the
meanwhile, lady, be as proud and virtuous as you like abroad, but
donot give I any air. as for Mrs. Crawley's character, I shallnot
demean myself or that most spotless and perfectly irreproachable lady
by even hint that it require a defence. you will be pleased to
receive she with the utmost cordiality, as you will receive all person
whom I present in this house. this house?" he break out with a laugh.
"who be the master of it? and what be it? this Temple of Virtue belong
to I. and if I invite all newgate or all Bedlam here, by ------ they
shall be welcome."
after this vigorous allocution, to one of which sort Lord Steyne
treat his "Hareem" whenever symptom of insubordination appear in
his household, the crestfallen woman have nothing for it but to obey.
Lady Gaunt write the invitation which his Lordship require, and she
and her mother-in-law drive in person, and with bitter and humiliate
heart, to leave the card on Mrs. Rawdon, the reception of which
cause that innocent woman so much pleasure.
there be family in London who would have sacrifice a year's income
to receive such an honour at the hand of those great lady. Mrs.
Frederick Bullock, for instance, would have go on her knee from May
Fair to Lombard Street, if Lady Steyne and Lady Gaunt have be wait
in the City to raise she up and say, "come to we next friday"--not to
one of the great crush and grand ball of Gaunt House, whither
everybody go, but to the sacred, unapproachable, mysterious,
delicious entertainment, to be admit to one of which be a
privilege, and an honour, and a blessing indeed.
severe, spotless, and beautiful, Lady Gaunt hold the very high rank
in Vanity Fair. the distinguished courtesy with which Lord Steyne
treat her charmed everybody who witness his behaviour, cause the
severe critic to admit how perfect a gentleman he be, and to own
that his Lordship's heart at least be in the right place.
the lady of Gaunt House call Lady Bareacres in to their aid, in
order to repulse the common enemy. one of Lady Gaunt's carriage go
to Hill Street for her ladyship's mother, all whose equipage be in
the hand of the bailiff, whose very jewel and wardrobe, it be say,
have be seize by those inexorable Israelites. Bareacres Castle be
theirs, too, with all its costly picture, furniture, and article of
vertu--the magnificent vandyke; the noble Reynolds picture; the
Lawrence portrait, tawdry and beautiful, and, thirty year ago, deem
as precious as work of real genius; the matchless Dancing Nymph of
Canova, for which Lady Bareacres have sit in her youth--Lady Bareacres
splendid then, and radiant in wealth, rank, and beauty--a toothless,
bald, old woman now--a mere rag of a former robe of state. her lord,
paint at the same time by Lawrence, as wave his sabre in front of
Bareacres Castle, and clothe in his uniform as Colonel of the
Thistlewood Yeomanry, be a withered, old, lean man in a greatcoat and
a Brutus wig, slink about Gray's Inn of morning chiefly and dining
alone at club. he do not like to dine with Steyne now. they have run
race of pleasure together in youth when Bareacres be the winner. but
Steyne have more bottom than he and have last he out. the Marquis be
ten time a great man now than the young Lord Gaunt of '85, and
Bareacres nowhere in the race--old, beaten, bankrupt, and break down.
he have borrow too much money of Steyne to find it pleasant to meet
his old comrade often. the latter, whenever he wish to be merry,
use jeeringly to ask Lady Gaunt why her father have not come to see
she. "he have not be here for four month," Lord Steyne would say. "I
can always tell by my cheque-book afterwards, when I get a visit from
Bareacres. what a comfort it be, my lady, I bank with one of my
son' father-in-law, and the other bank with I!"
of the other illustrious person whom Becky have the honour to encounter
on this her first presentation to the grand world, it do not become
the present historian to say much. there be his Excellency the Prince
of Peterwaradin, with his Princess--a nobleman tightly girth, with a
large military chest, on which the plaque of his order shine
magnificently, and wear the red collar of the Golden Fleece round
his neck. he be the owner of countless flock. "look at his face. I
think he must be descend from a sheep," Becky whisper to Lord
Steyne. indeed, his Excellency's countenance, long, solemn, and white,
with the ornament round his neck, bear some resemblance to that of a
venerable bell-wether.
there be Mr. John Paul Jefferson Jones, titularly attach to the
American Embassy and correspondent of the New York Demagogue, who, by
way of make himself agreeable to the company, ask Lady Steyne,
during a pause in the conversation at dinner, how his dear friend,
George Gaunt, like the Brazils? he and George have be most intimate
at Naples and have go up Vesuvius together. Mr. Jones write a full
and particular account of the dinner, which appear duly in the
Demagogue. he mention the name and title of all the guest, give
biographical sketch of the principal people. he describe the
person of the lady with great eloquence; the service of the table;
the size and costume of the servant; enumerate the dish and wine
serve; the ornament of the sideboard; and the probable value of the
plate. such a dinner he calculate could not be dish up under
fifteen or eighteen dollar per head. and he be in the habit, until
very lately, of send over protege, with letter of recommendation
to the present Marquis of Steyne, encourage to do so by the intimate
term on which he have live with his dear friend, the late lord. he
be most indignant that a young and insignificant aristocrat, the Earl
of Southdown, should have take the pas of he in their procession to
the dining-room. "just as I be step up to offer my hand to a
very pleasing and witty fashionable, the brilliant and exclusive Mrs.
Rawdon Crawley,"--he wrote--"the young patrician interpose between I
and the lady and whisk my Helen off without a word of apology. I be
fain to bring up the rear with the Colonel, the lady's husband, a stout
red-faced warrior who distinguish himself at Waterloo, where he have
well luck than befell some of his brother redcoat at New Orleans."
the Colonel's countenance on come into this polite society wear as
many blush as the face of a boy of sixteen assume when he be
confront with his sister's schoolfellow. it have be tell before
that honest Rawdon have not be much use at any period of his life to
lady' company. with the man at the Club or the mess room, he be
well enough; and could ride, bet, smoke, or play at billiard with the
bold of they. he have have his time for female friendship too, but
that be twenty year ago, and the lady be of the rank of those
with whom Young Marlow in the comedy be represent as have be
familiar before he become abash in the presence of Miss Hardcastle.
the time be such that one scarcely dare to allude to that kind of

know to exist as well as the Ring in Hyde Park or the Congregation at
St. James's--but which the most squeamish if not the most moral of

be now five-and-forty year of age, it have not be his lot in life to

except she and his kind sister Lady Jane, whose gentle nature have tame
and win he, scar the worthy Colonel, and on occasion of his first

to state that the weather be very hot. indeed Becky would have leave
he at home, but that virtue ordain that her husband should be by she
side to protect the timid and flutter little creature on her first
appearance in polite society.
on her first appearance Lord Steyne step forward, take her hand,
and greet she with great courtesy, and present she to Lady
Steyne, and their ladyship, her daughter. their ladyship make three
stately curtsy, and the eld lady to be sure give her hand to the
newcomer, but it be as cold and lifeless as marble.
Becky take it, however, with grateful humility, and perform a
reverence which would have do credit to the good dancer-master, put
herself at Lady Steyne's foot, as it be, by say that his Lordship
have be her father's early friend and patron, and that she, Becky,
have learn to honour and respect the Steyne family from the day of
her childhood. the fact be that Lord Steyne have once purchase a
couple of picture of the late Sharp, and the affectionate orphan could
never forget her gratitude for that favour.
the Lady Bareacres then come under Becky's cognizance--to whom the
Colonel's lady make also a most respectful obeisance: it be return

"I have the pleasure of make your ladyship's acquaintance at Brussels,

fortune to meet Lady Bareacres at the Duchess of Richmond's ball, the
night before the Battle of Waterloo. and I recollect your ladyship,
and my Lady Blanche, your daughter, sit in the carriage in the
porte-cochere at the Inn, wait for horse. I hope your ladyship's
diamond be safe."
everybody's eye look into their neighbour's. the famous diamond
have undergo a famous seizure, it appear, about which Becky, of

to look at picture with great energy.
when the Potentate from the Danube make his appearance, the
conversation be carry on in the french language, and the Lady
Bareacres and the young lady find, to their farth mortification,
that Mrs. Crawley be much well acquaint with that tongue, and

her friend with great interest the foreign personage think that she

finally, the procession be form in the order describe by the
american diplomatist, they march into the apartment where the banquet
be serve, and which, as I have promise the reader he shall enjoy it,
he shall have the liberty of order himself so as to suit his fancy.
but it be when the lady be alone that Becky know the tug of war
would come. and then indeed the little woman find herself in such a
situation as make she acknowledge the correctness of Lord Steyne's
caution to she to beware of the society of lady above her own sphere.
as they say, the person who hate Irishmen most be irishman; so,
assuredly, the great tyrant over woman be woman. when poor little
Becky, alone with the lady, go up to the fire-place whither the
great lady have repair, the great lady march away and take

and the stranger be treat with such cruelty finally, that even Lady

woman.
"Lord Steyne," say her ladyship, as her wan cheek glow with a
blush, "say you sing and play very beautifully, Mrs. Crawley--I wish
you would do I the kindness to sing to I."
"I will do anything that may give pleasure to my Lord Steyne or to
you," say rebecca, sincerely grateful, and seat herself at the
piano, begin to sing.
she sing religious song of Mozart, which have be early favourite of
Lady Steyne, and with such sweetness and tenderness that the lady,
linger round the piano, sit down by its side and listen until the
tear roll down her eye. it be true that the opposition lady at
the other end of the room keep up a loud and ceaseless buzzing and
talking, but the Lady Steyne do not hear those rumour. she be a
child again--and have wander back through a forty year' wilderness to
her convent garden. the chapel organ have peal the same tone, the

start when the jarring door be fling open, and with a loud laugh
from Lord Steyne, the man of the party enter full of gaiety.
he see at a glance what have happen in his absence, and be grateful

christian name, so as again to bring blush to her pale face--"my wife
say you have be sing like an angel," he say to Becky. now there
be angel of two kind, and both sort, it be say, be charming in
their way.
whatever the previous portion of the evening have be, the rest of that

the woman, her enemy, be leave quite alone. and Mr. Paul Jefferson
