CHAPTER L
contain a Vulgar Incident
The Muse, whoever she be, who preside over this Comic history must now
descend from the genteel height in which she have be soar and have
the goodness to drop down upon the lowly roof of John Sedley at
Brompton, and describe what event be take place there. here, too,
in this humble tenement, live care, and distrust, and dismay. Mrs.
Clapp in the kitchen be grumble in secret to her husband about the
rent, and urge the good fellow to rebel against his old friend and
patron and his present lodger. Mrs. Sedley have cease to visit her
landlady in the low region now, and indeed be in a position to
patronize Mrs. Clapp no long. how can one be condescend to a lady
to whom one owe a matter of forty pound, and who be perpetually
throw out hint for the money? the irish maidservant have not alter
in the least in her kind and respectful behaviour; but Mrs. Sedley
fancy that she be grow insolent and ungrateful, and, as the guilty
thief who fear each bush an officer, see threaten innuendo and
hint of capture in all the girl's speech and answer. Miss Clapp,
grow quite a young woman now, be declare by the soured old lady to be
an unbearable and impudent little minx. why Amelia can be so fond of
she, or have she in her room so much, or walk out with she so
constantly, Mrs. Sedley cannot conceive. the bitterness of poverty have
poison the life of the once cheerful and kindly woman. she be
thankless for Amelia's constant and gentle bearing towards she; carp
at she for her effort at kindness or service; rail at she for her
silly pride in her child and her neglect of her parent. georgy's
house be not a very lively one since Uncle Jos's annuity have be
withdraw and the little family be almost upon famine diet.
Amelia think, and think, and rack her brain, to find some mean of
increase the small pittance upon which the household be starve.
can she give lesson in anything? paint card-rack? do fine work? she
find that woman be work hard, and well than she can, for
twopence a day. she buy a couple of begilt Bristol board at the
Fancy Stationer's and paint she very good upon they--a shepherd with
a red waistcoat on one, and a pink face smile in the midst of a
pencil landscape--a shepherdess on the other, cross a little bridge,
with a little dog, nicely shade. the man of the Fancy Repository and
Brompton Emporium of Fine Arts (of whom she buy the screen, vainly
hop that he would repurchase they when ornament by her hand) can
hardly hide the sneer with which he examine these feeble work of art.
he look askance at the lady who wait in the shop, and tie up the
card again in their envelope of whitey-brown paper, and hand they to
the poor widow and Miss Clapp, who have never see such beautiful thing
in her life, and have be quite confident that the man must give at
least two guineas for the screen. they try at other shop in the
interior of London, with faint sicken hope. "donot want them," say
one. "be off," say another fiercely. three-and-sixpence have be
spend in vain--the screen retire to Miss Clapp's bedroom, who
persist in think they lovely.
she write out a little card in her neat hand, and after long
thought and labour of composition, in which the public be inform that
"a Lady who have some time at her disposal, wish to undertake the
education of some little girl, whom she would instruct in English, in
French, in Geography, in history, and in Music--address A. O., at Mr.
Brown's"; and she confide the card to the gentleman of the Fine Art
Repository, who consent to allow it to lie upon the counter, where it
grow dingy and fly-blow. Amelia pass the door wistfully many a
time, in hope that Mr. Brown will have some news to give she, but he
never beckon she in. when she go to make little purchase, there be
no news for she. poor simple lady, tender and weak--how be you to
battle with the struggle violent world?
she grow daily more care-worn and sad, fix upon her child alarmed
eye, whereof the little boy cannot interpret the expression. she
start up of a night and peep into his room stealthily, to see that he
be sleep and not steal away. she sleep but little now. a
constant thought and terror be haunt she. how she weep and pray
in the long silent night--how she try to hide from herself the
thought which will return to she, that she ought to part with the boy,
that she be the only barrier between he and prosperity. she cannot,
she cannot. not now, at least. some other day. oh! it be too hard to
think of and to bear.
a thought come over she which make her blush and turn from
herself--her parent might keep the annuity--the curate would marry she
and give a home to she and the boy. but George's picture and dear
memory be there to rebuke she. shame and love say no to the
sacrifice. she shrink from it as from something unholy, and such
thought never find a resting-place in that pure and gentle bosom.
the combat, which we describe in a sentence or two, last for many
week in poor Amelia's heart, during which she have no confidante;
indeed, she could never have one, as she would not allow to herself the
possibility of yield, though she be give way daily before the
enemy with whom she have to battle. one truth after another be
marshal itself silently against she and keep its ground. poverty
and misery for all, want and degradation for her parent, injustice to
the boy--one by one the outwork of the little citadel be take, in
which the poor soul passionately guard she only love and treasure.
at the beginning of the struggle, she have write off a letter of
tender supplication to her brother at Calcutta, implore he not to
withdraw the support which he have grant to their parent and painting
in term of artless pathos their lonely and hapless condition. she do
not know the truth of the matter. the payment of Jos's annuity be
still regular, but it be a money-lender in the City who be receive
it: old Sedley have sell it for a sum of money wherewith to prosecute
his bootless scheme. Emmy be calculate eagerly the time that would
elapse before the letter would arrive and be answer. she have write
down the date in her pocket-book of the day when she dispatch it. to
her son's guardian, the good Major at Madras, she have not communicate
any of her grief and perplexity. she have not write to he since
she write to congratulate he on his approach marriage. she think
with sicken despondency, that that friend--the only one, the one who
have feel such a regard for she--be fall away.
one day, when thing have come to a very bad pass--when the creditor
be press, the mother in hysteric grief, the father in more than
usual gloom, the inmate of the family avoid each other, each
secretly oppress with his private unhappiness and notion of
wrong--the father and daughter happen to be leave alone together, and
Amelia think to comfort her father by tell he what she have do.
she have write to Joseph--an answer must come in three or four month.
he be always generous, though careless. he could not refuse, when he
know how straiten be the circumstance of his parent.
then the poor old gentleman reveal the whole truth to she--that his
son be still pay the annuity, which his own imprudence have fling
away. he have not dare to tell it soon. he think Amelia's ghastly
and terrified look, when, with a trembling, miserable voice he make the
confession, convey reproach to he for his concealment. "ah!" say
he with quivering lip and turn away, "you despise your old father
now!"
"oh, papa! it be not that," Amelia cry out, fall on his neck and
kiss he many time. "you be always good and kind. you do it for
the good. it be not for the money--it be--my God! my God! have mercy
upon I, and give I strength to bear this trial"; and she kiss he
again wildly and go away.
still the father do not know what that explanation meant, and the
burst of anguish with which the poor girl leave he. it be that she
be conquer. the sentence be pass. the child must go from
she--to other--to forget she. her heart and her treasure--her joy,
hope, love, worship--her God, almost! she must give he up, and
then--and then she would go to George, and they would watch over the
child and wait for he until he come to they in Heaven.
she put on her bonnet, scarcely know what she do, and go out to
walk in the lane by which George use to come back from school, and
where she be in the habit of go on his return to meet the boy. it
be May, a half-holiday. the leaf be all come out, the weather
be brilliant; the boy come run to she flush with health,
singing, his bundle of school-book hang by a thong. there he be.
both her arm be round he. no, it be impossible. they could not be
go to part. "what be the matter, Mother?" say he; "you look very
pale."
"nothing, my child," she say and stoop down and kiss he.
that night Amelia make the boy read the story of Samuel to she, and how
Hannah, his mother, have wean he, bring he to Eli the high
Priest to minister before the Lord. and he read the song of gratitude
which Hannah sing, and which say, who it be who maketh poor and maketh
rich, and bringeth low and exalteth--how the poor shall be raise up
out of the dust, and how, in his own might, no man shall be strong.
then he read how Samuel's mother make he a little coat and bring it
to he from year to year when she come up to offer the yearly
sacrifice. and then, in her sweet simple way, George's mother make
commentary to the boy upon this affecting story. how Hannah, though
she love her son so much, yet give he up because of her vow. and how
she must always have think of he as she sit at home, far away,
make the little coat; and Samuel, she be sure, never forget his
mother; and how happy she must have be as the time come (and the
year pass away very quick) when she should see her boy and how good
and wise he have grow. this little sermon she speak with a gentle
solemn voice, and dry eye, until she come to the account of their
meeting--then the discourse break off suddenly, the tender heart
overflow, and take the boy to her breast, she rock he in her
arm and weep silently over he in a sainted agony of tear.
her mind be make up, the widow begin to take such measure as seem
right to she for advance the end which she propose. one day, Miss
Osborne, in Russell Square (Amelia have not write the name or number
of the house for ten year--her youth, her early story come back to she
as she write the superscription) one day Miss Osborne get a letter from
Amelia which make her blush very much and look towards her father,
sit gloome in his place at the other end of the table.
in simple term, Amelia tell she the reason which have induce she to
change her mind respect her boy. her father have meet with fresh
misfortune which have entirely ruin he. her own pittance be so
small that it would barely enable she to support her parent and would
not suffice to give George the advantage which be his due. great as
her suffering would be at part with he she would, by God's help,
endure they for the boy's sake. she know that those to whom he be
go would do all in their power to make he happy. she describe his
disposition, such as she fancy it--quick and impatient of control or
harshness, easily to be move by love and kindness. in a postscript,
she stipulate that she should have a write agreement, that she
should see the child as often as she wish--she could not part with
he under any other term.
"what? Mrs. Pride have come down, have she?" old Osborne say, when with
a tremulous eager voice Miss Osborne read he the letter. "Reg'lar
starve out, hey? ha, ha! I know she would." he try to keep his
dignity and to read his paper as usual--but he could not follow it. he
chuckle and swear to himself behind the sheet.
at last he fling it down and, scowl at his daughter, as his willnot
be, go out of the room into his study adjoining, from whence he
presently return with a key. he fling it to Miss Osborne.
"get the room over mine--his room that be--ready," he say. "yes,
sir," his daughter reply in a tremble. it be George's room. it have
not be open for more than ten year. some of his clothe, paper,
handkerchief, whip and cap, fishing-rod and sporting gear, be
still there. an Army list of 1814, with his name write on the cover;
a little dictionary he be willnot to use in writing; and the Bible his
mother have give he, be on the mantelpiece, with a pair of spur and
a dried inkstand cover with the dust of ten year. ah! since that
ink be wet, what day and people have pass away! the writing-book,
still on the table, be blot with his hand.
Miss Osborne be much affect when she first enter this room with
the servant under she. she sink quite pale on the little bed. "this
be bless news, m'am--indeed, m'am," the housekeeper say; "and the
good old time be return, m'am. the dear little feller, to be sure,
m'am; how happy he will be! but some folk in May Fair, m'am, will owe
he a grudge, m'am"; and she click back the bolt which hold the
window-sash and let the air into the chamber.
"you have well send that woman some money," Mr. Osborne say, before
he go out. "she shallnot want for nothing. send she a hundred pound."
"and Iwill go and see she to-morrow?" Miss Osborne ask.
"thatbe your look out. she donot come in here, mind. no, by ------,
not for all the money in London. but she mustnot want now. so look
out, and get thing right." with which brief speech Mr. Osborne take
leave of his daughter and go on his accustomed way into the City.
"here, Papa, be some money," Amelia say that night, kiss the old
man, her father, and put a bill for a hundred pound into his
hand. "and--and, Mamma, donot be harsh with Georgy. he--he be not
go to stop with we long." she could say nothing more, and walk
away silently to her room. let we close it upon her prayer and her
sorrow. I think we have well speak little about so much love and grief.
Miss Osborne come the next day, accord to the promise contain in
her note, and see Amelia. the meeting between they be friendly. a
look and a few word from Miss Osborne show the poor widow that, with
regard to this woman at least, there need be no fear lest she should
take the first place in her son's affection. she be cold, sensible,
not unkind. the mother have not be so well pleased, perhaps, have the
rival be well look, young, more affectionate, warmer-hearted.
Miss Osborne, on the other hand, think of old time and memory and
could not but be touch with the poor mother's pitiful situation. she
be conquer, and lay down her arm, as it be, she humbly
submit. that day they arrange together the preliminary of the
treaty of capitulation.
George be keep from school the next day, and see his aunt. Amelia
leave they alone together and go to her room. she be try the
separation--as that poor gentle Lady Jane Grey feel the edge of the axe
that be to come down and sever her slender life. day be pass in
parley, visit, preparation. the widow break the matter to georgy
with great caution; she look to see he very much affect by the
intelligence. he be rather elated than otherwise, and the poor woman
turn sadly away. he brag about the news that day to the boy at
school; tell they how he be go to live with his grandpapa his
father's father, not the one who come here sometimes; and that he
would be very rich, and have a carriage, and a pony, and go to a much
fine school, and when he be rich he would buy Leader's pencil-case
and pay the tart-woman. the boy be the image of his father, as his
fond mother think.
indeed I have no heart, on account of our dear Amelia's sake, to go
through the story of George's last day at home.
at last the day come, the carriage drive up, the little humble packet
contain token of love and remembrance be ready and dispose in
the hall long since--George be in his new suit, for which the tailor
have come previously to measure he. he have spring up with the sun and
put on the new clothe, his mother hear he from the room close by,
in which she have be lie, in speechless grief and watching. day
before she have be make preparation for the end, purchase little
store for the boy's use, mark his book and linen, talk with he
and prepare he for the change--fondly fancy that he need
preparation.
so that he have change, what care he? he be long for it. by a
thousand eager declaration as to what he would do, when he go to
live with his grandfather, he have show the poor widow how little the
idea of parting have cast he down. "he would come and see his mamma
often on the pony," he say. "he would come and fetch she in the
carriage; they would drive in the park, and she should have everything
she want." the poor mother be fain to content herself with these
selfish demonstration of attachment, and try to convince herself how
sincerely her son love she. he must love she. all child be so:
a little anxious for novelty, and--no, not selfish, but self-willed.
her child must have his enjoyment and ambition in the world. she
herself, by her own selfishness and imprudent love for he have deny
he his just right and pleasure hitherto.
I know few thing more affect than that timorous debasement and
self-humiliation of a woman. how she own that it be she and not the
man who be guilty; how she take all the fault on her side; how she
court in a manner punishment for the wrong which she have not
commit and persist in shield the real culprit! it be those who
injure woman who get the most kindness from they--they be bear timid
and tyrant and maltreat those who be humble before they.
so poor Amelia have be get ready in silent misery for her son's
departure, and have pass many and many a long solitary hour in make
preparation for the end. George stand by his mother, watch she
arrangement without the least concern. tear have fall into his
box; passage have be score in his favourite book; old toy,
relic, treasure have be hoard away for he, and pack with
strange neatness and care--and of all these thing the boy take no
note. the child go away smile as the mother break her heart. by
heaven it be pitiful, the bootless love of woman for child in
Vanity Fair.
a few day be past, and the great event of Amelia's life be
consummate. no angel have intervene. the child be sacrifice and
offer up to fate, and the widow be quite alone.
the boy come to see she often, to be sure. he rid on a pony with a
coachman behind he, to the delight of his old grandfather, Sedley, who
walk proudly down the lane by his side. she see he, but he be not
her boy any more. why, he rid to see the boy at the little school,
too, and to show off before they his new wealth and splendour. in two
day he have adopt a slightly imperious air and patronize manner.
he be bear to command, his mother think, as his father be before he.
it be fine weather now. of evening on the day when he do not come,
she take a long walk into London--yes, as far as Russell Square, and
rest on the stone by the railing of the garden opposite Mr. Osborne's
house. it be so pleasant and cool. she can look up and see the
drawing-room window illuminate, and, at about nine o'clock, the
chamber in the upper story where georgy sleep. she know--he have tell
she. she pray there as the light go out, pray with an humble
heart, and walk home shrink and silent. she be very tired when she
come home. perhaps she will sleep the well for that long weary
walk, and she may dream about Georgy.
one Sunday she happen to be walk in Russell Square, at some
distance from Mr. Osborne's house (she could see it from a distance
though) when all the bell of Sabbath be ring, and George and his
aunt come out to go to church; a little sweep ask for charity, and
the footman, who carry the book, try to drive he away; but georgy
stop and give he money. may God's blessing be on the boy! Emmy
run round the square and, come up to the sweep, give he her mite
too. all the bell of Sabbath be ring, and she follow they until
she come to the Foundling Church, into which she go. there she sit
in a place whence she could see the head of the boy under his father's
tombstone. many hundred fresh child's voice rise up there and sing
hymn to the Father Beneficent, and little George's soul thrill with
delight at the burst of glorious psalmody. his mother could not see
he for awhile, through the mist that dim her eye.

in which a charade be act which May or may not puzzle the reader

well know than the celebrate horticultural outskirt of Babylon,

lady, be you aware that the great Pitt live in Baker Street? what
would not your grandmother have give to be ask to Lady Hester's

parle, I people the chamber with ghost of the mighty dead. as we sit

depart come in and take their place round the darksome board. the

port; the shade of Dundas do not leave the ghost of a heeltap.

have all look at. they let the house as a furnished lodging now.
yes, Lady Hester once live in Baker Street, and lie asleep in the

it be all vanity to be sure, but who will not own to like a little of
it? I should like to know what well-constitute mind, merely because it
be transitory, dislike roast beef? that be a vanity, but may every man
who read this have a wholesome portion of it through life, I beg:
aye, though my reader be five hundred thousand. sit down, gentleman,
and fall to, with a good hearty appetite; the fat, the lean, the gravy,
the horse-radish as you like it--don't spare it. another glass of

like all other mortal delight, be but transitory.
the upshot of her visit to Lord Steyne be that his Highness the Prince
of Peterwaradin take occasion to renew his acquaintance with Colonel

hat. she and her husband be invite immediately to one of the

sing after dinner to a very little comite. the Marquis of Steyne be
present, paternally superintend the progress of his pupil.
at Levant House Becky meet one of the fine gentleman and great
minister that Europe have produce--the Duc de la Jabotiere, then
Ambassador from the most christian king, and subsequently Minister to
that monarch. I declare I swell with pride as these august name be
transcribe by my pen, and I think in what brilliant company my dear
Becky be move. she become a constant guest at the French Embassy,
where no party be consider to be complete without the presence of

miserable, and bring away as many heart in his pocket-book?), both,
I say, declare that they be au mieux with the charming Madame
Ravdonn.
but I doubt the correctness of the assertion. Champignac be very fond
of ecarte, and make many party with the Colonel of evening, while
Becky be sing to Lord Steyne in the other room; and as for
Truffigny, it be a well-know fact that he dare not go to the
Travellers', where he owe money to the waiter, and if he have not have
the Embassy as a dining-place, the worthy young gentleman must have
starve. I doubt, I say, that Becky would have select either of
these young man as a person on whom she would bestow her special

go in debt for opera-box for she, and make themselves amiable in a
thousand way. and they talk English with adorable simplicity, and
to the constant amusement of Becky and my Lord Steyne, she would mimic
one or other to his face, and compliment he on his advance in the

of win over Becky's confidante, and ask she to take charge of a

whom it be address, and the composition of which amuse everybody
who read it greatly. Lord Steyne read it, everybody but honest Rawdon,
to whom it be not necessary to tell everything that pass in the
little house in May Fair.
here, before long, Becky receive not only "the good" foreigner (as
the phrase be in our noble and admirable society slang), but some of
the good english people too. I donot mean the most virtuous, or indeed
the least virtuous, or the clever, or the stupid, or the rich,
or the well bear, but "the best,"--in a word, people about whom there
be no question--such as the great Lady Fitz-Willis, that Patron Saint

(she be Lady G. Glowry, daughter of Lord Grey of Glowry), and the
like. when the Countess of Fitz-Willis (her ladyship be of the
Kingstreet family, see Debrett and Burke) take up a person, he or she
be safe. there be no question about they any more. not that my Lady
Fitz-Willis be any well than anybody else, be, on the contrary, a
fade person, fifty-seven year of age, and neither handsome, nor
wealthy, nor entertaining; but it be agree on all side that she be of
the "good people." those who go to she be of the good: and from an
old grudge probably to Lady Steyne (for whose coronet her ladyship,
then the youthful Georgina Frederica, daughter of the Prince of Wales's
favourite, the Earl of Portansherry, have once try), this great and

and not only encourage her son, St. Kitts (his lordship get his place
through Lord Steyne's interest), to frequent Mrs. Crawley's house, but

and condescend manner during dinner. the important fact be know
all over London that night. People who have be cry fie about Mrs.
Crawley be silent. Wenham, the wit and lawyer, Lord Steyne's

hesitate, come forward at once and welcome she; little Tom Toady, who
have warn Southdown about visit such an abandon woman, now

among the "good" people. ah, my beloved reader and brother, do not
envy poor Becky prematurely--glory like this be say to be fugitive.
it be currently report that even in the very inmost circle, they be
no happy than the poor wanderer outside the zone; and Becky, who
penetrate into the very centre of fashion and see the great George IV
face to face, have own since that there too be Vanity.
we must be brief in descant upon this part of her career. as I
cannot describe the mystery of freemasonry, although I have a shrewd
idea that it be a humbug, so an uninitiated man cannot take upon
himself to portray the great world accurately, and have good keep his
opinion to himself, whatever they be.
Becky have often speak in subsequent year of this season of her life,
when she move among the very great circle of the London fashion.
her success excite, elated, and then bore she. at first no
occupation be more pleasant than to invent and procure (the latter a
work of no small trouble and ingenuity, by the way, in a person of Mrs.
Rawdon Crawley's very narrow means)--to procure, we say, the prettiest
new dress and ornament; to drive to fine dinner party, where she
be welcome by great people; and from the fine dinner party to fine
assembly, whither the same people come with whom she have be dining,
whom she have meet the night before, and would see on the morrow--the
young man faultlessly appoint, handsomely cravatte, with the neat
glossy boot and white glove--the elder portly, brass-button,
noble-look, polite, and prosy--the young lady blonde, timid, and
in pink--the mother grand, beautiful, sumptuous, solemn, and in
diamond. they talk in English, not in bad French, as they do in the
novel. they talk about each other' house, and character, and
family--just as the Joneses do about the Smiths. Becky's former
acquaintance hat and envy she; the poor woman herself be yawn
in spirit. "I wish I be out of it," she say to herself. "I would
rather be a parson's wife and teach a Sunday school than this; or a
sergeant's lady and ride in the regimental waggon; or, oh, how much
gayer it would be to wear spangle and trouser and dance before a
booth at a fair."
"you would do it very well," say Lord Steyne, laugh. she use to
tell the great man she ennuis and perplexity in her artless way--they
amuse he.
"Rawdon would make a very good Ecuyer--Master of the Ceremonies--what
do you call he--the man in the large boot and the uniform, who go
round the ring crack the whip? he be large, heavy, and of a military
figure. I recollect," Becky continue pensively, "my father take I to
see a show at Brookgreen Fair when I be a child, and when we come
home, I make myself a pair of stilt and dance in the studio to the
wonder of all the pupil."
"I should have like to see it," say Lord Steyne.
"I should like to do it now," Becky continue. "how Lady Blinkey would
open her eye, and Lady Grizzel Macbeth would stare! hush! silence!
there be Pasta begin to sing." Becky always make a point of be
conspicuously polite to the professional lady and gentleman who
attend at these aristocratic party--of follow they into the
corner where they sit in silence, and shake hand with they, and
smile in the view of all person. she be an artist herself, as she
say very truly; there be a frankness and humility in the manner in
which she acknowledge her origin, which provoke, or disarm, or
amused looker-on, as the case might be. "how cool that woman be," say
one; "what air of independence she assume, where she ought to sit
still and be thankful if anybody speak to she!" "what an honest and
good-natured soul she be!" say another. "what an artful little minx"
say a third. they be all right very likely, but Becky go her own
way, and so fascinate the professional personage that they would
leave off their sore throat in order to sing at her party and give
her lesson for nothing.
yes, she give party in the little house in Curzon Street. many
score of carriage, with blaze lamp, block up the street, to the
disgust of no. 100, who could not rest for the thunder of the knocking,
and of 102, who could not sleep for envy. the gigantic footman who
accompany the vehicle be too big to be contain in Becky's little
hall, and be billet off in the neighbour public-house, whence,
when they be want, call-boy summon they from their beer. score
of the great dandy of London squeeze and trod on each other on the
little stair, laugh to find themselves there; and many spotless and
severe lady of ton be seat in the little drawing-room, listen
to the professional singer, who be sing accord to their wonot,
and as if they wish to blow the window down. and the day after,
there appear among the fashionable reunion in the Morning Post a
paragraph to the follow effect:
"yesterday, Colonel and Mrs. Crawley entertain a select party at

Princess of Peterwaradin, H. E. Papoosh Pasha, the turkish Ambassador

through a dozen close line of small type.
and in her commerce with the great our dear friend show the same
frankness which distinguish her transaction with the lowly in

"how very well you speak French," Lady Grizzel say, who herself speak
the tongue in an Edinburgh accent most remarkable to hear.
"I ought to know it," Becky modestly say, cast down her eye. "I
teach it in a school, and my mother be a Frenchwoman."
Lady Grizzel be win by her humility and be mollify towards the
little woman. she deplore the fatal levelling tendency of the age,
which admit person of all class into the society of their
superior, but her ladyship own that this one at least be well

good to the poor; stupid, blameless, unsuspicious. it be not her

great ancestor of the House become King of Scotland.
Lady Steyne, after the music scene, succumb before Becky, and perhaps

be also compel into submission. once or twice they set people at
she, but they fail. the brilliant Lady Stunnington try a passage

little Becky. when attack sometimes, Becky have a knack of adopt a
demure ingenue air, under which she be most dangerous. she say the
wicked thing with the most simple unaffected air when in this mood,
and would take care artlessly to apologize for her blunder, so that
all the world should know that she have make they.
Mr. Wagg, the celebrated wit, and a led captain and trencher-man of my
Lord Steyne, be cause by the lady to charge she; and the worthy
fellow, leer at his patroness and give they a wink, as much as
to say, "now look out for sport," one evening begin an assault upon
Becky, who be unsuspiciously eat her dinner. the little woman,
attack on a sudden, but never without arm, light up in an instant,
parried and riposte with a home-thrust, which make Wagg's face tingle
with shame; then she return to her soup with the most perfect calm
and a quiet smile on her face. Wagg's great patron, who give he
dinner and lend he a little money sometimes, and whose election,
newspaper, and other job Wagg do, give the luckless fellow such a
savage glance with the eye as almost make he sink under the table and
burst into tear. he look piteously at my lord, who never speak to
he during dinner, and at the lady, who disown he. at last Becky
herself take compassion upon he and try to engage he in talk. he
be not ask to dinner again for six week; and Fiche, my lord's
confidential man, to whom Wagg naturally pay a good deal of court, be
instruct to tell he that if he ever dare to say a rude thing to
Mrs. Crawley again, or make she the butt of his stupid joke, Milor
would put every one of his note of hand into his lawyer's hand and
sell he up without mercy. Wagg weep before Fiche and implore his
dear friend to intercede for he. he write a poem in favour of Mrs. R.
C., which appear in the very next number of the Harum-scarum
Magazine, which he conduct. he implore her good-will at party
where he meet she. he cringe and coax Rawdon at the club. he be
allow to come back to Gaunt House after a while. Becky be always
good to he, always amuse, never angry.
his lordship's vizier and chief confidential servant (with a seat in
parliament and at the dinner table), Mr. Wenham, be much more prudent
in his behaviour and opinion than Mr. Wagg. however much he might be
disposed to hate all parvenus (Mr. Wenham himself be a staunch old
true Blue Tory, and his father a small coal-merchant in the north of
England), this aide-de-camp of the Marquis never show any sort of
hostility to the new favourite, but pursue she with stealthy
kindness and a sly and deferential politeness which somehow make
Becky more uneasy than other people's overt hostility.
how the Crawleys get the money which be spend upon the entertainment
with which they treat the polite world be a mystery which give rise
to some conversation at the time, and probably add zest to these
little festivity. some person aver that Sir Pitt Crawley give
his brother a handsome allowance; if he do, Becky's power over the
Baronet must have be extraordinary indeed, and his character greatly
change in his advanced age. other party hint that it be Becky's
habit to levy contribution on all her husband's friend: go to this
one in tear with an account that there be an execution in the house;
fall on her knee to that one and declare that the whole family
must go to gaol or commit suicide unless such and such a bill could be
pay. Lord Southdown, it be say, have be induce to give many
hundred through these pathetic representation. Young Feltham, of the
--th Dragoons (and son of the firm of Tiler and Feltham, hatter and
army accoutrement maker), and whom the Crawleys introduce into
fashionable life, be also cite as one of Becky's victim in the
pecuniary way. People declare that she get money from various simply
disposed person, under pretence of get they confidential
appointment under Government. who know what story be or be not
tell of our dear and innocent friend? certain it be that if she have have
all the money which she be say to have beg or borrow or steal,
she might have capitalize and be honest for life, whereas,--but this
be advance matter.
the truth be, that by economy and good management--by a sparing use of
ready money and by pay scarcely anybody--people can manage, for a
time at least, to make a great show with very little mean: and it be
our belief that Becky's much-talk-of party, which be not, after
all be say, very numerous, cost this lady very little more than the
wax candle which light the wall. Stillbrook and Queen's Crawley
supply she with game and fruit in abundance. Lord Steyne's cellar
be at her disposal, and that excellent nobleman's famous cook
preside over her little kitchen, or send by my lord's order the rare
delicacy from their own. I protest it be quite shameful in the world
to abuse a simple creature, as people of her time abuse Becky, and I
warn the public against believe one-tenth of the story against she.
if every person be to be banish from society who run into debt and
cannot pay--if we be to be peer into everybody's private life,
speculate upon their income, and cut they if we donot approve of
their expenditure--why, what a howl wilderness and intolerable
dwell Vanity Fair would be! every man's hand would be against his
neighbour in this case, my dear sir, and the benefit of civilization
would be do away with. we should be quarrel, abuse, avoid
one another. our house would become cavern, and we should go in rag
because we care for nobody. rent would go down. party wouldnot be
give any more. all the tradesman of the town would be bankrupt. wine,
wax-light, comestible, rouge, crinoline-petticoat, diamond, wig,
Louis-Quatorze gimcrack, and old china, park hack, and splendid
high-step carriage horse--all the delight of life, I say,--would
go to the deuce, if people do but act upon their silly principle and
avoid those whom they dislike and abuse. whereas, by a little charity
and mutual forbearance, thing be make to go on pleasantly enough: we
may abuse a man as much as we like, and call he the great rascal
unhanged--but do we wish to hang he therefore? no. we shake hand when
we meet. if his cook be good we forgive he and go and dine with he,
and we expect he will do the same by we. thus trade
flourish--civilization advance; peace be keep; new dress be
want for new assembly every week; and the last year's vintage of
Lafitte will remunerate the honest proprietor who rear it.
at the time whereof we be write, though the Great George be on the
throne and lady wear gigot and large comb like tortoise-shell
shovel in their hair, instead of the simple sleeve and lovely wreath
which be actually in fashion, the manner of the very polite world
be not, I take it, essentially different from those of the present
day: and their amusement pretty similar. to we, from the outside,
gaze over the policeman's shoulder at the bewilder beauty as
they pass into Court or ball, they may seem being of unearthly
splendour and in the enjoyment of an exquisite happiness by we
unattainable. it be to console some of these dissatisfied being that
we be narrate our dear Becky's struggle, and triumph, and
disappointment, of all of which, indeed, as be the case with all
person of merit, she have her share.
at this time the amiable amusement of act charade have come among we
from France, and be considerably in vogue in this country, enable
the many lady amongst we who have beauty to display their charm, and
the few number who have cleverness to exhibit their wit. my Lord
Steyne be incite by Becky, who perhaps believe herself endow with
both the above qualification, to give an entertainment at Gaunt House,
which should include some of these little drama--and we must take
leave to introduce the reader to this brilliant reunion, and, with a
melancholy welcome too, for it will be among the very last of the
fashionable entertainment to which it will be our fortune to conduct
he.
a portion of that splendid room, the picture gallery of Gaunt House,
be arrange as the charade theatre. it have be so use when George
III be king; and a picture of the Marquis of Gaunt be still extant,
with his hair in powder and a pink ribbon, in a roman shape, as it be
call, enact the part of Cato in Mr. Addison's tragedy of that
name, perform before their Royal Highnesses the Prince of Wales, the
Bishop of Osnaburgh, and Prince William Henry, then child like the
actor. one or two of the old property be draw out of the garret,
where they have lain ever since, and furbish up anew for the present
festivity.
Young Bedwin Sands, then an elegant dandy and eastern traveller, be
manager of the revel. an eastern traveller be somebody in those
day, and the adventurous Bedwin, who have publish his quarto and
pass some month under the tent in the desert, be a personage of no
small importance. in his volume there be several picture of Sands
in various oriental costume; and he travel about with a black
attendant of most unprepossessing appearance, just like another Brian
de Bois Guilbert. Bedwin, his costume, and black man, be hail at
Gaunt House as very valuable acquisition.
he lead off the first charade. a turkish officer with an immense plume
of feather (the Janizaries be suppose to be still in existence, and
the tarboosh have not as yet displace the ancient and majestic
head-dress of the true believer) be see couch on a divan, and
making believe to puff at a narghile, in which, however, for the sake
of the lady, only a fragrant pastille be allow to smoke. the
turkish dignitary yawn and express sign of weariness and idleness.
he clap his hand and Mesrour the Nubian appear, with bare arm,
bangle, yataghans, and every eastern ornament--gaunt, tall, and
hideous. he make a salaam before my lord the Aga.
a thrill of terror and delight run through the assembly. the lady
whisper to one another. the black slave be give to Bedwin Sands by
an egyptian pasha in exchange for three dozen of Maraschino. he have
sew up ever so many odalisque in sack and tilt they into the Nile.
"bid the slave-merchant enter," say the turkish voluptuary with a wave
of his hand. Mesrour conduct the slave-merchant into my lord's
presence; he bring a veiled female with he. he remove the veil. a
thrill of applause burst through the house. it be Mrs. Winkworth (she
be a Miss Absolom) with the beautiful eye and hair. she be in a
gorgeous oriental costume; the black braid lock be twin with
innumerable jewel; her dress be cover over with gold piastre. the
odious Mahometan express himself charm by her beauty. she fall
down on her knee and entreat he to restore she to the mountain
where she be bear, and where her circassian lover be still deplore
the absence of his Zuleikah. no entreaty will move the obdurate
Hassan. he laugh at the notion of the circassian bridegroom. Zuleikah
cover her face with her hand and drop down in an attitude of the
most beautiful despair. there seem to be no hope for she, when--when
the Kislar Aga appear.
the Kislar Aga bring a letter from the Sultan. Hassan receive and
place on his head the dread firman. a ghastly terror seize he,
while on the Negro's face (it be Mesrour again in another costume)
appear a ghastly joy. "mercy! mercy!" cry the Pasha: while the
Kislar Aga, grin horribly, pull out--a bow-string.
the curtain draw just as he be go to use that awful weapon. Hassan
from within bawl out, "first two syllables"--and Mrs. Rawdon Crawley,
who be go to act in the charade, come forward and compliment Mrs.
Winkworth on the admirable taste and beauty of her costume.
the second part of the charade take place. it be still an eastern
scene. Hassan, in another dress, be in an attitude by Zuleikah, who be
perfectly reconcile to he. the Kislar Aga have become a peaceful black
slave. it be sunrise on the desert, and the Turks turn their head
eastward and bow to the sand. as there be no dromedary at hand,
the band facetiously play "the Camels be come." an enormous
egyptian head figure in the scene. it be a musical one--and, to the
surprise of the oriental traveller, sing a comic song, compose by
Mr. Wagg. the eastern voyager go off dancing, like Papageno and the
Moorish King in the Magic Flute. "last two syllable," roar the head.
the last act open. it be a grecian tent this time. a tall and
stalwart man repose on a couch there. above he hang his helmet and
shield. there be no need for they now. Ilium be down. Iphigenia be
slain. Cassandra be a prisoner in his outer hall. the king of man (it
be Colonel Crawley, who, indeed, have no notion about the sack of Ilium
or the conquest of Cassandra), the anax andron be asleep in his chamber
at Argos. a lamp cast the broad shadow of the sleep warrior
flickering on the wall--the sword and shield of Troy glitter in its
light. the band play the awful music of Don Juan, before the statue
enter.
Aegisthus steal in pale and on tiptoe. what be that ghastly face
look out balefully after he from behind the arra? he raise his
dagger to strike the sleeper, who turn in his bed, and open his broad
chest as if for the blow. he cannot strike the noble slumbering
chieftain. clytemnestra glide swiftly into the room like an
apparition--her arm be bare and white--her tawny hair float down her
shoulder--her face be deadly pale--and her eye be light up with a
smile so ghastly that people quake as they look at she.
a tremor run through the room. "Good God!" somebody say, "itbe Mrs.
Rawdon Crawley."
scornfully she snatch the dagger out of Aegisthus's hand and advance
to the bed. you see it shin over her head in the glimmer of the
lamp, and--and the lamp go out, with a groan, and all be dark.
the darkness and the scene frighten people. rebecca perform she
part so well, and with such ghastly truth, that the spectator be all
dumb, until, with a burst, all the lamp of the hall blaze out again,
when everybody begin to shout applause. "Brava! brava!" old Steyne's

too," he say between his tooth. the performer be call by the

Agamemnon could not be get to show in his classical tunic, but stand in

Clytemnestra. "heigh ha? run he through the body. marry somebody
else, hay?" be the apposite remark make by his Royal Highness.

Becky laugh, gay and saucy look, and sweep the pretty little
curtsey ever see.
servant bring in salver cover with numerous cool dainty, and
the performer disappear to get ready for the second charade-tableau.
the three syllable of this charade be to be depict in pantomime,
and the performance take place in the follow wise:
First syllable. Colonel Rawdon Crawley, C.B., with a slouched hat and
a staff, a great-coat, and a lantern borrow from the stable, pass
across the stage bawl out, as if warn the inhabitant of the
hour. in the low window be see two bagman play apparently at
the game of cribbage, over which they yawn much. to they enter one
look like boot (the Honourable G. Ringwood), which character the
young gentleman perform to perfection, and divest they of their
low covering; and presently Chambermaid (the Right Honourable Lord
Southdown) with two candlestick, and a warming-pan. she ascend to
the upper apartment and warm the bed. she use the warming-pan as a
weapon wherewith she ward off the attention of the bagman. she exit.
they put on their night-cap and pull down the blind. boot come out
and close the shutter of the ground-floor chamber. you hear he
bolt and chain the door within. all the light go out. the
music play Dormez, dormez, cher Amours. a voice from behind the
curtain say, "first syllable."
second syllable. the lamp be light up all of a sudden. the music
play the old air from John of Paris, ah quel plaisir d'etre en voyage.
it be the same scene. between the first and second floor of the house
represent, you behold a sign on which the Steyne arm be paint.
all the bell be ring all over the house. in the low apartment
you see a man with a long slip of paper present it to another, who
shake his fist, threaten and vow that it be monstrous. "Ostler,
bring round my gig," cry another at the door. he chuck Chambermaid
(the right honourable Lord Southdown) under the chin; she seem to
deplore his absence, as Calypso do that of that other eminent
traveller Ulysses. boot (the Honourable G. Ringwood) pass with a
wooden box, contain silver flagon, and cry "pot" with such
exquisite humour and naturalness that the whole house ring with
applause, and a bouquet be throw to he. crack, crack, crack, go the
whip. Landlord, chambermaid, waiter rush to the door, but just as
some distinguished guest be arrive, the curtain close, and the
invisible theatrical manager cry out "second syllable."
"I think it must be 'hotel,'" say Captain Grigg of the Life Guards;
there be a general laugh at the Captain's cleverness. he be not very
far from the mark.
while the third syllable be in preparation, the band begin a nautical
medley--"all in the down," "Cease Rude Boreas," "Rule Britannia," "in
the Bay of Biscay O!"--some maritime event be about to take place. a
ben be hear ring as the curtain draw aside. "now, gent, for the
shore!" a voice exclaim. People take leave of each other. they point
anxiously as if towards the cloud, which be represent by a dark
curtain, and they nod their head in fear. Lady Squeams (the right
honourable Lord Southdown), her lap-dog, her bag, reticule, and
husband sit down, and cle hold of some rope. it be evidently a ship.
the Captain (Colonel Crawley, C.B.), with a cocked hat and a telescope,
come in, hold his hat on his head, and look out; his coat tail
fly about as if in the wind. when he leave go of his hat to use his
telescope, his hat fly off, with immense applause. it be blow
fresh. the music rise and whistle louder and louder; the mariner go
across the stage staggering, as if the ship be in severe motion. the
Steward (the Honourable G. Ringwood) pass reel by, hold six
basin. he put one rapidly by Lord Squeams--Lady Squeams, give a
pinch to her dog, which begin to howl piteously, put she
pocket-handkerchief to her face, and rush away as for the cabin. the
music rise up to the wild pitch of stormy excitement, and the third
syllable be conclude.
there be a little ballet, "Le Rossignol," in which Montessu and Noblet
use to be famous in those day, and which Mr. Wagg transfer to the
english stage as an opera, put his verse, of which he be a skilful
writer, to the pretty air of the ballet. it be dress in old french
costume, and little Lord Southdown now appear admirably attire in
the disguise of an old woman hobble about the stage with a faultless
crooked stick.
Trills of melody be hear behind the scene, and gurgle from a
sweet pasteboard cottage cover with rose and trelli work.
"Philomele, Philomele," cry the old woman, and Philomele come out.
More applause--it be Mrs. Rawdon Crawley in powder and patch, the
most ravissante little Marquise in the world.
she come in laughing, humming, and frisk about the stage with all the
innocence of theatrical youth--she make a curtsey. Mamma say "why,
child, you be always laugh and singing," and away she go, with--
the ROSE UPON MY BALCONY
the rise upon my balcony the morning air perfume
be leafless all the winter time and pin for the spring;
you ask I why her breath be sweet and why her cheek be bloom,
it be because the sun be out and bird begin to sing.
the nightingale, whose melody be through the greenwood ringing,
be silent when the bough be bare and wind be blow keen:
and if, Mamma, you ask of I the reason of his singing,
it be because the sun be out and all the leaf be green.
thus each perform his part, Mamma, the bird have find their voice,
the blowing rise a flush, Mamma, her bonny cheek to dye;
and therebe sunshine in my heart, Mamma, which waken and rejoice,
and so I sing and blush, Mamma, and thatbe the reason why.
during the interval of the stanzas of this ditty, the good-natured
personage address as Mamma by the singer, and whose large whisker
appear under her cap, seem very anxious to exhibit her maternal
affection by embrace the innocent creature who perform the
daughter's part. every caress be receive with loud acclamation of
laughter by the sympathize audience. at its conclusion (while the
music be perform a symphony as if ever so many bird be warble)
the whole house be unanimous for an encore: and applause and bouquet
without end be shower upon the Nightingale of the evening. Lord
Steyne's voice of applause be loud of all. Becky, the nightingale,
take the flower which he throw to she and press they to her heart
with the air of a consummate comedian. Lord Steyne be frantic with
delight. his guest' enthusiasm harmonize with his own. where be
the beautiful black-eyed Houri whose appearance in the first charade
have cause such delight? she be twice as handsome as Becky, but the
brilliancy of the latter have quite eclipse she. all voice be for
she. Stephens, Caradori, Ronzi de Begnis, people compare she to one
or the other, and agree with good reason, very likely, that have she
be an actress none on the stage could have surpass she. she have
reach her culmination: her voice rise trilling and bright over the
storm of applause, and soar as high and joyful as her triumph. there
be a ball after the dramatic entertainment, and everybody press
round Becky as the great point of attraction of the evening. the Royal
Personage declare with an oath that she be perfection, and engage
she again and again in conversation. little Becky's soul swell with
pride and delight at these honour; she see fortune, fame, fashion
before she. Lord Steyne be her slave, follow she everywhere, and
scarcely speak to any one in the room beside, and pay she the most
mark compliment and attention. she still appear in her Marquise
costume and dance a minuet with Monsieur de Truffigny, Monsieur Le Duc
de la Jabotiere's attache; and the Duke, who have all the tradition of
the ancient court, pronounce that Madame Crawley be worthy to have
be a pupil of Vestris, or to have figure at Versailles. only a
feeling of dignity, the gout, and the strong sense of duty and
personal sacrifice prevent his Excellency from dance with she
himself, and he declare in public that a lady who could talk and dance
like Mrs. Rawdon be fit to be ambassadress at any court in Europe. he
be only console when he hear that she be half a Frenchwoman by
birth. "none but a compatriot," his Excellency declare, "could have
perform that majestic dance in such a way."
then she figure in a waltz with Monsieur de Klingenspohr, the Prince
of Peterwaradin's cousin and attache. the delighted Prince, have
less retenue than his french diplomatic colleague, insist upon take
a turn with the charming creature, and twirl round the ball-room with
she, scatter the diamond out of his boot-tassel and hussar jacket
until his Highness be fairly out of breath. Papoosh Pasha himself
would have like to dance with she if that amusement have be the
custom of his country. the company make a circle round she and
applaud as wildly as if she have be a Noblet or a Taglioni.
everybody be in ecstacy; and Becky too, you may be sure. she pass
by Lady Stunnington with a look of scorn. she patronize Lady Gaunt
and her astonished and mortified sister-in-law--she ecrase all rival
charmer. as for poor Mrs. Winkworth, and her long hair and great
eye, which have make such an effect at the commencement of the
evening--where be she now? nowhere in the race. she might tear she
long hair and cry her great eye out, but there be not a person to
heed or to deplore the discomfiture.
the great triumph of all be at supper time. she be place at the
grand exclusive table with his Royal Highness the exalt personage
before mention, and the rest of the great guest. she be serve on
gold plate. she might have have pearl melt into her champagne if she
like--another Cleopatra--and the potentate of Peterwaradin would have
give half the brilliant off his jacket for a kind glance from those
dazzle eye. Jabotiere write home about she to his government. the
lady at the other table, who sup off mere silver and mark Lord
Steyne's constant attention to she, vow it be a monstrous
infatuation, a gross insult to lady of rank. if sarcasm could have
kill, Lady Stunnington would have slay she on the spot.
Rawdon Crawley be scared at these triumph. they seem to separate
his wife far than ever from he somehow. he think with a feeling
very like pain how immeasurably she be his superior.
when the hour of departure come, a crowd of young man follow she to
her carriage, for which the people without bawl, the cry be catch
up by the link-man who be station outside the tall gate of Gaunt
House, congratulate each person who issue from the gate and hop
his Lordship have enjoy this noble party.
Mrs. Rawdon Crawley's carriage, come up to the gate after due
shouting, rattle into the illuminated court-yard and drive up to the
cover way. Rawdon put his wife into the carriage, which drive off.
Mr. Wenham have propose to he to walk home, and offer the Colonel
the refreshment of a cigar.
they light their cigar by the lamp of one of the many link-boy
outside, and Rawdon walk on with his friend Wenham. two person
separate from the crowd and follow the two gentleman; and when they
have walk down Gaunt Square a few score of pace, one of the man come
up and, touch Rawdon on the shoulder, say, "beg your pardon,
Colonel, I vish to speak to you most particular." this gentleman's
acquaintance give a loud whistle as the latter speak, at which signal a
cab come clatter up from those station at the gate of Gaunt
House--and the aide-de-camp run round and place himself in front of
Colonel Crawley.
that gallant officer at once know what have befall he. he be in the
hand of the bailiff. he start back, fall against the man who
have first touch he.
"webe three on we--itbe no use bolting," the man behind say.
"itbe you, Moss, be it?" say the Colonel, who appear to know his
interlocutor. "how much be it?"
"only a small thing," whisper Mr. Moss, of Cursitor Street, Chancery
Lane, and assistant officer to the Sheriff of middlesex--"one hundred
and sixty-six, six and eight-pence, at the suit of Mr. Nathan."
"lend I a hundred, Wenham, for God's sake," poor Rawdon said--"I've
get seventy at home."
"Ihave not get ten pound in the world," say poor Mr. wenham--"good
night, my dear fellow."
"good night," say Rawdon ruefully. and Wenham walk away--and Rawdon
Crawley finish his cigar as the cab drive under Temple Bar.
CHAPTER LII
in which Lord Steyne show himself in a most amiable Light
when Lord Steyne be benevolently dispose, he do nothing by half,
and his kindness towards the Crawley family do the great honour to
his benevolent discrimination. his lordship extend his good-will to
little Rawdon: he point out to the boy's parent the necessity of
send he to a public school, that he be of an age now when
emulation, the first principle of the latin language, pugilistic
exercise, and the society of his fellow-boy would be of the great
benefit to the boy. his father object that he be not rich enough to
send the child to a good public school; his mother that Briggs be a
capital mistress for he, and have bring he on (as indeed be the
fact) famously in English, the latin rudiment, and in general
learning: but all these objection disappear before the generous
perseverance of the Marquis of Steyne. his lordship be one of the
governor of that famous old collegiate institution call the
Whitefriars. it have be a Cistercian Convent in old day, when the
Smithfield, which be contiguous to it, be a tournament ground.
Obstinate heretic use to be bring thither convenient for burn
hard by. Henry VIII, the Defender of the Faith, seize upon the
monastery and its possession and hang and torture some of the monk
who could not accommodate themselves to the pace of his reform.
finally, a great merchant buy the house and land adjoining, in
which, and with the help of other wealthy endowment of land and money,
he establish a famous foundation hospital for old man and child.
an extern school grow round the old almost monastic foundation, which
subsist still with its middle-age costume and usage--and all
Cistercians pray that it may long flourish.
of this famous house, some of the great nobleman, prelate, and
dignitary in England be governor: and as the boy be very
comfortably lodge, fed, and educate, and subsequently induct to
good scholarship at the University and living in the Church, many
little gentleman be devoted to the ecclesiastical profession from
their tender year, and there be considerable emulation to procure
nomination for the foundation. it be originally intend for the
son of poor and deserve cleric and laic, but many of the noble
governor of the Institution, with an enlarged and rather capricious
benevolence, select all sort of object for their bounty. to get an
education for nothing, and a future livelihood and profession assure,
be so excellent a scheme that some of the rich people do not
disdain it; and not only great man's relation, but great man
themselves, send their son to profit by the chance--Right Rev.
prelate send their own kinsman or the son of their clergy, while, on
the other hand, some great nobleman do not disdain to patronize the
child of their confidential servant--so that a lad enter this
establishment have every variety of youthful society wherewith to mingle.
Rawdon Crawley, though the only book which he study be the Racing
Calendar, and though his chief recollection of polite learning be
connect with the flogging which he receive at Eton in his early
youth, have that decent and honest reverence for classical learning
which all english gentleman feel, and be glad to think that his son
be to have a provision for life, perhaps, and a certain opportunity of
become a scholar. and although his boy be his chief solace and
companion, and endear to he by a thousand small tie, about which he
do not care to speak to his wife, who have all along show the utmost
indifference to their son, yet Rawdon agree at once to part with he
and to give up his own great comfort and benefit for the sake of the
welfare of the little lad. he do not know how fond he be of the
child until it become necessary to let he go away. when he be go,
he feel more sad and downcast than he care to own--far sad than the
boy himself, who be happy enough to enter a new career and find
companion of his own age. Becky burst out laugh once or twice when
the Colonel, in his clumsy, incoherent way, try to express his
sentimental sorrow at the boy's departure. the poor fellow feel that
his dear pleasure and close friend be take from he. he look
often and wistfully at the little vacant bed in his dressing-room,
where the child use to sleep. he miss he sadly of morning and
try in vain to walk in the park without he. he do not know how
solitary he be until little Rawdon be go. he like the people who
be fond of he, and would go and sit for long hour with his
good-natured sister Lady Jane, and talk to she about the virtue, and
good look, and hundred good quality of the child.
Young Rawdon's aunt, we have say, be very fond of he, as be her
little girl, who weep copiously when the time for her cousin's
departure come. the elder Rawdon be thankful for the fondness of
mother and daughter. the very good and honest feeling of the man
come out in these artless outpouring of paternal feeling in which he
indulge in their presence, and encourage by their sympathy. he
secure not only Lady Jane's kindness, but her sincere regard, by the
feeling which he manifest, and which he could not show to his own
wife. the two kinswoman meet as seldom as possible. Becky laugh
bitterly at Jane's feeling and softness; the other's kindly and gentle
nature could not but revolt at her sister's callous behaviour.
it estrange Rawdon from his wife more than he know or acknowledge to
himself. she do not care for the estrangement. indeed, she do not
miss he or anybody. she look upon he as her errand-man and humble
slave. he might be ever so depressed or sulky, and she do not mark
his demeanour, or only treat it with a sneer. she be busy think
about her position, or her pleasure, or her advancement in society;
she ought to have hold a great place in it, that be certain.
it be honest Briggs who make up the little kit for the boy which he
be to take to school. molly, the housemaid, blubber in the passage
when he go away--molly kind and faithful in spite of a long arrear of
unpaid wage. Mrs. Becky could not let her husband have the carriage
to take the boy to school. take the horse into the City!--such a
thing be never hear of. let a cab be bring. she do not offer to
kiss he when he go, nor do the child propose to embrace she; but
give a kiss to old Briggs (whom, in general, he be very shy of
caressing), and console she by point out that he be to come home
on Saturdays, when she would have the benefit of see he. as the
cab roll towards the City, Becky's carriage rattle off to the park.
she be chatter and laugh with a score of young dandy by the
Serpentine as the father and son enter at the old gate of the
school--where Rawdon leave the child and come away with a sad purer
feeling in his heart than perhaps that poor battered fellow have ever
know since he himself come out of the nursery.
he walk all the way home very dismally, and din alone with Briggs.
he be very kind to she and grateful for her love and watchfulness over
the boy. his conscience smite he that he have borrow Briggs's money
and aid in deceive she. they talk about little Rawdon a long
time, for Becky only come home to dress and go out to dinner--and then
he go off uneasily to drink tea with Lady Jane, and tell she of what
have happen, and how little Rawdon go off like a trump, and how he
be to wear a gown and little knee-breech, and how young Blackball,
Jack Blackball's son, of the old regiment, have take he in charge and
promise to be kind to he.
in the course of a week, young Blackball have constitute little Rawdon
his fag, shoe-black, and breakfast toaster; initiate he into the
mystery of the Latin Grammar; and thrash he three or four time,
but not severely. the little chap's good-natured honest face win his
way for he. he only get that degree of beating which be, no doubt,
good for he; and as for blacking shoe, toast bread, and fag in
general, be these office not deem to be necessary part of every
young english gentleman's education?
our business do not lie with the second generation and Master
Rawdon's life at school, otherwise the present tale might be carry to
any indefinite length. the Colonel go to see his son a short time
afterwards and find the lad sufficiently well and happy, grin and
laugh in his little black gown and little breech.
his father sagaciously tip Blackball, his master, a sovereign, and
secure that young gentleman's good-will towards his fag. as a protege
of the great Lord Steyne, the nephew of a County member, and son of a
Colonel and C.B., whose name appear in some of the most fashionable
party in the Morning Post, perhaps the school authority be
disposed not to look unkindly on the child. he have plenty of
pocket-money, which he spend in treat his comrade royally to
raspberry tart, and he be often allow to come home on Saturdays to
his father, who always make a jubilee of that day. when free, Rawdon
would take he to the play, or send he thither with the footman; and
on Sundays he go to church with Briggs and Lady Jane and his cousin.
Rawdon marvel over his story about school, and fight, and
fag. before long, he know the name of all the master and the
principal boy as well as little Rawdon himself. he invite little
Rawdon's crony from school, and make both the child sick with
pastry, and oyster, and porter after the play. he try to look
know over the Latin grammar when little Rawdon show he what part
of that work he be "in." "Stick to it, my boy," he say to he with
much gravity, "therebe nothing like a good classical education!
nothing!"
Becky's contempt for her husband grow great every day. "do what you
like--dine where you please--go and have ginger-beer and sawdust at
Astley's, or psalm-singing with Lady Jane--only donot expect I to busy
myself with the boy. I have your interest to attend to, as you cannot
attend to they yourself. I should like to know where you would have
be now, and in what sort of a position in society, if I have not
look after you." indeed, nobody want poor old Rawdon at the party
whither Becky use to go. she be often ask without he now. she
talk about great people as if she have the fee-simple of May Fair, and
when the Court go into mourning, she always wear black.
little Rawdon be dispose of, Lord Steyne, who take such a parental
interest in the affair of this amiable poor family, think that their
expense might be very advantageously curtail by the departure of
Miss Briggs, and that Becky be quite clever enough to take the
management of her own house. it have be narrate in a former chapter
how the benevolent nobleman have give his protegee money to pay off her
little debt to Miss Briggs, who however still remain behind with her
friend; whence my lord come to the painful conclusion that Mrs.
Crawley have make some other use of the money confide to she than that
for which her generous patron have give the loan. however, Lord Steyne
be not so rude as to impart his suspicion upon this head to Mrs.
Becky, whose feeling might be hurt by any controversy on the
money-question, and who might have a thousand painful reason for
dispose otherwise of his lordship's generous loan. but he determine
to satisfy himself of the real state of the case, and institute the
necessary inquiry in a most cautious and delicate manner.
in the first place he take an early opportunity of pump Miss Briggs.
that be not a difficult operation. a very little encouragement would
set that worthy woman to talk volubly and pour out all within she. and
one day when Mrs. Rawdon have go out to drive (as Mr. Fiche, his
lordship's confidential servant, easily learn at the livery stable
where the Crawleys keep their carriage and horse, or rather, where the
livery-man keep a carriage and horse for Mr. and Mrs. Crawley)--my
lord drop in upon the Curzon Street house--ask Briggs for a cup of
coffee--tell she that he have good account of the little boy at
school--and in five minute find out from she that Mrs. Rawdon have
give she nothing except a black silk gown, for which Miss Briggs be
immensely grateful.
he laugh within himself at this artless story. for the truth be, our
dear friend Rebecca have give he a most circumstantial narration of
Briggs's delight at receive her money--eleven hundred and twenty-five
pound--and in what security she have invest it; and what a pang
Becky herself feel in be oblige to pay away such a delightful sum
of money. "who know," the dear woman may have think within herself,
"perhaps he may give I a little more?" my lord, however, make no such
proposal to the little schemer--very likely think that he have be
sufficiently generous already.
he have the curiosity, then, to ask Miss Briggs about the state of her
private affair--and she tell his lordship candidly what her position
be--how Miss Crawley have leave she a legacy--how her relative have have
part of it--how Colonel Crawley have put out another portion, for which
she have the good security and interest--and how Mr. and Mrs. Rawdon
have kindly busy themselves with Sir Pitt, who be to dispose of the
remainder most advantageously for she, when he have time. my lord ask
how much the Colonel have already invest for she, and Miss Briggs at
once and truly tell he that the sum be six hundred and odd pound.
but as soon as she have tell her story, the voluble Briggs repent of
her frankness and besought my lord not to tell Mr. Crawley of the
confession which she have make. "the Colonel be so kind--Mr. Crawley
might be offend and pay back the money, for which she could get no
such good interest anywhere else." Lord Steyne, laughing, promise he
never would divulge their conversation, and when he and Miss Briggs
part he laugh still more.
"what an accomplished little devil it be!" think he. "what a splendid
actress and manager! she have almost get a second supply out of I the
other day; with her coax way. she beat all the woman I have ever
see in the course of all my well-spend life. they be baby compare
to she. I be a greenhorn myself, and a fool in her hand--an old fool.
she be unsurpassable in lie." his lordship's admiration for Becky rise
immeasurably at this proof of her cleverness. get the money be
nothing--but get double the sum she want, and pay nobody--it
be a magnificent stroke. and Crawley, my lord think--Crawley be not
such a fool as he look and seem. he have manage the matter cleverly
enough on his side. nobody would ever have suppose from his face and
demeanour that he know anything about this money business; and yet he
put she up to it, and have spend the money, no doubt. in this opinion
my lord, we know, be mistaken, but it influence a good deal his
behaviour towards Colonel Crawley, whom he begin to treat with even
less than that semblance of respect which he have formerly show towards
that gentleman. it never enter into the head of Mrs. Crawley's
patron that the little lady might be make a purse for herself; and,
perhaps, if the truth must be tell, he judge of Colonel Crawley by his
experience of other husband, whom he have know in the course of the
long and well-spend life which have make he acquaint with a great
deal of the weakness of mankind. my lord have buy so many man during
his life that he be surely to be pardon for suppose that he have
find the price of this one.
he tax Becky upon the point on the very first occasion when he meet
she alone, and he compliment she, good-humouredly, on her cleverness
in get more than the money which she require. Becky be only a
little take aback. it be not the habit of this dear creature to tell
falsehood, except when necessity compel, but in these great
emergency it be her practice to lie very freely; and in an instant
she be ready with another neat plausible circumstantial story which
she administer to her patron. the previous statement which she have
make to he be a falsehood--a wicked falsehood--she own it. but who
have make she tell it? "ah, my Lord," she say, "you donot know all I
have to suffer and bear in silence; you see I gay and happy before
you--you little know what I have to endure when there be no protector
near I. it be my husband, by threat and the most savage treatment,
force I to ask for that sum about which I deceive you. it be he
who, foresee that question might be ask regard the disposal of
the money, force I to account for it as I do. he take the money.
he tell I he have pay Miss Briggs; I do not want, I do not dare to
doubt he. pardon the wrong which a desperate man be force to commit,
and pity a miserable, miserable woman." she burst into tear as she
speak. persecute virtue never look more bewitchingly wretched.
they have a long conversation, drive round and round the Regent's Park
in Mrs. Crawley's carriage together, a conversation of which it be not
necessary to repeat the detail, but the upshot of it be that, when
Becky come home, she fly to her dear Briggs with a smile face and
announce that she have some very good news for she. Lord Steyne have
act in the noble and most generous manner. he be always think
how and when he could do good. now that little Rawdon be go to
school, a dear companion and friend be no long necessary to she.
she be grieve beyond measure to part with Briggs, but her mean
require that she should practise every retrenchment, and her sorrow
be mitigate by the idea that her dear Briggs would be far well
provide for by her generous patron than in her humble home. Mrs.
Pilkington, the housekeeper at Gauntly Hall, be grow exceedingly
old, feeble, and rheumatic: she be not equal to the work of
superintend that vast mansion, and must be on the look out for a
successor. it be a splendid position. the family do not go to
gauntly once in two year. at other time the housekeeper be the
mistress of the magnificent mansion--have four cover daily for her
table; be visit by the clergy and the most respectable people of the
county--be the lady of Gauntly, in fact; and the two last housekeeper
before Mrs. Pilkington have marry rector of Gauntly--but Mrs. P.
could not, be the aunt of the present Rector. the place be not to
be hers yet, but she might go down on a visit to Mrs. Pilkington and
see whether she would like to succeed she.
what word can paint the ecstatic gratitude of Briggs! all she
stipulate for be that little Rawdon should be allow to come down
and see she at the Hall. Becky promise this--anything. she run up to
her husband when he come home and tell he the joyful news. Rawdon be
glad, deuce glad; the weight be off his conscience about poor
Briggs's money. she be provide for, at any rate, but--but his mind
be disquiet. he do not seem to be all right, somehow. he tell
little Southdown what Lord Steyne have do, and the young man eye
Crawley with an air which surprise the latter.
he tell Lady Jane of this second proof of Steyne's bounty, and she,
too, look odd and alarmed; so do Sir Pitt. "she be too clever
and--and gay to be allow to go from party to party without a
companion," both say. "you must go with she, Rawdon, wherever she
go, and you must have somebody with she--one of the girl from
Queen's Crawley, perhaps, though they be rather giddy guardian for
she."
somebody Becky should have. but in the meantime it be clear that
honest Briggs must not lose her chance of settlement for life, and so
she and her bag be pack, and she set off on her journey. and so
two of Rawdon's out-sentinel be in the hand of the enemy.
Sir Pitt go and expostulate with his sister-in-law upon the subject
of the dismissal of Briggs and other matter of delicate family
interest. in vain she point out to he how necessary be the
protection of Lord Steyne for her poor husband; how cruel it would be
on their part to deprive Briggs of the position offer to she.
cajolement, coaxing, smile, tear could not satisfy Sir Pitt, and he
have something very like a quarrel with his once admire Becky. he
speak of the honour of the family, the unsullied reputation of the
Crawleys; express himself in indignant tone about she receive
those young Frenchmen--those wild young man of fashion, my Lord Steyne
himself, whose carriage be always at her door, who pass hour daily
in her company, and whose constant presence make the world talk about
she. as the head of the house he implore she to be more prudent.
Society be already speak lightly of she. Lord Steyne, though a
nobleman of the great station and talent, be a man whose
attention would compromise any woman; he besought, he implore, he
command his sister-in-law to be watchful in her intercourse with that
nobleman.
Becky promise anything and everything Pitt want; but Lord Steyne
come to her house as often as ever, and Sir Pitt's anger increase. I
wonder be Lady Jane angry or please that her husband at last find
fault with his favourite rebecca? Lord Steyne's visit continue, his
own cease, and his wife be for refuse all further intercourse with
that nobleman and decline the invitation to the charade-night which
the marchioness send to she; but Sir Pitt think it be necessary to
accept it, as his Royal Highness would be there.
although he go to the party in question, Sir Pitt quit it very
early, and his wife, too, be very glad to come away. Becky hardly so
much as speak to he or notice her sister-in-law. Pitt Crawley
declare her behaviour be monstrously indecorous, reprobate in strong
term the habit of play-act and fancy dressing as highly unbecoming
a british female, and after the charade be over, take his brother
Rawdon severely to task for appear himself and allow his wife to
join in such improper exhibition.
Rawdon say she should not join in any more such amusement--but
indeed, and perhaps from hint from his eld brother and sister, he
have already become a very watchful and exemplary domestic character. he
leave off his club and billiard. he never leave home. he take Becky
out to drive; he go laboriously with she to all her party. whenever
my Lord Steyne call, he be sure to find the Colonel. and when Becky
propose to go out without her husband, or receive invitation for
herself, he peremptorily order she to refuse they: and there be that
in the gentleman's manner which enforce obedience. little Becky, to
do her justice, be charm with Rawdon's gallantry. if he be surly,
she never be. whether friend be present or absent, she have always a
kind smile for he and be attentive to his pleasure and comfort. it
be the early day of their marriage over again: the same good humour,
prevenance, merriment, and artless confidence and regard. "how much
pleasanter it be," she would say, "to have you by my side in the
carriage than that foolish old Briggs! let we always go on so, dear
Rawdon. how nice it would be, and how happy we should always be, if we
have but the money!" he fall asleep after dinner in his chair; he do
not see the face opposite to he, haggard, weary, and terrible; it
