light up with fresh candid smile when he wake. it kiss he gaily.
he wonder that he have ever have suspicion. no, he never have
suspicion; all those dumb doubt and surly misgiving which have be
gather on his mind be mere idle jealousy. she be fond of he;
she always have be. as for she shin in society, it be no fault of
her; she be form to shine there. be there any woman who could
talk, or sing, or do anything like she? if she would but like the boy!
Rawdon think. but the mother and son never could be bring together.
and it be while Rawdon's mind be agitate with these doubt and
perplexity that the incident occur which be mention in the last
chapter, and the unfortunate Colonel find himself a prisoner away from
home.
CHAPTER LIII
a Rescue and a Catastrophe
Friend Rawdon drive on then to Mr. Moss's mansion in Cursitor Street,
and be duly induct into that dismal place of hospitality. Morning
be break over the cheerful house-top of Chancery Lane as the
rattle cab wake up the echo there. a little pink-eyed Jew-boy,
with a head as ruddy as the rise morn, let the party into the house,
and Rawdon be welcome to the ground-floor apartment by Mr. Moss, his
travel companion and host, who cheerfully ask he if he would
like a glass of something warm after his drive.
the Colonel be not so depressed as some mortal would be, who,
quit a palace and a placen uxor, find themselves bar into a
spunge-house; for, if the truth must be tell, he have be a lodger at
Mr. Moss's establishment once or twice before. we have not think it
necessary in the previous course of this narrative to mention these
trivial little domestic incident: but the reader may be assure that
they cannot unfrequently occur in the life of a man who live on nothing
a year.
upon his first visit to Mr. Moss, the Colonel, then a bachelor, have
be liberate by the generosity of his aunt; on the second mishap,
little Becky, with the great spirit and kindness, have borrow a sum
of money from Lord Southdown and have coax her husband's creditor (who
be her shawl, velvet-gown, lace pocket-handkerchief, trinket, and
gim-crack purveyor, indeed) to take a portion of the sum claim and
Rawdon's promissory note for the remainder: so on both these occasion
the capture and release have be conduct with the utmost gallantry on
all side, and Moss and the Colonel be therefore on the very good of
term.
"youwill find your old bed, Colonel, and everything comfortable," that
gentleman say, "as I may honestly say. you may be pretty sure its kep
air, and by the good of company, too. it be slep in the night afore
last by the Honorable Capting Famish, of the Fiftieth Dragoons, whose
Mar take he out, after a fortnight, jest to punish he, she say.
but, Law bless you, I promise you, he punish my champagne, and have a
party ere every night--reglar tip-top swell, down from the club and
the West End--Capting Ragg, the honorable Deuceace, who live in the
Temple, and some feller as know a good glass of wine, I warrant you.
Ihave get a Doctor of Diwinity upstairs, five gent in the coffee-room,
and Mrs. Moss have a tably-dy-hoty at half-past five, and a little
card or music afterwards, when we shall be most happy to see you."
"Iwill ring when I want anything," say Rawdon and go quietly to his
bedroom. he be an old soldier, we have say, and not to be disturb
by any little shock of fate. a weak man would have send off a
letter to his wife on the instant of his capture. "but what be the use
of disturb her night's rest?" think Rawdon. "she willnot know
whether I be in my room or not. it will be time enough to write to she
when she have have her sleep out, and I have have mine. itbe only a
hundred-and-seventy, and the deuce be in it if we cannot raise that."
and so, think about little Rawdon (whom he would not have know that
he be in such a queer place), the Colonel turn into the bed lately
occupy by Captain Famish and fall asleep. it be ten o'clock when he
wake up, and the ruddy-head youth bring he, with conscious pride,
a fine silver dressing-case, wherewith he might perform the operation
of shaving. indeed Mr. Moss's house, though somewhat dirty, be
splendid throughout. there be dirty tray, and wine-cooler en
permanence on the sideboard, huge dirty gilt cornice, with dingy
yellow satin hanging to the bar window which look into Cursitor
Street--vast and dirty gilt picture frame surround piece sport
and sacred, all of which work be by the great master--and
fetch the great price, too, in the bill transaction, in the
course of which they be sell and buy over and over again. the
Colonel's breakfast be serve to he in the same dingy and gorgeous
plat ware. Miss Moss, a dark-eyed maid in curl-paper, appear with
the teapot, and, smile, ask the Colonel how he have slep? and she
bring he in the Morning Post, with the name of all the great people
who have figure at Lord Steyne's entertainment the night before. it
contain a brilliant account of the festivity and of the beautiful
and accomplish Mrs. Rawdon Crawley's admirable personification.
after a lively chat with this lady (who sit on the edge of the
breakfast table in an easy attitude display the drapery of her
stocking and an ex-white satin shoe, which be down at heel), Colonel
Crawley call for pen and ink, and paper, and be ask how many
sheet, choose one which be bring to he between Miss Moss's own
finger and thumb. many a sheet have that dark-eyed damsel bring in;
many a poor fellow have scrawl and blot hurried line of entreaty
and pace up and down that awful room until his messenger bring back
the reply. poor man always use messenger instead of the post. who
have not have their letter, with the wafer wet, and the announcement
that a person be wait in the hall?
now on the score of his application, Rawdon have not many misgiving.
DEAR BECKY, (Rawdon write)
I hope you slept well. donot be FRIGHTENED if I donot bring you in
your COFFY. last night as I be come home smoaking, I meet with an
ACCADENT. I be nab by Moss of Cursitor Street--from whose GILT and
SPLENDID PARLER I write this--the same that have I this time two year.
Miss Moss bring in my tea--she be grow very FAT, and, as usual, have
her stockens DOWN AT HEAL.
itbe Nathan's business--a hundred-and-fifty--with cost,
hundred-and-seventy. please send I my desk and some CLOTHS--I'm in
pump and a white tye (something like Miss M's stockings)--i've seventy
in it. and as soon as you get this, drive to Nathan's--offer he
seventy-five down, and ASK HIM to RENEW--say Iwill take wine--we may as
well have some dinner sherry; but not PICTURS, theybe too dear.
if he willnot stand it. take my ticker and such of your thing as you
can spare, and send they to Balls--we must, of coarse, have the sum
to-night. it willnot do to let it stand over, as to-morrow's Sunday; the
bed here be not very CLEAN, and there may be other thing out against
I--I'm glad it an't Rawdon's Saturday for come home. God bless you.
your in haste, R. C. P.S. make haste and come.
this letter, seal with a wafer, be dispatch by one of the
messenger who be always hang about Mr. Moss's establishment, and
Rawdon, have see he depart, go out in the court-yard and smoke
his cigar with a tolerably easy mind--in spite of the bar
overhead--for Mr. Moss's court-yard be rail in like a cage, lest the
gentleman who be board with he should take a fancy to escape from
his hospitality.
three hour, he calculate, would be the utmost time require, before
Becky should arrive and open his prison door, and he pass these
pretty cheerfully in smoking, in read the paper, and in the
coffee-room with an acquaintance, Captain Walker, who happen to be
there, and with whom he cut for sixpence for some hour, with pretty
equal luck on either side.
but the day pass away and no messenger return--no Becky. Mr.
Moss's tably-dy-hoty be serve at the appoint hour of half-past
five, when such of the gentleman lodge in the house as could afford
to pay for the banquet come and partook of it in the splendid front
parlour before describe, and with which Mr. Crawley's temporary
lodging communicate, when Miss M. (Miss Hem, as her papa call she)
appear without the curl-paper of the morning, and Mrs. Hem do the
honour of a prime boil leg of mutton and turnip, of which the
Colonel eat with a very faint appetite. ask whether he would "stand"
a bottle of champagne for the company, he consent, and the lady
drink to his 'ealth, and Mr. Moss, in the most polite manner, "look
towards he."
in the midst of this repast, however, the doorbell be hear--young
Moss of the ruddy hair rise up with the key and answer the summon,
and come back, tell the Colonel that the messenger have return with
a bag, a desk and a letter, which he give he. "no ceramony, Colonel,
I beg," say Mrs. Moss with a wave of her hand, and he open the
letter rather tremulously. it be a beautiful letter, highly scented,
on a pink paper, and with a light green seal.
MON PAUVRE CHER PETIT, (Mrs. Crawley write)
I could not sleep one wink for think of what have become of my odious
old monstre, and only get to rest in the morning after send for Mr.
Blench (for I be in a fever), who give I a compose draught and leave
order with Finette that I should be disturb on no account. so that
my poor old man's messenger, who have bien mauvaise mine Finette say,
and sentoit le Genievre, remain in the hall for some hour wait my
bell. you may fancy my state when I read your poor dear old ill-spelt
letter.
Ill as I be, I instantly call for the carriage, and as soon as I be
dress (though I couldnot drink a drop of chocolate--I assure you I
couldnot without my monstre to bring it to I), I drive ventre a terre
to Nathan's. I see he--I weep--I cry--I fall at his odious knee.
nothing would mollify the horrid man. he would have all the money, he
say, or keep my poor monstre in prison. I drive home with the
intention of pay that triste visite chez mon oncle (when every
trinket I have should be at your disposal though they would not fetch a
hundred pound, for some, you know, be with ce cher oncle already),
and find Milor there with the bulgarian old sheep-face monster, who
have come to compliment I upon last night's performance. Paddington
come in, too, drawling and lisping and twiddle his hair; so do
Champignac, and his chef--everybody with foison of compliment and
pretty speech--plague poor I, who long to be rid of they, and
be think every moment of the time of mon pauvre prisonnier.
when they be go, I go down on my knee to Milor; tell he we be
go to pawn everything, and beg and pray he to give I two
hundred pound. he pish'd and psha'd in a fury--tell I not to be such
a fool as to pawn--and say he would see whether he could lend I the
money. at last he go away, promise that he would send it I in the
morning: when I will bring it to my poor old monster with a kiss from
his affectionate
BECKY
I be write in bed. oh I have such a headache and such a heartache!
when Rawdon read over this letter, he turn so red and look so
savage that the company at the table d'hote easily perceive that bad
news have reach he. all his suspicion, which he have be try to
banish, return upon he. she could not even go out and sell her
trinket to free he. she could laugh and talk about compliment pay
to she, whilst he be in prison. who have put he there? Wenham have
walk with he. be there.... he could hardly bear to think of what
he suspect. leave the room hurriedly, he run into his own--open
his desk, write two hurried line, which he direct to Sir Pitt or
Lady Crawley, and bade the messenger carry they at once to Gaunt
Street, bid he to take a cab, and promise he a guinea if he be
back in an hour.
in the note he besought his dear brother and sister, for the sake of
God, for the sake of his dear child and his honour, to come to he and
relieve he from his difficulty. he be in prison, he want a hundred
pound to set he free--he entreat they to come to he.
he go back to the dining-room after dispatch his messenger and
call for more wine. he laugh and talk with a strange
boisterousness, as the people think. sometimes he laugh madly at
his own fear and go on drink for an hour, listen all the while
for the carriage which be to bring his fate back.
at the expiration of that time, wheel be hear whirl up to the
gate--the young janitor go out with his gate-key. it be a lady
whom he let in at the bailiff's door.
"Colonel Crawley," she say, tremble very much. he, with a know
look, lock the outer door upon she--then unlocked and open the
inner one, and call out, "Colonel, yoube want," lead she into the
back parlour, which he occupy.
Rawdon come in from the dining-parlour where all those people be
carouse, into his back room; a flare of coarse light follow he
into the apartment where the lady stand, still very nervous.
"it be I, Rawdon," she say in a timid voice, which she strove to
render cheerful. "it be Jane." Rawdon be quite overcome by that kind
voice and presence. he run up to she--catch she in his arm--gasp
out some inarticulate word of thank and fairly sob on her
shoulder. she do not know the cause of his emotion.
the bill of Mr. Moss be quickly settle, perhaps to the
disappointment of that gentleman, who have count on have the Colonel
as his guest over Sunday at least; and Jane, with beam smile and
happiness in her eye, carry away Rawdon from the bailiff's house,
and they go homeward in the cab in which she have hasten to his
release. "Pitt be go to a parliamentary dinner," she say, "when
Rawdon's note come, and so, dear Rawdon, I--I come myself"; and she put
her kind hand in his. perhaps it be well for Rawdon Crawley that Pitt
be away at that dinner. Rawdon thank his sister a hundred time,
and with an ardour of gratitude which touch and almost alarmed that
soft-hearted woman. "oh," say he, in his rude, artless way, "you--you
donot know how Ibe change since Ihave know you, and--and little Rawdy.
I--I'd like to change somehow. you see I want--I want--to be--" he do
not finish the sentence, but she could interpret it. and that night
after he leave she, and as she sit by her own little boy's bed, she
pray humbly for that poor way-wear sinner.
Rawdon leave she and walk home rapidly. it be nine o'clock at night.
he run across the street and the great square of Vanity Fair, and at
length come up breathless opposite his own house. he start back and
fall against the railing, tremble as he look up. the drawing-room
window be blaze with light. she have say that she be in bed and
ill. he stand there for some time, the light from the room on his
pale face.
he take out his door-key and let himself into the house. he could hear
laughter in the upper room. he be in the ball-dress in which he have
be capture the night before. he go silently up the stair,
lean against the banister at the stair-head. nobody be stir
in the house besides--all the servant have be send away. Rawdon hear
laughter within--laughter and singing. Becky be sing a snatch of
the song of the night before; a hoarse voice shout "Brava!
Brava!"--it be Lord Steyne's.
Rawdon open the door and go in. a little table with a dinner be
lay out--and wine and plate. Steyne be hang over the sofa on
which Becky sit. the wretched woman be in a brilliant full toilette,
her arm and all her finger sparkle with bracelet and ring, and
the brilliant on her breast which Steyne have give she. he have she
hand in his, and be bow over it to kiss it, when Becky start up
with a faint scream as she catch sight of Rawdon's white face. at the
next instant she try a smile, a horrid smile, as if to welcome she
husband; and Steyne rise up, grind his tooth, pale, and with fury in
his look.
he, too, attempt a laugh--and come forward hold out his hand.
"what, come back! how d'ye do, Crawley?" he say, the nerve of his
mouth twitching as he try to grin at the intruder.
there be that in Rawdon's face which cause Becky to fle herself
before he. "I be innocent, Rawdon," she say; "before God, I be
innocent." she clung hold of his coat, of his hand; her own be all
cover with serpent, and ring, and bauble. "I be innocent. say I
be innocent," she say to Lord Steyne.
he think a trap have be lay for he, and be as furious with the
wife as with the husband. "you innocent! damn you," he scream out.
"you innocent! why every trinket you have on your body be pay for by
I. I have give you thousand of pound, which this fellow have spend
and for which he have sell you. innocent, by ----! yoube as innocent as
your mother, the ballet-girl, and your husband the bully. donot think
to frighten I as you have do other. make way, sir, and let I
pass"; and Lord Steyne seize up his hat, and, with flame in his eye,
and look his enemy fiercely in the face, march upon he, never for
a moment doubt that the other would give way.
but Rawdon Crawley spring out, seize he by the neckcloth, until
Steyne, almost strangle, writhe and bend under his arm. "you lie,
you dog!" say Rawdon. "you lie, you coward and villain!" and he strike
the peer twice over the face with his open hand and fling he bleed
to the ground. it be all do before Rebecca could interpose. she
stand there tremble before he. she admire her husband, strong,
brave, and victorious.
"come here," he say. she come up at once.
"take off those thing." she begin, tremble, pull the jewel from
her arm, and the ring from her shake finger, and hold they all in
a heap, quiver and look up at he. "throw they down," he say,
and she drop they. he tear the diamond ornament out of her breast
and fling it at Lord Steyne. it cut he on his bald forehead. Steyne
wear the scar to his die day.
"come upstairs," Rawdon say to his wife. "donot kill I, Rawdon," she
say. he laugh savagely. "I want to see if that man lie about the
money as he have about I. have he give you any?"
"no," say Rebecca, "that is--"
"give I your key," Rawdon answer, and they go out together.
Rebecca give he all the key but one, and she be in hope that he
would not have remark the absence of that. it belong to the little
desk which Amelia have give she in early day, and which she keep in a
secret place. but Rawdon fling open box and wardrobe, throw the
multifarious trumpery of their content here and there, and at last he
find the desk. the woman be force to open it. it contain paper,
love-letter many year old--all sort of small trinket and woman's
memoranda. and it contain a pocket-book with bank-note. some of
these be date ten year back, too, and one be quite a fresh one--a
note for a thousand pound which Lord Steyne have give she.
"do he give you this?" Rawdon say.
"yes," rebecca answer.
"Iwill send it to he to-day," Rawdon say (for day have dawn again,
and many hour have pass in this search), "and I will pay Briggs, who
be kind to the boy, and some of the debt. you will let I know where
I shall send the rest to you. you might have spar I a hundred
pound, Becky, out of all this--I have always share with you."
"I be innocent," say Becky. and he leave she without another word.
what be her thought when he leave she? she remain for hour after
he be go, the sunshine pour into the room, and rebecca sitting
alone on the bed's edge. the drawer be all open and their
content scatter about--dress and feather, scarf and trinket, a
heap of tumbled vanity lie in a wreck. her hair be fall over
her shoulder; her gown be tear where Rawdon have wrench the
brilliant out of it. she hear he go downstairs a few minute after
he leave she, and the door slam and close on he. she know he
would never come back. he be go forever. would he kill
himself?--she think--not until after he have meet Lord Steyne. she
think of her long past life, and all the dismal incident of it. ah,
how dreary it seem, how miserable, lonely and profitless! should she
take laudanum, and end it, to have do with all hope, scheme, debt,
and triumph? the French maid find she in this position--sit in
the midst of her miserable ruin with clasped hand and dry eye. the
woman be her accomplice and in Steyne's pay. "Mon Dieu, madame, what
have happen?" she ask.
what have happen? be she guilty or not? she say not, but who could
tell what be truth which come from those lip, or if that corrupt
heart be in this case pure?
all her lie and her scheme, an her selfishness and her wile, all her
wit and genius have come to this bankruptcy. the woman close the
curtain and, with some entreaty and show of kindness, persuade she
mistress to lie down on the bed. then she go below and gather up
the trinket which have be lie on the floor since Rebecca drop
they there at her husband's order, and Lord Steyne go away.
CHAPTER LIV
Sunday after the Battle
the mansion of Sir Pitt Crawley, in Great Gaunt Street, be just
begin to dress itself for the day, as Rawdon, in his evening
costume, which he have now wear two day, pass by the scared female
who be scour the step and enter into his brother's study. Lady
Jane, in her morning-gown, be up and above stair in the nursery
superintend the toilette of her child and listen to the
morning prayer which the little creature perform at her knee.
every morning she and they perform this duty privately, and before
the public ceremonial at which Sir Pitt preside and at which all the
people of the household be expect to assemble. Rawdon sit down in
the study before the Baronet's table, set out with the orderly blue
book and the letter, the neatly docket bill and symmetrical
pamphlet, the lock account-book, desk, and dispatch box, the
Bible, the Quarterly Review, and the Court Guide, which all stand as if
on parade await the inspection of their chief.
a book of family sermon, one of which Sir Pitt be in the habit of
administer to his family on Sunday morning, lie ready on the study
table, and await his judicious selection. and by the sermon-book
be the Observer newspaper, damp and neatly fold, and for Sir Pitt's
own private use. his gentleman alone take the opportunity of peruse
the newspaper before he lay it by his master's desk. before he have
bring it into the study that morning, he have read in the journal a
flame account of "festivity at Gaunt House," with the name of all
the distinguished personage invite by tho Marquis of Steyne to meet
his Royal Highness. have make comment upon this entertainment to
the housekeeper and her niece as they be take early tea and hot
butter toast in the former lady's apartment, and wonder how the
Rawding Crawleys could git on, the valet have damp and fold the
paper once more, so that it look quite fresh and innocent against the
arrival of the master of the house.
Poor Rawdon take up the paper and begin to try and read it until his
brother should arrive. but the print fall blank upon his eye, and he
do not know in the least what he be read. the Government news and
appointment (which Sir Pitt as a public man be bind to peruse,
otherwise he would by no mean permit the introduction of Sunday paper
into his household), the theatrical criticism, the fight for a hundred
pound a side between the Barking Butcher and the Tutbury Pet, the
Gaunt House chronicle itself, which contain a most complimentary
though guard account of the famous charade of which Mrs. Becky have
be the heroine--all these pass as in a haze before Rawdon, as he
sit wait the arrival of the chief of the family.
punctually, as the shrill-toned bell of the black marble study clock
begin to chime nine, Sir Pitt make his appearance, fresh, neat, smugly
shave, with a waxy clean face, and stiff shirt collar, his scanty hair
comb and oil, trim his nail as he descend the stair
majestically, in a starched cravat and a grey flannel dressing-gown--a
real old english gentleman, in a word--a model of neatness and every
propriety. he start when he see poor Rawdon in his study in tumbled
clothe, with blood-shot eye, and his hair over his face. he think
his brother be not sober, and have be out all night on some orgy.
"good gracious, Rawdon," he say, with a blank face, "what bring you
here at this time of the morning? why benot you at home?"
"home," say Rawdon with a wild laugh. "donot be frightened, Pitt. Ibe
not drunk. shut the door; I want to speak to you."
Pitt close the door and come up to the table, where he sit down in the
other arm-chair--that one place for the reception of the steward,
agent, or confidential visitor who come to transact business with the
Baronet--and trim his nail more vehemently than ever.
"Pitt, itbe all over with I," the Colonel say after a pause. "Ibe
do."
"I always say it would come to this," the Baronet cry peevishly, and
beat a tune with his clean-trim nail. "I warn you a thousand
time. I cannot help you any more. every shilling of my money be tie
up. even the hundred pound that Jane take you last night be
promise to my lawyer to-morrow morning, and the want of it will put I
to great inconvenience. I donot mean to say that I willnot assist you
ultimately. but as for pay your creditor in full, I might as well
hope to pay the National Debt. it be madness, sheer madness, to think
of such a thing. you must come to a compromise. itbe a painful thing
for the family, but everybody do it. there be George Kitely, Lord
Ragland's son, go through the Court last week, and be what they call
whitewash, I believe. Lord Ragland would not pay a shilling for he,
and--"
"itbe not money I want," Rawdon break in. "Ibe not come to you about
myself. never mind what happen to I."
"what be the matter, then?" say Pitt, somewhat relieved.
"itbe the boy," say Rawdon in a husky voice. "I want you to promise
I that you will take charge of he when Ibe go. that dear good wife
of yours have always be good to he; and hebe fonder of she than he be
of his . . .--damn it. look here, Pitt--you know that I be to have
have Miss Crawley's money. I benot bring up like a young brother,
but be always encourage to be extravagant and kep idle. but for this
I might have be quite a different man. I donot do my duty with the
regiment so bad. you know how I be throw over about the money, and
who get it."
"after the sacrifice I have make, and the manner in which I have stand
by you, I think this sort of reproach be useless," Sir Pitt say.
"your marriage be your own doing, not mine."
"thatbe over now," say Rawdon. "thatbe over now." and the word be
wrench from he with a groan, which make his brother start.
"Good God! be she dead?" Sir Pitt say with a voice of genuine alarm
and commiseration.
"I wish I be," Rawdon reply. "if it benot for little Rawdon Iwould
have cut my throat this morning--and that damn villain's too."
Sir Pitt instantly guess the truth and surmise that Lord Steyne be
the person whose life Rawdon wish to take. the Colonel tell his
senior briefly, and in broken accent, the circumstance of the case.
"it be a regular plan between that scoundrel and she," he say. "the
bailiff be put upon I; I be take as I be go out of his house;
when I write to she for money, she say she be ill in bed and put I
off to another day. and when I get home I find she in diamond and
sit with that villain alone." he then go on to describe hurriedly
the personal conflict with Lord Steyne. to an affair of that nature,
of course, he say, there be but one issue, and after his conference
with his brother, he be go away to make the necessary arrangement
for the meeting which must ensue. "and as it may end fatally with I,"
Rawdon say with a broken voice, "and as the boy have no mother, I must
leave he to you and Jane, Pitt--only it will be a comfort to I if you
will promise I to be his friend."
the eld brother be much affected, and shake Rawdon's hand with a
cordiality seldom exhibit by he. Rawdon pass his hand over his
shaggy eyebrow. "thank you, brother," say he. "I know I can trust
your word."
"I will, upon my honour," the Baronet say. and thus, and almost
mutely, this bargain be strike between they.
then Rawdon take out of his pocket the little pocket-book which he have
discover in Becky's desk, and from which he draw a bundle of the
note which it contain. "here's six hundred," he said--"you donot
know I be so rich. I want you to give the money to Briggs, who lend
it to we--and who be kind to the boy--and Ihave always feel ashamed of
have take the poor old woman's money. and herebe some more--I've
only keep back a few pound--which Becky may as well have, to get on
with." as he speak he take hold of the other note to give to his
brother, but his hand shake, and he be so agitated that the
pocket-book fall from he, and out of it the thousand-pound note which
have be the last of the unlucky Becky's winning.
Pitt stoop and pick they up, amazed at so much wealth. "not that,"
Rawdon say. "I hope to put a bullet into the man whom that belong
to." he have think to himself, it would be a fine revenge to wrap a
ball in the note and kill Steyne with it.
after this colloquy the brother once more shake hand and part. Lady
Jane have hear of the Colonel's arrival, and be wait for her
husband in the adjoining dining-room, with female instinct, augur
evil. the door of the dining-room happen to be leave open, and the
lady of course be issue from it as the two brother pass out of
the study. she hold out her hand to Rawdon and say she be glad he
be come to breakfast, though she could perceive, by his haggard
unshorn face and the dark look of her husband, that there be very
little question of breakfast between they. Rawdon mutter some
excuse about an engagement, squeeze hard the timid little hand which
his sister-in-law reach out to he. her implore eye could read
nothing but calamity in his face, but he go away without another
word. nor do Sir Pitt vouchsafe she any explanation. the child
come up to salute he, and he kiss they in his usual frigid manner.
the mother take both of they close to herself, and hold a hand of each
of they as they kneel down to prayer, which Sir Pitt read to they, and
to the servant in their Sunday suit or livery, range upon chair
on the other side of the hiss tea-urn. Breakfast be so late that
day, in consequence of the delay which have occur, that the
church-bell begin to ring whilst they be sit over their meal;
and Lady Jane be too ill, she say, to go to church, though her
thought have be entirely astray during the period of family devotion.
Rawdon Crawley meanwhile hurry on from Great Gaunt Street, and
knock at the great bronze Medusa's head which stand on the portal
of Gaunt House, bring out the purple Silenus in a red and silver
waistcoat who act as porter of that palace. the man be scared also
by the Colonel's dishevelled appearance, and bar the way as if
afraid that the other be go to force it. but Colonel Crawley only
take out a card and enjoin he particularly to send it in to Lord
Steyne, and to mark the address write on it, and say that Colonel
Crawley would be all day after one o'clock at the Regent Club in St.
James's Street--not at home. the fat red-faced man look after he
with astonishment as he stride away; so do the people in their Sunday
clothe who be out so early; the charity-boy with shin face,
the greengrocer loll at his door, and the publican shut his
shutter in the sunshine, against service commence. the people joke
at the cab-stand about his appearance, as he take a carriage there, and
tell the driver to drive he to Knightsbridge Barracks.
all the bell be jangle and tolling as he reach that place. he
might have see his old acquaintance Amelia on her way from Brompton to
Russell Square, have he be look out. troop of school be on
their march to church, the shiny pavement and outside of coach in
the suburb be throng with people out upon their Sunday pleasure;
but the Colonel be much too busy to take any heed of these phenomenon,
and, arrive at Knightsbridge, speedily make his way up to the room of
his old friend and comrade Captain Macmurdo, who Crawley find, to his
satisfaction, be in barrack.
Captain Macmurdo, a veteran officer and Waterloo man, greatly like by
his regiment, in which want of money alone prevent he from attain
the high rank, be enjoy the forenoon calmly in bed. he have
be at a fast supper-party, give the night before by Captain the
honourable George Cinqbars, at his house in Brompton Square, to several
young man of the regiment, and a number of lady of the corp de
ballet, and old Mac, who be at home with people of all age and rank,
and consort with general, dog-fancier, opera-dancer, bruiser, and
every kind of person, in a word, be rest himself after the night's
labour, and, not be on duty, be in bed.
his room be hang round with boxing, sporting, and dance picture,
present to he by comrade as they retire from the regiment, and
marry and settle into quiet life. and as he be now nearly fifty
year of age, twenty-four of which he have pass in the corps, he have a
singular museum. he be one of the good shot in England, and, for a
heavy man, one of the good rider; indeed, he and Crawley have be
rival when the latter be in the Army. to be brief, Mr. Macmurdo be
lie in bed, read in Bell's Life an account of that very fight
between the Tutbury Pet and the Barking Butcher, which have be before
mention--a venerable bristly warrior, with a little close-shave grey
head, with a silk nightcap, a red face and nose, and a great dye
moustache.
when Rawdon tell the Captain he want a friend, the latter know
perfectly well on what duty of friendship he be call to act, and
indeed have conduct score of affair for his acquaintance with the
great prudence and skill. his Royal Highness the late lamented
Commander-in-Chief have have the great regard for Macmurdo on this
account, and he be the common refuge of gentleman in trouble.
"whatbe the row about, Crawley, my boy?" say the old warrior. "no
more gambling business, hay, like that when we shoot Captain Marker?"
"itbe about--about my wife," Crawley answer, cast down his eye
and turn very red.
the other give a whistle. "I always say shewould throw you over," he
begin--indeed there be bet in the regiment and at the club
regard the probable fate of Colonel Crawley, so lightly be his
wife's character esteem by his comrade and the world; but see the
savage look with which Rawdon answer the expression of this opinion,
Macmurdo do not think fit to enlarge upon it far.
"be there no way out of it, old boy?" the Captain continue in a grave
tone. "be it only suspicion, you know, or--or what be it? any letter?
cannot you keep it quiet? good not make any noise about a thing of that
sort if you can help it." "think of his only find she out now," the
Captain think to himself, and remember a hundred particular
conversation at the mess-table, in which Mrs. Crawley's reputation have
be tear to shred.
"therebe no way but one out of it," Rawdon replied--"and therebe only a
way out of it for one of we, Mac--do you understand? I be put out of
the way--arrest--I find them alone together. I tell he he be a
liar and a coward, and knock he down and thrash he."
"serve he right," Macmurdo say. "who be it?"
Rawdon answer it be Lord Steyne.
"the deuce! a Marquis! they say he--that is, they say you--"
"what the devil do you mean?" roar out Rawdon; "do you mean that you
ever hear a fellow doubt about my wife and donot tell I, Mac?"
"the world's very censorious, old boy," the other reply. "what the
deuce be the good of my tell you what any tom-fool talk about?"
"it be damn unfriendly, Mac," say Rawdon, quite overcome; and,
cover his face with his hand, he give way to an emotion, the sight
of which cause the tough old campaigner opposite he to wince with
sympathy. "hold up, old boy," he say; "great man or not, wewill put a
bullet in he, damn he. as for woman, theybe all so."
"you donot know how fond I be of that one," Rawdon say,
half-inarticulately. "Damme, I follow she like a footman. I give up
everything I have to she. Ibe a beggar because I would marry she. by
Jove, sir, Ihave pawn my own watch in order to get she anything she
fancy; and she shebe be make a purse for herself all the time,
and grudge I a hundred pound to get I out of quod." he then fiercely
and incoherently, and with an agitation under which his counsellor have
never before see he labour, tell Macmurdo the circumstance of the
story. his adviser catch at some stray hint in it. "she may be
innocent, after all," he say. "she say so. Steyne have be a hundred
time alone with she in the house before."
"it may be so," Rawdon answer sadly, "but this donot look very
innocent": and he show the Captain the thousand-pound note which he
have find in Becky's pocket-book. "this be what he give she, Mac, and
she kep it unknown to I; and with this money in the house, she refuse
to stand by I when I be lock up." the Captain could not but own
that the secreting of the money have a very ugly look.
whilst they be engage in their conference, Rawdon dispatch Captain
Macmurdo's servant to Curzon Street, with an order to the domestic
there to give up a bag of clothe of which the Colonel have great need.
and during the man's absence, and with great labour and a Johnson's
Dictionary, which stand they in much stead, Rawdon and his second
compose a letter, which the latter be to send to Lord Steyne.
Captain Macmurdo have the honour of wait upon the Marquis of Steyne,
on the part of Colonel Rawdon Crawley, and beg to intimate that he
be empower by the Colonel to make any arrangement for the meeting
which, he have no doubt, it be his Lordship's intention to demand, and
which the circumstance of the morning have render inevitable.
Captain Macmurdo beg Lord Steyne, in the most polite manner, to
appoint a friend, with whom he (Captain M.M.) might communicate, and
desire that the meeting might take place with as little delay as
possible.
in a postscript the Captain state that he have in his possession a
bank-note for a large amount, which Colonel Crawley have reason to
suppose be the property of the Marquis of Steyne. and he be anxious,
on the Colonel's behalf, to give up the note to its owner.
by the time this note be compose, the Captain's servant return from
his mission to Colonel Crawley's house in Curzon Street, but without
the carpet-bag and portmanteau, for which he have be send, and with a
very puzzled and odd face.
"they willnot give them up," say the man; "therebe a regular shinty in
the house, and everything at six and seven. the landlord's come in
and take possession. the servant be a drinkin' up in the
drawingroom. they say--they say you have go off with the plate,
Colonel"--the man add after a pause--"one of the servant be off
already. and Simpson, the man as be very noisy and drunk indeed, say
nothing shall go out of the house until his wage be pay up."
the account of this little revolution in May Fair astonish and give a
little gaiety to an otherwise very triste conversation. the two
officer laugh at Rawdon's discomfiture.
"Ibe glad the little 'un benot at home," Rawdon say, bite his nail.
"you remember he, Mac, donot you, in the Riding School? how he sit the
kicker to be sure! donot he?"
"that he do, old boy," say the good-natured Captain.
little Rawdon be then sit, one of fifty gown boy, in the Chapel
of Whitefriars School, thinking, not about the sermon, but about go
home next Saturday, when his father would certainly tip he and perhaps
would take he to the play.
"hebe a regular trump, that boy," the father go on, still muse
about his son. "I say, Mac, if anything go wrong--if I drop--I
should like you to--to go and see he, you know, and say that I be
very fond of he, and that. and--dash it--old chap, give he these
gold sleeve-button: itbe all Ihave get." he cover his face with his
black hand, over which the tear roll and make furrow of white.
Mr. Macmurdo have also occasion to take off his silk night-cap and rub
it across his eye.
"go down and order some breakfast," he say to his man in a loud
cheerful voice. "whatwill you have, Crawley? some devil kidney and
a herring--letus say. and, Clay, lie out some dress thing for the
Colonel: we be always pretty much of a size, Rawdon, my boy, and
neither of we ride so light as we do when we first enter the corps."
with which, and leave the Colonel to dress himself, Macmurdo turn
round towards the wall, and resume the perusal of Bell's life, until
such time as his friend's toilette be complete and he be at liberty
to commence his own.
this, as he be about to meet a lord, Captain Macmurdo perform with
particular care. he wax his mustachio into a state of brilliant
polish and put on a tight cravat and a trim buff waistcoat, so that all
the young officer in the mess-room, whither Crawley have precede his
friend, compliment Mac on his appearance at breakfast and ask if he
be go to be married that Sunday.
CHAPTER LV
in which the same subject be pursue
Becky do not rally from the state of stupor and confusion in which the
event of the previous night have plunge her intrepid spirit until the
bell of the Curzon Street Chapels be ring for afternoon service,
and rise from her bed she begin to ply her own bell, in order to
summon the french maid who have leave she some hour before.
Mrs. Rawdon Crawley ring many time in vain; and though, on the last
occasion, she ring with such vehemence as to pull down the bell-rope,
Mademoiselle Fifine do not make her appearance--no, not though her
mistress, in a great pet, and with the bell-rope in her hand, come out
to the landing-place with her hair over her shoulder and scream out
repeatedly for her attendant.
the truth be, she have quit the premise for many hour, and upon
that permission which be call french leave among we after pick up
the trinket in the drawing-room, Mademoiselle have ascend to her own
apartment, pack and cord her own box there, trip out and
call a cab for herself, bring down her trunk with her own hand,
and without ever so much as ask the aid of any of the other
servant, who would probably have refuse it, as they hat she
cordially, and without wish any one of they good-bye, have make she
exit from Curzon Street.
the game, in her opinion, be over in that little domestic
establishment. Fifine go off in a cab, as we have know more exalted
person of her nation to do under similar circumstance: but, more
provident or lucky than these, she secure not only her own property,
but some of her mistress's (if indeed that lady could be say to have
any property at all)--and not only carry off the trinket before
allude to, and some favourite dress on which she have long keep she
eye, but four richly gild Louis Quatorze candlestick, six gilt album,
keepsake, and book of Beauty, a gold enamel snuff-box which have
once belong to Madame du Barri, and the sweet little inkstand and
mother-of-pearl blot book, which Becky use when she compose she
charming little pink note, have vanish from the premise in Curzon
Street together with Mademoiselle Fifine, and all the silver lay on
the table for the little festin which Rawdon interrupt. the plated
ware Mademoiselle leave behind she be too cumbrous, probably for which
reason, no doubt, she also leave the fire iron, the chimney-glass,
and the rosewood cottage piano.
a lady very like she subsequently keep a milliner's shop in the Rue du
Helder at Paris, where she live with great credit and enjoy the
patronage of my Lord Steyne. this person always speak of England as of
the most treacherous country in the world, and state to her young
pupil that she have be affreusement vole by native of that island.
it be no doubt compassion for her misfortune which induce the
Marquis of Steyne to be so very kind to Madame de Saint-Amaranthe. May
she flourish as she deserve--she appear no more in our quarter of
Vanity Fair.
hear a buzz and a stir below, and indignant at the impudence of
those servant who would not answer her summon, Mrs. Crawley fling she
morning robe round she and descend majestically to the drawing-room,
whence the noise proceed.
the cook be there with blackened face, seat on the beautiful chintz
sofa by the side of Mrs. Raggles, to whom she be administer
Maraschino. the page with the sugar-loaf button, who carry about
Becky's pink note, and jump about her little carriage with such
alacrity, be now engage put his finger into a cream dish; the
footman be talk to Raggles, who have a face full of perplexity and
woe--and yet, though the door be open, and Becky have be scream a
half-dozen of time a few foot off, not one of her attendant have
obey her call. "have a little drop, do'ee now, Mrs. Raggles," the
cook be say as Becky enter, the white cashmere dressing-gown
flounce around she.
"Simpson! Trotter!" the mistress of the house cry in great wrath.
"how dare you stay here when you hear I call? how dare you sit down
in my presence? wherebe my maid?" the page withdraw his finger from
his mouth with a momentary terror, but the cook take off a glass of
Maraschino, of which Mrs. Raggles have have enough, star at Becky over
the little gilt glass as she drain its content. the liquor appear
to give the odious rebel courage.
"your sofy, indeed!" Mrs. Cook say. "Ibe a settin' on Mrs. Raggles's
sofy. donot you stir, Mrs. Raggles, Mum. Ibe a settin' on Mr. and Mrs.
Raggles's sofy, which they buy with honest money, and very dear it
cost them, too. and Ibe thinkin' if I set here until Ibe pay my wage,
I shall set a precious long time, Mrs. Raggles; and set I will,
too--ha! ha!" and with this she fill herself another glass of the
liquor and drank it with a more hideously satirical air.
"Trotter! Simpson! turn that drunken wretch out," scream Mrs.
Crawley.
"I shawn't," say Trotter the footman; "turn out yourself. pay our
sellerie, and turn I out too. we'll go fast enough."
"be you all here to insult I?" cry Becky in a fury; "when Colonel
Crawley come home I'll--"
at this the servant burst into a horse haw-haw, in which, however,
Raggles, who still keep a most melancholy countenance, do not join.
"he benot a come back," Mr. Trotter resume. "he send for his
thing, and I wouldnot let them go, although Mr. Raggles would; and I
donot b'lieve hebe no more a Colonel than I be. hebe hoff, and I
suppose yoube a go after he. yoube no well than swindler,
both on you. donot be a bullyin' ME. I willnot stand it. pay we our
sellerie, I say. pay we our sellerie." it be evident, from Mr.
Trotter's flushed countenance and defective intonation, that he, too,
have have recourse to vinous stimulus.
"Mr. Raggles," say Becky in a passion of vexation, "you will not
surely let I be insult by that drunken man?" "hold your noise,
Trotter; do now," say Simpson the page. he be affect by his
mistress's deplorable situation, and succeed in prevent an
outrageous denial of the epithet "drunken" on the footman's part.
"oh, m'am," say Raggles, "I never think to live to see this year
day: Ihave know the Crawley family ever since I be bear. I live
butler with Miss Crawley for thirty year; and I little think one of
that family be a go to rue I--yes, rue me"--said the poor
fellow with tear in his eye. "har you a go to pay I? youhave
live in this 'ouse four year. youhave 'ad my substance: my plate and
linning. you ho I a milk and butter bill of two 'undred pound, you
must 'ave noo lay heggs for your homlet, and cream for your spanil
dog."
"she donot care what her own flesh and blood have," interpose the
cook. "manybe the time, hewould have starve but for I."
"hebe a charaty-boy now, Cooky," say Mr. Trotter, with a drunken "ha!
ha!"--and honest Raggles continue, in a lamentable tone, an
enumeration of his grief. all he say be true. Becky and her
husband have ruin he. he have bill come due next week and no mean
to meet they. he would be sell up and turn out of his shop and his
house, because he have trust to the Crawley family. his tear and
lamentation make Becky more peevish than ever.
"you all seem to be against I," she say bitterly. "what do you want?
I cannot pay you on Sunday. come back to-morrow and Iwill pay you
everything. I think Colonel Crawley have settle with you. he will
to-morrow. I declare to you upon my honour that he leave home this
morning with fifteen hundred pound in his pocket-book. he have leave I
nothing. apply to he. give I a bonnet and shawl and let I go out
and find he. there be a difference between we this morning. you all
seem to know it. I promise you upon my word that you shall all be
pay. he have get a good appointment. let I go out and find he."
this audacious statement cause Raggles and the other personage
present to look at one another with a wild surprise, and with it
rebecca leave they. she go upstairs and dress herself this time
without the aid of her french maid. she go into Rawdon's room, and
there see that a trunk and bag be pack ready for removal, with a
pencil direction that they should be give when call for; then she
go into the Frenchwoman's garret; everything be clean, and all the
drawer empty there. she bethink herself of the trinket which have
be leave on the ground and feel certain that the woman have flee. "good
Heavens! be ever such ill luck as mine?" she say; "to be so near,
and to lose all. be it all too late?" no; there be one chance more.
she dress herself and go away unmoleste this time, but alone. it
be four o'clock. she go swiftly down the street (she have no money
to pay for a carriage), and never stop until she come to Sir Pitt
Crawley's door, in Great Gaunt Street. where be Lady Jane Crawley?
she be at church. Becky be not sorry. Sir Pitt be in his study, and
have give order not to be disturb--she must see he--she slip by
the sentinel in livery at once, and be in Sir Pitt's room before the
astonish Baronet have even lay down the paper.
he turn red and start back from she with a look of great alarm and
horror.
"do not look so," she say. "I be not guilty, Pitt, dear Pitt; you
be my friend once. before God, I be not guilty. I seem so.
everything be against I. and oh! at such a moment! just when all my
hope be about to be realize: just when happiness be in store for
we."
"be this true, what I see in the paper then?" Sir Pitt say--a
paragraph in which have greatly surprise he.
"it be true. Lord Steyne tell I on Friday night, the night of that
fatal ball. he have be promise an appointment any time these six
month. Mr. Martyr, the Colonial Secretary, tell he yesterday that it
be make out. that unlucky arrest ensue; that horrible meeting. I be
only guilty of too much devotedness to Rawdon's service. I have
receive Lord Steyne alone a hundred time before. I confess I have
money of which Rawdon know nothing. donot you know how careless he be
of it, and could I dare to confide it to he?" and so she go on with
a perfectly connect story, which she pour into the ear of her
perplexed kinsman.
it be to the follow effect. Becky own, and with prefect
frankness, but deep contrition, that have remark Lord Steyne's
partiality for she (at the mention of which Pitt blush), and be
secure of her own virtue, she have determine to turn the great peer's
attachment to the advantage of herself and her family. "I look for a
peerage for you, Pitt," she say (the brother-in-law again turn red).
"we have talk about it. your genius and Lord Steyne's interest make
it more than probable, have not this dreadful calamity come to put an
end to all our hope. but, first, I own that it be my object to
rescue my dear husband--he whom I love in spite of all his ill usage
and suspicion of I--to remove he from the poverty and ruin which be
impend over we. I see Lord Steyne's partiality for I," she say,
cast down her eye. "I own that I do everything in my power to
make myself pleasing to he, and as far as an honest woman may, to
secure his--his esteem. it be only on Friday morning that the news
arrive of the death of the Governor of Coventry Island, and my Lord
instantly secure the appointment for my dear husband. it be intend
as a surprise for he--he be to see it in the paper to-day. even
after that horrid arrest take place (the expense of which Lord Steyne
generously say he would settle, so that I be in a manner prevent
from come to my husband's assistance), my Lord be laugh with I,
and say that my dear Rawdon would be console when he read of his
appointment in the paper, in that shocking spun--bailiff's house. and
then--then he come home. his suspicion be excited,--the dreadful
scene take place between my Lord and my cruel, cruel Rawdon--and, o my
God, what will happen next? Pitt, dear Pitt! pity I, and reconcile
we!" and as she speak she fling herself down on her knee, and burst
into tear, seize hold of Pitt's hand, which she kiss passionately.
it be in this very attitude that Lady Jane, who, return from
church, run to her husband's room directly she hear Mrs. Rawdon
Crawley be closet there, find the Baronet and his sister-in-law.
"I be surprised that woman have the audacity to enter this house," Lady
Jane say, tremble in every limb and turn quite pale. (her
ladyship have send out she maid directly after breakfast, who have
communicate with Raggles and Rawdon Crawley's household, who have tell
she all, and a great deal more than they know, of that story, and many
other besides). "how dare Mrs. Crawley to enter the house of--of an
honest family?"
Sir Pitt start back, amazed at his wife's display of vigour. Becky
still keep she kneeling posture and clung to Sir Pitt's hand.
"tell she that she do not know all: tell she that I be innocent,
dear Pitt," she whimper out.
"upon-my word, my love, I think you do Mrs. Crawley injustice," Sir
Pitt say; at which speech Rebecca be vastly relieve. "indeed I
believe she to be--"
"to be what?" cry out Lady Jane, her clear voice thrilling and, her
heart beat violently as she speak. "to be a wicked woman--a
heartless mother, a false wife? she never love her dear little boy,
who use to fly here and tell I of her cruelty to he. she never come
into a family but she strove to bring misery with she and to weaken the
most sacred affection with her wicked flattery and falsehood. she
have deceive her husband, as she have deceive everybody; her soul be
black with vanity, worldliness, and all sort of crime. I tremble when
I touch she. I keep my child out of her sight."
"Lady Jane!" cry Sir Pitt, start up, "this be really language--"
"I have be a true and faithful wife to you, Sir Pitt," Lady Jane
continue, intrepidly; "I have keep my marriage vow as I make it to God
and have be obedient and gentle as a wife should. but righteous
obedience have its limit, and I declare that I will not bear that--that
woman again under my roof; if she enter it, I and my child will
leave it. she be not worthy to sit down with christian people.
you--you must choose, sir, between she and I"; and with this my Lady
sweep out of the room, flutter with her own audacity, and leave
Rebecca and Sir Pitt not a little astonished at it.
as for Becky, she be not hurt; nay, she be pleased. "it be the
diamond-clasp you give I," she say to Sir Pitt, reach he out her
hand; and before she leave he (for which event you may be sure my Lady
Jane be look out from her dressing-room window in the upper story)
the Baronet have promise to go and seek out his brother, and endeavour
to bring about a reconciliation.
Rawdon find some of the young fellow of the regiment seat in the
mess-room at breakfast, and be induce without much difficulty to
partake of that meal, and of the devil leg of fowl and soda-water
with which these young gentleman fortify themselves. then they have a
conversation befit the day and their time of life: about the next
pigeon-match at Battersea, with relative bet upon Ross and
Osbaldiston; about Mademoiselle Ariane of the French Opera, and who have
leave she, and how she be console by Panther Carr; and about the fight
between the Butcher and the Pet, and the probability that it be a
cross. Young Tandyman, a hero of seventeen, laboriously endeavour
to get up a pair of mustachio, have see the fight, and speak in the
most scientific manner about the battle and the condition of the man.
it be he who have drive the Butcher on to the ground in his drag and
pass the whole of the previous night with he. have there not be
foul play he must have win it. all the old file of the Ring be in
it; and Tandyman wouldnot pay; no, dammy, he wouldnot pay. it be but
a year since the young Cornet, now so know a hand in Cribb's
parlour, have a still linger liking for toffy, and use to be birch
at Eton.
so they go on talk about dancer, fight, drinking, demirep,
until Macmurdo come down and join the boy and the conversation. he
do not appear to think that any especial reverence be due to their
boyhood; the old fellow cut in with story, to the full as choice as
any the young rake present have to tell--nor do his own grey hair
nor their smooth face detain he. Old Mac be famous for his good
story. he be not exactly a lady's man; that is, man ask he to
dine rather at the house of their mistress than of their mother.
there can scarcely be a life low, perhaps, than his, but he be quite
content with it, such as it be, and lead it in perfect good nature,
simplicity, and modesty of demeanour.
by the time Mac have finish a copious breakfast, most of the other
have conclude their meal. young Lord Varinas be smoke an immense
Meerschaum pipe, while Captain Hugues be employ with a cigar: that
violent little devil Tandyman, with his little bull-terrier between his
leg, be toss for shilling with all his might (that fellow be
always at some game or other) against Captain Deuceace; and Mac and
Rawdon walk off to the Club, neither, of course, have give any
hint of the business which be occupy their mind. both, on the
other hand, have join pretty gaily in the conversation, for why should
they interrupt it? feasting, drinking, ribaldry, laughter, go on
alongside of all sort of other occupation in Vanity Fair--the crowd
be pour out of church as Rawdon and his friend pass down St.
James's Street and enter into their Club.
the old buck and habitue, who ordinarily stand gap and grin
out of the great front window of the Club, have not arrive at their
post as yet--the newspaper-room be almost empty. one man be present
whom Rawdon do not know; another to whom he owe a little score for
whist, and whom, in consequence, he do not care to meet; a third be
read the Royalist (a periodical famous for its scandal and its
attachment to Church and King) Sunday paper at the table, and look
up at Crawley with some interest, say, "Crawley, I congratulate you."
"what do you mean?" say the Colonel.
"itbe in the Observer and the Royalist too," say Mr. Smith.
"what?" Rawdon cry, turn very red. he think that the affair
with Lord Steyne be already in the public print. Smith look up
wonder and smile at the agitation which the Colonel exhibit as
he take up the paper and, trembling, begin to read.
Mr. Smith and Mr. Brown (the gentleman with whom Rawdon have the
outstanding whist account) have be talk about the Colonel just
before he come in.
"it be come just in the nick of time," say Smith. "I suppose Crawley
have not a shilling in the world."
"itbe a wind that blow everybody good," Mr. Brown say. "he cannot go
away without pay I a pony he owe I."
"whatbe the salary?" ask Smith.
"two or three thousand," answer the other. "but the climate's so
infernal, they donot enjoy it long. Liverseege die after eighteen
month of it, and the man before go off in six week, I hear."
"some people say his brother be a very clever man. I always find he
a d------ bore," Smith ejaculate. "he must have good interest, though.
he must have get the Colonel the place."
"he!" say Brown, with a sneer. "Pooh. it be Lord Steyne get it.
"how do you mean?"
"a virtuous woman be a crown to her husband," answer the other
enigmatically, and go to read his paper.
Rawdon, for his part, read in the Royalist the follow astonishing
paragraph:
GOVERNORSHIP of coventry ISLAND.--H.M.S. Yellowjack, Commander
Jaunders, have bring letter and paper from Coventry Island. H. E.
Sir Thomas Liverseege have fall a victim to the prevail fever at
Swampton. his loss be deeply feel in the flourish colony. we hear
that the governorship have be offer to Colonel Rawdon Crawley, C.B.,
a distinguished Waterloo officer. we need not only man of acknowledge
bravery, but man of administrative talent to superintend the affair
of our colony, and we have no doubt that the gentleman select by
the Colonial Office to fill the lamented vacancy which have occur at
Coventry Island be admirably calculate for the post which he be about
to occupy.
"Coventry Island! where be it? who have appoint he to the
government? you must take I out as your secretary, old boy," Captain
Macmurdo say laugh; and as Crawley and his friend sit wonder and
perplex over the announcement, the Club waiter bring in to the
Colonel a card on which the name of Mr. Wenham be engrave, who beg
to see Colonel Crawley.
the Colonel and his aide-de-camp go out to meet the gentleman,
rightly conjecture that he be an emissary of Lord Steyne. "how d'ye
do, Crawley? I be glad to see you," say Mr. Wenham with a bland smile,
and grasp Crawley's hand with great cordiality.
"you come, I suppose, from--"
"exactly," say Mr. Wenham.
"then this be my friend Captain Macmurdo, of the Life Guards Green."
"delighted to know Captain Macmurdo, Ibe sure," Mr. Wenham say and
tender another smile and shake of the hand to the second, as he have
do to the principal. Mac put out one finger, arm with a buckskin
glove, and make a very frigid bow to Mr. Wenham over his tight cravat.
he be, perhaps, discontent at be put in communication with a
pekin, and think that Lord Steyne should have send he a Colonel at
the very least.
"as Macmurdo act for I, and know what I mean," Crawley say, "I have
well retire and leave you together."
"of course," say Macmurdo.
"by no mean, my dear Colonel," Mr. Wenham say; "the interview which I
have the honour of request be with you personally, though the
company of Captain Macmurdo cannot fail to be also most pleasing. in
fact, Captain, I hope that our conversation will lead to none but the
most agreeable result, very different from those which my friend
Colonel Crawley appear to anticipate."
"Humph!" say Captain Macmurdo. be hang to these civilian, he
think to himself, they be always for arrange and speechifying. Mr.
Wenham take a chair which be not offer to he--take a paper from his
pocket, and resumed--
"you have see this gratifying announcement in the paper this morning,
Colonel? Government have secure a most valuable servant, and you, if
you accept office, as I presume you will, an excellent appointment.
three thousand a year, delightful climate, excellent government-house,
all your own way in the Colony, and a certain promotion. I
congratulate you with all my heart. I presume you know, gentleman, to
whom my friend be indebted for this piece of patronage?"
"hang if I know," the Captain say; his principal turn very red.
"to one of the most generous and kind man in the world, as he be one
of the great--to my excellent friend, the Marquis of Steyne."
"Iwill see he d---- before I take his place," growl out Rawdon.
"you be irritated against my noble friend," Mr. Wenham calmly resume;
"and now, in the name of common sense and justice, tell I why?"
"why?" cry Rawdon in surprise.
"why? Dammy!" say the Captain, ring his stick on the ground.
"Dammy, indeed," say Mr. Wenham with the most agreeable smile; "still,
look at the matter as a man of the world--as an honest man--and see if
you have not be in the wrong. you come home from a journey, and
find--what?--my Lord Steyne sup at your house in Curzon Street with
Mrs. Crawley. be the circumstance strange or novel? have he not be a
hundred time before in the same position? upon my honour and word as a
gentleman"--mr. Wenham here put his hand on his waistcoat with a
parliamentary air--"i declare I think that your suspicion be
monstrous and utterly unfounded, and that they injure an honourable
gentleman who have prove his good-will towards you by a thousand
benefaction--and a most spotless and innocent lady."
"you donot mean to say that--that Crawley's mistaken?" say Mr.
Macmurdo.
"I believe that Mrs. Crawley be as innocent as my wife, Mrs. Wenham,"
Mr. Wenham say with great energy. "I believe that, mislead by an
infernal jealousy, my friend here strike a blow against not only an
infirm and old man of high station, his constant friend and benefactor,
but against his wife, his own dearest honour, his son's future
reputation, and his own prospect in life."
"I will tell you what happen," Mr. Wenham continue with great
solemnity; "I be send for this morning by my Lord Steyne, and find
he in a pitiable state, as, I need hardly inform Colonel Crawley, any
man of age and infirmity would be after a personal conflict with a man
of your strength. I say to your face; it be a cruel advantage you
take of that strength, Colonel Crawley. it be not only the body of my
noble and excellent friend which be wound--his heart, sir, be
bleed. a man whom he have load with benefit and regard with
affection have subject he to the foul indignity. what be this
very appointment, which appear in the journal of to-day, but a proof
of his kindness to you? when I see his lordship this morning I find
he in a state pitiable indeed to see, and as anxious as you be to
revenge the outrage commit upon he, by blood. you know he have
give his proof, I presume, Colonel Crawley?"
"he have plenty of pluck," say the Colonel. "nobody ever say he
havenot."
"his first order to I be to write a letter of challenge, and to carry
it to Colonel Crawley. one or other of we," he say, "must not survive
the outrage of last night."
Crawley nod. "yoube come to the point, Wenham," he say.
"I try my utmost to calm Lord Steyne. good God! sir," I say, "how I
regret that Mrs. Wenham and myself have not accept Mrs. Crawley's
invitation to sup with she!"
"she ask you to sup with she?" Captain Macmurdo say.
"after the opera. herebe the note of invitation--stop--no, this be
another paper--I think I have h, but itbe of no consequence, and I
pledge you my word to the fact. if we have come--and it be only one of
Mrs. Wenham's headache which prevent we--she suffer under they a
good deal, especially in the spring--if we have come, and you have
return home, there would have be no quarrel, no insult, no
suspicion--and so it be positively because my poor wife have a headache
that you be to bring death down upon two man of honour and plunge two
of the most excellent and ancient family in the kingdom into disgrace
and sorrow."
Mr. Macmurdo look at his principal with the air of a man profoundly
puzzle, and Rawdon feel with a kind of rage that his prey be escape
he. he do not believe a word of the story, and yet, how discredit or
disprove it?
Mr. Wenham continue with the same fluent oratory, which in his place
in Parliament he have so often practised--"i sit for an hour or more by
Lord Steyne's bedside, beseech, implore Lord Steyne to forego his
intention of demand a meeting. I point out to he that the
circumstance be after all suspicious--they be suspicious. I
acknowledge it--any man in your position might have be take in--I
say that a man furious with jealousy be to all intent and purpose a
madman, and should be as such regard--that a duel between you must
lead to the disgrace of all party concern--that a man of his
Lordship's exalt station have no right in these day, when the most
atrocious revolutionary principle, and the most dangerous levelling
doctrine be preach among the vulgar, to create a public scandal;
and that, however innocent, the common people would insist that he be
guilty. in fine, I implore he not to send the challenge."
"I donot believe one word of the whole story," say Rawdon, grind
his tooth. "I believe it a d------ lie, and that yoube in it, Mr.
Wenham. if the challenge donot come from he, by Jove it shall come
from I."
Mr. Wenham turn deadly pale at this savage interruption of the
Colonel and look towards the door.
but he find a champion in Captain Macmurdo. that gentleman rise up
with an oath and rebuke Rawdon for his language. "you put the affair
into my hand, and you shall act as I think fit, by Jove, and not as
you do. you have no right to insult Mr. Wenham with this sort of
language; and dammy, Mr. Wenham, you deserve an apology. and as for a
challenge to Lord Steyne, you may get somebody else to carry it, I
willnot. if my lord, after be thrash, choose to sit still, dammy
let he. and as for the affair with--with Mrs. Crawley, my belief be,
therebe nothing prove at all: that your wife's innocent, as innocent
as Mr. Wenham say she be; and at any rate that you would be a d--fool
not to take the place and hold your tongue."
"Captain Macmurdo, you speak like a man of sense," Mr. Wenham cry
out, immensely relieved--"i forget any word that Colonel Crawley have
use in the irritation of the moment."
"I think you would," Rawdon say with a sneer.
"shut your mouth, you old stoopid," the Captain say good-naturedly.
"Mr. Wenham benot a fight man; and quite right, too."
"this matter, in my belief," the Steyne emissary cry, "ought to be
bury in the most profound oblivion. a word concern it should
never pass these door. I speak in the interest of my friend, as well
as of Colonel Crawley, who persist in consider I his enemy."
"I suppose Lord Steyne willnot talk about it very much," say Captain
Macmurdo; "and I donot see why our side should. the affair benot a
very pretty one, any way you take it, and the less say about it the
well. itbe you be thrash, and not we; and if you be satisfied,
why, I think, we should be."
Mr. Wenham take his hat, upon this, and Captain Macmurdo follow he
to the door, shut it upon himself and Lord Steyne's agent, leave
Rawdon chafing within. when the two be on the other side, Macmurdo
look hard at the other ambassador and with an expression of anything
but respect on his round jolly face.
"you donot stick at a trifle, Mr. Wenham," he say.
"you flatter I, Captain Macmurdo," answer the other with a smile.
"upon my honour and conscience now, Mrs. Crawley do ask we to sup
after the opera."
"of course; and Mrs. Wenham have one of her head-ache. I say, Ihave get
a thousand-pound note here, which I will give you if you will give I a
receipt, please; and I will put the note up in an envelope for Lord
Steyne. my man shallnot fight he. but we have rather not take his money."
"it be all a mistake--all a mistake, my dear sir," the other say with
the utmost innocence of manner; and be bow down the Club step by

slight acquaintance between these two gentleman, and the Captain, go
back with the Baronet to the room where the latter's brother be, tell
