Lord Steyne and the Colonel.
Sir Pitt be well pleased, of course, at this intelligence, and
congratulate his brother warmly upon the peaceful issue of the affair,
make appropriate moral remark upon the evil of duel and the
unsatisfactory nature of that sort of settlement of dispute.
and after this preface, he try with all his eloquence to effect a
reconciliation between Rawdon and his wife. he recapitulate the
statement which Becky have make, point out the probability of their
truth, and assert his own firm belief in her innocence.
but Rawdon would not hear of it. "she have kep money conceal from I
these ten year," he say "she swear, last night only, she have none
from Steyne. she know it be all up, directly I find it. if shebe
not guilty, Pitt, shebe as bad as guilty, and Iwill never see she
again--never." his head sink down on his chest as he speak the word,
and he look quite broken and sad.
"poor old boy," Macmurdo say, shake his head.
Rawdon Crawley resist for some time the idea of take the place
which have be procure for he by so odious a patron, and be also for
remove the boy from the school where Lord Steyne's interest have
place he. he be induce, however, to acquiesce in these benefit by
the entreaty of his brother and Macmurdo, but mainly by the latter,
point out to he what a fury Steyne would be in to think that his
enemy's fortune be make through his mean.
when the Marquis of Steyne come abroad after his accident, the Colonial
Secretary bow up to he and congratulate himself and the Service
upon have make so excellent an appointment. these congratulation
be receive with a degree of gratitude which may be imagine on the
part of Lord Steyne.
the secret of the rencontre between he and Colonel Crawley be bury
in the profound oblivion, as Wenham say; that be, by the second
and the principal. but before that evening be over it be talk of
at fifty dinner-table in Vanity Fair. little Cackleby himself go to
seven evening party and tell the story with comment and emendation
at each place. how Mrs. Washington White revel in it! the
Bishopess of Ealing be shock beyond expression; the Bishop go and
write his name down in the visit-book at Gaunt House that very day.
little Southdown be sorry; so you may be sure be his sister Lady
Jane, very sorry. Lady Southdown write it off to her other daughter at
the Cape of Good Hope. it be town-talk for at least three day, and
be only keep out of the newspaper by the exertion of Mr. Wagg,
act upon a hint from Mr. Wenham.
the bailiff and broker seize upon poor Raggles in Curzon Street, and
the late fair tenant of that poor little mansion be in the
meanwhile--where? who care! who ask after a day or two? be she
guilty or not? we all know how charitable the world be, and how the
verdict of Vanity Fair go when there be a doubt. some people say
she have go to Naples in pursuit of Lord Steyne, whilst other aver
that his Lordship quit that city and flee to Palermo on hearing of
Becky's arrival; some say she be live in Bierstadt, and have become
a dame d'honneur to the Queen of Bulgaria; some that she be at
Boulogne; and other, at a boarding-house at Cheltenham.
Rawdon make she a tolerable annuity, and we may be sure that she be a
woman who could make a little money go a great way, as the saying be.
he would have pay his debt on leave England, could he have get any
Insurance Office to take his life, but the climate of Coventry Island
be so bad that he could borrow no money on the strength of his salary.
he remit, however, to his brother punctually, and write to his
little boy regularly every mail. he keep Macmurdo in cigar and send
over quantity of shell, cayenne pepper, hot pickle, guava jelly,
and colonial produce to Lady Jane. he send his brother home the Swamp
Town Gazette, in which the new Governor be praise with immense
enthusiasm; whereas the Swamp Town Sentinel, whose wife be not ask
to Government House, declare that his Excellency be a tyrant,
compare to whom Nero be an enlightened philanthropist. Little Rawdon
use to like to get the paper and read about his Excellency.
his mother never make any movement to see the child. he go home to
his aunt for sunday and holiday; he soon know every bird's nest about
Queen's Crawley, and ride out with Sir Huddlestone's hound, which he
admire so on his first well-remember visit to Hampshire.
CHAPTER LVI
Georgy be make a Gentleman
Georgy Osborne be now fairly establish in his grandfather's mansion
in Russell Square, occupant of his father's room in the house and heir
apparent of all the splendour there. the good look, gallant bearing,
and gentlemanlike appearance of the boy win the grandsire's heart for
he. Mr. Osborne be as proud of he as ever he have be of the elder
George.
the child have many more luxury and indulgence than have be award
his father. Osborne's commerce have prosper greatly of late year.
his wealth and importance in the City have very much increase. he have
be glad enough in former day to put the eld George to a good
private school; and a commission in the army for his son have be a
source of no small pride to he; for little George and his future
prospect the old man look much high. he would make a gentleman of
the little chap, be Mr. Osborne's constant saying regard little
Georgy. he see he in his mind's eye, a collegian, a Parliament man, a
Baronet, perhaps. the old man think he would die content if he
could see his grandson in a fair way to such honour. he would have
none but a tip-top college man to educate he--none of your quack and
pretender--no, no. a few year before, he use to be savage, and
inveigh against all parson, scholar, and the like declare that they
be a pack of humbug, and quack that benot fit to get their living
but by grind Latin and Greek, and a set of supercilious dog that
pretend to look down upon british merchant and gentleman, who could
buy up half a hundred of them. he would mourn now, in a very solemn
manner, that his own education have be neglect, and repeatedly point
out, in pompous oration to Georgy, the necessity and excellence of
classical acquirement.
when they meet at dinner the grandsire use to ask the lad what he have
be read during the day, and be greatly interested at the report
the boy give of his own study, pretend to understand little George
when he speak regard they. he make a hundred blunder and show
his ignorance many a time. it do not increase the respect which the
child have for his senior. a quick brain and a well education
elsewhere show the boy very soon that his grandsire be a dullard,
and he begin accordingly to command he and to look down upon he; for
his previous education, humble and contract as it have be, have make
a much well gentleman of Georgy than any plan of his grandfather
could make he. he have be bring up by a kind, weak, and tender
woman, who have no pride about anything but about he, and whose heart
be so pure and whose bearing be so meek and humble that she could not
but need be a true lady. she busy herself in gentle office and
quiet duty; if she never say brilliant thing, she never speak or
think unkind one; guileless and artless, love and pure, indeed how
could our poor little Amelia be other than a real gentlewoman!
Young Georgy lord over this soft and yield nature; and the
contrast of its simplicity and delicacy with the coarse pomposity of
the dull old man with whom he next come in contact make he lord over
the latter too. if he have be a Prince Royal he could not have be
well bring up to think well of himself.
whilst his mother be yearn after he at home, and I do believe
every hour of the day, and during most hour of the sad lonely night,
thinking of he, this young gentleman have a number of pleasure and
consolation administer to he, which make he for his part bear the
separation from Amelia very easily. little boy who cry when they be
go to school cry because they be go to a very uncomfortable
place. it be only a few who weep from sheer affection. when you think
that the eye of your childhood dry at the sight of a piece of
gingerbread, and that a plum cake be a compensation for the agony of
part with your mamma and sister, oh my friend and brother, you need
not be too confident of your own fine feeling.
well, then, Master George Osborne have every comfort and luxury that a
wealthy and lavish old grandfather think fit to provide. the
coachman be instruct to purchase for he the handsome pony which
could be buy for money, and on this George be teach to ride, first
at a riding-school, whence, after have perform satisfactorily
without stirrup, and over the leaping-bar, he be conduct through
the New Road to Regent's Park, and then to Hyde Park, where he ride in
state with Martin the coachman behind he. Old Osborne, who take
matter more easily in the City now, where he leave his affair to his
junior partner, would often ride out with Miss O. in the same
fashionable direction. as little georgy come canter up with his
dandified air and his heel down, his grandfather would nudge the lad's
aunt and say, "look, Miss O." and he would laugh, and his face would
grow red with pleasure, as he nod out of the window to the boy, as
the groom salute the carriage, and the footman salute Master George.
here too his aunt, Mrs. Frederick Bullock (whose chariot might daily be
see in the Ring, with bullock or emblazon on the panel and
harness, and three pasty-face little bullock, cover with cockade
and feather, star from the window) Mrs. Frederick Bullock, I say,
fling glance of the bitter hatred at the little upstart as he ride
by with his hand on his side and his hat on one ear, as proud as a lord.
though he be scarcely eleven year of age, Master George wear strap
and the most beautiful little boot like a man. he have gilt spur, and
a gold-head whip, and a fine pin in his handkerchief, and the neat
little kid glove which Lamb's Conduit Street could furnish. his mother
have give he a couple of neckcloth, and carefully hem and make
some little shirt for he; but when her Eli come to see the widow,
they be replace by much fine linen. he have little jewelled button
in the lawn shirt front. her humble present have be put aside--I
believe Miss Osborne have give they to the coachman's boy. Amelia
try to think she be pleased at the change. indeed, she be happy
and charm to see the boy look so beautiful.
she have have a little black profile of he do for a shilling, and this
be hang up by the side of another portrait over her bed. one day the
boy come on his accustomed visit, gallop down the little street at
Brompton, and bring, as usual, all the inhabitant to the window to
admire his splendour, and with great eagerness and a look of triumph in
his face, he pull a case out of his great-coat--it be a natty white
great-coat, with a cape and a velvet collar--pull out a red morocco
case, which he give she.
"I buy it with my own money, Mamma," he say. "I think youwould like
it."
Amelia open the case, and give a little cry of delighted affection,
seize the boy and embrace he a hundred time. it be a miniature-of
himself, very prettily do (though not half handsome enough, we may be
sure, the widow think). his grandfather have wish to have a picture
of he by an artist whose work, exhibit in a shop-window, in
Southampton Row, have catch the old gentleman's eye; and George, who
have plenty of money, bethink he of ask the painter how much a
copy of the little portrait would cost, say that he would pay for it
out of his own money and that he want to give it to his mother. the
pleased painter execute it for a small price, and old Osborne himself,
when he hear of the incident, growl out his satisfaction and give
the boy twice as many sovereign as he pay for the miniature.
but what be the grandfather's pleasure compare to Amelia's ecstacy?
that proof of the boy's affection charm she so that she think no
child in the world be like hers for goodness. for long week after,
the thought of his love make she happy. she sleep well with the
picture under her pillow, and how many many time do she kiss it and
weep and pray over it! a small kindness from those she love make that
timid heart grateful. since her part with George she have have no
such joy and consolation.
at his new home Master George rule like a lord; at dinner he invite
the lady to drink wine with the utmost coolness, and take off his
champagne in a way which charm his old grandfather. "look at he,"
the old man would say, nudge his neighbour with a delighted purple
face, "do you ever see such a chap? Lord, Lord! hewill be order a
dressing-case next, and razor to shave with; Ibe bless if he willnot."
the antic of the lad do not, however, delight Mr. Osborne's friend
so much as they please the old gentleman. it give Mr. Justice Coffin
no pleasure to hear georgy cut into the conversation and spoil his
story. Colonel Fogey be not interested in see the little boy half
tipsy. Mr. Sergeant Toffy's lady feel no particular gratitude, when,
with a twist of his elbow, he tilt a glass of port-wine over her
yellow satin and laugh at the disaster; nor be she well please,
although old Osborne be highly delighted, when georgy "whop" her
third boy (a young gentleman a year old than Georgy, and by chance
home for the holiday from Dr. Tickleus's at Ealing School) in Russell
Square. George's grandfather give the boy a couple of sovereign for
that feat and promise to reward he far for every boy above his
own size and age whom he whop in a similar manner. it be difficult
to say what good the old man see in these combat; he have a vague
notion that quarrel make boy hardy, and that tyranny be a useful
accomplishment for they to learn. english youth have be so educated
time out of mind, and we have hundred of thousand of apologist and
admirer of injustice, misery, and brutality, as perpetrate among
child. flush with praise and victory over Master Toffy, George
wish naturally to pursue his conquest far, and one day as he be
strut about in prodigiously dandified new clothe, near St.
Pancras, and a young baker's boy make sarcastic comment upon his
appearance, the youthful patrician pull off his dandy jacket with
great spirit, and give it in charge to the friend who accompany he
(Master Todd, of Great Coram Street, Russell Square, son of the junior
partner of the house of Osborne and Co.), George try to whop the
little baker. but the chance of war be unfavourable this time, and
the little baker whop Georgy, who come home with a rueful black eye
and all his fine shirt frill dabble with the claret draw from his own
little nose. he tell his grandfather that he have be in combat with a
giant, and frighten his poor mother at Brompton with long, and by no
mean authentic, account of the battle.
this young Todd, of Coram Street, Russell Square, be Master George's
great friend and admirer. they both have a taste for paint
theatrical character; for hardbake and raspberry tart; for slide
and skating in the Regent's Park and the Serpentine, when the weather
permit; for go to the play, whither they be often conduct, by
Mr. Osborne's order, by Rowson, Master George's appoint
body-servant, with whom they sit in great comfort in the pit.
in the company of this gentleman they visit all the principal
theatre of the metropolis; know the name of all the actor from Drury
Lane to Sadler's Wells; and perform, indeed, many of the play to the
Todd family and their youthful friend, with West's famous character,
on their pasteboard theatre. Rowson, the footman, who be of a
generous disposition, would not unfrequently, when in cash, treat his
young master to oyster after the play, and to a glass of rum-shrub for
a night-cap. we may be pretty certain that Mr. Rowson profit in his
turn by his young master's liberality and gratitude for the pleasure
to which the footman induct he.
a famous tailor from the West end of the town--Mr. Osborne would have
none of your City or Holborn bungler, he say, for the boy (though a
City tailor be good enough for HIM)--was summon to ornament little
George's person, and be tell to spare no expense in so do. so, Mr.
Woolsey, of Conduit Street, give a loose to his imagination and send
the child home fancy trouser, fancy waistcoat, and fancy jacket
enough to furnish a school of little dandy. georgy have little white
waistcoat for evening party, and little cut velvet waistcoat for
dinner, and a dear little darling shawl dressing-gown, for all the
world like a little man. he dress for dinner every day, "like a
regular West End swell," as his grandfather remark; one of the
domestic be affect to his special service, attend he at his
toilette, answer his bell, and bring he his letter always on a
silver tray.
georgy, after breakfast, would sit in the arm-chair in the dining-room
and read the Morning Post, just like a grow-up man. "how he DU dam
and swear," the servant would cry, delight at his precocity. those
who remember the Captain his father, declare Master George be his
Pa, every inch of he. he make the house lively by his activity, his
imperiousness, his scolding, and his good-nature.
George's education be confide to a neighbouring scholar and private
pedagogue who "prepare young nobleman and gentleman for the
Universities, the senate, and the learn profession: whose system
do not embrace the degrading corporal severity still practise at
the ancient place of education, and in whose family the pupil would
find the elegance of refined society and the confidence and affection
of a home." it be in this way that the Reverend Lawrence Veal of Hart
Street, Bloomsbury, and domestic Chaplain to the Earl of Bareacres,
strove with Mrs. Veal his wife to entice pupil.
by thus advertising and push sedulously, the domestic Chaplain and
his Lady generally succeed in have one or two scholar by they--who
pay a high figure and be think to be in uncommonly comfortable
quarter. there be a large West Indian, whom nobody come to see, with
a mahogany complexion, a woolly head, and an exceedingly dandyfie
appearance; there be another hulking boy of three-and-twenty whose
education have be neglect and whom Mr. and Mrs. Veal be to
introduce into the polite world; there be two son of Colonel Bangles
of the East India Company's Service: these four sit down to dinner at
Mrs. Veal's genteel board, when georgy be introduce to her
establishment.
georgy be, like some dozen other pupil, only a day boy; he arrive in
the morning under the guardianship of his friend Mr. Rowson, and if it
be fine, would ride away in the afternoon on his pony, follow by the
groom. the wealth of his grandfather be report in the school to be
prodigious. the Rev. Mr. Veal use to compliment georgy upon it
personally, warn he that he be destine for a high station; that
it become he to prepare, by sedulity and docility in youth, for the
lofty duty to which he would be call in mature age; that obedience
in the child be the good preparation for command in the man; and that
he therefore beg George would not bring toffee into the school and
ruin the health of the Masters Bangles, who have everything they want
at the elegant and abundant table of Mrs. Veal.
with respect to learning, "the Curriculum," as Mr. Veal love to call
it, be of prodigious extent, and the young gentleman in Hart Street
might learn a something of every know science. the Rev. Mr. Veal have
an orrery, an electrify machine, a turning lathe, a theatre (in the
wash-house), a chemical apparatus, and what he call a select library
of all the work of the good author of ancient and modern time and
language. he take the boy to the British Museum and descant upon
the antiquity and the specimen of natural history there, so that
audience would gather round he as he speak, and all Bloomsbury highly
admire he as a prodigiously well-inform man. and whenever he speak
(which he do almost always), he take care to produce the very fine
and long word of which the vocabulary give he the use, rightly
judge that it be as cheap to employ a handsome, large, and sonorous
epithet, as to use a little stingy one.
thus he would say to George in school, "I observe on my return home
from take the indulgence of an evening's scientific conversation with
my excellent friend Doctor Bulders--a true archaeologian, gentleman, a
true archaeologian--that the window of your venerate grandfather's
almost princely mansion in Russell Square be illuminate as if for
the purpose of festivity. be I right in my conjecture that Mr.
Osborne entertain a society of choose spirit round his sumptuous
board last night?"
Little Georgy, who have considerable humour, and use to mimic Mr. Veal
to his face with great spirit and dexterity, would reply that Mr. V.
be quite correct in his surmise.
"then those friend who have the honour of partaking of Mr. Osborne's
hospitality, gentleman, have no reason, I will lay any wager, to
complain of their repast. I myself have be more than once so
favour. (by the way, Master Osborne, you come a little late this
morning, and have be a defaulter in this respect more than once.) I
myself, I say, gentleman, humble as I be, have be find not unworthy
to share Mr. Osborne's elegant hospitality. and though I have feast
with the great and noble of the world--for I presume that I may call my
excellent friend and patron, the Right honourable George Earl of
Bareacres, one of the number--yet I assure you that the board of the
british merchant be to the full as richly serve, and his reception as
gratifying and noble. Mr. Bluck, sir, we will resume, if you please,
that passage of Eutropis, which be interrupt by the late arrival of
Master Osborne."
to this great man George's education be for some time entrust.
Amelia be bewilder by his phrase, but think he a prodigy of
learn. that poor widow make friend of Mrs. Veal, for reason of
her own. she like to be in the house and see georgy come to school
there. she like to be ask to Mrs. Veal's conversazioni, which take
place once a month (as you be inform on pink card, with AOHNH
engrave on they), and where the professor welcome his pupil and
their friend to weak tea and scientific conversation. poor little
Amelia never miss one of these entertainment and think they
delicious so long as she might have georgy sit by she. and she
would walk from Brompton in any weather, and embrace Mrs. Veal with
tearful gratitude for the delightful evening she have pass, when, the
company have retire and Georgy go off with Mr. Rowson, his
attendant, poor Mrs. Osborne put on her cloak and her shawl
preparatory to walk home.
as for the learning which georgy imbibe under this valuable master of
a hundred science, to judge from the weekly report which the lad take
home to his grandfather, his progress be remarkable. the name of a
score or more of desirable branch of knowledge be print in a
table, and the pupil's progress in each be mark by the professor.
in Greek Georgy be pronounce aristos, in latin optimus, in French
tre bien, and so forth; and everybody have prize for everything at the
end of the year. even Mr. Swartz, the wooly-headed young gentleman,
and half-brother to the Honourable Mrs. Mac Mull, and Mr. Bluck, the
neglect young pupil of three-and-twenty from the agricultural
district, and that idle young scapegrace of a Master Todd before
mention, receive little eighteen-penny book, with "Athene" engrave
on they, and a pompous latin inscription from the professor to his
young friend.
the family of this Master Todd be hanger-on of the house of Osborne.
the old gentleman have advance Todd from be a clerk to be a junior
partner in his establishment.
Mr. Osborne be the godfather of young Master Todd (who in subsequent
life write Mr. Osborne Todd on his card and become a man of decided
fashion), while Miss Osborne have accompany Miss Maria Todd to the
font, and give she protegee a prayer-book, a collection of tract, a
volume of very low church poetry, or some such memento of her goodness
every year. Miss O. drive the Todds out in her carriage now and then;
when they be ill, her footman, in large plush small and waistcoat,
bring jelly and delicacy from Russell Square to Coram Street.
Coram Street tremble and look up to Russell Square indeed, and Mrs.
Todd, who have a pretty hand at cut out paper trimming for haunch
of mutton, and could make flower, duck, &c., out of turnip and
carrot in a very creditable manner, would go to "the Square," as it
be call, and assist in the preparation incident to a great dinner,
without even so much as think of sit down to the banquet. if
any guest fail at the eleventh hour, Todd be ask to dine. Mrs.
Todd and Maria come across in the evening, slip in with a muffle
knock, and be in the drawing-room by the time Miss Osborne and the
lady under her convoy reach that apartment--and ready to fire off
duet and sing until the gentleman come up. poor Maria Todd; poor
young lady! how she have to work and thrum at these duet and sonata
in the Street, before they appear in public in the Square!
thus it seem to be decree by fate that georgy be to domineer over
everybody with whom he come in contact, and that friend, relative,
and domestic be all to bow the knee before the little fellow. it
must be own that he accommodate himself very willingly to this
arrangement. Most people do so. and Georgy like to play the part of
master and perhaps have a natural aptitude for it.
in Russell Square everybody be afraid of Mr. Osborne, and Mr. Osborne
be afraid of Georgy. the boy's dash manner, and offhand rattle
about book and learning, his likeness to his father (dead unreconciled
in Brussels yonder) awe the old gentleman and give the young boy the
mastery. the old man would start at some hereditary feature or tone
unconsciously use by the little lad, and fancy that George's father
be again before he. he try by indulgence to the grandson to make
up for harshness to the eld George. People be surprise at his
gentleness to the boy. he growl and swear at Miss Osborne as usual,
and would smile when George come down late for breakfast.
Miss Osborne, George's aunt, be a fade old spinster, break down by
more than forty year of dulness and coarse usage. it be easy for a
lad of spirit to master she. and whenever George want anything from
she, from the jam-pot in her cupboard to the cracked and dry old
colour in her paint-box (the old paint-box which she have have when she
be a pupil of Mr. Smee and be still almost young and blooming),
georgy take possession of the object of his desire, which obtain, he
take no further notice of his aunt.
for his friend and crony, he have a pompous old schoolmaster, who
flatter he, and a toady, his senior, whom he could thrash. it be
dear Mrs. Todd's delight to leave he with her young daughter, Rosa
Jemima, a darling child of eight year old. the little pair look so
well together, she would say (but not to the folk in "the Square," we
may be sure) "who know what might happen? donot they make a pretty
little couple?" the fond mother think.
the break-spirit, old, maternal grandfather be likewise subject to
the little tyrant. he could not help respect a lad who have such
fine clothe and ride with a groom behind he. georgy, on his side,
be in the constant habit of hear coarse abuse and vulgar satire
level at John Sedley by his pitiless old enemy, Mr. Osborne.
Osborne use to call the other the old pauper, the old coal-man, the
old bankrupt, and by many other such name of brutal contumely. how
be little George to respect a man so prostrate? a few month after he
be with his paternal grandfather, Mrs. Sedley die. there have be
little love between she and the child. he do not care to show much
grief. he come down to visit his mother in a fine new suit of
mourning, and be very angry that he could not go to a play upon which
he have set his heart.
the illness of that old lady have be the occupation and perhaps the
safeguard of Amelia. what do man know about woman's martyrdom? we
should go mad have we to endure the hundredth part of those daily pain
which be meekly bear by many woman. ceaseless slavery meeting with
no reward; constant gentleness and kindness meet by cruelty as constant;
love, labour, patience, watchfulness, without even so much as the
acknowledgement of a good word; all this, how many of they have to bear
in quiet, and appear abroad with cheerful face as if they feel
nothing. tender slave that they be, they must need be hypocrite
and weak.
from her chair Amelia's mother have take to her bed, which she have
never leave, and from which Mrs. Osborne herself be never absent except
when she run to see George. the old lady grudge she even those rare
visit; she, who have be a kind, smile, good-natured mother once, in
the day of her prosperity, but whom poverty and infirmity have break
down. her illness or estrangement do not affect Amelia. they rather
enable she to support the other calamity under which she be
suffer, and from the thought of which she be keep by the ceaseless
call of the invalid. Amelia bear her harshness quite gently; smooth
the uneasy pillow; be always ready with a soft answer to the watchful,
querulous voice; soothe the sufferer with word of hope, such as she
pious simple heart could well feel and utter, and close the eye that
have once look so tenderly upon she.
then all her time and tenderness be devoted to the consolation and
comfort of the bereaved old father, who be stun by the blow which
have befall he, and stand utterly alone in the world. his wife, his
honour, his fortune, everything he love well have fall away from he.
there be only Amelia to stand by and support with her gentle arm the
tottering, heart-break old man. we be not go to write the history:
it would be too dreary and stupid. I can see Vanity Fair yawn over
it d'avance.
one day as the young gentleman be assemble in the study at the Rev.
Mr. Veal's, and the domestic chaplain to the Right Honourable the Earl
of Bareacres be spout away as usual, a smart carriage drive up to
the door decorate with the statue of Athene, and two gentleman step
out. the young Masters Bangles rush to the window with a vague
notion that their father might have arrive from Bombay. the great
hulking scholar of three-and-twenty, who be cry secretly over a
passage of Eutropius, flatten his neglect nose against the pane
and look at the drag, as the laquais de place spring from the box and
let out the person in the carriage.
"itbe a fat one and a thin one," Mr. Bluck say as a thunder knock
come to the door.
everybody be interested, from the domestic chaplain himself, who hop
he see the father of some future pupil, down to Master Georgy, glad
of any pretext for lay his book down.
the boy in the shabby livery with the fade copper button, who always
thrust himself into the tight coat to open the door, come into the
study and say, "two gentleman want to see Master Osborne." the
professor have have a trifling altercation in the morning with that young
gentleman, owe to a difference about the introduction of cracker in
school-time; but his face resume its habitual expression of bland
courtesy as he say, "Master Osborne, I give you full permission to go
and see your carriage friend--to whom I beg you to convey the
respectful compliment of myself and Mrs. Veal."
Georgy go into the reception-room and see two stranger, whom he
look at with his head up, in his usual haughty manner. one be fat,
with mustachio, and the other be lean and long, in a blue frock-coat,
with a brown face and a grizzle head.
"my God, how like he be!" say the long gentleman with a start. "can
you guess who we be, George?"
the boy's face flush up, as it do usually when he be move, and his
eye brighten. "I donot know the other," he say, "but I should
think you must be Major Dobbin."
indeed it be our old friend. his voice tremble with pleasure as he
greet the boy, and take both the other's hand in his own, draw the
lad to he.
"your mother have talk to you about I--have she?" he say.
"that she have," georgy answer, "hundred and hundred of time."
CHAPTER LVII
Eothen
it be one of the many cause for personal pride with which old Osborne
choose to recreate himself that Sedley, his ancient rival, enemy, and
benefactor, be in his last day so utterly defeat and humiliate as
to be force to accept pecuniary obligation at the hand of the man
who have most injure and insult he. the successful man of the world
curse the old pauper and relieve he from time to time. as he
furnish George with money for his mother, he give the boy to
understand by hint, deliver in his brutal, coarse way, that George's
maternal grandfather be but a wretched old bankrupt and dependant, and
that John Sedley might thank the man to whom he already owe ever so
much money for the aid which his generosity now choose to administer.
George carry the pompous supply to his mother and the shattered old
widower whom it be now the main business of her life to tend and
comfort. the little fellow patronize the feeble and disappointed old
man.
it may have show a want of "proper pride" in Amelia that she choose to
accept these money benefit at the hand of her father's enemy. but
proper pride and this poor lady have never have much acquaintance
together. a disposition naturally simple and demand protection; a
long course of poverty and humility, of daily privation, and hard
word, of kind office and no return, have be her lot ever since
womanhood almost, or since her luckless marriage with George Osborne.
you who see your better bear up under this shame every day, meekly
suffering under the slight of fortune, gentle and unpitied, poor, and
rather despise for their poverty, do you ever step down from your
prosperity and wash the foot of these poor weary beggar? the very
thought of they be odious and low. "there must be class--there must
be rich and poor," Dives say, smack his claret (it be well if he
even send the broken meat out to Lazarus sit under the window).
very true; but think how mysterious and often unaccountable it be--that
lottery of life which give to this man the purple and fine linen and
send to the other rag for garment and dog for comforter.
so I must own that, without much repining, on the contrary with
something akin to gratitude, Amelia take the crumb that her father-in-law
let drop now and then, and with they feed her own parent.
directly she understand it to be her duty, it be this young woman's
nature (lady, she be but thirty still, and we choose to call she a
young woman even at that age) it be, I say, her nature to sacrifice
herself and to fle all that she have at the foot of the beloved
object. during what long thankless night have she work out her
finger for little georgy whilst at home with she; what buffet,
scorn, privation, poverty have she endure for father and mother!
and in the midst of all these solitary resignation and unseen
sacrifice, she do not respect herself any more than the world
respect she, but I believe thought in her heart that she be a
poor-spirit, despicable little creature, whose luck in life be only
too good for her merit. o you poor woman! o you poor secret martyr
and victim, whose life be a torture, who be stretch on rack in
your bedroom, and who lie your head down on the block daily at the
drawing-room table; every man who watch your pain, or peer into
those dark place where the torture be administer to you, must pity
you--and--and thank God that he have a beard. I recollect see, year
ago, at the prison for idiot and madman at Bicetre, near Paris, a
poor wretch bend down under the bondage of his imprisonment and his
personal infirmity, to whom one of our party give a halfpenny worth of
snuff in a cornet or "screw" of paper. the kindness be too much for
the poor epileptic creature. he cry in an anguish of delight and
gratitude: if anybody give you and I a thousand a year, or save our
life, we could not be so affected. and so, if you properly tyrannize
over a woman, you will find a h'p'orth of kindness act upon she and
bring tear into her eye, as though you be an angel benefit she.
some such boon as these be the good which Fortune allot to poor
little Amelia. her life, begin not unprosperously, have come down to
this--to a mean prison and a long, ignoble bondage. little George
visit her captivity sometimes and console it with feeble gleam of
encouragement. Russell Square be the boundary of her prison: she
might walk thither occasionally, but be always back to sleep in her
cell at night; to perform cheerless duty; to watch by thankless
sick-bed; to suffer the harassment and tyranny of querulous
disappoint old age. how many thousand of people be there, woman
for the most part, who be doom to endure this long slavery?--who be
hospital nurse without wage--sister of Charity, if you like, without
the romance and the sentiment of sacrifice--who strive, fast, watch,
and suffer, unpitied, and fade away ignobly and unknown.
the hidden and awful wisdom which apportion the destiny of mankind
be pleased so to humiliate and cast down the tender, good, and wise,
and to set up the selfish, the foolish, or the wicked. oh, be humble,
my brother, in your prosperity! be gentle with those who be less
lucky, if not more deserving. think, what right have you to be
scornful, whose virtue be a deficiency of temptation, whose success may
be a chance, whose rank may be an ancestor's accident, whose prosperity
be very likely a satire.
they bury Amelia's mother in the churchyard at Brompton, upon just
such a rainy, dark day as Amelia recollect when first she have be
there to marry George. her little boy sit by her side in pompous new
sable. she remember the old pew-woman and clerk. her thought be
away in other time as the parson read. but that she hold George's hand
in her own, perhaps she would have like to change place with....
then, as usual, she feel ashamed of her selfish thought and pray
inwardly to be strengthen to do her duty.
so she determine with all her might and strength to try and make she
old father happy. she slave, toil, patch, and mend, sing and
play backgammon, read out the newspaper, cook dish, for old
Sedley, walk he out sedulously into Kensington Gardens or the
Brompton Lanes, listen to his story with untiring smile and
affectionate hypocrisy, or sit musing by his side and commune with
her own thought and reminiscence, as the old man, feeble and
querulous, sun himself on the garden bench and prattle about his
wrong or his sorrow. what sad, unsatisfactory thought those of the
widow be! the child run up and down the slope and broad
path in the garden remind she of George, who be take from she;
the first George be take from she; her selfish, guilty love, in both
instance, have be rebuke and bitterly chastised. she strove to think
it be right that she should be so punish. she be such a miserable
wicked sinner. she be quite alone in the world.
I know that the account of this kind of solitary imprisonment be
insufferably tedious, unless there be some cheerful or humorous
incident to enliven it--a tender gaoler, for instance, or a waggish
commandant of the fortress, or a mouse to come out and play about
Latude's beard and whisker, or a subterranean passage under the
castle, dig by Trenck with his nail and a toothpick: the historian
have no such enliven incident to relate in the narrative of Amelia's
captivity. Fancy she, if you please, during this period, very sad, but
always ready to smile when speak to; in a very mean, poor, not to say
vulgar position of life; sing song, make pudding, play card,
mend stocking, for her old father's benefit. so, never mind,
whether she be a heroine or no; or you and I, however old, scold,
and bankrupt--may we have in our last day a kind soft shoulder on
which to lean and a gentle hand to soothe our gouty old pillow.
Old Sedley grow very fond of his daughter after his wife's death, and
Amelia have her consolation in do her duty by the old man.
but we be not go to leave these two people long in such a low and
ungenteel station of life. well day, as far as worldly prosperity
go, be in store for both. perhaps the ingenious reader have guess
who be the stout gentleman who call upon georgy at his school in
company with our old friend Major Dobbin. it be another old
acquaintance return to England, and at a time when his presence be
likely to be of great comfort to his relative there.
major Dobbin have easily succeed in get leave from his
good-natured commandant to proceed to Madras, and thence probably to
Europe, on urgent private affair, never cease travel night and day
until he reach his journey's end, and have direct his march with such
celerity that he arrive at Madras in a high fever. his servant who
accompany he bring he to the house of the friend with whom he have
resolve to stay until his departure for Europe in a state of delirium;
and it be think for many, many day that he would never travel
far than the burying-ground of the church of St. George's, where
the troop should fire a salvo over his grave, and where many a gallant
officer lie far away from his home.
here, as the poor fellow lie tossing in his fever, the people who
watch he might have hear he rave about Amelia. the idea that he
should never see she again depress he in his lucid hour. he
think his last day be come, and he make his solemn preparation for
departure, set his affair in this world in order and leave the
little property of which he be possess to those whom he most desire
to benefit. the friend in whose house he be locate witness his
testament. he desire to be bury with a little brown hair-chain
which he wear round his neck and which, if the truth must be know, he
have get from Amelia's maid at Brussels, when the young widow's hair be
cut off, during the fever which prostrate she after the death of
George Osborne on the plateau at Mount St. John.
he recover, rally, relapse again, have undergo such a process
of blood-letting and calomel as show the strength of his original
constitution. he be almost a skeleton when they put he on board the
Ramchunder East Indiaman, Captain Bragg, from Calcutta, touch at
Madras, and so weak and prostrate that his friend who have tend he
through his illness prophesy that the honest Major would never
survive the voyage, and that he would pass some morning, shroud in
flag and hammock, over the ship's side, and carry down to the sea
with he the relic that he wear at his heart. but whether it be the
sea air, or the hope which spring up in he afresh, from the day that
the ship spread her canvas and stand out of the road towards home, our
friend begin to amend, and he be quite well (though as gaunt as a
greyhound) before they reach the cape. "Kirk will be disappoint of
his majority this time," he say with a smile; "he will expect to find
himself gazette by the time the regiment reach home." for it must be
premise that while the Major be lie ill at Madras, have make such
prodigious haste to go thither, the gallant --th, which have pass many
year abroad, which after its return from the West Indies have be
baulk of its stay at home by the Waterloo campaign, and have be
order from Flanders to India, have receive order home; and the Major
might have accompany his comrade, have he choose to wait for their
arrival at Madras.
perhaps he be not inclined to put himself in his exhausted state again
under the guardianship of Glorvina. "I think Miss O'Dowd would have
do for I," he say laughingly to a fellow-passenger, "if we have have
she on board, and when she have sink I, she would have fall upon you,
depend upon it, and carry you in as a prize to Southampton, Jos, my
boy."
for indeed it be no other than our stout friend who be also a
passenger on board the ramchunder. he have pass ten year in Bengal.
constant dinner, tiffin, pale ale and claret, the prodigious labour
of cutcherry, and the refreshment of brandy-pawnee which he be force
to take there, have their effect upon Waterloo Sedley. a voyage to
Europe be pronounce necessary for he--and have serve his full
time in India and have fine appointment which have enable he to lay by
a considerable sum of money, he be free to come home and stay with a
good pension, or to return and resume that rank in the service to which
his seniority and his vast talent entitle he.
he be rather thin than when we last see he, but have gain in
majesty and solemnity of demeanour. he have resume the mustachio to
which his service at Waterloo entitle he, and swagger about on
deck in a magnificent velvet cap with a gold band and a profuse
ornamentation of pin and jewellery about his person. he take breakfast
in his cabin and dress as solemnly to appear on the quarter-deck as
if he be go to turn out for Bond Street, or the course at
Calcutta. he bring a native servant with he, who be his valet and
pipe-bearer and who wear the Sedley crest in silver on his turban.
that oriental menial have a wretched life under the tyranny of Jos
Sedley. Jos be as vain of his person as a woman, and take as long a
time at his toilette as any fading beauty. the youngster among the
passenger, Young Chaffers of the 150th, and poor little rickett,
come home after his third fever, use to draw out Sedley at the
cuddy-table and make he tell prodigious story about himself and his
exploit against tiger and Napoleon. he be great when he visit the
Emperor's tomb at Longwood, when to these gentleman and the young
officer of the ship, Major Dobbin not be by, he describe the whole
battle of Waterloo and all but announce that Napoleon never would have
go to Saint Helena at all but for he, Jos Sedley.
after leave St. Helena he become very generous, dispose of a great
quantity of ship store, claret, preserved meat, and great cask
pack with soda-water, bring out for his private delectation. there
be no lady on board; the Major give the pas of precedency to the
civilian, so that he be the first dignitary at table, and treat by
Captain Bragg and the officer of the ramchunder with the respect which
his rank warrant. he disappear rather in a panic during a
two-day' gale, in which he have the porthole of his cabin batten
down, and remain in his cot read the Washerwoman of Finchley
Common, leave on board the ramchunder by the Right Honourable the Lady
Emily Hornblower, wife of the Rev. Silas Hornblower, when on their
passage out to the Cape, where the Reverend gentleman be a missionary;
but, for common reading, he have bring a stock of novel and play
which he lend to the rest of the ship, and render himself agreeable
to all by his kindness and condescension.
many and many a night as the ship be cut through the roar dark
sea, the moon and star shin overhead and the bell sing out the
watch, Mr. Sedley and the Major would sit on the quarter-deck of the
vessel talk about home, as the Major smoke his cheroot and the
civilian puff at the hookah which his servant prepare for he.
in these conversation it be wonderful with what perseverance and
ingenuity Major Dobbin would manage to bring the talk round to the
subject of Amelia and her little boy. Jos, a little testy about his
father's misfortune and unceremonious application to he, be soothe
down by the Major, who point out the elder's ill fortune and old
age. he would not perhaps like to live with the old couple, whose way
and hour might not agree with those of a young man, accustom to
different society (Jos bow at this compliment); but, the Major
point out, how advantageous it would be for Jos Sedley to have a
house of his own in London, and not a mere bachelor's establishment as
before; how his sister Amelia would be the very person to preside over
it; how elegant, how gentle she be, and of what refine good manner.
he recount story of the success which Mrs. George Osborne have have
in former day at Brussels, and in London, where she be much admire
by people of very great fashion; and he then hint how become it
would be for Jos to send Georgy to a good school and make a man of he,
for his mother and her parent would be sure to spoil he. in a word,
this artful Major make the civilian promise to take charge of Amelia
and her unprotected child. he do not know as yet what event have
happen in the little Sedley family, and how death have remove the
mother, and rich have carry off George from Amelia. but the fact be
that every day and always, this love-smite and middle-aged gentleman
be think about Mrs. Osborne, and his whole heart be bent upon
do her good. he coax, wheedle, cajole, and compliment Jos
Sedley with a perseverance and cordiality of which he be not aware
himself, very likely; but some man who have unmarried sister or
daughter even, may remember how uncommonly agreeable gentleman be to
the male relation when they be court the female; and perhaps this
rogue of a Dobbin be urge by a similar hypocrisy.
the truth be, when Major Dobbin come on board the Ramchumder, very
sick, and for the three day she lie in the Madras Roads, he do not
begin to rally, nor do even the appearance and recognition of his old
acquaintance, Mr. Sedley, on board much cheer he, until after a
conversation which they have one day, as the Major be lay languidly on
the deck. he say then he think he be doom; he have leave a little
something to his godson in his will, and he trust Mrs. Osborne would
remember he kindly and be happy in the marriage she be about to make.
"Married? not the least," Jos answer; "he have hear from she: she
make no mention of the marriage, and by the way, it be curious, she
write to say that Major Dobbin be go to be marry, and hop that
he would be happy." what be the date of Sedley's letter from
Europe? the civilian fetch they. they be two month later than the
Major's; and the ship's surgeon congratulate himself upon the
treatment adopt by he towards his new patient, who have be
consign to shipboard by the Madras practitioner with very small hope
indeed; for, from that day, the very day that he change the draught,
major Dobbin begin to mend. and thus it be that deserve officer,
Captain Kirk, be disappoint of his majority.
after they pass St. Helena, Major Dobbin's gaiety and strength be
such as to astonish all his fellow passenger. he lark with the
midshipman, play single-stick with the mate, run up the shroud like
a boy, sing a comic song one night to the amusement of the whole party
assemble over their grog after supper, and render himself so gay,
lively, and amiable that even Captain Bragg, who think there be
nothing in his passenger, and consider he be a poor-spirited feller
at first, be constrain to own that the Major be a reserved but
well-inform and meritorious officer. "he benot get distangy manner,
dammy," Bragg observe to his first mate; "he wouldnot do at Government
House, Roper, where his Lordship and Lady William be as kind to I,
and shake hand with I before the whole company, and ask I at
dinner to take beer with he, before the Commander-in-Chief himself; he
benot get manner, but therebe something about him--" and thus Captain
Bragg show that he possess discrimination as a man, as well as
ability as a commander.
but a calm take place when the ramchunder be within ten day' sail
of England, Dobbin become so impatient and ill-humour as to surprise
those comrade who have before admire his vivacity and good temper. he
do not recover until the breeze spring up again, and be in a highly
excited state when the pilot come on board. good God, how his heart
beat as the two friendly spire of Southampton come in sight.
CHAPTER LVIII
our Friend the Major
our Major have render himself so popular on board the ramchunder that
when he and Mr. Sedley descend into the welcome shore-boat which be
to take they from the ship, the whole crew, man and officer, the great
Captain Bragg himself lead off, give three cheer for Major Dobbin,
who blush very much and duck his head in token of thank. Jos, who
very likely think the cheer be for himself, take off his
gold-laced cap and wave it majestically to his friend, and they be
pull to shore and land with great dignity at the pier, whence they
proceed to the Royal George Hotel.
although the sight of that magnificent round of beef, and the silver
tankard suggestive of real british home-brew ale and porter, which
perennially greet the eye of the traveller return from foreign
part who enter the coffee-room of the George, be so invigorating and
delightful that a man enter such a comfortable snug homely English
inn might well like to stop some day there, yet Dobbin begin to talk
about a post-chaise instantly, and be no soon at Southampton than he
wish to be on the road to London. Jos, however, would not hear of
move that evening. why be he to pass a night in a post-chaise
instead of a great large undulating downy feather-bed which be there
ready to replace the horrid little narrow crib in which the portly
bengal gentleman have be confine during the voyage? he could not
think of move till his baggage be clear, or of travel until he
could do so with his chillum. so the Major be force to wait over
that night, and dispatch a letter to his family announce his
arrival, entreat from Jos a promise to write to his own friend.
Jos promise, but donot keep his promise. the Captain, the surgeon,
and one or two passenger come and din with our two gentleman at the
inn, Jos exert himself in a sumptuous way in order the dinner and
promise to go to town the next day with the Major. the landlord say
it do his eye good to see Mr. Sedley take off his first pint of
porter. if I have time and dare to enter into digression, I would
write a chapter about that first pint of porter drink upon english
ground. ah, how good it be! it be worth-while to leave home for a
year, just to enjoy that one draught.
Major Dobbin make his appearance the next morning very neatly shave
and dress, accord to his willnot. indeed, it be so early in the
morning that nobody be up in the house except that wonderful boot of
an inn who never seem to want sleep; and the Major could hear the
snore of the various inmate of the house roar through the
corridor as he creak about in those dim passage. then the
sleepless Boots go shirk round from door to door, gather up at
each the Bluchers, Wellingtons, Oxonians, which stand outside. then
Jos's native servant arise and begin to get ready his master's
ponderous dressing apparatus and prepare his hookah; then the
maidservant get up, and meet the dark man in the passage,
shriek, and mistook he for the devil. he and Dobbin stumble over
their pail in the passage as they be scour the deck of the
Royal George. when the first unshorn waiter appear and unbarred the
door of the inn, the Major think that the time for departure be
arrive, and order a post-chaise to be fetch instantly, that they
might set off.
he then direct his step to Mr. Sedley's room and open the curtain
of the great large family bed wherein Mr. Jos be snore. "come, up!
Sedley," the Major say, "itbe time to be off; the chaise will be at
the door in half an hour."
Jos growl from under the counterpane to know what the time be; but
when he at last extort from the blush Major (who never tell fib,
however they might be to his advantage) what be the real hour of the
morning, he break out into a volley of bad language, which we will not
repeat here, but by which he give Dobbin to understand that he would
jeopardy his soul if he get up at that moment, that the Major might go
and be hang, that he would not travel with Dobbin, and that it be
most unkind and ungentlemanlike to disturb a man out of his sleep in
that way; on which the discomfited Major be oblige to retreat,
leave Jos to resume his interrupted slumber.
the chaise come up presently, and the Major would wait no long.
if he have be an english nobleman travel on a pleasure tour, or a
newspaper courier bear dispatch (government message be generally
carry much more quietly), he could not have travel more quickly.
the post-boy wonder at the fee he fling amongst they. how happy and
green the country look as the chaise whirl rapidly from mile-stone
to mile-stone, through neat country town where landlord come out to
welcome he with smile and bow; by pretty roadside inn, where the
sign hang on the elm, and horse and waggoner be drink under
the chequered shadow of the tree; by old hall and park; rustic
hamlet cluster round ancient grey church--and through the charming
friendly english landscape. be there any in the world like it? to a
traveller return home it look so kind--it seem to shake hand with
you as you pass through it. well, Major Dobbin pass through all this
from Southampton to London, and without note much beyond the
milestone along the road. you see he be so eager to see his parent
at Camberwell.
he grudge the time lose between Piccadilly and his old haunt at the
slaughter', whither he drive faithfully. long year have pass since
he see it last, since he and George, as young man, have enjoy many a
feast, and hold many a revel there. he have now pass into the stage
of old-fellow-hood. his hair be grizzle, and many a passion and
feeling of his youth have grow grey in that interval. there, however,
stand the old waiter at the door, in the same greasy black suit, with
the same double chin and flaccid face, with the same huge bunch of
seal at his fob, rattle his money in his pocket as before, and
receive the Major as if he have go away only a week ago. "put the
Major's thing in twenty-three, thatbe his room," John say, exhibit
not the least surprise. "roast fowl for your dinner, I suppose. you
benot get marry? they say you be marry--the Scotch surgeon of
yours be here. no, it be Captain Humby of the thirty-third, as be
quarter with the --th in Injee. like any warm water? what do you come
in a chay for--ain't the coach good enough?" and with this, the
faithful waiter, who know and remember every officer who use the
house, and with whom ten year be but as yesterday, lead the way up to
Dobbin's old room, where stand the great moreen bed, and the shabby
carpet, a think more dingy, and all the old black furniture cover
with faded chintz, just as the Major recollect they in his youth.
he remember George pace up and down the room, and bite his nail,
and swear that the Governor must come round, and that if he donot,
he donot care a straw, on the day before he be marry. he could
fancy he walk in, bang the door of Dobbin's room, and his own
hard by--
"you benot get young," John say, calmly survey his friend of former
day.
Dobbin laugh. "ten year and a fever donot make a man young, John,"
he say. "it be you that be always young--no, you be always old."
"what become of Captain Osborne's widow?" John say. "fine young
fellow that. Lord, how he use to spend his money. he never come back
after that day he be march from here. he owe I three pound at
this minute. look here, I have it in my book. 'April 10, 1815,
Captain Osborne: '3 pound.' I wonder whether his father would pay
I," and so say, John of the Slaughters' pull out the very morocco
pocket-book in which he have note his loan to the Captain, upon a
greasy fade page still extant, with many other scrawl memoranda
regard the bygone frequenter of the house.
have induct his customer into the room, John retire with perfect
calmness; and Major Dobbin, not without a blush and a grin at his own
absurdity, choose out of his kit the very smart and most become
civil costume he possess, and laugh at his own tan face and grey
hair, as he survey they in the dreary little toilet-glass on the
dressing-table.
"Ibe glad old John donot forget I," he think. "shewill know I, too,
I hope." and he sally out of the inn, bend his step once more in
the direction of Brompton.
every minute incident of his last meeting with Amelia be present to
the constant man's mind as he walk towards her house. the arch and
the Achilles statue be up since he have last be in Piccadilly; a
hundred change have occur which his eye and mind vaguely note. he
begin to tremble as he walk up the lane from Brompton, that
well-remember lane lead to the street where she live. be she
go to be marry or not? if he be to meet she with the little
boy--Good God, what should he do? he see a woman come to he with a
child of five year old--be that she? he begin to shake at the mere
possibility. when he come up to the row of house, at last, where she
live, and to the gate, he catch hold of it and pause. he might have
hear the thumping of his own heart. "may God Almighty bless she,
whatever have happen," he think to himself. "Psha! she may be go
from here," he say and go in through the gate.
the window of the parlour which she use to occupy be open, and there
be no inmate in the room. the Major think he recognize the
piano, though, with the picture over it, as it use to be in former
day, and his perturbation be renew. Mr. Clapp's brass plate be
still on the door, at the knocker of which Dobbin perform a summon.
a buxom-look lass of sixteen, with bright eye and purple cheek,
come to answer the knock and look hard at the Major as he leant back
against the little porch.
he be as pale as a ghost and could hardly falter out the words--"doe
Mrs. Osborne live here?"
she look he hard in the face for a moment--and then turn white
too--say, "Lord bless I--itbe Major Dobbin." she hold out both her
hand shaking--"don't you remember I?" she say. "I use to call you
Major Sugarplums." on which, and I believe it be for the first time
that he ever so conduct himself in his life, the Major take the girl
in his arm and kiss she. she begin to laugh and cry hysterically,
and call out "Ma, Pa!" with all her voice, bring up those worthy
people, who have already be survey the Major from the casement of
the ornamental kitchen, and be astonish to find their daughter in
the little passage in the embrace of a great tall man in a blue
frock-coat and white duck trouser.
"Ibe an old friend," he say--not without blush though. "donot you
remember I, Mrs. Clapp, and those good cake you use to make for tea?
donot you recollect I, Clapp? Ibe George's godfather, and just come
back from India." a great shaking of hand ensue--Mrs. Clapp be
greatly affect and delight; she call upon heaven to interpose a
vast many time in that passage.
the landlord and landlady of the house lead the worthy Major into the
Sedleys' room (whereof he remember every single article of furniture,
from the old brass ornament piano, once a natty little instrument,
Stothard maker, to the screen and the alabaster miniature tombstone,
in the midst of which tick Mr. Sedley's gold watch), and there, as he
sit down in the lodger's vacant arm-chair, the father, the mother, and
the daughter, with a thousand ejaculatory break in the narrative,
inform Major Dobbin of what we know already, but of particular in
Amelia's history of which he be not aware--namely of Mrs. Sedley's
death, of George's reconcilement with his grandfather Osborne, of the
way in which the widow take on at leave he, and of other particular
of her life. twice or thrice he be go to ask about the marriage
question, but his heart fail he. he do not care to lay it bare to
these people. finally, he be inform that Mrs. O. be go to walk
with her pa in Kensington Gardens, whither she always go with the old
gentleman (who be very weak and peevish now, and lead she a sad life,
though she behave to he like an angel, to be sure), of a fine
afternoon, after dinner.
"Ibe very much press for time," the Major say, "and have business
to-night of importance. I should like to see Mrs. Osborne tho'.
suppose Miss Polly would come with I and show I the way?"
Miss Polly be charm and astonish at this proposal. she know the
way. she would show Major Dobbin. she have often be with Mr. Sedley
when Mrs. O. be go--be go Russell Square way--and know the bench
where he like to sit. she bounce away to her apartment and appear
presently in her good bonnet and her mamma's yellow shawl and large
pebble brooch, of which she assume the loan in order to make herself a
worthy companion for the Major.
that officer, then, in his blue frock-coat and buckskin glove, give
the young lady his arm, and they walk away very gaily. he be glad
to have a friend at hand for the scene which he dread somehow. he
ask a thousand more question from his companion about Amelia: his
kind heart grieve to think that she should have have to part with her
son. how do she bear it? do she see he often? be Mr. Sedley pretty
comfortable now in a worldly point of view? polly answer all these
question of Major Sugarplums to the very good of her power.
and in the midst of their walk an incident occur which, though very
simple in its nature, be productive of the great delight to Major
Dobbin. a pale young man with feeble whisker and a stiff white
neckcloth come walk down the lane, en sandwich--have a lady, that
be, on each arm. one be a tall and command middle-aged female,
with feature and a complexion similar to those of the clergyman of the
Church of England by whose side she march, and the other a stunt
little woman with a dark face, ornament by a fine new bonnet and
white ribbon, and in a smart pelisse, with a rich gold watch in the
midst of her person. the gentleman, pinion as he be by these two
lady, carry far a parasol, shawl, and basket, so that his arm
be entirely engage, and of course he be unable to touch his hat in
acknowledgement of the curtsey with which Miss Mary Clapp greet he.
he merely bow his head in reply to her salutation, which the two
lady return with a patronizing air, and at the same time look
severely at the individual in the blue coat and bamboo cane who
accompany Miss Polly.
"whobe that?" ask the Major, amuse by the group, and after he have
make way for the three to pass up the lane. Mary look at he rather
roguishly.
"that be our curate, the Reverend Mr. Binny (a twitch from major
Dobbin), and his sister Miss B. Lord bless we, how she do use to
worret we at Sunday-school; and the other lady, the little one with a
cast in her eye and the handsome watch, be Mrs. Binny--Miss Grits that
be; her pa be a grocer, and keep the little Original Gold Tea Pot in
Kensington Gravel Pits. they be marry last month, and be just
come back from Margate. shebe five thousand pound to her fortune; but
she and Miss B., who make the match, have quarrel already."
if the Major have twitch before, he start now, and slap the
bamboo on the ground with an emphasis which make Miss Clapp cry, "law,"
and laugh too. he stand for a moment, silent, with open mouth, look
after the retreat young couple, while Miss Mary tell their history;
but he do not hear beyond the announcement of the reverend gentleman's
marriage; his head be swim with felicity. after this rencontre he
begin to walk double quick towards the place of his destination--and
yet they be too soon (for he be in a great tremor at the idea of a
meeting for which he have be long any time these ten
years)--through the Brompton lane, and enter at the little old
portal in Kensington Garden wall.
"there they be," say Miss Polly, and she feel he again start back on
her arm. she be a confidante at once of the whole business. she know
the story as well as if she have read it in one of her favourite
novel-book--Fatherless Fanny, or the Scottish Chiefs.
"suppose you be to run on and tell she," the Major say. Polly run
forward, her yellow shawl stream in the breeze.
Old Sedley be seat on a bench, his handkerchief place over his

old time to which Amelia have listen and award a patient smile many
a time before. she could of late think of her own affair, and smile
or make other mark of recognition of her father's story, scarcely
hear a word of the old man's tale. as Mary come bounce along, and
Amelia catch sight of she, she start up from her bench. her first
thought be that something have happen to Georgy, but the sight of the
messenger's eager and happy face dissipate that fear in the timorous
mother's bosom.
"News! News!" cry the emissary of Major Dobbin. "hebe come! hebe
come!"
"who be come?" say Emmy, still think of her son.
"look there," answer Miss Clapp, turn round and pointing; in which
direction Amelia look, see Dobbin's lean figure and long shadow
stalk across the grass. Amelia start in her turn, blush up,
and, of course, begin to cry. at all this simple little creature's
fete, the grandes eaux be accustom to play. he look at she--oh,
how fondly--as she come run towards he, her hand before she,
ready to give they to he. she benot change. she be a little pale,
a little stouter in figure. her eye be the same, the kind trustful
eye. there be scarce three line of silver in her soft brown hair.
she give he both her hand as she look up flush and smile

never leave she? she must have yield: she could not but have obey
he.
"I--i've another arrival to announce," he say after a pause.
"Mrs. Dobbin?" Amelia say, make a movement back--why donot he speak?
"no," he say, let her hand go: "who have tell you those lie? I
mean, your brother Jos come in the same ship with I, and be come home
to make you all happy."
"Papa, papa!" Emmy cry out, "here be news! my brother be in
England. he be come to take care of you. here be Major Dobbin."
Mr. Sedley start up, shake a great deal and gather up his
thought. then he step forward and make an old-fashioned bow to the
Major, whom he call Mr. Dobbin, and hop his worthy father, Sir
William, be quite well. he propose to call upon Sir William, who have
do he the honour of a visit a short time ago. Sir William have not
call upon the old gentleman for eight year--it be that visit he be
think of return.
"he be very much shake," Emmy whisper as Dobbin go up and
cordially shake hand with the old man.
although he have such particular business in London that evening, the

come home and partake of tea. Amelia put her arm under that of her

walk very slowly and tell a number of ancient history about himself
and his poor Bessy, his former prosperity, and his bankruptcy. his
thought, as be usual with fail old man, be quite in former time.
the present, with the exception of the one catastrophe which he feel,
he know little about. the Major be glad to let he talk on. his eye
be fix upon the figure in front of he--the dear little figure
always present to his imagination and in his prayer, and visit his
dream wakeful or slumber.
Amelia be very happy, smile, and active all that evening, perform
her duty as hostess of the little entertainment with the utmost grace
and propriety, as Dobbin think. his eye follow she about as they
sit in the twilight. how many a time have he long for that moment and
thought of she far away under hot wind and in weary march, gentle
and happy, kindly minister to the want of old age, and decorate
poverty with sweet submission--as he see she now. I do not say that
his taste be the high, or that it be the duty of great intellect
to be content with a bread-and-butter paradise, such as suffice our
simple old friend; but his desire be of this sort, whether for good
or bad, and, with Amelia to help he, he be as ready to drink as many
cup of tea as Doctor Johnson.
Amelia see this propensity, laughingly encourage it and look
exceedingly roguish as she administer to he cup after cup. it be
true she do not know that the Major have have no dinner and that the

and George have sit many a time carousing, when she be a child just

the first thing Mrs. Osborne show the Major be Georgy's miniature,

Dobbin tell he all, and a little more perhaps than all, that have
happen on board the ramchunder, and exaggerate Jos's benevolent

in his old day. the truth be that during the voyage the Major have

and in fine I be ashamed to say that the Major stretch the truth so

at his accustomed hour Mr. Sedley begin to doze in his chair, and then

with great eagerness--it relate exclusively to georgy. she do not
talk at all about her own suffering at break from he, for indeed,
this worthy woman, though she be half-kill by the separation from
the child, yet think it be very wicked in she to repine at lose
he; but everything concern he, his virtue, talent, and
prospect, she pour out. she describe his angelic beauty; narrate
a hundred instance of his generosity and greatness of mind whilst
live with she; how a Royal Duchess have stop and admire he in
Kensington Gardens; how splendidly he be care for now, and how he have
a groom and a pony; what quickness and cleverness he have, and what a
prodigiously well-read and delightful person the Reverend Lawrence Veal
be, George's master. "he know everything," Amelia say. "he have the
most delightful party. you who be so learn yourself, and have
read so much, and be so clever and accomplished--don't shake your head
and say no--he always use to say you be--you will be charm with
Mr. Veal's party. the last Tuesday in every month. he say there be
no place in the bar or the senate that Georgy may not aspire to. look
here," and she go to the piano-drawer and draw out a theme of
Georgy's composition. this great effort of genius, which be still in
the possession of George's mother, be as follow:
on Selfishness--of all the vice which degrade the human character,
selfishness be the most odious and contemptible. an undue love of self
lead to the most monstrous crime and occasion the great
misfortune both in States and Families. as a selfish man will
impoverish his family and often bring they to ruin, so a selfish king
bring ruin on his people and often plunge they into war.
example: the selfishness of Achilles, as remark by the poet Homer,
occasion a thousand woe to the Greeks--muri Achaiois alge
etheke--(hom. Il. a. 2). the selfishness of the late Napoleon Bonaparte
occasion innumerable war in Europe and cause he to perish,
himself, in a miserable island--that of Saint Helena in the Atlantic
Ocean.
we see by these example that we be not to consult our own interest
and ambition, but that we be to consider the interest of other as
well as our own.
George S. Osborne Athene House, 24 April, 1827
"think of he write such a hand, and quote Greek too, at his age,"
the delighted mother say. "oh, William," she add, hold out her
hand to the Major, "what a treasure Heaven have give I in that boy!
he be the comfort of my life--and he be the image of--of he thatbe
go!"

when he get back to the slaughter', the roast fowl be of course cold,
