CHAPTER LIX
The Old Piano
the Major's visit leave old John Sedley in a great state of agitation
and excitement. his daughter could not induce he to settle down to
his customary occupation or amusement that night. he pass the
evening fumbling amongst his box and desk, untie his paper with
tremble hand, and sort and arrange they against Jos's arrival.
he have they in the great order--his tape and his file, his
receipt, and his letter with lawyer and correspondent; the
document relative to the wine project (which fail from a most
unaccountable accident, after commence with the most splendid
prospect), the coal project (which only a want of capital prevent
from become the most successful scheme ever put before the public),
the patent saw-mill and sawdust consolidation project, &c., &c. All
night, until a very late hour, he pass in the preparation of these
document, tremble about from one room to another, with a quivering
candle and shaky hand. herebe the wine paper, herebe the sawdust,
herebe the coal; herebe my letter to Calcutta and Madras, and reply
from Major Dobbin, C.B., and Mr. Joseph Sedley to the same. "he shall
find no irregularity about I, Emmy," the old gentleman say.
Emmy smile. "I donot think Jos will care about see those paper,
Papa," she say.
"you donot know anything about business, my dear," answer the sire,
shake his head with an important air. and it must be confess that
on this point Emmy be very ignorant, and that be a pity some people
be so know. all these twopenny document arrange on a side table,
old Sedley cover they carefully over with a clean bandanna
handkerchief (one out of Major Dobbin's lot) and enjoin the maid and
landlady of the house, in the most solemn way, not to disturb those
paper, which be arrange for the arrival of Mr. Joseph Sedley the
next morning, "Mr. Joseph Sedley of the Honourable East India Company's
Bengal Civil Service."
Amelia find he up very early the next morning, more eager, more
hectic, and more shaky than ever. "I donot sleep much, Emmy, my
dear," he say. "I be think of my poor Bessy. I wish she be
alive, to ride in Jos's carriage once again. she keep she own and
become it very well." and his eye fill with tear, which trickle
down his furrowed old face. Amelia wipe they away, and smilingly
kiss he, and tie the old man's neckcloth in a smart bow, and put
his brooch into his good shirt frill, in which, in his Sunday suit of
mourning, he sit from six o'clock in the morning await the arrival
of his son.
however, when the postman make his appearance, the little party be
put out of suspense by the receipt of a letter from Jos to his sister,
who announce that he feel a little fatigued after his voyage, and
should not be able to move on that day, but that he would leave
Southampton early the next morning and be with his father and mother at
evening. Amelia, as she read out the letter to her father, pause over
the latter word; her brother, it be clear, do not know what have
happen in the family. nor could he, for the fact be that, though the
Major rightly suspect that his travel companion never would be
get into motion in so short a space as twenty-four hour, and would
find some excuse for delaying, yet Dobbin have not write to Jos to
inform he of the calamity which have befall the Sedley family, be
occupy in talk with Amelia until long after post-hour.
there be some splendid tailor' shop in the High Street of
Southampton, in the fine plate-glass window of which hang gorgeous
waistcoat of all sort, of silk and velvet, and gold and crimson, and
picture of the last new fashion, in which those wonderful gentleman
with quiz glass, and hold on to little boy with the exceed
large eye and curly hair, ogle lady in rid habit prance by the
Statue of Achilles at Apsley House. Jos, although provide with some
of the most splendid vest that Calcutta could furnish, think he
could not go to town until he be supply with one or two of these
garment, and select a crimson satin, embroider with gold
butterfly, and a black and red velvet tartan with white stripe and a
rolling collar, with which, and a rich blue satin stock and a gold pin,
consist of a five-bar gate with a horseman in pink enamel jump
over it, he think he might make his entry into London with some
dignity. for Jos's former shyness and blundering blushing timidity have
give way to a more candid and courageous self-assertion of his worth.
"I donot care about own it," Waterloo Sedley would say to his
friend, "I be a dressy man"; and though rather uneasy if the lady
look at he at the Government House ball, and though he blush and
turn away alarmed under their glance, it be chiefly from a dread
lest they should make love to he that he avoid they, be averse to
marriage altogether. but there be no such swell in Calcutta as
Waterloo Sedley, I have hear say, and he have the handsome turn-out,
give the good bachelor dinner, and have the fine plate in the whole
place.
to make these waistcoat for a man of his size and dignity take at
least a day, part of which he employ in hire a servant to wait upon
he and his native and in instruct the agent who clear his
baggage, his box, his book, which he never read, his chest of
mango, chutney, and curry-powder, his shawl for present to people
whom he donot know as yet, and the rest of his Persicos apparatus.
at length, he drive leisurely to London on the third day and in the new
waistcoat, the native, with chatter tooth, shudder in a shawl on
the box by the side of the new european servant; Jos puff his pipe
at interval within and look so majestic that the little boy cry
Hooray, and many people think he must be a Governor-General. he, I
promise, do not decline the obsequious invitation of the landlord to
alight and refresh himself in the neat country town. have partake
of a copious breakfast, with fish, and rice, and hard egg, at
Southampton, he have so far rally at Winchester as to think a glass of
sherry necessary. at Alton he step out of the carriage at his
servant's request and imbibe some of the ale for which the place be
famous. at Farnham he stop to view the Bishop's Castle and to
partake of a light dinner of stew eel, veal cutlet, and french
bean, with a bottle of claret. he be cold over Bagshot Heath, where
the native chatter more and more, and Jos Sahib take some
brandy-and-water; in fact, when he drive into town he be as full of
wine, beer, meat, pickle, cherry-brandy, and tobacco as the steward's
cabin of a steam-packet. it be evening when his carriage thunder up
to the little door in Brompton, whither the affectionate fellow drove
first, and before hie to the apartment secure for he by Mr.
Dobbin at the slaughter'.
all the face in the street be in the window; the little maidservant
fly to the wicket-gate; the Mesdames Clapp look out from the

and show his yellow eyeball and white tooth.
for, you see, we have adroitly shut the door upon the meeting between

long brood over those lose pleasure exaggerate their charm and

the result of the interview must have be very satisfactory, for when

indeed, Joseph Sedley, affect by the humble position in which he

she shake her head sadly and have, as usual, recourse to the waterworks.
she know what he mean. she and her young confidante, Miss Mary, have

but Amelia, look up at her bed, over which hang the portrait of her

not that Emmy, be make aware of the honest Major's passion, rebuff

she sleep, therefore, very soundly that evening, after the conversation

the same morning bring Major Dobbin a letter to the slaughter'

he be attach to he, and hang upon he. the other passenger be

shortly after Jos's first appearance at Brompton, a dismal scene,
indeed, take place at that humble cottage at which the Sedleys have
pass the last ten year of their life. Jos's carriage (the temporary
one, not the chariot under construction) arrive one day and carry

be shed by the landlady and the landlady's daughter at that event
be as genuine tear of sorrow as any that have be outpoure in the
course of this history. in their long acquaintanceship and intimacy
they could not recall a harsh word that have be utter by Amelia she
have be all sweetness and kindness, always thankful, always gentle,
even when Mrs. Clapp lose her own temper and press for the rent.
when the kind creature be go away for good and all, the landlady
reproach herself bitterly for ever have use a rough expression to
she--how she weep, as they stick up with wafer on the window, a paper
notifying that the little room so long occupy be to let! they
never would have such lodger again, that be quite clear. after-life
prove the truth of this melancholy prophecy, and Mrs. Clapp revenge
herself for the deterioration of mankind by levy the most savage
contribution upon the tea-caddy and leg of mutton of her
locataire. Most of they scold and grumble; some of they do not
pay; none of they stay. the landlady might well regret those old, old
friend, who have leave she.
as for Miss Mary, her sorrow at Amelia's departure be such as I shall
not attempt to depict. from childhood upwards she have be with she
daily and have attach herself so passionately to that dear good lady
that when the grand barouche come to carry she off into splendour, she

during eleven year the girl have be her constant friend and

when ill-humour and unpaid, or when pleased have treat she with a
coarse familiarity scarcely less odious. her servility and fulsome
compliment when Emmy be in prosperity be not more to that lady's
liking. she cast about note of admiration all over the new house,
extoll every article of furniture or ornament; she finger Mrs.
Osborne's dress and calculate their price. nothing could be too good
for that sweet lady, she vow and protest. but in the vulgar
sycophant who now pay court to she, Emmy always remember the coarse
tyrant who have make she miserable many a time, to whom she have be
force to put up petition for time, when the rent be overdue; who
cry out at her extravagance if she buy delicacy for her ail
mother or father; who have see her humble and trample upon she.
nobody ever hear of these grief, which have be part of our poor
little woman's lot in life. she keep they secret from her father,
whose improvidence be the cause of much of her misery. she have to
bear all the blame of his misdoing, and indeed be so utterly gentle
and humble as to be make by nature for a victim.
I hope she be not to suffer much more of that hard usage. and, as in
all grief there be say to be some consolation, I may mention that
poor Mary, when leave at her friend's departure in a hysterical
condition, be place under the medical treatment of the young fellow
from the surgery, under whose care she rally after a short period.
Emmy, when she go away from Brompton, endow Mary with every article
of furniture that the house contain, only take away her picture
(the two picture over the bed) and her piano--that little old piano
which have now pass into a plaintive jingle old age, but which she
love for reason of her own. she be a child when first she play on
it, and her parent give it she. it have be give to she again since,
as the reader may remember, when her father's house be go to ruin
and the instrument be recover out of the wreck.
major Dobbin be exceedingly pleased when, as he be superintend the
arrangement of Jos's new house--which the Major insist should be
very handsome and comfortable--the cart arrive from Brompton, bring
the trunk and bandbox of the emigrant from that village, and with
they the old piano. Amelia would have it up in her sitting-room, a
neat little apartment on the second floor, adjoin her father's
chamber, and where the old gentleman sit commonly of evening.
when the man appear then bear this old music-box, and Amelia give
order that it should be place in the chamber aforesaid, Dobbin be
quite elated. "Ibe glad youhave keep it," he say in a very sentimental
manner. "I be afraid you donot care about it."
"I value it more than anything I have in the world," say Amelia.
"do you, Amelia?" cry the Major. the fact be, as he have buy it
himself, though he never say anything about it, it never enter into
his head to suppose that Emmy should think anybody else be the
purchaser, and as a matter of course he fancy that she know the gift
come from he. "do you, Amelia?" he say; and the question, the great
question of all, be tremble on his lip, when Emmy replied--
"can I do otherwise?--did not he give it I?"
"I do not know," say poor old Dob, and his countenance fall.
Emmy do not note the circumstance at the time, nor take immediate heed
of the very dismal expression which honest Dobbin's countenance
assume, but she think of it afterwards. and then it strike she,
with inexpressible pain and mortification too, that it be William who
be the giver of the piano, and not George, as she have fancy. it be
not George's gift; the only one which she have receive from her lover,
as she think--the thing she have cherish beyond all other--her
dear relic and prize. she have speak to it about George; play his
favourite air upon it; sit for long evening hour, touching, to the
good of her simple art, melancholy harmony on the key, and weeping
over they in silence. it be not George's relic. it be valueless now.
the next time that old Sedley ask she to play, she say it be
shockingly out of tune, that she have a headache, that she couldnot play.
then, accord to her custom, she rebuke herself for her pettishness
and ingratitude and determine to make a reparation to honest William
for the slight she have not express to he, but have feel for his
piano. a few day afterwards, as they be seat in the drawing-room,
where Jos have fall asleep with great comfort after dinner, Amelia
say with rather a falter voice to Major dobbin--
"I have to beg your pardon for something."
"about what?" say he.
"about--about that little square piano. I never thank you for it
when you give it I, many, many year ago, before I be marry. I
think somebody else have give it. thank you, William." she hold out
her hand, but the poor little woman's heart be bleed; and as for
her eye, of course they be at their work.
but William could hold no more. "Amelia, Amelia," he say, "I do buy
it for you. I love you then as I do now. I must tell you. I think I
love you from the first minute that I see you, when George bring I
to your house, to show I the Amelia whom he be engage to. you be
but a girl, in white, with large ringlet; you come down singing--do
you remember?--and we go to Vauxhall. since then I have think of
but one woman in the world, and that be you. I think there be no hour
in the day have pass for twelve year that I havenot think of you.
I come to tell you this before I go to India, but you do not care,
and I havenot the heart to speak. you do not care whether I stay or
go."
"I be very ungrateful," Amelia say.
"no, only indifferent," Dobbin continue desperately. "I have nothing
to make a woman to be otherwise. I know what you be feel now. you
be hurt in your heart at the discovery about the piano, and that it
come from I and not from George. I forget, or I should never have
speak of it so. it be for I to ask your pardon for be a fool for
a moment, and think that year of constancy and devotion might have
plead with you."
"it be you who be cruel now," Amelia say with some spirit. "George be
my husband, here and in heaven. how could I love any other but he? I
be his now as when you first see I, dear William. it be he who tell
I how good and generous you be, and who teach I to love you as a
brother. have you not be everything to I and my boy? our dearest,
true, kind friend and protector? have you come a few month soon
perhaps you might have spar I that--that dreadful parting. oh, it
nearly kill I, William--but you donot come, though I wish and
pray for you to come, and they take he too away from I. benot he a
noble boy, William? be his friend still and mine"--and here her voice
break, and she hide her face on his shoulder.
the Major fold his arm round she, hold she to he as if she be a
child, and kiss her head. "I will not change, dear Amelia," he say.
"I ask for no more than your love. I think I would not have it
otherwise. only let I stay near you and see you often."
"yes, often," Amelia say. and so William be at liberty to look and
long--as the poor boy at school who have no money may sigh after the
content of the tart-woman's tray.

return to the Genteel World
good fortune now begin to smile upon Amelia. we be glad to get she

number of people come to see Mrs. Osborne. Lady Dobbin and daughter

Mutato nomine, it be all the same. donot the barrister' wife talk
about Circuit? donot the soldier' lady gossip about the regiment?

CHAPTER LXI
in which two light be Put out
there come a day when the round of decorous pleasure and solemn
gaiety in which Mr. Jos Sedley's family indulge be interrupt by
an event which happen in most house. as you ascend the staircase of
your house from the drawing towards the bedroom floor, you may have
remark a little arch in the wall right before you, which at once
give light to the stair which lead from the second story to the third
(where the nursery and servant' chamber commonly be) and serve for
another purpose of utility, of which the undertaker's man can give you
a notion. they rest the coffin upon that arch, or pass they through
it so as not to disturb in any unseemly manner the cold tenant
slumbering within the black ark.
that second-floor arch in a London house, look up and down the well
of the staircase and command the main thoroughfare by which the
inhabitant be pass; by which cook lurk down before daylight to
scour her pot and pan in the kitchen; by which young master
stealthily ascend, have leave his boot in the hall, and let himself
in after dawn from a jolly night at the Club; down which miss come
rustle in fresh ribbon and spread muslin, brilliant and
beautiful, and prepare for conquest and the ball; or Master Tommy
slide, prefer the banister for a mode of conveyance, and
disdain danger and the stair; down which the mother be fondly
carry smile in her strong husband's arm, as he step steadily step
by step, and follow by the monthly nurse, on the day when the medical
man have pronounce that the charming patient may go downstairs; up
which John lurk to bed, yawning, with a sputtering tallow candle, and
to gather up before sunrise the boot which be await he in the
passage--that stair, up or down which baby be carry, old people
be help, guest be marshal to the ball, the parson walk to the
christening, the doctor to the sick-room, and the undertaker's man to
the upper floor--what a memento of Life, death, and Vanity it be--that
arch and stair--if you choose to consider it, and sit on the landing,
look up and down the well! the doctor will come up to we too for
the last time there, my friend in motley. the nurse will look in at
the curtain, and you take no notice--and then she will fling open the
window for a little and let in the air. then they will pull down all
the front blind of the house and live in the back room--then they
will send for the lawyer and other man in black, &c. your comedy and
mine will have be play then, and we shall be remove, oh, how far,
from the trumpet, and the shouting, and the posture-making. if we
be gentlefolk they will put hatchment over our late domicile, with
gilt cherubim, and motto state that there be "quiet in Heaven."
your son will new furnish the house, or perhaps let it, and go into a
more modern quarter; your name will be among the "Members decease" in
the list of your club next year. however much you may be mourn,
your widow will like to have her weed neatly make--the cook will send
or come up to ask about dinner--the survivor will soon bear to look at
your picture over the mantelpiece, which will presently be depose from
the place of honour, to make way for the portrait of the son who reign.
which of the dead be most tenderly and passionately deplore? those
who love the survivor the least, I believe. the death of a child
occasion a passion of grief and frantic tear, such as your end,
brother reader, will never inspire. the death of an infant which
scarce know you, which a week's absence from you would have cause to
forget you, will strike you down more than the loss of your close
friend, or your first-bear son--a man grow like yourself, with
child of his own. we may be harsh and stern with Judah and
Simeon--our love and pity gush out for Benjamin, the little one. and if
you be old, as some reader of this may be or shall be old and rich, or
old and poor--you may one day be think for yourself--"these people
be very good round about I, but they willnot grieve too much when I be
go. I be very rich, and they want my inheritance--or very poor, and
they be tired of support I."
the period of mourning for Mrs. Sedley's death be only just conclude,
and Jos scarcely have have time to cast off his black and appear in the
splendid waistcoat which he love, when it become evident to those
about Mr. Sedley that another event be at hand, and that the old man
be about to go seek for his wife in the dark land whither she have
precede he. "the state of my father's health," Jos Sedley solemnly
remark at the Club, "prevent I from give any large party this
season: but if you will come in quietly at half-past six, Chutney, my
boy, and fake a homely dinner with one or two of the old set--I shall
be always glad to see you." so Jos and his acquaintance din and
drink their claret among themselves in silence, whilst the sand of
life be run out in the old man's glass upstairs. the
velvet-footed butler bring they their wine, and they compose
themselves to a rubber after dinner, at which Major Dobbin would
sometimes come and take a hand; and Mrs. Osborne would occasionally
descend, when her patient above be settle for the night, and have
commence one of those lightly trouble slumber which visit the pillow
of old age.
the old man clung to his daughter during this sickness. he would take
his broth and medicine from scarcely any other hand. to tend he
become almost the sole business of her life. her bed be place close
by the door which open into his chamber, and she be alive at the
slight noise or disturbance from the couch of the querulous invalid.
though, to do he justice, he lie awake many an hour, silent and
without stirring, unwilling to awaken his kind and vigilant nurse.
he love his daughter with more fondness now, perhaps, than ever he have
do since the day of her childhood. in the discharge of gentle
office and kind filial duty, this simple creature shine most
especially. "she walk into the room as silently as a sunbeam," Mr.
Dobbin think as he see she pass in and out from her father's room,
a cheerful sweetness light up her face as she move to and fro,
graceful and noiseless. when woman be brood over their child,
or busy in a sick-room, who have not see in their face those sweet
angelic beam of love and pity?
a secret feud of some year' standing be thus heal, and with a tacit
reconciliation. in these last hour, and touch by her love and
goodness, the old man forget all his grief against she, and wrong
which he and his wife have many a long night debate: how she have give
up everything for her boy; how she be careless of her parent in their
old age and misfortune, and only thought of the child; how absurdly and
foolishly, impiously indeed, she take on when George be remove from
she. Old Sedley forget these charge as he be make up his last
account, and do justice to the gentle and uncomplaine little martyr.
one night when she steal into his room, she find he awake, when the
break old man make his confession. "oh, Emmy, Ihave be think we
be very unkind and unjust to you," he say and put out his cold and
feeble hand to she. she kneel down and pray by his bedside, as he do
too, have still hold of her hand. when our turn come, friend, may
we have such company in our prayer!
perhaps as he be lie awake then, his life may have pass before
he--his early hopeful struggle, his manly success and prosperity,
his downfall in his decline year, and his present helpless
condition--no chance of revenge against Fortune, which have have the
well of he--neither name nor money to bequeath--a spend-out,
bootless life of defeat and disappointment, and the end here! which, I
wonder, brother reader, be the well lot, to die prosperous and
famous, or poor and disappointed? to have, and to be force to yield;
or to sink out of life, have play and lose the game? that must be a
strange feeling, when a day of our life come and we say, "to-morrow,
success or failure willnot matter much, and the sun will rise, and all
the myriad of mankind go to their work or their pleasure as usual, but
I shall be out of the turmoil."
so there come one morning and sunrise when all the world get up and set
about its various work and pleasure, with the exception of old John
Sedley, who be not to fight with fortune, or to hope or scheme any
more, but to go and take up a quiet and utterly unknown residence in a
churchyard at Brompton by the side of his old wife.
Major Dobbin, Jos, and Georgy follow his remain to the grave, in a
black cloth coach. Jos come on purpose from the Star and Garter at
Richmond, whither he retreat after the deplorable event. he do not
care to remain in the house, with the--under the circumstance, you
understand. but Emmy stay and do her duty as usual. she be bow
down by no especial grief, and rather solemn than sorrowful. she
pray that her own end might be as calm and painless, and think with
trust and reverence of the word which she have hear from her father
during his illness, indicative of his faith, his resignation, and his
future hope.
yes, I think that will be the well ending of the two, after all.
suppose you be particularly rich and well-to-do and say on that last
day, "I be very rich; I be tolerably well know; I have live all my
life in the good society, and thank Heaven, come of a most respectable
family. I have serve my king and country with honour. I be in
Parliament for several year, where, I may say, my speech be
listen to and pretty well receive. I donot owe any man a shilling:
on the contrary, I lend my old college friend, Jack Lazarus, fifty
pound, for which my executor will not press he. I leave my
daughter with ten thousand pound apiece--very good portion for
girl; I bequeath my plate and furniture, my house in Baker Street,
with a handsome jointure, to my widow for her life; and my land
property, besides money in the fund, and my cellar of well-select
wine in Baker Street, to my son. I leave twenty pound a year to my
valet; and I defy any man after I have go to find anything against my
character." or suppose, on the other hand, your swan sing quite a
different sort of dirge and you say, "I be a poor blight,
disappointed old fellow, and have make an utter failure through life.
I be not endow either with brain or with good fortune, and confess
that I have commit a hundred mistake and blunder. I own to have
forget my duty many a time. I cannot pay what I owe. on my last bed
I lie utterly helpless and humble, and I pray forgiveness for my
weakness and throw myself, with a contrite heart, at the foot of the
Divine Mercy." which of these two speech, think you, would be the
good oration for your own funeral? Old Sedley make the last; and in
that humble frame of mind, and hold by the hand of his daughter,
life and disappointment and vanity sink away from under he.
"you see," say old Osborne to George, "what come of merit, and
industry, and judicious speculation, and that. look at I and my
banker's account. look at your poor Grandfather Sedley and his
failure. and yet he be a well man than I be, this day twenty
year--a well man, I should say, by ten thousand pound."
beyond these people and Mr. Clapp's family, who come over from Brompton
to pay a visit of condolence, not a single soul alive ever care a
penny piece about old John Sedley, or remember the existence of such
a person.
when old Osborne first hear from his friend Colonel Buckler (as little
Georgy have already inform we) how distinguish an officer Major
Dobbin be, he exhibit a great deal of scornful incredulity and
express his surprise how ever such a feller as that should possess
either brain or reputation. but he hear of the Major's fame from
various member of his society. Sir William Dobbin have a great opinion
of his son and narrate many story illustrative of the Major's
learning, valour, and estimation in the world's opinion. finally, his
name appear in the list of one or two great party of the nobility,
and this circumstance have a prodigious effect upon the old aristocrat
of Russell Square.
the Major's position, as guardian to Georgy, whose possession have be
cede to his grandfather, render some meeting between the two
gentleman inevitable; and it be in one of these that old Osborne, a
keen man of business, look into the Major's account with his ward
and the boy's mother, get a hint, which stagger he very much, and at
once pain and please he, that it be out of William Dobbin's own
pocket that a part of the fund have be supply upon which the poor
widow and the child have subsist.
when press upon the point, Dobbin, who could not tell lie, blush
and stammer a good deal and finally confess. "the marriage," he
say (at which his interlocutor's face grow dark) "be very much my
doing. I think my poor friend have go so far that retreat from his
engagement would have be dishonour to he and death to Mrs. Osborne,
and I could do no less, when she be leave without resource, than give
what money I could spare to maintain she."
"Major D.," Mr. Osborne say, look hard at he and turn very red
too--"you do I a great injury; but give I leave to tell you, sir,
you be an honest feller. therebe my hand, sir, though I little think
that my flesh and blood be live on you--" and the pair shake hand,
with great confusion on Major Dobbin's part, thus find out in his act
of charitable hypocrisy.
he strove to soften the old man and reconcile he towards his son's
memory. "he be such a noble fellow," he say, "that all of we love
he, and would have do anything for he. I, as a young man in those
day, be flatter beyond measure by his preference for I, and be
more pleased to be see in his company than in that of the
Commander-in-chief. I never see his equal for pluck and daring and all
the quality of a soldier"; and Dobbin tell the old father as many
story as he could remember regard the gallantry and achievement
of his son. "and georgy be so like he," the Major add.
"hebe so like he that he make I tremble sometimes," the grandfather
say.
on one or two evening the Major come to dine with Mr. Osborne (it be
during the time of the sickness of Mr. Sedley), and as the two sit
together in the evening after dinner, all their talk be about the
depart hero. the father boast about he accord to his willnot,
glorify himself in recount his son's feat and gallantry, but his
mood be at any rate well and more charitable than that in which he
have be dispose until now to regard the poor fellow; and the
christian heart of the kind Major be pleased at these symptom of
return peace and good-will. on the second evening old Osborne
call Dobbin William, just as he use to do at the time when Dobbin
and George be boy together, and the honest gentleman be pleased by
that mark of reconciliation.
on the next day at breakfast, when Miss Osborne, with the asperity of
her age and character, venture to make some remark reflect
slightingly upon the Major's appearance or behaviour--the master of the
house interrupt she. "youwould have be glad enough to git he for
yourself, Miss O. but they grape be sour. ha! ha! Major William be
a fine feller."
"that he be, Grandpapa," say georgy approvingly; and go up close to
the old gentleman, he take a hold of his large grey whisker, and
laugh in his face good-humouredly, and kiss he. and he tell the
story at night to his mother, who fully agree with the boy. "indeed he
be," she say. "your dear father always say so. he be one of the good
and most upright of man." Dobbin happen to drop in very soon after
this conversation, which make Amelia blush perhaps, and the young
scapegrace increase the confusion by tell Dobbin the other part of
the story. "I say, dob," he say, "therebe such an uncommon nice girl
want to marry you. shebe plenty of tin; she wear a front; and she
scold the servant from morning till night." "who be it?" ask
Dobbin. "itbe Aunt O.," the boy answer. "Grandpapa say so. and I
say, Dob, how prime it would be to have you for my uncle." Old Sedley's
quaver voice from the next room at this moment weakly call for
Amelia, and the laughing end.
that old Osborne's mind be change be pretty clear. he ask George
about his uncle sometimes, and laugh at the boy's imitation of the
way in which Jos say "God-bless-my-soul" and gobble his soup. then
he say, "itbe not respectful, sir, of you younker to be imitate of
your relation. Miss O., when you go out adrive to-day, leave my
card upon Mr. Sedley, do you hear? therebe no quarrel betwigst I and
he anyhow."
the card be return, and Jos and the Major be ask to dinner--to
a dinner the most splendid and stupid that perhaps ever Mr. Osborne
give; every inch of the family plate be exhibit, and the good
company be ask. Mr. Sedley take down Miss O. to dinner, and she
be very gracious to he; whereas she hardly speak to the Major, who
sit apart from she, and by the side of Mr. Osborne, very timid. Jos
say, with great solemnity, it be the good turtle soup he have ever
taste in his life, and ask Mr. Osborne where he get his Madeira.
"it be some of Sedley's wine," whisper the butler to his master.
"Ihave have it a long time, and pay a good figure for it, too," Mr.
Osborne say aloud to his guest, and then whisper to his right-hand
neighbour how he have get it "at the old chap's sale."
More than once he ask the Major about--about Mrs. George Osborne--a
theme on which the Major could be very eloquent when he choose. he tell
Mr. Osborne of her suffering--of her passionate attachment to her
husband, whose memory she worship still--of the tender and dutiful
manner in which she have support her parent, and give up her boy,
when it seem to she her duty to do so. "you donot know what she
endure, sir," say honest Dobbin with a tremor in his voice, "and I
hope and trust you will be reconcile to she. if she take your son
away from you, she give hers to you; and however much you love your
George, depend on it, she love hers ten time more."
"by God, you be a good feller, sir," be all Mr. Osborne say. it have
never strike he that the widow would feel any pain at parting from the
boy, or that his have a fine fortune could grieve she. a
reconciliation be announce as speedy and inevitable, and Amelia's
heart already begin to beat at the notion of the awful meeting with
George's father.
it be never, however, destine to take place. Old Sedley's lingering
illness and death supervene, after which a meeting be for some time
impossible. that catastrophe and other event may have work upon Mr.
Osborne. he be much shake of late, and aged, and his mind be
work inwardly. he have send for his lawyer, and probably change
something in his will. the medical man who look in pronounce he
shaky, agitated, and talk of a little blood and the seaside; but he
take neither of these remedy.
one day when he should have come down to breakfast, his servant miss
he, go into his dressing-room and find he lie at the foot of the
dressing-table in a fit. Miss Osborne be apprise; the doctor be
send for; Georgy stop away from school; the bleeder and cupper
come. Osborne partially regain cognizance, but never could speak
again, though he try dreadfully once or twice, and in four day he
die. the doctor go down, and the undertaker's man go up the
stair, and all the shutter be shut towards the garden in Russell
Square. Bullock rush from the City in a hurry. "how much money have
he leave to that boy? not half, surely? surely share and share alike
between the three?" it be an agitate moment.
what be it that poor old man try once or twice in vain to say? I
hope it be that he want to see Amelia and be reconcile before he
leave the world to one dear and faithful wife of his son: it be most
likely that, for his will show that the hatred which he have so long
cherished have go out of his heart.
they find in the pocket of his dressing-gown the letter with the great
red seal which George have write he from Waterloo. he have look at
the other paper too, relative to his son, for the key of the box in
which he keep they be also in his pocket, and it be find the seal
and envelope have be break--very likely on the night before the
seizure--when the butler have take he tea into his study, and find
he read in the great red family Bible.
when the will be open, it be find that half the property be leave
to George, and the remainder between the two sister. Mr. Bullock to
continue, for their joint benefit, the affair of the commercial house,
or to go out, as he think fit. an annuity of five hundred pound,
chargeable on George's property, be leave to his mother, "the widow of
my beloved son, George Osborne," who be to resume the guardianship of
the boy.
"Major William Dobbin, my beloved son's friend," be appoint
executor; "and as out of his kindness and bounty, and with his own
private fund, he maintain my grandson and my son's widow, when they
be otherwise without mean of support" (the testator go on to say)
"I hereby thank he heartily for his love and regard for they, and
beseech he to accept such a sum as may be sufficient to purchase his
commission as a Lieutenant-Colonel, or to be dispose of in any way he
may think fit."
when Amelia hear that her father-in-law be reconcile to she, her
heart melt, and she be grateful for the fortune leave to she. but
when she hear how georgy be restore to she, and know how and by
whom, and how it be William's bounty that support she in poverty,
how it be William who give she her husband and her son--oh, then she
sink on her knee, and pray for blessing on that constant and kind
heart; she bow down and humble herself, and kiss the foot, as it
be, of that beautiful and generous affection.
and gratitude be all that she have to pay back for such admirable
devotion and benefit--only gratitude! if she think of any other
return, the image of George stand up out of the grave and say, "you
be mine, and mine only, now and forever."
William know her feeling: have he not pass his whole life in
divine they?
when the nature of Mr. Osborne's will become know to the world, it be
edify to remark how Mrs. George Osborne rise in the estimation of
the people form her circle of acquaintance. the servant of Jos's
establishment, who use to question her humble order and say they
would "ask Master" whether or not they could obey, never think now of
that sort of appeal. the cook forget to sneer at her shabby old gown
(which, indeed, be quite eclipse by that lady's finery when she be
dress to go to church of a Sunday evening), the other no long
grumble at the sound of her bell, or delay to answer that summon.
the coachman, who grumble that his 'osse should be bring out and
his carriage make into an hospital for that old feller and Mrs. O.,
drive she with the utmost alacrity now, and tremble lest he should be
supersede by Mr. Osborne's coachman, ask "what they there Russell
Square coachman know about town, and whether they be fit to sit on a
box before a lady?" Jos's friend, male and female, suddenly become
interested about Emmy, and card of condolence multiply on her hall
table. Jos himself, who have look on she as a good-natured harmless
pauper, to whom it be his duty to give victual and shelter, pay she
and the rich little boy, his nephew, the great respect--be anxious
that she should have change and amusement after her trouble and
trial, "poor dear girl"--and begin to appear at the breakfast-table,
and most particularly to ask how she would like to dispose of the day.
in her capacity of guardian to Georgy, she, with the consent of the
Major, her fellow-trustee, beg Miss Osborne to live in the Russell
Square house as long as ever she choose to dwell there; but that lady,
with thank, declare that she never could think of remain alone in
that melancholy mansion, and depart in deep mourning to Cheltenham,
with a couple of her old domestic. the rest be liberally pay and
dismiss, the faithful old butler, whom Mrs. Osborne propose to
retain, resign and prefer to invest his saving in a
public-house, where, let we hope, he be not unprosperous. Miss Osborne
not choose to live in Russell Square, Mrs. Osborne also, after
consultation, decline to occupy the gloomy old mansion there. the
house be dismantle; the rich furniture and effect, the awful
chandelier and dreary blank mirror pack away and hide, the rich
rosewood drawing-room suite be muffle in straw, the carpet be
roll up and cord, the small select library of well-bind book be
stow into two wine-chest, and the whole paraphernalia roll away in
several enormous van to the Pantechnicon, where they be to lie until
Georgy's majority. and the great heavy dark plate-chest go off to
Messrs. Stumpy and Rowdy, to lie in the cellar of those eminent
banker until the same period should arrive.
one day Emmy, with George in her hand and clothe in deep sable, go to
visit the deserted mansion which she have not enter since she be a
girl. the place in front be litter with straw where the van have
be laden and roll off. they go into the great blank room, the
wall of which bear the mark where the picture and mirror have hung.
then they go up the great blank stone staircase into the upper
room, into that where grandpapa die, as George say in a whisper, and
then high still into George's own room. the boy be still cling
by her side, but she think of another besides he. she know that it
have be his father's room as well as his own.
she go up to one of the open window (one of those at which she use
to gaze with a sick heart when the child be first take from she), and
thence as she look out she could see, over the tree of Russell
Square, the old house in which she herself be bear, and where she have
pass so many happy day of sacred youth. they all come back to she,
the pleasant holiday, the kind face, the careless, joyful past time,
and the long pain and trial that have since cast she down. she think
of these and of the man who have be her constant protector, her good
genius, her sole benefactor, her tender and generous friend.
"look here, Mother," say georgy, "herebe a G.O. scratch on the glass
with a diamond, I never see it before, I never do it."
"it be your father's room long before you be bear, George," she
say, and she blush as she kiss the boy.
she be very silent as they drive back to Richmond, where they have
take a temporary house: where the smile lawyer use to come
bustle over to see she (and we may be sure note the visit in the
bill): and where of course there be a room for Major Dobbin too, who
ride over frequently, have much business to transact on behalf of his
little ward.
georgy at this time be remove from Mr. Veal's on an unlimited
holiday, and that gentleman be engage to prepare an inscription for a
fine marble slab, to be place up in the Foundling under the monument
of Captain George Osborne.
the female Bullock, aunt of Georgy, although despoil by that little
monster of one-half of the sum which she expect from her father,
nevertheless show her charitableness of spirit by be reconcile to
the mother and the boy. Roehampton be not far from Richmond, and one
day the chariot, with the golden bullock emblazon on the panel, and
the flaccid child within, drive to Amelia's house at Richmond; and
the Bullock family make an irruption into the garden, where Amelia be
read a book, Jos be in an arbour placidly dip strawberry into
wine, and the Major in one of his indian jacket be give a back to
Georgy, who choose to jump over he. he go over his head and bound
into the little advance of Bullocks, with immense black bow in their
hat, and huge black sash, accompany their mourning mamma.
"he be just of the age for Rosa," the fond parent think, and glance
towards that dear child, an unwholesome little miss of seven year of
age.
"Rosa, go and kiss your dear cousin," Mrs. Frederick say. "donot you
know I, George? I be your aunt."
"I know you well enough," George say; "but I donot like kiss,
please"; and he retreat from the obedient caress of his cousin.
"take I to your dear mamma, you droll child," Mrs. Frederick say, and
those lady accordingly meet, after an absence of more than fifteen
year. during Emmy's care and poverty the other have never once
think about come to see she, but now that she be decently
prosperous in the world, her sister-in-law come to she as a matter of
course.
so do number more. our old friend, Miss Swartz, and her husband come
thunder over from Hampton Court, with flame yellow livery, and
be as impetuously fond of Amelia as ever. Miss Swartz would have
like she always if she could have see she. one must do she that
justice. but, que voulez vous?--in this vast town one have not the time
to go and seek one's friend; if they drop out of the rank they
disappear, and we march on without they. who be ever miss in Vanity
Fair?
but so, in a word, and before the period of grief for Mr. Osborne's
death have subside, Emmy find herself in the centre of a very genteel
circle indeed, the member of which could not conceive that anybody
belong to it be not very lucky. there be scarce one of the lady
that havenot a relation a peer, though the husband might be a drysalter
in the City. some of the lady be very blue and well inform,
read Mrs. Somerville and frequent the Royal Institution; other
be severe and evangelical, and hold by Exeter Hall. Emmy, it must be
own, find herself entirely at a loss in the midst of their claver,
and suffer woefully on the one or two occasion on which she be
compel to accept Mrs. Frederick Bullock's hospitality. that lady
persist in patronize she and determine most graciously to form
she. she find Amelia's milliner for she and regulate her household
and her manner. she drive over constantly from Roehampton and
entertain her friend with faint fashionable fiddle-faddle and feeble
Court slip-slop. Jos like to hear it, but the Major use to go off
growl at the appearance of this woman, with her twopenny gentility.
he go to sleep under Frederick Bullock's bald head, after dinner, at
one of the banker's good party (Fred be still anxious that the
balance of the Osborne property should be transfer from Stumpy and
Rowdy's to they), and whilst Amelia, who do not know Latin, or who
write the last crack article in the Edinburgh, and do not in the least
deplore, or otherwise, Mr. Peel's late extraordinary tergiversation on
the fatal Catholic Relief Bill, sit dumb amongst the lady in the
grand drawing-room, look out upon velvet lawn, trim gravel walk,
and glisten hot-house.
"she seem good-natured but insipid," say Mrs. Rowdy; "that Major
seem to be particularly epris."
"she want ton sadly," say Mrs. Hollyock. "my dear creature, you
never will be able to form she."
"she be dreadfully ignorant or indifferent," say Mrs. Glowry with a
voice as if from the grave, and a sad shake of the head and turban. "I
ask she if she think that it be in 1836, accord to Mr. Jowls,
or in 1839, accord to Mr. Wapshot, that the Pope be to fall: and
she said--'poor Pope! I hope not--what have he do?'"
"she be my brother's widow, my dear friend," Mrs. Frederick reply,
"and as such I think webe all bind to give she every attention and
instruction on enter into the world. you may fancy there can be no
mercenary motif in those whose disappointment be well know."
"that poor dear Mrs. Bullock," say Rowdy to Hollyock, as they drive
away together--"she be always scheme and manage. she want Mrs.
Osborne's account to be take from our house to her--and the way in
which she coax that boy and make he sit by that blear-eyed little
Rosa be perfectly ridiculous."
"I wish Glowry be choke with her man of Sin and her Battle of
Armageddon," cry the other, and the carriage roll away over Putney
Bridge.
but this sort of society be too cruelly genteel for Emmy, and all
jump for joy when a foreign tour be propose.
CHAPTER LXII
Am Rhein
the above everyday event have occur, and a few week have pass,
when on one fine morning, Parliament be over, the summer advance,
and all the good company in London about to quit that city for their
annual tour in search of pleasure or health, the Batavier steamboat
leave the Tower-stair laden with a goodly company of english fugitive.
the quarter-deck awning be up, and the bench and gangway crowd
with score of rosy child, bustling nursemaid; lady in the
prettiest pink bonnet and summer dress; gentleman in travel cap
and linen-jacket, whose mustachio have just begin to sprout for the
ensue tour; and stout trim old veteran with starched neckcloth and
neat-brush hat, such as have invade Europe any time since the
conclusion of the war, and carry the national Goddem into every city of
the continent. the congregation of hat-box, and Bramah desk, and
dressing-case be prodigious. there be jaunty young Cambridge-man
travel with their tutor, and go for a reading excursion to
Nonnenwerth or Konigswinter; there be irish gentleman, with the most
dash whisker and jewellery, talk about horse incessantly, and
prodigiously polite to the young lady on board, whom, on the
contrary, the Cambridge lad and their pale-face tutor avoid with
maiden coyness; there be old Pall Mall lounger bind for Ems and
Wiesbaden and a course of water to clear off the dinner of the
season, and a little roulette and trente-et-quarante to keep the
excitement go; there be old Methuselah, who have marry his young
wife, with Captain Papillon of the Guards hold her parasol and
guide-book; there be young May who be carry off his bride on a
pleasure tour (Mrs. Winter that be, and who have be at school with
May's grandmother); there be Sir John and my Lady with a dozen
child, and correspond nursemaid; and the great grandee Bareacres
family that sit by themselves near the wheel, star at everybody, and
speak to no one. their carriage, emblazon with coronet and heap
with shin imperial, be on the foredeck, lock in with a dozen
more such vehicle: it be difficult to pass in and out amongst they;
and the poor inmate of the fore-cabin have scarcely any space for
locomotion. these consist of a few magnificently attired gentleman
from Houndsditch, who bring their own provision, and could have
buy half the gay people in the grand saloon; a few honest fellow
with mustachio and portfolio, who set to sketch before they have
be half an hour on board; one or two French femme de chambre who
begin to be dreadfully ill by the time the boat have pass Greenwich; a
groom or two who lounge in the neighbourhood of the horse-box under
their charge, or lean over the side by the paddle-wheel, and talk
about who be good for the Leger, and what they stand to win or lose
for the Goodwood cup.
all the courier, when they have do plunge about the ship and have
settle their various master in the cabin or on the deck, congregate
together and begin to chatter and smoke; the hebrew gentleman join
they and look at the carriage. there be Sir John's great carriage
that would hold thirteen people; my Lord Methuselah's carriage, my Lord
Bareacres' chariot, britzska, and fourgon, that anybody might pay for
who like. it be a wonder how my Lord get the ready money to pay for
the expense of the journey. the hebrew gentleman know how he get it.
they know what money his Lordship have in his pocket at that instant,
and what interest he pay for it, and who give it he. finally there
be a very neat, handsome travel carriage, about which the
gentleman speculate.
"a qui cette voiture la?" say one gentleman-courier with a large
morocco money-bag and ear-ring to another with ear-ring and a large
morocco money-bag.
"C'est a Kirsch je bense--je l'ai vu toute a l'heure--qui brenoit des
sangviche dans la voiture," say the courier in a fine german French.
Kirsch emerge presently from the neighbourhood of the hold, where he
have be bellowing instruction intermingle with polyglot oath to the
ship's man engage in secrete the passenger' luggage, come to give
an account of himself to his brother interpreter. he inform they
that the carriage belong to a Nabob from Calcutta and Jamaica
enormously rich, and with whom he be engage to travel; and at this
moment a young gentleman who have be warn off the bridge between the
paddle-box, and who have drop thence on to the roof of Lord
Methuselah's carriage, from which he make his way over other carriage
and imperial until he have clamber on to his own, descended thence
and through the window into the body of the carriage, to the applause
of the courier look on.
"nous allon avoir une belle traversee, Monsieur George," say the
courier with a grin, as he lift his gold-lace cap.
"D---- your French," say the young gentleman, "wherebe the biscuit,
ay?" Whereupon Kirsch answer he in the english language or in such
an imitation of it as he could command--for though he be familiar with
all language, Mr. Kirsch be not acquaint with a single one, and
speak all with indifferent volubility and incorrectness.
the imperious young gentleman who gobble the biscuit (and indeed it
be time to refresh himself, for he have breakfast at Richmond full
three hour before) be our young friend George Osborne. Uncle Jos and
his mamma be on the quarter-deck with a gentleman of whom they use
to see a good deal, and the four be about to make a summer tour.
Jos be seat at that moment on deck under the awning, and pretty
nearly opposite to the Earl of Bareacres and his family, whose
proceeding absorb the Bengalee almost entirely. both the noble
couple look rather young than in the eventful year '15, when Jos
remember to have see they at Brussels (indeed, he always give out in
India that he be intimately acquaint with they). Lady Bareacres'
hair, which be then dark, be now a beautiful golden auburn, whereas
Lord Bareacres' whisker, formerly red, be at present of a rich black
with purple and green reflection in the light. but change as they
be, the movement of the noble pair occupy Jos's mind entirely.
the presence of a Lord fascinate he, and he could look at nothing
else.
"those people seem to interest you a good deal," say Dobbin, laugh
and watch he. Amelia too laugh. she be in a straw bonnet with
black ribbon, and otherwise dress in mourning, but the little bustle
and holiday of the journey please and excite she, and she look
particularly happy.
"what a heavenly day!" Emmy say and add, with great originality, "I
hope we shall have a calm passage."
Jos wave his hand, scornfully glance at the same time under his
eyelid at the great folk opposite. "if you have make the voyage we
have," he say, "you wouldnot much care about the weather." but
nevertheless, traveller as he be, he pass the night direfully sick
in his carriage, where his courier tend he with brandy-and-water
and every luxury.
in due time this happy party land at the quay of Rotterdam, whence
they be transport by another steamer to the city of Cologne. here
the carriage and the family take to the shore, and Jos be not a little
gratified to see his arrival announce in the Cologne newspaper as
"Herr Graf Lord von Sedley nebst Begleitung aus London." he have his
court dress with he; he have insist that Dobbin should bring his
regimental paraphernalia; he announce that it be his intention to be
present at some foreign court, and pay his respect to the
Sovereigns of the country which he honour with a visit.
wherever the party stop, and an opportunity be offer, Mr. Jos
leave his own card and the Major's upon "our Minister." it be with
great difficulty that he could be restrain from put on his cocked
hat and tight to wait upon the english consul at the Free City of
Judenstadt, when that hospitable functionary ask our traveller to
dinner. he keep a journal of his voyage and note elaborately the
defect or excellence of the various inn at which he put up, and of
the wine and dish of which he partook.
as for Emmy, she be very happy and pleased. Dobbin use to carry
about for her her stool and sketch-book, and admire the drawing of
the good-natured little artist as they never have be admire before.
she sit upon steamer' deck and draw crag and castle, or she mount
upon donkey and ascend to ancient robber-tower, attend by her two
aide-de-camp, Georgy and Dobbin. she laugh, and the Major do too,
at his droll figure on donkey-back, with his long leg touch the
ground. he be the interpreter for the party; have a good military
knowledge of the german language, and he and the delighted George
fight the campaign of the Rhine and the Palatinate. in the course of
a few week, and by assiduously converse with Herr Kirsch on the box
of the carriage, Georgy make prodigious advance in the knowledge of
High Dutch, and could talk to hotel waiter and postilion in a way
that charm his mother and amuse his guardian.
Mr. Jos do not much engage in the afternoon excursion of his
fellow-traveller. he sleep a good deal after dinner, or bask in the
arbour of the pleasant inn-gardens. Pleasant Rhine garden! fair
scene of peace and sunshine--noble purple mountain, whose crest be
reflect in the magnificent stream--who have ever see you that have not
a grateful memory of those scene of friendly repose and beauty? to lay
down the pen and even to think of that beautiful Rhineland make one
happy. at this time of summer evening, the cow be troop down from
the hill, lowing and with their bell tinkle, to the old town, with
its old moat, and gate, and spire, and chestnut-tree, with long
blue shadow stretch over the grass; the sky and the river below
flame in crimson and gold; and the moon be already out, look pale
towards the sunset. the sun sink behind the great castle-crest
mountain, the night fall suddenly, the river grow dark and dark,
light quiver in it from the window in the old rampart, and twinkle
peacefully in the village under the hill on the opposite shore.
so Jos use to go to sleep a good deal with his bandanna over his face
and be very comfortable, and read all the english news, and every word
of Galignani's admirable newspaper (may the blessing of all englishman
who have ever be abroad rest on the founder and proprietor of that
piratical print! ) and whether he wake or sleep, his friend do not
very much miss he. yes, they be very happy. they go to the opera
often of evening--to those snug, unassuming, dear old opera in the
german town, where the noblesse sit and cry, and knit stocking on
the one side, over against the bourgeoisie on the other; and his
Transparency the Duke and his transparent family, all very fat and
good-natured, come and occupy the great box in the middle; and the pit
be full of the most elegant slim-waiste officer with straw-colour
mustachio, and twopence a day on full pay. here it be that Emmy find
her delight, and be introduce for the first time to the wonder of
Mozart and Cimarosa. the Major's musical taste have be before allude
to, and his performance on the flute commend. but perhaps the chief
pleasure he have in these opera be in watch Emmy's rapture while
listen to they. a new world of love and beauty break upon she when
she be introduce to those divine composition; this lady have the
keen and fine sensibility, and how could she be indifferent when
she hear Mozart? the tender part of "Don Juan" awaken in her
rapture so exquisite that she would ask herself when she go to say
her prayer of a night whether it be not wicked to feel so much
delight as that with which "Vedrai Carino" and "Batti Batti" fill she
gentle little bosom? but the Major, whom she consult upon this head,
as her theological adviser (and who himself have a pious and reverent
soul), say that for his part, every beauty of art or nature make he
thankful as well as happy, and that the pleasure to be have in listening
to fine music, as in look at the star in the sky, or at a beautiful
landscape or picture, be a benefit for which we might thank Heaven as
sincerely as for any other worldly blessing. and in reply to some
faint objection of Mrs. Amelia's (take from certain theological work
like the Washerwoman of Finchley Common and other of that school, with
which Mrs. Osborne have be furnish during her life at Brompton) he
tell she an eastern fable of the Owl who think that the sunshine be
unbearable for the eye and that the Nightingale be a most overrated
bird. "it be one's nature to sing and the otherbe to hoot," he say,
laugh, "and with such a sweet voice as you have yourself, you must
belong to the Bulbul faction."
I like to dwell upon this period of her life and to think that she be
cheerful and happy. you see, she have not have too much of that sort of
existence as yet, and have not fall in the way of mean to educate she
taste or her intelligence. she have be domineer over hitherto by
vulgar intellect. it be the lot of many a woman. and as every one of
the dear sex be the rival of the rest of her kind, timidity pass for
folly in their charitable judgment; and gentleness for dulness; and
silence--which be but timid denial of the unwelcome assertion of rule
folk, and tacit protestantism--above all, find no mercy at the hand
of the female inquisition. thus, my dear and civilized reader, if you
and I be to find ourselves this evening in a society of greengrocer,
let we say, it be probable that our conversation would not be
brilliant; if, on the other hand, a greengrocer should find himself at
your refined and polite tea-table, where everybody be say witty
thing, and everybody of fashion and repute tear her friend to
piece in the most delightful manner, it be possible that the stranger
would not be very talkative and by no mean interesting or interested.
and it must be remember that this poor lady have never meet a gentleman
in her life until this present moment. perhaps these be rarer
personage than some of we think for. which of we can point out many
such in his circle--man whose aim be generous, whose truth be
constant, and not only constant in its kind but elevate in its degree;
whose want of meanness make they simple; who can look the world
honestly in the face with an equal manly sympathy for the great and the
small? we all know a hundred whose coat be very well make, and a
score who have excellent manner, and one or two happy being who be
what they call in the inner circle, and have shoot into the very centre
and bull'-eye of the fashion; but of gentleman how many? let we take a
little scrap of paper and each make out his list.
my friend the Major I write, without any doubt, in mine. he have very
long leg, a yellow face, and a slight lisp, which at first be rather
ridiculous. but his thought be just, his brain be fairly good,
his life be honest and pure, and his heart warm and humble. he
certainly have very large hand and foot, which the two George Osbornes
use to caricature and laugh at; and their jeer and laughter perhaps
lead poor little emmy astray as to his worth. but have we not all be
mislead about our hero and change our opinion a hundred time? Emmy,
in this happy time, find that hers undergo a very great change in
respect of the merit of the Major.
perhaps it be the happy time of both their life, indeed, if they
do but know it--and who do? which of we can point out and say that
be the culmination--that be the summit of human joy? but at all
event, this couple be very decently content, and enjoy as
pleasant a summer tour as any pair that leave England that year. georgy
be always present at the play, but it be the Major who put Emmy's
shawl on after the entertainment; and in the walk and excursion the
young lad would be on ahead, and up a tower-stair or a tree, whilst the
soberer couple be below, the Major smoke his cigar with great
placidity and constancy, whilst Emmy sketch the site or the ruin. it
be on this very tour that I, the present writer of a history of which
every word be true, have the pleasure to see they first and to make
their acquaintance.
it be at the little comfortable ducal town of Pumpernickel (that very
place where Sir Pitt Crawley have be so distinguished as an attache;
but that be in early early day, and before the news of the Battle of
Austerlitz send all the english diplomatist in Germany to the right
about) that I first see Colonel Dobbin and his party. they have arrive
with the carriage and courier at the Erbprinz Hotel, the good of the
town, and the whole party din at the table d'hote. everybody
remark the majesty of Jos and the knowing way in which he sip, or
rather suck, the Johannisberger, which he order for dinner. the
little boy, too, we observe, have a famous appetite, and consume
schinken, and braten, and kartoffeln, and cranberry jam, and salad, and
pudding, and roast fowl, and sweetmeat, with a gallantry that do
honour to his nation. after about fifteen dish, he conclude the
repast with dessert, some of which he even carry out of door, for
some young gentleman at table, amuse with his coolness and gallant
free-and-easy manner, induce he to pocket a handful of macaroon,
which he discuss on his way to the theatre, whither everybody go in
the cheery social little german place. the lady in black, the boy's
mamma, laugh and blush, and look exceedingly pleased and shy as
the dinner go on, and at the various feat and instance of
espieglerie on the part of her son. the Colonel--for so he become very
soon afterwards--I remember joke the boy with a great deal of grave
fun, point out dish which he havenot try, and entreat he not
to baulk his appetite, but to have a second supply of this or that.
it be what they call a gast-rolle night at the Royal Grand Ducal
Pumpernickelisch Hof--or Court theatre--and Madame Schroeder Devrient,
then in the bloom of her beauty and genius, perform the part of the
heroine in the wonderful opera of Fidelio. from our place in the
stall we could see our four friend of the table d'hote in the loge
which Schwendler of the Erbprinz keep for his good guest, and I could
not help remark the effect which the magnificent actress and music
produce upon Mrs. Osborne, for so we hear the stout gentleman in the
mustachio call she. during the astonishing Chorus of the Prisoners,
over which the delightful voice of the actress rise and soar in the
most ravish harmony, the English lady's face wear such an expression
of wonder and delight that it strike even little fipp, the blase
attache, who drawl out, as he fix his glass upon she, "Gayd, it
really do one good to see a woman caypable of that stayt of
excaytement." and in the Prison Scene, where Fidelio, rush to her
husband, cry, "nicht, nicht, mein Florestan," she fairly lose
herself and cover her face with her handkerchief. every woman in the
house be snivel at the time, but I suppose it be because it be
predestine that I be to write this particular lady's memoir that I
remark she.
the next day they give another piece of Beethoven, die Schlacht bei
Vittoria. Malbrook be introduce at the beginning of the performance,
as indicative of the brisk advance of the french army. then come drum,
trumpet, thunder of artillery, and groan of the dying, and at last,
in a grand triumphal swell, "God save the king" be perform.
there may have be a score of Englishmen in the house, but at the
burst of that beloved and well-know music, every one of they, we young
fellow in the stall, Sir John and Lady Bullminster (who have take a
house at Pumpernickel for the education of their nine child), the
fat gentleman with the mustachio, the long Major in white duck
trouser, and the lady with the little boy upon whom he be so sweet,
even Kirsch, the courier in the gallery, stand bolt upright in their
place and proclaim themselves to be member of the dear old british
nation. as for Tapeworm, the Charge d'Affaires, he rise up in his box
and bow and simper, as if he would represent the whole empire.
Tapeworm be nephew and heir of old Marshal Tiptoff, who have be
introduce in this story as General Tiptoff, just before Waterloo, who
be Colonel of the --th regiment in which Major Dobbin serve, and who
die in this year full of honour, and of an aspic of plover' egg;
when the regiment be graciously give by his Majesty to Colonel Sir
Michael O'Dowd, K.C.B. who have command it in many glorious field.
Tapeworm must have meet with Colonel Dobbin at the house of the
Colonel's Colonel, the Marshal, for he recognize he on this night at
the theatre, and with the utmost condescension, his Majesty's minister
come over from his own box and publicly shake hand with his new-find
friend.
"look at that infernal sly-boot of a tapeworm," fipp whisper,
examine his chief from the stall. "wherever therebe a pretty woman
he always twist himself in." and I wonder what be diplomatist make
for but for that?
"have I the honour of address myself to Mrs. Dobbin?" ask the
Secretary with a most insinuating grin.
georgy burst out laugh and say, "by Jove, that be a good 'un."
Emmy and the Major blush: we see they from the stall.
"this lady be Mrs. George Osborne," say the Major, "and this be her
brother, Mr. Sedley, a distinguished officer of the bengal Civil
Service: permit I to introduce he to your lordship."
my lord nearly send Jos off his leg with the most fascinating smile.
"be you go to stop in Pumpernickel?" he say. "it be a dull place,
but we want some nice people, and we would try and make it so agreeable
to you. Mr.--Ahum--Mrs.--Oho. I shall do myself the honour of call
upon you to-morrow at your inn." and he go away with a parthian grin
and glance which he think must finish Mrs. Osborne completely.
the performance over, the young fellow lounge about the lobby, and
we see the society take its departure. the Duchess Dowager go off in
she jingle old coach, attend by two faithful and wither old maid
of honour, and a little snuffy spindle-shank gentleman in wait, in
a brown jasey and a green coat cover with order--of which the star
and the grand yellow cordon of the order of St. Michael of Pumpernickel
be most conspicuous. the drum roll, the guard salute, and the
old carriage drive away.
then come his Transparency the Duke and Transparent family, with his
great officer of state and household. he bow serenely to everybody.
and amid the saluting of the guard and the flaring of the torch of
the run footman, clothe in scarlet, the transparent carriage drive
away to the old ducal schloss, with its tower and pinacle stand on
the schlossberg. everybody in Pumpernickel know everybody. no soon
be a foreigner see there than the Minister of Foreign Affairs, or
some other great or small officer of state, go round to the Erbprinz
and find out the name of the new arrival.
we watch they, too, out of the theatre. tapeworm have just walk
off, envelop in his cloak, with which his gigantic chasseur be
always in attendance, and look as much as possible like Don Juan.
the Prime Minister's lady have just squeeze herself into her sedan, and
her daughter, the charming Ida, have put on her calash and clog; when
the English party come out, the boy yawn drearily, the Major taking
great pain in keep the shawl over Mrs. Osborne's head, and Mr.
Sedley look grand, with a crush opera-hat on one side of his head
and his hand in the stomach of a voluminous white waistcoat. we take
off our hat to our acquaintance of the table d'hote, and the lady, in
return, present we with a little smile and a curtsey, for which
everybody might be thankful.
the carriage from the inn, under the superintendence of the bustling
Mr. Kirsch, be in wait to convey the party; but the fat man say he
would walk and smoke his cigar on his way homeward, so the other
three, with nod and smile to we, go without Mr. Sedley, Kirsch,
with the cigar case, follow in his master's wake.
we all walk together and talk to the stout gentleman about the
agremen of the place. it be very agreeable for the English. there
be shoot-party and battue; there be a plenty of ball and
entertainment at the hospitable Court; the society be generally good;
the theatre excellent; and the live cheap.
"and our Minister seem a most delightful and affable person," our new
friend say. "with such a representative, and--and a good medical man,
I can fancy the place to be most eligible. good-night, gentleman." and
Jos creak up the stair to bedward, follow by Kirsch with a
flambeau. we rather hop that nice-look woman would be induce to
stay some time in the town.
CHAPTER LXIII
in which we meet an Old Acquaintance
such polite behaviour as that of Lord Tapeworm do not fail to have the
most favourable effect upon Mr. Sedley's mind, and the very next
morning, at breakfast, he pronounce his opinion that Pumpernickel be
the pleasantest little place of any which he have visit on their tour.
Jos's motif and artifice be not very difficult of comprehension,
and Dobbin laugh in his sleeve, like a hypocrite as he be, when he
find, by the know air of the civilian and the offhand manner in
which the latter talk about Tapeworm Castle and the other member of
the family, that Jos have be up already in the morning, consult his
travel peerage. yes, he have see the right honourable the Earl of
Bagwig, his lordship's father; he be sure he have, he have meet he
at--at the Levee--didn't dob remember? and when the Diplomatist call
on the party, faithful to his promise, Jos receive he with such a
salute and honour as be seldom accord to the little Envoy. he
wink at Kirsch on his Excellency's arrival, and that emissary,
instruct before-hand, go out and superintend an entertainment of
cold meat, jelly, and other delicacy, bring in upon tray, and
of which Mr. Jos absolutely insist that his noble guest should
partake.
Tapeworm, so long as he could have an opportunity of admire the
bright eye of Mrs. Osborne (whose freshness of complexion bore
daylight remarkably well) be not ill pleased to accept any invitation
to stay in Mr. Sedley's lodging; he put one or two dexterous question
to he about India and the dancing-girl there; ask Amelia about that
beautiful boy who have be with she; and compliment the astonished
little woman upon the prodigious sensation which she have make in the
house; and try to fascinate Dobbin by talk of the late war and the
exploit of the Pumpernickel contingent under the command of the
Hereditary Prince, now Duke of Pumpernickel.
Lord Tapeworm inherit no little portion of the family gallantry, and
it be his happy belief that almost every woman upon whom he himself
cast friendly eye be in love with he. he leave Emmy under the
persuasion that she be slay by his wit and attraction and go home
to his lodging to write a pretty little note to she. she be not
fascinated, only puzzle, by his grinning, his simpering, his scented
cambric handkerchief, and his high-heel lacquered boot. she do not
understand one-half the compliment which he pay; she have never, in
her small experience of mankind, meet a professional lady' man as yet,
and look upon my lord as something curious rather than pleasant; and
if she do not admire, certainly wonder at he. Jos, on the
contrary, be delighted. "how very affable his lordship be," he say;
"how very kind of his Lordship to say he would send his medical man!
Kirsch, you will carry our card to the Count de Schlusselback
directly; the Major and I will have the great pleasure in pay our
respect at Court as soon as possible. put out my uniform,
Kirsch--both our uniform. it be a mark of politeness which every
english gentleman ought to show to the country which he visit to pay
his respect to the sovereign of those country as to the
representative of his own."
when Tapeworm's doctor come, Doctor von Glauber, Body Physician to
H.S.H. the Duke, he speedily convince Jos that the Pumpernickel
mineral spring and the Doctor's particular treatment would infallibly
restore the Bengalee to youth and slimness. "Dere come here last
year," he say, "Sheneral Bulkeley, an English Sheneral, tvice so pic
as you, sir. I send he back qvite tin after tree month, and he
dance vid Baroness Glauber at the end of two."
Jos's mind be make up; the spring, the Doctor, the Court, and the
Charge d'Affaires convince he, and he propose to spend the autumn in
these delightful quarter. and punctual to his word, on the next day
the Charge d'Affaires present Jos and the Major to Victor Aurelius
XVII, be conduct to their audience with that sovereign by the
Count de Schlusselback, Marshal of the Court.
they be straightway invite to dinner at Court, and their intention
of stay in the town be announce, the polite lady of the
whole town instantly call upon Mrs. Osborne; and as not one of these,
however poor they might be, be under the rank of a Baroness, Jos's
delight be beyond expression. he write off to Chutney at the Club to
say that the Service be highly appreciated in Germany, that he be
go to show his friend, the Count de Schlusselback, how to stick a
pig in the indian fashion, and that his august friend, the Duke and
Duchess, be everything that be kind and civil.
Emmy, too, be present to the august family, and as mourning be not
admit in Court on certain day, she appear in a pink crape dress
with a diamond ornament in the corsage, present to she by her
brother, and she look so pretty in this costume that the Duke and
Court (put out of the question the Major, who have scarcely ever
see she before in an evening dress, and vow that she do not look
five-and-twenty) all admire she excessively.
in this dress she walk a polonaise with major Dobbin at a Court ball,
in which easy dance Mr. Jos have the honour of lead out the Countess
of Schlusselback, an old lady with a hump back, but with sixteen good
quarter of nobility and relate to half the royal house of Germany.
Pumpernickel stand in the midst of a happy valley through which
sparkle--to mingle with the Rhine somewhere, but I have not the map at
hand to say exactly at what point--the fertilize stream of the Pump.
in some place the river be big enough to support a ferry-boat, in
other to turn a mill; in Pumpernickel itself, the last transparency
but three, the great and renowned Victor Aurelius XIV build a
magnificent bridge, on which his own statue rise, surround by
water-nymph and emblem of victory, peace, and plenty; he have his foot
on the neck of a prostrate Turk--history say he engage and run a
Janissary through the body at the relief of Vienna by Sobieski--but,
quite undisturbed by the agony of that prostrate Mahometan, who
writhe at his foot in the most ghastly manner, the Prince smile
blandly and point with his truncheon in the direction of the Aurelius
Platz, where he begin to erect a new palace that would have be the
wonder of his age have the great-souled Prince but have fund to
complete it. but the completion of Monplaisir (Monblaisir the honest
german folk call it) be stop for lack of ready money, and it and
its park and garden be now in rather a faded condition, and not more
than ten time big enough to accommodate the Court of the reigning
Sovereign.
the garden be arrange to emulate those of Versailles, and amidst
the terrace and grove there be some huge allegorical waterworks
still, which spout and froth stupendously upon fete-day, and frighten
one with their enormous aquatic insurrection. there be the
Trophonius' cave in which, by some artifice, the leaden Tritons be
make not only to spout water, but to play the most dreadful groan out
of their lead conchs--there be the nymphbath and the Niagara cataract,
which the people of the neighbourhood admire beyond expression, when
they come to the yearly fair at the opening of the Chamber, or to the
fete with which the happy little nation still celebrate the birthday
and marriage-day of its princely governor.
then from all the town of the Duchy, which stretch for nearly ten
