mile--from Bolkum, which lie on its western frontier bidding defiance
to Prussia, from Grogwitz, where the Prince have a hunting-lodge, and
where his dominion be separate by the Pump River from those of the
neighbour Prince of Potzenthal; from all the little village, which
besides these three great city, dot over the happy principality--from
the farm and the mill along the Pump come troop of people in red
petticoat and velvet head-dress, or with three-cornered hat and
pipe in their mouth, who flock to the Residenz and share in the
pleasure of the fair and the festivity there. then the theatre be
open for nothing, then the water of Monblaisir begin to play (it be
lucky that there be company to behold they, for one would be afraid to
see they alone)--then there come mountebank and rid troop (the way
in which his Transparency be fascinate by one of the horse-rider be
well know, and it be believe that La Petite Vivandiere, as she be
call, be a spy in the french interest), and the delighted people be
permit to march through room after room of the Grand Ducal palace
and admire the slippery floor, the rich hanging, and the spittoon at
the door of all the innumerable chamber. there be one pavilion at
Monblaisir which Aurelius Victor XV have arrange--a great Prince but
too fond of pleasure--and which I be tell be a perfect wonder of
licentious elegance. it be paint with the story of Bacchus and
Ariadne, and the table work in and out of the room by mean of a
windlass, so that the company be serve without any intervention of
domestic. but the place be shut up by Barbara, Aurelius XV's widow,
a severe and devout Princess of the House of Bolkum and Regent of the
Duchy during her son's glorious minority, and after the death of her
husband, cut off in the pride of his pleasure.
the theatre of Pumpernickel be know and famous in that quarter of
Germany. it languish a little when the present Duke in his youth
insist upon have his own opera play there, and it be say one
day, in a fury, from his place in the orchestra, when he attend a
rehearsal, break a bassoon on the head of the Chapel Master, who be
conduct, and lead too slow; and during which time the Duchess Sophia
write domestic comedy, which must have be very dreary to witness.
but the Prince execute his music in private now, and the Duchess only
give away her play to the foreigner of distinction who visit she
kind little Court.
it be conduct with no small comfort and splendour. when there be
ball, though there may be four hundred people at supper, there be a
servant in scarlet and lace to attend upon every four, and every one be
serve on silver. there be festival and entertainment go
continually on, and the Duke have his chamberlain and equerry, and
the Duchess her mistress of the wardrobe and lady of honour, just
like any other and more potent potentate.
the Constitution be or be a moderate despotism, temper by a Chamber
that might or might not be elect. I never certainly could hear of
its sitting in my time at Pumpernickel. the Prime Minister have
lodging in a second floor, and the Foreign Secretary occupy the
comfortable lodging over Zwieback's Conditorey. the army consist of
a magnificent band that also do duty on the stage, where it be quite
pleasant to see the worthy fellow march in turkish dress with
rouge on and wooden scimitar, or as roman warrior with ophicleide
and trombone--to see they again, I say, at night, after one have
listen to they all the morning in the Aurelius Platz, where they
perform opposite the cafe where we breakfast. besides the band,
there be a rich and numerous staff of officer, and, I believe, a few
man. besides the regular sentry, three or four man, habit as
hussar, use to do duty at the Palace, but I never see they on
horseback, and au fait, what be the use of cavalry in a time of
profound peace?--and whither the deuce should the hussar ride?
everybody--everybody that be noble of course, for as for the bourgeois
we could not quite be expect to take notice of THEM--visit his
neighbour. H. E. Madame de Burst receive once a week, H. E. Madame de
Schnurrbart have her night--the theatre be open twice a week, the Court
graciously receive once, so that a man's life might in fact be a
perfect round of pleasure in the unpretending Pumpernickel way.
that there be feud in the place, no one can deny. politic run very
high at Pumpernickel, and party be very bitter. there be the
Strumpff faction and the Lederlung party, the one support by our
envoy and the other by the French Charge d'Affaires, M. de Macabau.
indeed it suffice for our Minister to stand up for Madame Strumpff,
who be clearly the great singer of the two, and have three more note
in her voice than Madame Lederlung her rival--it suffice, I say, for
our Minister to advance any opinion to have it instantly contradict
by the french diplomatist.
everybody in the town be range in one or other of these faction. the
Lederlung be a prettyish little creature certainly, and her voice
(what there be of it) be very sweet, and there be no doubt that the
Strumpff be not in her first youth and beauty, and certainly too
stout; when she come on in the last scene of the Sonnambula, for
instance, in her night-chemise with a lamp in her hand, and have to go
out of the window, and pass over the plank of the mill, it be all she
could do to squeeze out of the window, and the plank use to bend and
creak again under her weight--but how she pour out the finale of the
opera! and with what a burst of feeling she rush into Elvino's
arm--almost fit to smother he! whereas the little Lederlung--but a
truce to this gossip--the fact be that these two woman be the two
flag of the French and the English party at Pumpernickel, and the
society be divide in its allegiance to those two great nation.
we have on our side the Home Minister, the Master of the Horse, the
Duke's private Secretary, and the Prince's tutor; whereas of the French
party be the Foreign Minister, the Commander-in-Chief's Lady, who have
serve under Napoleon, and the Hof-Marschall and his wife, who be glad
enough to get the fashion from Pans, and always have they and her cap
by M. de Macabau's courier. the Secretary of his Chancery be little
Grignac, a young fellow, as malicious as Satan, and who make
caricature of Tapeworm in all the-album of the place.
their headquarters and table d'hote be establish at the Pariser
Hof, the other inn of the town; and though, of course, these gentleman
be oblige to be civil in public, yet they cut at each other with
epigram that be as sharp as razor, as I have see a couple of
wrestler in Devonshire, lash at each other's shin and never
show their agony upon a muscle of their face. neither Tapeworm nor
Macabau ever send home a dispatch to his government without a most
savage series of attack upon his rival. for instance, on our side we
would write, "the interest of Great Britain in this place, and
throughout the whole of Germany, be perille by the continuance in
office of the present french envoy; this man be of a character so
infamous that he will stick at no falsehood, or hesitate at no crime,
to attain his end. he poison the mind of the Court against the
English minister, represent the conduct of Great Britain in the most
odious and atrocious light, and be unhappily back by a minister whose
ignorance and necessity be as notorious as his influence be fatal."
on their side they would say, "M. de Tapeworm continue his system of
stupid insular arrogance and vulgar falsehood against the great
nation in the world. yesterday he be hear to speak lightly of her
Royal Highness Madame the Duchess of Berri; on a former occasion he
insult the heroic Duke of Angouleme and dare to insinuate that
H.R.H. the Duke of Orleans be conspire against the august throne of
the lily. his gold be prodigate in every direction which his stupid
menace fail to frighten. by one and the other, he have win over
creature of the Court here--and, in fine, Pumpernickel will not be
quiet, Germany tranquil, France respect, or Europe content until this
poisonous viper be crush under heel": and so on. when one side or
the other have write any particularly spicy dispatch, news of it be
sure to slip out.
before the winter be far advanced, it be actually on record that Emmy
take a night and receive company with great propriety and modesty.
she have a french master, who compliment she upon the purity of her
accent and her facility of learning; the fact be she have learn long
ago and ground herself subsequently in the grammar so as to be able
to teach it to George; and Madam Strumpff come to give her lesson in
singing, which she perform so well and with such a true voice that
the Major's window, who have lodging opposite under the Prime
Minister, be always open to hear the lesson. some of the german
lady, who be very sentimental and simple in their taste, fall in
love with she and begin to call she du at once. these be trivial
detail, but they relate to happy time. the Major make himself
George's tutor and read Caesar and mathematic with he, and they have a
german master and ride out of evening by the side of Emmy's
carriage--she be always too timid, and make a dreadful outcry at the
slight disturbance on horse-back. so she drive about with one of
her dear german friend, and Jos asleep on the back-seat of the
barouche.
he be become very sweet upon the Grafinn Fanny de Butterbrod, a very
gentle tender-hearted and unassuming young creature, a canoness and
Countess in her own right, but with scarcely ten pound per year to her
fortune, and Fanny for her part declare that to be Amelia's sister be
the great delight that Heaven could bestow on she, and Jos might
have put a Countess's shield and coronet by the side of his own arm on
his carriage and fork; when--when event occur, and those grand
fete give upon the marriage of the Hereditary Prince of Pumpernickel
with the lovely Princess Amelia of Humbourg-Schlippenschloppen take
place.
at this festival the magnificence display be such as have not be
know in the little german place since the day of the prodigal Victor
XIV. all the neighbour Princes, Princesses, and Grandees be
invite to the feast. bed rise to half a crown per night in
Pumpernickel, and the Army be exhaust in provide guard of honour
for the Highnesses, serenity, and excellency who arrive from all
quarter. the Princess be marry by proxy, at her father's
residence, by the Count de Schlusselback. snuff-box be give away
in profusion (as we learn from the Court jeweller, who sell and
afterwards buy they again), and bushel of the Order of Saint
Michael of Pumpernickel be send to the noble of the Court, while
hamper of the cordon and decoration of the Wheel of St. Catherine of
Schlippenschloppen be bring to ours. the french envoy get both.
"he be cover with ribbon like a prize cart-horse," Tapeworm say,
who be not allow by the rule of his service to take any
decoration: "let he have the cordon; but with whom be the victory?"
the fact be, it be a triumph of british diplomacy, the french party
have propose and try their utmost to carry a marriage with a
Princess of the House of Potztausend-Donnerwetter, whom, as a matter
of course, we oppose.
everybody be ask to the fete of the marriage. garland and
triumphal arch be hang across the road to welcome the young bride.
the great Saint Michael's Fountain run with uncommonly sour wine, while
that in the Artillery Place froth with beer. the great water
play; and pole be put up in the park and garden for the happy
peasantry, which they might climb at their leisure, carry off
watch, silver fork, prize sausage hang with pink ribbon, &c., at
the top. georgy get one, wrench it off, have swarm up the pole
to the delight of the spectator, and slide down with the rapidity of
a fall of water. but it be for the glory's sake merely. the boy give
the sausage to a peasant, who have very nearly seize it, and stand at
the foot of the mast, blubber, because he be unsuccessful.
at the french Chancellerie they have six more lampion in their
illumination than our have; but our transparency, which represent the
young Couple advance and Discord fly away, with the most ludicrous
likeness to the french Ambassador, beat the french picture hollow; and
I have no doubt get Tapeworm the advancement and the Cross of the Bath
which he subsequently attain.
crowd of foreigner arrive for the fete, and of English, of course.
besides the Court ball, public ball be give at the Town Hall and
the Redoute, and in the former place there be a room for
trente-et-quarante and roulette establish, for the week of the
festivity only, and by one of the great german company from Ems or
Aix-la-Chapelle. the officer or inhabitant of the town be not
allow to play at these game, but stranger, peasant, lady be
admit, and any one who choose to lose or win money.
that little scapegrace Georgy Osborne amongst other, whose pocket
be always full of dollar and whose relation be away at the grand
festival of the Court, come to the Stadthaus Ball in company of his
uncle's courier, Mr. Kirsch, and have only peep into a play-room at
Baden-Baden when he hang on Dobbin's arm, and where, of course, he be
not permit to gamble, come eagerly to this part of the entertainment
and hanker round the table where the croupier and the punter be
at work. woman be play; they be mask, some of they; this
license be allow in these wild time of carnival.
a woman with light hair, in a low dress by no mean so fresh as it have
be, and with a black mask on, through the eyelet of which her eye
twinkle strangely, be seat at one of the roulette-table with a
card and a pin and a couple of florin before she. as the croupier
call out the colour and number, she prick on the card with great
care and regularity, and only venture her money on the colour after
the red or black have come up a certain number of time. it be strange
to look at she.
but in spite of her care and assiduity she guess wrong and the last
two florin follow each other under the croupier's rake, as he cry
out with his inexorable voice the win colour and number. she give
a sigh, a shrug with her shoulder, which be already too much out of
her gown, and dash the pin through the card on to the table, sit
thrum it for a while. then she look round she and see Georgy's
honest face star at the scene. the little scamp! what business have
he to be there?
when she see the boy, at whose face she look hard through her shin
eye and mask, she say, "Monsieur n'est pas joueur?"
"Non, Madame," say the boy; but she must have know, from his accent,
of what country he be, for she answer he with a slight foreign
tone. "you have nevare play--will you do I a littl' favor?"
"what be it?" say georgy, blush again. Mr. Kirsch be at work for
his part at the rouge et noir and do not see his young master.
"play this for I, if you please; put it on any number, any number."
and she take from her bosom a purse, and out of it a gold piece, the
only coin there, and she put it into George's hand. the boy laugh
and do as he be bid.
the number come up sure enough. there be a power that arrange that,
they say, for beginner.
"thank you," say she, pull the money towards she, "thank you. what
be your name?"
"my name's Osborne," say Georgy, and be finger in his own pocket
for dollar, and just about to make a trial, when the Major, in his
uniform, and Jos, en Marquis, from the Court ball, make their
appearance. other people, find the entertainment stupid and
prefer the fun at the Stadthaus, have quit the Palace ball
early; but it be probable the Major and Jos have go home and find
the boy's absence, for the former instantly go up to he and, take
he by the shoulder, pull he briskly back from the place of
temptation. then, look round the room, he see Kirsch employ as we
have say, and go up to he, ask how he dare to bring Mr. George
to such a place.
"laissez-moi tranquille," say Mr. Kirsch, very much excited by play
and wine. "Il faut s'amuser, parbleu. Je ne suis pas au service de
Monsieur."
see his condition the Major do not choose to argue with the man,
but content himself with draw away George and ask Jos if he
would come away. he be stand close by the lady in the mask, who
be play with pretty good luck now, and look on much interested
at the game.
"hadnot you well come, Jos," the Major say, "with George and I?"
"Iwill stop and go home with that rascal, Kirsch," Jos say; and for the
same reason of modesty, which he think ought to be preserve before
the boy, Dobbin do not care to remonstrate with Jos, but leave he and
walk home with Georgy.
"do you play?" ask the Major when they be out and on their way
home.
the boy say "no."
"give I your word of honour as a gentleman that you never will."
"why?" say the boy; "it seem very good fun." and, in a very eloquent
and impressive manner, the Major show he why he shouldnot, and would
have enforce his precept by the example of Georgy's own father, have
he like to say anything that should reflect on the other's memory.
when he have house he, he go to bed and see his light, in the little
room outside of Amelia's, presently disappear. Amelia's follow half
an hour afterwards. I donot know what make the major note it so
accurately.
Jos, however, remain behind over the play-table; he be no gambler,
but not averse to the little excitement of the sport now and then, and
he have some napoleon chink in the embroider pocket of his court
waistcoat. he put down one over the fair shoulder of the little
gambler before he, and they win. she make a little movement to make
room for he by her side, and just take the skirt of her gown from a
vacant chair there.
"come and give I good luck," she say, still in a foreign accent,
quite different from that frank and perfectly english "thank you," with
which she have salute Georgy's coup in her favour. the portly
gentleman, look round to see that nobody of rank observe he, sit
down; he muttered--"ah, really, well now, God bless my soul. Ibe very
fortunate; Ibe sure to give you good fortune," and other word of
compliment and confusion. "do you play much?" the foreign mask say.
"I put a Nap or two down," say Jos with a superb air, fling down a
gold piece.
"yes; ay nap after dinner," say the mask archly. but Jos look
frighten, she continue, in her pretty french accent, "you do not
play to win. no more do I. I play to forget, but I cannot. I cannot
forget old time, monsieur. your little nephew be the image of his
father; and you--you be not change--but yes, you be. everybody
change, everybody forget; nobody have any heart."
"Good God, who be it?" ask Jos in a flutter.
"cannot you guess, Joseph Sedley?" say the little woman in a sad voice,
and undo her mask, she look at he. "you have forget I."
"good heaven! Mrs. Crawley!" gasp out Jos.
"rebecca," say the other, put her hand on his; but she follow
the game still, all the time she be look at he.
"I be stop at the Elephant," she continue. "ask for Madame de
Raudon. I see my dear Amelia to-day; how pretty she look, and how
happy! so do you! everybody but I, who be wretche, Joseph Sedley."
and she put her money over from the red to the black, as if by a chance
movement of her hand, and while she be wipe her eye with a
pocket-handkerchief fringe with tear lace.
the red come up again, and she lose the whole of that stake. "come
away," she say. "come with I a little--we be old friend, be we
not, dear Mr. Sedley?"
and Mr. Kirsch have lose all his money by this time, follow his
master out into the moonlight, where the illumination be wink out
and the transparency over our mission be scarcely visible.
CHAPTER LXIV
A Vagabond chapter
we must pass over a part of Mrs. Rebecca Crawley's biography with that
lightness and delicacy which the world demand--the moral world, that
have, perhaps, no particular objection to vice, but an insuperable
repugnance to hear vice call by its proper name. there be thing
we do and know perfectly well in Vanity Fair, though we never speak of
they: as the Ahrimanians worship the devil, but donot mention he:
and a polite public will no more bear to read an authentic description
of vice than a truly refined English or american female will permit the
word breeche to be pronounce in her chaste hearing. and yet, madam,
both be walk the world before our face every day, without much
shock we. if you be to blush every time they go by, what
complexion you would have! it be only when their naughty name be
call out that your modesty have any occasion to show alarm or sense of
outrage, and it have be the wish of the present writer, all through
this story, deferentially to submit to the fashion at present
prevailing, and only to hint at the existence of wickedness in a light,
easy, and agreeable manner, so that nobody's fine feeling may be
offend. I defy any one to say that our Becky, who have certainly some
vice, have not be present to the public in a perfectly genteel and
inoffensive manner. in describe this Siren, sing and smile,
coax and cajoling, the author, with modest pride, ask his reader
all round, have he once forget the law of politeness, and show the
monster's hideous tail above water? no! those who like may peep down
under wave that be pretty transparent and see it writhe and
twirling, diabolically hideous and slimy, flap amongst bone, or
curl round corps; but above the waterline, I ask, have not
everything be proper, agreeable, and decorous, and have any the most
squeamish immoralist in Vanity Fair a right to cry fie? when, however,
the Siren disappear and dive below, down among the dead man, the
water of course grow turbid over she, and it be labour lose to look
into it ever so curiously. they look pretty enough when they sit upon
a rock, twang their harp and comb their hair, and sing, and
beckon to you to come and hold the look-glass; but when they sink
into their native element, depend on it, those mermaid be about no
good, and we have well not examine the fiendish marine cannibal,
revel and feast on their wretched pickle victim. and so, when
Becky be out of the way, be sure that she be not particularly well
employ, and that the less that be say about her doing be in fact
the well.
if we be to give a full account of her proceeding during a couple of
year that follow after the Curzon Street catastrophe, there might be
some reason for people to say this book be improper. the action of
very vain, heartless, pleasure-seek people be very often improper
(as be many of your, my friend with the grave face and spotless
reputation--but that be merely by the way); and what be those of a
woman without faith--or love--or character? and I be inclined to think
that there be a period in Mrs Becky's life when she be seize, not by
remorse, but by a kind of despair, and absolutely neglect her person
and do not even care for her reputation.
this abattement and degradation do not take place all at once; it be
bring about by degree, after her calamity, and after many struggle
to keep up--as a man who go overboard hang on to a spar whilst any
hope be leave, and then fling it away and go down, when he find that
struggle be in vain.
she linger about London whilst her husband be make preparation
for his departure to his seat of government, and it be believe make
more than one attempt to see her brother-in-law, Sir Pitt Crawley, and
to work upon his feeling, which she have almost enlist in her favour.
as Sir Pitt and Mr. Wenham be walk down to the House of Commons,
the latter spy Mrs. Rawdon in a black veil, and lurk near the
palace of the legislature. she sneak away when her eye meet those of
Wenham, and indeed never succeed in her design upon the Baronet.
probably Lady Jane interpose. I have hear that she quite astonish
her husband by the spirit which she exhibit in this quarrel, and her
determination to disown Mrs. Becky. of her own movement, she invite
Rawdon to come and stop in Gaunt Street until his departure for
Coventry Island, know that with he for a guard Mrs. Becky would not
try to force her door; and she look curiously at the superscription
of all the letter which arrive for Sir Pitt, lest he and his
sister-in-law should be correspond. not but that Rebecca could have
write have she a mind, but she do not try to see or to write to Pitt
at his own house, and after one or two attempt consent to his demand
that the correspondence regard her conjugal difference should be
carry on by lawyer only.
the fact be that Pitt's mind have be poison against she. a short
time after Lord Steyne's accident Wenham have be with the Baronet and
give he such a biography of Mrs. Becky as have astonish the member
for Queen's Crawley. he know everything regard she: who her father
be; in what year her mother dance at the opera; what have be her
previous history; and what her conduct during her married life--as I
have no doubt that the great part of the story be false and dictate
by interested malevolence, it shall not be repeat here. but Becky
be leave with a sad sad reputation in the esteem of a country gentleman
and relative who have be once rather partial to she.
the revenue of the Governor of Coventry Island be not large. a part
of they be set aside by his Excellency for the payment of certain
outstanding debt and liability, the charge incident on his high
situation require considerable expense; finally, it be find that he
could not spare to his wife more than three hundred pound a year,
which he propose to pay to she on an undertaking that she would never
trouble he. otherwise, scandal, separation, doctor' Commons would
ensue. but it be Mr. Wenham's business, Lord Steyne's business,
Rawdon's, everybody's--to get she out of the country, and hush up a
most disagreeable affair.
she be probably so much occupy in arrange these affair of
business with her husband's lawyer that she forget to take any step
whatever about her son, the little Rawdon, and do not even once
propose to go and see he. that young gentleman be consign to the
entire guardianship of his aunt and uncle, the former of whom have
always possess a great share of the child's affection. his mamma
write he a neat letter from Boulogne, when she quit England, in
which she request he to mind his book, and say she be go to
take a Continental tour, during which she would have the pleasure of
write to he again. but she never do for a year afterwards, and
not, indeed, until Sir Pitt's only boy, always sickly, die of
hooping-cough and measle--then Rawdon's mamma write the most
affectionate composition to her darling son, who be make heir of
Queen's Crawley by this accident, and draw more closely than ever to
the kind lady, whose tender heart have already adopt he. Rawdon
Crawley, then grow a tall, fine lad, blush when he get the letter.
"oh, Aunt Jane, you be my mother!" he say; "and not--and not that
one." but he write back a kind and respectful letter to Mrs. Rebecca,
then live at a boarding-house at Florence. but we be advance
matter.
our darling Becky's first flight be not very far. she perch upon
the french coast at Boulogne, that refuge of so much exiled English
innocence, and there live in rather a genteel, widow manner, with a
femme de chambre and a couple of room, at an hotel. she din at the
table d'hote, where people think her very pleasant, and where she
entertain her neighbour by story of her brother, Sir Pitt, and her
great London acquaintance, talk that easy, fashionable slip-slop
which have so much effect upon certain folk of small breeding. she
pass with many of they for a person of importance; she give little
tea-party in her private room and share in the innocent amusement
of the place in sea-bathing, and in jaunt in open carriage, in
stroll on the sand, and in visit to the play. Mrs. Burjoice, the
printer's lady, who be board with her family at the hotel for the
summer, and to whom her Burjoice come of a Saturday and Sunday, vote
her charming, until that little rogue of a Burjoice begin to pay she
too much attention. but there be nothing in the story, only that
Becky be always affable, easy, and good-natured--and with man
especially.
number of people be go abroad as usual at the end of the season,
and Becky have plenty of opportunity of find out by the behaviour
of her acquaintance of the great London world the opinion of "society"
as regard her conduct. one day it be Lady Partlet and her daughter
whom Becky confront as she be walk modestly on Boulogne pier, the
cliff of Albion shin in the distance across the deep blue sea.
Lady Partlet marshal all her daughter round she with a sweep of her
parasol and retreat from the pier, dart savage glance at poor
little Becky who stand alone there.
on another day the packet come in. it have be blow fresh, and it
always suit Becky's humour to see the droll woe-begone face of the
people as they emerge from the boat. Lady Slingstone happen to be
on board this day. her ladyship have be exceedingly ill in her
carriage, and be greatly exhausted and scarcely fit to walk up the
plank from the ship to the pier. but all her energy rally the
instant she see Becky smile roguishly under a pink bonnet, and give
she a glance of scorn such as would have shrivel up most woman, she
walk into the Custom House quite unsupported. Becky only laugh:
but I donot think she like it. she feel she be alone, quite alone,
and the far-off shin cliff of England be impassable to she.
the behaviour of the man have undergo too I donot know what change.
Grinstone show his tooth and laugh in her face with a familiarity
that be not pleasant. little Bob Suckling, who be cap in hand to she
three month before, and would walk a mile in the rain to see for her
carriage in the line at Gaunt House, be talk to Fitzoof of the
Guards (Lord Heehaw's son) one day upon the jetty, as Becky take she
walk there. little Bobby nod to she over his shoulder, without
move his hat, and continue his conversation with the heir of Heehaw.
Tom Raikes try to walk into her sitting-room at the inn with a cigar
in his mouth, but she close the door upon he, and would have lock
it, only that his finger be inside. she begin to feel that she be
very lonely indeed. "if HE'D be here," she say, "those coward
would never have dare to insult I." she think about "he" with
great sadness and perhaps long--about his honest, stupid, constant
kindness and fidelity; his never-cease obedience; his good humour;
his bravery and courage. very likely she cry, for she be
particularly lively, and have put on a little extra rouge, when she come
down to dinner.
she rouge regularly now; and--and her maid get Cognac for she besides
that which be charge in the hotel bill.
perhaps the insult of the man be not, however, so intolerable to she
as the sympathy of certain woman. Mrs. Crackenbury and Mrs. Washington
White pass through Boulogne on their way to Switzerland. the party
be protect by Colonel Horner, young Beaumoris, and of course old
Crackenbury, and Mrs. White's little girl. they do not avoid she.
they giggle, cackle, tattle, condole, console, and patronize she
until they drive she almost wild with rage. to be patronize by they!
she think, as they go away simper after kiss she. and she
hear Beaumoris's laugh ring on the stair and know quite well how to
interpret his hilarity.
it be after this visit that Becky, who have pay her weekly bill,
Becky who have make herself agreeable to everybody in the house, who
smile at the landlady, call the waiter "monsieur," and pay the
chambermaid in politeness and apology, what far more than
compensate for a little niggardliness in point of money (of which
Becky never be free), that Becky, we say, receive a notice to quit
from the landlord, who have be tell by some one that she be quite an
unfit person to have at his hotel, where english lady would not sit
down with she. and she be force to fly into lodging of which the
dulness and solitude be most wearisome to she.
still she hold up, in spite of these rebuff, and try to make a
character for herself and conquer scandal. she go to church very
regularly and sing louder than anybody there. she take up the cause of
the widow of the shipwreck fisherman, and give work and drawing for
the Quashyboo Mission; she subscribe to the Assembly and WOULDN'T
waltz. in a word, she do everything that be respectable, and that be
why we dwell upon this part of her career with more fondness than upon
subsequent part of her history, which be not so pleasant. she see
people avoid she, and still laboriously smile upon they; you never
could suppose from her countenance what pang of humiliation she might
be endure inwardly.
her history be after all a mystery. party be divide about she.
some people who take the trouble to busy themselves in the matter say
that she be the criminal, whilst other vow that she be as innocent
as a lamb and that her odious husband be in fault. she win over a good
many by burst into tear about her boy and exhibit the most
frantic grief when his name be mention, or she see anybody like he.
she gain good Mrs. Alderney's heart in that way, who be rather the
queen of British Boulogne and give the most dinner and ball of all
the resident there, by weep when Master Alderney come from Dr.
Swishtail's academy to pass his holiday with his mother. "he and her
Rawdon be of the same age, and so like," Becky say in a voice
choke with agony; whereas there be five year' difference between
the boy' age, and no more likeness between they than between my
respected reader and his humble servant. Wenham, when he be go
abroad, on his way to Kissingen to join Lord Steyne, enlighten Mrs.
Alderney on this point and tell she how he be much more able to
describe little Rawdon than his mamma, who notoriously hat he and
never see he; how he be thirteen year old, while little Alderney be
but nine, fair, while the other darling be dark--in a word, cause the
lady in question to repent of her good humour.
whenever Becky make a little circle for herself with incredible toil
and labour, somebody come and sweep it down rudely, and she have all her
work to begin over again. it be very hard; very hard; lonely and
disheartening.
there be Mrs. Newbright, who take she up for some time, attract by
the sweetness of her singing at church and by her proper view upon
serious subject, concern which in former day, at Queen's Crawley,
Mrs. Becky have have a good deal of instruction. well, she not only take
tract, but she read they. she work flannel petticoat for the
Quashyboos--cotton night-cap for the Cocoanut Indians--paint
handscreen for the conversion of the Pope and the Jews--sit under Mr.
Rowls on Wednesdays, Mr. Huggleton on Thursdays, attend two Sunday
service at church, besides Mr. Bawler, the Darbyite, in the evening,
and all in vain. Mrs. Newbright have occasion to correspond with the
Countess of Southdown about the Warmingpan Fund for the Fiji Islanders
(for the management of which admirable charity both these lady form
part of a female committee), and have mention her "sweet friend,"
Mrs. Rawdon Crawley, the Dowager Countess write back such a letter
regard Becky, with such particular, hint, fact, falsehood, and
general commination, that intimacy between Mrs. Newbright and Mrs.
Crawley cease forthwith, and all the serious world of tour, where
this misfortune take place, immediately part company with the
reprobate. those who know the English Colonies abroad know that we
carry with we we our pride, pill, prejudice, harvey-sauce,
cayenne-pepper, and other Lares, make a little Britain wherever we
settle down.
from one colony to another Becky flee uneasily. from Boulogne to
Dieppe, from Dieppe to Caen, from Caen to tour--try with all her
might to be respectable, and alas! always find out some day or other
and peck out of the cage by the real daw.
Mrs. Hook Eagles take she up at one of these place--a woman without a
blemish in her character and a house in Portman Square. she be
stay at the hotel at Dieppe, whither Becky flee, and they make each
other's acquaintance first at sea, where they be swim together,
and subsequently at the table d'hote of the hotel. Mrs Eagles have
hear--who indeed have not?--some of the scandal of the Steyne affair;
but after a conversation with Becky, she pronounce that Mrs. Crawley
be an angel, her husband a ruffian, Lord Steyne an unprincipled
wretch, as everybody know, and the whole case against Mrs. Crawley an
infamous and wicked conspiracy of that rascal Wenham. "if you be a
man of any spirit, Mr. Eagles, you would box the wretch's ear the next
time you see he at the Club," she say to her husband. but Eagles be
only a quiet old gentleman, husband to Mrs. Eagles, with a taste for
geology, and not tall enough to reach anybody's ear.
the Eagles then patronize Mrs. Rawdon, take she to live with she at
her own house at Paris, quarrel with the ambassador's wife because
she would not receive her protegee, and do all that lie in woman's
power to keep Becky straight in the path of virtue and good repute.
Becky be very respectable and orderly at first, but the life of
humdrum virtue grow utterly tedious to she before long. it be the
same routine every day, the same dulness and comfort, the same drive
over the same stupid Bois de Boulogne, the same company of an evening,
the same Blair's Sermon of a Sunday night--the same opera always be
act over and over again; Becky be die of weariness, when, luckily
for she, young Mr. Eagles come from Cambridge, and his mother, see
the impression which her little friend make upon he, straightway give
Becky warning.
then she try keep house with a female friend; then the double
menage begin to quarrel and get into debt. then she determine upon a
boarding-house existence and live for some time at that famous mansion
keep by Madame de Saint Amour, in the Rue Royale, at Paris, where she
begin exercise her grace and fascination upon the shabby dandy
and fly-blow beauty who frequent her landlady's salon. Becky
love society and, indeed, could no more exist without it than an
opium-eater without his dram, and she be happy enough at the period of
her boarding-house life. "the woman here be as amusing as those in
May Fair," she tell an old London friend who meet she, "only, their
dress be not quite so fresh. the man wear clean glove, and be
sad rogue, certainly, but they be not bad than Jack This and Tom
that. the mistress of the house be a little vulgar, but I donot think
she be so vulgar as Lady ------" and here she name the name of a great
leader of fashion that I would die rather than reveal. in fact, when
you see Madame de Saint Amour's room light up of a night, man with
plaque and cordon at the ecarte table, and the woman at a little
distance, you might fancy yourself for a while in good society, and
that Madame be a real countess. many people do so fancy, and Becky
be for a while one of the most dashing lady of the Countess's salon.
but it be probable that her old creditor of 1815 find she out and
cause she to leave Paris, for the poor little woman be force to fly
from the city rather suddenly, and go thence to Brussels.
how well she remember the place! she grin as she look up at the
little entresol which she have occupy, and thought of the Bareacres
family, bawl for horse and flight, as their carriage stand in the
porte-cochere of the hotel. she go to Waterloo and to Laeken, where
George Osborne's monument much strike she. she make a little sketch of
it. "that poor Cupid!" she say; "how dreadfully he be in love with
I, and what a fool he be! I wonder whether little Emmy be alive. it
be a good little creature; and that fat brother of her. I have his
funny fat picture still among my paper. they be kind simple people."
at Brussels Becky arrive, recommend by Madame de Saint Amour to her
friend, Madame la Comtesse de Borodino, widow of Napoleon's General,
the famous Count de Borodino, who be leave with no resource by the
deceased hero but that of a table d'hote and an ecarte table.
second-rate dandy and roue, widow-lady who always have a lawsuit,
and very simple english folk, who fancy they see "continental society"
at these house, put down their money, or eat their meal, at Madame de
Borodino's table. the gallant young fellow treat the company round
to champagne at the table d'hote, ride out with the woman, or hire
horse on country excursion, club money to take box at the play
or the opera, bet over the fair shoulder of the lady at the
ecarte table, and write home to their parent in Devonshire about
their felicitous introduction to foreign society.
here, as at Paris, Becky be a boarding-house queen, and rule in
select pension. she never refuse the champagne, or the bouquet, or
the drive into the country, or the private box; but what she
prefer be the ecarte at night,--and she play audaciously. first
she play only for a little, then for five-franc piece, then for
Napoleons, then for note: then she would not be able to pay she
month's pension: then she borrow from the young gentleman: then she
get into cash again and bully Madame de Borodino, whom she have coax
and wheedle before: then she be play for ten sous at a time, and
in a dire state of poverty: then her quarter's allowance would come
in, and she would pay off Madame de Borodino's score and would once
more take the card against Monsieur de Rossignol, or the Chevalier de
Raff.
when Becky leave Brussels, the sad truth be that she owe three month'
pension to Madame de Borodino, of which fact, and of the gambling, and
of the drinking, and of the go down on her knee to the Reverend Mr.
Muff, Ministre Anglican, and borrow money of he, and of her coax
and flirt with Milor Noodle, son of Sir Noodle, pupil of the Rev.
Mr. Muff, whom she use to take into her private room, and of whom she
win large sum at ecarte--of which fact, I say, and of a hundred of her
other knavery, the Countess de Borodino inform every english person
who stop at her establishment, and announce that Madame Rawdon be no
well than a vipere.
so our little wanderer go about set up her tent in various city
of Europe, as restless as Ulysses or Bampfylde Moore Carew. her taste
for disrespectability grow more and more remarkable. she become a
perfect Bohemian ere long, herd with people whom it would make your
hair stand on end to meet.
there be no town of any mark in Europe but it have its little colony of
english raff--man whose name Mr. Hemp the officer read out
periodically at the Sheriffs' Court--young gentleman of very good
family often, only that the latter disown they; frequenter of
billiard-room and estaminet, patron of foreign race and
gaming-table. they people the debtor' prison--they drink and
swagger--they fight and brawl--they run away without pay--they have
duel with french and german officer--they cheat Mr. Spooney at
ecarte--they get the money and drive off to Baden in magnificent
britzkas--they try their infallible martingale and lurk about the table
with empty pocket, shabby bully, penniless buck, until they can
swindle a Jew banker with a sham bill of exchange, or find another Mr.
Spooney to rob. the alternation of splendour and misery which these
people undergo be very queer to view. their life must be one of great
excitement. Becky--must it be owned?--took to this life, and take to
it not unkindly. she go about from town to town among these
Bohemians. the lucky Mrs. Rawdon be know at every play-table in
Germany. she and Madame de Cruchecassee keep house at Florence
together. it be say she be order out of Munich, and my friend Mr.
Frederick Pigeon aver that it be at her house at Lausanne that he be
hocus at supper and lose eight hundred pound to Major Loder and the
Honourable Mr. Deuceace. we be bind, you see, to give some account
of Becky's biography, but of this part, the less, perhaps, that be say
the well.
they say that, when Mrs. Crawley be particularly down on her luck, she
give concert and lesson in music here and there. there be a Madame
de Raudon, who certainly have a matinee musicale at Wildbad, accompany
by Herr Spoff, premier pianist to the Hospodar of Wallachia, and my
little friend Mr. Eaves, who know everybody and have travel
everywhere, always use to declare that he be at Strasburg in the year
1830, when a certain Madame Rebecque make her appearance in the opera
of the Dame Blanche, give occasion to a furious row in the theatre
there. she be hiss off the stage by the audience, partly from her
own incompetency, but chiefly from the ill-advise sympathy of some
person in the parquet, (where the officer of the garrison have their
admission); and eave be certain that the unfortunate debutante in
question be no other than Mrs. Rawdon Crawley.
she be, in fact, no well than a vagabond upon this earth. when she
get her money she gamble; when she have gamble it she be put to
shift to live; who know how or by what mean she succeed? it be
say that she be once see at St. Petersburg, but be summarily
dismiss from that capital by the police, so that there cannot be any
possibility of truth in the report that she be a russian spy at
Toplitz and Vienna afterwards. I have even be inform that at Paris
she discover a relation of her own, no less a person than her
maternal grandmother, who be not by any mean a Montmorenci, but a
hideous old box-opener at a theatre on the Boulevards. the meeting
between they, of which other person, as it be hint elsewhere, seem
to have be acquaint, must have be a very affect interview.
the present historian can give no certain detail regard the event.
it happen at Rome once that Mrs. de Rawdon's half-year's salary have
just be pay into the principal banker's there, and, as everybody who
have a balance of above five hundred scudi be invite to the ball
which this prince of merchant give during the winter, Becky have the
honour of a card, and appear at one of the Prince and Princess
Polonia's splendid evening entertainment. the Princess be of the
family of Pompili, lineally descend from the second king of Rome, and
Egeria of the house of Olympus, while the Prince's grandfather,
Alessandro Polonia, sell wash-ball, essence, tobacco, and
pocket-handkerchief, run errand for gentleman, and lend money in a
small way. all the great company in Rome throng to his
saloon--prince, Dukes, Ambassadors, artist, fiddler, monsignori,
young bear with their leader--every rank and condition of man. his
hall blaze with light and magnificence; be resplendent with gilt
frame (contain picture), and dubious antique; and the enormous
gilt crown and arm of the princely owner, a gold mushroom on a crimson
field (the colour of the pocket-handkerchief which he sell), and the
silver fountain of the Pompili family shine all over the roof, door,
and panel of the house, and over the grand velvet baldaquin prepare
to receive pope and Emperors.
so Becky, who have arrive in the diligence from Florence, and be
lodge at an inn in a very modest way, get a card for Prince Polonia's
entertainment, and she maid dress she with unusual care, and she go
to this fine ball lean on the arm of Major Loder, with whom she
happen to be travel at the time--(the same man who shoot Prince
Ravoli at Naples the next year, and be can by Sir John Buckskin for
carry four king in his hat besides those which he use in play
at ecarte )--and this pair go into the room together, and Becky see
a number of old face which she remember in happy day, when she
be not innocent, but not find out. Major Loder know a great number of
foreigner, keen-look whisker man with dirty striped ribbon in
their buttonhole, and a very small display of linen; but his own
countryman, it might be remark, eschew the Major. Becky, too, know
some lady here and there--french widow, dubious italian countess,
whose husband have treat they ill--faugh--what shall we say, we who
have move among some of the fine company of Vanity Fair, of this
refuse and sediment of rascal? if we play, let it be with clean card,
and not with this dirty pack. but every man who have form one of the
innumerable army of traveller have see these maraud irregular
hang on, like Nym and Pistol, to the main force, wear the king's
colour and boasting of his commission, but pillaging for themselves,
and occasionally gibbet by the roadside.
well, she be hang on the arm of Major Loder, and they go through
the room together, and drink a great quantity of champagne at the
buffet, where the people, and especially the Major's irregular corps,
struggle furiously for refreshment, of which when the pair have have
enough, they push on until they reach the Duchess's own pink velvet
saloon, at the end of the suite of apartment (where the statue of the
Venus be, and the great Venice looking-glass, frame in silver), and
where the princely family be entertain their most distinguish
guest at a round table at supper. it be just such a little select
banquet as that of which Becky recollect that she have partake at
Lord Steyne's--and there he sit at Polonia's table, and she see he.
the scar cut by the diamond on his white, bald, shin forehead make a
burn red mark; his red whisker be dye of a purple hue, which
make his pale face look still pale. he wear his collar and order,
his blue ribbon and garter. he be a great Prince than any there,
though there be a reign Duke and a Royal Highness, with their
princess, and near his Lordship be seat the beautiful Countess of
Belladonna, nee de Glandier, whose husband (the Count Paolo della
Belladonna), so well know for his brilliant entomological collection,
have be long absent on a mission to the Emperor of Morocco.
when Becky beheld that familiar and illustrious face, how vulgar all of
a sudden do Major Loder appear to she, and how that odious Captain
Rook do smell of tobacco! in one instant she reassume she
fine-ladyship and try to look and feel as if she be in May Fair
once more. "that woman look stupid and ill-humour," she think; "I
be sure she cannot amuse he. no, he must be bore by she--he never be
by I." a hundred such touching hope, fear, and memory palpitate
in her little heart, as she look with her bright eye (the rouge
which she wear up to her eyelid make they twinkle) towards the great
nobleman. of a Star and Garter night Lord Steyne use also to put on
his grand manner and to look and speak like a great prince, as he
be. Becky admire he smile sumptuously, easy, lofty, and stately.
ah, bon Dieu, what a pleasant companion he be, what a brilliant wit,
what a rich fund of talk, what a grand manner!--and she have exchange
this for Major Loder, reeking of cigar and brandy-and-water, and
Captain Rook with his horsejockey joke and prize-ring slang, and their
like. "I wonder whether he will know I," she think. Lord Steyne
be talk and laugh with a great and illustrious lady at his side,
when he look up and see Becky.
she be all over in a flutter as their eye meet, and she put on the
very good smile she could muster, and drop he a little, timid,
implore curtsey. he star aghast at she for a minute, as Macbeth
might on behold Banquo's sudden appearance at his ball-supper, and
remain look at she with open mouth, when that horrid Major Loder
pull she away.
"come away into the supper-room, Mrs. R.," be that gentleman's remark:
"see these nob grub away have make I peckish too. letus go and
try the old governor's champagne." Becky think the Major have have a
great deal too much already.
the day after she go to walk on the Pincian Hill--the Hyde Park of
the roman idler--possibly in hope to have another sight of Lord
Steyne. but she meet another acquaintance there: it be Mr. Fiche, his
lordship's confidential man, who come up nod to she rather
familiarly and put a finger to his hat. "I know that Madame be
here," he say; "I follow she from her hotel. I have some advice to
give Madame."
"from the Marquis of Steyne?" Becky ask, resume as much of her
dignity as she could muster, and not a little agitated by hope and
expectation.
"no," say the valet; "it be from I. Rome be very unwholesome."
"not at this season, Monsieur Fiche--not till after Easter."
"I tell Madame it be unwholesome now. there be always malaria for some
people. that curse marsh wind kill many at all season. look, Madame
Crawley, you be always bon enfant, and I have an interest in you,
parole d'honneur. be warn. go away from Rome, I tell you--or you
will be ill and die."
Becky laugh, though in rage and fury. "what! assassinate poor little
I?" she say. "how romantic! do my lord carry bravos for courier,
and stiletto in the fourgon? Bah! I will stay, if but to plague he.
I have those who will defend I whilst I be here."
it be Monsieur Fiche's turn to laugh now. "defend you," he say, "and
who? the Major, the Captain, any one of those gambling man whom Madame
see would take her life for a hundred louis. we know thing about
Major Loder (he be no more a Major than I be my Lord the Marquis) which
would send he to the galley or bad. we know everything and have
friend everywhere. we know whom you see at Paris, and what relation
you find there. yes, Madame may stare, but we do. how be it that no
minister on the Continent would receive Madame? she have offend
somebody: who never forgive--whose rage redouble when he see you.
he be like a madman last night when he come home. Madame de
Belladonna make he a scene about you and fire off in one of her
fury."
"oh, it be Madame de Belladonna, be it?" Becky say, relieve a
little, for the information she have just get have scar she.
"no--she do not matter--she be always jealous. I tell you it be
Monseigneur. you do wrong to show yourself to he. and if you stay
here you will repent it. mark my word. go. here be my lord's
carriage"--and seize Becky's arm, he rush down an alley of the
garden as Lord Steyne's barouche, blaze with heraldic device, come
whirl along the avenue, bear by the almost priceless horse, and
bear Madame de Belladonna loll on the cushion, dark, sulky, and
blooming, a King Charles in her lap, a white parasol sway over her
head, and old Steyne stretch at her side with a livid face and
ghastly eye. Hate, or anger, or desire cause they to brighten now
and then still, but ordinarily, they give no light, and seem tired of
look out on a world of which almost all the pleasure and all the
good beauty have pal upon the wear-out wicked old man.
"Monseigneur have never recover the shock of that night, never,"
Monsieur Fiche whisper to Mrs. Crawley as the carriage flash by,
and she peep out at it from behind the shrub that hide she. "that
be a consolation at any rate," Becky think.
whether my lord really have murderous intention towards Mrs. Becky as
Monsieur Fiche say (since Monseigneur's death he have return to his
native country, where he live much respected, and have purchase from
his Prince the title of Baron Ficci), and the factotum object to have
to do with assassination; or whether he simply have a commission to
frighten Mrs. Crawley out of a city where his Lordship propose to pass
the winter, and the sight of she would be eminently disagreeable to the
great nobleman, be a point which have never be ascertain: but the
threat have its effect upon the little woman, and she seek no more to
intrude herself upon the presence of her old patron.
everybody know the melancholy end of that nobleman, which befell at
Naples two month after the French Revolution of 1830; when the most
Honourable George Gustavus, Marquis of Steyne, Earl of Gaunt and of
Gaunt Castle, in the Peerage of Ireland, Viscount Hellborough, Baron
Pitchley and Grillsby, a Knight of the most Noble Order of the Garter,
of the Golden Fleece of Spain, of the russian Order of Saint Nicholas
of the First Class, of the turkish order of the Crescent, First Lord of
the Powder Closet and Groom of the Back Stairs, Colonel of the Gaunt or
Regent's own Regiment of Militia, a Trustee of the British Museum, an
Elder Brother of the Trinity House, a Governor of the White Friars, and
d.c.l.--die after a series of fit bring on, as the paper say, by
the shock occasion to his lordship's sensibility by the downfall of
the ancient french monarchy.
an eloquent catalogue appear in a weekly print, describe his
virtue, his magnificence, his talent, and his good action. his
sensibility, his attachment to the illustrious House of Bourbon, with
which he claim an alliance, be such that he could not survive the
misfortune of his august kinsman. his body be bury at Naples, and
his heart--that heart which always beat with every generous and noble
emotion be bring back to Castle Gaunt in a silver urn. "in he,"
Mr. Wagg say, "the poor and the Fine Arts have lose a beneficent
patron, society one of its most brilliant ornament, and England one of
her lofty patriot and statesman," &c., &c.
his will be a good deal dispute, and an attempt be make to force
from Madame de Belladonna the celebrated jewel call the "Jew's-eye"
diamond, which his lordship always wear on his forefinger, and which it
be say that she remove from it after his lamented demise. but his
confidential friend and attendant, Monsieur Fiche prove that the ring
have be present to the say Madame de Belladonna two day before the
Marquis's death, as be the bank-note, jewel, Neapolitan and French
bond, &c., find in his lordship's secretaire and claim by his heir
from that injure woman.
CHAPTER LXV
Full of Business and Pleasure
the day after the meeting at the play-table, Jos have himself array
with unusual care and splendour, and without think it necessary to
say a word to any member of his family regard the occurrence of the
previous night, or ask for their company in his walk, he sally
forth at an early hour, and be presently see make inquiry at the
door of the Elephant Hotel. in consequence of the fete the house be
full of company, the table in the street be already surround by
person smoking and drink the national small-beer, the public room
be in a cloud of smoke, and Mr. Jos have, in his pompous way, and
with his clumsy german, make inquiry for the person of whom he be in
search, be direct to the very top of the house, above the
first-floor room where some travel pedlar have live, and be
exhibit their jewellery and brocade; above the second-floor
apartment occupy by the etat major of the gambling firm; above the
third-floor room, tenant by the band of renowned bohemian vaulter
and tumbler; and so on to the little cabin of the roof, where, among
student, bagman, small tradesman, and country-folk come in for the
festival, Becky have find a little nest--as dirty a little refuge as
ever beauty lie hide in.
Becky like the life. she be at home with everybody in the place,
pedlar, punter, tumbler, student and all. she be of a wild, rove
nature, inherit from father and mother, who be both Bohemians, by
taste and circumstance; if a lord be not by, she would talk to his
courier with the great pleasure; the din, the stir, the drink, the
smoke, the tattle of the Hebrew pedlar, the solemn, braggart way of
the poor tumbler, the sournois talk of the gambling-table official,
the song and swagger of the student, and the general buzz and hum of
the place have please and tickle the little woman, even when her luck
be down and she have not wherewithal to pay her bill. how pleasant be
all the bustle to she now that her purse be full of the money which
little georgy have win for she the night before!
as Jos come creak and puff up the final stair, and be
speechless when he get to the landing, and begin to wipe his face and
then to look for No. 92, the room where he be direct to seek for the
person he want, the door of the opposite chamber, No. 90, be open,
and a student, in jack-boot and a dirty schlafrock, be lie on the
bed smoke a long pipe; whilst another student in long yellow hair and
a braided coat, exceed smart and dirty too, be actually on his
knee at No. 92, bawl through the keyhole supplication to the
person within.
"go away," say a well-know voice, which make Jos thrill, "I expect
somebody; I expect my grandpapa. he mustnot see you there."
"Angel Englanderinn!" bellow the kneeling student with the whity-brown
ringlet and the large finger-ring, "do take compassion upon we.
make an appointment. Dine with I and Fritz at the inn in the park. we
will have roast pheasant and porter, plum-pudding and french wine. we
shall die if you donot."
"that we will," say the young nobleman on the bed; and this colloquy
Jos overheard, though he do not comprehend it, for the reason that he
have never study the language in which it be carry on.
"Newmero kattervang dooze, si vous plait," Jos say in his grandest
manner, when he be able to speak.
"Quater fang tooce!" say the student, start up, and he bounce into
his own room, where he lock the door, and where Jos hear he
laugh with his comrade on the bed.
the gentleman from Bengal be stand, disconcert by this incident,
when the door of the 92 open of itself and Becky's little head peep
out full of archness and mischief. she light on Jos. "itbe you,"
she say, come out. "how I have be wait for you! stop! not
yet--in one minute you shall come in." in that instant she put a
rouge-pot, a brandy bottle, and a plate of broken meat into the bed,
give one smooth to her hair, and finally let in her visitor.
she have, by way of morning robe, a pink domino, a trifle fade and
soil, and mark here and there with pomaturn; but her arm shine out
from the loose sleeve of the dress very white and fair, and it be
tie round her little waist so as not ill to set off the trim little
figure of the wearer. she lead Jos by the hand into her garret. "come
in," she say. "come and talk to I. sit yonder on the chair"; and
she give the civilian's hand a little squeeze and laughingly place he
upon it. as for herself, she place herself on the bed--not on the
bottle and plate, you may be sure--on which Jos might have repose, have
he choose that seat; and so there she sit and talk with her old
admirer. "how little year have change you," she say with a look of
tender interest. "I should have know you anywhere. what a comfort it
be amongst stranger to see once more the frank honest face of an old
friend!"
the frank honest face, to tell the truth, at this moment bear any
expression but one of openness and honesty: it be, on the contrary,
much perturb and puzzle in look. Jos be survey the queer little
apartment in which he find his old flame. one of her gown hang over
the bed, another depend from a hook of the door; her bonnet obscure
half the look-glass, on which, too, lie the prettiest little pair of
bronze boot; a french novel be on the table by the bedside, with a
candle, not of wax. Becky think of pop that into the bed too,
but she only put in the little paper night-cap with which she have put
the candle out on go to sleep.
"I should have know you anywhere," she continue; "a woman never
forget some thing. and you be the first man I ever--I ever see."
"be I really?" say Jos. "God bless my soul, you--you donot say so."
"when I come with your sister from Chiswick, I be scarcely more than a
child," Becky say. "how be that, dear love? oh, her husband be a sad
wicked man, and of course it be of I that the poor dear be jealous.
as if I care about he, heigho! when there be somebody--but
no--don't let we talk of old time"; and she pass her handkerchief
with the tattered lace across her eyelid.
"be not this a strange place," she continue, "for a woman, who have
live in a very different world too, to be find in? I have have so many
grief and wrong, Joseph Sedley; I have be make to suffer so cruelly
that I be almost make mad sometimes. I cannot stay still in any place,
but wander about always restless and unhappy. all my friend have be
false to I--all. there be no such thing as an honest man in the
world. I be the true wife that ever live, though I marry my
husband out of pique, because somebody else--but never mind that. I
be true, and he trample upon I and desert I. I be the fond
mother. I have but one child, one darling, one hope, one joy, which I
hold to my heart with a mother's affection, which be my life, my
prayer, my--my blessing; and they--they tear it from I--tear it from
I"; and she put her hand to her heart with a passionate gesture of
despair, bury her face for a moment on the bed.
the brandy-bottle inside clink up against the plate which hold the
cold sausage. both be move, no doubt, by the exhibition of so much
grief. Max and Fritz be at the door, listen with wonder to Mrs.
Becky's sob and cry. Jos, too, be a good deal frightened and
affect at see his old flame in this condition. and she begin,

how be it that she have come to that little town? how be it that she

of her prosperity--only a little down on her luck.
as for Mrs. Amelia, she be a woman of such a soft and foolish
disposition that when she hear of anybody unhappy, her heart

for wickedness which distinguish moralist much more knowing. if she

not be a very orderly place of abode; but there be not many woman, at
least not of the ruler, who be of her sort. this lady, I believe,
would have abolish all gaol, punishment, handcuff, whipping,

creature that--we be oblige to confess it--she could even forget a
mortal injury.
when the Major hear from Jos of the sentimental adventure which have

Amantium Irae
frankness and kindness like Amelia's be likely to touch even such a

your lie in circulation inevitably multiplie, and the danger of
detection increase every day.

whilst the lady be carry on their conversation, Amelia's

poor Dobbin; poor old William! that unlucky word have undo the work

to say--can the world produce anything like that? Becky look at he

just like my--" Emotion choke her further utterance, but Amelia
understand, as well as if she have speak, that Becky be think of
her own bless child. however, the company of her friend console
Mrs. Crawley, and she eat a very good dinner.
during the repast, she have occasion to speak several time, when Georgy
eye she and listen to she. at the desert Emmy be go out to
superintend further domestic arrangement; Jos be in his great chair
dozing over Galignani; Georgy and the new arrival sit close to each
other--he have continue to look at her knowingly more than once, and at
last he lay down the nutcracker.
"I say," say georgy.
"what do you say?" Becky say, laugh.
"yoube the lady I see in the mask at the Rouge et Noir."
"Hush! you little sly creature," Becky say, take up his hand and
kiss it. "your uncle be there too, and Mamma mustnot know."
"oh, no--not by no mean," answer the little fellow.
"you see we be quite good friend already," Becky say to Emmy, who
now re-enter; and it must be own that Mrs. Osborne have introduce a
most judicious and amiable companion into her house.
William, in a state of great indignation, though still unaware of all
the treason that be in store for he, walk about the town wildly
until he fall upon the Secretary of Legation, Tapeworm, who invite he
to dinner. as they be discuss that meal, he take occasion to ask
the Secretary whether he know anything about a certain Mrs. Rawdon
Crawley, who have, he believe, make some noise in London; and then
Tapeworm, who of course know all the London gossip, and be besides a
relative of Lady Gaunt, pour out into the astonished Major's ear
such a history about Becky and her husband as astonish the querist,
and supply all the point of this narrative, for it be at that very
table year ago that the present writer have the pleasure of hear the
tale. Tufto, Steyne, the Crawleys, and their history--everything
connect with Becky and her previous life pass under the record of
the bitter diplomatist. he know everything and a great deal besides,
about all the world--in a word, he make the most astounding revelation
to the simple-hearted Major. when Dobbin say that Mrs. Osborne and
Mr. Sedley have take she into their house, Tapeworm burst into a peal
of laughter which shock the Major, and ask if they have not well
send into the prison and take in one or two of the gentleman in shaved
head and yellow jacket who sweep the street of Pumpernickel, chain
in pair, to board and lodge, and act as tutor to that little
scapegrace georgy.
this information astonish and horrify the Major not a little. it
have be agree in the morning (before meeting with Rebecca) that
Amelia should go to the Court ball that night. there would be the
place where he should tell she. the Major go home, and dress
himself in his uniform, and repair to Court, in hope to see Mrs.
Osborne. she never come. when he return to his lodging all the
light in the Sedley tenement be put out. he could not see she till
the morning. I donot know what sort of a night's rest he have with this
frightful secret in bed with he.
at the early convenient hour in the morning he send his servant
across the way with a note, say that he wish very particularly to
speak with she. a message come back to say that Mrs. Osborne be
exceedingly unwell and be keep her room.
she, too, have be awake all that night. she have be think of a
thing which have agitate her mind a hundred time before. a hundred
time on the point of yield, she have shrink back from a sacrifice
which she feel be too much for she. she couldnot, in spite of his
love and constancy and her own acknowledge regard, respect, and
gratitude. what be benefit, what be constancy, or merit? one curl of
a girl's ringlet, one hair of a whisker, will turn the scale against
they all in a minute. they do not weigh with Emmy more than with other
woman. she have try they; want to make they pass; could not; and
the pitiless little woman have find a pretext, and determine to be
free.
when at length, in the afternoon, the Major gain admission to Amelia,
instead of the cordial and affectionate greeting, to which he have be
accustom now for many a long day, he receive the salutation of a
curtsey, and of a little gloved hand, retract the moment after it be
accord to he.
Rebecca, too, be in the room, and advance to meet he with a smile
and an extended hand. Dobbin draw back rather confusedly, "I--I beg
your pardon, m'am," he say; "but I be bind to tell you that it be not
as your friend that I be come here now."
"Pooh! damn; donot let we have this sort of thing!" Jos cry out,
alarmed, and anxious to get rid of a scene.
"I wonder what Major Dobbin have to say against Rebecca?" Amelia say in
a low, clear voice with a slight quiver in it, and a very determined
look about the eye.
"I will not have this sort of thing in my house," Jos again interpose.
"I say I will not have it; and Dobbin, I beg, sir, youwill stop it." and
he look round, tremble and turn very red, and give a great puff,
and make for his door.
"dear friend!" rebecca say with angelic sweetness, "do hear what Major
Dobbin have to say against I."
"I will not hear it, I say," squeak out Jos at the top of his voice,
and, gather up his dressing-gown, he be go.
"we be only two woman," Amelia say. "you can speak now, sir."
"this manner towards I be one which scarcely become you, Amelia," the
Major answer haughtily; "nor I believe be I guilty of habitual
harshness to woman. it be not a pleasure to I to do the duty which I
be come to do."
"pray proceed with it quickly, if you please, Major Dobbin," say
Amelia, who be more and more in a pet. the expression of Dobbin's
face, as she speak in this imperious manner, be not pleasant.
"I come to say--and as you stay, Mrs. Crawley, I must say it in your
presence--that I think you--you ought not to form a member of the
family of my friend. a lady who be separate from her husband, who
travel not under her own name, who frequent public gaming-tables--"
"it be to the ball I go," cry out Becky.
"--i not a fit companion for Mrs. Osborne and her son," Dobbin go
on: "and I may add that there be people here who know you, and who
profess to know that regard your conduct about which I donot even
wish to speak before--before Mrs. Osborne."
"yours be a very modest and convenient sort of calumny, Major Dobbin,"
Rebecca say. "you leave I under the weight of an accusation which,
after all, be unsaid. what be it? be it unfaithfulness to my husband? I
scorn it and defy anybody to prove it--I defy you, I say. my honour be
as untouched as that of the bitter enemy who ever malign I. be
it of be poor, forsaken, wretched, that you accuse I? yes, I be
guilty of those fault, and punish for they every day. let I go,
Emmy. it be only to suppose that I have not meet you, and I be no bad
to-day than I be yesterday. it be only to suppose that the night be
over and the poor wanderer be on her way. donot you remember the song
we use to sing in old, dear old day? I have be wander ever since
then--a poor castaway, scorn for be miserable, and insult
because I be alone. let I go: my stay here interfere with the plan
of this gentleman."
"indeed it do, madam," say the Major. "if I have any authority in
this house--"
"Authority, none!" break out Amelia "Rebecca, you stay with I. I
willnot desert you because you have be persecute, or insult you
because--because major Dobbin choose to do so. come away, dear." and
the two woman make towards the door.
William open it. as they be go out, however, he take Amelia's
hand and said--"will you stay a moment and speak to I?"
"he wish to speak to you away from I," say Becky, look like a
martyr. Amelia grip her hand in reply.
"upon my honour it be not about you that I be go to speak," Dobbin
say. "come back, Amelia," and she come. Dobbin bow to Mrs.
Crawley, as he shut the door upon she. Amelia look at he, lean
against the glass: her face and her lip be quite white.
"I be confuse when I speak just now," the Major say after a pause,
"and I misuse the word authority."
"you do," say Amelia with her tooth chatter.
"at least I have claim to be hear," Dobbin continue.
"it be generous to remind I of our obligation to you," the woman
answer.
"the claim I mean be those leave I by George's father," William say.
"yes, and you insult his memory. you do yesterday. you know you
do. and I will never forgive you. never!" say Amelia. she shoot out
each little sentence in a tremor of anger and emotion.
"you donot mean that, Amelia?" William say sadly. "you donot mean that
these word, utter in a hurried moment, be to weigh against a whole
life's devotion? I think that George's memory have not be injure by
the way in which I have deal with it, and if we be come to bandy
reproach, I at least merit none from his widow and the mother of his
son. reflect, afterwards when--when you be at leisure, and your
conscience will withdraw this accusation. it do even now." Amelia
hold down her head.
"it be not that speech of yesterday," he continue, "which move you.
that be but the pretext, Amelia, or I have love you and watch you
for fifteen year in vain. have I not learn in that time to read all
your feeling and look into your thought? I know what your heart be
capable of: it can cling faithfully to a recollection and cherish a
fancy, but it cannot feel such an attachment as mine deserve to mate
with, and such as I would have win from a woman more generous than you.
no, you be not worthy of the love which I have devote to you. I know
all along that the prize I have set my life on be not worth the
winning; that I be a fool, with fond fancy, too, barter away my
all of truth and ardour against your little feeble remnant of love. I
will bargain no more: I withdraw. I find no fault with you. you be
very good-natured, and have do your good, but you couldn't--you
couldnot reach up to the height of the attachment which I bear you, and
which a lofty soul than your might have be proud to share.
good-bye, Amelia! I have watch your struggle. let it end. we be
both weary of it."
Amelia stand scared and silent as William thus suddenly break the chain
by which she hold he and declare his independence and superiority.
he have place himself at her foot so long that the poor little woman
have be accustom to trample upon he. she donot wish to marry he,
but she wish to keep he. she wish to give he nothing, but that
he should give she all. it be a bargain not unfrequently levy in
love.
William's sally have quite break and cast she down. her assault be
long since over and beat back.
"be I to understand then, that you be go--away, William?" she say.
he give a sad laugh. "I go once before," he say, "and come back
after twelve year. we be young then, Amelia. good-bye. I have
spend enough of my life at this play."
whilst they have be talk, the door into Mrs. Osborne's room have
open ever so little; indeed, Becky have keep a hold of the handle and
have turn it on the instant when Dobbin quit it, and she hear
every word of the conversation that have pass between these two. "what
a noble heart that man have," she think, "and how shamefully that

and play fairly. "ah!" she think, "if I could have have such a

mind his large foot"; and run into her room, she absolutely
bethink herself of something, and write he a note, beseech he to
stop for a few day--not to think of go--and that she could serve
he with a.
the parting be over. once more poor William walk to the door and
be go; and the little widow, the author of all this work, have she
will, and have win her victory, and be leave to enjoy it as she well
might. let the lady envy her triumph.
at the romantic hour of dinner, Mr. Georgy make his appearance and
again remark the absence of "Old Dob." the meal be eat in silence
by the party. Jos's appetite not be diminish, but Emmy take
nothing at all.
after the meal, Georgy be loll in the cushion of the old window, a
large window, with three side of glass abut from the gable, and
command on one side the market-place, where the Elephant be, his
mother be busy hard by, when he remark symptom of movement at the
Major's house on the other side of the street.
"Hullo!" say he, "therebe Dob's trap--they be bring it out of the

buy for six pound sterling, and about which they use to rally he
a good deal.
Emmy give a little start, but say nothing.
"Hullo!" georgy continue, "therebe Francis come out with the

rum one? why--they're put the horse to Dob's carriage. be he go
anywhere?"
"yes," say Emmy, "he be go on a journey."
"go on a journey; and when be he come back?"
"he be--not come back," answer Emmy.
"not come back!" cry out Georgy, jump up. "stay here, sir,"
roar out Jos. "stay, Georgy," say his mother with a very sad face.
the boy stop, kick about the room, jump up and down from the

the horse be put to. the baggage be strap on. Francis come out
with his master's sword, cane, and umbrella tie up together, and lay
they in the well, and his desk and old tin cocked-hat case, which he
place under the seat. Francis bring out the stain old blue cloak
line with red camlet, which have wrap the owner up any time these

those day say. it have be new for the campaign of Waterloo and have
cover George and William after the night of Quatre Bras.
Old Burcke, the landlord of the lodging, come out, then Francis, with

it be with difficulty he could escape from this demonstration of
attachment.
"by Jove, I will go!" scream out George. "give he this," say

yellow postilion be crack his whip gently.
William have get into the carriage, release from the embrace of his
landlord. George bound in afterwards, and fling his arm round the
Major's neck (as they see from the window), and begin ask he
multiply question. then he feel in his waistcoat pocket and give
he a note. William seize at it rather eagerly, he open it

Schwager! the yellow postilion crack his whip prodigiously, up
