head on his breast. he never look up as they pass under Amelia's
window, and Georgy, leave alone in the street, burst out cry in the
face of all the crowd.
Emmy's maid hear he howl again during the night and bring he
some preserve apricot to console he. she mingle her lamentation
with his. all the poor, all the humble, all honest folk, all good man
who know he, love that kind-hearted and simple gentleman.
as for Emmy, have she not do her duty? she have her picture of George
for a consolation.
CHAPTER LXVII
which contain Births, Marriages, and death
whatever Becky's private plan might be by which Dobbin's true love be
to be crown with success, the little woman think that the secret
might keep, and indeed, be by no mean so much interested about
anybody's welfare as about her own, she have a great number of thing
pertain to herself to consider, and which concern she a great deal
more than Major Dobbin's happiness in this life.
she find herself suddenly and unexpectedly in snug comfortable
quarter, surround by friend, kindness, and good-natured simple
people such as she have not meet with for many a long day; and, wanderer
as she be by force and inclination, there be moment when rest be
pleasant to she. as the most harden Arab that ever career across
the desert over the hump of a dromedary like to repose sometimes under
the date-tree by the water, or to come into the city, walk into the
bazaar, refresh himself in the bath, and say his prayer in the
mosque, before he go out again maraud, so Jos's tent and pilau
be pleasant to this little Ishmaelite. she picket her steed, hung
up her weapon, and warm herself comfortably by his fire. the halt
in that roving, restless life be inexpressibly soothing and pleasant
to she.
so, please herself, she try with all her might to please everybody;
and we know that she be eminent and successful as a practitioner in
the art of give pleasure. as for Jos, even in that little interview
in the garret at the Elephant Inn, she have find mean to win back a
great deal of his good-will. in the course of a week, the civilian be
her swear slave and frantic admirer. he donot go to sleep after
dinner, as his custom be in the much less lively society of Amelia.
he drive out with Becky in his open carriage. he ask little party
and invent festivity to do her honour.
Tapeworm, the Charge d'Affaires, who have abuse she so cruelly, come to
dine with Jos, and then come every day to pay his respect to Becky.
Poor Emmy, who be never very talkative, and more glum and silent than
ever after Dobbin's departure, be quite forget when this superior
genius make her appearance. the french Minister be as much charm
with she as his english rival. the german lady, never particularly
squeamish as regard moral, especially in english people, be
delighted with the cleverness and wit of Mrs. Osborne's charming
friend, and though she do not ask to go to Court, yet the most august
and Transparent Personages there hear of her fascination and be
quite curious to know she. when it become know that she be noble, of
an ancient english family, that her husband be a Colonel of the Guard,
Excellenz and Governor of an island, only separate from his lady by
one of those trifle difference which be of little account in a
country where Werther be still read and the Wahlverwandtschaften of
Goethe be consider an edify moral book, nobody think of refuse
to receive she in the very high society of the little Duchy; and the
lady be even more ready to call she du and to swear eternal
friendship for she than they have be to bestow the same inestimable
benefit upon Amelia. Love and Liberty be interpret by those simple
Germans in a way which honest folk in Yorkshire and Somersetshire
little understand, and a lady might, in some philosophic and civilized
town, be divorce ever so many time from her respective husband and
keep her character in society. Jos's house never be so pleasant since
he have a house of his own as Rebecca cause it to be. she sing, she
play, she laugh, she talk in two or three language, she bring
everybody to the house, and she make Jos believe that it be his own
great social talent and wit which gather the society of the place
round about he.
as for Emmy, who find herself not in the least mistress of her own
house, except when the bill be to be pay, Becky soon discover the
way to soothe and please she. she talk to she perpetually about
Major Dobbin send about his business, and make no scruple of declare
her admiration for that excellent, high-minded gentleman, and of
tell Emmy that she have behave most cruelly regard he. Emmy
defend her conduct and show that it be dictate only by the pure
religious principle; that a woman once, &c., and to such an angel as
he whom she have have the good fortune to marry, be marry forever;
but she have no objection to hear the Major praise as much as ever
Becky choose to praise he, and indeed, bring the conversation round
to the Dobbin subject a score of time every day.
mean be easily find to win the favour of Georgy and the servant.
Amelia's maid, it have be say, be heart and soul in favour of the
generous Major. have at first dislike Becky for be the mean of
dismiss he from the presence of her mistress, she be reconcile to
Mrs. Crawley subsequently, because the latter become William's most
ardent admirer and champion. and in those nightly conclave in which
the two lady indulge after their party, and while Miss Payne be
"brush their 'air," as she call the yellow lock of the one and
the soft brown tress of the other, this girl always put in her word
for that dear good gentleman Major Dobbin. her advocacy do not make
Amelia angry any more than Rebecca's admiration of he. she make
George write to he constantly and persist in send Mamma's kind
love in a postscript. and as she look at her husband's portrait of
night, it no long reproach she--perhaps she reproach it, now
William be go.
Emmy be not very happy after her heroic sacrifice. she be very
distraite, nervous, silent, and ill to please. the family have never
know she so peevish. she grow pale and ill. she use to try to sing
certain song ("Einsam bin ich nicht alleine," be one of they, that
tender love-song of Weber's which in old-fashioned day, young lady,
and when you be scarcely bear, show that those who live before you
know too how to love and to sing) certain song, I say, to which the
Major be partial; and as she warble they in the twilight in the
drawing-room, she would break off in the midst of the song, and walk
into her neighbouring apartment, and there, no doubt, take refuge in
the miniature of her husband.
some book still subsist, after Dobbin's departure, with his name
write in they; a german dictionary, for instance, with "William
Dobbin, --th Reg.," in the fly-leaf; a guide-book with his initial;
and one or two other volume which belong to the Major. Emmy clear
these away and put they on the drawer, where she place her work-box,
her desk, her Bible, and prayer-book, under the picture of the two
george. and the Major, on go away, have leave his glove behind
he, it be a fact that georgy, rummage his mother's desk some time
afterwards, find the glove neatly fold up and put away in what they
call the secret-drawer of the desk.
not care for society, and mop there a great deal, Emmy's chief
pleasure in the summer evening be to take long walk with Georgy
(during which rebecca be leave to the society of Mr. Joseph), and then
the mother and son use to talk about the Major in a way which even
make the boy smile. she tell he that she think Major William be
the good man in all the world--the gentle and the kind, the
brave and the humblest. over and over again she tell he how they
owe everything which they possess in the world to that kind friend's
benevolent care of they; how he have befriend they all through their
poverty and misfortune; watch over they when nobody care for they;
how all his comrade admire he though he never speak of his own
gallant action; how Georgy's father trust he beyond all other man,
and have be constantly befriend by the good William. "why, when
your papa be a little boy," she say, "he often tell I that it be
William who defend he against a tyrant at the school where they
be; and their friendship never cease from that day until the last,
when your dear father fall."
"do Dobbin kill the man who kill papa?" georgy say. "Ibe sure he
do, or he would if he could have catch he, wouldnot he, Mother? when
Ibe in the Army, willnot I hate the french?--that's all."
in such colloquy the mother and the child pass a great deal of
their time together. the artless woman have make a confidant of the
boy. he be as much William's friend as everybody else who know he
well.
by the way, Mrs. Becky, not to be behind hand in sentiment, have get a
miniature too hang up in her room, to the surprise and amusement of
most people, and the delight of the original, who be no other than our
friend Jos. on her first come to favour the Sedleys with a visit,
the little woman, who have arrive with a remarkably small shabby kit,
be perhaps ashamed of the meanness of her trunk and bandbox, and
often speak with great respect about her baggage leave behind at
Leipzig, which she must have from that city. when a traveller talk to
you perpetually about the splendour of his luggage, which he do not
happen to have with he, my son, beware of that traveller! he be, ten
to one, an impostor.
neither Jos nor Emmy know this important maxim. it seem to they of
no consequence whether Becky have a quantity of very fine clothe in
invisible trunk; but as her present supply be exceedingly shabby,
Emmy supply she out of her own store, or take she to the good
milliner in the town and there fit she out. it be no more torn
collar now, I promise you, and fade silk trail off at the
shoulder. Becky change her habit with her situation in life--the
rouge-pot be suspend--another excitement to which she have accustom
herself be also put aside, or at least only indulge in in privacy, as
when she be prevail on by Jos of a summer evening, Emmy and the boy
be absent on their walk, to take a little spirit-and-water. but if
she do not indulge--the courier do: that rascal Kirsch could not be
keep from the bottle, nor could he tell how much he take when he
apply to it. he be sometimes surprise himself at the way in which
Mr. Sedley's Cognac diminish. well, well, this be a painful subject.
Becky do not very likely indulge so much as she use before she
enter a decorous family.
at last the much-brag-about box arrive from Leipzig; three of
they not by any mean large or splendid; nor do Becky appear to take
out any sort of dress or ornament from the box when they do
arrive. but out of one, which contain a mass of her paper (it be
that very box which Rawdon Crawley have ransack in his furious hunt
for Becky's conceal money), she take a picture with great glee, which
she pin up in her room, and to which she introduce Jos. it be the
portrait of a gentleman in pencil, his face have the advantage of
be paint up in pink. he be rid on an elephant away from some
cocoa-nut tree and a pagoda: it be an eastern scene.
"God bless my soul, it be my portrait," Jos cry out. it be he
indeed, bloom in youth and beauty, in a nankeen jacket of the cut of
1804. it be the old picture that use to hang up in Russell Square.
"I buy it," say Becky in a voice tremble with emotion; "I go to
see if I could be of any use to my kind friend. I have never part
with that picture--I never will."
"willnot you?" Jos cry with a look of unutterable rapture and
satisfaction. "do you really now value it for my sake?"
"you know I do, well enough," say Becky; "but why speak--why
think--why look back! it be too late now!"
that evening's conversation be delicious for Jos. Emmy only come in to
go to bed very tired and unwell. Jos and his fair guest have a charming
tete-a-tete, and his sister could hear, as she lie awake in her
adjoining chamber, rebecca singing over to Jos the old song of 1815.
he do not sleep, for a wonder, that night, any more than Amelia.
it be June, and, by consequence, high season in London; Jos, who read
the incomparable Galignani (the exile's good friend) through every day,
use to favour the lady with extract from his paper during their
breakfast. every week in this paper there be a full account of
military movement, in which Jos, as a man who have see service, be
especially interested. on one occasion he read out--"arrival of the
--th regiment. Gravesend, June 20.--The Ramchunder, East Indiaman,
come into the river this morning, have on board 14 officer, and 132
rank and file of this gallant corps. they have be absent from
England fourteen year, have be embark the year after Waterloo,
in which glorious conflict they take an active part, and have
subsequently distinguish themselves in the burmese war. the veteran
colonel, Sir Michael O'Dowd, K.C.B., with his lady and sister, land
here yesterday, with Captains Posky, Stubble, Macraw, Malony;
Lieutenants Smith, Jones, Thompson, F. Thomson; Ensigns Hicks and
Grady; the band on the pier play the national anthem, and the crowd
loudly cheer the gallant veteran as they go into Wayte's hotel,
where a sumptuous banquet be provide for the defender of Old
England. during the repast, which we need not say be serve up in
Wayte's good style, the cheering continue so enthusiastically that
Lady O'Dowd and the Colonel come forward to the balcony and drank the
health of their fellow-countryman in a bumper of Wayte's good claret."
on a second occasion Jos read a brief announcement--Major Dobbin have
join the --th regiment at Chatham; and subsequently he promulgate
account of the presentation at the Drawing-room of Colonel Sir
Michael O'Dowd, K.C.B., Lady O'Dowd (by Mrs. Malloy Malony of
Ballymalony), and Miss Glorvina O'Dowd (by Lady O'Dowd). almost
directly after this, Dobbin's name appear among the Lieutenant-Colonels:
for old Marshal Tiptoff have die during the passage of the
--th from Madras, and the Sovereign be pleased to advance Colonel Sir
Michael O'Dowd to the rank of Major-General on his return to England,
with an intimation that he should be Colonel of the distinguished
regiment which he have so long command.
Amelia have be make aware of some of these movement. the
correspondence between George and his guardian have not cease by any
mean: William have even write once or twice to she since his
departure, but in a manner so unconstrainedly cold that the poor woman
feel now in her turn that she have lose her power over he and that, as
he have say, he be free. he have leave she, and she be wretche. the
memory of his almost countless service, and lofty and affectionate
regard, now present itself to she and rebuke her day and night. she
brood over those recollection accord to she willnot, see the purity
and beauty of the affection with which she have trifle, and reproach
herself for have fling away such a treasure.
it be go indeed. William have spend it all out. he love her no
more, he think, as he have love she. he never could again. that sort
of regard, which he have proffer to she for so many faithful year,
cannot be fling down and shatter and mend so as to show no scar.
the little heedless tyrant have so destroy it. no, William think
again and again, "it be myself I delude and persist in cajole;
have she be worthy of the love I give she, she would have return it
long ago. it be a fond mistake. benot the whole course of life make
up of such? and suppose I have win she, should I not have be
disenchant the day after my victory? why pine, or be ashamed of my
defeat?" the more he think of this long passage of his life, the more
clearly he see his deception. "Iwill go into harness again," he say,
"and do my duty in that state of life in which it have please Heaven to
place I. I will see that the button of the recruit be properly
bright and that the sergeant make no mistake in their account. I
will dine at mess and listen to the Scotch surgeon tell his story.
when I be old and broken, I will go on half-pay, and my old sister
shall scold I. I have geliebt und gelebet, as the girl in
'Wallenstein' say. I be do. pay the bill and get I a cigar:
find out what there be at the play to-night, Francis; to-morrow we
cross by the Batavier." he make the above speech, whereof Francis only
hear the last two line, pace up and down the Boompjes at Rotterdam.
the Batavier be lie in the basin. he could see the place on the
quarter-deck where he and Emmy have sit on the happy voyage out. what
have that little Mrs. Crawley to say to he? Psha; to-morrow we will put
to sea, and return to England, home, and duty!
after June all the little Court Society of Pumpernickel use to
separate, accord to the german plan, and make for a hundred
watering-place, where they drink at the well, ride upon donkey,
gamble at the redoute if they have money and a mind, rush with
hundred of their kind to gourmandise at the table d'hote, and idle
away the summer. the english diplomatist go off to Teoplitz and
Kissingen, their french rival shut up their chancellerie and whisk
away to their darling Boulevard de Gand. the transparent reigning
family take too to the water, or retire to their hunting lodge.
everybody go away have any pretension to politeness, and of
course, with they, Doctor von Glauber, the Court Doctor, and his
Baroness. the season for the bath be the most productive period
of the Doctor's practice--he unite business with pleasure, and his
chief place of resort be Ostend, which be much frequent by german,
and where the Doctor treat himself and his spouse to what he call a
"dib" in the sea.
his interesting patient, Jos, be a regular milch-cow to the Doctor,
and he easily persuade the civilian, both for his own health's sake
and that of his charming sister, which be really very much shatter,
to pass the summer at that hideous seaport town. Emmy do not care
where she go much. georgy jump at the idea of a move. as for
Becky, she come as a matter of course in the fourth place inside of the
fine barouche Mr. Jos have buy, the two domestic be on the box in
front. she might have some misgiving about the friend whom she should
meet at Ostend, and who might be likely to tell ugly story--but bah!
she be strong enough to hold her own. she have cast such an anchor in
Jos now as would require a strong storm to shake. that incident of the
picture have finish he. Becky take down her elephant and put it into
the little box which she have have from Amelia ever so many year ago.
Emmy also come off with her Lares--her two picture--and the party,
finally, be, lodge in an exceedingly dear and uncomfortable house at
Ostend.
there Amelia begin to take bath and get what good she could from they,
and though score of people of Becky's acquaintance pass she and cut
she, yet Mrs. Osborne, who walk about with she, and who know nobody,
be not aware of the treatment experience by the friend whom she have
choose so judiciously as a companion; indeed, Becky never think fit
to tell she what be pass under her innocent eye.
some of Mrs. Rawdon Crawley's acquaintance, however, acknowledge she
readily enough,--perhaps more readily than she would have desire.
among those be Major Loder (unattached), and Captain Rook (late of
the Rifles), who might be see any day on the Dike, smoking and star
at the woman, and who speedily get an introduction to the hospitable
board and select circle of Mr. Joseph Sedley. in fact they would take
no denial; they burst into the house whether Becky be at home or not,
walk into Mrs. Osborne's drawing-room, which they perfume with their
coat and mustachio, call Jos "old buck," and invade his
dinner-table, and laugh and drink for long hour there.
"what can they mean?" ask Georgy, who do not like these gentleman.
"I hear the Major say to Mrs. Crawley yesterday, 'no, no, Becky, you
shallnot keep the old buck to yourself. we must have the bone in, or,
dammy, Iwill split.' what could the Major mean, Mamma?"
"Major! donot call he Major!" Emmy say. "Ibe sure I cannot tell what
he mean." his presence and that of his friend inspire the little lady
with intolerable terror and aversion. they pay her tipsy compliment;
they leer at she over the dinner-table. and the Captain make she
advance that fill she with sicken dismay, nor would she ever see
he unless she have George by her side.
Rebecca, to do her justice, never would let either of these man remain
alone with Amelia; the Major be disengage too, and swear he would be
the winner of she. a couple of ruffian be fight for this innocent
creature, gamble for she at her own table, and though she be not
aware of the rascal' design upon she, yet she feel a horror and
uneasiness in their presence and long to fly.
she besought, she entreat Jos to go. not he. he be slow of
movement, tie to his doctor, and perhaps to some other lead-string.
at least Becky be not anxious to go to England.
at last she take a great resolution--make the great plunge. she write
off a letter to a friend whom she have on the other side of the water, a
letter about which she do not speak a word to anybody, which she
carry herself to the post under her shawl; nor be any remark make
about it, only that she look very much flushed and agitated when
Georgy meet she, and she kiss he, and hang over he a great deal that
night. she do not come out of her room after her return from her
walk. Becky think it be Major Loder and the Captain who frighten
she.
"she mustnot stop here," Becky reason with herself. "she must go
away, the silly little fool. she be still whimper after that gaby
of a husband--dead (and serve right!) these fifteen year. she shallnot
marry either of these man. itbe too bad of Loder. no; she shall marry
the bamboo cane, Iwill settle it this very night."
so Becky take a cup of tea to Amelia in her private apartment and find
that lady in the company of her miniature, and in a most melancholy
and nervous condition. she lay down the cup of tea.
"thank you," say Amelia.
"listen to I, Amelia," say Becky, march up and down the room
before the other and survey she with a sort of contemptuous
kindness. "I want to talk to you. you must go away from here and from
the impertinence of these man. I willnot have you harass by they:
and they will insult you if you stay. I tell you they be rascal: man
fit to send to the hulk. never mind how I know they. I know
everybody. Jos cannot protect you; he be too weak and want a protector
himself. you be no more fit to live in the world than a baby in arm.
you must marry, or you and your precious boy will go to ruin. you must
have a husband, you fool; and one of the good gentleman I ever see have
offer you a hundred time, and you have reject he, you silly,
heartless, ungrateful little creature!"
"I try--I try my good, indeed I do, Rebecca," say Amelia
deprecatingly, "but I couldnot forget--"; and she finish the sentence
by look up at the portrait.
"couldnot forget HIM!" cry out Becky, "that selfish humbug, that
low-breed cockney dandy, that pad booby, who have neither wit, nor
manner, nor heart, and be no more to be compare to your friend with
the bamboo cane than you be to Queen Elizabeth. why, the man be
weary of you, and would have jilt you, but that Dobbin force he to
keep his word. he own it to I. he never care for you. he use to
sneer about you to I, time after time, and make love to I the week
after he marry you."
"itbe false! itbe false! rebecca," cry out Amelia, start up.
"look there, you fool," Becky say, still with provoke good humour,
and take a little paper out of her belt, she open it and fling it
into Emmy's lap. "you know his handwriting. he write that to
I--want I to run away with he--give it I under your nose, the day
before he be shoot--and serve he right!" Becky repeat.
Emmy do not hear she; she be look at the letter. it be that which
George have put into the bouquet and give to Becky on the night of the
Duchess of Richmond's ball. it be as she say: the foolish young man
have ask she to fly.
Emmy's head sink down, and for almost the last time in which she shall
be call upon to weep in this history, she commence that work. she
head fall to her bosom, and her hand go up to her eye; and there
for a while, she give way to her emotion, as Becky stand on and
regard she. who shall analyse those tear and say whether they be
sweet or bitter? be she most grieve because the idol of her life be
tumble down and shiver at her foot, or indignant that her love have
be so despised, or glad because the barrier be remove which modesty
have place between she and a new, a real affection? "there be nothing
to forbid I now," she think. "I may love he with all my heart now.
oh, I will, I will, if he will but let I and forgive I." I believe it
be this feeling rush over all the other which agitate that gentle
little bosom.
indeed, she do not cry so much as Becky expect--the other soothe
and kiss she--a rare mark of sympathy with Mrs. Becky. she treat
Emmy like a child and pat her head. "and now let we get pen and ink
and write to he to come this minute," she say.
"I--I write to he this morning," Emmy say, blush exceedingly.
Becky scream with laughter--"un biglietto," she sing out with Rosina,
"eccolo qua!"--the whole house echo with her shrill singing.
two morning after this little scene, although the day be rainy and
gusty, and Amelia have have an exceedingly wakeful night, listen to
the wind roaring, and pity all traveller by land and by water, yet
she get up early and insist upon take a walk on the Dike with
Georgy; and there she pace as the rain beat into her face, and she
look out westward across the dark sea line and over the swollen
billow which come tumble and froth to the shore. neither speak
much, except now and then, when the boy say a few word to his timid
companion, indicative of sympathy and protection.
"I hope he willnot cross in such weather," Emmy say.
"I bet ten to one he do," the boy answer. "look, Mother, therebe
the smoke of the steamer." it be that signal, sure enough.
but though the steamer be under way, he might not be on board; he
might not have get the letter; he might not choose to come. a hundred
fear pour one over the other into the little heart, as fast as the
wave on to the Dike.
the boat follow the smoke into sight. georgy have a dandy telescope
and get the vessel under view in the most skilful manner. and he make
appropriate nautical comment upon the manner of the approach of the
steamer as she come near and nearer, dipping and rise in the water.
the signal of an english steamer in sight go flutter up to the
mast on the pier. I daresay Mrs. Amelia's heart be in a similar
flutter.
Emmy try to look through the telescope over George's shoulder, but
she could make nothing of it. she only see a black eclipse bob up
and down before her eye.
George take the glass again and rake the vessel. "how she do pitch!"
he say. "there go a wave slap over her bow. therebe only two
people on deck besides the steersman. therebe a man lie down, and
a--chap in a--cloak with a--Hooray!--it's Dob, by Jingo!" he clap to
the telescope and fling his arm round his mother. as for that lady,
let we say what she do in the word of a favourite poet--"Dakruoen
gelasasa." she be sure it be William. it could be no other. what
she have say about hop that he would not come be all hypocrisy. of
course he would come; what could he do else but come? she know he would
come.
the ship come swiftly near and nearer. as they go in to meet she
at the landing-place at the quay, Emmy's knee tremble so that she
scarcely could run. she would have like to kneel down and say she
prayer of thank there. oh, she think, she would be all her life
say they!
it be such a bad day that as the vessel come alongside of the quay
there be no idler abroad, scarcely even a commissioner on the look
out for the few passenger in the steamer. that young scapegrace
George have flee too, and as the gentleman in the old cloak line with
red stuff step on to the shore, there be scarcely any one present
to see what take place, which be briefly this:
a lady in a dripping white bonnet and shawl, with her two little hand
out before she, go up to he, and in the next minute she have
altogether disappear under the fold of the old cloak, and be
kiss one of his hand with all her might; whilst the other, I
suppose, be engage in hold she to his heart (which her head just
about reach) and in prevent she from tumble down. she be
murmur something about--forgive--dear William--dear, dear, dearest
friend--kiss, kiss, kiss, and so forth--and in fact go on under the
cloak in an absurd manner.
when Emmy emerge from it, she still keep tight hold of one of
William's hand, and look up in his face. it be full of sadness and
tender love and pity. she understand its reproach and hang down her
head.
"it be time you send for I, dear Amelia," he say.
"you will never go again, William?"
"no, never," he answer, and press the dear little soul once more to
his heart.
as they issue out of the custom-house precinct, Georgy break out on
they, with his telescope up to his eye, and a loud laugh of welcome; he
dance round the couple and perform many facetious antic as he lead
they up to the house. Jos benot up yet; Becky not visible (though she
look at they through the blind). georgy run off to see about
breakfast. Emmy, whose shawl and bonnet be off in the passage in the
hand of Mrs. Payne, now go to undo the clasp of William's cloak,
and--we will, if you please, go with George, and look after breakfast
for the Colonel. the vessel be in port. he have get the prize he have
be try for all his life. the bird have come in at last. there it
be with its head on his shoulder, billing and coo close up to his
heart, with soft outstretche flutter wing. this be what he have
ask for every day and hour for eighteen year. this be what he pin
after. here it be--the summit, the end--the last page of the third
volume. good-bye, Colonel--God bless you, honest william!--farewell,
dear Amelia--grow green again, tender little parasite, round the rugged
old oak to which you cling!
perhaps it be compunction towards the kind and simple creature, who
have be the first in life to defend she, perhaps it be a dislike to
all such sentimental scene--but Rebecca, satisfied with her part in
the transaction, never present herself before Colonel Dobbin and the
lady whom he marry. "particular business," she say, take she to
bruge, whither she go, and only Georgy and his uncle be present at
the marriage ceremony. when it be over, and Georgy have rejoin his
parent, Mrs. Becky return (just for a few day) to comfort the
solitary bachelor, Joseph Sedley. he prefer a continental life, he
say, and decline to join in housekeeping with his sister and her
husband.
Emmy be very glad in her heart to think that she have write to her
husband before she read or know of that letter of George's. "I know it
all along," William say; "but could I use that weapon against the poor
fellow's memory? it be that which make I suffer so when you--"
"never speak of that day again," Emmy cry out, so contrite and humble
that William turn off the conversation by his account of Glorvina and
dear old Peggy O'Dowd, with whom he be sit when the letter of
recall reach he. "if you havenot send for I," he add with a
laugh, "who know what Glorvina's name might be now?"
at present it be Glorvina Posky (now Mrs. Major Posky); she take he on
the death of his first wife, have resolve never to marry out of the
regiment. Lady O'Dowd be also so attached to it that, she say, if
anything be to happen to Mick, bedad shewould come back and marry some
of them. but the Major-General be quite well and live in great
splendour at O'Dowdstown, with a pack of beagle, and (with the
exception of perhaps their neighbour, Hoggarty of Castle Hoggarty) he
be the first man of his county. her ladyship still dance jig, and
insist on stand up with the Master of the Horse at the Lord
Lieutenant's last ball. both she and Glorvina declare that Dobbin have
use the latter SHEAMFULLY, but Posky fall in, Glorvina be
console, and a beautiful turban from Paris appease the wrath of Lady
O'Dowd.
when Colonel Dobbin quit the service, which he do immediately after
his marriage, he rent a pretty little country place in Hampshire, not
far from Queen's Crawley, where, after the passing of the Reform Bill,
Sir Pitt and his family constantly reside now. all idea of a peerage
be out of the question, the Baronet's two seat in Parliament be
lose. he be both out of pocket and out of spirit by that
catastrophe, fail in his health, and prophesy the speedy ruin of
the Empire.
Lady Jane and Mrs. Dobbin become great friend--there be a perpetual
crossing of pony-chaise between the Hall and the Evergreens, the
Colonel's place (rent of his friend Major Ponto, who be abroad with
his family). her ladyship be godmother to Mrs. Dobbin's child, which
bear her name, and be christen by the Rev. James Crawley, who
succeed his father in the living: and a pretty close friendship
subsist between the two lad, George and Rawdon, who hunt and shoot
together in the vacation, be both enter of the same college at
Cambridge, and quarrel with each other about Lady Jane's daughter,
with whom they be both, of course, in love. a match between George
and that young lady be long a favourite scheme of both the matron,
though I have hear that Miss Crawley herself incline towards her
cousin.
Mrs. Rawdon Crawley's name be never mention by either family. there
be reason why all should be silent regard she. for wherever Mr.
Joseph Sedley go, she travel likewise, and that infatuated man
seem to be entirely her slave. the Colonel's lawyer inform he
that his brother-in-law have effect a heavy insurance upon his life,
whence it be probable that he have be raise money to discharge
debt. he procure prolonged leave of absence from the East India
House, and indeed, his infirmity be daily increase.
on hear the news about the insurance, Amelia, in a good deal of
alarm, entreat her husband to go to Brussels, where Jos then be, and
inquire into the state of his affair. the Colonel quit home with
reluctance (for he be deeply immerse in his history of the Punjaub
which still occupy he, and much alarmed about his little daughter,
whom he idolize, and who be just recover from the chicken-pox) and
go to Brussels and find Jos live at one of the enormous hotel in
that city. Mrs. Crawley, who have her carriage, give entertainment,
and live in a very genteel manner, occupy another suite of
apartment in the same hotel.
the Colonel, of course, do not desire to see that lady, or even think
proper to notify his arrival at Brussels, except privately to Jos by a
message through his valet. Jos beg the Colonel to come and see he
that night, when Mrs. Crawley would be at a soiree, and when they could
meet alone. he find his brother-in-law in a condition of pitiable
infirmity--and dreadfully afraid of Rebecca, though eager in his
praise of she. she tend he through a series of unheard-of
illness with a fidelity most admirable. she have be a daughter to
he. "but--but--oh, for God's sake, do come and live near I,
and--and--see I sometimes," whimper out the unfortunate man.
the Colonel's brow darken at this. "we cannot, Jos," he say.
"consider the circumstance, Amelia cannot visit you."
"I swear to you--I swear to you on the Bible," gasp out Joseph,
want to kiss the book, "that she be as innocent as a child, as
spotless as your own wife."
"it may be so," say the Colonel gloomily, "but Emmy cannot come to you.
be a man, Jos: break off this disreputable connection. come home to
your family. we hear your affair be involve."
"involve!" cry Jos. "who have tell such calumny? all my money be
place out most advantageously. Mrs. Crawley--that be--I mean--it be
lay out to the good interest."
"you be not in debt, then? why do you insure your life?"
"I think--a little present to she--in case anything happen; and you
know my health be so delicate--common gratitude you know--and I intend
to leave all my money to you--and I can spare it out of my income,
indeed I can," cry out William's weak brother-in-law.
the Colonel beseech Jos to fly at once--to go back to India, whither
Mrs. Crawley could not follow he; to do anything to break off a
connection which might have the most fatal consequence to he.
Jos clasp his hand and cry, "he would go back to India. he would
do anything, only he must have time: they mustnot say anything to Mrs.
Crawley--she'd--she'd kill I if she know it. you donot know what a
terrible woman she be," the poor wretch say.
"then, why not come away with I?" say Dobbin in reply; but Jos have
not the courage. "he would see Dobbin again in the morning; he must on
no account say that he have be there. he must go now. Becky might
come in." and Dobbin quit he, full of foreboding.
he never see Jos more. three month afterwards Joseph Sedley die at
Aix-la-Chapelle. it be find that all his property have be muddle
away in speculation, and be represent by valueless share in
different bubble company. all his available asset be the two
thousand pound for which his life be insure, and which be leave
equally between his beloved "sister Amelia, wife of, &c., and his
friend and invaluable attendant during sickness, Rebecca, wife of
Lieutenant-Colonel Rawdon Crawley, C.B.," who be appoint
administratrix.
the solicitor of the insurance company swear it be the black case
that ever have come before he, talk of send a commission to Aix to
examine into the death, and the Company refuse payment of the policy.
but Mrs., or Lady Crawley, as she style herself, come to town at once
(attend with her solicitor, Messrs. Burke, Thurtell, and Hayes, of
Thavies Inn) and dare the Company to refuse the payment. they invite
examination, they declare that she be the object of an infamous
conspiracy, which have be pursue she all through life, and triumph
finally. the money be pay, and her character establish, but
Colonel Dobbin send back his share of the legacy to the insurance
office and rigidly decline to hold any communication with Rebecca.
she never be Lady Crawley, though she continue so to call herself.
his Excellency Colonel Rawdon Crawley die of yellow fever at Coventry
Island, most deeply beloved and deplore, and six week before the
demise of his brother, Sir Pitt. the estate consequently devolve upon
the present Sir Rawdon Crawley, Bart.
he, too, have decline to see his mother, to whom he make a liberal
allowance, and who, besides, appear to be very wealthy. the Baronet
live entirely at Queen's Crawley, with Lady Jane and her daughter,
whilst Rebecca, Lady Crawley, chiefly hang about Bath and Cheltenham,
where a very strong party of excellent people consider she to be a most
injure woman. she have her enemy. who have not? her life be her
answer to they. she busy herself in work of piety. she go to
church, and never without a footman. her name be in all the Charity
Lists. the destitute orange-girl, the neglect washerwoman, the
distressed muffin-man find in she a fast and generous friend. she be
always have stall at Fancy Fairs for the benefit of these hapless
being. Emmy, her child, and the Colonel, come to London some
time back, find themselves suddenly before she at one of these fair.
she cast down her eye demurely and smile as they start away from
she; Emmy scurry off on the arm of George (now grow a dash young
gentleman) and the Colonel seize up his little Janey, of whom he be
fonder than of anything in the world--fonder even than of his history
of the Punjaub.
"fonder than he be of I," Emmy think with a sigh but he never say a
word to Amelia that be not kind and gentle, or thought of a want of
her that he do not try to gratify.
ah! Vanitas Vanitatum! which of we be happy in this world? which of
we have his desire? or, have it, be satisfied?--come, child, let we
shut up the box and the puppet, for our play be play out.
